cardiovascular-system.title: Cardiovascular System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### The Heart
- **Location**: Mediastinum, between lungs; approximately 2/3 lies left of midline
- **Size**: Roughly the size of a fist (~12 cm long, ~250-350 g)
- **Pericardium**: Three-layered sac
  - Fibrous pericardium (outer, tough connective tissue)
  - Parietal pericardium (serous membrane lining the fibrous layer)
  - Visceral pericardium (epicardium; directly on heart surface)
  - **Pericardial cavity**: Contains ~15-50 mL serous fluid to reduce friction

#### Heart Wall Layers
| Layer | Composition | Clinical Relevance |
|-------|------------|-------------------|
| Epicardium | Visceral pericardium; fat, coronary vessels | Coronary arteries run here |
| Myocardium | Cardiac muscle; bulk of heart wall | Site of Myocardial Infarction |
| Endocardium | Simple squamous epithelium | Lines chambers; continuous with vessel endothelium; site of Endocarditis |

#### Chambers
- **Right atrium (RA)**: Receives deoxygenated blood via SVC, IVC, coronary sinus
- **Right ventricle (RV)**: Pumps blood to lungs via pulmonary trunk; thinner wall (~3-5 mm)
- **Left atrium (LA)**: Receives oxygenated blood via 4 pulmonary veins
- **Left ventricle (LV)**: Pumps blood to systemic circulation via aorta; thickest wall (~13-15 mm)
- **Interventricular septum**: Separates ventricles; muscular and membranous portions

#### Valves
| Valve | Type | Location | Auscultation Point |
|-------|------|----------|-------------------|
| Tricuspid | AV (3 cusps) | RA to RV | Left sternal border, 4th ICS |
| Mitral (bicuspid) | AV (2 cusps) | LA to LV | Left MCL, 5th ICS (apex) |
| Pulmonic | Semilunar (3 cusps) | RV to pulmonary trunk | Left sternal border, 2nd ICS |
| Aortic | Semilunar (3 cusps) | LV to aorta | Right sternal border, 2nd ICS |

- **Chordae tendineae**: Fibrous cords anchoring AV valve leaflets to papillary muscles
- **Papillary muscles**: Prevent valve prolapse during systole

#### Conduction System
1. **SA node** (sinoatrial): Right atrium, near SVC opening; intrinsic rate 60-100 bpm; the "pacemaker"
2. **AV node** (atrioventricular): Floor of right atrium, near interatrial septum; intrinsic rate 40-60 bpm; delays impulse ~0.1 sec
3. **Bundle of His** (AV bundle): Passes through fibrous skeleton into interventricular septum
4. **Right and left bundle branches**: Travel down interventricular septum
5. **Purkinje fibers**: Spread through ventricular myocardium; intrinsic rate 20-40 bpm

#### Blood Vessels
**Arteries**
- **Elastic (conducting) arteries**: Aorta, pulmonary trunk, common carotid, subclavian, common iliac; thick walls with elastic laminae; maintain pressure during diastole (Windkessel effect)
- **Muscular (distributing) arteries**: Most named arteries; more smooth muscle, less elastic tissue; regulate regional blood flow
- **Arterioles**: Smallest arteries; primary site of peripheral resistance; regulate blood flow to capillary beds

**Capillaries**
- **Continuous**: Most common; tight junctions; found in muscle, skin, lungs, CNS (form Blood-Brain Barrier)
- **Fenestrated**: Pores (fenestrations) in endothelium; found in kidneys, intestines, endocrine glands; allow rapid exchange
- **Sinusoidal (discontinuous)**: Large, irregular gaps; found in liver, spleen, bone marrow; allow passage of large molecules and cells

**Veins**
- Thinner walls than arteries; larger lumens; contain valves to prevent backflow
- **Venules** → **Medium veins** → **Large veins** (SVC, IVC)
- Hold ~60-65% of total blood volume ("capacitance vessels")

### Blood Supply

#### Coronary Circulation
| Artery | Branches | Territory Supplied |
|--------|----------|-------------------|
| Left main coronary artery (LMCA) | LAD (left anterior descending) + LCx (left circumflex) | |
| LAD | Diagonal branches, septal perforators | Anterior LV wall, anterior 2/3 of septum, apex |
| LCx | Obtuse marginal branches | Lateral and posterior LV wall, LA |
| Right coronary artery (RCA) | Acute marginal, PDA (in 85% - right dominant) | RA, RV, inferior LV, posterior 1/3 septum, SA node (60%), AV node (80%) |

- **Clinical Pearl**: LAD is the "widow maker" -- occlusion causes massive anterior MI
- Coronary arteries fill during **diastole** (compressed during systole)

#### Coronary Venous Drainage
- Great cardiac vein → Coronary sinus → Right atrium
- Small and middle cardiac veins also drain into coronary sinus
- Anterior cardiac veins drain directly into RA

#### Blood (Ch 19)

**Composition**
- **Plasma** (~55% of whole blood)
  - 92% water
  - 7% plasma proteins: albumin (60%), globulins (36%), fibrinogen (4%)
  - Electrolytes, nutrients, gases, hormones, waste products
- **Formed elements** (~45% of whole blood)
  - RBCs (erythrocytes): 4.2-6.1 million/mcL
  - WBCs (leukocytes): 5,000-10,000/mcL
  - Platelets (thrombocytes): 150,000-400,000/mcL

**Erythrocytes (RBCs)**
- Biconcave discs; anucleate; 120-day lifespan
- Contain **hemoglobin** (Hgb): 4 heme groups (each with Fe2+) + 4 globin chains
  - Oxyhemoglobin (HbO2): bright red
  - Deoxyhemoglobin (Hb): dark red/blue
  - Carboxyhemoglobin (HbCO): cherry red (carbon monoxide poisoning)
  - Methemoglobin (MetHb): Fe3+, cannot carry O2
- **Erythropoiesis**: Kidney senses hypoxia → releases **erythropoietin (EPO)** → stimulates red bone marrow → reticulocytes → mature RBCs in ~1-2 days
- Requires: iron, vitamin B12, folic acid, vitamin B6

**Leukocytes (WBCs)**
| Type | % of WBCs | Function | Clinical Significance |
|------|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Neutrophils** | 60-70% | Phagocytosis; first responders | Elevated: bacterial infection; "Left shift" = immature bands |
| **Lymphocytes** | 20-25% | T-cells, B-cells, NK cells; adaptive immunity | Elevated: viral infection |
| **Monocytes** | 3-8% | Become macrophages; phagocytosis | Elevated: chronic inflammation |
| **Eosinophils** | 2-4% | Parasitic defense; allergy modulation | Elevated: parasites, allergies |
| **Basophils** | 0.5-1% | Release histamine and heparin | Elevated: allergic reactions |

- Mnemonic: **N**ever **L**et **M**onkeys **E**at **B**ananas (most to least abundant)

**Platelets (Thrombocytes)**
- Cell fragments from megakaryocytes; lifespan 8-12 days
- Essential for primary hemostasis (platelet plug formation)

**Hemostasis** (3 stages)
1. **Vascular spasm**: Smooth muscle contraction; reduces blood flow
2. **Platelet plug formation**: Adhesion (via vWF) → activation → aggregation
3. **Coagulation cascade**:
   - Intrinsic pathway (contact activation; measured by **PTT/aPTT**)
   - Extrinsic pathway (tissue factor; measured by **PT/INR**)
   - Common pathway → prothrombin → **thrombin** → fibrinogen → **fibrin** clot
   - Clot retraction and fibrinolysis (plasmin dissolves clot)

**Blood Types**
| Type | Antigens on RBC | Antibodies in Plasma | Can Receive | Can Donate To |
|------|----------------|---------------------|-------------|---------------|
| A | A antigen | Anti-B | A, O | A, AB |
| B | B antigen | Anti-A | B, O | B, AB |
| AB | A and B antigens | Neither (universal recipient) | A, B, AB, O | AB |
| O | Neither | Anti-A and Anti-B (universal donor) | O | A, B, AB, O |

- **Rh factor**: D antigen; Rh+ (present) or Rh- (absent)
- **Clinical Pearl**: Rh- mother with Rh+ fetus → risk of hemolytic disease of the newborn; give **RhoGAM** (anti-D immunoglobulin) at 28 weeks and within 72 hours postpartum

### Innervation
- **Sympathetic** (cardioacceleratory center, medulla):
  - SA node, AV node, myocardium
  - **Norepinephrine** on beta-1 receptors → increases HR (positive chronotropy), increases contractility (positive inotropy), increases conduction velocity (positive dromotropy)
- **Parasympathetic** (cardioinhibitory center, medulla):
  - Vagus nerve (CN X) to SA node and AV node
  - **Acetylcholine** on muscarinic (M2) receptors → decreases HR (negative chronotropy), slows AV conduction (negative dromotropy)
  - Minimal effect on ventricular contractility
- **Baroreceptor reflex**: Stretch receptors in carotid sinus and aortic arch detect pressure changes → reflex adjustment of HR and vasomotor tone
- **Chemoreceptors**: Carotid and aortic bodies; detect changes in O2, CO2, pH

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — BP = CO × SVR; everything in cardiovascular physiology is a manipulation of these two variables. Electricity and Excitability — the heart's mechanical pump is driven by coordinated electrical signaling; SA node automaticity sets rate while the specialized conduction system ensures synchronous contraction. Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — the cardiovascular system's primary purpose is DO2: CO × (Hgb × SaO2 × 1.34); cardiac output is one of the two levers.*

### Normal Function

#### Cardiac Cycle
- **Systole** (contraction/ejection): ~0.3 sec
  - Isovolumetric contraction → ventricular ejection
- **Diastole** (relaxation/filling): ~0.5 sec
  - Isovolumetric relaxation → rapid ventricular filling → atrial contraction ("atrial kick" contributes ~20-25% of ventricular filling)
- At 75 bpm, one complete cycle = ~0.8 sec

#### Heart Sounds
| Sound | Cause | Timing |
|-------|-------|--------|
| S1 ("lub") | Closure of AV valves (mitral and tricuspid) | Beginning of systole |
| S2 ("dub") | Closure of semilunar valves (aortic and pulmonic) | End of systole |
| S3 | Rapid ventricular filling (normal in young adults; pathologic >40 yo) | Early diastole; associated with Heart Failure |
| S4 | Atrial contraction against stiff ventricle | Late diastole; associated with Hypertension, Aortic Stenosis |

- **Murmurs**: Turbulent blood flow through abnormal valves or septal defects; graded I-VI

#### Cardiac Output
- **CO = HR x SV** (normal: 4-8 L/min)
- **Stroke Volume (SV)**: Volume ejected per beat (~70 mL); determined by:
  - **Preload**: Degree of ventricular stretch at end-diastole (Frank-Starling mechanism: increased stretch → increased contractile force, up to a point)
  - **Afterload**: Resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood (primarily determined by SVR/aortic pressure)
  - **Contractility**: Intrinsic strength of contraction (inotropy)
- **Ejection Fraction (EF)**: SV/EDV x 100; normal = 55-70%
  - <40% = reduced EF (Heart Failure with reduced EF, HFrEF)
  - 40-49% = mildly reduced EF (HFmrEF)
  - ≥50% = preserved EF (HFpEF)

#### Blood Pressure
- **BP = CO x SVR** (systemic vascular resistance)
- Normal: <120/80 mmHg
- **Elevated**: 120-129/<80
- **Stage 1 HTN**: 130-139/80-89
- **Stage 2 HTN**: ≥140/≥90
- **Hypertensive crisis**: >180/>120
- **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)**: MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP); normal 70-105 mmHg; minimum for organ perfusion ~60 mmHg

#### Hemodynamics
- Blood flow governed by **pressure gradient** and **resistance** (Poiseuille's law)
- Resistance is most influenced by **vessel radius** (r^4 relationship)
- Laminar vs. turbulent flow; turbulence increases with velocity, vessel diameter, and decreased viscosity
- **Capillary exchange**: Starling forces
  - **Hydrostatic pressure**: Pushes fluid OUT of capillary
  - **Oncotic (colloid osmotic) pressure**: Pulls fluid INTO capillary (primarily albumin)
  - Net filtration at arteriolar end; net reabsorption at venular end
  - Excess filtered fluid returned via Lymphatic and Immune System

#### Venous Return
- Mechanisms: skeletal muscle pump, respiratory pump, venous valves, venoconstriction, cardiac suction
- **Clinical Pearl**: Immobility impairs the skeletal muscle pump → venous stasis → risk of Deep Vein Thrombosis

### Key Regulatory Mechanisms

#### Short-term Regulation (Neural and Hormonal)
- **Baroreceptor reflex**: Rapid BP adjustment (seconds)
- **Chemoreceptor reflex**: Responds to hypoxemia, hypercapnia, acidosis
- **Sympathetic activation**: Increases HR, contractility, SVR (vasoconstriction)
- **Parasympathetic activation**: Decreases HR
- **Adrenal medulla**: Epinephrine and norepinephrine release

#### Long-term Regulation (Renal and Hormonal)
- **RAAS (Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System)**:
  - Low renal perfusion → renin → angiotensinogen → angiotensin I → (ACE in lungs) → **angiotensin II** → vasoconstriction + aldosterone release → Na+/H2O retention → increased blood volume and BP
- **ADH (Antidiuretic hormone/vasopressin)**: Released from posterior pituitary → water retention in collecting ducts; also causes vasoconstriction at high levels
- **ANP/BNP (Atrial/Brain Natriuretic Peptides)**: Released by atrial/ventricular stretch → vasodilation, natriuresis, diuresis → decrease blood volume and BP
  - **BNP/NT-proBNP**: Used as diagnostic marker for Heart Failure
- **Aldosterone**: From adrenal cortex → Na+ retention, K+ excretion in kidneys

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges

| Lab Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Hemoglobin (Hgb) | M: 14-18 g/dL; F: 12-16 g/dL | Low: Anemia; High: polycythemia |
| Hematocrit (Hct) | M: 42-52%; F: 37-47% | ~3x Hgb value |
| RBC count | M: 4.7-6.1 million/mcL; F: 4.2-5.4 million/mcL | |
| WBC count | 5,000-10,000/mcL | Elevated: infection, inflammation; Low: immunosuppression |
| Platelet count | 150,000-400,000/mcL | Low: bleeding risk; High: clotting risk |
| PT | 11-13.5 seconds | Extrinsic pathway; monitors Warfarin therapy |
| INR | 0.8-1.1 (therapeutic on warfarin: 2-3) | Standardized PT ratio |
| aPTT | 30-40 seconds | Intrinsic pathway; monitors Heparin therapy |
| Fibrinogen | 200-400 mg/dL | Low in DIC, liver disease |
| D-dimer | <500 ng/mL | Elevated: fibrinolysis; used to rule out DVT/PE |
| Troponin I/T | <0.04 ng/mL (varies by assay) | Elevated: myocardial injury (Myocardial Infarction) |
| CK-MB | <5% of total CK | Cardiac muscle damage marker |
| BNP | <100 pg/mL | >400 pg/mL strongly suggests Heart Failure |
| NT-proBNP | <300 pg/mL (age-dependent) | Heart failure marker |
| Total cholesterol | <200 mg/dL | Cardiovascular risk factor |
| LDL | <100 mg/dL (optimal) | "Bad" cholesterol; target for statin therapy |
| HDL | >40 mg/dL (M); >50 mg/dL (F) | "Good" cholesterol |
| Triglycerides | <150 mg/dL | Elevated: cardiovascular risk |
| CRP/hs-CRP | <1.0 mg/L (low risk) | Inflammatory marker; cardiovascular risk |
| Lactate | 0.5-2.0 mmol/L | Elevated: tissue hypoperfusion, Shock |
| Type & Screen / Crossmatch | — | Required before transfusion |
| Reticulocyte count | 0.5-2.5% | Bone marrow RBC production; elevated in hemolysis/bleeding |

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- **Chest pain**: PQRST assessment (Provocation, Quality, Region/Radiation, Severity, Timing)
  - Cardiac chest pain: substernal pressure/squeezing, radiating to left arm/jaw/back, associated with dyspnea, diaphoresis, nausea
  - Women and diabetics may present with **atypical symptoms**: fatigue, dyspnea, indigestion, back pain
- **Dyspnea**: On exertion, at rest, orthopnea (number of pillows), PND (paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea)
- **Edema**: Location, pitting vs. non-pitting, timing
- **Palpitations**: Regular vs. irregular, duration, associated symptoms
- **Syncope/presyncope**: Vasovagal, orthostatic, cardiac arrhythmia
- **Claudication**: Leg pain with walking (peripheral arterial disease)
- **Risk factors**: HTN, DM, hyperlipidemia, smoking, obesity, family history, sedentary lifestyle, age, gender

### Physical Exam
- **Inspection**: JVD (>4 cm above sternal angle at 45 degrees = elevated), skin color (pallor, cyanosis, mottling), capillary refill (<3 sec normal), edema
- **Palpation**: PMI (point of maximal impulse, normally 5th ICS at MCL; displaced = cardiomegaly), peripheral pulses (0-4+ scale), temperature of extremities
- **Auscultation**:
  - Heart sounds in 5 areas: Aortic (R 2nd ICS), Pulmonic (L 2nd ICS), Erb's point (L 3rd ICS), Tricuspid (L 4th ICS), Mitral/Apex (L 5th ICS MCL)
  - Mnemonic: **A**ll **P**atients **E**njoy **T**aking **M**edicine
  - Note murmurs, gallops (S3, S4), friction rubs (Pericarditis)
- **Blood pressure**: Both arms; orthostatic vitals (drop ≥20 systolic or ≥10 diastolic on standing = orthostatic hypotension)
- **Peripheral vascular**: Pulse quality, bruits (carotid), Ankle-Brachial Index (<0.9 = PAD), Homan's sign (unreliable for DVT)

### Key Diagnostics
| Test | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| 12-lead ECG | Rhythm, rate, ischemia, MI (ST changes), conduction abnormalities |
| Echocardiogram | Chamber size, EF, wall motion, valve function, pericardial effusion |
| Chest X-ray | Heart size (cardiothoracic ratio), pulmonary congestion, pleural effusions |
| Cardiac catheterization | Gold standard for coronary artery assessment; angioplasty/stenting |
| Stress test (exercise or pharmacologic) | Exercise tolerance, ischemia provocation |
| CT angiography | Non-invasive coronary assessment, aortic dissection, PE |
| Holter/event monitor | Ambulatory rhythm monitoring (24-48 hr or longer) |
| TEE (transesophageal echo) | Better visualization of posterior structures, valves, clots in LA |
| Cardiac MRI | Tissue characterization, viability assessment |
| Venous duplex ultrasound | DVT evaluation |
| Arterial Doppler | PAD evaluation |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Cardiac
- Coronary Artery Disease
- Myocardial Infarction (STEMI, NSTEMI)
- Angina Pectoris (stable, unstable)
- Heart Failure (HFrEF, HFpEF)
- Atrial Fibrillation
- Atrial Flutter
- Supraventricular Tachycardia
- Ventricular Tachycardia
- Ventricular Fibrillation
- Heart Block (1st, 2nd, 3rd degree)
- Hypertension
- Valvular Heart Disease (stenosis, regurgitation)
- Endocarditis
- Pericarditis
- Cardiomyopathy
- Cardiac Tamponade
- Aortic Aneurysm
- Aortic Dissection

### Vascular
- Peripheral Arterial Disease
- Deep Vein Thrombosis
- Pulmonary Embolism
- Varicose Veins
- Chronic Venous Insufficiency
- Shock (cardiogenic, hypovolemic, distributive, obstructive)
- Raynaud's Phenomenon

### Hematologic
- Anemia (iron deficiency, B12 deficiency, folate deficiency, aplastic, hemolytic, sickle cell)
- Polycythemia Vera
- Thrombocytopenia
- Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation
- Hemophilia
- Von Willebrand Disease
- Sickle Cell Disease
- Transfusion Reactions

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
### Cardiac / Antihypertensive
- Beta-Blockers (metoprolol, atenolol, carvedilol) -- decrease HR, contractility, BP; first-line for HFrEF, post-MI, rate control
- ACE Inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril) -- block RAAS; decrease afterload/preload; first-line for HFrEF, HTN, post-MI; monitor for cough, hyperkalemia, angioedema
- ARBs (losartan, valsartan) -- alternative to ACE-I; block angiotensin II receptors
- Calcium Channel Blockers (amlodipine, diltiazem, verapamil) -- vasodilation, decreased HR (non-dihydropyridines)
- Diuretics -- thiazides (HCTZ), loop (furosemide), K+-sparing (spironolactone); volume management
- Nitrates (nitroglycerin, isosorbide) -- venodilation; decrease preload; angina relief
- Digoxin -- positive inotropy, negative chronotropy; used in HF and afib; narrow therapeutic index (0.8-2.0 ng/mL)
- Antiarrhythmics (amiodarone, lidocaine, adenosine, procainamide)
- ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan) -- neprilysin inhibitor + ARB; HFrEF
- Hydralazine / Isosorbide Dinitrate -- vasodilators; HFrEF in African Americans
- SGLT2 Inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin) -- cardiorenal benefit in HFrEF

### Anticoagulants / Antiplatelets
- Aspirin -- irreversible COX inhibitor; antiplatelet; ACS, secondary prevention
- Clopidogrel (Plavix) -- P2Y12 inhibitor; dual antiplatelet therapy post-stent
- Heparin (unfractionated and LMWH/enoxaparin) -- potentiates antithrombin III; acute anticoagulation; monitor aPTT (UFH) or anti-Xa (LMWH)
- Warfarin (Coumadin) -- vitamin K antagonist; monitor INR (goal 2-3); interacts with many foods/drugs
- DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) -- direct oral anticoagulants; afib, DVT/PE; fewer monitoring requirements
- Thrombolytics (alteplase, tenecteplase) -- fibrinolytics; used in STEMI, massive PE, acute ischemic stroke

### Lipid-Lowering
- Statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin) -- HMG-CoA reductase inhibitors; first-line for hyperlipidemia; monitor LFTs, CK
- PCSK9 Inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab)
- Ezetimibe -- cholesterol absorption inhibitor
- Fibrates (fenofibrate) -- primarily lower triglycerides

### Hematologic
- Iron Supplements (ferrous sulfate, IV iron)
- Erythropoiesis-Stimulating Agents (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin)
- Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin)
- Folic Acid
- Blood Products (PRBCs, FFP, platelets, cryoprecipitate)

---

clinicalPearls: **Troponin** is the gold standard biomarker for myocardial injury; rises within 3-6 hours, peaks at 12-24 hours, remains elevated 7-14 days
**12-lead ECG** should be obtained within **10 minutes** of presentation with chest pain
In acute MI, think **MONA** (Morphine, Oxygen if SpO2 <94%, Nitroglycerin, Aspirin) -- note: routine morphine use is now debated
**Right-sided MI** (associated with inferior MI): Do NOT give nitroglycerin or diuretics; these patients are preload-dependent; give fluids
**Orthostatic vital signs**: Check after lying flat for 5 minutes, then immediately upon standing and after 3 minutes standing
Normal sinus rhythm on ECG: regular R-R intervals, P wave before every QRS, QRS <0.12 sec, PR interval 0.12-0.20 sec
**Virchow's triad** for thrombosis: stasis, hypercoagulability, endothelial injury
**Allen's test**: Assess collateral ulnar blood flow before radial artery procedures (ABG, arterial line)
When hanging blood products: use a **Y-type tubing** with **0.9% NS** only (never LR or dextrose); complete transfusion within **4 hours**; monitor vitals per protocol (baseline, 15 min, 30 min, end)

**Transfusion reaction signs**: fever, chills, urticaria, tachycardia, hypotension, back/flank pain, hemoglobinuria → STOP transfusion immediately, maintain IV access with NS, notify provider and blood bank
Potassium imbalances directly affect cardiac rhythm:
[[Hyperkalemia]]: Peaked T waves → widened QRS → sine wave → cardiac arrest; treat with calcium gluconate, insulin/glucose, albuterol, kayexalate, dialysis
[[Hypokalemia]]: Flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression → risk of torsades de pointes
**Never** give IV potassium as a bolus push; always dilute and infuse slowly; max peripheral rate usually 10 mEq/hr
Assess for **Homan's sign** is no longer recommended as a DVT screening tool due to poor sensitivity and specificity; use Wells criteria and D-dimer instead

endocrine-system.title: Endocrine System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Hypothalamus
- Located in diencephalon, below thalamus, forms floor and walls of 3rd ventricle
- Master regulator: Links nervous system to endocrine system via pituitary gland
- Releases **releasing hormones** (CRH, TRH, GnRH, GHRH) and **inhibiting hormones** (somatostatin, dopamine) into hypothalamic-hypophyseal portal system
- Also produces ADH and oxytocin (stored in posterior pituitary)

#### Pituitary Gland (Hypophysis)
- Located in sella turcica of sphenoid bone; connected to hypothalamus via infundibulum (pituitary stalk)

| Division | Hormones | Target / Function |
|----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Anterior pituitary (adenohypophysis)** | | |
| | Growth Hormone (GH) | Liver, bone, muscle; promotes growth, protein synthesis, lipolysis; stimulates IGF-1 |
| | Thyroid-Stimulating Hormone (TSH) | Thyroid gland; stimulates T3/T4 synthesis and release |
| | Adrenocorticotropic Hormone (ACTH) | Adrenal cortex; stimulates cortisol release |
| | Follicle-Stimulating Hormone (FSH) | Ovaries/testes; follicle development, spermatogenesis |
| | Luteinizing Hormone (LH) | Ovaries/testes; ovulation, testosterone production |
| | Prolactin (PRL) | Mammary glands; milk production |
| **Posterior pituitary (neurohypophysis)** | | Stores and releases hormones made by hypothalamus |
| | Antidiuretic Hormone (ADH / vasopressin) | Kidneys (collecting ducts); water reabsorption; also vasoconstriction at high levels |
| | Oxytocin | Uterus (contractions), mammary glands (milk ejection); bonding |

- **Mnemonic (anterior pituitary)**: FLAT PiG -- FSH, LH, ACTH, TSH, Prolactin, GH

#### Thyroid Gland
- Butterfly-shaped; anterior neck overlying trachea (C5-T1 level); two lobes connected by isthmus
- **Follicular cells**: Produce T3 (triiodothyronine) and T4 (thyroxine); require iodine
- **Parafollicular cells (C cells)**: Produce calcitonin (lowers serum calcium; opposes PTH)
- T4 is the predominant circulating form; converted to active T3 peripherally
- Thyroid hormones regulate metabolic rate, thermogenesis, growth, and development

#### Parathyroid Glands
- 4 small glands on posterior surface of thyroid
- **Chief cells**: Produce parathyroid hormone (PTH)
- **PTH actions**: Increases serum calcium by (1) stimulating osteoclast bone resorption, (2) increasing renal calcium reabsorption, (3) stimulating renal 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D production (increases intestinal calcium absorption)
- **Clinical Pearl**: Accidental removal during thyroidectomy can cause hypocalcemia -- monitor for Chvostek's sign (facial twitching on CN VII tap) and Trousseau's sign (carpopedal spasm with BP cuff inflation)

#### Adrenal Glands
- Sit atop each kidney; composed of cortex (outer) and medulla (inner)

| Layer | Hormones | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Cortex -- Zona Glomerulosa** | Aldosterone (mineralocorticoid) | Na+ retention, K+ excretion, water retention → increases BP |
| **Cortex -- Zona Fasciculata** | Cortisol (glucocorticoid) | Glucose metabolism, anti-inflammatory, stress response, immunosuppression |
| **Cortex -- Zona Reticularis** | Androgens (DHEA) | Secondary sex characteristics |
| **Medulla** | Epinephrine (80%), norepinephrine (20%) | Sympathetic fight-or-flight response |

- **Mnemonic (cortex layers)**: GFR -- Glomerulosa, Fasciculata, Reticularis; "Salt, Sugar, Sex" (from superficial to deep)
- **Cortisol diurnal rhythm**: Highest in early morning (~6-8 AM), lowest at midnight

#### Pancreas (Endocrine)
- **Islets of Langerhans** (~1-2% of pancreatic mass; ~1 million islets)
  - **Alpha cells**: Glucagon (increases blood glucose via glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis)
  - **Beta cells**: Insulin (decreases blood glucose via cellular glucose uptake, glycogenesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis)
  - **Delta cells**: Somatostatin (inhibits both insulin and glucagon secretion)
  - **PP cells**: Pancreatic polypeptide (regulates pancreatic exocrine secretion)

#### Gonads
- **Ovaries**: Estrogen, progesterone (see Reproductive System)
- **Testes**: Testosterone (see Reproductive System)

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — most peptide hormones (glucagon, TSH, LH, FSH, PTH) signal via GPCRs; lipid-soluble hormones (steroids, thyroid) use nuclear receptors. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — endocrine regulation IS negative feedback: HPT axis, HPA axis, HPG axis are all classic feedback loops; gland secretes hormone → hormone acts → hormone feeds back to suppress further secretion.*

### Hormone Types
| Type | Characteristics | Examples | Receptor Location |
|------|----------------|---------|-------------------|
| Peptide/protein | Water-soluble; cannot cross cell membrane; act via second messengers (cAMP, IP3) | Insulin, GH, ADH, PTH, ACTH | Cell surface receptors |
| Steroid | Lipid-soluble (cholesterol-derived); cross cell membrane; alter gene transcription | Cortisol, aldosterone, estrogen, testosterone, vitamin D | Intracellular (nuclear) receptors |
| Amine | Derived from tyrosine or tryptophan | Thyroid hormones (T3/T4), catecholamines (epi, NE), melatonin | T3/T4: nuclear; catecholamines: cell surface |

### Feedback Loops

#### Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Adrenal (HPA) Axis
- Hypothalamus → CRH → Anterior pituitary → ACTH → Adrenal cortex → **Cortisol**
- Cortisol exerts negative feedback on both hypothalamus and anterior pituitary
- Disruption: Excess = Cushing Syndrome; deficiency = Addison Disease

#### Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Thyroid (HPT) Axis
- Hypothalamus → TRH → Anterior pituitary → TSH → Thyroid → **T3/T4**
- T3/T4 exert negative feedback on hypothalamus and anterior pituitary
- **Primary hypothyroidism**: Low T3/T4, HIGH TSH (gland failure)
- **Secondary hypothyroidism**: Low T3/T4, LOW TSH (pituitary failure)
- **Graves disease**: Autoantibodies stimulate TSH receptors → high T3/T4, LOW TSH

#### Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal (HPG) Axis
- Hypothalamus → GnRH → Anterior pituitary → FSH/LH → Gonads → **Estrogen/Progesterone/Testosterone**
- Sex steroids exert negative feedback (except positive feedback of estrogen surge → LH surge → ovulation)

### Insulin and Glucagon Regulation
| State | Dominant Hormone | Effects |
|-------|-----------------|---------|
| **Fed (postprandial)** | Insulin | Promotes glucose uptake (GLUT4 translocation in muscle/adipose), glycogenesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis; inhibits gluconeogenesis |
| **Fasting** | Glucagon | Promotes glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis, lipolysis, ketogenesis |

- **Normal fasting glucose**: 70-100 mg/dL
- **Insulin does NOT require** for glucose uptake in: brain, liver, RBCs, kidneys (GLUT1 and GLUT2 transporters are insulin-independent)
- **Counter-regulatory hormones** (oppose insulin): Glucagon, cortisol, GH, epinephrine

### ADH (Vasopressin) Regulation
- Stimulus for release: Increased serum osmolality (detected by hypothalamic osmoreceptors), decreased blood volume/pressure
- Action: Inserts aquaporin-2 channels in renal collecting duct → water reabsorption → concentrated urine
- Deficiency: Diabetes Insipidus (central or nephrogenic) → dilute polyuria, hypernatremia
- Excess (SIADH): Water retention → dilutional hyponatremia

### Aldosterone Regulation
- Stimulated by: Angiotensin II (RAAS), hyperkalemia, ACTH (minor)
- Action: Collecting duct → Na+ reabsorption, K+ and H+ secretion
- Connected to Renal System RAAS and Cardiovascular System blood pressure regulation

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- **Metabolic symptoms**: Polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia (3 Ps of diabetes), unexplained weight changes, fatigue, heat/cold intolerance
- **Growth/development**: Growth patterns, menstrual history, sexual development milestones
- **Skin changes**: Striae, hyperpigmentation (Addison Disease), acanthosis nigricans (insulin resistance), pretibial myxedema (Graves Disease)
- **Medication history**: Corticosteroid use (exogenous Cushing Syndrome), thyroid medications, insulin/oral hypoglycemics
- **Family history**: Diabetes, thyroid disease, autoimmune conditions

### Physical Exam
- **Vital signs**: HTN (Cushing's, pheochromocytoma), tachycardia (hyperthyroidism), orthostatic hypotension (Addison's)
- **Thyroid palpation**: Size, symmetry, nodules, tenderness; auscultate for bruit (Graves)
- **Eyes**: Exophthalmos / proptosis (Graves Disease), periorbital edema (Hypothyroidism)
- **Skin**: Turgor, moisture (diaphoresis vs. dry), pigmentation changes, wound healing
- **Neurological**: Deep tendon reflexes (hyperactive in hyperthyroidism, delayed relaxation in hypothyroidism), tremor
- **Body habitus**: Central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump (Cushing Syndrome); thin, weight loss (hyperthyroidism, DKA, Addison's)

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges

| Lab Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Fasting blood glucose | 70-100 mg/dL | >=126 on two occasions = diabetes mellitus |
| Random blood glucose | <200 mg/dL | >=200 + symptoms = diabetes mellitus |
| HbA1c | 4-5.6% | 5.7-6.4% = prediabetes; >=6.5% = diabetes; reflects ~3-month average glucose |
| TSH | 0.4-4.0 mIU/L | High: primary hypothyroidism; Low: hyperthyroidism or secondary hypothyroidism |
| Free T4 | 0.8-1.8 ng/dL | Confirms thyroid status; preferred over total T4 |
| Free T3 | 2.3-4.2 pg/mL | May be elevated in T3 thyrotoxicosis |
| Cortisol (AM) | 5-25 mcg/dL | High: Cushing's; Low: Addison's; 24-hr urine or salivary cortisol for screening |
| ACTH | 10-60 pg/mL | Differentiates Cushing's: high ACTH = pituitary or ectopic; low ACTH = adrenal tumor |
| Serum sodium | 136-145 mEq/L | Low: SIADH, Addison's; High: diabetes insipidus |
| Serum potassium | 3.5-5.0 mEq/L | High: Addison's, DKA; Low: Cushing's, aldosteronism |
| Serum calcium | 8.5-10.5 mg/dL | High: hyperparathyroidism; Low: hypoparathyroidism, post-thyroidectomy |
| Serum phosphorus | 2.5-4.5 mg/dL | Inversely related to calcium (PTH-mediated) |
| PTH (intact) | 15-65 pg/mL | High + high Ca = primary hyperparathyroidism |
| Serum osmolality | 275-295 mOsm/kg | High: DI, hyperglycemia; Low: SIADH |
| Urine specific gravity | 1.005-1.030 | Low (<1.005): DI; High (>1.030): dehydration, SIADH |

### Key Diagnostics
| Test | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| Oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) | Gestational diabetes screening; 2-hr value >=200 = diabetes |
| Dexamethasone suppression test | Screen for Cushing's (cortisol fails to suppress) |
| ACTH stimulation test (cosyntropin) | Diagnose Addison's (cortisol fails to rise) |
| Thyroid uptake and scan (RAI) | Differentiate causes of hyperthyroidism (diffuse = Graves; focal = toxic nodule) |
| Thyroid ultrasound | Evaluate nodules, guide FNA biopsy |
| Water deprivation test | Differentiate central vs. nephrogenic DI |
| Pituitary MRI | Evaluate pituitary tumors, empty sella |
| CT adrenals | Adrenal masses, hyperplasia |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Diabetes
- Type 1 Diabetes (autoimmune beta-cell destruction; insulin-dependent)
- Type 2 Diabetes (insulin resistance ± relative insulin deficiency)
- Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA: primarily T1DM; hyperglycemia + metabolic acidosis + ketonemia)
- Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Syndrome (HHS: primarily T2DM; severe hyperglycemia + hyperosmolality + dehydration without significant ketosis)
- Hypoglycemia (glucose <70 mg/dL; treat with Rule of 15)

### Thyroid
- Hypothyroidism (Hashimoto's thyroiditis most common cause; fatigue, cold intolerance, weight gain, bradycardia, constipation)
- Graves Disease (autoimmune hyperthyroidism; weight loss, heat intolerance, tachycardia, tremor, exophthalmos)
- Thyroid Storm (life-threatening thyrotoxicosis; high fever, extreme tachycardia, altered mental status)
- Myxedema Coma (severe hypothyroidism; hypothermia, bradycardia, altered LOC)

### Adrenal
- Cushing Syndrome (cortisol excess; central obesity, moon facies, buffalo hump, striae, hyperglycemia, immunosuppression)
- Addison Disease (primary adrenal insufficiency; hypotension, hyperpigmentation, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, fatigue)
- Adrenal Crisis (acute adrenal insufficiency; shock, severe hypotension -- EMERGENCY; treat with IV hydrocortisone and fluids)
- Pheochromocytoma (catecholamine-secreting adrenal medulla tumor; paroxysmal HTN, headache, diaphoresis, tachycardia)

### Pituitary / Hypothalamic
- Diabetes Insipidus (ADH deficiency or resistance; polyuria, polydipsia, hypernatremia)
- SIADH (excess ADH; water retention, dilutional hyponatremia)
- Pituitary Adenoma (may cause hormone excess or deficiency; visual field defects from optic chiasm compression)

### Parathyroid
- Hyperparathyroidism (PTH excess; hypercalcemia: "bones, stones, groans, psychiatric moans")
- Hypoparathyroidism (PTH deficiency; hypocalcemia: tetany, seizures, QT prolongation)

### Reproductive Endocrine
- PCOS (androgen excess, insulin resistance, menstrual irregularity, polycystic ovaries)

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
### Diabetes Medications
- Insulin -- types: rapid-acting (lispro, aspart; onset 15 min), short-acting (regular; onset 30 min), intermediate (NPH; onset 1-2 hr), long-acting (glargine, detemir; onset 1-2 hr, no peak)
  - **Clinical Pearl**: Only regular insulin can be given IV; always verify dose with another nurse; never shake insulin vials
- Oral Hypoglycemics -- metformin (first-line T2DM; reduces hepatic glucose output; hold before contrast; risk of lactic acidosis), sulfonylureas (glipizide, glyburide; stimulate insulin release; hypoglycemia risk), DPP-4 inhibitors, GLP-1 agonists (semaglutide, liraglutide), SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin, dapagliflozin; cardiorenal benefits)

### Thyroid Medications
- Thyroid Medications -- levothyroxine (T4 replacement; take on empty stomach 30-60 min before breakfast; monitor TSH q6-8 weeks initially), methimazole/PTU (antithyroid drugs; PTU preferred in 1st trimester pregnancy), radioactive iodine (ablative therapy for Graves)

### Adrenal / Steroid Medications
- Systemic Corticosteroids (prednisone, hydrocortisone, dexamethasone) -- replacement in Addison's; anti-inflammatory; many side effects with chronic use (hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, immunosuppression, adrenal suppression); NEVER stop abruptly if on chronic therapy (taper required to avoid adrenal crisis)
- **Fludrocortisone**: Mineralocorticoid replacement for Addison's

### Calcium / Bone Metabolism
- **Calcium + Vitamin D supplementation**: For hypoparathyroidism, osteoporosis prevention
- **Calcitonin**: For acute hypercalcemia (limited long-term efficacy)
- **Bisphosphonates**: For hypercalcemia of malignancy and osteoporosis (see Musculoskeletal System)

---

clinicalPearls: **DKA vs. HHS**: DKA = type 1 (usually), ketones present, pH <7.30, rapid onset; HHS = type 2, no significant ketones, serum osm >320, more gradual onset with severe dehydration; both need IV fluids, insulin drip, and potassium replacement
**Rule of 15**: For hypoglycemia (<70 mg/dL): Give 15 g fast-acting carbs → recheck glucose in 15 minutes → repeat if still <70; follow with protein/complex carb snack once stable

**Sick day rules for diabetes**: Never stop insulin entirely during illness; monitor glucose more frequently (q4h); stay hydrated; contact provider if glucose >300, persistent vomiting, or signs of DKA
**Steroid taper**: Patients on systemic corticosteroids for >2 weeks should NEVER stop abruptly; gradual taper required to allow HPA axis recovery; risk of adrenal crisis with sudden discontinuation

**Thyroid storm triggers**: Surgery, infection, trauma, radioactive iodine therapy, medication non-adherence; treat with beta-blockers, PTU, iodine (given AFTER PTU), corticosteroids, cooling measures

**Myxedema coma triggers**: Infection, cold exposure, sedatives, non-adherence to levothyroxine; treat with IV levothyroxine, IV hydrocortisone (rule out concurrent adrenal insufficiency), supportive care

**Adrenal crisis**: Can be triggered by abrupt steroid withdrawal, physiologic stress in patients with adrenal insufficiency, or pituitary apoplexy; treat immediately with IV hydrocortisone 100 mg bolus + NS fluid resuscitation
**Morning cortisol draw**: Must be collected at 6-8 AM (peak cortisol time) for accurate interpretation
**Insulin storage**: Unopened vials in refrigerator; in-use vials at room temperature for 28-42 days (varies by type); never freeze; inspect for particles or discoloration
Patients with [[Diabetes Insipidus]] may have urine output exceeding 3-20 L/day; monitor strict I&Os, daily weights, serum sodium, and urine specific gravity

gastrointestinal-system.title: Gastrointestinal System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Oral Cavity
- **Teeth**: Mechanical digestion (mastication); 32 permanent teeth (incisors, canines, premolars, molars)
- **Tongue**: Manipulates food; taste buds (CN VII anterior 2/3, CN IX posterior 1/3); assists in swallowing
- **Salivary glands**: Parotid (serous, largest), submandibular (mixed), sublingual (mucous)
  - Saliva: Water, mucus, salivary amylase (begins starch digestion), lysozyme, IgA
  - ~1-1.5 L/day; pH 6.2-7.4

#### Esophagus
- Muscular tube ~25 cm from pharynx to stomach; passes through diaphragm at esophageal hiatus (T10)
- **Upper esophageal sphincter (UES)**: Skeletal muscle; prevents air entry
- **Lower esophageal sphincter (LES)**: Smooth muscle; prevents gastric reflux; incompetent LES → GERD
- Mucosal lining: Stratified squamous epithelium (transitions to columnar at gastroesophageal junction)
- **Clinical Pearl**: Barrett's esophagus = metaplasia of squamous → columnar epithelium from chronic GERD; precancerous (risk of esophageal adenocarcinoma)

#### Stomach
- Regions: Cardia, fundus, body, antrum (pyloric region)
- **Pyloric sphincter**: Controls gastric emptying into duodenum
- **Rugae**: Mucosal folds allowing stomach expansion (~1-1.5 L capacity)
- **Gastric glands**:
  - **Parietal cells**: HCl (activates pepsinogen, kills bacteria, denatures proteins) and intrinsic factor (required for vitamin B12 absorption in ileum)
  - **Chief cells**: Pepsinogen (activated to pepsin by HCl; begins protein digestion)
  - **G cells** (antrum): Gastrin (stimulates HCl secretion)
  - **Mucous neck cells**: Alkaline mucus barrier (protects gastric mucosa from acid)
  - **D cells**: Somatostatin (inhibits gastric acid secretion)
- **Clinical Pearl**: Loss of parietal cells (autoimmune gastritis) → loss of intrinsic factor → pernicious anemia (B12 deficiency)

#### Small Intestine
- ~6 meters long; primary site of chemical digestion and nutrient absorption
- Surface area amplification: Circular folds (plicae circulares) → villi → microvilli (brush border)

| Segment | Length | Key Features |
|---------|--------|-------------|
| **Duodenum** | ~25 cm (C-shaped) | Receives chyme from stomach; ampulla of Vater (common bile duct + pancreatic duct); Brunner's glands (alkaline mucus to neutralize gastric acid); most chemical digestion occurs here |
| **Jejunum** | ~2.5 m | Primary absorption site; most nutrient absorption (carbs, amino acids, fatty acids, iron, folate); thickest walls, most plicae circulares |
| **Ileum** | ~3.5 m | Absorbs bile salts, vitamin B12 (with intrinsic factor), remaining nutrients; Peyer's patches (lymphoid tissue / MALT); ileocecal valve |

#### Large Intestine
- ~1.5 m long; cecum → ascending colon → transverse colon → descending colon → sigmoid colon → rectum → anal canal
- **Cecum**: Pouch at junction with ileum; appendix attached (lymphoid tissue)
- **Functions**: Water and electrolyte absorption, vitamin K synthesis (by gut flora), feces formation and storage
- **Haustra**: Segmental pouches from incomplete longitudinal muscle (teniae coli)
- **Internal anal sphincter**: Smooth muscle (involuntary)
- **External anal sphincter**: Skeletal muscle (voluntary); pudendal nerve (S2-S4)

#### Liver
- Largest internal organ (~1.5 kg); right upper quadrant; 4 lobes (right, left, caudate, quadrate)
- **Dual blood supply**: Hepatic artery (25%, oxygenated) and portal vein (75%, nutrient-rich from GI tract)
- **Functional unit**: Hepatic lobule; hepatocytes arranged around central vein; portal triads at periphery (hepatic artery branch, portal vein branch, bile duct)
- Functions: Bile production (~500-1000 mL/day), drug/toxin metabolism, protein synthesis (albumin, clotting factors), glycogen storage, bilirubin conjugation, cholesterol metabolism, ammonia conversion to urea

#### Gallbladder
- Pear-shaped; on inferior surface of liver; stores and concentrates bile (~30-50 mL capacity)
- **Bile**: Bile salts (emulsify fats), bilirubin, cholesterol, phospholipids
- Stimulated by **cholecystokinin (CCK)** from duodenal I-cells in response to fat/protein in chyme
- **Common bile duct** = cystic duct + common hepatic duct → enters duodenum at ampulla of Vater (sphincter of Oddi)

#### Pancreas (Exocrine)
- Retroperitoneal organ; head (in C-curve of duodenum), body, tail (near spleen)
- **Exocrine** (~98% of pancreatic tissue): Acinar cells produce digestive enzymes
  - Pancreatic lipase (fats), trypsinogen/chymotrypsinogen (proteins, activated in duodenum), pancreatic amylase (starch), nucleases
  - Ductal cells produce bicarbonate-rich fluid (neutralizes gastric acid)
- Main pancreatic duct (of Wirsung) joins common bile duct at ampulla of Vater
- **Endocrine** function: See Endocrine System

#### Peritoneum
- **Visceral peritoneum**: Covers organs
- **Parietal peritoneum**: Lines abdominal wall
- **Peritoneal cavity**: Potential space containing serous fluid
- **Mesentery**: Double fold of peritoneum; suspends intestines; carries blood vessels, nerves, lymphatics
- **Retroperitoneal structures**: Kidneys, adrenals, aorta, IVC, pancreas (most), duodenum (2nd-4th parts), ascending/descending colon, ureters, rectum
  - Mnemonic: **SAD PUCKER** -- Suprarenal glands, Aorta/IVC, Duodenum (2nd-4th), Pancreas, Ureters, Colon (asc/desc), Kidneys, Esophagus, Rectum

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — the GI tract is the primary site of drug absorption; gastric pH, motility, surface area, and first-pass hepatic metabolism all determine oral bioavailability. Nutrition and Metabolism — digestion is the enzymatic breakdown of macronutrients into absorbable units; read the Nutrition concept for the biochemical detail of each nutrient pathway.*

### Digestion

#### Mechanical Digestion
- Mastication (oral cavity), churning and peristalsis (stomach), segmentation (small intestine)
- **Peristalsis**: Coordinated smooth muscle contractions (longitudinal relaxation + circular contraction behind bolus) propelling contents forward
- **Segmentation**: Mixing contractions in small intestine; increases contact with mucosal surface

#### Chemical Digestion
| Nutrient | Enzymes/Agents | Location | Products |
|----------|---------------|----------|----------|
| Carbohydrates | Salivary amylase | Mouth | Maltose, dextrins |
| | Pancreatic amylase | Small intestine | Maltose, dextrins |
| | Brush border enzymes (maltase, sucrase, lactase) | Small intestine | Monosaccharides (glucose, fructose, galactose) |
| Proteins | Pepsin (from pepsinogen + HCl) | Stomach | Peptide fragments |
| | Trypsin, chymotrypsin, carboxypeptidase | Small intestine | Small peptides, amino acids |
| | Brush border peptidases | Small intestine | Amino acids |
| Fats | Lingual lipase | Mouth (minor) | Fatty acids, glycerol |
| | Bile salts (emulsification) + pancreatic lipase | Small intestine | Monoglycerides, fatty acids → micelles → absorbed |

### Absorption
- **Small intestine**: >90% of all nutrient absorption
  - Carbohydrates and amino acids → capillaries → portal vein → liver
  - Fats → lacteals (lymphatic) → thoracic duct → systemic circulation (bypasses liver initially)
  - Iron: Duodenum; enhanced by vitamin C; inhibited by calcium, tannins
  - Calcium: Duodenum; requires vitamin D
  - Vitamin B12: Terminal ileum; requires intrinsic factor
  - Bile salts: Terminal ileum (enterohepatic circulation recycles ~95% of bile salts)
- **Large intestine**: Water (~1.5 L/day), Na+, K+, vitamin K (bacterial synthesis)

### Hepatic Function and Portal Circulation
- **Portal circulation**: GI blood (from stomach, intestines, spleen, pancreas) → portal vein → liver sinusoids → hepatic veins → IVC
- **First-pass metabolism**: Oral drugs absorbed from GI tract pass through liver before systemic circulation; may be extensively metabolized
- **Bilirubin metabolism**: Heme (from RBC breakdown) → unconjugated bilirubin (insoluble, bound to albumin) → liver conjugates with glucuronic acid → conjugated bilirubin (water-soluble) → excreted in bile → intestinal bacteria convert to urobilinogen → stercobilin (feces color) or reabsorbed and excreted as urobilin (urine color)
- **Clinical Pearl**: Jaundice occurs when total bilirubin >2.5-3 mg/dL; prehepatic (hemolysis → elevated unconjugated), hepatic (hepatocellular damage), or posthepatic (obstruction → elevated conjugated/direct)

### GI Hormones
| Hormone | Source | Stimulus | Action |
|---------|--------|----------|--------|
| Gastrin | G cells (antrum) | Stomach distension, protein | Stimulates HCl and pepsinogen secretion |
| CCK | I cells (duodenum) | Fat and protein in chyme | Stimulates gallbladder contraction, pancreatic enzyme secretion, inhibits gastric emptying |
| Secretin | S cells (duodenum) | Acid in chyme | Stimulates pancreatic bicarbonate secretion, inhibits gastric acid |
| GIP | K cells (duodenum/jejunum) | Glucose, fatty acids | Stimulates insulin release (incretin effect), inhibits gastric acid |
| Motilin | Mo cells (duodenum/jejunum) | Fasting state | Stimulates migrating motor complex (MMC); interdigestive housekeeping |

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- **Abdominal pain**: PQRST; location (quadrant mapping important); associated symptoms (N/V, fever, bowel changes, hematochezia, melena)
- **Nausea/vomiting**: Frequency, timing (relation to meals), character (bilious, feculent, bloody/coffee-ground), associated symptoms
- **Bowel habits**: Frequency, consistency (Bristol Stool Chart), changes, blood, mucus, urgency, incontinence
- **Swallowing**: Dysphagia (solids vs. liquids vs. both; progressive vs. intermittent), odynophagia
- **Appetite/weight changes**: Anorexia, early satiety, unintentional weight loss
- **History of**: Alcohol use, NSAID use, liver disease, GI surgery, family history of GI cancers, inflammatory bowel disease

### Physical Exam -- Abdominal Assessment (in correct order)

**1. Inspection**
- Contour (flat, rounded, distended, scaphoid), symmetry, visible peristalsis, pulsations, skin (striae, scars, spider angiomata, caput medusae [portal HTN], ecchymosis -- Grey Turner [flanks] or Cullen [periumbilical] signs in hemorrhagic pancreatitis)

**2. Auscultation** (performed BEFORE palpation/percussion to avoid altering bowel sounds)
- **Bowel sounds**: All 4 quadrants; normal = 5-30 clicks/gurgles per minute; listen for at least 2 minutes before declaring absent
  - Hyperactive: Early bowel obstruction, diarrhea, GI bleed
  - Hypoactive/absent: Paralytic ileus, peritonitis, post-surgical
  - High-pitched "tinkling": Mechanical bowel obstruction
- **Vascular bruits**: Aortic, renal arteries (stenosis)

**3. Percussion**
- **Tympanic**: Gas-filled bowel (normal over most of abdomen)
- **Dull**: Solid organs (liver, spleen), fluid, masses, full bladder
- **Liver span**: 6-12 cm at right MCL; >12 cm = hepatomegaly
- **Shifting dullness** and **fluid wave**: Ascites detection

**4. Palpation** (light then deep)
- Light: Tenderness, guarding (voluntary vs. involuntary), masses
- Deep: Organ size (liver edge, spleen tip), masses, aortic pulsation
- **Rebound tenderness**: Pain on release of pressure → peritoneal inflammation → may indicate peritonitis
- **Murphy's sign**: Inspiratory arrest during RUQ palpation → acute cholecystitis
- **McBurney's point tenderness**: 1/3 distance from ASIS to umbilicus → appendicitis
- **Rovsing's sign**: RLQ pain with LLQ palpation → appendicitis
- **Psoas sign**: RLQ pain with right hip extension → retrocecal appendicitis

### Nutritional Screening
- **Albumin**: 3.5-5.0 g/dL (half-life ~20 days; reflects chronic nutritional status but also affected by inflammation, liver disease)
- **Prealbumin**: 15-36 mg/dL (half-life ~2 days; better marker of acute nutritional changes)
- **BMI**: Underweight <18.5, normal 18.5-24.9, overweight 25-29.9, obese >=30
- Screening tools: Malnutrition Universal Screening Tool (MUST), Subjective Global Assessment (SGA)

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges
| Lab Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| AST (SGOT) | 10-40 U/L | Hepatocellular damage (also cardiac, skeletal muscle) |
| ALT (SGPT) | 7-56 U/L | More specific for liver damage |
| ALP (alkaline phosphatase) | 44-147 U/L | Elevated: biliary obstruction, bone disease |
| GGT | 9-48 U/L | Elevated: biliary disease, alcohol use |
| Total bilirubin | 0.1-1.2 mg/dL | Elevated: hemolysis, hepatocellular disease, obstruction |
| Direct (conjugated) bilirubin | 0-0.3 mg/dL | Elevated: obstructive jaundice |
| Albumin | 3.5-5.0 g/dL | Low: liver disease, malnutrition, nephrotic syndrome |
| PT/INR | PT 11-13.5 sec; INR 0.8-1.1 | Elevated: liver failure (cannot synthesize clotting factors) |
| Ammonia | 15-45 mcg/dL | Elevated: Hepatic Encephalopathy, liver failure |
| Lipase | 0-160 U/L | Elevated: acute pancreatitis (more specific than amylase) |
| Amylase | 25-125 U/L | Elevated: pancreatitis (also elevated in salivary, renal disease) |
| Stool occult blood (FOBT/FIT) | Negative | Positive: GI bleeding, colorectal cancer screening |
| H. pylori testing | Negative | Urea breath test, stool antigen, or endoscopic biopsy |
| CEA | <5 ng/mL | Tumor marker (colorectal cancer monitoring; not screening) |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Esophageal/Gastric
- GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease)
- Peptic Ulcer Disease (gastric and duodenal ulcers)
- H. Pylori Infection
- Upper GI Bleeding (hematemesis, melena; variceal vs. non-variceal)
- Esophageal Varices
- Gastroparesis (delayed gastric emptying; common in diabetic neuropathy)

### Intestinal
- Crohn's Disease (transmural inflammation; any GI segment, skip lesions; fistulas, strictures)
- Ulcerative Colitis (mucosal inflammation; continuous, starting at rectum; bloody diarrhea)
- Small Bowel Obstruction
- Diverticulitis
- Celiac Disease
- Irritable Bowel Syndrome
- Clostridium Difficile Infection
- Colorectal Cancer

### Hepatic/Biliary
- NAFLD (non-alcoholic fatty liver disease) / NASH
- Cirrhosis
- Hepatic Encephalopathy (altered mental status from ammonia accumulation; lactulose is first-line)
- Hepatitis (A, B, C)
- Cholelithiasis / Cholecystitis
- Acute Pancreatitis
- Chronic Pancreatitis

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
### Acid Suppression
- Proton Pump Inhibitors (omeprazole, pantoprazole, esomeprazole) -- irreversibly inhibit H+/K+ ATPase on parietal cells; most potent acid suppression; take 30 min before meals; long-term risks (C. diff, fractures, hypomagnesemia, B12 deficiency)
- **H2 Receptor Antagonists** (famotidine, ranitidine [withdrawn]) -- block histamine receptors on parietal cells; less potent than PPIs
- **Antacids** (calcium carbonate, magnesium hydroxide, aluminum hydroxide) -- neutralize existing acid; rapid but short-acting relief

### Antiemetics
- Antiemetics -- ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist; first-line, check QTc), promethazine (phenothiazine; IM preferred, avoid IV extravasation), metoclopramide (prokinetic + antiemetic; risk of tardive dyskinesia), scopolamine (anticholinergic; motion sickness)

### Bowel Medications
- Laxatives and Bowel Preps -- bulk-forming (psyllium), osmotic (polyethylene glycol, lactulose), stimulant (bisacodyl, senna), stool softeners (docusate); lactulose also used for Hepatic Encephalopathy (promotes ammonia excretion)
- **Antidiarrheals**: Loperamide (avoid in C. diff and dysentery), bismuth subsalicylate

### GI-Specific
- **Sucralfate**: Mucosal protectant; coats ulcer base; take on empty stomach; avoid with other medications (impairs absorption)
- **Misoprostol**: PGE1 analog; prevents NSAID-induced ulcers; contraindicated in pregnancy (abortifacient)
- **H. pylori triple therapy**: PPI + clarithromycin + amoxicillin (or metronidazole) for 14 days
- **5-ASA agents** (mesalamine, sulfasalazine): Maintenance therapy for Ulcerative Colitis
- **Immunomodulators/biologics** (azathioprine, infliximab, adalimumab): For inflammatory bowel disease

---

clinicalPearls: **Abdominal assessment order** is UNIQUE: Inspect → Auscultate → Percuss → Palpate (auscultate BEFORE palpation/percussion to avoid stimulating bowel sounds)
**NG tube placement verification**: Aspirate gastric contents and check pH (<5.5); X-ray confirmation per facility policy before first use for feeding; auscultation alone is NOT reliable
**NPO patient care**: Oral care q2h; assess for dehydration; monitor electrolytes; document strict I&Os
**Acute abdomen red flags**: Rigid, board-like abdomen (peritonitis); absent bowel sounds; rebound tenderness; fever; tachycardia; hemodynamic instability → surgical consultation

**Coffee-ground emesis**: Partially digested blood from upper GI bleed; dark, tarry stools (melena) also indicate upper GI source; bright red blood per rectum (hematochezia) usually lower GI bleed but can be massive upper GI bleed
**Paracentesis**: Therapeutic removal of ascitic fluid; position patient upright; monitor for hypotension; may need albumin replacement if >5 L removed

**Hepatic encephalopathy grading**: Grade I (confusion, mood change) → Grade II (drowsiness, inappropriate behavior) → Grade III (somnolent, marked confusion) → Grade IV (coma); treat with lactulose (goal 2-3 soft stools/day) and rifaximin
**Post-operative ileus**: Normal for 24-72 hours after abdominal surgery; encourage early ambulation; hypokalemia can worsen ileus -- correct electrolytes

**Pancreatitis nursing care**: NPO initially, aggressive IV fluid resuscitation, pain management (avoid morphine controversy -- hydromorphone or fentanyl preferred), monitor for complications (pseudocyst, necrosis, ARDS, renal failure)
**Ranson's criteria** or **APACHE II score**: Predict severity and mortality in acute pancreatitis

integumentary-system.title: Integumentary System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Skin Layers
| Layer | Structure | Key Features |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Epidermis** | Stratified squamous epithelium; avascular | 5 layers (from deep to superficial): stratum basale, spinosum, granulosum, lucidum (thick skin only), corneum; keratinocytes (90%), melanocytes, Langerhans cells (immune), Merkel cells (touch); turnover ~28-30 days |
| **Dermis** | Connective tissue; vascularized | **Papillary layer** (superficial): Loose CT, dermal papillae (fingerprints), capillary loops, Meissner's corpuscles (light touch); **Reticular layer** (deep): Dense irregular CT, collagen + elastin fibers (strength + elasticity), hair follicles, sweat glands, sebaceous glands, Pacinian corpuscles (deep pressure) |
| **Hypodermis (Subcutaneous)** | Not technically skin; deep to dermis | Adipose tissue (insulation, energy storage, shock absorption); anchors skin to underlying structures; SubQ injection site |

#### Epidermis — Key Cell Types
| Cell | Location | Function |
|------|----------|----------|
| **Keratinocytes** | All layers; originate in stratum basale | Produce keratin (waterproofing); tight junctions form barrier; majority of epidermal cells |
| **Melanocytes** | Stratum basale | Produce melanin → transferred to keratinocytes via melanosomes; UV protection; melanin amount (not melanocyte number) determines skin color |
| **Langerhans cells** | Stratum spinosum | Dendritic cells; antigen presentation; immune surveillance; first line of adaptive immunity in skin |
| **Merkel cells** | Stratum basale | Sensory touch receptors; slow-adapting mechanoreceptors |

#### Accessory Structures
- **Hair**: Shaft + root + follicle; arrector pili muscle (goosebumps); growth phases: anagen (active growth), catagen (regression), telogen (rest/shedding)
- **Nails**: Keratinized epithelial cells; nail matrix → nail plate; pink color from underlying capillary bed; capillary refill time (CRT) assessed at nail bed
- **Sweat glands**:
  - **Eccrine**: Most of body; watery sweat; thermoregulation; open directly to skin surface
  - **Apocrine**: Axillae, groin, areolae; thicker secretion into hair follicle; activated at puberty; bacterial breakdown → body odor
- **Sebaceous glands**: Attached to hair follicles; produce sebum (oil); lubricate skin/hair; obstruction → acne

#### Blood Supply
- Dermal plexuses supply papillary and reticular dermis
- Critical for thermoregulation: Vasodilation → heat dissipation; vasoconstriction → heat conservation
- Skin receives ~5% of cardiac output (can increase dramatically for cooling)

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Wound Healing — the skin's response to injury follows a precise sequence (hemostasis → inflammation → proliferation → remodeling); disruption of any phase leads to chronic wounds or excessive scarring. Inflammation and Immune Response — the skin is the body's outermost immune interface; keratinocytes, Langerhans cells, and mast cells coordinate the innate inflammatory response to breach of the barrier.*

### Functions of the Integumentary System
| Function | Mechanism |
|----------|-----------|
| **Protection** | Physical barrier (keratin, tight junctions); chemical barrier (acidic pH ~4-6, antimicrobial peptides); biological barrier (Langerhans cells, resident microbiome) |
| **Thermoregulation** | Eccrine sweat glands (evaporative cooling); dermal vasodilation/constriction; insulation (adipose tissue, body hair) |
| **Sensation** | Mechanoreceptors (touch, pressure, vibration), thermoreceptors, nociceptors (pain), proprioceptors |
| **Vitamin D synthesis** | UV-B → 7-dehydrocholesterol in epidermis → cholecalciferol (D3) → liver (25-OH) → kidney (1,25-OH calcitriol) |
| **Excretion** | Minor: urea, salt, water via sweat glands |
| **Immune function** | Skin-associated lymphoid tissue (SALT); Langerhans cells; antimicrobial peptides (defensins) |
| **Absorption** | Limited: lipid-soluble substances, medications (transdermal patches -- fentanyl, nitroglycerin, estrogen, nicotine, scopolamine) |

### Wound Healing Phases
See Wound Healing for detailed coverage. Summary:
1. **Hemostasis** (immediate): Vasoconstriction, platelet plug, fibrin clot
2. **Inflammation** (0-3 days): Neutrophils → macrophages; debris removal; redness, warmth, swelling, pain
3. **Proliferation** (3-21 days): Granulation tissue, angiogenesis, collagen deposition, epithelialization, wound contraction
4. **Remodeling/Maturation** (21 days - 2 years): Collagen reorganization; scar strengthens (max ~80% of original tensile strength)

**Healing types:**
- **Primary intention**: Clean, closed edges (surgical incision, sutured laceration); minimal scarring
- **Secondary intention**: Open wound fills from bottom up with granulation tissue (pressure injury, abscess cavity); larger scar
- **Tertiary intention (delayed primary)**: Left open initially (contaminated wound), then closed once clean

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- Skin complaints: Rash, lesions, itching, color changes, wounds, hair/nail changes
- Sun exposure history, tanning bed use
- Previous skin cancer, family history
- Allergies (contact dermatitis triggers)
- Medications affecting skin (corticosteroids → thin/fragile; anticoagulants → bruising; chemotherapy → mucositis, alopecia)
- Wound history: Duration, treatment, healing progress, recurrence

### Physical Exam — Skin Assessment
- **Inspection**: Color (pallor, cyanosis, jaundice, erythema, hyperpigmentation), moisture, lesion characteristics (type, distribution, pattern, color, borders)
- **Palpation**: Temperature, texture, turgor (tenting = dehydration -- assess over sternum or forehead in elderly, not dorsal hand), moisture, edema, thickness
- **Capillary refill time**: < 3 seconds normal; > 3 seconds suggests poor peripheral perfusion

### Describing Skin Lesions
| Primary Lesion | Size/Features | Example |
|---------------|-------------|---------|
| Macule | Flat, < 1 cm, color change | Freckle, flat mole |
| Patch | Flat, > 1 cm | Vitiligo, port-wine stain |
| Papule | Elevated, solid, < 1 cm | Wart, elevated mole |
| Plaque | Elevated, flat-topped, > 1 cm | Psoriasis |
| Nodule | Solid, 1-2 cm, extends into dermis | Lipoma, cyst |
| Vesicle | Fluid-filled, < 1 cm | Herpes, varicella |
| Bulla | Fluid-filled, > 1 cm | Burn blister, pemphigus |
| Pustule | Pus-filled | Acne, impetigo |
| Wheal | Irregular, edematous, transient | Hive (urticaria) |

### Wound Assessment (Document Every Assessment)
- **Location and size**: Length × width × depth (in cm); clock orientation for undermining/tunneling (12 o'clock = toward head)
- **Wound bed**: % granulation (red, beefy), epithelial (pink/pearly), slough (yellow), eschar (black)
- **Exudate**: Amount (none, scant, moderate, copious), type (serous, sanguineous, serosanguineous, purulent)
- **Periwound skin**: Intact, macerated, erythematous, indurated, calloused
- **Odor**: Foul odor suggests infection or necrosis
- **Pain**: At wound site and with dressing changes

### Pressure Injury Staging (NPUAP)
| Stage | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| **Stage 1** | Intact skin with non-blanchable erythema; may be warmer/cooler, firmer/softer than surrounding skin |
| **Stage 2** | Partial-thickness; loss of dermis; shallow open wound with red-pink bed; may present as intact or ruptured serum-filled blister; NO slough or eschar |
| **Stage 3** | Full-thickness; subcutaneous fat may be visible; slough may be present but does NOT obscure depth; may include undermining/tunneling; bone/tendon/muscle NOT visible |
| **Stage 4** | Full-thickness with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle; slough or eschar may be present; often includes undermining/tunneling |
| **Unstageable** | Full-thickness; wound base covered by slough and/or eschar so true depth cannot be determined; must debride to stage |
| **Deep Tissue Injury (DTI)** | Intact or non-intact skin with persistent non-blanchable deep red/maroon/purple discoloration or blood-filled blister; damage from pressure/shear to underlying tissue |

### Braden Scale (Pressure Injury Risk)
| Parameter | Scoring |
|-----------|---------|
| Sensory perception, moisture, activity, mobility, nutrition, friction/shear | 6-23; lower score = higher risk; ≤ 18 = at risk; ≤ 12 = high risk |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Wounds and Pressure Injuries
- Pressure injuries (see staging above); sacrum, heels, ischial tuberosities, occiput most common
- Chronic Wounds; surgical wounds; Burns

### Infections
- Cellulitis (spreading infection of dermis/SubQ; erythema, warmth, swelling, pain; S. aureus, Group A strep)
- Abscess (localized collection; I&D + antibiotics)
- Impetigo (superficial; honey-crusted; contagious; children)
- MRSA skin infections (see MRSA)
- Herpes zoster (shingles; reactivated VZV; dermatomal distribution; vesicular; post-herpetic neuralgia)
- Fungal (tinea, candidiasis)

### Inflammatory
- Contact Dermatitis (irritant vs. allergic); eczema/atopic dermatitis; psoriasis; Urticaria

### Malignant
- Basal cell carcinoma (most common skin cancer; pearly, rolled borders; rarely metastasizes)
- Squamous cell carcinoma (scaly, crusty lesion; can metastasize; UV exposure + immunosuppression)
- Melanoma (most lethal; ABCDEs: Asymmetry, Border irregularity, Color variation, Diameter > 6mm, Evolving; early detection critical)

### Thermal Injury
- Burns -- see Conditions note for full management

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
- **Topical corticosteroids** (hydrocortisone, triamcinolone, clobetasol): Anti-inflammatory; potency ranges from mild (OTC hydrocortisone) to super-potent (clobetasol); skin atrophy with prolonged use; do not use high potency on face/intertriginous areas
- **Topical antibiotics** (mupirocin, bacitracin): Mupirocin = decolonization of MRSA nasal carriage; wound infection prevention
- **Wound care products**: Hydrocolloids (light exudate, shallow wounds), foams (moderate-heavy exudate), alginates (heavily draining wounds), hydrogels (dry wound beds), silver-containing (antimicrobial), negative pressure wound therapy (VAC)
- **Burn medications**: Silver sulfadiazine (topical antimicrobial for burns; check sulfa allergy; can cause leukopenia); mafenide acetate (penetrates eschar; painful; metabolic acidosis risk)
- Systemic Corticosteroids -- autoimmune skin conditions, severe contact dermatitis

---

clinicalPearls: **Skin is your window to systemic disease.** Jaundice = liver/biliary. Cyanosis = hypoxemia. Pallor = anemia. Petechiae = thrombocytopenia or DIC. Mottling = poor perfusion/shock. Diaphoresis = pain, sympathetic activation, hypoglycemia. Bronze skin = hemochromatosis or Addison's.

**"Stage DOWN, not UP."** Pressure injuries do NOT heal by going backward through stages (e.g., Stage 4 does NOT become Stage 3). Once staged, document healing as "Stage 4 pressure injury with granulation filling 50% of wound bed." Reverse staging is incorrect.

**Pressure injury prevention is a nursing-driven quality measure.** Reposition q2h, use pressure-redistribution surfaces, manage moisture, optimize nutrition (especially protein and vitamin C), and keep Braden Scale current.

**Blanching vs. non-blanching erythema** is the difference between reactive hyperemia (normal -- blood returns after pressure relief) and Stage 1 pressure injury (tissue damage). Press with a gloved finger -- if redness resolves, it's blanching. If it persists, it's Stage 1.

**Burns: Rule of 9's for adults** (head 9%, each arm 9%, chest 18%, back 18%, each leg 18%, perineum 1%). Parkland formula for fluid resuscitation: 4 mL × kg × %TBSA burned → give half in first 8 hours from time of burn (not time of admission).

**Melanoma ABCDEs are for EVERYONE.** Teach patients to do regular skin self-exams. New or changing moles in adults warrant evaluation. Most melanomas are found by patients or partners, not clinicians.
**Skin tears in elderly/steroid-thinned skin** are incredibly common and preventable. Handle gently, use paper tape or silicone tape, avoid adhesive bandages on fragile skin, pad bed rails.

**NEVER put a red medication (betadine) on a wound you need to assess** for color of the wound bed -- it obscures granulation assessment. Use wound cleansing with normal saline or commercial wound cleanser.

lymphatic-and-immune-system.title: Lymphatic and Immune System

sections.anatomy:
### Lymphatic Structures

#### Lymphatic Vessels
- Parallel the venous system; one-way drainage toward the heart
- Begin as blind-ended **lymphatic capillaries** in tissue spaces → collect excess interstitial fluid (lymph)
- Lymphatic capillaries → collecting vessels (with valves) → lymph trunks → **lymphatic ducts**
- **Right lymphatic duct**: Drains right upper body → right subclavian vein
- **Thoracic duct (left lymphatic duct)**: Drains everything else (~75% of body) → left subclavian vein; largest lymphatic vessel; begins at cisterna chyli
- **Lymph**: Clear to pale-yellow fluid; similar to plasma but lower protein; contains lymphocytes, macrophages; ~3 L/day returned to circulation

#### Lymph Nodes
- ~600 in the body; bean-shaped, 1-25 mm
- Clustered along lymphatic vessels; major groups: cervical, axillary, inguinal, mesenteric, mediastinal
- **Structure**: Capsule → cortex (B-cell follicles/germinal centers) → paracortex (T-cells) → medulla (medullary cords with plasma cells and macrophages) → efferent lymphatic vessel
- **Function**: Filter lymph; site of immune cell activation; trap pathogens, cancer cells, foreign particles
- Swollen/tender nodes = active immune response (lymphadenopathy); hard/fixed/non-tender nodes = concern for malignancy

#### Lymphoid Organs
| Organ | Type | Function |
|-------|------|----------|
| **Thymus** | Primary | T-cell maturation and selection; most active in childhood; involutes with age (replaced by adipose); positive and negative selection ensures self-tolerance |
| **Bone marrow** | Primary | Site of all blood cell production (hematopoiesis); B-cell maturation |
| **Spleen** | Secondary | Largest lymphoid organ; filters blood (not lymph); removes old/damaged RBCs; reservoir for platelets (~1/3 stored); immune surveillance; **asplenic patients are at high risk for encapsulated organism infections** (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, N. meningitidis) |
| **Tonsils** | Secondary | Pharyngeal lymphoid tissue (palatine, pharyngeal/adenoids, lingual); first line of defense against ingested/inhaled pathogens |
| **Peyer's patches** | Secondary | Lymphoid tissue in ileum; monitor intestinal antigens; gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) |
| **MALT** | Secondary | Mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue throughout respiratory, GI, and urogenital tracts |

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — the immune system's core function is executing the inflammatory response; innate immunity provides the immediate first wave, adaptive immunity the targeted second wave. Hemodynamics — lymphatics return the ~3 L/day of Starling-filtered interstitial fluid back to circulation; lymphatic obstruction (cancer, filariasis) causes lymphedema by disrupting this hydrostatic/oncotic balance.*

### Innate (Non-Specific) Immunity
Present at birth; responds immediately; no memory; same response every time

| Component | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Physical barriers** | Skin (keratin, acidic pH), mucous membranes, cilia, tears, saliva, gastric acid |
| **Chemical barriers** | Lysozyme (tears, saliva), defensins (antimicrobial peptides), complement proteins, interferons |
| **Cellular defenses** | Neutrophils (first responders; phagocytosis), macrophages (phagocytosis + antigen presentation), natural killer (NK) cells (kill virus-infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization), dendritic cells (antigen presentation to T-cells), eosinophils (parasites, allergic response), basophils/mast cells (histamine release) |
| **Inflammation** | See Inflammation and Immune Response; cardinal signs: redness, heat, swelling, pain, loss of function |
| **Fever** | Pyrogens (IL-1, IL-6, TNF) reset hypothalamic set point; enhances immune function; inhibits pathogen growth |
| **Complement system** | ~30 plasma proteins; cascade activation → opsonization, membrane attack complex (MAC → cell lysis), chemotaxis, inflammation amplification |

### Adaptive (Specific) Immunity
Develops after exposure; specific to antigen; has MEMORY; takes days to mount primary response; faster on subsequent exposures

#### Humoral Immunity (B-Cells → Antibodies)
- B-cells mature in bone marrow
- Activated by antigen binding + T-helper cell interaction → proliferate → differentiate into **plasma cells** (secrete antibodies) and **memory B-cells**
- **Antibodies (Immunoglobulins)**:

| Class | Location | Function |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **IgG** | Blood, tissue fluids | Most abundant (75-80%); crosses placenta (passive immunity to fetus); activates complement; opsonization; secondary immune response |
| **IgA** | Mucosal secretions (saliva, tears, breast milk, GI/respiratory mucosa) | Mucosal immunity; prevents pathogen attachment; passive immunity via breast milk |
| **IgM** | Blood | Largest antibody; first produced in primary immune response; ABO blood group antibodies; potent complement activator |
| **IgE** | Bound to mast cells/basophils | Allergic reactions (Type I hypersensitivity); parasite defense; cross-linking triggers histamine release → anaphylaxis |
| **IgD** | B-cell surface | B-cell activation; function still being studied |

> **Primary response**: IgM first (days), then IgG (weeks). **Secondary response**: Rapid IgG surge from memory B-cells -- faster, stronger, longer-lasting.

#### Cell-Mediated Immunity (T-Cells)
- T-cells mature in thymus
- **CD4+ T-helper cells**: Orchestrate immune response; secrete cytokines; activate B-cells, macrophages, and cytotoxic T-cells; targeted by HIV
- **CD8+ Cytotoxic T-cells**: Directly kill virus-infected cells and tumor cells (perforin/granzyme mechanism)
- **Regulatory T-cells (Tregs)**: Suppress immune response; prevent autoimmunity; maintain self-tolerance
- **Memory T-cells**: Long-lived; rapid response on re-exposure

### Hypersensitivity Reactions
| Type | Mechanism | Timing | Examples |
|------|-----------|--------|---------|
| **Type I (Immediate/Anaphylactic)** | IgE-mediated; mast cell degranulation → histamine | Minutes | Anaphylaxis, allergic rhinitis, asthma, urticaria, food allergy |
| **Type II (Cytotoxic)** | IgG/IgM antibodies against cell surface antigens | Hours | Hemolytic transfusion reaction, hemolytic disease of newborn, autoimmune hemolytic anemia, Goodpasture's, myasthenia gravis |
| **Type III (Immune Complex)** | Antigen-antibody complexes deposit in tissues → complement activation → inflammation | Hours to days | Serum sickness, lupus nephritis, rheumatoid arthritis |
| **Type IV (Delayed/Cell-Mediated)** | T-cell mediated; no antibodies involved | 24-72 hours | Contact dermatitis, TB skin test (PPD), transplant rejection, graft-versus-host disease |

### Vaccination
- **Active immunity**: Body produces its own antibodies and memory cells after antigen exposure (natural infection or vaccination); LONG-LASTING
- **Passive immunity**: Preformed antibodies given (maternal IgG across placenta, breast milk IgA, immunoglobulin infusions); TEMPORARY (weeks to months)
- **Vaccine types**: Live attenuated (MMR, varicella -- contraindicated in immunocompromised), inactivated, subunit/conjugate, mRNA (COVID-19), toxoid (tetanus, diphtheria)

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- Recurrent infections (frequency, severity, unusual organisms)
- Allergies (type, severity, triggers, history of anaphylaxis, EpiPen availability)
- Autoimmune disease history or family history
- Immunosuppressive medications (corticosteroids, chemotherapy, biologics, transplant drugs)
- HIV status, CD4 count if known
- Vaccination history
- Splenectomy history
- Cancer history (hematologic malignancies especially)

### Physical Exam
- **Lymph node assessment**: Palpate cervical, supraclavicular, axillary, inguinal nodes; note size, tenderness, mobility, consistency
  - Tender + mobile + soft = reactive/infectious
  - Hard + fixed + non-tender = concerning for malignancy
  - Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy is ALWAYS concerning (Virchow's node on left = abdominal malignancy)
- **Spleen**: Usually not palpable; palpable spleen = enlarged (splenomegaly); percuss Traube's space
- **Skin**: Rashes, petechiae, urticaria, wounds (healing or non-healing)
- **Oropharynx**: Oral thrush (candidiasis suggests immunosuppression), tonsillar enlargement
- **Respiratory**: Auscultate for wheezing (allergic/asthma), crackles (infection)

### Key Lab Values
| Test | Normal/Use |
|------|-----------|
| **WBC (white blood cell count)** | 4,500-11,000/μL |
| **WBC differential** | Neutrophils 55-70%, Lymphocytes 20-40%, Monocytes 2-8%, Eosinophils 1-4%, Basophils 0.5-1% |
| **ANC (absolute neutrophil count)** | >1500/μL normal; <1000 = neutropenic; <500 = severe neutropenia (high infection risk) |
| **CD4 count** | 500-1500 cells/μL; <200 = AIDS-defining |
| **Immunoglobulin levels** | IgG, IgA, IgM quantitation for immunodeficiency workup |
| **ESR / CRP** | Nonspecific inflammation markers |
| **Complement (C3, C4, CH50)** | Low in active autoimmune disease (SLE); complement consumption |
| **ANA, RF, anti-CCP, anti-dsDNA** | Autoimmune disease markers |
| **Blood cultures** | Infection identification |
| **Allergy testing** | Skin prick testing, serum-specific IgE (RAST) |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Immunodeficiency
- **Primary (congenital)**: SCID, X-linked agammaglobulinemia, DiGeorge syndrome (thymic aplasia), selective IgA deficiency (most common; anaphylaxis risk with blood products)
- **Secondary (acquired)**: HIV/AIDS (see HIV), iatrogenic (chemotherapy, corticosteroids, transplant immunosuppression), malnutrition, extremes of age, chronic disease

### Hypersensitivity/Allergic
- Anaphylaxis (Type I; epinephrine IM is FIRST-LINE; see Shock)
- Allergic rhinitis, asthma, eczema (atopic triad)
- Urticaria, Contact Dermatitis
- Serum sickness (Type III)

### Autoimmune
- Systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, Type 1 diabetes, Graves disease, myasthenia gravis, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, celiac disease

### Lymphoproliferative
- Lymphoma (Hodgkin's, Non-Hodgkin's), leukemia (see ALL), multiple myeloma (see Multiple Myeloma)

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
- **Epinephrine**: First-line anaphylaxis treatment (0.3-0.5 mg IM anterolateral thigh); auto-injector (EpiPen) for outpatient
- **Antihistamines**: H1 blockers (diphenhydramine, cetirizine, loratadine) for allergic reactions; see Antiemetics for H1 role in nausea
- Systemic Corticosteroids: Broad immunosuppression; autoimmune diseases, transplant, allergic reactions (adjunct, not first-line for anaphylaxis)
