- **Immunosuppressants**: Cyclosporine, tacrolimus, mycophenolate, azathioprine (transplant rejection prevention)
- **Biologics**: TNF inhibitors (adalimumab), IL inhibitors, B-cell depleting (rituximab), checkpoint inhibitors (cancer immunotherapy)
- Antivirals -- HIV ART for immune reconstitution
- Vaccines and immunoglobulin preparations

---

clinicalPearls: **Neutropenic precautions when ANC < 500:** No fresh flowers/plants in room, no raw fruits/vegetables, strict hand hygiene, private room, report ANY fever (≥ 100.4°F/38°C) immediately -- neutropenic fever is a medical emergency requiring blood cultures and broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour.
**Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment for anaphylaxis.** Antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts. Do NOT delay epinephrine for diphenhydramine. Every minute of delay increases mortality.

**Asplenic patients need vaccination** against encapsulated organisms: Pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23), Meningococcal (MenACWY + MenB), and Haemophilus influenzae type B (Hib). They also need a plan for urgent antibiotics if fever develops.

**"Left shift"** on CBC differential = increased bands (immature neutrophils) → indicates acute bacterial infection. The bone marrow is releasing immature cells to fight infection faster than it can mature them.
**CD4 < 200 defines AIDS** and triggers prophylaxis: TMP-SMX for PJP pneumonia; when CD4 < 100, add prophylaxis for toxoplasmosis; CD4 < 50, add azithromycin for MAC.
**Autoimmune diseases tend to cluster.** A patient with one autoimmune condition (Hashimoto's, Type 1 DM, celiac) is at higher risk for others. Screen accordingly.

**Transplant patients are immunosuppressed for LIFE.** They need lifelong monitoring for infection, malignancy, and medication side effects. Medication adherence is critical -- one missed dose of tacrolimus can trigger acute rejection.

**Type IV hypersensitivity is the only type that is T-cell mediated (no antibodies).** It takes 24-72 hours to develop -- that's why the PPD (TB skin test) is read at 48-72 hours, and why contact dermatitis doesn't appear until the day after exposure.

musculoskeletal-system.title: Musculoskeletal System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Bones
- **Total**: 206 bones in the adult skeleton
- **Axial skeleton** (80 bones): Skull (22), hyoid (1), vertebral column (26), thoracic cage (25 -- sternum + 24 ribs)
- **Appendicular skeleton** (126 bones): Upper and lower extremities, pectoral and pelvic girdles
- **Bone types by shape**: Long (femur, humerus), short (carpals, tarsals), flat (sternum, cranial), irregular (vertebrae, facial), sesamoid (patella)

#### Bone Tissue
- **Compact (cortical) bone**: Dense outer layer; 80% of skeletal mass; provides strength and protection; osteons (Haversian systems) are the structural unit
- **Spongy (cancellous/trabecular) bone**: Inner lattice-like structure; contains red marrow (hematopoiesis); found in epiphyses, vertebral bodies, flat bones
- **Periosteum**: Dense fibrous membrane covering bone exterior; contains osteoblasts, blood vessels, nerves; site of ligament/tendon attachment; critical for fracture healing
- **Endosteum**: Thin membrane lining medullary cavity; contains osteoblasts and osteoclasts

#### Bone Cells
| Cell Type | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Osteoblasts** | Build bone; secrete osteoid (collagen matrix) which mineralizes; become osteocytes when trapped in matrix |
| **Osteocytes** | Mature bone cells; maintain bone matrix; sense mechanical stress (mechanotransduction); most abundant bone cell |
| **Osteoclasts** | Break down bone (resorption); large multinucleated cells derived from monocyte lineage; regulated by RANK/RANKL/OPG pathway |

#### Bone Remodeling
- Continuous process of osteoclast resorption + osteoblast formation
- Regulated by: Mechanical stress (Wolff's law -- bone adapts to loads placed on it), PTH (stimulates osteoclasts → ↑ Ca2+), calcitonin (inhibits osteoclasts → ↓ Ca2+), vitamin D (promotes calcium absorption), estrogen (inhibits osteoclasts -- loss at menopause → osteoporosis)
- Peak bone mass achieved ~25-30 years; gradual loss thereafter

#### Joints (Articulations)
| Classification | Type | Examples | Movement |
|---------------|------|---------|----------|
| **Fibrous** | Sutures, syndesmoses | Skull sutures, distal tibiofibular | None to minimal |
| **Cartilaginous** | Synchondroses, symphyses | Epiphyseal plates, pubic symphysis, intervertebral discs | Minimal |
| **Synovial** | Diarthrotic (freely moveable) | Shoulder, hip, knee, elbow, wrist | Full ROM |

#### Synovial Joint Structure
- **Articular cartilage**: Hyaline cartilage covering articular surfaces; avascular → poor healing
- **Joint capsule**: Outer fibrous layer (structural) + inner synovial membrane (secretes synovial fluid)
- **Synovial fluid**: Lubricates, nourishes cartilage, shock absorption
- **Ligaments**: Bone to bone; provide stability; collagenous; limited blood supply → slow healing
- **Tendons**: Muscle to bone; transmit force; more organized collagen than ligaments
- **Menisci/Labra**: Fibrocartilage structures that deepen sockets and improve congruence (knee menisci, hip/shoulder labrum)
- **Bursae**: Fluid-filled sacs; reduce friction between tissues (subacromial, prepatellar, olecranon, trochanteric)

#### Skeletal Muscle
- ~600+ skeletal muscles; ~40% of body weight
- **Structure**: Muscle → fascicles → muscle fibers (cells) → myofibrils → sarcomeres (functional contractile unit)
- **Sarcomere**: Actin (thin filaments) and myosin (thick filaments); Z-line to Z-line; sliding filament mechanism
- **Motor unit**: One motor neuron + all muscle fibers it innervates; small motor units = fine control (eyes); large motor units = gross movement (quadriceps)
- **Neuromuscular junction (NMJ)**: Motor neuron releases acetylcholine (ACh) → binds nicotinic receptors on motor end plate → Na+ influx → depolarization → contraction; site targeted by neuromuscular blockers, myasthenia gravis antibodies, botulism toxin

#### Major Muscles — Clinical Relevance
| Muscle/Group | Action | Clinical Note |
|-------------|--------|--------------|
| Diaphragm | Primary respiratory muscle (C3-C5 phrenic nerve) | Injury above C3-5 → ventilator dependent |
| Deltoid | Shoulder abduction | IM injection site; axillary nerve (C5-C6) |
| Quadriceps | Knee extension | Atrophy rapid with immobility; essential for ambulation |
| Hamstrings | Knee flexion | Common sports injury |
| Gastrocnemius/Soleus | Plantarflexion | Calf pump for venous return; assess for DVT |
| Gluteus medius | Hip abduction | Preferred IM injection site (ventrogluteal) |

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability — muscle contraction is initiated by an action potential propagating along the sarcolemma; the electrical signal triggers calcium release from the SR, which activates the actin-myosin cross-bridge cycle. Nutrition and Metabolism — muscle contraction is an ATP-dependent process; fast-twitch fibers rely on anaerobic glycolysis (generates lactate); slow-twitch fibers rely on oxidative phosphorylation.*

### Muscle Contraction (Sliding Filament Mechanism)
1. Motor neuron releases ACh at NMJ → binds nicotinic receptors → Na+ influx → action potential along sarcolemma → down T-tubules
2. T-tubule depolarization → sarcoplasmic reticulum releases Ca2+
3. Ca2+ binds troponin → tropomyosin shifts → exposes myosin-binding sites on actin
4. Myosin heads bind actin → power stroke (requires ATP) → filaments slide → sarcomere shortens
5. ATP binds myosin → releases actin → cycle repeats as long as Ca2+ and ATP are present
6. When stimulation stops: Ca2+ pumped back into SR; tropomyosin re-covers binding sites → relaxation

> **Rigor mortis**: After death, ATP depleted → myosin can't release actin → persistent contraction → stiffness

### Muscle Metabolism
- **ATP sources**: Creatine phosphate (immediate, ~10 seconds), anaerobic glycolysis (30-60 seconds, produces lactate), aerobic oxidative phosphorylation (sustained activity, requires O2)
- **Oxygen debt**: After intense exercise, continued elevated O2 consumption to replenish creatine phosphate, convert lactate, and restore myoglobin O2

### Calcium and Bone Homeostasis
| Hormone | Source | Effect on Ca2+ | Mechanism |
|---------|--------|---------------|-----------|
| **PTH** | Parathyroid glands | ↑ Ca2+ | Stimulates osteoclasts (bone resorption); increases renal Ca2+ reabsorption; activates vitamin D (↑ intestinal Ca2+ absorption) |
| **Calcitonin** | Thyroid C-cells | ↓ Ca2+ | Inhibits osteoclasts; increases renal Ca2+ excretion; minor role clinically |
| **Vitamin D (calcitriol)** | Skin → liver → kidney activation | ↑ Ca2+ | Increases intestinal absorption of Ca2+ and PO4 |

See Fluid and Electrolyte Balance and Endocrine System for full Ca2+/PO4 regulation.

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- **Pain**: Location, onset, mechanism of injury, aggravating/relieving factors, quality (sharp, aching, burning), radiation
- **Functional impact**: ADL limitations, mobility, weight-bearing status, assistive devices
- **Risk factors**: Osteoporosis risk (age, sex, menopause, corticosteroid use, smoking, sedentary lifestyle, family history, low BMI)
- **Injury mechanism**: FOOSH (fall on outstretched hand), twisting, direct blow, overuse, pathologic

### Physical Exam
- **Inspection**: Symmetry, alignment, deformity, swelling, ecchymosis, muscle atrophy, skin integrity, open wounds over fractures
- **Palpation**: Tenderness, warmth, crepitus, pulses distal to injury, sensation
- **ROM**: Active (patient moves) vs. passive (examiner moves); compare bilateral; document in degrees
- **Strength testing**: Grade 0-5 scale (0 = no contraction; 3 = against gravity; 5 = full strength against resistance)
- **Special tests**: Lachman's (ACL), McMurray's (meniscus), drawer tests, Phalen's/Tinel's (carpal tunnel), Thompson's (Achilles rupture), straight leg raise (sciatica)

### Neurovascular Assessment (The 6 P's)
Critical for fractures, casts, compartment syndrome:
| P | Finding | Significance |
|---|---------|-------------|
| **Pain** | Disproportionate to injury, worsens with passive stretch | Earliest and most reliable sign of compartment syndrome |
| **Pressure** | Compartment feels tense, firm | Elevated compartment pressure |
| **Paresthesia** | Numbness, tingling distal to injury | Nerve compression |
| **Paralysis** | Inability to move distal extremity | LATE sign -- nerve/muscle damage |
| **Pallor** | Pale, cool extremity | Vascular compromise |
| **Pulselessness** | Absent distal pulses | VERY LATE sign -- irreversible damage likely |

> **Compartment syndrome is a SURGICAL EMERGENCY.** Pain out of proportion + pain with passive stretch → call surgery immediately. Do NOT wait for pulselessness. Normal pressure < 30 mmHg or within 30 mmHg of diastolic (delta pressure).

### Key Lab Values / Imaging
| Test | Normal/Use |
|------|-----------|
| Calcium | 8.5-10.5 mg/dL |
| Phosphorus | 2.5-4.5 mg/dL |
| Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) | 44-147 IU/L; elevated in fracture healing, Paget's disease, bone mets |
| CK (creatine kinase) | 22-198 IU/L; elevated in muscle damage (rhabdomyolysis, MI) |
| Myoglobin | Elevated in rhabdomyolysis → causes AKI (precipitates in renal tubules) |
| ESR/CRP | Elevated in infection (osteomyelitis), inflammatory arthritis |
| Uric acid | 3.5-7.2 mg/dL; elevated in gout |
| DEXA scan | Bone density: T-score > -1.0 normal; -1.0 to -2.5 osteopenia; < -2.5 osteoporosis |
| X-ray | First-line imaging for fractures; AP and lateral views minimum |
| MRI | Soft tissue injury (ligaments, tendons, meniscus, spinal cord); stress fractures not visible on X-ray |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Traumatic
- Fractures (open vs. closed; displaced vs. non-displaced; comminuted, spiral, transverse, oblique, greenstick, pathologic)
- Dislocations/subluxations
- Sprains (ligament injury: Grade I-III) and strains (muscle/tendon injury)
- Compartment syndrome
- Rhabdomyolysis (muscle breakdown → myoglobin release → AKI; CK > 5x normal; treatment: aggressive IV NS hydration)

### Degenerative
- Osteoarthritis (wear-and-tear; weight-bearing joints; Heberden's nodes = DIP, Bouchard's nodes = PIP)
- Degenerative disc disease
- Spinal stenosis

### Metabolic Bone Disease
- Osteoporosis (T-score < -2.5; fragility fractures; hip fracture = high mortality in elderly)
- Paget's disease (accelerated bone remodeling → enlarged, deformed, weak bones; elevated ALP)
- Osteomalacia / Rickets (vitamin D deficiency → impaired mineralization)

### Inflammatory
- Rheumatoid arthritis (autoimmune; symmetrical small joints; morning stiffness > 30 min; RF and anti-CCP positive)
- Gout (monosodium urate crystal deposition; podagra = first MTP joint; elevated uric acid; treat acute with colchicine, NSAIDs, or corticosteroids; prevent with allopurinol, febuxostat)

### Infectious
- Osteomyelitis (bone infection; S. aureus most common; elevated ESR/CRP; MRI for diagnosis; prolonged IV antibiotics 4-6 weeks)

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
- NSAIDs -- first-line for musculoskeletal pain and inflammation
- Opioid Analgesics -- acute fracture pain, post-surgical
- Systemic Corticosteroids -- rheumatoid arthritis flares, gout; long-term use → osteoporosis
- **Bisphosphonates** (alendronate, risedronate, zoledronic acid): Inhibit osteoclasts; first-line for osteoporosis; take with full glass of water, remain upright 30 min (esophageal erosion); rare: osteonecrosis of jaw, atypical femur fractures
- **Denosumab (Prolia)**: RANKL inhibitor; SubQ q6 months; alternative to bisphosphonates; rebound bone loss if discontinued abruptly
- **Colchicine**: Acute gout flares; inhibits neutrophil migration; GI side effects (diarrhea) dose-limiting
- **Allopurinol/Febuxostat**: Xanthine oxidase inhibitors; chronic gout prevention; reduce uric acid; do NOT start during acute flare
- **DMARDs** (methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine): Rheumatoid arthritis; slow disease progression; methotrexate: hepatotoxic, myelosuppressive, teratogenic
- **Biologics** (adalimumab, etanercept, infliximab): TNF inhibitors for RA; infection risk (screen for TB before starting)
- **Muscle relaxants** (cyclobenzaprine, baclofen, tizanidine): Short-term adjunct for muscle spasm; sedating; avoid in elderly (Beers criteria)

---

clinicalPearls: **Compartment syndrome: Pain with PASSIVE stretch is the key finding.** If you passively extend a patient's fingers or toes and they scream with pain → think compartment syndrome. Don't wait for the 6 P's to all appear -- by the time you lose pulses, tissue is dead. Cut the cast, release the dressing, call surgery.

**Fat embolism syndrome** can follow long bone fractures (especially femur) 24-72 hours post-injury. Classic triad: respiratory distress, neurologic changes (confusion), petechial rash (chest, axillae, conjunctivae). Treatment is supportive.

**Hip fractures in the elderly are life-threatening.** One-year mortality after hip fracture is ~20-30%. Prioritize surgical repair within 24-48 hours, early mobilization, DVT prophylaxis, delirium prevention, and fall risk reduction.

**Rhabdomyolysis = hydrate aggressively.** CK > 5,000 IU/L with risk factors (crush injury, prolonged immobility, statins, seizures, extreme exertion) → IV NS at 200-300 mL/hr targeting urine output 200-300 mL/hr. Myoglobin precipitates in renal tubules → AKI.
**Never ice for more than 20 minutes** at a time. Ice reduces swelling and pain but prolonged application can cause frostbite or tissue damage. 20 min on, 20 min off.

**Bisphosphonates and jaw osteonecrosis**: Risk is highest with IV bisphosphonates for cancer. Dental exam recommended before starting. Report jaw pain, exposed bone, or non-healing dental extraction sites.

**Osteoporosis is a silent disease** until the first fracture. DEXA screening recommended for all women ≥ 65, men ≥ 70, and younger patients with risk factors. Prevention: Weight-bearing exercise, adequate calcium (1000-1200 mg/day) and vitamin D (600-800 IU/day), fall prevention.

**Cast care education**: Do not put anything inside the cast (skin breakdown, infection); report increasing pain, numbness, tingling, color change, or foul smell; keep dry (fiberglass casts can be dried; plaster cannot get wet).

nervous-system.title: Nervous System

sections.anatomy:
### Central Nervous System (CNS)

#### Brain Regions
| Region | Key Structures | Functions |
|--------|---------------|-----------|
| Cerebrum | Frontal, parietal, temporal, occipital lobes | Higher-order processing, motor, sensory, language, memory |
| Frontal lobe | Prefrontal cortex, premotor cortex, primary motor cortex (precentral gyrus), Broca's area | Personality, judgment, planning, voluntary motor, speech production |
| Parietal lobe | Primary somatosensory cortex (postcentral gyrus), sensory association areas | Sensory integration, spatial awareness, proprioception |
| Temporal lobe | Primary auditory cortex, Wernicke's area, hippocampus, amygdala | Hearing, language comprehension, memory formation, emotion |
| Occipital lobe | Primary visual cortex | Vision processing |
| Diencephalon | Thalamus, hypothalamus, epithalamus (pineal gland) | Sensory relay (thalamus), homeostasis/endocrine control (hypothalamus), melatonin (pineal) |
| Brainstem | Midbrain, pons, medulla oblongata | CN nuclei, reticular activating system (consciousness), vital centers (cardiac, respiratory, vasomotor) |
| Cerebellum | Vermis, hemispheres, flocculonodular lobe | Coordination, balance, fine motor, posture, motor learning |
| Limbic system | Hippocampus, amygdala, cingulate gyrus, fornix | Emotion, memory consolidation, motivation |

- **Clinical Pearl**: Left hemisphere is dominant for language in ~95% of right-handed and ~70% of left-handed individuals
- **Broca's area** (frontal): Motor speech production; damage = expressive (non-fluent) aphasia
- **Wernicke's area** (temporal): Speech comprehension; damage = receptive (fluent) aphasia

#### Spinal Cord
- Extends from foramen magnum to L1-L2 (conus medullaris); cauda equina continues below
- **31 spinal nerve pairs**: 8 cervical, 12 thoracic, 5 lumbar, 5 sacral, 1 coccygeal
- **Gray matter** (central H-shape): Anterior (ventral) horns = motor neurons; posterior (dorsal) horns = sensory neurons; lateral horns (T1-L2) = sympathetic preganglionic neurons
- **White matter** (surrounding): Ascending (sensory) and descending (motor) tracts
- **Key dermatome landmarks**: C4 = shoulders, T4 = nipple line, T10 = umbilicus, L1 = inguinal region, S2-S4 = perianal ("saddle area")

### Peripheral Nervous System (PNS)

#### Somatic Nervous System
- Voluntary control of skeletal muscle
- Sensory (afferent) neurons carry input from receptors to CNS
- Motor (efferent) neurons carry output from CNS to skeletal muscles
- **Single motor neuron** pathway; always excitatory (ACh at neuromuscular junction)

#### Autonomic Nervous System (ANS)
| Feature | Sympathetic ("Fight or Flight") | Parasympathetic ("Rest and Digest") |
|---------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------------|
| Origin | Thoracolumbar (T1-L2) | Craniosacral (CN III, VII, IX, X + S2-S4) |
| Preganglionic neurotransmitter | ACh (nicotinic receptors) | ACh (nicotinic receptors) |
| Postganglionic neurotransmitter | Norepinephrine (adrenergic receptors) | ACh (muscarinic receptors) |
| Heart | Increases HR, contractility (beta-1) | Decreases HR (vagal tone via CN X) |
| Lungs | Bronchodilation (beta-2) | Bronchoconstriction |
| GI | Decreases motility and secretions | Increases motility and secretions |
| Pupils | Mydriasis (dilation) -- alpha-1 | Miosis (constriction) |
| Blood vessels | Vasoconstriction (alpha-1); vasodilation in skeletal muscle (beta-2) | Minimal direct effect |
| Adrenal medulla | Releases epinephrine (80%) and norepinephrine (20%) | No innervation |

- **Adrenergic receptor summary**: Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction, mydriasis), Alpha-2 (presynaptic inhibition), Beta-1 (cardiac stimulation), Beta-2 (bronchodilation, vasodilation), Beta-3 (lipolysis)

### Cranial Nerves

| CN | Name | Type | Function | Clinical Assessment |
|----|------|------|----------|-------------------|
| I | Olfactory | Sensory | Smell | Test each nostril with familiar scent (coffee, vanilla) |
| II | Optic | Sensory | Vision | Visual acuity (Snellen chart), visual fields (confrontation), fundoscopy |
| III | Oculomotor | Motor | Eye movement (superior/medial/inferior rectus, inferior oblique), eyelid elevation, pupil constriction | Pupil response (PERRLA), ptosis, eye tracking (H-pattern) |
| IV | Trochlear | Motor | Superior oblique (downward and inward gaze) | Look down and in; diplopia on stairs |
| V | Trigeminal | Both | Facial sensation (V1 ophthalmic, V2 maxillary, V3 mandibular), muscles of mastication | Light touch 3 divisions, corneal reflex (afferent), clench jaw |
| VI | Abducens | Motor | Lateral rectus (lateral gaze) | Lateral eye movement |
| VII | Facial | Both | Facial expression, taste anterior 2/3 tongue, lacrimation, salivation | Raise eyebrows, smile, puff cheeks, close eyes tightly; taste |
| VIII | Vestibulocochlear | Sensory | Hearing (cochlear), balance (vestibular) | Weber and Rinne tests, whispered voice, Romberg |
| IX | Glossopharyngeal | Both | Taste posterior 1/3 tongue, pharyngeal sensation, parotid gland, carotid baroreceptors | Gag reflex (afferent), "aah" |
| X | Vagus | Both | Pharynx, larynx, heart, lungs, GI (to splenic flexure); parasympathetic to thoracic/abdominal viscera | Gag reflex (efferent), voice quality (hoarseness), swallow, uvula midline |
| XI | Accessory (Spinal Accessory) | Motor | Sternocleidomastoid, trapezius | Shoulder shrug against resistance, turn head against resistance |
| XII | Hypoglossal | Motor | Tongue movement | Stick out tongue (deviates toward lesion side); tongue strength |

- **Mnemonic (names)**: Oh Oh Oh To Touch And Feel Very Good Velvet, AH!
- **Mnemonic (type)**: Some Say Marry Money, But My Brother Says Big Brains Matter More

### Meninges, CSF, and BBB

#### Meninges (outermost to innermost)
1. **Dura mater**: Tough, fibrous; forms falx cerebri and tentorium cerebelli; epidural space (potential space) above it; **subdural space** between dura and arachnoid
2. **Arachnoid mater**: Web-like; **subarachnoid space** contains CSF and major cerebral vessels
3. **Pia mater**: Delicate, directly adherent to brain and spinal cord surface

#### Cerebrospinal Fluid (CSF)
- Produced by **choroid plexus** in ventricles (~500 mL/day; ~150 mL circulating at any time)
- Flow: Lateral ventricles → interventricular foramina (of Monro) → 3rd ventricle → cerebral aqueduct (of Sylvius) → 4th ventricle → subarachnoid space → absorbed by arachnoid granulations into dural venous sinuses

| CSF Parameter | Normal Value |
|---------------|-------------|
| Appearance | Clear, colorless |
| Opening pressure | 70-180 mmH2O (lumbar puncture, lateral decubitus) |
| Protein | 15-45 mg/dL |
| Glucose | 40-70 mg/dL (approximately 60% of serum glucose) |
| WBC | 0-5 cells/mcL (lymphocytes) |
| RBC | 0 |

- **Clinical Pearl**: Xanthochromia (yellow CSF) indicates prior subarachnoid hemorrhage (bilirubin from RBC breakdown)

#### Blood-Brain Barrier (BBB)
- Formed by tight junctions of capillary endothelial cells, basement membrane, and astrocyte foot processes
- Selectively permeable: Allows lipid-soluble molecules, O2, CO2, glucose (via transporters); blocks most pathogens, large molecules, and many drugs
- **Absent in**: Circumventricular organs (area postrema/chemoreceptor trigger zone, hypothalamic nuclei)
- Disrupted in: Meningitis, Traumatic Brain Injury, brain tumors, stroke

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability — neuronal signaling IS action potential propagation; every sensation, thought, and motor command is a sequence of membrane depolarizations traveling along axons. Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — the ANS operates via GPCR signaling at target organs; sympathetic (NE → α/β receptors) and parasympathetic (ACh → muscarinic receptors) divisions oppose each other to maintain homeostasis.*

### Neuron Structure and Function
- **Cell body (soma)**: Contains nucleus, Nissl bodies (rough ER); integrative center
- **Dendrites**: Receive signals (graded potentials)
- **Axon**: Conducts action potentials away from cell body
- **Myelin sheath**: Schwann cells (PNS) or oligodendrocytes (CNS); insulates axon
- **Nodes of Ranvier**: Gaps in myelin; saltatory conduction (speeds transmission)
- **Synapse**: Axon terminal → synaptic cleft → postsynaptic membrane

### Action Potential
1. **Resting membrane potential**: -70 mV (maintained by Na+/K+ ATPase pump: 3 Na+ out, 2 K+ in)
2. **Depolarization**: Stimulus reaches threshold (-55 mV) → voltage-gated Na+ channels open → Na+ influx → membrane becomes positive (~+30 mV)
3. **Repolarization**: Na+ channels inactivate; voltage-gated K+ channels open → K+ efflux → returns toward resting potential
4. **Hyperpolarization**: Brief overshoot below -70 mV as K+ channels close slowly
5. **Absolute refractory period**: No new AP can be generated (Na+ channels inactivated)
6. **Relative refractory period**: Stronger-than-normal stimulus needed

- **All-or-none principle**: AP either fires completely or not at all; intensity coded by frequency

### Neurotransmitters
| Neurotransmitter | Location/Pathway | Function | Clinical Relevance |
|-----------------|-----------------|----------|-------------------|
| Acetylcholine (ACh) | NMJ, autonomic ganglia, basal forebrain | Muscle contraction, memory, parasympathetic effects | Decreased in Alzheimer disease; target in Myasthenia Gravis |
| Norepinephrine (NE) | Locus coeruleus, sympathetic postganglionic | Alertness, attention, fight-or-flight | Target of SNRIs, tricyclics; excess in pheochromocytoma |
| Dopamine | Substantia nigra → striatum; VTA → mesolimbic/mesocortical | Movement, reward, motivation, executive function | Decreased in Parkinson disease; excess in schizophrenia (mesolimbic) |
| Serotonin (5-HT) | Raphe nuclei | Mood, sleep, appetite, pain modulation | Target of SSRIs; serotonin syndrome with excess |
| GABA | Widespread inhibitory (cortex, basal ganglia, cerebellum) | Primary inhibitory NT in brain | Target of benzodiazepines, barbiturates, Antiepileptics |
| Glutamate | Widespread excitatory | Primary excitatory NT in brain; learning, memory | Excitotoxicity in stroke and TBI |
| Endorphins/enkephalins | PAG, spinal cord dorsal horn | Pain modulation (endogenous opioids) | Target of Opioid Analgesics |

### Motor and Sensory Pathways

#### Descending (Motor) Tracts
| Tract | Origin | Decussation | Function |
|-------|--------|-------------|----------|
| Lateral corticospinal | Primary motor cortex | Pyramidal decussation (medulla) | Voluntary fine motor (contralateral limbs) |
| Anterior corticospinal | Primary motor cortex | Spinal cord level | Voluntary motor (axial/proximal muscles) |
| Rubrospinal | Red nucleus (midbrain) | Midbrain | Flexor muscle tone (upper extremities) |
| Vestibulospinal | Vestibular nuclei | Ipsilateral | Extensor muscle tone, balance, posture |
| Reticulospinal | Reticular formation | Bilateral | Posture, muscle tone, autonomic |

- **Clinical Pearl**: Upper motor neuron (UMN) lesion signs = spasticity, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski, clonus. Lower motor neuron (LMN) lesion signs = flaccidity, hyporeflexia, fasciculations, atrophy

#### Ascending (Sensory) Tracts
| Tract | Sensory Modality | 1st Order Neuron | Decussation | Clinical Test |
|-------|-----------------|-----------------|-------------|---------------|
| Dorsal columns (fasciculus gracilis/cuneatus) | Fine touch, proprioception, vibration | Dorsal root ganglion → medulla | Medulla (medial lemniscus) | Tuning fork, position sense, Romberg |
| Lateral spinothalamic | Pain and temperature | Dorsal root ganglion → spinal cord | Spinal cord (at level of entry) | Pinprick, temperature |
| Anterior spinothalamic | Crude touch, pressure | Dorsal root ganglion → spinal cord | Spinal cord | Light touch |

### Reflexes
- **Reflex arc**: Receptor → afferent neuron → integration center (spinal cord) → efferent neuron → effector
- **Deep tendon reflexes (DTRs)**: Graded 0-4+ (0 = absent, 1+ = diminished, 2+ = normal, 3+ = brisk, 4+ = clonus)
- **Key reflexes**: Biceps (C5-C6), triceps (C7-C8), brachioradialis (C5-C6), patellar (L3-L4), Achilles (S1-S2)
- **Babinski sign**: Stroking lateral plantar surface; positive = great toe dorsiflexion + fanning of toes; normal in infants <2 years; in adults indicates UMN lesion

---
sections.assessment:
### Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS)
| Component | Response | Score |
|-----------|----------|-------|
| **Eye Opening** | Spontaneous | 4 |
| | To voice | 3 |
| | To pain | 2 |
| | None | 1 |
| **Verbal Response** | Oriented | 5 |
| | Confused | 4 |
| | Inappropriate words | 3 |
| | Incomprehensible sounds | 2 |
| | None | 1 |
| **Motor Response** | Obeys commands | 6 |
| | Localizes pain | 5 |
| | Withdrawal (flexion) | 4 |
| | Abnormal flexion (decorticate) | 3 |
| | Extension (decerebrate) | 2 |
| | None | 1 |

- **Total**: 3-15; mild TBI = 13-15; moderate = 9-12; severe = 3-8; intubation typically at GCS <=8

### Pupil Assessment (PERRLA)
- **Size**: Normal 2-5 mm; document in mm; compare bilaterally; anisocoria >1 mm is abnormal
- **Shape**: Round normal; ovoid may indicate increased ICP
- **Reactivity**: Brisk, sluggish, fixed (non-reactive)
- **Clinical significance**:
  - Fixed, dilated (blown) unilateral pupil → uncal herniation (CN III compression) -- EMERGENCY
  - Bilateral fixed, dilated → severe brainstem injury or drug effect (atropine, sympathomimetics)
  - Pinpoint bilateral → opioid toxicity, pontine hemorrhage
  - Bilateral midposition, fixed → midbrain damage

### Level of Consciousness (LOC)
- Most sensitive indicator of neurological change
- **Continuum**: Alert → Lethargic (drowsy, easily aroused) → Obtunded (difficult to arouse) → Stuporous (arouses only with vigorous stimuli) → Comatose (unarousable)

### Neurological Examination Components
- **Mental status**: Orientation (person, place, time, situation), attention, memory, language, abstract reasoning
- **Cranial nerves**: Test all 12 as outlined in anatomy section above
- **Motor exam**: Muscle bulk, tone (spasticity vs. flaccidity vs. rigidity), strength (0-5 scale per MRC grading), involuntary movements (tremor, fasciculations, myoclonus)
- **Sensory exam**: Light touch, pinprick, temperature, vibration, proprioception; test dermatomal pattern
- **Cerebellar function**: Finger-to-nose, heel-to-shin, rapid alternating movements (dysdiadochokinesia), gait (wide-based = cerebellar ataxia)
- **Romberg test**: Patient stands feet together, eyes closed; positive (sway/fall) = posterior column (proprioceptive) dysfunction
- **Pronator drift**: Arms outstretched, palms up, eyes closed; pronation and downward drift indicates contralateral UMN lesion (subtle weakness)
- **Gait assessment**: Observe for ataxia, hemiparetic gait (circumduction), shuffling (parkinsonian), steppage (foot drop)

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges
| Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|------|-------------|----------------------|
| CSF protein | 15-45 mg/dL | Elevated: infection, hemorrhage, demyelination |
| CSF glucose | 40-70 mg/dL | Low: bacterial Meningitis |
| CSF WBC | 0-5/mcL | Elevated: infection, inflammation |
| Serum sodium | 136-145 mEq/L | Critical for neurological function; hyponatremia causes cerebral edema |
| Serum osmolality | 275-295 mOsm/kg | Monitor with mannitol or hypertonic saline |
| Ammonia | 15-45 mcg/dL | Elevated: Hepatic Encephalopathy |
| Antiepileptic drug levels | Drug-specific | Therapeutic drug monitoring |
| ICP (intracranial pressure) | 5-15 mmHg | >20 mmHg requires intervention |
| CPP (cerebral perfusion pressure) | 60-100 mmHg | CPP = MAP - ICP; goal >60 mmHg |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Cerebrovascular
- Ischemic Stroke (thrombotic, embolic, lacunar)
- Intracerebral Hemorrhage
- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage
- Transient Ischemic Attack

### Seizure Disorders
- Epilepsy
- Status Epilepticus

### Neurocognitive / Altered Mental Status
- Delirium
- Alzheimer Disease
- Parkinson Disease

### Neuromuscular
- Myasthenia Gravis
- Guillain-Barre Syndrome
- Multiple Sclerosis
- Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis

### Traumatic / Structural
- Traumatic Brain Injury
- Spinal Cord Injury
- Increased Intracranial Pressure

### Infectious
- Meningitis (bacterial, viral, fungal)
- Encephalitis
- Brain Abscess

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
### Antiseizure
- Antiepileptics (phenytoin, levetiracetam, valproic acid, carbamazepine, lacosamide, lamotrigine) -- monitor drug levels for phenytoin (10-20 mcg/mL) and valproic acid (50-100 mcg/mL)

### Analgesics
- Opioid Analgesics (morphine, fentanyl, hydromorphone) -- monitor respiratory depression, sedation, pupil constriction; naloxone for reversal
- Non-Opioid Analgesics (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, gabapentin, pregabalin)

### Sedation and Anxiolysis
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics (benzodiazepines: lorazepam, midazolam, diazepam; propofol, dexmedetomidine) -- monitor for respiratory depression; flumazenil reverses benzodiazepines

### Neuroprotective / ICP Management
- **Osmotic diuretics**: Mannitol (0.25-1 g/kg IV; monitor serum osmolality <320, renal function)
- **Hypertonic saline**: 3% or 23.4% NaCl; draws fluid from brain tissue; monitor sodium closely
- Systemic Corticosteroids (dexamethasone) -- for vasogenic edema (tumor-related); NOT for cytotoxic edema (stroke)

### Thrombolytics / Stroke
- **Alteplase (tPA)**: IV within 4.5 hours of ischemic stroke onset; door-to-needle time goal <60 min; monitor for hemorrhagic conversion

### Neuromuscular
- **Anticholinesterases** (pyridostigmine) -- Myasthenia Gravis management
- **Dopaminergic agents** (levodopa/carbidopa) -- Parkinson Disease

---

clinicalPearls: **Stroke assessment**: Use **FAST** (Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911); in hospital use **NIHSS** for severity grading
**Cushing's triad** (late sign of increased ICP): Hypertension (widening pulse pressure), bradycardia, irregular respirations -- this is a LATE and ominous finding

**Seizure precautions**: Padded side rails, suction at bedside, oxygen available, IV access; during seizure: protect from injury, do NOT restrain or put anything in mouth, time the seizure, turn to side after (recovery position)
For suspected [[Spinal Cord Injury]]: Maintain spinal immobilization (C-spine precautions) until cleared; log-roll only
**Neurogenic shock** (from [[Spinal Cord Injury]] above T6): Hypotension + bradycardia + warm, flushed skin (loss of sympathetic tone) -- opposite of hypovolemic shock

**Autonomic dysreflexia** (SCI at T6 or above): Sudden severe HTN, pounding headache, bradycardia, flushing/sweating above injury level -- EMERGENCY; sit patient up, remove noxious stimulus (check for bladder distension, fecal impaction, tight clothing)
**Decorticate posturing** (flexion): Arms flexed, legs extended; indicates damage above brainstem (cortical)
**Decerebrate posturing** (extension): Arms and legs extended; indicates brainstem damage; worse prognosis
Assess neurological status (neuro checks) **at minimum q1h** in acute neurological conditions; report any decline immediately

**CN VII palsy differentiation**: UMN (stroke) = contralateral lower face weakness only (forehead spared due to bilateral innervation); LMN (Bell's palsy) = ipsilateral entire face weakness (forehead involved)
**Brown-Sequard syndrome** (hemisection of spinal cord): Ipsilateral motor loss and proprioception loss; contralateral pain and temperature loss below the lesion

renal-system.title: Renal System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Kidneys
- **Location**: Retroperitoneal, T12-L3; right kidney slightly lower than left (displaced by liver)
- **Size**: ~11 cm long, ~6 cm wide, ~3 cm thick; ~150 g each
- **External**: Renal capsule (fibrous), perirenal fat, renal fascia
- **Hilum**: Medial concavity; renal artery, renal vein, ureter, lymphatics, nerves enter/exit

#### Internal Kidney Anatomy
- **Renal cortex**: Outer region; contains glomeruli, proximal and distal convoluted tubules
- **Renal medulla**: Inner region; contains renal pyramids (8-18 per kidney); pyramids contain loops of Henle and collecting ducts
- **Renal papilla**: Tip of each pyramid; drains into minor calyx
- **Minor calyces → Major calyces → Renal pelvis → Ureter**
- **Renal columns**: Extensions of cortical tissue between pyramids

#### Blood Supply
- **Renal arteries**: Branch from abdominal aorta; receive ~20-25% of cardiac output (~1200 mL/min)
- Flow: Renal artery → segmental arteries → interlobar arteries → arcuate arteries → interlobular (cortical radiate) arteries → afferent arterioles → **glomerulus** → efferent arterioles → peritubular capillaries (cortex) or **vasa recta** (medulla) → renal vein → IVC

#### The Nephron (Functional Unit)
- ~1 million nephrons per kidney
- **Types**: Cortical nephrons (~85%; short loops of Henle) and juxtamedullary nephrons (~15%; long loops of Henle deep into medulla; critical for urine concentration)

| Nephron Segment | Location | Function |
|----------------|----------|----------|
| **Renal corpuscle** (Bowman's capsule + glomerulus) | Cortex | Filtration of blood → filtrate |
| **Proximal convoluted tubule (PCT)** | Cortex | Reabsorbs ~65% of filtrate: Na+, K+, Cl-, HCO3-, glucose, amino acids, water; secretes H+, drugs, toxins |
| **Loop of Henle -- descending limb** | Medulla | Permeable to water (water reabsorption); impermeable to solutes → filtrate becomes concentrated |
| **Loop of Henle -- ascending limb (thick)** | Medulla → cortex | Impermeable to water; actively reabsorbs Na+, K+, Cl- (Na-K-2Cl cotransporter; site of **loop diuretics**) → filtrate becomes dilute |
| **Distal convoluted tubule (DCT)** | Cortex | Fine-tuning: Na+ reabsorption (aldosterone-responsive); Ca2+ reabsorption (PTH-responsive); K+/H+ secretion; site of **thiazide diuretics** |
| **Collecting duct** | Cortex → medulla | Final water reabsorption (ADH-responsive; aquaporin-2 channels); K+ secretion (aldosterone); H+/HCO3- regulation; site of **K+-sparing diuretics** |

#### Juxtaglomerular Apparatus (JGA)
- Located at junction of afferent arteriole and DCT
- **Juxtaglomerular (JG) cells**: Modified smooth muscle in afferent arteriole; secrete **renin** in response to decreased renal perfusion, decreased NaCl at macula densa, or sympathetic stimulation
- **Macula densa**: Specialized cells in DCT wall; sense NaCl concentration in tubular fluid
- Initiates the **RAAS** cascade (see Physiology below)

#### Ureters
- ~25-30 cm long muscular tubes; retroperitoneal; peristalsis moves urine from renal pelvis to bladder
- Three anatomic narrowings (common sites for ureteral stone obstruction): Ureteropelvic junction, pelvic brim crossing, ureterovesical junction

#### Urinary Bladder
- Muscular reservoir; capacity ~300-500 mL (urge to void at ~200-300 mL)
- **Detrusor muscle**: Smooth muscle; contracts during micturition (parasympathetic, S2-S4)
- **Trigone**: Smooth triangular area formed by two ureteral orifices and internal urethral orifice
- **Internal urethral sphincter**: Smooth muscle (involuntary)
- **External urethral sphincter**: Skeletal muscle (voluntary); pudendal nerve (S2-S4)

#### Urethra
- Female: ~3-4 cm (shorter → higher UTI risk)
- Male: ~18-20 cm (prostatic, membranous, spongy/penile portions)

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — the kidney is the master regulator of all fluid compartments; it sets ECF volume, osmolality, and electrolyte composition through filtration, reabsorption, and secretion. Acid-Base Balance — the kidney provides the slow (days) but powerful metabolic limb of acid-base regulation by reclaiming or excreting HCO₃⁻. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — virtually every homeostatic loop in the body eventually acts through or is corrected by the kidney.*

### Urine Formation (Three Processes)

#### 1. Glomerular Filtration
- **Filtration membrane**: Fenestrated capillary endothelium → basement membrane → podocyte filtration slits
- Filters freely: Water, electrolytes, glucose, amino acids, urea, creatinine
- Does NOT filter: Large proteins (albumin), blood cells, platelets
- **GFR (Glomerular Filtration Rate)**: ~125 mL/min or ~180 L/day; only ~1-2 L becomes urine (99% reabsorbed)
- **Net Filtration Pressure (NFP)**: Glomerular hydrostatic pressure (~55 mmHg) - Capsular hydrostatic pressure (~15 mmHg) - Glomerular oncotic pressure (~30 mmHg) = ~10 mmHg
- **GFR regulation**: Myogenic mechanism (intrinsic), tubuloglomerular feedback (macula densa), sympathetic nervous system, RAAS

#### 2. Tubular Reabsorption
- ~99% of filtrate reabsorbed; most occurs in PCT
- **Obligatory reabsorption**: PCT (water follows solutes osmotically; not hormonally controlled)
- **Facultative reabsorption**: Collecting duct (water reabsorption controlled by ADH)
- **Transport maximum (Tm)**: Maximum rate of reabsorption for a substance; glucose Tm ~375 mg/min; exceeded when blood glucose >180 mg/dL → glucosuria (renal threshold)

#### 3. Tubular Secretion
- Movement of substances from peritubular capillaries → tubular fluid
- Secretes: H+, K+, NH4+, drugs (penicillin, diuretics), organic acids, creatinine
- Critical for acid-base balance and drug elimination

### Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS)
1. Decreased renal perfusion / decreased NaCl at macula densa / sympathetic activation → JG cells release **renin**
2. Renin converts angiotensinogen (from liver) → **angiotensin I**
3. ACE (in pulmonary capillaries) converts angiotensin I → **angiotensin II**
4. Angiotensin II effects:
   - Potent vasoconstriction → increases SVR and BP
   - Stimulates aldosterone release from adrenal cortex → Na+/water retention, K+ excretion
   - Stimulates ADH release → water retention
   - Stimulates thirst
   - Promotes cardiac and vascular remodeling (long-term)
- **Pharmacologic targets**: ACE Inhibitors block step 3; ARBs block angiotensin II receptors

### ADH (Vasopressin) and Water Balance
- Released from posterior pituitary in response to increased serum osmolality (detected by hypothalamic osmoreceptors) or decreased blood volume
- Acts on collecting duct → inserts aquaporin-2 channels → water reabsorption → concentrated urine
- Absence of ADH → dilute urine → Diabetes Insipidus (see Endocrine System)
- Excess ADH → dilutional hyponatremia → SIADH (see Endocrine System)

### Acid-Base Regulation (see also Acid-Base Balance)
- Kidneys are the **slow but powerful** compensators (hours to days, vs. lungs which respond in minutes)
- **Reabsorb HCO3-**: PCT (~80-90%), thick ascending limb, collecting duct
- **Secrete H+**: PCT and collecting duct (intercalated cells)
- **Generate new HCO3-**: Ammoniagenesis (NH3 + H+ → NH4+ excreted in urine); phosphate buffer system
- **Metabolic acidosis**: Kidneys increase H+ secretion, increase HCO3- reabsorption, increase ammoniagenesis
- **Metabolic alkalosis**: Kidneys decrease H+ secretion, increase HCO3- excretion

### Other Renal Endocrine Functions
| Function | Mechanism |
|----------|-----------|
| **Erythropoietin (EPO)** | Produced by peritubular fibroblasts in response to hypoxia; stimulates RBC production in bone marrow; deficiency in CKD → anemia |
| **Vitamin D activation** | 25-hydroxyvitamin D (from liver) → **1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol)** by 1-alpha-hydroxylase in PCT; stimulated by PTH; promotes intestinal calcium absorption |
| **Prostaglandins** | Maintain renal blood flow (vasodilation of afferent arteriole); NSAIDs inhibit → reduced GFR; especially dangerous in CKD |

### Electrolyte Homeostasis
| Electrolyte | Normal Range | Renal Handling |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|
| Sodium (Na+) | 136-145 mEq/L | Reabsorbed at PCT, loop of Henle, DCT (aldosterone-dependent), collecting duct |
| Potassium (K+) | 3.5-5.0 mEq/L | Reabsorbed at PCT and loop; secreted at DCT/collecting duct (aldosterone-dependent) |
| Calcium (Ca2+) | 8.5-10.5 mg/dL | Reabsorbed at PCT (passive), thick ascending limb, DCT (PTH-dependent) |
| Phosphorus (PO4) | 2.5-4.5 mg/dL | Reabsorbed at PCT (PTH inhibits reabsorption → increases phosphorus excretion) |
| Magnesium (Mg2+) | 1.5-2.5 mg/dL | Reabsorbed at thick ascending limb (60-70%), DCT |
| Bicarbonate (HCO3-) | 22-26 mEq/L | Reabsorbed at PCT (~80-90%), remaining at loop and collecting duct |

---
sections.assessment:
### History
- **Urinary symptoms**: Dysuria, frequency, urgency, hesitancy, nocturia, incontinence, hematuria, flank pain
- **Fluid status**: Edema (peripheral, periorbital, sacral), weight gain, dyspnea, orthopnea
- **Risk factors**: Diabetes, hypertension, nephrotoxic drug exposure (NSAIDs, aminoglycosides, contrast dye), family history of kidney disease, recurrent UTIs, kidney stones
- **Dialysis history**: Type (hemodialysis or peritoneal), access site, schedule

### Physical Exam
- **Fluid status**: Daily weights (most reliable indicator of fluid balance; 1 kg = ~1 L fluid), edema (pitting scale 1+-4+), JVD, lung auscultation (crackles with fluid overload), blood pressure (HTN common in CKD)
- **Skin**: Uremic frost (rare; severe uremia), pruritus, pallor (anemia), bruising
- **Costovertebral angle (CVA) tenderness**: Fist percussion at CVA → tenderness suggests pyelonephritis or nephrolithiasis
- **Bladder assessment**: Palpation/percussion for distension; bladder scan for post-void residual (>100-150 mL suggests retention)

### Intake and Output (I&O) Monitoring
- **Normal urine output**: 0.5-1.0 mL/kg/hr (adults); minimum acceptable ~30 mL/hr
- **Oliguria**: <400 mL/24 hr or <0.5 mL/kg/hr
- **Anuria**: <100 mL/24 hr
- **Polyuria**: >3 L/24 hr
- Strict I&Os: Document ALL intake (oral, IV, tube feeds, blood products) and ALL output (urine, drains, emesis, stool)

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges

| Lab Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| BUN (blood urea nitrogen) | 7-20 mg/dL | Elevated: dehydration, kidney failure, GI bleed, high protein diet; BUN:Cr ratio >20:1 suggests prerenal cause |
| Serum creatinine | 0.6-1.2 mg/dL (varies by age, sex, muscle mass) | Elevated: decreased kidney function; most reliable marker of GFR |
| eGFR | >90 mL/min/1.73 m2 | CKD staging: G1 >=90, G2 60-89, G3a 45-59, G3b 30-44, G4 15-29, G5 <15 (kidney failure) |
| BUN:Creatinine ratio | 10:1 - 20:1 | >20:1 = prerenal (dehydration, HF, renal artery stenosis); <10:1 = intrinsic renal damage, malnutrition |
| Serum sodium | 136-145 mEq/L | Reflects water balance more than sodium balance |
| Serum potassium | 3.5-5.0 mEq/L | Critical: <3.0 or >6.0 = cardiac risk; monitor closely in AKI/CKD |
| Serum calcium | 8.5-10.5 mg/dL | Low in CKD (impaired vitamin D activation) |
| Serum phosphorus | 2.5-4.5 mg/dL | High in CKD (impaired excretion) |
| Serum magnesium | 1.5-2.5 mg/dL | Affected by loop diuretics (wasting) and CKD |
| Urinalysis (UA) | See below | Screen for infection, blood, protein, glucose, casts |
| Urine specific gravity | 1.005-1.030 | Dilute: DI, fluid overload; Concentrated: dehydration |
| Urine protein | <150 mg/24 hr | Proteinuria: glomerular damage; albuminuria is early marker of Diabetic Kidney Disease |
| Urine osmolality | 300-900 mOsm/kg | Assesses concentrating ability |
| Urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (UACR) | <30 mg/g | 30-300 = moderately increased albuminuria; >300 = severely increased |

#### Urinalysis Components
| Component | Normal | Abnormal Significance |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Color | Pale to dark yellow | Red/brown: hematuria, myoglobinuria; orange: rifampin, phenazopyridine |
| Clarity | Clear | Cloudy: infection, crystals |
| pH | 4.5-8.0 | Acidic in high-protein diet, metabolic acidosis; alkaline in UTI (urease-producing organisms), metabolic alkalosis |
| Protein | Negative/trace | Positive: glomerular disease, nephrotic syndrome, preeclampsia |
| Glucose | Negative | Positive: diabetes (blood glucose >180 mg/dL), pregnancy |
| Ketones | Negative | Positive: DKA, starvation, low-carb diet |
| Blood | Negative | Positive: UTI, stones, glomerulonephritis, trauma, tumor |
| WBC/nitrites/leukocyte esterase | Negative | Positive: UTI |
| Casts | Rare hyaline | RBC casts: glomerulonephritis; WBC casts: pyelonephritis; muddy brown granular casts: ATN |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Acute
- Acute Kidney Injury (prerenal, intrarenal/intrinsic, postrenal)
  - Prerenal: Decreased perfusion (hypovolemia, HF, sepsis, renal artery stenosis)
  - Intrarenal: Parenchymal damage (ATN from ischemia or nephrotoxins, glomerulonephritis, interstitial nephritis)
  - Postrenal: Obstruction (BPH, stones, tumor, blood clots)
- Urinary Tract Infections (cystitis, pyelonephritis)
- Nephrolithiasis (kidney stones: calcium oxalate most common)

### Chronic
- Chronic Kidney Disease (progressive, irreversible loss of nephron function)
- Diabetic Kidney Disease (leading cause of CKD/ESRD; microalbuminuria is earliest sign)
- Hypertensive Nephrosclerosis
- Membranous Nephropathy
- Polycystic Kidney Disease
- Nephrotic Syndrome (massive proteinuria >3.5 g/day, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia)
- Nephritic Syndrome (hematuria, RBC casts, mild proteinuria, HTN, oliguria)

### Electrolyte Disorders (see also Fluid and Electrolyte Balance)
- Hyperkalemia / Hypokalemia
- Hypernatremia / Hyponatremia
- Hypercalcemia / Hypocalcemia

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
### Diuretics
- Diuretics
  - **Loop diuretics** (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide): Block Na-K-2Cl transporter in thick ascending limb; most potent; risk of hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, ototoxicity; "ceiling" doses exist
  - **Thiazide diuretics** (hydrochlorothiazide, chlorthalidone, metolazone): Block Na-Cl transporter in DCT; first-line for HTN; risk of hypokalemia, hyperuricemia, hyperglycemia, hypercalcemia
  - **Potassium-sparing** (spironolactone, eplerenone, amiloride, triamterene): Act on collecting duct; spironolactone blocks aldosterone receptors (also used in HFrEF and cirrhotic ascites); risk of hyperkalemia
  - **Osmotic** (mannitol): Freely filtered, not reabsorbed; pulls water into tubules; used for increased ICP; monitor osmolality

### RAAS Blockers
- ACE Inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril) -- renoprotective in diabetic nephropathy; reduce proteinuria; monitor Cr (10-30% rise acceptable), potassium; contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis and pregnancy; side effects: cough, angioedema
- ARBs (losartan, valsartan, irbesartan) -- alternative to ACE-I; same renoprotective benefits; no cough; same contraindications

### Other Renal Medications
- **Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents** (epoetin alfa, darbepoetin): For anemia of CKD; target Hgb 10-11.5 g/dL; ensure adequate iron stores first
- **Phosphate binders** (calcium acetate, sevelamer, lanthanum): For hyperphosphatemia in CKD; take WITH meals to bind dietary phosphorus
- **Vitamin D analogs** (calcitriol, paricalcitol): For secondary hyperparathyroidism in CKD
- **Sodium bicarbonate**: For metabolic acidosis in CKD (target HCO3- >22 mEq/L)
- **Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate)**: For chronic hyperkalemia; not for acute emergencies

---

clinicalPearls: **Daily weight** is the single most reliable indicator of fluid balance; weigh same time daily, same scale, same clothing; 1 kg weight change = ~1 L fluid gain or loss

**Contrast-induced nephropathy (CIN)**: Risk with IV contrast dye; pre-hydrate with isotonic saline; hold metformin before and 48 hours after contrast; monitor creatinine; consider NAC in high-risk patients (evidence debated)
**Nephrotoxic medications to monitor**: Aminoglycosides (gentamicin -- check trough levels), vancomycin (check trough or AUC), NSAIDs, ACE-I/ARBs (monitor Cr), amphotericin B, IV contrast, cisplatin
**AKI vs. CKD differentiation**: AKI = acute onset, potentially reversible, normal-sized kidneys on ultrasound; CKD = chronic, bilateral small kidneys, anemia, hyperphosphatemia, bone disease present

**Foley catheter**: Use only when indicated (acute urinary retention, strict I&O in critically ill, perioperative, open sacral wounds in incontinent patients); remove as soon as possible to reduce CAUTI risk
**Hemodialysis access**: AV fistula (preferred long-term; "thrill and bruit" = patent; NO blood pressure, venipuncture, or IV access in fistula arm), AV graft, or central venous catheter (temporary)
**Peritoneal dialysis**: Uses peritoneum as dialysis membrane; patient performs exchanges at home; monitor for peritonitis (cloudy effluent, abdominal pain, fever)

**CKD complications** to manage: Anemia (EPO + iron), hyperphosphatemia (phosphate binders), hypocalcemia (vitamin D), secondary hyperparathyroidism, metabolic acidosis (bicarbonate), fluid overload (diuretics/dialysis), hyperkalemia (dietary restriction, medications)
**Prerenal AKI**: BUN:Cr >20:1, urine Na <20, FENa <1%, concentrated urine (high specific gravity); responds to volume resuscitation
**Intrarenal AKI (ATN)**: BUN:Cr ~10:1, urine Na >40, FENa >2%, muddy brown granular casts; may need dialysis support
When potassium is >6.0 mEq/L, obtain **stat ECG** and continuous monitoring; peaked T waves are the earliest ECG change

reproductive-system.title: Reproductive System

sections.anatomy:
### Female Reproductive Structures

#### External Genitalia (Vulva)
- **Mons pubis**: Fatty pad over pubic symphysis
- **Labia majora/minora**: Outer (fatty, hair-bearing) and inner (thin, vascular) folds protecting vestibule
- **Clitoris**: Erectile tissue; primary organ of sexual pleasure; homologous to male glans penis
- **Vestibule**: Contains urethral meatus and vaginal orifice
- **Bartholin's glands**: Bilateral; secrete mucus for lubrication; can become obstructed → Bartholin's cyst/abscess
- **Perineum**: Area between vaginal orifice and anus; site of episiotomy/laceration during childbirth

#### Internal Structures
| Structure | Features |
|-----------|----------|
| **Vagina** | Muscular canal ~8-10 cm; acidic pH (3.8-4.5) maintained by Lactobacillus (protective against infection); distensible for birth canal |
| **Cervix** | Lower portion of uterus; opens into vagina; external os (visible on speculum exam); produces mucus; site of Pap smear; dilates during labor (0-10 cm) |
| **Uterus** | Pear-shaped muscular organ; **fundus** (top), **body** (middle), **cervix** (lower); **myometrium** (smooth muscle -- contractions), **endometrium** (inner lining -- sheds during menstruation, implantation site) |
| **Fallopian tubes (oviducts)** | ~10 cm; **fimbriae** sweep oocyte from ovary; **ampulla** = usual site of fertilization; **isthmus** connects to uterus; ciliated epithelium propels egg; ectopic pregnancy most common here |
| **Ovaries** | Paired, almond-sized; produce oocytes and hormones (estrogen, progesterone, androgens); follicle development and ovulation; connected by ovarian ligament |

#### Breast (Mammary Gland)
- Modified sweat gland; overlies pectoralis major
- **Lobules** (milk production) → **lactiferous ducts** → **lactiferous sinuses** → nipple
- Estrogen → duct development; progesterone → alveolar development; prolactin → milk production; oxytocin → milk ejection (let-down reflex)
- Clinical: Breast self-awareness; mammography screening; most common cancer in women

### Male Reproductive Structures

#### External
| Structure | Features |
|-----------|----------|
| **Penis** | Shaft + glans; **corpora cavernosa** (2, erectile) + **corpus spongiosum** (1, surrounds urethra); erection = parasympathetic → nitric oxide → vasodilation → engorgement |
| **Scrotum** | Holds testes outside body (~2-3°C below core temp for spermatogenesis); **cremaster muscle** elevates testes (cold); **dartos muscle** wrinkles scrotal skin |

#### Internal
| Structure | Features |
|-----------|----------|
| **Testes** | Produce sperm (spermatogenesis in seminiferous tubules) and testosterone (Leydig cells); ~500 million sperm/day |
| **Epididymis** | Coiled tube on posterior testis; sperm maturation and storage (~20 days) |
| **Vas deferens (ductus deferens)** | Muscular tube; transports sperm from epididymis to ejaculatory duct; cut during vasectomy |
| **Seminal vesicles** | Produce ~60% of semen volume; fructose-rich alkaline fluid for sperm nourishment |
| **Prostate gland** | Walnut-sized; below bladder, surrounds urethra; produces ~30% of semen; alkaline secretion neutralizes vaginal acidity; enlarges with age → BPH; prostate cancer screening (PSA, DRE) |
| **Bulbourethral (Cowper's) glands** | Pre-ejaculatory mucus; lubricates and neutralizes urethral acidity |

---
sections.physiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — reproductive hormones signal via GPCRs (LH, FSH, GnRH at their receptors) and nuclear receptors (estrogen, progesterone, testosterone act intracellularly). Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the HPG axis (hypothalamus → GnRH → pituitary → LH/FSH → gonads → sex steroids → feedback suppression) is the paradigmatic negative feedback loop; the ovarian cycle adds a unique positive feedback component at the LH surge.*

### Hormonal Regulation

#### Hypothalamic-Pituitary-Gonadal (HPG) Axis
1. **Hypothalamus** → GnRH (gonadotropin-releasing hormone; pulsatile)
2. **Anterior pituitary** → FSH (follicle-stimulating hormone) + LH (luteinizing hormone)
3. **Gonads** → Sex steroids (estrogen, progesterone, testosterone) → negative feedback to hypothalamus/pituitary

#### Female Hormones
| Hormone | Source | Functions |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Estrogen** | Ovarian follicle (granulosa cells); placenta in pregnancy | Secondary sex characteristics; endometrial proliferation; bone density maintenance; lipid profile (cardioprotective); skin moisture |
| **Progesterone** | Corpus luteum; placenta in pregnancy | Stabilizes endometrium for implantation; maintains early pregnancy; thermogenic (↑ basal body temp after ovulation); breast alveolar development |
| **FSH** | Anterior pituitary | Stimulates follicular development and estrogen production |
| **LH** | Anterior pituitary | LH surge triggers ovulation; maintains corpus luteum |

#### Male Hormones
| Hormone | Source | Functions |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| **Testosterone** | Leydig cells (testes); small amount from adrenals | Spermatogenesis, secondary sex characteristics (muscle mass, body hair, voice deepening), libido, bone density, erythropoiesis |
| **FSH** | Anterior pituitary | Stimulates Sertoli cells → support spermatogenesis |
| **LH** | Anterior pituitary | Stimulates Leydig cells → testosterone production |
| **Inhibin** | Sertoli cells | Negative feedback on FSH |

### Menstrual Cycle (~28 days)
| Phase | Days | Ovarian Events | Uterine Events | Hormones |
|-------|------|----------------|----------------|----------|
| **Menstrual** | 1-5 | Follicles beginning development | Endometrial shedding (menses) | Estrogen and progesterone at lowest |
| **Follicular/Proliferative** | 1-13 | Dominant follicle matures; estrogen rises | Endometrium thickens (proliferates) | ↑ Estrogen → ↑ FSH/LH |
| **Ovulation** | Day 14 (~) | LH surge → follicle ruptures → oocyte released | -- | LH peak; estrogen peak triggers LH surge |
| **Luteal/Secretory** | 15-28 | Corpus luteum forms → produces progesterone | Endometrium becomes secretory (glandular, vascular); prepares for implantation | ↑ Progesterone + estrogen |
| **If no fertilization** | Day 28 | Corpus luteum degenerates → ↓ progesterone/estrogen | Spiral arteries constrict → ischemia → shedding (Day 1 again) | Hormone withdrawal triggers menses |
| **If fertilization** | -- | hCG from embryo rescues corpus luteum | Endometrium maintained for implantation | hCG maintains progesterone until placenta takes over (~10-12 weeks) |

### Pregnancy Physiology (Key Changes)
| System | Change |
|--------|--------|
| Cardiovascular | ↑ CO 30-50%, ↑ blood volume 40-50%, ↑ HR; physiologic anemia (dilutional); supine hypotension syndrome (uterus compresses IVC in 3rd trimester → lie on left side) |
| Respiratory | ↑ Tidal volume (↑ O2 delivery); ↑ minute ventilation; mild respiratory alkalosis; diaphragm elevates |
| Renal | ↑ GFR 50% → ↓ creatinine (normal Cr in pregnancy ~0.5); physiologic hydronephrosis (progesterone dilates ureters) |
| GI | Nausea/vomiting (hCG), GERD (relaxed LES from progesterone), constipation |
| Hematologic | Hypercoagulable state (↑ clotting factors; ↑ VTE risk); physiologic leukocytosis |

---
sections.assessment:
### Female
- **History**: Menstrual history (LMP, cycle regularity, flow, dysmenorrhea), obstetric history (GTPAL: Gravida, Term, Preterm, Abortions, Living), contraception, STI history, abnormal bleeding, pelvic pain, breast complaints, menopausal symptoms, sexual health
- **Physical exam**: Breast exam (inspection, palpation, lymph nodes); pelvic exam (external inspection, speculum exam with Pap smear, bimanual exam); fundal height in pregnancy
- **Screening**: Pap smear q3 years starting age 21 (or co-testing with HPV q5 years 25-65); mammography starting age 40-50 (guideline-dependent); STI screening per risk

### Male
- **History**: Urinary symptoms (BPH screen), sexual function (erectile dysfunction, libido), testicular masses or pain, STI history, PSA history
- **Physical exam**: Testicular exam (palpate for masses, tenderness); DRE (prostate size, nodules, tenderness)
- **Screening**: PSA + DRE for prostate cancer (shared decision-making starting age 50 or 40-45 if high risk); testicular self-exam education in young men

### Key Lab Values
| Test | Use |
|------|-----|
| hCG (serum/urine) | Pregnancy detection; doubles q48-72 hr in early normal pregnancy; ectopic pregnancy = slow rise |
| Pap smear | Cervical cancer screening (cytology) |
| HPV testing | Co-testing with Pap; high-risk strains (16, 18) cause cervical cancer |
| PSA | Prostate-specific antigen; elevated in BPH, prostatitis, and prostate cancer (not specific) |
| STI panel | Gonorrhea, chlamydia (NAAT), syphilis (RPR/VDRL, FTA-ABS), HIV, hepatitis B/C |
| FSH/LH/Estradiol | Fertility workup, menopause confirmation |
| Testosterone (total/free) | Male hypogonadism |
| Semen analysis | Male fertility (count, motility, morphology) |

---
sections.common-conditions:
### Female
- Menstrual disorders (amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, PMDD)
- Polycystic Ovary Syndrome (anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries)
- Endometriosis (ectopic endometrial tissue → cyclic pain, infertility)
- Uterine fibroids (leiomyomas -- benign; menorrhagia, pelvic pressure)
- Cervical cancer (HPV 16/18; preventable with vaccination)
- Ovarian cancer (often late diagnosis; CA-125 marker)
- Breast cancer (most common cancer in women; BRCA1/2 mutations)
- Preeclampsia (HTN + proteinuria after 20 weeks; can progress to eclampsia with seizures; HELLP syndrome)
- Ectopic pregnancy (most common in fallopian tube; surgical emergency if ruptured)
- STIs (see HIV, Syphilis)

### Male
- BPH (benign prostatic hyperplasia; urinary retention, frequency, nocturia; treated with alpha-blockers, 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors, or TURP)
- Prostate cancer (most common cancer in men; slow-growing; Gleason score for grading)
- Testicular cancer (most common in young men 15-35; painless mass; excellent cure rate with treatment)
- Erectile dysfunction (vascular, neurogenic, psychogenic, medications; PDE5 inhibitors -- sildenafil, tadalafil)
- Epididymitis/orchitis

### Both
- STIs: Chlamydia, gonorrhea, syphilis, herpes, HPV, HIV, trichomoniasis
- Infertility (~15% of couples)

---
sections.pharmacology-connections:
- **Oral contraceptives**: Combined (estrogen + progestin) or progestin-only; suppress ovulation (LH surge); thicken cervical mucus; thin endometrium; increased VTE risk with estrogen-containing (contraindicated in smokers >35, migraine with aura, history of VTE)
- **Emergency contraception**: Levonorgestrel (Plan B -- most effective within 72 hr); ulipristal (ella -- effective up to 120 hr); copper IUD (most effective)
- **Oxytocin (Pitocin)**: Induces/augments labor; causes uterine contraction; postpartum hemorrhage treatment; titrate carefully (uterine hyperstimulation → fetal distress)
- **Magnesium sulfate**: Seizure prophylaxis/treatment in preeclampsia/eclampsia; tocolytic (delays preterm labor); neuroprotection for preterm fetus; monitor for toxicity (loss of DTRs → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest); antidote: calcium gluconate
- **Terbutaline**: Beta-2 agonist tocolytic for acute preterm labor management
- **PDE5 inhibitors** (sildenafil, tadalafil): Erectile dysfunction; contraindicated with nitrates (severe hypotension)
- **Alpha-blockers** (tamsulosin): BPH; relax smooth muscle in prostate/bladder neck; orthostatic hypotension
- **5-alpha-reductase inhibitors** (finasteride, dutasteride): BPH and male pattern baldness; reduce DHT; takes months for effect; teratogenic (pregnant women should not handle crushed tablets)
- **HPV vaccine** (Gardasil 9): Covers 9 HPV types; prevents cervical, anal, oropharyngeal, penile cancers and genital warts; recommended ages 9-26 (can extend to 45 per shared decision)

---

clinicalPearls: **Ectopic pregnancy must be on the differential for ANY reproductive-age woman with abdominal pain.** Ruptured ectopic is a surgical emergency with life-threatening hemorrhage. Always ask LMP and consider pregnancy test.

**Magnesium sulfate toxicity follows a predictable pattern:** First DTRs disappear → then respiratory depression → then cardiac arrest. Nursing assessment: Check patellar DTRs, respiratory rate, and urine output (≥30 mL/hr) before each dose. Keep calcium gluconate at bedside.

**The hypercoagulable state of pregnancy** means DVT and PE risk is 5-10x higher than non-pregnant women. Risk continues 6-12 weeks postpartum. Virchow's triad: stasis (uterine compression of IVC), endothelial damage (delivery), hypercoagulability (increased clotting factors).

**Testicular torsion is a urologic emergency.** Sudden onset severe testicular pain in a young male = torsion until proven otherwise. Salvage rate drops significantly after 6 hours. "High-riding" testis with absent cremasteric reflex → surgical exploration.

**HPV vaccination prevents cancer.** This is one of the few vaccines that directly prevents cancer (cervical, anal, oropharyngeal, penile). Strongly recommend for all eligible patients regardless of sex.

**Postpartum hemorrhage (PPH)**: Leading cause of maternal death worldwide. "Tone, Trauma, Tissue, Thrombin" -- the 4 T's of PPH etiology. Uterine atony is the most common cause → fundal massage + oxytocin first-line.

**PSA is not diagnostic for prostate cancer.** It's elevated in BPH, prostatitis, after vigorous exercise or ejaculation, and cancer. Shared decision-making about screening is key -- discuss benefits (early detection) and harms (false positives, overdiagnosis, biopsy risks).

**BRCA1/2 mutation carriers** have dramatically elevated lifetime risk of breast (60-80%) and ovarian cancer (15-40%). Genetic counseling and consideration of enhanced screening or risk-reducing surgery.

respiratory-system.title: Respiratory System

sections.anatomy:
### Key Structures

#### Upper Respiratory Tract
- **Nose and nasal cavity**
  - Lined with respiratory mucosa (pseudostratified ciliated columnar epithelium with goblet cells)
  - **Nasal conchae** (superior, middle, inferior turbinates): Increase surface area; warm, humidify, and filter inspired air
  - **Paranasal sinuses**: Frontal, maxillary, ethmoid, sphenoid; lighten skull, resonance, produce mucus
  - **Olfactory epithelium**: Superior nasal concha; CN I (Nervous System)
- **Pharynx** (throat) -- three regions:
  - **Nasopharynx**: Posterior to nasal cavity; pharyngeal tonsil (adenoid); Eustachian tube opening
  - **Oropharynx**: Posterior to oral cavity; palatine and lingual tonsils
  - **Laryngopharynx (hypopharynx)**: Inferior; connects to larynx anteriorly and esophagus posteriorly
- **Larynx** (voice box)
  - 9 cartilages; most prominent:
    - **Thyroid cartilage** ("Adam's apple")
    - **Cricoid cartilage** (complete ring; landmark for emergency cricothyrotomy)
    - **Epiglottis** (elastic cartilage; covers glottis during swallowing to prevent aspiration)
    - **Arytenoid cartilages** (vocal cord attachment and movement)
  - **Vocal cords (vocal folds)**: True vocal cords for phonation; glottis = opening between them
  - **Innervation**: Recurrent laryngeal nerve (branch of CN X / vagus); damage → hoarseness

#### Lower Respiratory Tract
- **Trachea**
  - ~10-12 cm long, ~2.5 cm diameter
  - 16-20 C-shaped hyaline cartilage rings (open posteriorly against esophagus; smooth muscle = trachealis)
  - Lined with respiratory mucosa; **mucociliary escalator** moves debris upward
- **Carina**: Ridge at tracheal bifurcation (at T4-T5 level); highly sensitive to stimulation (cough reflex)
  - **Clinical Pearl**: Right mainstem bronchus is wider, shorter, and more vertical than left → aspirated objects and right mainstem intubation more common on the right
- **Bronchial tree**:
  - Primary (mainstem) bronchi → secondary (lobar) bronchi → tertiary (segmental) bronchi → bronchioles → terminal bronchioles → respiratory bronchioles → alveolar ducts → alveolar sacs → alveoli
  - Cartilage gradually decreases; smooth muscle increases (bronchioles are entirely smooth muscle -- site of bronchospasm in Asthma)
- **Conducting zone**: Nose → terminal bronchioles; no gas exchange; **anatomic dead space** (~150 mL)
- **Respiratory zone**: Respiratory bronchioles → alveoli; site of gas exchange

#### Alveoli
- ~300-500 million alveoli; total surface area ~70 m2
- **Type I alveolar cells (pneumocytes)**: Simple squamous; ~95% of surface area; gas exchange
- **Type II alveolar cells (pneumocytes)**: Cuboidal; produce **surfactant** (reduces surface tension, prevents alveolar collapse)
  - Surfactant production begins ~24-28 weeks gestation; adequate levels by ~35 weeks
  - Deficiency → Neonatal Respiratory Distress Syndrome
- **Alveolar macrophages (dust cells)**: Phagocytize pathogens and debris
- **Respiratory membrane** (blood-air barrier): ~0.5 mcm thick
  - Alveolar epithelium → fused basement membranes → capillary endothelium

#### Lungs
- **Right lung**: 3 lobes (superior, middle, inferior); 2 fissures (horizontal, oblique); 10 bronchopulmonary segments
- **Left lung**: 2 lobes (superior, inferior); 1 fissure (oblique); 8-9 bronchopulmonary segments
  - **Cardiac notch**: Concavity on left lung accommodating heart
  - **Lingula**: Tongue-like projection of left superior lobe (corresponds to right middle lobe)
- **Hilum**: Medial surface; entry/exit of bronchi, pulmonary vessels, nerves, lymphatics
- **Pleural membranes**:
  - **Visceral pleura**: Adheres to lung surface
  - **Parietal pleura**: Lines thoracic wall, diaphragm, mediastinum
  - **Pleural cavity**: Potential space with ~5-15 mL serous fluid; creates negative intrapleural pressure
  - **Clinical Pearl**: Loss of negative intrapleural pressure → lung collapse (Pneumothorax)

#### Muscles of Respiration
| Muscle | Innervation | Function |
|--------|------------|----------|
| Diaphragm (primary) | Phrenic nerve (C3-C5) | Contraction → flattens → increases thoracic volume (75% of quiet breathing) |
| External intercostals | Intercostal nerves (T1-T11) | Elevate ribs → increase AP and lateral diameter |
| Internal intercostals | Intercostal nerves | Depress ribs (forced expiration) |
| Sternocleidomastoid | CN XI (accessory) | Accessory muscle; elevates sternum |
| Scalenes | Cervical nerves | Accessory muscle; elevates first two ribs |
| Abdominals (rectus, obliques, transversus) | Thoracoabdominal nerves | Forced expiration; increase intra-abdominal pressure |

- **Clinical Pearl**: Mnemonic for phrenic nerve: "C3, 4, 5 keeps the diaphragm alive"; high spinal cord injuries above C3 → respiratory paralysis

### Blood Supply

#### Pulmonary Circulation (Gas Exchange)
- **Pulmonary arteries**: Carry deoxygenated blood from RV to lungs (only arteries carrying deoxygenated blood)
- **Pulmonary capillaries**: Surround alveoli; site of gas exchange
- **Pulmonary veins** (4): Carry oxygenated blood from lungs to LA (only veins carrying oxygenated blood)
- Low-pressure system (~25/10 mmHg; mean ~15 mmHg)

#### Bronchial Circulation (Nutritive)
- **Bronchial arteries**: Arise from thoracic aorta; supply bronchial walls, pleura, connective tissue
- **Bronchial veins**: Some drain into azygos system; some drain into pulmonary veins (contributes to normal anatomic shunt)

### Innervation
- **Parasympathetic (vagus nerve, CN X)**:
  - Bronchoconstriction (smooth muscle contraction)
  - Increased mucus secretion
  - Vasodilation of bronchial vessels
- **Sympathetic**:
  - Bronchodilation (beta-2 receptors on bronchial smooth muscle)
  - Decreased mucus secretion
  - Vasoconstriction of bronchial vessels
- **Cough reflex**: Vagal afferents from larynx, trachea, bronchi → medulla → explosive expiration
- **Hering-Breuer reflex**: Stretch receptors in bronchioles; limits over-inflation during deep inspiration

---
sections.physiology:
### Normal Function

#### Ventilation (Breathing Mechanics)

**Boyle's Law**: At constant temperature, pressure and volume are inversely related (P1V1 = P2V2)

| Phase | Diaphragm | Thoracic Volume | Intrapulmonary Pressure | Air Flow |
|-------|-----------|----------------|------------------------|----------|
| Inspiration | Contracts (flattens) | Increases | Decreases below atmospheric | Air flows IN |
| Expiration | Relaxes (dome shape) | Decreases | Increases above atmospheric | Air flows OUT |

- **Quiet inspiration**: Active process (diaphragm + external intercostals)
- **Quiet expiration**: Passive process (elastic recoil of lungs and chest wall)
- **Forced inspiration**: Accessory muscles recruited (SCM, scalenes, pectoralis minor)
- **Forced expiration**: Active contraction of internal intercostals and abdominals

#### Lung Volumes and Capacities
| Measurement | Definition | Normal Value |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| Tidal Volume (TV) | Volume of normal quiet breath | ~500 mL |
| Inspiratory Reserve Volume (IRV) | Extra air inspired beyond TV | ~3,100 mL |
| Expiratory Reserve Volume (ERV) | Extra air expired beyond TV | ~1,200 mL |
| Residual Volume (RV) | Air remaining after maximal expiration | ~1,200 mL |
| Inspiratory Capacity (IC) | TV + IRV | ~3,600 mL |
| Functional Residual Capacity (FRC) | ERV + RV | ~2,400 mL |
| Vital Capacity (VC) | TV + IRV + ERV | ~4,800 mL |
| Total Lung Capacity (TLC) | VC + RV | ~6,000 mL |
| Minute Ventilation | TV x RR | ~6,000 mL/min (500 x 12) |
| Alveolar Ventilation | (TV - dead space) x RR | ~4,200 mL/min (350 x 12) |

- **FEV1**: Volume expired in first second of forced expiration; normal >80% predicted
- **FEV1/FVC ratio**: Normal >70%; reduced in obstructive disease (COPD, Asthma); normal or increased in restrictive disease

> *First principles: Oxygenation and Ventilation O2 and CO2 move down partial pressure gradients across a 0.5 mcm membrane. Oxygenation and Ventilation Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction actively redirects blood away from hypoventilated alveoli to preserve V/Q matching; disruption of V/Q matching is the most common mechanism of hypoxemia in pulmonary disease.*

#### External Respiration (Pulmonary Gas Exchange)
- **Dalton's Law**: Total pressure = sum of partial pressures of individual gases
- **Henry's Law**: Gas dissolves in liquid in proportion to its partial pressure

| Gas | Atmospheric Air | Alveolar Air | Arterial Blood | Venous Blood |
|-----|----------------|-------------|----------------|--------------|
| PO2 | 159 mmHg | 104 mmHg | 95-100 mmHg | 40 mmHg |
| PCO2 | 0.3 mmHg | 40 mmHg | 35-45 mmHg | 46 mmHg |

- O2 diffuses from alveoli (high PO2) → pulmonary capillary blood (low PO2)
- CO2 diffuses from pulmonary capillary blood (high PCO2) → alveoli (lower PCO2)
- CO2 diffuses ~20x faster than O2 (greater solubility)

#### Oxygen Transport
- **98.5% bound to hemoglobin** (each Hgb carries up to 4 O2 molecules)
- **1.5% dissolved in plasma** (measured as PaO2)
- **Oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve**: Sigmoidal shape
  - **Right shift** (decreased O2 affinity; O2 unloading at tissues): Increased temp, increased PCO2, increased H+ (decreased pH), increased 2,3-DPG
  - **Left shift** (increased O2 affinity; O2 stays bound): Decreased temp, decreased PCO2, decreased H+ (increased pH), decreased 2,3-DPG, fetal hemoglobin, CO poisoning
  - Mnemonic: "**R**ight shift = **R**elease oxygen to tissues"
- **SaO2/SpO2**: Oxygen saturation; normal 95-100%; <90% = hypoxemia requiring intervention

#### Carbon Dioxide Transport
- **70% as bicarbonate (HCO3-)**: CO2 + H2O → (carbonic anhydrase) → H2CO3 → H+ + HCO3- (chloride shift in RBCs)
- **23% bound to hemoglobin** (carbaminohemoglobin; binds to globin, not heme)
- **7% dissolved in plasma** (measured as PaCO2)

#### Internal Respiration (Tissue Gas Exchange)
- O2 diffuses from systemic capillaries (high PO2) → tissues (low PO2)
- CO2 diffuses from tissues (high PCO2) → systemic capillaries (lower PCO2)

### Key Regulatory Mechanisms

#### Neural Control of Breathing
- **Medullary respiratory center**:
  - **Ventral respiratory group (VRG)**: Primary rhythm generator; drives active inspiration and forced expiration
  - **Dorsal respiratory group (DRG)**: Integrates sensory input; modifies breathing rhythm
- **Pontine respiratory centers**:
  - **Pneumotaxic center**: Limits inspiration; increases respiratory rate
  - **Apneustic center**: Promotes prolonged inspiration (usually overridden by pneumotaxic center)

#### Chemical Control
| Stimulus | Receptor Location | Response |
|----------|------------------|----------|
| Increased PaCO2 (hypercapnia) | Central chemoreceptors (medulla; respond to CSF pH/H+) | PRIMARY drive to breathe; increased rate and depth |
| Decreased PaO2 (hypoxemia) | Peripheral chemoreceptors (carotid and aortic bodies) | Secondary drive; significant when PaO2 <60 mmHg |
| Decreased pH (acidosis) | Both central and peripheral | Increased ventilation (compensatory respiratory alkalosis) |

- **Clinical Pearl**: In chronic CO2 retainers (COPD), the central chemoreceptors become desensitized to CO2; breathing is driven primarily by **hypoxic drive** (peripheral chemoreceptors). High-flow O2 can abolish hypoxic drive → respiratory depression. Use **low-flow O2** (1-2 L/min via nasal cannula) and target SpO2 88-92%

#### Ventilation-Perfusion (V/Q) Matching
- Ideal V/Q ratio = ~0.8
- **Dead space** (high V/Q): Ventilated but not perfused (e.g., Pulmonary Embolism)
- **Shunt** (low V/Q): Perfused but not ventilated (e.g., Atelectasis, Pneumonia)
- **Hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV)**: Local response to redirect blood away from poorly ventilated alveoli → maintains V/Q matching
  - Opposite of systemic vasculature (where hypoxia causes vasodilation)

### Key Lab Values / Normal Ranges

| Lab Test | Normal Range | Clinical Significance |
|----------|-------------|----------------------|
| PaO2 | 80-100 mmHg | Partial pressure of O2 in arterial blood |
| PaCO2 | 35-45 mmHg | Partial pressure of CO2; reflects ventilation |
| pH | 7.35-7.45 | Acid-base status |
| HCO3- | 22-26 mEq/L | Metabolic component |
| SaO2 / SpO2 | 95-100% | Oxygen saturation |
| FEV1 | >80% predicted | Lung function; reduced in obstruction |
| FEV1/FVC | >70% | Obstructive vs. restrictive pattern |
| Peak flow | Varies by age/height/sex | Asthma monitoring |
