The pupillary light reflex is a neural circuit: light → retina → CN II → pretectal nucleus → CN III (parasympathetic efferents) → sphincter pupillae contracts → constriction. When ICP rises and uncal herniation compresses CN III, the parasympathetic fibers (outer surface of the nerve) fail first → reflex arc breaks → sympathetic tone unopposed → **blown pupil**. Checking the pupillary reflex is assessing the integrity of a specific neural circuit in real time.

*See also: NTICU Shadowing Prep Guide · ICP Monitoring and Management · Antiepileptics · Sedatives and Anxiolytics · Intracerebral Hemorrhage · Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury*

---
sections.references:
- Guyton and Hall: Textbook of Medical Physiology — Chapters on Membrane Potentials, Cardiac Electrophysiology
- Boron and Boulpaep: Medical Physiology — Excitable Cells
- ECCguidelines.heart.org — ACLS Algorithms
- Katzung: Basic & Clinical Pharmacology — Antiarrhythmics chapter

---

*See also: Antiarrhythmics · Antiepileptics · Local Anesthetics · Neuromuscular Blocking Agents · Hyperkalemia · Cardiovascular System · Nervous System · Fluid and Electrolyte Balance*

clinicalPearls: **Every arrhythmia is an electricity problem.** Either the impulse is forming abnormally (automaticity), conducting abnormally (re-entry, block), or the substrate is electrically unstable (ischemia, electrolyte imbalance). When you can identify WHICH electricity problem, the treatment becomes logical.

**Calcium is the membrane stabilizer, not the potassium fixer.** In hyperkalemia, IV calcium doesn't change the potassium level at all — it raises the threshold potential, widening the gap between the depolarized RMP and threshold. This buys you time. The insulin, bicarb, and dialysis actually move the potassium.

**Local anesthetics and antiarrhythmics are the SAME drugs.** Lidocaine is both a local anesthetic (blocks Na+ channels in peripheral nerves) and a Class IB antiarrhythmic (blocks Na+ channels in cardiac tissue). Same mechanism, different target tissue.

**The "funny current" is why your heart beats without your brain.** SA node pacemaker cells spontaneously depolarize through the If channel. This is why a denervated transplanted heart still beats. Beta-blockers slow this current → lower heart rate.

**Defibrillation is a mass reset.** The shock simultaneously depolarizes every cardiac cell, putting them all into the refractory period at the same time. When they recover together, the SA node (fastest pacemaker) takes over → organized rhythm restored.

**R-on-T kills.** A premature ventricular beat landing during the relative refractory period (the T wave) can trigger VF because some cells can re-excite while others can't → chaotic re-entry. This is why synchronized cardioversion avoids the T wave.

enzymes-and-biological-catalysis.title: Enzymes and Biological Catalysis

sections.the-physics-why-enzymes-exist:
Chemical reactions require **activation energy** — a minimum energy input to get the reaction started. Even reactions that would release energy (exergonic) need this initial push.

**Enzymes lower the activation energy** without being consumed. They don't change WHAT reaction happens — they change HOW FAST it happens. The reaction would occur eventually without the enzyme; the enzyme makes it happen in milliseconds instead of centuries.

> **Analogy:** A boulder on top of a hill will roll down eventually (thermodynamically favorable). But there's a lip at the edge it needs to get over first (activation energy). The enzyme smooths that lip, letting the boulder roll immediately.

### Key Enzyme Properties

| Property | What It Means | Clinical Relevance |
|----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Specificity** | Each enzyme catalyzes ONE reaction (or a narrow set) | This is why enzyme deficiencies cause very specific diseases |
| **Saturability** | At maximum substrate concentration, the enzyme is fully occupied — adding more substrate doesn't increase rate | This is why drug metabolism plateaus at high doses (zero-order kinetics). See Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics |
| **Regulation** | Enzyme activity can be increased or decreased by activators, inhibitors, and gene expression | This is how the body controls metabolic pathways and how drugs exert their effects |
| **Not consumed** | Enzymes are recycled — one molecule can catalyze millions of reactions | This is why small amounts of enzyme have enormous effects |

---
sections.enzyme-kinetics-the-math-that-matters-cl:
### Michaelis-Menten Kinetics

Most enzymes follow **Michaelis-Menten** kinetics:

- At **low substrate**: Reaction rate increases linearly with substrate (first-order kinetics — the more substrate, the faster the reaction)
- At **high substrate**: All enzyme active sites are occupied → rate plateaus at **Vmax** (zero-order kinetics — adding more substrate doesn't help)

Two critical parameters:
- **Vmax** = maximum reaction rate when enzyme is fully saturated
- **Km** = substrate concentration at which reaction is at HALF Vmax (this measures enzyme-substrate affinity — lower Km = higher affinity)

> **Why this matters for drugs:** Most drugs are metabolized by enzymes (CYP450 system). At therapeutic doses, there's plenty of enzyme available → first-order kinetics (constant fraction eliminated per unit time → predictable half-life). At TOXIC doses, enzymes saturate → zero-order kinetics (constant AMOUNT eliminated per unit time → drug accumulates dangerously).

### The Alcohol and Phenytoin Problem

**Ethanol** and **phenytoin** are the classic examples of drugs that saturate their metabolizing enzymes at THERAPEUTIC/RECREATIONAL doses:

| Drug | Enzyme | What Happens | Clinical Impact |
|------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Ethanol** | Alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) | ADH saturates at ~1 drink/hour. Beyond that → zero-order (constant ~15-20 mg/dL/hr clearance) | You can't "speed up" alcohol metabolism. Time is the only treatment. Predictable BAC decline |
| **Phenytoin** | CYP2C9 | Saturates within therapeutic range. Small dose increases → disproportionately large level increases | Therapeutic range is very narrow. A 10% dose increase can cause a 50% level increase → toxicity |

---
sections.the-cyp450-system-the-master-drug-metabo:
The **cytochrome P450 (CYP)** enzyme family is the most clinically important enzyme system for nurses to understand. Located primarily in the liver, these enzymes metabolize ~75% of all drugs.

### Major CYP Enzymes

| Enzyme | Drugs It Metabolizes | Why You Care |
|--------|---------------------|-------------|
| **CYP3A4** | ~50% of all drugs: midazolam, fentanyl, amiodarone, statins, CCBs, cyclosporine, many antibiotics | The big one. Inhibit or induce this → MASSIVE drug interactions |
| **CYP2D6** | Codeine → morphine, tramadol, metoprolol, many antidepressants, tamoxifen | Genetic polymorphism: poor metabolizers get no effect from codeine; ultra-rapid metabolizers get toxic levels |
| **CYP2C9** | Warfarin, phenytoin, NSAIDs, losartan | Warfarin dosing varies 10-fold based on CYP2C9 genetics |
| **CYP2C19** | Clopidogrel (activation), PPIs, diazepam | Poor metabolizers can't activate clopidogrel → stent thrombosis |
| **CYP1A2** | Caffeine, theophylline, clozapine, warfarin (minor) | Induced by smoking → smokers clear these drugs faster |
| **CYP2E1** | Ethanol, acetaminophen (toxic metabolite NAPQI) | Chronic alcohol use induces CYP2E1 → MORE NAPQI from acetaminophen → liver toxicity at lower APAP doses |

### Inhibitors and Inducers — Drug Interactions

**Inhibitors** BLOCK the enzyme → drug levels INCREASE (the drug isn't being metabolized):

| Inhibitor | Enzyme Blocked | Dangerous Interaction |
|-----------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Fluconazole** | CYP2C9, CYP3A4 | ↑ Warfarin levels → bleeding |
| **Erythromycin/clarithromycin** | CYP3A4 | ↑ Statin levels → rhabdomyolysis |
| **Grapefruit juice** | CYP3A4 (intestinal) | ↑ Many drug levels (CCBs, statins, immunosuppressants) |
| **Fluoxetine (Prozac)** | CYP2D6 | ↑ Metoprolol, codeine/tramadol effects unpredictable |
| **Cimetidine** | Multiple CYPs | ↑ Many drug levels (why ranitidine/famotidine are preferred) |

**Inducers** INCREASE enzyme production → drug levels DECREASE (the drug is metabolized faster):

| Inducer | Enzyme Induced | Dangerous Interaction |
|---------|---------------|----------------------|
| **Rifampin** | CYP3A4, CYP2C9, nearly all CYPs | ↓ EVERYTHING: warfarin, OCP, HIV drugs, immunosuppressants |
| **Carbamazepine** | CYP3A4 | ↓ OCP efficacy (pregnancy risk), ↓ other anticonvulsants |
| **Phenytoin** | CYP3A4 | ↓ OCP, warfarin, cyclosporine |
| **St. John's Wort** | CYP3A4 | ↓ OCP, immunosuppressants, HIV drugs |
| **Smoking (tobacco)** | CYP1A2 | ↓ Clozapine, theophylline, caffeine. Quitting smoking = INCREASED drug levels |

> **Memory aid — the "dirty" drugs:** The most dangerous enzyme inhibitors/inducers are antibiotics (rifampin, macrolides, fluoroquinolones, azole antifungals), anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), and HIV medications. Always check interactions when starting any of these.

---
sections.enzyme-deficiency-disease:
When a genetic mutation causes an enzyme to be absent or dysfunctional, the substrate accumulates and the product is missing:

| Disease | Missing/Deficient Enzyme | What Accumulates | Clinical Effect |
|---------|------------------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Phenylketonuria (PKU)** | Phenylalanine hydroxylase | Phenylalanine | Intellectual disability if untreated (newborn screening catches this) |
| **G6PD deficiency** | Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase | Oxidative damage to RBCs (can't regenerate glutathione) | Hemolytic anemia triggered by oxidative stress (sulfonamides, fava beans, infection) |
| **Gaucher disease** | Glucocerebrosidase | Glucocerebroside in macrophages | Hepatosplenomegaly, bone disease |
| **Acute intermittent porphyria** | Porphobilinogen deaminase | Porphobilinogen, ALA | Abdominal pain, neuro-psychiatric symptoms, dark urine |
| **Malignant hyperthermia** | RYR1 mutation (ryanodine receptor dysfunction) | Ca2+ in sarcoplasm (uncontrolled release) | Hyperthermia, rigidity, rhabdomyolysis, death if untreated. Triggered by succinylcholine and volatile anesthetics |

> **G6PD deficiency matters in the ED** — these patients can hemolyze after receiving certain drugs (sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, dapsone, rasburicase) or during severe infection/DKA. Check G6PD before giving rasburicase for Tumor Lysis Syndrome.

---
sections.enzymes-as-diagnostic-markers:
When cells are damaged, their intracellular enzymes leak into the blood. We measure these to identify WHICH organ is injured:

| Enzyme | Source | What Elevated Levels Mean |
|--------|--------|--------------------------|
| **Troponin (I or T)** | Cardiac myocytes | Myocardial injury — STEMI, NSTEMI, myocarditis, PE, sepsis (demand ischemia) |
| **AST (SGOT)** | Liver, heart, muscle, kidney | Non-specific. Liver disease, MI, rhabdomyolysis, hemolysis |
| **ALT (SGPT)** | Liver (most specific) | Hepatocellular damage — hepatitis, Drug-Induced Liver Injury, ischemic hepatitis |
| **Alkaline phosphatase (ALP)** | Liver (biliary), bone | Cholestasis, bone disease. Biliary obstruction = ALP + GGT both elevated |
| **GGT** | Liver (biliary) | Cholestasis, alcohol use. Helps distinguish liver ALP from bone ALP |
| **Lipase** | Pancreas | Acute Pancreatitis (>3x upper limit of normal is diagnostic) |
| **CK (creatine kinase)** | Skeletal muscle, cardiac muscle | Rhabdomyolysis, trauma, seizures, statins. CK-MB fraction = cardiac specific (though troponin is now preferred) |
| **LDH** | Ubiquitous | Non-specific tissue damage. Hemolysis, Tumor Lysis Syndrome, lymphoma, liver disease |
| **Amylase** | Pancreas, salivary glands | Pancreatitis (less specific than lipase — also elevated in salivary pathology, bowel obstruction, renal failure) |

> **Why troponin revolutionized cardiology:** Troponin is almost exclusively from cardiac myocytes. A rising troponin pattern = active myocardial injury. But remember: troponin elevation ≠ ACS. Sepsis, PE, renal failure, and myocarditis all cause troponin elevation through demand ischemia or direct myocyte injury.

---
sections.enzymes-as-drug-targets:
Many drugs work by directly inhibiting a specific enzyme:

| Drug Class | Target Enzyme | What Inhibition Does | Clinical Use |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|-------------|
| ACE Inhibitors | Angiotensin-converting enzyme | ↓ Angiotensin II → ↓ vasoconstriction + ↓ aldosterone | Heart failure, hypertension, renal protection |
| Statins | HMG-CoA reductase | ↓ Cholesterol synthesis | Hyperlipidemia, ACS secondary prevention |
| PPIs | H+/K+ ATPase (proton pump) | ↓ Gastric acid secretion | GERD, peptic ulcer, GI bleeding |
| NSAIDs | Cyclooxygenase (COX-1, COX-2) | ↓ Prostaglandin synthesis → ↓ pain, inflammation, platelet aggregation | Pain, inflammation, fever, antiplatelet (aspirin) |
| Warfarin | Vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1) | ↓ Vitamin K recycling → ↓ clotting factor synthesis | Anticoagulation (AF, VTE, mechanical valves) |
| NAC | Replaces glutathione (not enzyme inhibitor per se) | Detoxifies NAPQI (the toxic APAP metabolite from CYP2E1) | Acetaminophen overdose |
| Aspirin | COX-1 (irreversible) | Permanently blocks thromboxane A2 in platelets → ↓ aggregation | ACS, stroke prevention |

---
sections.connections-to-other-first-principles:
- **Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology** — Many signaling pathways use enzymes as effectors (kinases, phosphatases, phospholipases). Receptor activation → enzyme cascade → cellular response.
- **Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics** — Drug metabolism IS enzyme kinetics. First-order vs. zero-order kinetics, half-life, drug interactions — all enzyme concepts.
- **Genetics and Protein Expression** — Enzymes are proteins. Genetic mutations → abnormal enzyme → inborn errors of metabolism. Pharmacogenomics predicts drug metabolism.
- **Coagulation and Hemostasis** — The clotting cascade is a series of enzyme activations (serine proteases). Each factor activates the next. Warfarin, heparin, and DOACs all target specific enzymes in this cascade.
- **Homeostasis and Feedback Loops** — Enzyme regulation (allosteric, competitive, feedback inhibition) is how metabolic pathways self-regulate.

---
sections.references:
- Lehninger: Principles of Biochemistry — Enzyme Kinetics
- Katzung: Basic & Clinical Pharmacology — Drug Metabolism, CYP450 System
- Guyton and Hall: Textbook of Medical Physiology — Enzyme function

---

*See also: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics · Coagulation and Hemostasis · Genetics and Protein Expression · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops · ACE Inhibitors · Statins · NSAIDs · Antidotes and Toxicology Agents*

clinicalPearls: **"The dose makes the poison" is an enzyme saturation problem.** Acetaminophen is safe at therapeutic doses because glucuronidation and sulfation enzymes handle it. At overdose, those enzymes saturate → CYP2E1 produces NAPQI faster than glutathione can neutralize it → liver necrosis. NAC works by replenishing glutathione.

**Rifampin is the nuclear bomb of drug interactions.** It induces nearly every CYP enzyme. When you start rifampin, assume EVERY other drug's level will drop. When you stop rifampin, assume everything will rise. Check EVERYTHING.

**When a patient quits smoking, their drug levels go UP.** Tobacco smoke (not nicotine) induces CYP1A2. Quitting → CYP1A2 activity drops → clozapine, theophylline, and caffeine levels rise. Nicotine patches don't cause this — it's the combustion products.

**Lab enzymes tell you WHAT is dying, not WHY.** Elevated troponin = myocardial injury. Elevated lipase = pancreatic injury. But you still need clinical context to determine the CAUSE. Troponin rises in sepsis, PE, renal failure — not just ACS.

**G6PD deficiency is the enzyme deficiency you'll see most in the ED.** It's the most common enzyme deficiency worldwide. These patients hemolyze when exposed to oxidative stress (certain drugs, infection, DKA). Always ask about it before giving sulfonamides or nitrofurantoin.

epidemiology-basics.title: Epidemiology Basics

sections.definition: Epidemiology is the study of the distribution and determinants of disease in populations. Where clinical medicine focuses on treating the individual patient, epidemiology asks: *Who is getting sick, where, when, why, and what can we do about it at the population level?* For nurses, epidemiology provides the framework for understanding disease patterns, interpreting risk data, evaluating screening and prevention strategies, and recognizing outbreaks. It is the scientific backbone of public health and evidence-based practice.
sections.key-principles:
### Core Measures of Disease Frequency

| Measure | Definition | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Prevalence** | Proportion of population with disease at a point in time | (Existing cases / Total population) × multiplier | Planning healthcare resources; estimating disease burden |
| **Incidence** | Number of NEW cases in a time period | (New cases / Population at risk) × multiplier | Measuring disease risk; evaluating prevention |
| **Incidence rate** | New cases per person-time at risk | New cases / Total person-time at risk | Accounts for varying follow-up times |
| **Mortality rate** | Deaths in population per time period | (Deaths / Total population) × multiplier | Overall health indicator |
| **Case fatality rate** | Proportion of cases that die | (Deaths from disease / Total cases) × 100% | Severity/lethality of a disease |
| **Attack rate** | Incidence during a specific outbreak | (Cases in exposed / Total exposed) × 100% | Outbreak investigation |

> **Prevalence vs. Incidence:** Prevalence = snapshot (how many have it right now). Incidence = movie (how many are getting it over time). A disease can have high prevalence but low incidence if it's chronic (e.g., diabetes). A disease can have low prevalence but high incidence if it kills quickly or resolves fast.

### Measures of Association (Risk)

| Measure | What It Compares | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Relative Risk (RR)** | Incidence in exposed vs. unexposed | RR = 1 (no association); RR > 1 (increased risk); RR < 1 (protective) |
| **Odds Ratio (OR)** | Odds of exposure in cases vs. controls | Approximates RR in rare diseases; used in case-control studies |
| **Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR)** | Difference in event rates between groups | Clinically meaningful -- tells you the actual magnitude of benefit |
| **Number Needed to Treat (NNT)** | 1 / ARR | How many patients must be treated for one to benefit; lower = better |
| **Number Needed to Harm (NNH)** | 1 / Absolute Risk Increase | How many patients treated before one is harmed; higher = safer |

> **Clinical example:** If Drug A reduces MI risk from 8% to 5%, ARR = 3%, NNT = 33. You need to treat 33 patients for 3 years to prevent 1 MI. This context helps patients make informed decisions.

### Study Designs -- Hierarchy of Evidence

Listed from strongest to weakest evidence:

**1. Systematic Review / Meta-Analysis**
- Combines results from multiple studies
- Highest level of evidence when well-conducted
- Cochrane Reviews are the gold standard

**2. Randomized Controlled Trial (RCT)**
- Gold standard for testing interventions
- Random assignment minimizes bias and confounding
- Blinding (single, double, triple) further reduces bias
- Limitations: expensive, time-consuming, ethical constraints

**3. Cohort Study (Prospective or Retrospective)**
- Follows exposed vs. unexposed groups over time
- Can calculate relative risk directly
- Prospective = stronger but takes years; retrospective = faster but relies on existing records
- Example: Framingham Heart Study (prospective cohort)

**4. Case-Control Study**
- Starts with cases (have disease) and controls (don't), looks back at exposures
- Uses odds ratios; good for rare diseases
- Susceptible to recall bias
- Example: Identifying thalidomide as cause of birth defects

**5. Cross-Sectional Study**
- Snapshot of disease and exposure at one point in time
- Measures prevalence, not incidence
- Cannot establish causation or temporal sequence
- Example: NHANES (National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey)

**6. Case Report / Case Series**
- Description of individual or small group of patients
- Generates hypotheses but cannot test them
- Important for identifying new diseases or rare adverse effects

### Screening and Prevention

**Levels of Prevention:**
| Level | Goal | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary** | Prevent disease before it occurs | Immunizations, smoking cessation, seatbelts, hand hygiene, health education |
| **Secondary** | Detect disease early (asymptomatic phase) | Mammography, colonoscopy, A1C screening, blood pressure checks, PPD/TB screening |
| **Tertiary** | Reduce complications of existing disease | Cardiac rehab post-MI, diabetic foot care, stroke rehab, chronic disease management |

**Screening Test Performance:**

| Metric | Definition | Clinical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** | True positives / (True positives + False negatives) | How well the test catches disease; high sensitivity = good for ruling OUT (SnNOut) |
| **Specificity** | True negatives / (True negatives + False positives) | How well the test identifies healthy people; high specificity = good for ruling IN (SpPIn) |
| **PPV** | True positives / All positive results | Probability a positive test = true disease; affected by prevalence |
| **NPV** | True negatives / All negative results | Probability a negative test = truly healthy; affected by prevalence |

> **SnNOut / SpPIn:** A highly **Sn**sitive test, when **N**egative, helps rule **Out** disease. A highly **Sp**ecific test, when **P**ositive, helps rule **In** disease.

### Key Epidemiologic Concepts

**Chain of Infection:**
1. Infectious agent (pathogen)
2. Reservoir (where it lives -- human, animal, environmental)
3. Portal of exit (how it leaves -- respiratory, GI, blood, skin)
4. Mode of transmission (contact, droplet, airborne, vector, vehicle)
5. Portal of entry (how it enters new host)
6. Susceptible host (immunocompromised, unvaccinated, extremes of age)

Breaking any link in the chain prevents transmission. See Infection and Infectious Disease.

**Herd Immunity:**
- When enough of the population is immune (through vaccination or natural infection), transmission is disrupted, protecting those who cannot be vaccinated
- Threshold varies by pathogen: measles ~95%, polio ~80-85%, influenza ~75-80%

**Endemic vs. Epidemic vs. Pandemic:**
- **Endemic:** Constant baseline presence in a region (e.g., malaria in sub-Saharan Africa)
- **Epidemic:** Incidence exceeding expected levels in a population (e.g., Ebola outbreaks)
- **Pandemic:** Epidemic across multiple countries/continents (e.g., COVID-19, H1N1)

### Bias and Confounding

| Type | Definition | Example | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Selection bias** | Systematic error in how participants are chosen | Studying ICU patients and generalizing to all hospitalized patients | Random sampling; clear inclusion/exclusion criteria |
| **Recall bias** | Cases remember exposures differently than controls | Mothers of children with birth defects recall medication use more thoroughly | Prospective design; objective exposure data |
| **Observer bias** | Researcher's expectations influence measurement | Knowing which group is treatment affects outcome assessment | Blinding (double-blind ideal) |
| **Confounding** | A third variable creates false association | Coffee drinking appears to cause cancer, but smokers drink more coffee | Randomization; stratification; multivariate analysis |
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Applying Epidemiology at the Bedside

- **Interpreting lab results:** Sensitivity and specificity help you understand what a positive or negative troponin, D-dimer, or procalcitonin actually means for your patient
- **Risk communication:** NNT and ARR help explain treatment benefits in meaningful terms during patient education
- **Infection control:** Understanding chain of infection drives isolation precautions, hand hygiene compliance, and outbreak response
- **Evidence-based practice:** Knowing the hierarchy of evidence helps you critically appraise research and apply best practices (see Nursing Process)
- **Community health nursing:** Prevalence and incidence data guide resource allocation, program planning, and health policy

### Reportable Conditions
Nurses are legally required to report certain communicable diseases to public health authorities. Requirements vary by state, but commonly reportable conditions include:
- TB, HIV/AIDS, hepatitis A/B/C, syphilis, gonorrhea, chlamydia
- Measles, pertussis, meningococcal disease
- Foodborne outbreaks (Salmonella, E. coli, Listeria)
- Novel/emerging infections
sections.related-systems:
- Infection and Infectious Disease
- Inflammation and Immune Response
- Nursing Process

clinicalPearls: **Prevalence depends on both incidence and duration.** A cure that works fast lowers prevalence. A treatment that keeps people alive longer (but doesn't cure) actually increases prevalence. This is why prevalence alone can be misleading.

**Statistical significance ≠ clinical significance.** A massive RCT can find a "statistically significant" BP reduction of 1 mmHg. That's real but clinically meaningless. Always look at effect size, ARR, and NNT.

**PPV crashes when prevalence is low.** A 99% sensitive, 99% specific test applied to a disease with 0.1% prevalence will have a PPV of only ~9%. Most positive results will be false positives. This is why we don't screen everyone for everything.

**Correlation is not causation.** Bradford Hill criteria help evaluate causality: strength of association, consistency, specificity, temporality, biological gradient, plausibility, coherence, experiment, analogy. Temporality (exposure before outcome) is the only absolute requirement.

**Hand hygiene is the single most effective primary prevention measure in healthcare settings.** Full stop. Before and after every patient contact, before aseptic procedures, after body fluid exposure, after touching patient surroundings.

**NNT gives informed consent real meaning.** Telling a patient "this medication reduces your risk" is vague. Telling them "we'd need to treat 50 people like you for 5 years to prevent one stroke" lets them weigh the benefit against side effects.

**Outbreak investigation follows a standard sequence:** Confirm the diagnosis → define and count cases → orient by time/place/person (epi curve) → develop and test hypotheses → implement control measures → communicate findings.

fluid-and-electrolyte-balance.title: Fluid and Electrolyte Balance

sections.definition: Fluid and electrolyte balance is the physiologic regulation of water volume, distribution, and solute concentration across body compartments. Approximately 60% of adult body weight is water (varies by age, sex, and body composition). Disruptions in fluid and electrolyte balance are among the most common clinical problems encountered across all care settings and directly impact cardiac, neurological, and renal function.
sections.key-principles:
### Body Fluid Compartments

| Compartment | % Body Weight | Volume (70 kg adult) | Key Electrolytes |
|-------------|--------------|---------------------|------------------|
| **Intracellular (ICF)** | ~40% | ~28 L | K+, Mg2+, PO4 3- |
| **Extracellular (ECF)** | ~20% | ~14 L | Na+, Cl-, HCO3- |
| - Interstitial | ~15% | ~10.5 L | Similar to plasma minus proteins |
| - Intravascular (plasma) | ~5% | ~3.5 L | Na+, Cl-, albumin |
| - Transcellular | ~1-2% | ~1 L | CSF, synovial, pleural, peritoneal |

### Fluid Movement Mechanisms
- **Osmosis**: Water moves from low to high solute concentration across semipermeable membranes
- **Diffusion**: Solutes move from high to low concentration
- **Filtration**: Fluid moves due to hydrostatic pressure gradient (Starling forces)
- **Active transport**: Energy-dependent movement against concentration gradient (Na+/K+ ATPase pump)

### Osmolality
- **Serum osmolality**: 275 - 295 mOsm/kg
- Calculated: **2(Na+) + (BUN/2.8) + (Glucose/18)**
- Effective osmolality (tonicity) primarily determined by Na+
- **Osmol gap** = measured - calculated osmolality (normal < 10); elevated in toxic alcohol ingestion

### IV Fluid Types

| Fluid | Tonicity | Osmolality | Key Use |
|-------|----------|-----------|---------|
| **0.9% NaCl (NS)** | Isotonic | 308 | Volume resuscitation, blood product compatibility |
| **Lactated Ringer's (LR)** | Isotonic | 273 | Volume resuscitation, surgical patients |
| **D5W** | Isotonic in bag, hypotonic in body | 252 | Free water replacement, medication dilution |
| **0.45% NaCl (1/2 NS)** | Hypotonic | 154 | Free water deficit, maintenance with D5 |
| **3% NaCl** | Hypertonic | 1026 | Severe/symptomatic hyponatremia (ICU only) |
| **D5 0.45% NaCl** | Hypertonic in bag, isotonic in body | 406 | Maintenance fluid |
| **Albumin 5%** | Isotonic colloid | 290 | Plasma volume expansion |

**Key Rule**: Isotonic fluids stay in ECF. Hypotonic fluids shift into cells. Hypertonic fluids pull water out of cells.

### Electrolyte Reference Ranges and Imbalances

#### Sodium (Na+): 136 - 145 mEq/L
*Primary ECF cation; determines serum osmolality and fluid distribution*

| | Hyponatremia (< 136) | Hypernatremia (> 145) |
|--|----------------------|----------------------|
| **Causes** | SIADH, water intoxication, heart failure, cirrhosis, diuretics, adrenal insufficiency | Dehydration, diabetes insipidus, excess Na+ intake, osmotic diuresis |
| **Symptoms** | Headache, confusion, nausea, lethargy, seizures, cerebral edema | Thirst, restlessness, irritability, confusion, seizures, coma |
| **Nursing** | Fluid restriction, monitor for seizures; correct slowly (< 8-10 mEq/L per 24h to avoid osmotic demyelination) | Free water replacement; correct slowly (< 10-12 mEq/L per 24h to avoid cerebral edema) |

#### Potassium (K+): 3.5 - 5.0 mEq/L
*Primary ICF cation; critical for cardiac and neuromuscular function*

| | Hypokalemia (< 3.5) | Hyperkalemia (> 5.0) |
|--|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Causes** | Diuretics (loop/thiazide), vomiting, diarrhea, alkalosis, insulin, refeeding | Renal failure, K-sparing diuretics, acidosis, tissue destruction (crush, burns), ACE inhibitors |
| **ECG Changes** | Flattened T waves, U waves, ST depression, prolonged QT | Peaked T waves -> widened QRS -> sine wave -> V-fib/asystole |
| **Symptoms** | Muscle weakness, cramps, ileus, dysrhythmias, respiratory muscle weakness | Muscle weakness, paresthesias, bradycardia, cardiac arrest |
| **Nursing** | Oral/IV replacement (max 10 mEq/hr peripheral, 20 mEq/hr central); never push IV K+ | Calcium gluconate (cardiac membrane stabilizer), insulin + D50, kayexalate, dialysis |

**Critical**: K+ < 3.0 or > 6.0 mEq/L is a medical emergency. Always check K+ before giving digoxin.

#### Calcium (Ca2+): Total 8.5 - 10.5 mg/dL; Ionized 4.5 - 5.5 mg/dL
*Bone structure, muscle contraction, coagulation, neuromuscular excitability*

| | Hypocalcemia (< 8.5) | Hypercalcemia (> 10.5) |
|--|---------------------|----------------------|
| **Causes** | Hypoparathyroidism, vitamin D deficiency, pancreatitis, renal failure, alkalosis, massive transfusion | Hyperparathyroidism, malignancy, immobility, excess vitamin D, thiazide diuretics |
| **Symptoms** | Tetany, Chvostek sign, Trousseau sign, seizures, prolonged QT, laryngospasm | "Stones, bones, groans, moans" -- renal calculi, bone pain, constipation/N/V, confusion/lethargy, shortened QT |
| **Nursing** | IV calcium gluconate (peripheral safe) or calcium chloride (central line); seizure precautions | IV NS hydration, loop diuretics, bisphosphonates, calcitonin; fall precautions |

**Remember**: Always correct for albumin: Corrected Ca2+ = measured Ca2+ + 0.8(4.0 - albumin)

#### Magnesium (Mg2+): 1.5 - 2.5 mEq/L
*Cofactor for 300+ enzyme systems; neuromuscular and cardiac function*

| | Hypomagnesemia (< 1.5) | Hypermagnesemia (> 2.5) |
|--|------------------------|------------------------|
| **Causes** | Alcoholism, malnutrition, diuretics, diarrhea, proton pump inhibitors | Renal failure, excess Mg2+ administration (eclampsia treatment) |
| **Symptoms** | Tremors, hyperreflexia, tetany, dysrhythmias (torsades), seizures | Hyporeflexia, lethargy, bradycardia, hypotension, respiratory depression |
| **Nursing** | IV Mg2+ sulfate (monitor for hypotension); correct before K+ (refractory hypokalemia often due to low Mg2+) | Stop Mg2+ infusion, IV calcium gluconate, loop diuretics, dialysis |

#### Phosphorus (PO4 3-): 2.5 - 4.5 mg/dL
*ATP production, bone structure; inverse relationship with calcium*

| | Hypophosphatemia (< 2.5) | Hyperphosphatemia (> 4.5) |
|--|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Causes** | Refeeding syndrome, alcoholism, DKA treatment, antacids | Renal failure, tumor lysis syndrome, hypoparathyroidism |
| **Symptoms** | Muscle weakness, respiratory failure, rhabdomyolysis, hemolytic anemia, confusion | Usually asymptomatic; symptoms related to secondary hypocalcemia (tetany) |
| **Nursing** | Oral/IV phosphate replacement; monitor respiratory status | Phosphate binders with meals (calcium carbonate, sevelamer); dietary restriction |

### Fluid Volume Imbalances

| | Fluid Volume Deficit (Dehydration/Hypovolemia) | Fluid Volume Excess (Hypervolemia) |
|--|-----------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------|
| **Causes** | Hemorrhage, vomiting, diarrhea, burns, diuresis, third-spacing | Heart failure, renal failure, excess IV fluids, cirrhosis |
| **Assessment** | Tachycardia, hypotension, orthostatic changes, poor skin turgor, dry mucous membranes, decreased UO, elevated BUN/Cr ratio, concentrated urine | Edema (peripheral, pulmonary), weight gain, JVD, crackles, dyspnea, bounding pulse, S3 heart sound |
| **Labs** | Elevated Hct, elevated BUN, elevated serum osmolality, elevated urine specific gravity | Decreased Hct (dilutional), decreased serum osmolality, BNP elevation |
| **Management** | Isotonic fluid resuscitation (NS or LR), treat underlying cause | Fluid/Na+ restriction, diuretics, I&O monitoring, daily weights |

### Key Formulas
- **Total body water (TBW)** = 0.6 x body weight (kg) in males; 0.5 x in females
- **Free water deficit** = TBW x [(serum Na+/140) - 1]
- **Fluid balance** = Intake - Output (target even or per order)
- **1 L of water weighs 1 kg** -- daily weights are the most accurate measure of fluid status
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Nursing Assessment Priorities
- **Daily weights** (same time, same scale, same clothing) -- most reliable indicator of fluid status
- **Strict I&O** -- include all oral, IV, tube feedings, and output (urine, drains, emesis, stool)
- **Vital sign trends** -- orthostatic vitals for hypovolemia assessment
- **Skin/mucous membrane assessment** -- turgor, moisture, edema grading (1+ to 4+)
- **Neurovascular checks** for electrolyte imbalances
- **Cardiac monitoring** for K+, Ca2+, and Mg2+ abnormalities
- **Lung sounds** -- crackles suggest fluid overload
- **Urine specific gravity**: 1.005-1.030 (concentrated = dehydration; dilute = overhydration)

### Critical Lab Monitoring
- **BMP/CMP** for electrolytes at minimum q12-24h in acute settings
- **Ionized calcium** is more accurate than total calcium (not affected by albumin)
- **BUN:Cr ratio** > 20:1 suggests prerenal azotemia/dehydration
- **Lactate** as marker of perfusion adequacy
sections.related-systems:
- Renal System
- Cardiovascular System
- Endocrine System
- Acid-Base Balance
sections.related-conditions:
- Heart Failure
- Acute Kidney Injury
- Chronic Kidney Disease
- Diabetic Ketoacidosis

- Diabetes Insipidus

clinicalPearls: "A liter a day keeps the surgeon at bay" -- but always assess before blindly replacing fluid
Hypokalemia that is refractory to K+ replacement often has underlying hypomagnesemia -- always check and correct Mg2+ first
Rapid correction of hyponatremia risks osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) -- correct no faster than 8-10 mEq/L per 24 hours
In hyperkalemia, calcium does NOT lower K+ -- it stabilizes the cardiac membrane while you use other methods to shift/remove K+
Third-spacing (ascites, pleural effusion, edema) means the patient can be intravascularly depleted despite total body fluid excess
Always assess renal function before giving Mg2+ or K+ supplementation -- accumulation risk with renal impairment
D5W is isotonic in the bag but becomes hypotonic once infused (glucose is metabolized, leaving free water)
*Add from daily practice*

genetics-and-protein-expression.title: Genetics and Protein Expression

sections.the-central-dogma-dna-rna-protein:
### Step 1: DNA — The Master Blueprint

DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) stores all genetic instructions in a **double helix** made of four nucleotide bases:

| Base | Pairs With |
|------|-----------|
| **Adenine (A)** | **Thymine (T)** |
| **Guanine (G)** | **Cytosine (C)** |

A **gene** = a specific segment of DNA that codes for one protein (or functional RNA). Humans have ~20,000-25,000 genes distributed across 23 pairs of chromosomes.

### Step 2: Transcription — DNA → mRNA

In the cell nucleus, DNA is "read" and copied into messenger RNA (mRNA):
1. RNA polymerase binds to the gene's promoter region
2. DNA double helix unwinds
3. One strand is copied into a complementary mRNA sequence (A→U, T→A, G→C, C→G — note: RNA uses **uracil** instead of thymine)
4. mRNA is processed (introns removed, exons spliced together, poly-A tail added)
5. Mature mRNA exits the nucleus

### Step 3: Translation — mRNA → Protein

At the ribosome (in the cytoplasm), mRNA is "read" and translated into a protein:
1. Ribosome reads mRNA in three-base units called **codons**
2. Each codon specifies one amino acid (e.g., AUG = methionine/start)
3. Transfer RNA (tRNA) brings the correct amino acid to the ribosome
4. Amino acids are linked into a polypeptide chain
5. The chain folds into its 3D shape → functional protein

> **Why this matters:** Every enzyme discussed in Enzymes and Biological Catalysis, every receptor in Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology, every hemoglobin molecule carrying oxygen, every antibody fighting infection — all are proteins built through this process. A single nucleotide change in the DNA can alter the protein → disease.

---
sections.when-the-blueprint-goes-wrong-types-of-m:
### Point Mutations (Single Nucleotide Changes)

| Type | What Happens | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Missense** | One amino acid changed to another | **Sickle cell disease:** GAG → GTG in beta-globin gene → glutamic acid → valine → HbS → sickling |
| **Nonsense** | Codon changed to a STOP codon → truncated protein | Some cystic fibrosis mutations → truncated CFTR protein |
| **Silent** | Nucleotide changes but amino acid stays the same (redundancy in genetic code) | No clinical effect |

### Larger Mutations

| Type | What Happens | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Deletion** | Nucleotides removed → frame shift or protein loss | Duchenne muscular dystrophy (dystrophin gene deletion) |
| **Insertion** | Extra nucleotides added → frame shift | Some hemophilia mutations |
| **Trinucleotide repeat expansion** | Repeated sequences grow longer across generations | Huntington's disease (CAG repeats) |
| **Chromosomal** | Entire chromosome segments duplicated, deleted, or translocated | Down syndrome (trisomy 21), Philadelphia chromosome (CML) |

---
sections.inheritance-patterns-how-mutations-are-p:
| Pattern | Mechanism | Examples | Clinical Clue |
|---------|-----------|---------|---------------|
| **Autosomal dominant** | ONE copy of mutant gene = disease. 50% chance each child affected | Huntington's, Marfan syndrome, BRCA1/2, achondroplasia | Affected individuals in every generation; M=F |
| **Autosomal recessive** | TWO copies needed for disease. Carriers (one copy) are unaffected | Sickle Cell Disease, cystic fibrosis, PKU, some hemophilias | May skip generations; carriers prevalent in specific populations |
| **X-linked recessive** | Gene on X chromosome. Males (XY) affected with one copy; females are carriers | Hemophilia A&B, G6PD deficiency, Duchenne MD | Males predominantly affected; no male-to-male transmission |
| **Mitochondrial** | Mitochondrial DNA (inherited from mother only) | MELAS, Leber hereditary optic neuropathy | Maternal inheritance — affected mother passes to ALL children |

---
sections.pharmacogenomics-why-patients-respond-di:
**Pharmacogenomics** = how genetic variation affects drug metabolism, efficacy, and toxicity. This is the clinical frontier where genetics directly impacts your ED practice.

### CYP450 Polymorphisms

CYP enzymes (see Enzymes and Biological Catalysis) have genetic variants that determine how fast a patient metabolizes drugs:

| Metabolizer Type | Enzyme Activity | Drug Effect | Clinical Impact |
|-----------------|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Poor metabolizer (PM)** | Little or no enzyme activity | Pro-drugs DON'T work; active drugs ACCUMULATE | Codeine → no morphine conversion → no pain relief. Standard doses of active drugs → toxicity |
| **Intermediate metabolizer (IM)** | Reduced activity | Variable — between PM and normal | May need dose adjustments |
| **Normal/Extensive metabolizer (EM)** | Normal activity | Expected drug response | Standard dosing |
| **Ultra-rapid metabolizer (UM)** | Excessive activity | Pro-drugs converted TOO fast → toxicity; active drugs cleared TOO fast → subtherapeutic | Codeine → rapid morphine surge → respiratory depression (deaths in children). Standard doses of active drugs → treatment failure |

### High-Impact Pharmacogenomic Examples

| Gene | Drug Affected | Clinical Impact |
|------|--------------|----------------|
| **CYP2D6** | Codeine, tramadol | PM: no analgesic effect. UM: dangerous morphine levels (FDA Black Box for children) |
| **CYP2C19** | Clopidogrel (Plavix) | PM: can't activate clopidogrel → stent thrombosis. Use prasugrel or ticagrelor instead |
| **CYP2C9 + VKORC1** | Warfarin | Variants → 10-fold dosing variation. FDA recommends genotyping |
| **HLA-B*5701** | Abacavir (HIV drug) | Positive → life-threatening hypersensitivity reaction. MUST test before prescribing |
| **HLA-B*1502** | Carbamazepine | Positive (common in Southeast Asian populations) → Stevens-Johnson syndrome/TEN. MUST test |
| **TPMT/NUDT15** | Azathioprine, 6-mercaptopurine | PM: severe myelosuppression → life-threatening neutropenia |
| **G6PD** | Sulfonamides, nitrofurantoin, rasburicase, dapsone | Deficiency → oxidative hemolysis. Test before prescribing these drugs |

> **In the ED, the most relevant pharmacogenomics are CYP2D6 (codeine/tramadol) and G6PD (before rasburicase for Tumor Lysis Syndrome).** Formal pharmacogenomic testing is increasingly done in specialty clinics, but awareness of these variations helps you understand why "the usual dose" doesn't work for every patient.

---
sections.genetic-diseases-youll-see-in-the-ed:
### Sickle Cell Disease — The Textbook Single-Gene Disease

One nucleotide change (GAG → GTG) in the beta-globin gene → one amino acid change (glutamic acid → valine) → hemoglobin S → RBC sickling under low O2 conditions → vaso-occlusive crises, hemolytic anemia, organ damage.

**Why understanding the genetics matters clinically:** See Sickle Cell Disease for full details, but the genetics explain:
- **Sickle cell TRAIT (HbAS)** = one normal copy + one sickle copy → usually asymptomatic (carrier state). Protective against malaria → explains high prevalence in African populations
- **Sickle cell DISEASE (HbSS)** = two sickle copies → symptomatic disease
- **HbSC disease** = one sickle + one hemoglobin C → milder disease than HbSS

### Other Genetic Conditions in the ED

| Condition | Genetic Basis | ED Relevance |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Cystic fibrosis** | CFTR gene mutations (autosomal recessive) | Respiratory exacerbations, pneumonia, electrolyte abnormalities |
| **Hemophilia A** | Factor VIII gene (X-linked recessive) | Bleeding emergencies, head trauma requires immediate factor replacement |
| **G6PD deficiency** | G6PD gene (X-linked recessive) | Hemolytic anemia triggered by drugs, infection, fava beans |
| **Marfan syndrome** | FBN1 gene (autosomal dominant) | Aortic dissection, aortic root dilation — think of it in tall, young patients with chest pain |
| **Long QT syndrome** | Multiple genes (KCNQ1, KCNH2, SCN5A) | Syncope, Torsades de Pointes, sudden cardiac death in young patients |
| **Malignant hyperthermia** | RYR1 gene (autosomal dominant) | Triggered by succinylcholine/volatile anesthetics → hyperthermia + rigidity → dantrolene |

---
sections.biologics-drugs-made-from-genetic-engine:
**Biologics** are therapeutic proteins manufactured using recombinant DNA technology — living cells are genetically engineered to produce the desired protein.

| Type | What They Are | Examples | Clinical Use |
|------|-------------|---------|-------------|
| **Monoclonal antibodies** | Lab-made antibodies targeting specific proteins | Trastuzumab (HER2), infliximab (TNF-α), rituximab (CD20) | Cancer, autoimmune disease |
| **Recombinant proteins** | Human proteins produced by genetically engineered cells | Insulin (Humalog, Lantus), EPO (epoetin alfa), tPA (alteplase) | Diabetes, anemia, stroke/STEMI |
| **Fusion proteins** | Two proteins combined into one molecule | Etanercept (TNF receptor + IgG Fc) | Autoimmune disease |

**Naming conventions for monoclonal antibodies:**

| Suffix | Origin | Example |
|--------|--------|---------|
| **-omab** | Mouse | Muromonab |
| **-ximab** | Chimeric (mouse/human) | Infliximab, rituximab |
| **-zumab** | Humanized | Trastuzumab, bevacizumab |
| **-umab** | Fully human | Adalimumab, pembrolizumab |

> **Modern insulin is a biologic.** Before recombinant DNA technology, insulin was extracted from pig and cow pancreases. Now, human insulin genes are inserted into bacteria or yeast → they produce human insulin → purified and used therapeutically. This is why modern insulin causes fewer allergic reactions.

---
sections.gene-therapy-and-the-future:
Gene therapy aims to fix genetic diseases by delivering correct genes or editing faulty ones:

| Approach | How It Works | Status |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Gene replacement** | Deliver a working copy of the gene via viral vector | FDA-approved for SMA (onasemnogene), inherited retinal dystrophy (voretigene) |
| **CRISPR gene editing** | Directly cut and repair faulty DNA sequences | First CRISPR therapy approved 2023: casgevy for sickle cell disease and beta-thalassemia |
| **mRNA therapy** | Deliver mRNA instructions for the body to make a therapeutic protein | COVID-19 vaccines (Pfizer, Moderna) — proof of concept for the platform |
| **Antisense oligonucleotides** | Block mRNA from being translated into harmful protein | Nusinersen for SMA, eteplirsen for Duchenne MD |

---
sections.connections-to-other-first-principles:
- **Enzymes and Biological Catalysis** — Enzymes ARE proteins built from genetic instructions. Enzyme deficiencies (PKU, G6PD, Gaucher) are genetic diseases.
- **Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology** — Receptors are proteins. Receptor polymorphisms → variable drug response (pharmacogenomics).
- **Cell Proliferation and Death** — Cell cycle regulators (p53, Rb, BRCA) are proteins encoded by genes. Mutations in these → cancer.
- **Homeostasis and Feedback Loops** — Hormones, enzymes, and receptors that maintain homeostasis are all gene products. Genetic diseases disrupt specific homeostatic mechanisms.
- **Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics** — Drug metabolism depends on CYP450 enzymes, which are genetically variable. Pharmacogenomics bridges genetics and PK/PD.

---
sections.references:
- Alberts et al: Molecular Biology of the Cell — Gene Expression chapters
- Katzung: Basic & Clinical Pharmacology — Pharmacogenomics
- CPIC Guidelines (Clinical Pharmacogenetics Implementation Consortium)
- Robbins & Cotran: Pathologic Basis of Disease — Genetic Diseases

---

*See also: Enzymes and Biological Catalysis · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Proliferation and Death · Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics · Sickle Cell Disease · Hemophilia · Immunobiologics and Biologics*

clinicalPearls: **Sickle cell disease is ONE amino acid change.** Glutamic acid → valine in position 6 of beta-globin. One nucleotide, one amino acid, one devastating disease. This is the power (and vulnerability) of the genetic code.

**Codeine is a PRODRUG — it does nothing until CYP2D6 converts it to morphine.** A CYP2D6 poor metabolizer gets zero pain relief from codeine. An ultra-rapid metabolizer gets a potentially fatal morphine surge. This is why codeine is increasingly avoided, especially in children (FDA Black Box).

**Ask about family history of anesthesia reactions.** Malignant hyperthermia is autosomal dominant. If a patient reports a family member who "had a bad reaction to anesthesia" or "developed a high fever during surgery," this could be MH susceptibility → avoid succinylcholine and volatile anesthetics.

**Biologics end in -mab (monoclonal antibody).** If you see a drug name ending in -mab, it's a targeted protein therapy. Knowing the target helps you predict side effects: anti-TNF → immunosuppression → infection risk; anti-VEGF → bleeding/hypertension.

**The future of medicine is personalized.** Pharmacogenomic testing is becoming standard in some settings (clopidogrel before PCI, HLA testing before carbamazepine or abacavir). We're moving from "one size fits all" dosing to genetically tailored therapy.

**G6PD testing should be reflexive before rasburicase.** G6PD deficiency is the world's most common enzyme deficiency (~400 million people). Rasburicase (for [[Tumor Lysis Syndrome]]) generates hydrogen peroxide as a byproduct → causes massive hemolysis in G6PD-deficient patients. Always check.

health-assessment-foundations.title: Health Assessment Foundations

sections.definition: Health assessment is the systematic collection, analysis, and interpretation of patient data to identify health status, risks, and needs. It is the foundation of the Nursing Process and drives all clinical decision-making. A thorough assessment combines subjective data (what the patient reports) with objective data (what you observe, measure, and examine) to create a complete clinical picture. Assessment is not a one-time event -- it is continuous, iterative, and adapts to the patient's changing condition.
sections.key-principles:
### Types of Assessment

**Comprehensive (Head-to-Toe)**
- Complete systematic examination of all body systems
- Performed on admission, initial encounter, or annual wellness visits
- Establishes baseline for comparison during subsequent assessments
- Typically follows a cephalocaudal (head-to-toe) and proximal-to-distal approach

**Focused Assessment**
- Targeted examination of a specific body system or complaint
- Performed when a patient presents with a specific symptom or concern
- Used for follow-up on known conditions or post-intervention evaluation
- Example: lung auscultation after bronchodilator administration

**Rapid/Emergency Assessment**
- ABCDE approach: Airway, Breathing, Circulation, Disability, Exposure
- Primary survey in trauma or acute decompensation
- Goal: identify and intervene on life-threatening conditions immediately
- Secondary survey follows once patient is stabilized

**Shift Assessment**
- Abbreviated systematic check at beginning of shift and as needed
- Focuses on relevant systems based on diagnosis and plan of care
- Compares current status to previous shift's findings -- trending is key

### The Four Techniques of Physical Examination

Performed in this order for all systems **except** the abdomen:

| Technique | What It Involves | Key Points |
|---|---|---|
| **Inspection** | Visual observation | Always performed first; compare bilateral structures; note color, size, shape, symmetry, movement |
| **Palpation** | Touch to assess texture, temperature, moisture, vibration, masses | Light palpation before deep; use dorsal hand for temperature; watch the patient's face for pain response |
| **Percussion** | Tapping to produce sound; evaluates underlying tissue density | Resonance (normal lung), dullness (solid organ/fluid), tympany (air-filled), hyperresonance (pneumothorax, emphysema) |
| **Auscultation** | Listening with stethoscope | Diaphragm for high-pitched sounds (breath sounds, S1/S2, bowel sounds); bell for low-pitched (S3, S4, bruits, murmurs) |

> **Abdomen exception:** Inspect → Auscultate → Percuss → Palpate (palpation/percussion can alter bowel sounds)

### Subjective Data Collection (Health History)

**OLDCARTS** mnemonic for symptom analysis:
- **O**nset: When did it start? Sudden or gradual?
- **L**ocation: Where exactly? Does it radiate?
- **D**uration: How long does it last? Constant or intermittent?
- **C**haracteristics: What does it feel/look like? Sharp, dull, burning, pressure?
- **A**ggravating factors: What makes it worse?
- **R**elieving factors: What makes it better? What have you tried?
- **T**reatment: What medications or interventions have been used?
- **S**everity: Rate on 0-10 scale

**Key History Components:**
- Chief complaint (in patient's own words)
- History of present illness (HPI)
- Past medical/surgical history
- Medications (including OTC, supplements, herbals)
- Allergies (with reaction type -- true allergy vs. intolerance)
- Family history (especially first-degree relatives)
- Social history: tobacco, alcohol, drugs, occupation, living situation, support system
- Review of systems (ROS): systematic questioning by body system

### Vital Signs -- The "Fifth Vital Sign" Era

| Vital Sign           | Normal Adult Range         | Key Considerations                                                                                                               |
| -------------------- | -------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
| **Temperature**      | 36.1-38.0°C (97.0-100.4°F) | Route matters: rectal most accurate; oral most common; temporal/tympanic for screening                                           |
| **Heart Rate**       | 60-100 bpm                 | Note rhythm regularity; bradycardia/tachycardia thresholds depend on patient's baseline                                          |
| **Respiratory Rate** | 12-20 breaths/min          | Count for full 60 seconds; most commonly falsified vital sign -- actually watch the chest                                        |
| **Blood Pressure**   | <120/80 mmHg (normal)      | Correct cuff size critical (80% bladder length of arm circumference); orthostatic measurements if indicated                      |
| **SpO2**             | 95-100%                    | Unreliable with poor perfusion, nail polish, CO poisoning, severe anemia; does not reflect ventilation (need capnography or ABG) |
| **Pain**             | 0-10 scale                 | "Fifth vital sign"; use appropriate scale for population (numeric, Wong-Baker FACES, FLACC, CPOT for intubated)                  |

### Documentation Frameworks

**SBAR** (communication/handoff):
- **S**ituation: What is happening right now?
- **B**ackground: What is the relevant clinical context?
- **A**ssessment: What do I think the problem is?
- **R**ecommendation: What do I think should be done?

**DAR** (focus charting):
- **D**ata: Subjective and objective findings
- **A**ction: Nursing interventions performed
- **R**esponse: Patient's response to interventions
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Normal vs. Abnormal Findings -- Common Red Flags

| System | Normal | Abnormal/Concerning |
|---|---|---|
| **Neuro** | Alert, oriented x4, PERRLA, equal grip/push strength | Asymmetric findings, new confusion, pupil changes, pronator drift |
| **Cardiac** | S1/S2 regular rate and rhythm, no murmurs | New murmur, S3 (volume overload), S4 (stiff ventricle), JVD, peripheral edema |
| **Respiratory** | Clear bilateral breath sounds, symmetric chest expansion | Crackles (fluid), wheezes (bronchoconstriction), absent sounds (pneumothorax), accessory muscle use |
| **Abdominal** | Soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds x4 quadrants | Rigid/guarding, rebound tenderness, absent bowel sounds (ileus), hyperactive (obstruction) |
| **Skin** | Warm, dry, intact, appropriate color | Cyanosis, jaundice, mottling, tenting (dehydration), new wounds/breakdown, petechiae, diaphoresis |
| **Vascular** | Palpable peripheral pulses, CRT <3 sec, warm extremities | Absent/diminished pulses, cool extremities, unilateral edema (DVT), Homans' sign unreliable |

### Focused Assessment Scenarios

**Chest pain:** Cardiac monitor, 12-lead ECG, assess location/quality/radiation, troponin, vital signs, lung sounds (crackles = fluid backup), Hemodynamics assessment if central line present

**Shortness of breath:** SpO2, respiratory rate/pattern/effort, lung sounds, accessory muscle use, position preference, ABG if severe (see Acid-Base Balance, Oxygenation and Ventilation)

**Altered mental status:** Blood glucose FIRST (most reversible cause), neuro exam, pupil check, GCS or NIH Stroke Scale, temp (rule out sepsis/infection), medication review, see Shock for perfusion-related causes

**Post-operative:** Airway patency, level of consciousness, surgical site (bleeding, drainage, integrity), pain level, vital signs q15min → q30min → q1hr, I&O, neurovascular checks if extremity surgery

clinicalPearls: **Trending is everything.** A single set of vitals tells you very little -- the trajectory tells you if the patient is getting better or worse. A BP of 100/60 may be perfectly normal for one patient and an emergency for another.

**Assess the patient, not the monitor.** Alarms and numbers provide data, but look at the patient first. A "normal" SpO2 on a patient who is diaphoretic, anxious, and using accessory muscles demands intervention.
**RR is the most sensitive early warning sign** for clinical deterioration. Tachypnea often precedes changes in HR, BP, and SpO2 by hours. Count it properly for a full 60 seconds.
**Always check blood glucose for altered mental status.** It's fast, cheap, and hypoglycemia is immediately reversible. Don't chase zebras before ruling out the basics.
**Bilateral comparison is your best friend.** Asymmetry in breath sounds, pulses, pupil size, grip strength, or limb circumference is almost always significant.
**Pain assessment requires the right tool.** Numeric scales don't work for cognitively impaired, pediatric, or intubated patients. Know FLACC (pediatric), CPOT (intubated), and PAINAD (dementia).
**If the patient says something is wrong, believe them.** Even when vitals look normal, patient intuition about their own body is a valid clinical finding. Document it and investigate.
**Skin is the largest organ and the most visible.** Color changes (pallor, cyanosis, jaundice, mottling), temperature, moisture, and turgor give you rapid systemic information without any equipment.

hemodynamics.title: Hemodynamics

sections.definition: Hemodynamics is the study of blood flow and the forces involved in circulating blood through the cardiovascular system. Understanding hemodynamic principles is essential for recognizing and managing cardiovascular compromise, guiding fluid resuscitation, titrating vasoactive medications, and optimizing tissue perfusion. These principles underpin the assessment and management of every critically ill patient.
sections.key-principles:
### Fundamental Equation
```
Blood Pressure = Cardiac Output x Systemic Vascular Resistance
        (BP)           (CO)              (SVR)
```
This is the hemodynamic equivalent of Ohm's Law (V = I x R). All hemodynamic management targets one or both of these variables.

### Cardiac Output (CO)
- **Definition**: Volume of blood ejected by the heart per minute
- **Normal**: 4 - 8 L/min
- **CO = Heart Rate (HR) x Stroke Volume (SV)**
- **Cardiac Index (CI)** = CO / Body Surface Area (BSA); normal **2.5 - 4.0 L/min/m2**

### Determinants of Stroke Volume

| Factor | Definition | Influences | Clinical Correlates |
|--------|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| **Preload** | Volume/stretch of ventricle at end-diastole | Venous return, blood volume, venous tone | CVP, PAWP, volume status |
| **Afterload** | Resistance the ventricle must overcome to eject blood | SVR (LV), PVR (RV), aortic stenosis | SVR, MAP, vascular tone |
| **Contractility** | Intrinsic force of myocardial contraction (independent of preload/afterload) | Inotropes, ischemia, acidosis, electrolytes | Ejection fraction, SV |

### Frank-Starling Law
- As preload (ventricular end-diastolic volume) increases, stroke volume increases -- up to a point
- Beyond the optimal stretch point, further increases in preload cause a **decline** in stroke volume (the descending limb of the Starling curve)
- In heart failure, the curve is flattened and shifted right -- higher filling pressures produce less output
- Clinical application: Volume resuscitation improves CO only when the patient is on the ascending limb of the curve ("fluid responsive")

### Key Hemodynamic Parameters

| Parameter | Normal Range | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------|-------------|
| **Heart Rate (HR)** | 60 - 100 bpm | ECG monitor, pulse |
| **Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)** | 65 - 100 mmHg | MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP) or (SBP + 2DBP)/3 |
| **Cardiac Output (CO)** | 4 - 8 L/min | Thermodilution, echocardiography |
| **Cardiac Index (CI)** | 2.5 - 4.0 L/min/m2 | CO / BSA |
| **Stroke Volume (SV)** | 60 - 100 mL/beat | CO / HR |
| **Stroke Volume Index (SVI)** | 33 - 47 mL/beat/m2 | SV / BSA |
| **SVR** | 800 - 1200 dynes/sec/cm-5 | 80 x (MAP - CVP) / CO |
| **PVR** | 100 - 250 dynes/sec/cm-5 | 80 x (MPAP - PAWP) / CO |
| **CVP/RAP** | 2 - 6 mmHg | Central venous catheter |
| **PAWP (Wedge)** | 8 - 12 mmHg | Pulmonary artery catheter |
| **PA Pressure** | 15-25 / 8-15 mmHg | Pulmonary artery catheter |
| **Ejection Fraction (EF)** | 55 - 70% | Echocardiography |
| **SvO2 / ScvO2** | 60-80% / 65-80% | PA catheter / central venous |

### Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)
- **MAP = DBP + 1/3(SBP - DBP)** or equivalently **(SBP + 2DBP) / 3**
- MAP >= 65 mmHg is the minimum target for adequate organ perfusion
- MAP is the driving pressure for tissue perfusion and is more clinically meaningful than SBP alone
- Cerebral, renal, and coronary perfusion are MAP-dependent

### Systemic Vascular Resistance (SVR)
- Reflects arteriolar tone (afterload)
- **SVR = 80 x (MAP - CVP) / CO**
- Elevated SVR: vasoconstriction (cold shock, cardiogenic shock, hypothermia)
- Low SVR: vasodilation (warm septic shock, anaphylaxis, neurogenic shock)

### Hemodynamic Monitoring Methods

| Method | Invasiveness | What It Measures |
|--------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Arterial line** | Invasive | Continuous BP, MAP, waveform analysis |
| **CVP (central line)** | Invasive | Right atrial pressure (preload estimate) |
| **Pulmonary artery catheter (Swan-Ganz)** | Invasive | CO, CI, SVR, PVR, PAWP, SvO2 |
| **Echocardiography** | Non-invasive/minimally | EF, wall motion, valvular function, volume status |
| **NICOM/FloTrac** | Minimally invasive | CO, SV, SVV via pulse contour analysis |
| **Passive leg raise (PLR)** | Non-invasive | Fluid responsiveness (auto-transfusion of ~300 mL) |

### Hemodynamic Profiles by Shock Type

| Shock Type | CO/CI | SVR | CVP/PAWP | SvO2 |
|-----------|-------|-----|----------|------|
| **Hypovolemic** | Decreased | Increased | Decreased | Decreased |
| **Cardiogenic** | Decreased | Increased | Increased | Decreased |
| **Septic (warm)** | Increased early; Decreased late | Decreased | Variable | Increased early; Decreased late |
| **Anaphylactic** | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased |
| **Neurogenic** | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased | Decreased |
| **Obstructive** | Decreased | Increased | Increased (R-sided) | Decreased |

### Vasoactive Medications and Hemodynamic Effects

| Agent | Primary Receptors | HR | Contractility | SVR | Key Use |
|-------|------------------|-----|--------------|-----|---------|
| **Norepinephrine** | Alpha1 > Beta1 | +/- | + | +++ | First-line vasopressor in septic shock |
| **Epinephrine** | Beta1, Beta2, Alpha1 | ++ | ++ | ++ | Anaphylaxis, cardiac arrest, refractory shock |
| **Vasopressin** | V1 receptors | 0 | 0 | ++ | Adjunct in septic shock (catecholamine-sparing) |
| **Phenylephrine** | Pure Alpha1 | - (reflex) | 0 | +++ | Pure vasodilation without tachycardia |
| **Dopamine** | Dose-dependent (D, Beta, Alpha) | ++ | ++ | + to ++ | Less favored; higher dysrhythmia risk |
| **Dobutamine** | Beta1 > Beta2 | + | +++ | - (decreases) | Cardiogenic shock, low CO states |
| **Milrinone** | PDE3 inhibitor | + | ++ | -- (decreases) | Acute decompensated HF, pulm HTN |
| **Nitroprusside** | Direct vasodilator | 0 | 0 | --- | Hypertensive emergency (afterload reduction) |
| **Nitroglycerin** | Venodilator > arterial | 0 | 0 | - | Preload reduction, acute coronary syndrome |
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Nursing Assessment Priorities
- **Continuous hemodynamic monitoring** in critical care: arterial waveform, ECG, SpO2
- **Trend analysis** is more valuable than single values
- **Assess perfusion**: level of consciousness, urine output (>0.5 mL/kg/hr), capillary refill (<3 sec), skin color/temperature, lactate
- **Zero and level transducers** at the phlebostatic axis (4th ICS, mid-axillary line) q shift and with position changes
- **Square wave test** to verify arterial line damping (overdamped = falsely low systolic; underdamped = falsely high systolic)

### Fluid Responsiveness Assessment
- **Static measures** (CVP, PAWP) are poor predictors of fluid responsiveness
- **Dynamic measures** are preferred:
  - **Passive leg raise (PLR)**: Raise legs to 45 degrees with trunk flat; >10% increase in CO/SV = fluid responsive
  - **Stroke volume variation (SVV)**: >13% variation with mechanical ventilation suggests fluid responsiveness
  - **Pulse pressure variation (PPV)**: >13% variation suggests fluid responsiveness
  - **IVC ultrasound**: Collapsibility index >50% (spontaneously breathing) or distensibility >18% (mechanically ventilated)

### Critical Nursing Interventions
- Titrate vasopressors to MAP target (typically >= 65 mmHg)
- Administer IV fluids as ordered while monitoring for fluid overload
- Position changes affect hemodynamics -- always note position when recording values
- Report significant trends: falling CI, rising SVR, declining SvO2/ScvO2
- Ensure alarm parameters are appropriately set and functional
