clinicalPearls: MAP is the most clinically meaningful pressure -- focus on MAP trending, not isolated SBP/DBP values
CVP alone does NOT reliably predict fluid responsiveness; use dynamic assessments (PLR, SVV, PPV)
A "normal" blood pressure does not mean adequate perfusion -- the body compensates by raising SVR until late decompensation
Rising lactate and falling ScvO2 are early indicators of inadequate tissue oxygen delivery, often before BP drops
In cardiogenic shock, fluids can worsen the situation -- the problem is pump failure, not volume deficit
Always think "what are the components of CO?" when troubleshooting hemodynamic instability: HR, preload, afterload, contractility
Dobutamine LOWERS SVR -- do not use as sole agent in hypotension; pair with a vasopressor if needed
*Add from daily practice*

homeostasis-and-feedback-loops.title: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops

sections.definition:
**Homeostasis** = the tendency of biological systems to maintain internal stability while adjusting to changing external conditions.

This is not passive equilibrium — it's **active, energy-consuming regulation**. The Na+/K+ ATPase pump uses ~25% of your basal metabolic rate just to maintain ion gradients. Temperature regulation, pH buffering, glucose metabolism — all require constant energy expenditure.

When homeostatic mechanisms fail faster than the body can compensate → **disease**.
When they fail completely → **death**.

> **Why this matters in the ED:** Almost every critical patient you see is someone whose feedback loops have been overwhelmed. Sepsis = thermoregulation + hemodynamic feedback overwhelmed by infection. DKA = glucose regulation failed. Shock = cardiovascular feedback loops failing to maintain perfusion. Your treatments are either restoring the broken loop or substituting for it externally (vasopressors = external blood pressure regulation; ventilator = external respiratory regulation; insulin drip = external glucose regulation).

---
sections.feedback-loop-architecture:
Every feedback loop has the same components:

```
Stimulus (change in variable)
    ↓
Sensor/Receptor (detects the change)
    ↓
Afferent pathway (signal to control center)
    ↓
Control Center (integrates info, makes decision)
    ↓
Efferent pathway (signal to effector)
    ↓
Effector (produces response)
    ↓
Response (corrects or amplifies the change)
    ↓
[loops back to sensor]
```

### Negative Feedback — The Workhorse (95% of physiology)

**Negative feedback opposes the stimulus.** The response moves the variable BACK toward the set point.

| Variable | Sensor | Control Center | Effector | Response |
|----------|--------|---------------|----------|----------|
| **Blood pressure ↑** | Baroreceptors (carotid sinus, aortic arch) | Medulla (cardiovascular center) | Heart, blood vessels | ↓ Heart rate, vasodilation → BP returns to normal |
| **Blood glucose ↑** | Beta cells of pancreas | Beta cells (sensor AND controller) | Liver, muscle, adipose | Insulin → glucose uptake → glucose falls |
| **Blood glucose ↓** | Alpha cells of pancreas | Alpha cells | Liver | Glucagon → glycogenolysis → glucose rises |
| **Body temp ↑** | Hypothalamic thermoreceptors | Hypothalamus | Sweat glands, cutaneous vasculature | Sweating, vasodilation → heat dissipation → temp falls |
| **pCO2 ↑ (acidosis)** | Central chemoreceptors (medulla), peripheral (carotid bodies) | Respiratory center (medulla) | Diaphragm, intercostals | ↑ Respiratory rate and depth → CO2 blown off → pH rises |
| **Serum Ca2+ ↓** | Parathyroid glands | Parathyroid glands | Bone, kidney, intestine | PTH release → Ca2+ resorption from bone, reabsorption in kidney → Ca2+ rises |

> **The thermostat analogy:** Set your home thermostat to 72°F. When temp drops to 70°F, the furnace turns ON (effector). When temp reaches 72°F, furnace turns OFF. The response (heating) opposes the stimulus (cooling). This is negative feedback.

### Positive Feedback — Rare, Explosive, and Dangerous

**Positive feedback amplifies the stimulus.** The response drives the variable FURTHER from the starting point. This creates an escalating cascade that only stops when an external event interrupts it.

Normal positive feedback is rare and always has a defined endpoint:

| Process | Mechanism | What Stops It |
|---------|-----------|---------------|
| **Blood clotting** | Each clotting factor activates the next → amplifying cascade → thrombin generation accelerates exponentially | Anticoagulant factors (protein C, antithrombin III), clot completion |
| **Labor contractions** | Oxytocin → uterine contraction → pushes baby against cervix → more oxytocin → stronger contraction | Delivery of baby |
| **Action potential depolarization** | Na+ entry → depolarization → MORE Na+ channels open → MORE depolarization | Na+ channel inactivation (built-in safety mechanism) |
| **Lactation** | Suckling → oxytocin → milk ejection → more suckling | Baby stops feeding |

> **When positive feedback goes wrong in disease:** This is where things get terrifying.
> - **DIC** = clotting cascade activates globally → consumes all clotting factors → paradoxical bleeding. Positive feedback without the normal braking mechanism.
> - **Septic shock** = inflammatory cascade → vasodilation → hypotension → tissue ischemia → MORE inflammation → MORE vasodilation. The loop accelerates until death or we break it.
> - **Malignant hyperthermia** = uncontrolled Ca2+ release in skeletal muscle → heat generation → MORE Ca2+ release → temperature spirals to 43°C+. Only dantrolene (blocking the Ca2+ channel) breaks the loop.

---
sections.set-points-where-the-body-wants-to-be:
Every homeostatic variable has a set point (or set range) — the target value the body defends.

| Variable | Normal Set Point/Range | Life-Threatening Range |
|----------|----------------------|----------------------|
| Core temperature | 36.5-37.5°C (97.7-99.5°F) | <32°C or >41°C |
| Arterial pH | 7.35-7.45 | <6.8 or >7.8 |
| Serum K+ | 3.5-5.0 mEq/L | <2.5 or >6.5 |
| Serum Na+ | 135-145 mEq/L | <120 or >160 |
| Blood glucose | 70-100 mg/dL (fasting) | <40 or >600 |
| PaO2 | 80-100 mmHg | <60 |
| PaCO2 | 35-45 mmHg | <20 or >80 |
| MAP | 70-105 mmHg | <65 |
| Serum Ca2+ (ionized) | 4.5-5.5 mg/dL | <3.0 or >7.0 |

### Set Point Alteration

Sometimes the set point itself changes:
- **Fever:** Pyrogens (from infection) shift the hypothalamic set point UPWARD. The body then uses its normal feedback mechanisms (shivering, vasoconstriction) to RAISE temperature to the new set point. This is why patients with fever feel cold — their actual temperature is below their new set point.
- **Chronic hypertension:** Baroreceptor set point gradually resets higher. The body now "defends" a higher BP as normal. This is why acute lowering of BP in chronic hypertension can cause symptoms — the brain interprets normal BP as hypotension.
- **Reset osmostat in pregnancy:** The osmolality set point drops → pregnant patients normally run Na+ ~5 mEq/L lower than non-pregnant.

---
sections.compensation-when-one-system-covers-for-:
When a primary homeostatic mechanism fails, the body recruits SECONDARY systems to compensate. This is the basis of compensatory physiology:

### Acid-Base Compensation (the clearest example)

| Primary Disorder | What Failed | Compensatory System | How It Compensates |
|-----------------|------------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Metabolic acidosis** (pH ↓, HCO3- ↓) | Bicarbonate consumed or lost | Respiratory system | Hyperventilation → ↓ CO2 → partially restores pH (Kussmaul breathing in Diabetic Ketoacidosis) |
| **Metabolic alkalosis** (pH ↑, HCO3- ↑) | Excess bicarbonate or H+ loss | Respiratory system | Hypoventilation → ↑ CO2 → partially lowers pH |
| **Respiratory acidosis** (pH ↓, CO2 ↑) | Lungs can't clear CO2 | Renal system | Kidneys retain HCO3- and excrete H+ (takes 3-5 days for full compensation) |
| **Respiratory alkalosis** (pH ↓, CO2 ↓) | Excessive ventilation | Renal system | Kidneys excrete HCO3- |

> **Key insight:** Compensation NEVER fully corrects the pH — it only moves it toward normal. If the pH is perfectly normal with an abnormal CO2 and HCO3-, that's a **mixed disorder**, not compensation. See Acid-Base Balance for detailed analysis.

### Hemodynamic Compensation

When cardiac output drops (e.g., heart failure):
1. **Baroreceptor reflex** → ↑ sympathetic tone → tachycardia + vasoconstriction (minutes)
2. **RAAS activation** → angiotensin II + aldosterone → Na+/water retention → ↑ preload (hours)
3. **ADH release** → water retention → ↑ preload (hours)
4. **Cardiac remodeling** → hypertrophy → ↑ contractility (weeks-months)

Each compensation maintains output temporarily but creates new problems:
- Tachycardia ↑ myocardial O2 demand
- Vasoconstriction ↑ afterload → harder for the failing heart to pump
- Fluid retention → pulmonary edema
- Hypertrophy → stiff, arrhythmia-prone ventricle

> **This is why heart failure is a downward spiral** — and why ACE Inhibitors, Beta-Blockers, and Diuretics work. They interrupt the MALADAPTIVE compensation: ACEIs block RAAS, beta-blockers block sympathetic overdrive, diuretics offload excess volume. You're not treating the failing heart — you're treating the body's harmful compensatory response TO the failing heart.

---
sections.decompensation-when-the-system-breaks:
**Compensation** = the body is maintaining the variable in (or near) the acceptable range, but at a cost.
**Decompensation** = the compensatory mechanisms are exhausted and the variable is now drifting outside the survivable range.

The transition from compensated → decompensated is often **sudden and nonlinear**:

| Scenario | Compensated State | Decompensation Trigger | What You See |
|----------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------|
| **Heart failure** | Tachycardia + RAAS holding MAP >65 | Infection, missed medications, dietary indiscretion | Flash pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock |
| **Chronic liver disease** | Increased cardiac output, decreased SVR maintaining perfusion | GI bleed, infection, medication | Hepatic encephalopathy, hepatorenal syndrome |
| **Sepsis** | Tachycardia + vasoconstriction maintaining MAP | Inflammatory cascade overwhelms compensation | Vasodilatory shock, organ failure |
| **Respiratory failure** | Accessory muscle use, tachypnea maintaining SpO2 | Muscle fatigue, worsening pathology | Sudden respiratory arrest (often without warning) |

> **ED pearl:** A patient who is tachycardic and "maintaining" their blood pressure is NOT stable — they are COMPENSATING. The tachycardia IS the red flag. When compensation fails, the crash is sudden. Don't be reassured by a normal BP in a tachycardic patient.

---
sections.iatrogenic-disruption-when-we-break-the-:
Sometimes our treatments disrupt feedback loops:

| Intervention | Feedback Loop Disrupted | Consequence |
|-------------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Chronic steroid use** → sudden stop | HPA axis suppressed (exogenous cortisol → ↓ ACTH → adrenal atrophy) | Adrenal crisis — no cortisol production |
| **Chronic opioid use** → naloxone | Locus coeruleus suppressed → sudden release | Opioid Withdrawal — sympathetic storm |
| **Aggressive BP lowering in chronic HTN** | Baroreceptor set point was high → normal BP = "low" to the brain | Syncope, stroke, AKI |
| **Rapid Na+ correction in hyponatremia** | Brain adapted to low osmolality → rapid ↑ Na+ = osmotic myelinolysis | Central pontine myelinolysis |
| **Insulin without K+ monitoring** | Insulin drives K+ into cells → serum K+ plummets | Hypokalemia → arrhythmia |
| **Loop diuretics** | Volume depletion → activates RAAS → RAAS worsens heart failure | Diuretic resistance, cardiorenal syndrome |

> **This is why "start low, go slow" exists.** Rapidly correcting a chronic abnormality is often more dangerous than the abnormality itself, because the body has ADAPTED its set points and feedback loops to the abnormal state.

---
sections.connections-to-other-first-principles:
- **Electricity and Excitability** — Membrane potential is a homeostatic variable maintained by the Na+/K+ ATPase. Electrolyte disturbances disrupt this homeostasis.
- **Oxygen Delivery and Consumption** — DO2/VO2 balance is maintained by cardiac output, hemoglobin, and respiratory feedback loops.
- **Acid-Base Balance** — The most elegant multi-system compensatory mechanism in the body.
- **Fluid and Electrolyte Balance** — Volume and osmolality regulation through ADH, RAAS, and ANP feedback loops.
- **Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology** — Receptors are the SENSORS in feedback loops. Hormones and neurotransmitters are the SIGNALS.
- **Inflammation and Immune Response** — Inflammation is a positive feedback cascade that SHOULD be self-limiting. When it isn't → sepsis, SIRS, autoimmunity.

---
sections.nticu-application-homeostasis-in-the-neu:
The Neuro Trauma ICU is essentially a series of broken feedback loops being managed externally by nurses, physicians, and machines.

### ICP and the Monro-Kellie Doctrine — A Rigid-Box Homeostasis Failure
Normally the brain maintains relatively constant ICP through small adjustments in CSF displacement and venous blood volume — a negative-feedback system. Hemorrhage, edema, or hydrocephalus overwhelm this buffer. The pressure-volume curve is exponential: initially flat (compensation), then nearly vertical (decompensation). The clinical correlate is the "talk and die" presentation of epidural hematoma — the patient is compensated (normal consciousness), then suddenly decompensates when the buffer is exhausted. **See your note: the transition from compensated to decompensated is often sudden and nonlinear.**

### Cushing's Triad — The Medulla's Emergency Feedback Response
When ICP rises to herniation levels, the medulla is compressed and senses near-zero cerebral perfusion. It fires a massive sympathetic surge (the **Cushing response**) — the last-ditch homeostatic attempt to maintain CPP:
- **↑ SBP:** Maximal vasoconstriction to force blood into the skull against rising ICP
- **Bradycardia:** The baroreceptors sense extreme hypertension and activate the vagal response — a reflex loop intact above the injury
- **Irregular breathing:** The respiratory control center in the medulla/pons is failing

Cushing's Triad is a negative-feedback loop (baroreceptors responding to extreme hypertension) superimposed on a failing organ (the brainstem trying to save itself). It is not a treatment response — it is a sign that the feedback system has hit its absolute limit.

### Neurogenic Shock — Incomplete Sympathetic Loop After SCI
Spinal cord injury above T6 severs the descending sympathetic outflow. The baroreceptor afferent limb (sensing hypotension) remains intact. The control center (medulla) remains intact. But the **efferent limb** (sympathetic nerves to heart and vessels) is disconnected below the injury. The loop senses hypotension and tries to respond — but the response can't reach its target. The patient has bradycardia (vagal unopposed) and vasodilation (no sympathetic tone below injury). Vasopressors substitute for the missing efferent limb.

### Autonomic Dysreflexia — Runaway Positive Feedback Without Inhibitory Brake
After SCI at T6 or above, noxious stimuli below the injury trigger a massive sympathetic discharge with no descending inhibition to brake it. BP escalates (positive feedback, unchecked). The baroreceptors sense this and fire vagal slowing (bradycardia, flushing above injury) — but the inhibitory signal cannot descend past the injury to shut off the sympathetic outflow below. The loop becomes **unregulated positive feedback** → hypertensive crisis. Treatment is removing the trigger (turning off the input to the runaway loop).

### ADH Feedback Loop — Three Ways It Fails in Neuro Injury
Normal: hypothalamus senses ↑ osmolality → ADH release → kidney retains water → osmolality falls → ADH release stops (negative feedback).
- **DI:** Hypothalamus/posterior pituitary destroyed → ADH production fails → loop can't respond → osmolality rises unchecked
- **SIADH:** Injured brain fires ADH continuously regardless of osmolality → the sensor is broken → loop can't turn off → osmolality falls unchecked
- **CSW:** Brain releases natriuretic peptides → kidneys dump Na⁺ → volume depletes → looks like SIADH on labs but is volume-depleted (not euvolemic) → opposite treatment

### Fever in Neuro ICU — A Resetting Thermostat That's Actively Harmful
Fever is not a broken thermostat — it's a reset thermostat (pyrogens shift the hypothalamic set point upward). Normally this is adaptive (hostile environment for pathogens). In the injured brain, fever is catastrophic: each 1°C ↑ raises CMRO2 ~7%, exceeding what the marginal perfusion of the penumbra can support. The homeostatic defense of a higher temperature set point is maladaptive when the organ being "protected" is already at the edge of ischemic failure. We override this with antipyretics and cooling — deliberately lowering the set point back to normal.

*See also: NTICU Shadowing Prep Guide · ICP Monitoring and Management · Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury · Fluid and Electrolyte Balance*

---
sections.references:
- Guyton and Hall: Textbook of Medical Physiology — Chapters on Homeostasis, Feedback Control
- Boron and Boulpaep: Medical Physiology — Homeostasis chapter
- Marieb and Hoehn: Human Anatomy & Physiology — Feedback mechanisms

---

*See also: Acid-Base Balance · Fluid and Electrolyte Balance · Hemodynamics · Shock · Sepsis · Electricity and Excitability · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption*

clinicalPearls: **Disease IS broken homeostasis.** When you can identify WHICH feedback loop failed and WHERE it broke (sensor? signal? effector? set point?), the treatment logic follows naturally.

**Compensation masks severity.** A "normal" blood pressure in a patient who is tachycardic at 130, diaphoretic, and confused is NOT reassuring — it means their sympathetic nervous system is working overtime to maintain a failing system. When compensation exhausts, the crash is sudden.

**"Start low, go slow" is a homeostasis principle.** The body adapts to chronic abnormalities by resetting its feedback loops. Rapid correction (of sodium, blood pressure, glucose, steroids) can be more dangerous than the original problem because you're fighting adapted set points.

**Every vasopressor is an external feedback loop.** When you titrate levophed to MAP >65, you are manually doing what the baroreceptor reflex can no longer do. The ventilator is an external respiratory feedback loop. The insulin drip is an external glucose feedback loop. Critical care nursing is the art of running external homeostatic systems.

**Positive feedback in disease = emergency.** If you can identify a positive feedback loop driving your patient's deterioration (DIC, septic shock, malignant hyperthermia, thyroid storm), the treatment priority is breaking the loop — not just treating individual symptoms.

**Fever is NOT a broken thermostat.** It's a RESET thermostat. The body is actively defending a higher temperature because pyrogens shifted the set point. This is why antipyretics work — they lower the set point back to normal, and the body's normal cooling mechanisms take over.

infection-and-infectious-disease.title: Infection and Infectious Disease

sections.definition: Infection occurs when a pathogenic organism invades a susceptible host, multiplies, and causes tissue damage or a systemic response. Understanding the chain of infection is fundamental to prevention, early recognition, and treatment. The progression from local infection to systemic sepsis represents one of the most critical clinical deterioration pathways in acute care.
sections.key-principles:
### Chain of Infection (6 Links)
All six links must be present for transmission to occur. Breaking **any one link** prevents infection.

| Link | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Infectious Agent** | Pathogen capable of causing disease (bacteria, virus, fungus, parasite) | *Staphylococcus aureus*, SARS-CoV-2 |
| **2. Reservoir** | Where the organism lives and reproduces | Humans, animals, soil, water, fomites |
| **3. Portal of Exit** | Route pathogen leaves the reservoir | Respiratory tract, blood, skin, GI tract |
| **4. Mode of Transmission** | How pathogen travels to new host (direct contact, droplet, airborne, vehicle, vector) | Coughing, needlestick, contaminated food |
| **5. Portal of Entry** | Route pathogen enters the new host | Mucous membranes, broken skin, respiratory tract |
| **6. Susceptible Host** | Host lacking adequate resistance | Immunocompromised, extremes of age, chronic disease |

### Standard Precautions
Applied to **ALL patients regardless of diagnosis**:
- Hand hygiene (soap/water or alcohol-based rub)
- PPE based on anticipated exposure (gloves, gown, mask, eye protection)
- Respiratory hygiene and cough etiquette
- Safe injection practices
- Sharps safety and proper disposal
- Environmental cleaning and disinfection
- Sterile technique for invasive procedures

### Transmission-Based Precautions

| Type | When | PPE/Measures | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact** | Organisms spread by direct or indirect contact | Gloves + gown; dedicated equipment | MRSA, VRE, C. difficile, scabies |
| **Droplet** | Large particles (>5 um) that travel <3 feet | Surgical mask within 6 ft of patient | Influenza, pertussis, meningococcal meningitis |
| **Airborne** | Small particles (<5 um) that remain suspended | N95 respirator + negative-pressure room (AIIR) | TB, measles, varicella, disseminated herpes zoster |

### Antimicrobial Resistance Mechanisms
- **Enzymatic inactivation** -- beta-lactamases destroy beta-lactam ring (e.g., ESBL, carbapenemases)
- **Target modification** -- altered penicillin-binding proteins (PBP2a in MRSA)
- **Efflux pumps** -- actively pump antibiotic out of the cell
- **Decreased permeability** -- porin channel mutations reduce drug entry
- **Biofilm formation** -- polymicrobial communities resistant to penetration

### Antibiotic Stewardship Principles
- Obtain cultures **before** starting antibiotics when possible
- Empiric broad-spectrum therapy narrowed ("de-escalation") based on culture and sensitivity
- Appropriate dose, route, duration; avoid unnecessary prolonged courses
- Monitor therapeutic drug levels (vancomycin trough, aminoglycoside peaks/troughs)
- Reassess at 48-72 hours ("antibiotic timeout")
- See Antibiotics Overview for drug-specific guidance

### Sepsis Continuum

| Stage | Definition |
|---|---|
| **SIRS** | >=2 of: Temp >38.3C or <36C; HR >90; RR >20 or PaCO2 <32; WBC >12k or <4k or >10% bands |
| **Sepsis** | Life-threatening organ dysfunction caused by dysregulated host response to infection (Sepsis-3: suspected infection + SOFA score increase >=2) |
| **Severe Sepsis** | (Legacy term) Sepsis with organ dysfunction, hypoperfusion, or hypotension |
| **Septic Shock** | Sepsis with persistent hypotension requiring vasopressors to maintain MAP >=65 AND lactate >2 mmol/L despite adequate fluid resuscitation |

### qSOFA (Quick SOFA) -- Bedside Screening
Score 1 point for each (>=2 suggests poor outcome):
- Systolic BP <=100 mmHg
- Respiratory rate >=22/min
- Altered mental status (GCS <15)

### Surviving Sepsis Campaign -- Hour-1 Bundle
- Measure **lactate** level (remeasure if >2 mmol/L)
- Obtain **blood cultures** before antibiotics
- Administer **broad-spectrum antibiotics**
- Begin **30 mL/kg crystalloid** for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L
- Apply **vasopressors** if hypotensive during or after fluid resuscitation (target MAP >=65)
sections.clinical-relevance: Sepsis is a leading cause of in-hospital mortality. Early recognition and aggressive treatment within the first hour significantly improve survival. Nurses are often the first to detect subtle changes (altered mental status, tachycardia, new-onset confusion in elderly) that signal developing sepsis. Serial lactate monitoring guides resuscitation adequacy. See Inflammation and Immune Response for the underlying pathophysiology of the systemic inflammatory cascade.

clinicalPearls: **Always obtain cultures before antibiotics** -- but never delay antibiotics to obtain cultures in an unstable patient.
Lactate is a marker of tissue hypoperfusion, not infection itself. Elevated lactate with suspected infection = high mortality risk.
C. difficile requires **soap and water** hand hygiene; alcohol-based rubs do not kill spores.
Negative-pressure rooms exchange air 6-12 times per hour; door must remain closed.
Procalcitonin can help distinguish bacterial infection from other causes of SIRS but is not definitive alone.
In septic shock, norepinephrine is the **first-line vasopressor** per Surviving Sepsis guidelines.
Antibiotic resistance is accelerated by incomplete courses, inappropriate prescribing, and agricultural overuse.

inflammation-and-immune-response.title: Inflammation and Immune Response

sections.definition: The immune system is the body's defense network against pathogens, foreign substances, and abnormal cells. It operates through two interconnected arms: innate (nonspecific) and adaptive (specific) immunity. Inflammation is a fundamental protective response that initiates healing but, when dysregulated, contributes to tissue damage and chronic disease. Understanding immune function is critical for nursing care across all settings -- from interpreting lab values to managing immunosuppressed patients to recognizing systemic inflammatory response.
sections.key-principles:
### Innate (Nonspecific) Immunity
The first line of defense; present from birth, does not require prior exposure, and responds immediately.

**Physical and Chemical Barriers:**
- Skin (largest immune organ), mucous membranes
- Cilia (mucociliary escalator)
- Gastric acid, lysozymes in tears/saliva
- Normal flora (competitive inhibition)
- Sebaceous gland secretions

**Cellular Components:**

| Cell Type | Function |
|-----------|----------|
| **Neutrophils** | First responders (60-70% of WBCs); phagocytosis of bacteria; short-lived (6-10 hours in circulation) |
| **Monocytes/Macrophages** | Phagocytosis, antigen presentation, cytokine production; monocytes become macrophages in tissue |
| **Natural Killer (NK) cells** | Kill virus-infected and tumor cells without prior sensitization |
| **Eosinophils** | Parasitic infections, allergic responses, modulate inflammation |
| **Basophils/Mast cells** | Release histamine, heparin; involved in allergic and anaphylactic responses |
| **Dendritic cells** | Antigen-presenting cells (bridge between innate and adaptive immunity) |

**Complement System:**
- ~30 plasma proteins that cascade when activated
- Three activation pathways: **Classical** (antibody-antigen), **Alternative** (spontaneous), **Lectin** (mannose-binding)
- Functions: **Opsonization** (C3b marks pathogens), **Chemotaxis** (C5a attracts WBCs), **Membrane Attack Complex** (C5b-9 lyses cells), **Inflammation** (C3a, C5a = anaphylatoxins)

### The Inflammatory Response

**Cardinal Signs of Inflammation (Celsus + Virchow):**

| Sign | Latin | Mechanism |
|------|-------|-----------|
| **Redness** | Rubor | Vasodilation increases blood flow |
| **Heat** | Calor | Increased blood flow + metabolic activity |
| **Swelling** | Tumor | Increased capillary permeability -> fluid shift to tissues |
| **Pain** | Dolor | Chemical mediators stimulate nerve endings; pressure from edema |
| **Loss of function** | Functio laesa | Pain + swelling limit movement/use |

**Inflammatory Cascade:**
1. **Tissue injury** -> release of chemical mediators
2. **Vascular response**: Transient vasoconstriction followed by vasodilation and increased capillary permeability
3. **Cellular response**: Margination -> pavementing -> emigration/diapedesis -> chemotaxis -> phagocytosis
4. **Exudate formation**: Fluid, proteins, WBCs accumulate at site
5. **Resolution or progression** to chronic inflammation

**Key Chemical Mediators:**

| Mediator | Source | Effects |
|----------|--------|---------|
| **Histamine** | Mast cells, basophils | Vasodilation, increased permeability, bronchoconstriction |
| **Prostaglandins** | Arachidonic acid (COX pathway) | Pain, fever, vasodilation, platelet aggregation |
| **Leukotrienes** | Arachidonic acid (LOX pathway) | Bronchoconstriction, increased permeability, chemotaxis |
| **Cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha)** | Macrophages, lymphocytes | Fever, acute phase response, WBC activation |
| **Bradykinin** | Plasma kininogens | Pain, vasodilation, increased permeability |
| **Nitric oxide** | Endothelial cells | Vasodilation |

### Adaptive (Specific) Immunity
Develops over time; requires antigen exposure; has memory for faster future response.

**Key Features:**
- **Specificity**: Targets specific antigens
- **Memory**: Faster, stronger response on re-exposure
- **Self-tolerance**: Distinguishes self from non-self
- **Diversity**: Can recognize millions of unique antigens

**Two Arms of Adaptive Immunity:**

| | Cell-Mediated (Cellular) | Humoral (Antibody-Mediated) |
|--|-------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Primary cells** | T lymphocytes | B lymphocytes -> Plasma cells |
| **Maturation** | Thymus | Bone marrow |
| **Targets** | Intracellular pathogens, viruses, fungi, tumor cells, transplant rejection | Extracellular pathogens, bacteria, toxins |
| **Effector mechanism** | Direct cell killing, cytokine release | Antibody production |

**T Cell Subtypes:**

| Type | Marker | Function |
|------|--------|----------|
| **Helper T cells** | CD4+ | Activate B cells, cytotoxic T cells, macrophages; secrete cytokines |
| **Cytotoxic T cells** | CD8+ | Directly kill infected/abnormal cells |
| **Regulatory T cells** | CD4+CD25+ | Suppress immune response; maintain self-tolerance |
| **Memory T cells** | CD4+ or CD8+ | Long-lived; rapid response on re-exposure |

**Immunoglobulins (Antibodies):**

| Class | Location | Function | Clinical Note |
|-------|----------|----------|--------------|
| **IgG** | Blood, tissue | Most abundant (75-80%); crosses placenta; secondary response | Passive immunity to newborn |
| **IgA** | Secretions (saliva, tears, breast milk, GI/respiratory mucosa) | Mucosal immunity | First defense at mucosal surfaces |
| **IgM** | Blood | First antibody produced; primary response; activates complement | Elevated = acute infection |
| **IgE** | Tissue, basophils, mast cells | Allergic reactions, parasitic defense | Elevated in allergy/anaphylaxis |
| **IgD** | B cell surface | B cell activation | Minor role |

### WBC Differential - Clinical Interpretation

| WBC Type | Normal % | Elevated In | Decreased In |
|----------|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **Neutrophils** | 55 - 70% | Bacterial infection, stress, inflammation, steroids | Neutropenic (chemo, aplastic anemia) |
| **Lymphocytes** | 20 - 40% | Viral infection, chronic infection, leukemia | HIV/AIDS, steroids, immunosuppression |
| **Monocytes** | 2 - 8% | Chronic infection, inflammatory disease | Corticosteroid therapy |
| **Eosinophils** | 1 - 4% | Allergic reactions, parasitic infection, asthma | Acute stress, steroids |
| **Basophils** | 0.5 - 1% | Allergic reactions, CML | Acute infection, steroids |

**Normal WBC count**: 5,000 - 10,000/mm3
- **Leukocytosis**: > 10,000 (infection, inflammation, stress, steroids, leukemia)
- **Leukopenia**: < 5,000 (bone marrow suppression, chemotherapy, overwhelming infection)
- **Left shift**: Increased bands (immature neutrophils) > 10% -- indicates acute bacterial infection, bone marrow releasing immature cells to meet demand

### Acute Phase Response
- Systemic response to inflammation triggered by IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha
- **Fever**: Pyrogens reset hypothalamic thermostat
- **Elevated CRP and ESR**: Nonspecific markers of inflammation
- **Elevated fibrinogen, ferritin, hepcidin**
- **Decreased albumin and transferrin** (negative acute phase reactants)
- **Leukocytosis with left shift**
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Nursing Assessment Priorities
- **Monitor WBC count and differential** -- trending is more valuable than single values
- **Assess for signs of infection**: Fever, tachycardia, localized redness/swelling/warmth, purulent drainage
- **In immunocompromised patients**: Infection may present without typical signs (no fever, no pus, no elevated WBC) -- monitor for subtle changes
- **CRP and procalcitonin** trending for infection/sepsis monitoring
- **Nutritional status** -- malnutrition impairs immune function
- **Medication review** -- steroids, chemotherapy, biologics all affect immune response

### Key Immune-Related Labs

| Lab | Normal | Significance |
|-----|--------|-------------|
| **WBC** | 5,000 - 10,000/mm3 | Total white cell count |
| **ANC** | > 1,500/mm3 | Absolute neutrophil count; < 500 = severe neutropenia |
| **CRP** | < 1.0 mg/dL | Acute phase reactant; rises in 6-12 hrs |
| **ESR** | 0-20 mm/hr (M), 0-30 (F) | Nonspecific inflammation marker; slower to change |
| **Procalcitonin** | < 0.1 ng/mL | Bacterial infection marker; helps distinguish bacterial vs. viral |
| **Lactate** | < 2 mmol/L | Tissue perfusion marker |
| **CD4 count** | 500 - 1500/mm3 | T helper cell count; < 200 = AIDS-defining |

### Neutropenic Precautions (ANC < 500)
- Private room with positive pressure if available
- Strict hand hygiene for all visitors and staff
- No fresh flowers, raw fruits/vegetables, standing water
- Avoid invasive procedures when possible
- Monitor temperature q4h; report fever (> 38.3C single or > 38.0C sustained for 1 hr) -- this is a medical emergency
- Initiate broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour of fever onset

clinicalPearls: A "left shift" (elevated bands) is one of the earliest lab indicators of acute bacterial infection -- report it even if WBC total is normal
Immunocompromised patients (neutropenic, HIV, transplant) may not mount a fever or elevate WBC -- maintain high clinical suspicion with any subtle change
Steroids are potent immunosuppressants: they increase neutrophil count (demargination) while actually suppressing immune function -- a high WBC on steroids does NOT mean infection is resolving
CRP responds faster than ESR (rises in 6-12 hours vs. 24-48 hours) and is more specific for acute bacterial processes
Procalcitonin helps differentiate bacterial from viral infection and can guide antibiotic de-escalation

The inflammatory response can become self-perpetuating and destructive (as in ARDS, SIRS, septic shock) -- it is the dysregulated immune response, not the pathogen itself, that often causes organ damage

Fever is generally beneficial up to a point (enhances immune function, inhibits microbial growth) -- routine antipyretic use is increasingly questioned except for patient comfort or hemodynamic instability
*Add from daily practice*

nursing-process.title: Nursing Process

sections.definition: The nursing process is a systematic, evidence-based framework for delivering patient-centered care. It provides a structured approach to clinical decision-making through five iterative phases: Assessment, Diagnosis, Planning, Implementation, and Evaluation (ADPIE). The nursing process is dynamic and cyclical -- new assessment data continuously refine diagnoses, plans, and interventions. It forms the legal and professional foundation of nursing practice and documentation.
sections.key-principles:
### ADPIE -- The Five Phases

**1. Assessment**
- Systematic, continuous collection of subjective and objective data
- **Subjective data**: patient-reported symptoms, history, concerns (e.g., "I feel short of breath")
- **Objective data**: measurable, observable findings (e.g., SpO2 89%, RR 28, use of accessory muscles)
- Types: comprehensive (admission), focused (problem-specific), shift/ongoing
- Sources: patient interview, physical examination, medical records, lab/diagnostic results, family input
- See Health Assessment Foundations for detailed assessment techniques

**2. Diagnosis (Nursing Diagnosis)**
- Clinical judgment about actual or potential health problems that nurses can independently treat
- Format: Problem **related to** etiology **as evidenced by** defining characteristics
- Example: "Impaired gas exchange related to alveolar-capillary membrane changes as evidenced by SpO2 89%, PaO2 58 mmHg, and restlessness"
- Types: actual, risk, health promotion, syndrome
- Distinct from medical diagnosis; focuses on human response to health conditions

**3. Planning**
- Establish **priority** nursing diagnoses
- Set measurable, patient-centered goals/outcomes with timeframes (SMART goals)
- Short-term goals: achievable within hours to days (e.g., "SpO2 >94% within 2 hours of intervention")
- Long-term goals: achievable over weeks to months (e.g., "Patient will ambulate 200 feet independently by discharge")
- Select evidence-based nursing interventions to achieve goals
- Develop individualized care plan (may use standardized care plans or clinical pathways as starting points)

**4. Implementation**
- Execute planned interventions; may be independent, dependent, or collaborative
- **Independent**: nurse-initiated actions (repositioning, education, ambulation, assessment)
- **Dependent**: physician/provider-ordered actions (medication administration, IV fluid infusion)
- **Collaborative**: multidisciplinary actions (PT/OT referrals, dietitian consults, care conferences)
- Document all interventions and patient responses in real time

**5. Evaluation**
- Compare patient outcomes to established goals
- **Goal met**: continue plan or discontinue if resolved
- **Goal partially met**: modify plan, reassess, adjust interventions or timeframes
- **Goal not met**: reassess data, re-examine diagnosis and plan, consider barriers, establish new interventions
- Evaluation data becomes new assessment data, restarting the cycle

### Clinical Reasoning and Critical Thinking

Clinical reasoning is the cognitive process of analyzing patient data, recognizing patterns, and making sound clinical judgments. Key components:
- **Recognizing cues**: identifying relevant data from assessment
- **Analyzing cues**: linking data to underlying pathophysiology and patient context
- **Prioritizing hypotheses**: determining most likely and most dangerous explanations
- **Generating solutions**: identifying appropriate interventions
- **Taking action**: implementing the chosen intervention
- **Evaluating outcomes**: assessing whether the intervention was effective

Avoid **anchoring bias** (fixating on initial impression), **confirmation bias** (seeking only data that supports hypothesis), and **premature closure** (accepting a diagnosis before sufficient evidence).

### Prioritization Frameworks

| Framework | Application |
|---|---|
| **ABCs (Airway, Breathing, Circulation)** | First-line triage and acute assessment; airway always takes priority unless cardiac arrest (C-A-B in BLS) |
| **Maslow's Hierarchy** | Physiologic needs first (oxygenation, circulation, nutrition), then safety, belonging, esteem, self-actualization |
| **Acute vs. Chronic** | New/acute changes take priority over stable chronic conditions |
| **Stable vs. Unstable** | Unstable patients assessed and treated before stable patients |
| **Actual vs. Potential** | Actual problems generally take priority over risk-for problems |

### Delegation -- The 5 Rights

| Right | Question to Ask |
|---|---|
| **Right task** | Is this task within the scope of practice of the delegate? Can it be delegated per state nurse practice act and facility policy? |
| **Right circumstance** | Is the patient stable enough? Is the setting appropriate? Are resources available? |
| **Right person** | Does the delegate have the competency and training for this task? |
| **Right direction/communication** | Have I provided clear, specific instructions including what to do, when, and what to report? |
| **Right supervision/evaluation** | Am I available for questions? Will I follow up and evaluate the outcome? |

**Cannot delegate**: assessment, nursing diagnosis, care planning, evaluation, patient education, tasks requiring clinical judgment. **Can delegate to UAP/CNA**: vital signs (stable patients), I&O measurement, hygiene, ambulation, blood glucose monitoring, specimen collection.

### SBAR Communication

| Component | Content |
|---|---|
| **Situation** | "I am calling about [patient name] in [room]. The problem is [brief description of current concern]." |
| **Background** | Relevant history: admitting diagnosis, pertinent medical history, current treatments, allergies, code status |
| **Assessment** | Your clinical assessment: vital signs, lab values, physical findings, what you think is happening |
| **Recommendation** | What you are requesting: "I would like you to come evaluate the patient" or "I think we need [specific order/intervention]" |

**Handoff**: use I-SBAR-R or standardized handoff tool; include pending tasks, outstanding labs, anticipated changes, safety concerns, code status, and family communication needs.
sections.clinical-relevance: The nursing process is not just a theoretical framework -- it is applied every moment of clinical practice. Triage in the ED uses rapid assessment and prioritization. Bedside shift report applies SBAR and promotes continuity. Clinical deterioration requires rapid cycling through ADPIE. Effective delegation allows the RN to focus on tasks requiring clinical judgment while ensuring safe care delivery.

sections.related-conditions: - All conditions in this knowledge base apply the nursing process

clinicalPearls: **Assessment is continuous**, not a one-time event. Subtle changes in mental status, vital sign trends, or urine output may signal early deterioration hours before overt decompensation.
The nursing process is a **legal framework** -- documentation of assessment, diagnosis, plan, interventions, and evaluation provides the legal record of care delivered.
When in doubt about prioritization, ask: "What will kill this patient first?" -- address that first.
Delegation does not transfer **accountability** -- the RN remains accountable for the outcomes of delegated tasks.
SBAR reduces communication errors. State your recommendation clearly; "I need an order for..." is more effective than "I was wondering if maybe..."
Reassess after every intervention. A medication given without follow-up assessment is incomplete nursing care.
Clinical reasoning improves with experience, deliberate practice, and reflective debriefing after complex patient situations.
Prioritization on NCLEX and in practice: apply ABCs first, then Maslow, then acute before chronic, unstable before stable.

nutrition-and-metabolism.title: Nutrition and Metabolism

sections.definition: Nutrition provides the substrates for energy production, tissue repair, immune function, and homeostasis. Metabolism encompasses the biochemical pathways that convert nutrients into usable energy (catabolism) or build complex molecules (anabolism). Critically ill and hospitalized patients are at high risk for malnutrition, which increases morbidity, delays wound healing, impairs immune function, and prolongs hospital stays. Nurses must be proficient in nutritional screening, assessment, and management of enteral and parenteral nutrition.
sections.key-principles:
### Macronutrients

| Nutrient | Energy Yield | Functions | Sources |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Carbohydrates** | 4 kcal/g | Primary energy source; glucose is obligate fuel for brain and RBCs; glycogen storage in liver and muscle | Grains, fruits, vegetables, sugars |
| **Protein** | 4 kcal/g | Structural (muscle, enzymes, antibodies, transport proteins); wound healing; immune function; not a preferred energy source | Meat, dairy, legumes, eggs |
| **Fat** | 9 kcal/g | Concentrated energy source; cell membrane structure; hormone synthesis; fat-soluble vitamin absorption (A, D, E, K); insulation | Oils, nuts, fatty fish, dairy |

### Key Metabolic Pathways

| Pathway | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Glycolysis** | Cytoplasm | Glucose to pyruvate; yields 2 ATP (net); anaerobic conditions produce lactate |
| **Krebs cycle (TCA)** | Mitochondrial matrix | Acetyl-CoA oxidized; generates NADH and FADH2 for electron transport chain |
| **Oxidative phosphorylation** | Inner mitochondrial membrane | Electron transport chain; generates ~34 ATP per glucose; requires O2 |
| **Gluconeogenesis** | Liver (primarily) | Synthesis of glucose from non-carbohydrate sources (amino acids, lactate, glycerol); critical during fasting/starvation |
| **Glycogenolysis** | Liver, muscle | Glycogen breakdown to glucose; hepatic glycogen maintains blood glucose; muscle glycogen fuels local contraction |
| **Lipogenesis** | Liver, adipose tissue | Conversion of excess glucose/amino acids to fatty acids for storage as triglycerides |
| **Beta-oxidation** | Mitochondria | Fatty acid breakdown to acetyl-CoA; major energy source during prolonged fasting |

### Micronutrients of Clinical Significance
- **Vitamin C**: collagen synthesis, antioxidant, iron absorption; deficiency = scurvy (impaired wound healing)
- **Vitamin D**: calcium/phosphorus homeostasis, bone minerite; deficiency common in ICU patients
- **Vitamin K**: synthesis of clotting factors II, VII, IX, X; deficiency = coagulopathy
- **B12 (cobalamin)**: DNA synthesis, neurologic function; deficiency = megaloblastic anemia, neuropathy (see B12 Deficiency)
- **Folate**: DNA synthesis; deficiency = megaloblastic anemia, neural tube defects in pregnancy
- **Iron**: oxygen transport (hemoglobin); deficiency = microcytic anemia
- **Zinc**: wound healing, immune function, cell division
- **Thiamine (B1)**: carbohydrate metabolism; deficiency = Wernicke encephalopathy, beriberi; critical to replace BEFORE glucose in malnourished patients
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Nutritional Assessment

| Marker | Normal | Half-Life | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Albumin** | 3.5-5.0 g/dL | ~20 days | Reflects chronic nutritional status; also affected by inflammation, liver disease, fluid status |
| **Prealbumin (transthyretin)** | 16-40 mg/dL | ~2 days | Better marker of acute nutritional changes; more responsive to nutritional interventions |
| **Transferrin** | 200-360 mg/dL | ~8 days | Intermediate marker; also reflects iron status |
| **Nitrogen balance** | Positive = anabolic | N/A | 24-hr urine urea nitrogen: N balance = protein intake (g)/6.25 - (UUN + 4); goal is positive balance in healing patients |
| **BMI** | 18.5-24.9 kg/m^2 | N/A | Underweight <18.5, overweight 25-29.9, obese >=30 (see Obesity) |

### Malnutrition Screening
- Screen all patients within 24 hours of admission using validated tool (e.g., Malnutrition Screening Tool, NRS-2002, MUST)
- Risk factors: unintentional weight loss >5% in 1 month or >10% in 6 months, poor oral intake, chronic disease, NPO status, critical illness
- Refer to registered dietitian for comprehensive nutritional assessment when screening positive

### Enteral Nutrition (EN)
Preferred route when GI tract is functional ("if the gut works, use it"):

| Access | Indication | Duration |
|---|---|---|
| **Nasogastric (NG/OG)** | Short-term (<4 weeks); functional stomach | Most common; aspiration risk |
| **Nasoduodenal/nasojejunal** | Gastroparesis, high aspiration risk, pancreatitis | Post-pyloric reduces aspiration risk |
| **Gastrostomy (PEG)** | Long-term (>4 weeks); functional stomach | Surgical or endoscopic placement |
| **Jejunostomy (PEJ)** | Long-term; need to bypass stomach | Surgical placement |

**EN Complications**: aspiration, diarrhea, tube clogging, abdominal distension, electrolyte imbalances, hyperglycemia, refeeding syndrome
**Nursing considerations**: verify tube placement (X-ray for initial; pH testing for ongoing), elevate HOB 30-45 degrees, check gastric residual volumes per protocol, flush tube before/after medications

### Parenteral Nutrition (PN/TPN)
Used when GI tract is non-functional, inaccessible, or insufficient:
- **Components**: dextrose (carbohydrate), amino acids (protein), lipid emulsions (fat), electrolytes, vitamins, trace elements, water
- **Central line** required for concentrations >10% dextrose (hyperosmolar; peripheral PN limited to <900 mOsm/L)
- **Monitoring**: daily weights, strict I&O, BMP (glucose, electrolytes) q6h initially then daily, triglycerides, LFTs weekly, blood glucose q4-6h

### Refeeding Syndrome
A potentially fatal shift in fluids and electrolytes when nutrition is restarted after prolonged starvation or severe malnutrition:
- **Mechanism**: insulin surge with refeeding drives potassium, phosphorus, and magnesium intracellularly; thiamine is rapidly consumed
- **Hallmark**: severe **hypophosphatemia**, also hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia
- **Complications**: cardiac arrhythmias, heart failure, respiratory failure, seizures, rhabdomyolysis, death
- **Prevention**: identify at-risk patients, start feeding at low caloric rate (10-20 kcal/kg/day), advance slowly over 5-7 days, supplement thiamine/electrolytes prophylactically, monitor electrolytes BID initially

- Gastrointestinal System
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance

clinicalPearls: **"Feed early, feed enterally"** -- early EN (within 24-48 hours of ICU admission) improves outcomes in critically ill patients.
Always replace **thiamine before dextrose** in malnourished or alcoholic patients to prevent Wernicke encephalopathy.
Albumin is a poor acute nutritional marker because it is a negative acute-phase reactant (drops during inflammation regardless of nutrition). Use prealbumin for short-term monitoring.
Hyperglycemia is the most common metabolic complication of TPN; insulin may need to be added to the TPN bag or administered as a drip.
Aspiration precautions during tube feeding: HOB >=30 degrees, check residuals, hold if residual >500 mL (per institutional policy).
Caloric needs increase significantly with burns (up to 2x basal), sepsis, trauma, and major surgery; consult dietitian for precise calculations.
NPO patients still need IV dextrose to prevent ketosis and protein catabolism if fasting is prolonged.
Refeeding syndrome risk is highest in the first 72 hours of refeeding; phosphorus levels should be monitored closely.

oxygen-delivery-and-consumption-do2vo2.title: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption (DO2/VO2)

sections.the-equation-that-explains-everything:
### Oxygen Delivery (DO2)

**DO2 = CO × CaO2**

Where:
- **CO** = Cardiac Output = HR × SV (Heart Rate × Stroke Volume)
- **CaO2** = Arterial Oxygen Content = (Hgb × SaO2 × 1.34) + (PaO2 × 0.003)

Breaking it down:

| Component | What It Means | Normal Value |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|
| **HR** | Heart rate | 60-100 bpm |
| **SV** | Stroke volume (mL per beat) | ~70 mL |
| **CO** | Cardiac output (L/min) = HR × SV | ~5 L/min |
| **Hgb** | Hemoglobin concentration (g/dL) | 12-16 g/dL |
| **SaO2** | Arterial oxygen saturation (%) | 95-100% |
| **1.34** | mL of O2 carried per gram of Hgb (Hüfner's constant) | Fixed |
| **PaO2** | Partial pressure of dissolved O2 (mmHg) | 80-100 mmHg |
| **0.003** | Solubility coefficient for dissolved O2 | Fixed |
| **CaO2** | Total O2 content of arterial blood (mL O2/dL blood) | ~20 mL O2/dL |
| **DO2** | Total oxygen delivery (mL O2/min) | ~1000 mL O2/min |

> **Critical insight:** The dissolved O2 term (PaO2 × 0.003) contributes almost NOTHING. At PaO2 100 mmHg, dissolved O2 = 0.3 mL/dL. At Hgb 14 and SaO2 98%, bound O2 = 18.4 mL/dL. **99% of oxygen delivery depends on hemoglobin and cardiac output, NOT PaO2.** This is why giving a patient 100% FiO2 barely matters if their hemoglobin is 5 or their cardiac output is 2.

### Oxygen Consumption (VO2)

**VO2** = the total amount of oxygen consumed by tissues per minute.

Normal VO2 ≈ **250 mL O2/min** at rest.

VO2 increases with:
- Fever (↑ 10% per °C above normal)
- Sepsis (metabolic demand skyrockets)
- Shivering, seizures, agitation
- Work of breathing
- Burns, trauma

VO2 decreases with:
- Hypothermia
- Sedation, paralysis
- General anesthesia

### The Extraction Ratio

**O2 Extraction Ratio (O2ER) = VO2 / DO2**

Normal: ~25% (the body uses only 25% of delivered oxygen at rest — there's a 4:1 reserve)

| O2ER | Meaning | Clinical State |
|------|---------|---------------|
| **25%** | Normal — large oxygen reserve | Healthy at rest |
| **25-50%** | Increased extraction — body compensating for ↓ DO2 | Compensated shock, anemia, early heart failure |
| **50-70%** | Critical extraction — approaching the limit | Decompensating shock |
| **>70%** | Supply-dependent VO2 — tissues cannot get enough O2 | Organ failure, lactic acidosis, death imminent |

---
sections.the-do2vo2-relationship-the-most-importa:
```
VO2
 ↑
 |          ______________ ← Supply-independent (normal)
 |         /                  VO2 is constant regardless of DO2
 |        /                   (body takes what it needs)
 |       /
 |      /  ← Supply-dependent (shock)
 |     /     VO2 drops because DO2 is too low
 |    /      Anaerobic metabolism begins
 |   /       Lactate rises
 |  /
 |_/________________________________→ DO2
          ↑
     Critical DO2
     (~330 mL/min)
```

**Above critical DO2:** Tissues extract what they need. Excess delivery is a safety margin. VO2 is "supply-independent."

**Below critical DO2:** Tissues can't extract enough. VO2 falls with DO2. Anaerobic metabolism begins. Lactate accumulates. This is **shock** — regardless of what the blood pressure reads.

> **The definition of shock is NOT low blood pressure.** Shock is inadequate tissue oxygen delivery relative to metabolic demand. A patient can be in shock with a "normal" BP (compensated shock) or have low BP without shock (healthy athlete, medication effect).

---
sections.the-three-levers-of-oxygen-delivery:
When DO2 is inadequate, you have exactly THREE things you can fix:

### Lever 1: Cardiac Output (CO = HR × SV)

| Problem | Why DO2 Drops | How to Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **Heart rate too low** (bradycardia) | Fewer beats = less output | Atropine, pacing, chronotropes |
| **Heart rate too high** (extreme tachycardia) | Not enough filling time → ↓ SV | Rate control, volume, treat underlying cause |
| **Stroke volume ↓ — Preload problem** | Not enough blood returning to heart (hypovolemia, hemorrhage) | **Volume** — crystalloid, blood products |
| **Stroke volume ↓ — Contractility problem** | Heart can't squeeze effectively (cardiogenic shock, MI) | **Inotropes** — dobutamine, milrinone |
| **Stroke volume ↓ — Afterload problem** | Heart can't overcome resistance (massive vasoconstriction) OR has no resistance to push against (vasodilatory shock) | Vasodilators (↓ afterload) or vasopressors (↑ afterload) depending on the problem |

### Lever 2: Hemoglobin (Hgb)

| Problem | Why DO2 Drops | How to Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **Anemia** (low Hgb) | Fewer oxygen carriers | **Transfuse** pRBCs. Each unit ↑ Hgb ~1 g/dL |
| **Dyshemoglobins** (CO poisoning, methemoglobinemia) | Hgb present but can't carry O2 | High-flow O2, hyperbaric O2 (CO), methylene blue (metHgb) |
| **Sickle cell crisis** | Hgb present but sickled cells can't flow through capillaries | Hydration, pain management, exchange transfusion |

> **Transfusion threshold:** Evidence supports restrictive strategy (Hgb 7 g/dL for most patients, 8 for ACS). But in active hemorrhagic shock, don't wait for a number — transfuse based on clinical status.

### Lever 3: Oxygen Saturation (SaO2)

| Problem | Why DO2 Drops | How to Fix |
|---------|-------------|-----------|
| **Hypoxemia** (low PaO2 → low SaO2) | Hgb not fully saturated | Supplemental O2, ventilatory support, treat underlying cause |
| **V/Q mismatch** | Blood flowing past unventilated lung (pneumonia, PE, atelectasis) | Treat cause, position optimization, PEEP |
| **Shunt** | Blood bypassing lungs entirely (intracardiac shunt, severe ARDS) | Doesn't respond to O2 alone — need PEEP, prone positioning |
| **Diffusion impairment** | Thickened alveolar membrane (fibrosis, pulmonary edema) | Supplemental O2, treat underlying cause, diuresis for edema |

> **The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve matters here.** SaO2 stays >90% until PaO2 drops below ~60 mmHg. Then it falls off a cliff. This is why PaO2 of 90 vs 300 makes almost no difference in DO2 (SaO2 is ~98-100% for both), but PaO2 of 60 vs 50 is the difference between adequate and critical.

---
sections.shock-through-the-do2vo2-lens:
Every type of shock is a DO2 problem:

| Shock Type | Which Lever Broke? | Primary Deficit | Initial Resuscitation |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Hypovolemic** | CO ↓ (↓ preload) | Volume loss → ↓ SV | Volume (crystalloid, then blood if hemorrhagic) |
| **Cardiogenic** | CO ↓ (↓ contractility) | Pump failure | Inotropes (dobutamine), mechanical support, avoid excess volume |
| **Distributive** (septic) | CO may be ↑ but O2 extraction impaired + vasodilation | Maldistribution of flow + mitochondrial dysfunction | Volume → vasopressors → source control |
| **Obstructive** | CO ↓ (mechanical obstruction to flow) | Something blocking cardiac filling or output | Treat the obstruction (needle decompression for tension pneumo, tPA for massive PE, pericardiocentesis for tamponade) |

> **Septic shock is unique** — DO2 may actually be INCREASED (hyperdynamic state, high CO), but tissues STILL can't use oxygen because of microvascular dysfunction and mitochondrial poisoning. This is why septic shock patients have elevated mixed venous O2 — the oxygen is being delivered but not extracted. See Sepsis.

---
sections.monitoring-do2vo2-at-the-bedside:
### Lactate — The Most Important Number

**Lactate** is the byproduct of anaerobic metabolism. When DO2 < VO2 → cells switch to anaerobic glycolysis → lactate accumulates.

| Lactate Level | Interpretation | Action |
|--------------|----------------|--------|
| **<2 mmol/L** | Normal | — |
| **2-4 mmol/L** | Mild elevation — may indicate tissue hypoperfusion or other causes (liver failure, medications, seizures) | Investigate cause; serial trending |
| **>4 mmol/L** | Severe — likely inadequate DO2 | Aggressive resuscitation. This patient is in or heading toward shock |
| **Rising lactate** | Worsening tissue perfusion regardless of absolute value | Reassess and escalate — current interventions are failing |

> **Lactate clearance is more important than absolute value.** A lactate of 6 that drops to 3 in 6 hours = improving. A lactate of 3 that rises to 5 = deteriorating. Trend, don't snapshot.

### ScvO2 (Central Venous Oxygen Saturation)

Measured from a central line in the superior vena cava. Reflects the oxygen left in venous blood AFTER tissues have extracted what they need.

| ScvO2 | Interpretation |
|-------|---------------|
| **65-75%** | Normal — tissues are extracting ~25-35% of delivered O2 |
| **<65%** | Tissues are extracting more than normal → DO2 is inadequate → ↑ CO, transfuse, or ↑ SaO2 |
| **>80%** | Tissues are NOT extracting O2 → microvascular dysfunction (sepsis), cyanide poisoning, or decreased VO2 (hypothermia, death) |

### Other Bedside Markers

| Marker | What It Tells You |
|--------|------------------|
| **Blood pressure / MAP** | Perfusion pressure (but NOT the same as DO2 — BP can be "normal" in shock) |
| **Heart rate** | Compensatory response to ↓ DO2 — tachycardia = the body trying to maintain CO |
| **Urine output** | Renal perfusion marker (>0.5 mL/kg/hr target) |
| **Mental status** | Cerebral perfusion marker |
| **Skin perfusion** | Cap refill, mottling, temperature — peripheral vs central perfusion gradient |
| **Base deficit** | Metabolic acidosis from anaerobic metabolism (similar info to lactate) |

---
sections.clinical-decision-framework:
When a patient is "not looking right," think DO2/VO2:

1. **Is DO2 adequate?** Check: HR, BP (MAP), Hgb, SaO2, lactate
2. **If NO → which lever is broken?**
   - SaO2 low → oxygenation problem → supplemental O2, ventilatory support
   - Hgb low → bleeding or chronic anemia → transfuse
   - CO low → WHY?
     - Preload problem (volume depleted) → give volume
     - Pump problem (MI, cardiomyopathy) → inotropes
     - Afterload problem (vasodilation or obstruction) → vasopressors or treat obstruction
3. **Is VO2 too high?** Fever, agitation, shivering, seizures → treat the demand side (antipyretics, sedation, anticonvulsants, paralysis as last resort)
4. **Reassess and trend** → lactate clearance, ScvO2, clinical exam

---
sections.connections-to-other-first-principles:
- **Hemodynamics** — CO, SVR, preload, afterload, contractility ARE the hemodynamic components of DO2
- **Electricity and Excitability** — Arrhythmias directly impair CO → impair DO2
- **Homeostasis and Feedback Loops** — DO2/VO2 balance is maintained by baroreceptor reflex, chemoreceptor reflex, RAAS, and respiratory feedback loops
- **Acid-Base Balance** — Anaerobic metabolism from inadequate DO2 → lactic acidosis → metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation (Kussmaul breathing)
- **Oxygenation and Ventilation** — The SaO2 component of DO2 depends on ventilation and gas exchange

---
sections.nticu-application-do2vo2-in-the-neuro-ic:
The brain is the most metabolically demanding organ in the body at rest (~20% of total body oxygen consumption), and the least tolerant of inadequate delivery. The NTICU is where the consequences of DO2 failure are most immediately catastrophic and least reversible.

### Why MAP Goals Are Higher in NTICU
In general critical care, MAP ≥65 mmHg is sufficient for most organs. In NTICU, the brain is inside a rigid skull with elevated ICP. **CPP = MAP − ICP.** If ICP is 20 and MAP is 65, CPP is only 45 — below the cerebral autoregulation threshold. The brain becomes ischemic despite a "normal" MAP. Every NTICU MAP goal (TBI: ≥70–80; SCI: ≥85–90) is a DO2 goal — ensuring enough driving pressure to maintain cerebral blood flow despite the ICP counterforce.

### Why Hypotension and Hypoxia Are Double-Hit Injuries in TBI
The DO2 equation: **DO2 = CO × (Hgb × SaO2 × 1.34)**. In TBI:
- Hypotension → ↓ CO → ↓ DO2 → penumbra neurons lose their marginal oxygen supply → die
- Hypoxia → ↓ SaO2 → ↓ CaO2 → same result
A single brief episode of either (SBP <90 or SpO2 <90%) in the first 24h after TBI doubles mortality. These aren't just vital sign parameters — they are directly killing neurons that survived the primary injury.

### Why Fever Is a VO2 Crisis
VO2 increases ~7% per 1°C. In a febrile neuro patient, the DO2 side of the equation is fixed or limited (cerebral blood flow is already maxed by compensatory mechanisms or limited by ICP). When VO2 surges from fever and the brain cannot proportionally increase DO2, the extraction ratio exceeds its limit → anaerobic metabolism → lactate → acidosis → worsening injury. Fever management in the NTICU is literally a VO2 reduction strategy.

### Why Seizures Are a Metabolic Emergency
During a generalized seizure, cerebral VO2 increases 3–4× above baseline — the highest possible demand state for neurons. DO2 cannot match this demand → lactate rises → local acidosis → excitotoxic Ca²⁺ influx → cell death. This is why status epilepticus causes permanent brain damage quickly, and why antiseizure treatment is an oxygen delivery intervention as much as an electrical one.

### Why Sedation and Paralysis Lower ICP Through VO2 Reduction
Sedation (propofol, dexmedetomidine) and neuromuscular blockade reduce global VO2 significantly — particularly by eliminating agitation, shivering, and respiratory work. Lower VO2 → less cerebral blood flow needed to match demand → cerebral blood volume decreases → ICP falls. Barbiturate coma takes this to the extreme: CMRO2 drops to ~50% of baseline → ICP drops substantially. This is a VO2 lever on ICP.

### Lactate in NTICU
Elevated lactate in a neuro patient can mean:
1. **Systemic DO2 < VO2** (shock, inadequate resuscitation) — same as any ICU patient
2. **Cerebral ischemia** (focal or global — can't measure directly at bedside)
3. **Seizure-related** (transient, clears as seizure resolves)
4. **Neurogenic** (certain brain injuries release catecholamines → drive glycolysis → lactate without true ischemia)
Trending lactate in NTICU is valuable but requires context — you must correlate with the clinical picture, not treat the number in isolation.

*See also: NTICU Shadowing Prep Guide · ICP Monitoring and Management · Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury · Hemodynamics · Shock*

---
sections.references:
- Marino PL: The ICU Book, 4th ed — Oxygen Transport chapters
- Guyton and Hall: Textbook of Medical Physiology — Oxygen Delivery and Consumption
- Rivers E, et al: Early Goal-Directed Therapy in Severe Sepsis and Septic Shock (NEJM 2001)

---

*See also: Hemodynamics · Shock · Sepsis · Acid-Base Balance · Oxygenation and Ventilation · Vasopressors and Inotropes · Blood Products and Transfusion · Cardiovascular System · Respiratory System*

clinicalPearls: **SpO2 of 100% means NOTHING about tissue oxygenation.** A patient with Hgb 4 and CO 2 L/min has a perfect SpO2 but is dying of inadequate DO2. Saturation tells you about one lever (SaO2); you need all three levers to assess delivery.

**Lactate is your DO2/VO2 report card.** It tells you whether tissues are getting enough oxygen, regardless of what the BP, HR, and SpO2 show. A rising lactate in a "stable-looking" patient is a red flag that compensation is failing.

**The oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve has a cliff.** Above PaO2 60 (SaO2 ~90%), the curve is flat — increasing PaO2 barely changes SaO2. Below 60, it drops steeply. This is why PaO2 60 / SpO2 90% is the critical threshold and why we target SpO2 >92% (staying above the cliff).

**Transfusing for a Hgb of 7 is NOT about the number.** It's about DO2. If a patient has Hgb 7 with normal CO and no symptoms → they're compensating fine. If they have Hgb 9 with active bleeding, tachycardia, and rising lactate → they need blood. Treat the physiology, not the number.

**Tachycardia is the first sign of inadequate DO2.** The baroreceptor reflex increases HR to maintain CO when SV drops. A tachycardic patient with a "normal" BP is COMPENSATING — not stable. Anticipate the crash.

**In sepsis, the problem isn't just DO2 — it's O2 extraction.** Septic patients often have HIGH cardiac output but STILL develop lactic acidosis because mitochondria are poisoned and microvascular flow is disrupted. This is why an ScvO2 >80% in a septic patient is a BAD sign — the oxygen is being delivered but not used.

oxygenation-and-ventilation.title: Oxygenation and Ventilation

sections.definition: Oxygenation is the process of delivering oxygen to the tissues, while ventilation is the mechanical movement of air in and out of the lungs. These are distinct but interdependent processes. Adequate oxygenation depends on inspired O2, alveolar ventilation, diffusion across the alveolar-capillary membrane, oxygen transport in blood, and cellular uptake. Understanding these principles is essential for managing any patient with respiratory compromise.
sections.key-principles:
### Ventilation vs. Oxygenation vs. Respiration

| Term | Definition | Assessed By |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Ventilation** | Movement of air between atmosphere and alveoli | PaCO2, ETCO2, respiratory rate/pattern |
| **Oxygenation** | O2 delivery to and uptake by tissues | PaO2, SpO2, SaO2 |
| **Respiration** | Gas exchange at cellular level (O2 use, CO2 production) | Lactate, SvO2/ScvO2 |

### Oxygen Transport
- **Dissolved O2 in plasma**: PaO2 (only ~3% of total O2 carried)
- **Bound to hemoglobin**: SaO2 (97% of total O2 carried)
- **Oxygen content (CaO2)** = (1.34 x Hgb x SaO2) + (0.003 x PaO2)
  - Normal: ~20 mL O2/dL blood
- **Oxygen delivery (DO2)** = CaO2 x CO x 10
  - Normal: ~1000 mL O2/min
- **Oxygen consumption (VO2)**: ~250 mL O2/min
- **O2 extraction ratio** = VO2/DO2; normal ~25%

### Oxyhemoglobin Dissociation Curve
Describes the relationship between PaO2 and SaO2 (hemoglobin's affinity for O2).

**The S-shaped curve has critical clinical implications:**
- **Flat upper portion** (PaO2 60-100): Large changes in PaO2 produce small changes in SaO2 -- a safety margin
- **Steep middle portion** (PaO2 20-60): Small changes in PaO2 produce large drops in SaO2 -- danger zone
- **Critical point**: PaO2 of 60 mmHg corresponds to SaO2 of ~90% -- below this, desaturation is rapid

**Shifts in the Curve:**

| Right Shift (decreased affinity, easier O2 release to tissues) | Left Shift (increased affinity, harder O2 release) |
|---------------------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------------|
| Increased temperature (fever) | Decreased temperature (hypothermia) |
| Increased PaCO2 (Bohr effect) | Decreased PaCO2 |
| Decreased pH (acidosis) | Increased pH (alkalosis) |
| Increased 2,3-DPG | Decreased 2,3-DPG |
| | Carbon monoxide (CO) poisoning |
| | Fetal hemoglobin |

**Mnemonic for RIGHT shift**: "Right = Release" (O2 is released more easily to tissues)

### Ventilation-Perfusion (V/Q) Matching
- Normal V/Q ratio = **0.8** (ventilation slightly less than perfusion)
- **Dead space** (V/Q = infinity): ventilated but not perfused (e.g., PE)
- **Shunt** (V/Q = 0): perfused but not ventilated (e.g., atelectasis, pneumonia, ARDS)
- **V/Q mismatch**: Most common cause of hypoxemia; typically responsive to supplemental O2
- **True shunt**: Not responsive to supplemental O2 (blood bypasses ventilated alveoli completely)

### Gas Exchange at the Alveolar-Capillary Membrane
- Driven by partial pressure gradients
- **Alveolar gas equation**: PAO2 = FiO2(Patm - PH2O) - (PaCO2/RQ)
  - Simplified at sea level on room air: PAO2 = 150 - (PaCO2 x 1.25)
- **A-a gradient** = PAO2 - PaO2
  - Normal: < 10 mmHg (increases with age: expected = age/4 + 4)
  - Elevated A-a gradient suggests pulmonary pathology (V/Q mismatch, shunt, diffusion impairment)
  - Normal A-a gradient with hypoxemia suggests hypoventilation or low FiO2

### PaO2/FiO2 (P/F) Ratio
- Quick assessment of oxygenation efficiency
- **Normal**: > 400
- **ALI**: < 300
- **ARDS (mild)**: 200 - 300
- **ARDS (moderate)**: 100 - 200
- **ARDS (severe)**: < 100
- Example: PaO2 of 80 on 40% FiO2 = P/F of 200

### Normal Respiratory Values

| Parameter | Normal |
|-----------|--------|
| Respiratory rate | 12 - 20 breaths/min |
| Tidal volume (VT) | ~500 mL (6-8 mL/kg IBW) |
| Minute ventilation (VE) | 5 - 10 L/min (RR x VT) |
| FRC | ~2400 mL |
| SpO2 | 95 - 100% |
| PaO2 | 80 - 100 mmHg |
| PaCO2 | 35 - 45 mmHg |
| ETCO2 | 35 - 45 mmHg |

### Oxygen Delivery Devices

| Device | Flow Rate | FiO2 | Key Features |
|--------|----------|------|-------------|
| **Nasal cannula** | 1 - 6 L/min | 24 - 44% | Each L/min adds ~4% FiO2; comfortable, allows eating/talking |
| **Simple face mask** | 5 - 10 L/min | 40 - 60% | Minimum 5 L/min to prevent CO2 rebreathing |
| **Venturi mask** | 4 - 12 L/min | 24 - 50% (precise) | Color-coded adapters; precise FiO2 for COPD patients |
| **Non-rebreather (NRB)** | 10 - 15 L/min | 60 - 100% | Reservoir bag must stay inflated; one-way valves |
| **High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC)** | 20 - 60 L/min | 21 - 100% | Heated/humidified; generates ~1 cmH2O PEEP per 10 L/min; dead space washout |
| **CPAP** | N/A | 21 - 100% | Continuous positive airway pressure; splints airways open |
| **BiPAP** | N/A | 21 - 100% | IPAP + EPAP; augments ventilation + oxygenation |
| **Mechanical ventilator** | N/A | 21 - 100% | Full ventilatory support; precise control of all parameters |

### Mechanical Ventilation Basics

**Common Modes:**

| Mode | Description | Key Feature |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **AC (Assist-Control)** | Every breath (patient or machine triggered) delivers set VT or pressure | Full support; risk of breath stacking |
| **SIMV** | Set number of mandatory breaths; patient can breathe spontaneously between | Allows weaning; patient does some work |
| **PSV (Pressure Support)** | Patient triggers all breaths; machine augments with set pressure | Weaning mode; requires intact respiratory drive |
| **PRVC** | Pressure-regulated volume control; targets VT using variable pressure | Combines benefits of volume and pressure modes |

**Key Ventilator Settings:**

| Setting | Typical Initial | Purpose |
|---------|----------------|---------|
| **VT (tidal volume)** | 6 - 8 mL/kg IBW | Lower VT reduces barotrauma (lung-protective) |
| **RR** | 12 - 16 breaths/min | Controls minute ventilation (affects PaCO2) |
| **FiO2** | Start 100%, wean to <60% | Oxygenation; prolonged >60% causes O2 toxicity |
| **PEEP** | 5 - 20 cmH2O | Prevents alveolar collapse; improves oxygenation |
| **I:E Ratio** | 1:2 | Inspiratory to expiratory time |
| **Peak Pressure** | < 40 cmH2O target | High pressures risk barotrauma |
| **Plateau Pressure** | < 30 cmH2O target | Reflects alveolar pressure; key lung-protective target |

**Remember:**
- **FiO2 and PEEP** control oxygenation
- **VT and RR (minute ventilation)** control ventilation (CO2 removal)

### Lung-Protective Ventilation (ARDSNet Protocol)
- VT 4 - 6 mL/kg IBW
- Plateau pressure < 30 cmH2O
- Permissive hypercapnia acceptable (pH > 7.20)
- PEEP titrated per FiO2/PEEP table
- Target SpO2 88 - 95% or PaO2 55 - 80 mmHg
sections.clinical-relevance:
### Nursing Assessment Priorities
- **Respiratory rate, depth, pattern, effort** -- use of accessory muscles, nasal flaring, tripod positioning
- **SpO2 monitoring** -- remember SpO2 has limitations (CO poisoning, poor perfusion, nail polish, motion)
- **Auscultation**: Crackles (fluid/infection), wheezes (bronchospasm), diminished/absent (pneumothorax, effusion)
- **Cough assessment**: Productive vs. dry; sputum color, amount, consistency
- **Work of breathing**: Intercostal retractions, paradoxical breathing, diaphoresis
- **Mental status**: Restlessness and confusion are early signs of hypoxia; somnolence is a sign of CO2 retention

### Critical Assessments for Ventilated Patients
- Ventilator settings and waveforms q1-2h
- SpO2, ETCO2 continuous monitoring
- ABG per protocol or with clinical changes
- Oral care q2-4h (VAP prevention)
- HOB elevation 30-45 degrees
- Sedation assessment (RASS, SAS)
- Daily spontaneous breathing trial (SBT) readiness assessment
- Cuff pressure monitoring (20-30 cmH2O)
- Assess for patient-ventilator dyssynchrony

### DOPE Mnemonic for Acute Ventilator Deterioration
- **D** - Displacement (ETT dislodged or in right mainstem)
- **O** - Obstruction (secretions, biting, kinking)
- **P** - Pneumothorax
- **E** - Equipment failure (disconnect, O2 source, ventilator malfunction)

- COPD

clinicalPearls: SpO2 of 90% = PaO2 of ~60 mmHg -- remember the critical point on the oxyhemoglobin curve; below this, desaturation accelerates rapidly
In COPD patients, the hypoxic drive may be the primary stimulus for breathing -- use precise FiO2 (Venturi mask) and titrate carefully, but NEVER withhold O2 from a hypoxic patient
A rising PaCO2 in an acutely ill patient (e.g., asthma exacerbation) who was previously tachypneic is an ominous sign of respiratory muscle fatigue -- prepare for intubation
PEEP recruits collapsed alveoli and improves oxygenation but can decrease venous return and cardiac output -- monitor hemodynamics
High-flow nasal cannula is increasingly used as a bridge to avoid intubation -- watch for failure criteria (persistent tachypnea, desaturation, rising work of breathing)
In CO poisoning, SpO2 is falsely reassuring -- it reads carboxyhemoglobin as oxyhemoglobin; obtain co-oximetry on ABG
Permissive hypercapnia (accepting higher CO2) is protective in ARDS -- the goal is to avoid ventilator-induced lung injury, not to normalize all lab values
*Add from daily practice*

pain-physiology-and-management.title: Pain Physiology and Management

sections.definition: Pain is a subjective, multidimensional experience defined as an unpleasant sensory and emotional experience associated with actual or potential tissue damage (IASP). It serves a protective function but can become maladaptive in chronic states. Effective pain management requires understanding the neurophysiology of nociception, comprehensive assessment using validated tools, and a multimodal approach combining pharmacologic and non-pharmacologic strategies.
sections.key-principles:
### Nociception -- Four Stages

| Stage | Process | Key Structures |
|---|---|---|
| **Transduction** | Noxious stimuli (thermal, mechanical, chemical) converted to electrical signals at peripheral nociceptors | Free nerve endings; release of bradykinin, prostaglandins, substance P, histamine |
| **Transmission** | Electrical impulses travel along peripheral nerves to the spinal cord and brain | A-delta fibers (sharp, fast, well-localized), C fibers (dull, slow, diffuse); dorsal horn of spinal cord; spinothalamic tract |
| **Perception** | Conscious awareness and interpretation of pain in the brain | Thalamus (relay), somatosensory cortex (location/intensity), limbic system (emotional response) |
| **Modulation** | Descending inhibitory or facilitatory pathways alter pain signal transmission | Endogenous opioids (endorphins, enkephalins, dynorphins); serotonin, norepinephrine from brainstem descending pathways; GABA interneurons |

### Gate Control Theory (Melzack & Wall)
- Non-nociceptive input (touch, pressure via large A-beta fibers) can "close the gate" in the dorsal horn, inhibiting pain signal transmission
- Explains why rubbing an injury reduces pain; basis for TENS therapy and massage
- Descending pathways from the brain can also open or close the gate (cognitive/emotional influence on pain)

### Pain Classification

| Category | Characteristics |
|---|---|
| **Acute pain** | Time-limited, identifiable cause, protective function, typically <3 months |
| **Chronic pain** | Persists beyond expected healing (>3 months), may lack identifiable cause, associated with CNS sensitization |
| **Nociceptive (somatic)** | Well-localized, aching/throbbing, from skin/bone/muscle/joints; responds to NSAIDs and opioids |
| **Nociceptive (visceral)** | Diffuse, cramping/pressure, from internal organs; often with referred pain patterns |
| **Neuropathic** | Burning, shooting, "electric shock," tingling; from nerve damage/dysfunction; responds to adjuvants (Antiepileptics, SNRIs) |

### Pain Assessment Tools

| Tool | Population | Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Numeric Rating Scale (NRS)** | Adults, verbal, cognitively intact | 0-10 self-report |
| **Wong-Baker FACES** | Children >=3 years, cognitively impaired adults | 6 faces from smiling to crying |
| **FLACC** | Infants/children (2 mo - 7 yr), nonverbal | Face, Legs, Activity, Cry, Consolability (0-10) |
| **CPOT** | Intubated/sedated ICU patients | Facial expression, body movements, muscle tension, ventilator compliance (or vocalization) (0-8) |
| **BPS (Behavioral Pain Scale)** | Intubated ICU patients | Facial expression, upper limb movements, compliance with ventilation (3-12) |

**Key assessment principles**: Pain is what the patient says it is. Self-report is the gold standard. Assess pain with activity (functional pain), not just at rest. Document location, quality, intensity, timing, aggravating/alleviating factors (OLDCARTS/PQRST).
sections.clinical-relevance:
### WHO Analgesic Ladder (Modified)

| Step | Pain Severity | Agents |
|---|---|---|
| **Step 1** | Mild (1-3) | Non-opioids: acetaminophen, NSAIDs +/- adjuvants |
| **Step 2** | Moderate (4-6) | Weak opioids (tramadol, codeine) + non-opioids +/- adjuvants |
| **Step 3** | Severe (7-10) | Strong opioids (morphine, hydromorphone, fentanyl) + non-opioids +/- adjuvants |

### Multimodal Analgesia
Combining agents with different mechanisms to improve pain control while reducing opioid requirements and side effects:
