**Hydroxocobalamin will turn the patient and ALL their body fluids red** -- urine, tears, sweat. Warn the patient and staff. It also interferes with co-oximetry readings (SpO2, COHb, MetHb) for 24-48 hours -- alert the lab.

**Physostigmine vs "wait it out" for anticholinergic toxicity** -- physostigmine dramatically reverses delirium, agitation, and tachycardia within minutes. It is underutilized due to historical fear of TCA-induced asystole. If you have CONFIRMED the ingestion is NOT a TCA (no QRS widening on ECG), physostigmine is safe and effective.

**HIE takes 15-45 minutes to show hemodynamic effect** -- do not abandon the therapy early. Continue dextrose infusion and titrate insulin up. The mechanism (enhanced myocardial glucose transport) takes time.

**Activated charcoal is underused for appropriate presentations** -- if a patient presents within 1 hour of ingesting a charcoal-adsorbable drug and has a protected airway, charcoal can reduce absorption by 50-70%. Stop deliberating and give it.

**Whole bowel irrigation is the decontamination method for sustained-release preparations and body packers** -- GoLYTELY 1-2 L/hr via NGT until clear rectal effluent. Time-consuming but effective for drugs that charcoal does not bind (iron, lithium) or slow-release formulations.

**Salicylate poisoning is sneaky and lethal** -- patients may look well initially despite toxic levels. Respiratory alkalosis (early) masking metabolic acidosis (late) is the classic pattern. Urinary alkalinization (NaHCO3 infusion targeting urine pH >7.5) is the treatment; hemodialysis for levels >90 mg/dL, renal failure, altered mental status, or pulmonary edema.

antiemetics.title: Antiemetics

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

Nausea and vomiting are mediated by multiple receptor systems -- different antiemetics target different pathways:

| Receptor Target | Drug Class | Location |
|----------------|-----------|----------|
| **Serotonin (5-HT3) antagonist** | Ondansetron, granisetron | Vagal afferents, chemoreceptor trigger zone (CTZ) |
| **Dopamine (D2) antagonist** | Prochlorperazine, metoclopramide, haloperidol | CTZ, GI tract |
| **Histamine (H1) antagonist** | Promethazine, diphenhydramine, meclizine | Vestibular system, vomiting center |
| **Muscarinic (M1) antagonist** | Scopolamine | Vestibular system, vomiting center |
| **NK1 receptor antagonist** | Aprepitant, fosaprepitant | Vomiting center (delayed chemotherapy N/V) |
| **Cannabinoid (CB1) agonist** | Dronabinol | Vomiting center |
| **Corticosteroid** | Dexamethasone | Unknown mechanism (synergistic with 5-HT3 antagonists) |
sections.indications:
- Post-operative nausea and vomiting (PONV)
- Chemotherapy-induced nausea and vomiting (CINV) -- acute and delayed
- Motion sickness
- Gastroparesis (metoclopramide)
- Radiation-induced nausea
- General nausea from various causes (medications, pregnancy, GI illness)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Best For | Key Points |
|------|-------|-------------|----------|-----------|
| **Ondansetron (Zofran)** | PO/IV/ODT | 4-8 mg q6-8h PRN; chemo: 8-24 mg with protocol | PONV, CINV (acute), general nausea | Most commonly used antiemetic; well tolerated; QTc prolongation at high doses (max single IV dose 16 mg); constipation; headache |
| **Granisetron (Kytril)** | PO/IV/Patch | IV: 1 mg; Patch: 1 patch q7 days | CINV, PONV | Longer acting than ondansetron; transdermal patch useful for multi-day chemo |
| **Prochlorperazine (Compazine)** | PO/IV/IM/PR | 5-10 mg q6-8h | General nausea, migraine-associated N/V | Phenothiazine; EPS risk (dystonia, akathisia); avoid in Parkinson's; effective for migraine |
| **Promethazine (Phenergan)** | PO/IV/IM/PR | 12.5-25 mg q4-6h | General nausea, motion sickness | Black Box: severe tissue injury with IV extravasation or intra-arterial injection; give deep IM or diluted slow IV; very sedating; anticholinergic |
| **Metoclopramide (Reglan)** | PO/IV | 10 mg q6h AC | Gastroparesis, PONV, CINV | Prokinetic (increases GI motility); Black Box: tardive dyskinesia with >12 weeks use; EPS risk; avoid in Parkinson's, bowel obstruction |
| **Scopolamine (Transderm-Scop)** | Transdermal patch | 1 patch behind ear q72h; apply 4 hr before travel | Motion sickness, PONV | Anticholinergic side effects (dry mouth, blurred vision, urinary retention, drowsiness); avoid in elderly and glaucoma |
| **Aprepitant (Emend)** | PO / IV (fosaprepitant) | PO: 125 mg day 1, then 80 mg days 2-3 | Delayed CINV (highly emetogenic chemo) | NK1 antagonist; used with 5-HT3 + dexamethasone for highly emetogenic regimens; CYP3A4 interactions (reduces warfarin, OCP efficacy) |
| **Dexamethasone** | PO/IV | 4-12 mg (varies by protocol) | CINV (synergistic with 5-HT3 antagonists), PONV | Mechanism unclear; hyperglycemia; insomnia; used as single dose or short course for antiemetic purposes |
| **Droperidol (Inapsine)** | IV/IM | 0.625-2.5 mg | ED nausea (all causes), post-op N/V, migraine | Butyrophenone (D2 antagonist, same class as haloperidol); potent antiemetic + mild sedation; Black Box for QTc prolongation (controversial — risk is low at antiemetic doses); obtain baseline ECG if feasible; ED workhorse antiemetic — often more effective than ondansetron |
| **Haloperidol (Haldol)** | IV/IM/PO | 0.5-2 mg IV for N/V | Refractory nausea, ICU nausea, agitation with nausea | D2 antagonist; longer acting than droperidol; QTc prolongation; EPS risk; often used when droperidol unavailable |
| **Dronabinol (Marinol)** | PO | 2.5-5 mg BID | Refractory CINV, appetite stimulation | Synthetic THC; Schedule III; drowsiness, dizziness, euphoria; last-line for N/V |
sections.monitoring:
| Parameter | Drug-Specific |
|-----------|--------------|
| QTc interval | Ondansetron (especially doses > 16 mg IV or with other QTc-prolonging drugs) |
| EPS symptoms | Metoclopramide, prochlorperazine (dystonia, akathisia, tardive dyskinesia) |
| Mental status/sedation | Promethazine, scopolamine (anticholinergic effects) |
| Bowel function | Ondansetron (constipation); metoclopramide (increased motility -- contraindicated in obstruction) |
| Blood glucose | Dexamethasone (hyperglycemia, especially diabetics) |
| IV site integrity | Promethazine (tissue necrosis risk) |
sections.adverse-effects-by-class:
| Class | Common | Serious |
|-------|--------|---------|
| **5-HT3 antagonists** | Constipation, headache | QTc prolongation (ondansetron); serotonin syndrome (rare, with concurrent serotonergic drugs) |
| **D2 antagonists** | Sedation, dry mouth | EPS (acute dystonia, akathisia); tardive dyskinesia (metoclopramide Black Box); neuroleptic malignant syndrome (rare) |
| **Antihistamines** | Sedation, dry mouth, blurred vision | Promethazine: severe tissue injury (Black Box -- IV/IA); respiratory depression in children < 2 (Black Box) |
| **Anticholinergics** | Dry mouth, blurred vision, constipation, urinary retention | Confusion/delirium in elderly; glaucoma exacerbation |
| **NK1 antagonists** | Fatigue, diarrhea, hiccups | Drug interactions (CYP3A4); reduced efficacy of warfarin and OCPs |
sections.contraindications:
- **Ondansetron:** Congenital long QT syndrome; concurrent QTc-prolonging drugs (relative)
- **Metoclopramide:** GI obstruction, perforation, hemorrhage; Parkinson's disease; seizure disorder; pheochromocytoma; concurrent use > 12 weeks
- **Prochlorperazine:** Parkinson's disease; CNS depression
- **Promethazine:** Children < 2 years (Black Box -- respiratory depression/death); intra-arterial injection
- **Scopolamine:** Narrow-angle glaucoma; GI/urinary obstruction
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Identify cause of nausea (medication side effect, post-op, chemo, vestibular, GI, pregnancy, increased ICP)
  - Assess hydration status and electrolytes if persistent vomiting
  - Review medication list for drug interactions and QTc-prolonging drugs
  - Check for Parkinson's disease before giving D2 antagonists
- **Administration:**
  - Ondansetron ODT: Place on tongue to dissolve (does NOT need water); good for patients who can't keep pills down
  - Promethazine IV: MUST dilute and give slowly through large-bore IV; max 25 mg/dose; NEVER give IM in deltoid (prefer deep IM in gluteus); tissue necrosis can lead to amputation
  - Metoclopramide: Give 30 min before meals for gastroparesis; diphenhydramine can prevent/treat EPS
  - Scopolamine patch: Apply to hairless area behind ear; wash hands after handling (pupil dilation if touched eyes)
  - CINV protocol: Pre-medicate BEFORE chemotherapy per emetogenic risk (high = 5-HT3 + NK1 + dexamethasone)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Non-pharmacologic measures: small frequent meals, avoid spicy/greasy foods, ginger, deep breathing, cool cloth
  - Scopolamine: Do not touch patch then eyes; remove old patch before applying new one
  - Report involuntary movements (EPS -- metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)
  - Ondansetron may cause constipation -- maintain fluid intake and activity
sections.related-concepts:
- Gastrointestinal System
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics
sections.clinical-applications-seen:
*Real clinical scenarios where you've seen these drugs used. Links to Clinical Encounters Log.*

| Drug | Clinical Context | Encounter |
|------|-----------------|-----------|
| Droperidol | Opioid withdrawal nausea — D2 antagonist antiemesis + mild sedation. More effective than ondansetron for this indication | 2026-02-09 |

---

clinicalPearls: **Ondansetron is the go-to antiemetic in most settings** -- effective, well-tolerated, non-sedating, available PO/IV/ODT. But remember it causes constipation and can prolong QTc.

**Promethazine IV is one of the most dangerous injectable drugs in the hospital.** Tissue necrosis from extravasation or arterial injection can require amputation. ISMP recommends IM or PO routes whenever possible. If IV is necessary: dilute, give slowly through a running line, and watch the IV site closely.

**Metoclopramide > 12 weeks = tardive dyskinesia risk (Black Box).** This is often irreversible. For chronic gastroparesis, use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration. Many patients are kept on it indefinitely without reassessment.

**CINV prevention is ALWAYS better than rescue.** Give antiemetics prophylactically before chemotherapy based on emetogenic potential. Highly emetogenic regimens (cisplatin) require a 3-drug combination: 5-HT3 + NK1 + dexamethasone.

**EPS from D2 antagonists can be treated with diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV.** Acute dystonia (torticollis, oculogyric crisis) is frightening for patients but responds quickly. Some providers pre-medicate with diphenhydramine before giving prochlorperazine.
**For motion sickness, use anticholinergics (scopolamine) or antihistamines (meclizine).** 5-HT3 antagonists like ondansetron are NOT effective for motion sickness -- wrong pathway.

**In pregnancy, first-line antiemetics are:** doxylamine + pyridoxine (B6) (Diclegis/Bonjesta); ondansetron is second-line (debate about first-trimester cleft palate risk -- likely minimal). Avoid metoclopramide if possible.
**Ondansetron ODT (orally disintegrating tablet) dissolves on the tongue** -- ideal for patients who are actively vomiting and can't swallow a pill. Don't give it with water; let it dissolve.

antiepileptics.title: Antiepileptics
antiepileptics.drugClass: Neurologic / Anticonvulsant (also called Anti-Seizure Medications, ASMs)

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

Varies by drug -- multiple mechanisms exist:
- **Sodium channel blockade** (phenytoin, carbamazepine, lacosamide, lamotrigine): Stabilize inactive state of voltage-gated Na+ channels → prevent repetitive neuronal firing
- **Calcium channel modulation** (gabapentin, pregabalin): Bind α2δ subunit of voltage-gated Ca2+ channels → reduce excitatory neurotransmitter release
- **GABA enhancement** (benzodiazepines, phenobarbital, valproate): Increase GABAergic inhibition
- **SV2A binding** (levetiracetam): Modulates synaptic vesicle protein 2A → reduces neurotransmitter release
- **Glutamate antagonism** (perampanel): Blocks AMPA glutamate receptors
sections.indications:
- Epilepsy (focal, generalized, absence, myoclonic, tonic-clonic)
- Status epilepticus (benzodiazepines first-line → then fosphenytoin/levetiracetam/valproate)
- Seizure prophylaxis (post-neurosurgery, traumatic brain injury, brain tumors)
- Neuropathic pain (gabapentin, pregabalin, carbamazepine)

> *First principles: Pain Physiology and Management — neuropathic pain arises from aberrant neuronal firing in injured or sensitized peripheral/central neurons (ectopic discharges, central sensitization), not from nociceptor activation; it responds poorly to opioids and NSAIDs but responds to agents that reduce neuronal excitability (calcium channel modulators, sodium channel stabilizers)*

- Bipolar disorder (valproate, lamotrigine, carbamazepine) -- see Mood Stabilizers
- Migraine prophylaxis (valproate, topiramate)
- Trigeminal Neuralgia (carbamazepine first-line)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Typical Dose | Monitoring | Key Difference |
|------|-------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Levetiracetam (Keppra)** | 500-1500 mg BID | Generally no levels needed | First-line for many seizure types; few drug interactions; renally cleared; behavioral side effects (irritability, aggression) |
| **Phenytoin (Dilantin)** | Load: 15-20 mg/kg; Maint: 300-400 mg/day | Free phenytoin level (goal 1-2 mcg/mL); total 10-20 mcg/mL | Zero-order kinetics (small dose changes → big level changes); highly protein bound; many drug interactions; gingival hyperplasia |
| **Fosphenytoin (Cerebyx)** | Dosed in PE (phenytoin equivalents) | Same as phenytoin | Prodrug of phenytoin; can give IM and faster IV (150 PE/min vs 50 mg/min); less hypotension and phlebitis |
| **Valproate (Depakote)** | 500-2000 mg/day in divided doses | Trough level 50-100 mcg/mL; LFTs; CBC; ammonia | Broad-spectrum; weight gain; hepatotoxicity (Black Box); pancreatitis; teratogenic (neural tube defects -- Black Box); thrombocytopenia; hyperammonemia |
| **Carbamazepine (Tegretol)** | 400-1200 mg/day in divided doses | Level 4-12 mcg/mL; CBC; LFTs; Na+ | CYP3A4 inducer (many interactions); auto-induces own metabolism; hyponatremia (SIADH); aplastic anemia/agranulocytosis (rare); HLA-B*1502 testing (SJS risk in Asian patients) |
| **Lamotrigine (Lamictal)** | Titrate slowly: start 25 mg/day | Generally no levels needed | Requires VERY slow titration (risk of SJS/TEN -- Black Box); dose depends on concurrent meds (valproate doubles levels); effective for bipolar depression |
| **Gabapentin (Neurontin)** | 300-1200 mg TID | None routine | Primarily for neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia; not effective for most epilepsy monotherapy; renally cleared; dizziness/sedation; increasingly Schedule V in some states (misuse) |
| **Pregabalin (Lyrica)** | 75-300 mg BID | None routine | Similar to gabapentin but more predictable absorption; Schedule V; neuropathic pain, fibromyalgia, anxiety (off-label) |
| **Topiramate (Topamax)** | 100-400 mg/day | Bicarb if symptoms | Cognitive dulling ("Dopamax"); weight LOSS (unique); kidney stones; metabolic acidosis; teratogenic (cleft palate) |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common (class-wide)
- Sedation, dizziness, fatigue
- GI upset (nausea, vomiting)
- Cognitive effects (difficulty concentrating, word-finding)
- Ataxia (especially phenytoin, carbamazepine at high levels)

### Drug-Specific Serious Effects
| Drug | Serious Effect | Details |
|------|---------------|---------|
| Phenytoin | Purple glove syndrome | Extravasation → tissue necrosis; IV only in large veins with saline; NEVER in hand/wrist |
| Phenytoin | Cardiovascular toxicity | Rapid IV infusion → bradycardia, hypotension, cardiac arrest; max rate 50 mg/min; cardiac monitor required |
| Valproate | Hepatotoxicity (Black Box) | Highest risk: children < 2 on polytherapy; monitor LFTs |
| Valproate | Teratogenicity (Black Box) | Neural tube defects (spina bifida); contraindicated in pregnancy if alternatives exist |
| Valproate | Hyperammonemia | Can occur without LFT elevation; check ammonia if altered mental status develops |
| Carbamazepine | SJS/TEN | HLA-B*1502 in patients of Asian descent; test BEFORE starting |
| Carbamazepine | Aplastic anemia/Agranulocytosis | Rare but serious; monitor CBC |
| Lamotrigine | SJS/TEN (Black Box) | Risk highest with rapid titration or concurrent valproate; rash = stop drug and evaluate |
| Topiramate | Kidney stones | Carbonic anhydrase inhibition → alkaline urine; hydrate aggressively |
| Levetiracetam | Behavioral changes | Irritability, aggression, mood swings; sometimes called "Keppra rage" |
sections.contraindications:
- Known hypersensitivity
- Valproate: Pregnancy (if alternatives available); hepatic disease; urea cycle disorders
- Carbamazepine: Bone marrow suppression; concurrent MAOIs; HLA-B*1502 positive
- Phenytoin: Sinus bradycardia, SA block, 2nd/3rd degree AV block
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Document seizure type, frequency, duration, last seizure, current regimen
  - Check drug levels (phenytoin, valproate, carbamazepine) at trough (before AM dose)
  - For phenytoin: check albumin -- correct level in hypoalbuminemia or use FREE level
  - Pregnancy status in women of childbearing age (many ASMs are teratogenic)
- **Administration:**
  - Phenytoin IV: ONLY in normal saline (precipitates in dextrose); max 50 mg/min; cardiac monitor; large-bore IV in large vein
  - Fosphenytoin: Can give in saline or dextrose; up to 150 PE/min; still needs cardiac monitoring
  - Lamotrigine: Follow titration schedule precisely; ANY rash → hold drug and contact provider
  - Never abruptly discontinue ASMs (seizure rebound/status epilepticus)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take consistently at same times daily
  - Do not stop abruptly -- taper under medical supervision
  - Report rash immediately (especially with lamotrigine, carbamazepine, phenytoin)
  - Valproate: Report abdominal pain, jaundice, unusual bruising (hepatotoxicity, pancreatitis, thrombocytopenia)
  - Phenytoin: Meticulous oral hygiene (gingival hyperplasia); avoid alcohol
  - Seizure safety: driving restrictions (state-dependent), swimming precautions, no heights unattended
sections.related-concepts:
- Nervous System
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics

clinicalPearls: **Phenytoin has zero-order kinetics.** Unlike most drugs, small dose increases can cause disproportionately large level increases. Going from 300 mg to 400 mg daily is NOT a 33% increase in level -- it can double or triple it.
**Always use FREE phenytoin levels** in patients with low albumin (ICU, liver disease, nephrotic syndrome, elderly). Total level can look "therapeutic" while free level is toxic.

**"Keppra rage" is real.** Levetiracetam can cause significant irritability and aggression, especially in patients with psychiatric history. Pyridoxine (B6) supplementation may help. Consider brivaracetam as alternative.
**Lamotrigine titration is not optional.** The slow titration exists to prevent Stevens-Johnson Syndrome. If a patient misses doses for > 5 days, you must RESTART the titration from the beginning.
**Valproate + altered mental status = check ammonia.** Hyperammonemic encephalopathy can occur even with normal LFTs and therapeutic drug levels. Treat with lactulose or L-carnitine.

**Status epilepticus protocol:** Benzos first (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV) → if still seizing: fosphenytoin, levetiracetam, or valproate → if refractory: anesthetic agents (propofol, midazolam infusion, pentobarbital) with continuous EEG.

**Drug interactions with phenytoin and carbamazepine are extensive** -- both are major CYP inducers. They reduce the effectiveness of warfarin, oral contraceptives, DOACs, many antibiotics, and other ASMs. Always check interactions.

antifungals.title: Antifungals

sections.mechanism:
- **Azoles (fluconazole, voriconazole, posaconazole, isavuconazole):** Inhibit fungal CYP450 enzyme lanosterol 14α-demethylase → block ergosterol synthesis → disrupted cell membrane integrity. Fungistatic (fungicidal at high doses for some).
- **Polyenes (amphotericin B, nystatin):** Bind directly to ergosterol in fungal cell membrane → create pores → cell lysis. Fungicidal.
- **Echinocandins (caspofungin, micafungin, anidulafungin):** Inhibit β-1,3-glucan synthase → disrupt fungal cell wall synthesis. Fungicidal against Candida; fungistatic against Aspergillus.
sections.indications:
- **Fluconazole:** Candidal infections (mucocutaneous, esophageal, candidemia, UTI); cryptococcal meningitis (maintenance)
- **Voriconazole:** Invasive aspergillosis (first-line); serious fungal infections in immunocompromised
- **Amphotericin B:** Severe/life-threatening systemic fungal infections; cryptococcal meningitis (induction); mucormycosis (first-line)
- **Echinocandins:** Candidemia/invasive candidiasis (first-line for hospitalized patients); esophageal candidiasis (refractory)
- **Nystatin:** Oral thrush (swish and swallow); topical cutaneous candidiasis
- See Invasive Candidiasis for detailed management
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Fluconazole (Diflucan)** | PO/IV | Oropharyngeal: 200 mg x1 then 100 mg daily; Candidemia: 800 mg load then 400 mg daily | Excellent oral bioavailability (PO = IV); renally cleared (dose adjust); good CSF penetration; many CYP450 interactions; NO activity against Aspergillus or Mucor |
| **Voriconazole (Vfend)** | PO/IV | Load 6 mg/kg q12h x2 then 4 mg/kg q12h | First-line invasive aspergillosis; visual disturbances common (transient); hepatotoxic; MANY drug interactions (CYP2C19/3A4); therapeutic drug monitoring required; photosensitivity → skin cancer risk with prolonged use |
| **Posaconazole (Noxafil)** | PO/IV | Prophylaxis: 300 mg BID x1 then 300 mg daily | Broadest azole (Aspergillus, Mucor, Candida); used for prophylaxis in immunocompromised; requires therapeutic drug monitoring |
| **Amphotericin B lipid complex (Abelcet) / liposomal (AmBisome)** | IV | 3-5 mg/kg/day (liposomal) | "The big gun" for life-threatening fungal infections; lipid formulations less nephrotoxic than conventional (deoxycholate); still causes infusion reactions, nephrotoxicity, electrolyte wasting |
| **Caspofungin (Cancidas)** | IV | 70 mg load then 50 mg daily | First-line for candidemia in hemodynamically unstable patients; very well tolerated; NO oral formulation; hepatically metabolized; NO CNS penetration |
| **Micafungin (Mycamine)** | IV | 100-150 mg daily | Similar to caspofungin; Candida prophylaxis in stem cell transplant |
| **Nystatin** | Topical/PO | Oral suspension: 5 mL QID swish and swallow | NOT absorbed systemically; only for mucocutaneous candidiasis; safe but limited |
sections.monitoring:
| Drug | Key Monitoring |
|------|---------------|
| Amphotericin B | BMP daily (Cr, K+, Mg2+) during infusion; CBC; LFTs; pre-medicate for infusion reactions |
| Fluconazole | LFTs baseline and periodically; renal function for dose adjustment |
| Voriconazole | Trough levels (goal 2-5.5 mcg/mL); LFTs; visual symptoms; drug interactions |
| Posaconazole | Trough levels (goal >0.7 mcg/mL for prophylaxis; >1.0 for treatment); LFTs |
| Echinocandins | LFTs (generally well tolerated) |
sections.adverseEffects:
> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — azole antifungals are potent CYP3A4 inhibitors; can dramatically increase levels of co-administered drugs*

| Class | Common | Serious |
|-------|--------|---------|
| **Azoles** | GI upset, headache, rash | Hepatotoxicity (all azoles; monitor LFTs); QTc prolongation; CYP450 interactions (fluconazole inhibits CYP2C9, 3A4); voriconazole: visual disturbances (30%), photosensitivity, fluorosis |
| **Amphotericin B** | Infusion reactions (fever, rigors, chills -- "shake and bake"); nausea | Nephrotoxicity (dose-limiting); hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia (renal wasting); anemia (suppresses EPO); phlebitis |
| **Echinocandins** | Headache, GI upset, phlebitis | Hepatotoxicity (rare); histamine-like reactions (rare) |
sections.contraindications:
- **Azoles:** Concurrent medications with QTc prolongation risk (varies by agent); severe hepatic impairment; voriconazole IV formulation: avoid if CrCl < 50 (cyclodextrin vehicle accumulates)
- **Amphotericin B:** No absolute contraindication when life-threatening fungal infection (benefit outweighs risk); relative: pre-existing renal impairment
- **Echinocandins:** Known hypersensitivity
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Amphotericin B:**
  - Pre-medication protocol: Acetaminophen + diphenhydramine + meperidine (for rigors) 30 min before infusion
  - Normal saline bolus (500 mL-1L) before and after infusion to reduce nephrotoxicity
  - Monitor vitals q15min during first 30 min of infusion, then q30min-1hr
  - Daily BMP: Replace K+ and Mg2+ aggressively (amphotericin causes severe wasting)
  - D5W compatible only (NOT normal saline for conventional amphotericin -- causes precipitation)
- **Azoles:**
  - Drug interaction check is ESSENTIAL (fluconazole and voriconazole interact with warfarin, phenytoin, cyclosporine, statins, benzodiazepines, and many others)
  - Voriconazole: Take 1 hour before or after meals; report visual changes; protect skin from sun
  - Fluconazole: One of the most drug interaction-prone medications -- always verify
- **Echinocandins:**
  - IV only -- no oral formulations available
  - Generally the most well-tolerated systemic antifungal class
  - Cannot treat CNS fungal infections (poor CSF penetration)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Nystatin oral suspension: Swish thoroughly in mouth for 1-2 minutes before swallowing
  - Report jaundice, dark urine, severe fatigue (hepatotoxicity -- all systemic antifungals)
  - Prolonged courses common for invasive fungal infections (weeks to months)
sections.related-concepts:
- Infection and Infectious Disease
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics
- Antibiotics Overview

clinicalPearls: **Echinocandins are first-line for candidemia** in hemodynamically unstable or critically ill patients. Fluconazole is appropriate for stable patients with susceptible Candida species.

**Amphotericin B is "the drug you use when nothing else works."** It's toxic but covers almost everything fungal. Lipid formulations (AmBisome, Abelcet) are MUCH less nephrotoxic than conventional deoxycholate -- always use lipid formulations when available.
**Fluconazole does NOT cover Aspergillus.** This is a critical knowledge gap. An immunocompromised patient with suspected invasive pulmonary aspergillosis needs voriconazole, not fluconazole.

**Echinocandins don't penetrate the CNS or urinary tract.** For fungal meningitis → amphotericin B + flucytosine (induction) then fluconazole (consolidation). For Candida UTI → fluconazole (renally concentrated).
**"Shake and bake" with amphotericin** refers to the classic rigors and fever during infusion. Pre-medication and slow infusion rate help. Meperidine 25 mg IV can abort severe rigors.
**Voriconazole therapeutic drug monitoring is mandatory** for serious infections. Levels < 2 are subtherapeutic; levels > 5.5 are associated with neurotoxicity and hepatotoxicity.

**Azole-drug interactions can be lethal.** Fluconazole dramatically increases warfarin levels (↑ INR), simvastatin levels (rhabdomyolysis), and many others. ALWAYS check interactions when starting an azole.

antiplatelet-agents.title: Antiplatelet Agents

sections.mechanism:
- **Aspirin:** Irreversibly inhibits COX-1, blocking thromboxane A2 (TXA2) synthesis; prevents platelet aggregation for platelet lifespan (~7-10 days)
- **Clopidogrel (Plavix):** Irreversibly blocks P2Y12 ADP receptor on platelets; prodrug requiring CYP2C19 activation
- **Ticagrelor (Brilinta):** Reversibly binds P2Y12 receptor; NOT a prodrug (no CYP2C19 dependency)
- **Prasugrel (Effient):** Irreversibly blocks P2Y12; faster onset and more potent than clopidogrel

> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — antiplatelet agents interrupt the platelet plug: aspirin blocks TXA2-driven activation and shape change; P2Y12 inhibitors block ADP-mediated amplification and GPIIb/IIIa-dependent cross-linking that forms the plug*
sections.indications:
- Acute Coronary Syndromes (ACS) -- DAPT (dual antiplatelet therapy)
- Post-PCI / coronary stenting (DAPT mandatory)
- Secondary prevention of Stroke / TIA (aspirin, clopidogrel)
- Peripheral Arterial Disease (aspirin or clopidogrel)
- Primary prevention: Low-dose ASA only if high CV risk and low bleeding risk (per guidelines, more restrictive now)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Aspirin | PO | ACS: 162-325 mg loading, then 81 mg daily; prevention: 81 mg daily | Irreversible COX-1 inhibition; cheapest; cornerstone of DAPT |
| Clopidogrel | PO | 300-600 mg loading, then 75 mg daily | Prodrug; ~2-14% are CYP2C19 poor metabolizers (reduced efficacy); avoid with omeprazole |
| Ticagrelor | PO | 180 mg loading, then 90 mg BID | Reversible; aspirin dose MUST be <= 100 mg/day with ticagrelor; causes dyspnea |
| Prasugrel | PO | 60 mg loading, then 10 mg daily (5 mg if < 60 kg) | Most potent; contraindicated if prior TIA/stroke; avoid if >= 75 yo (bleeding risk) |
sections.dapt-protocols:
- **Post-ACS (medical management):** ASA + P2Y12 inhibitor for 12 months minimum
- **Post-PCI with DES (drug-eluting stent):** ASA + P2Y12 inhibitor for 6-12 months (can shorten to 3-6 months if high bleeding risk)
- **Post-PCI with BMS (bare-metal stent):** ASA + P2Y12 inhibitor for minimum 1 month
- **Preferred P2Y12 in ACS:** Ticagrelor or prasugrel over clopidogrel (stronger evidence)
- ASA is continued indefinitely; P2Y12 inhibitor is time-limited
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Aspirin:** Onset 15-30 min (chewed); platelet effect lasts ~7-10 days (platelet lifespan)
- **Clopidogrel:** Onset 2-6 hr (loading dose speeds this); active metabolite via CYP2C19
- **Ticagrelor:** Onset ~30 min; reversible binding (platelets recover faster after discontinuation, ~3-5 days)
- **Prasugrel:** Onset 30 min; faster and more consistent than clopidogrel
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Bleeding (all agents) -- bruising, epistaxis, GI bleeding, prolonged bleeding from cuts
- GI upset, dyspepsia (aspirin)
- Ticagrelor-specific: Dyspnea (benign, ~14%), ventricular pauses, hyperuricemia
- Headache (ticagrelor)

### Serious/Black Box
- **Ticagrelor (Black Box):** Do NOT use aspirin > 100 mg/day (reduces ticagrelor effectiveness); maintenance ASA must be 81 mg
- **Prasugrel (Black Box):** Generally not recommended for age >= 75 or weight < 60 kg (increased fatal bleeding); CONTRAINDICATED with prior TIA/stroke
- **Clopidogrel (Black Box):** Reduced effectiveness in CYP2C19 poor metabolizers; consider genetic testing or alternative agent
- Major hemorrhage, intracranial hemorrhage (all agents)
sections.contraindications:
- Active pathologic bleeding (PUD, ICH)
- Prasugrel: Prior TIA or stroke (Black Box)
- Aspirin: True aspirin allergy; children with viral illness (Reye syndrome)
- Severe hepatic impairment (ticagrelor)
sections.perioperative-management:
- **Elective surgery:** Hold clopidogrel/prasugrel 5-7 days before; hold ticagrelor 3-5 days before
- **ASA:** Often continued perioperatively for cardiac patients (surgeon and cardiologist should discuss)
- **Urgent surgery on DAPT:** Platelet transfusion may be needed; balance bleeding vs stent thrombosis risk
- **Post-stent patients:** NEVER discontinue DAPT prematurely without cardiology consultation -- stent thrombosis can be fatal
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - CBC/platelets at baseline
  - Signs of bleeding: stool guaiac, bruising, petechiae, gum bleeding, hematuria
  - Hemoglobin/hematocrit if bleeding suspected
  - No routine lab monitoring (unlike anticoagulants)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Do not stop without provider guidance (especially post-stent)
  - Avoid NSAIDs (increased bleeding risk); use acetaminophen for pain
  - Report: Black/tarry stools, blood in urine, excessive bruising, prolonged bleeding
  - Ticagrelor: Take BID; mild dyspnea is expected and usually resolves
  - Dental work: Inform dentist of antiplatelet therapy
- **Administration Notes:**
  - ACS loading dose: Have patient CHEW aspirin (faster absorption from buccal mucosa)
  - Clopidogrel: Avoid omeprazole/esomeprazole (CYP2C19 inhibition reduces efficacy); pantoprazole OK
  - Ticagrelor: Can be crushed and mixed with water for NG administration
  - Prasugrel: Only initiate after coronary anatomy known (catheterization)

clinicalPearls: **Stent thrombosis** from premature DAPT discontinuation has 20-40% mortality -- reinforce adherence
Clopidogrel + PPI: Use pantoprazole (not omeprazole) -- clinically significant CYP2C19 interaction
CYP2C19 genetic testing increasingly available; poor metabolizers should get ticagrelor or prasugrel instead of clopidogrel
Ticagrelor dyspnea: Adenosine-mediated; usually self-limiting; discontinue only if intolerable
Aspirin desensitization protocols exist for patients with ASA allergy who need antiplatelet therapy (e.g., pre-PCI)
**Hold parameters:** Hold and notify if active bleeding, platelet count < 100K, or pre-procedure per surgical/cardiology guidance

antipsychotics.title: Antipsychotics

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

- **First-generation antipsychotics (FGAs/typicals):** Primarily block dopamine D2 receptors in mesolimbic pathway → reduce positive symptoms (hallucinations, delusions, disorganized thinking). Also block D2 in other pathways → side effects (EPS, prolactin elevation).
- **Second-generation antipsychotics (SGAs/atypicals):** Block both dopamine D2 AND serotonin 5-HT2A receptors → treat positive symptoms with lower EPS risk; partial effectiveness for negative symptoms; also bind histamine, alpha-1, muscarinic receptors (metabolic side effects).

### Dopamine Pathways and Clinical Effects
| Pathway | Function | D2 Blockade Effect |
|---------|----------|-------------------|
| **Mesolimbic** | Reward, motivation | Therapeutic: reduces positive symptoms |
| **Mesocortical** | Cognition, executive function | Worsens negative/cognitive symptoms |
| **Nigrostriatal** | Motor control | EPS: dystonia, akathisia, parkinsonism, tardive dyskinesia |
| **Tuberoinfundibular** | Prolactin regulation | Hyperprolactinemia: galactorrhea, amenorrhea, gynecomastia, sexual dysfunction |
sections.indications:
- Schizophrenia (positive and negative symptoms)
- Acute psychosis / agitation (any cause)
- Bipolar mania (olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, risperidone)
- Bipolar depression (quetiapine, lurasidone, cariprazine)
- Treatment-resistant depression (augmentation with aripiprazole, quetiapine, brexpiprazole)
- Delirium-associated agitation (haloperidol -- though evidence mixed; avoid in Parkinson's/LBD)
- Tourette syndrome (haloperidol, aripiprazole)
- Nausea/vomiting (prochlorperazine, haloperidol -- see Antiemetics)
- ICU agitation (haloperidol, quetiapine)
sections.keyDrugs:
### First-Generation (Typical)
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Potency | Key Points |
|------|-------|-------------|---------|-----------|
| **Haloperidol (Haldol)** | PO/IM/IV | Acute: 2-10 mg IM q4-8h; Maint: 5-20 mg/day PO | High | Most commonly used FGA; high EPS risk; low sedation; QTc prolongation (especially IV); decanoate form for long-acting IM (monthly) |
| **Chlorpromazine (Thorazine)** | PO/IM | 200-800 mg/day divided | Low | More sedating; more anticholinergic; orthostatic hypotension; photosensitivity; historical significance (first antipsychotic) |
| **Fluphenazine** | PO/IM | 5-20 mg/day; Decanoate: q2-4 weeks | High | Long-acting IM injection available (adherence) |

### Second-Generation (Atypical)
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Points |
|------|-------|-------------|-----------|
| **Olanzapine (Zyprexa)** | PO/IM | 10-20 mg/day | Effective but significant metabolic effects (weight gain, diabetes, dyslipidemia); very sedating; IM for acute agitation; "Zyprexa Zydis" ODT for patients who refuse PO |
| **Quetiapine (Seroquel)** | PO | 150-800 mg/day (divided or XR) | Very sedating (used off-label for insomnia at low doses 25-100 mg); metabolic effects; diabetic ketoacidosis risk; lower EPS; good for bipolar depression |
| **Risperidone (Risperdal)** | PO/IM | 2-8 mg/day; Risperdal Consta q2 weeks IM | More EPS than other SGAs at higher doses; highest prolactin elevation of SGAs; long-acting injectable available |
| **Aripiprazole (Abilify)** | PO/IM | 10-30 mg/day; Abilify Maintena monthly IM | Partial D2 agonist (unique); lower metabolic effects; less sedation; akathisia common; augmentation for depression |
| **Ziprasidone (Geodon)** | PO/IM | 40-80 mg BID (with food) | Most QTc prolongation of SGAs; least weight gain; MUST take with food (500 kcal for absorption); IM for acute agitation |
| **Lurasidone (Latuda)** | PO | 40-160 mg daily (with food 350 kcal) | FDA-approved for bipolar depression; lower metabolic effects; MUST take with food |
| **Clozapine (Clozaril)** | PO | 300-900 mg/day (slow titration) | ONLY for treatment-resistant schizophrenia; most effective antipsychotic; reduces suicidality; REMS program due to agranulocytosis risk (1-2%); mandatory ANC monitoring; metabolic syndrome; myocarditis; seizures; constipation → ileus |
sections.monitoring:
| Parameter | Frequency | Drugs |
|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Fasting glucose, A1C, lipid panel** | Baseline, 3 months, then annually | All SGAs (especially olanzapine, clozapine, quetiapine) |
| **Weight/BMI/Waist circumference** | Baseline, monthly x3, then quarterly | All antipsychotics |
| **ECG/QTc** | Baseline and PRN | Haloperidol (especially IV), ziprasidone, thioridazine |
| **ANC (absolute neutrophil count)** | Weekly x6 months, biweekly x6 months, then monthly | **Clozapine ONLY** -- REMS requirement; ANC < 1500 = hold; < 1000 = discontinue |
| **Prolactin** | If symptomatic | Risperidone (highest risk), haloperidol, all FGAs |
| **Abnormal Involuntary Movement Scale (AIMS)** | q6-12 months | All antipsychotics (tardive dyskinesia screening) |
| **LFTs** | Baseline, periodically | Clozapine, olanzapine |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Extrapyramidal Symptoms (EPS) — Higher Risk with FGAs
| Type | Timing | Presentation | Treatment |
|------|--------|-------------|-----------|
| **Acute dystonia** | Hours to days | Sustained muscle contraction (torticollis, oculogyric crisis, laryngospasm) | Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV/IM or benztropine 1-2 mg IV/IM; rapid response |
| **Akathisia** | Days to weeks | Subjective restlessness; inability to sit still; pacing | Reduce dose; beta-blocker (propranolol); benzodiazepine; often misdiagnosed as psychotic agitation |
| **Parkinsonism** | Weeks to months | Bradykinesia, rigidity, tremor, shuffling gait, masked facies | Reduce dose; benztropine or trihexyphenidyl; switch to lower-EPS agent |
| **Tardive dyskinesia (TD)** | Months to years | Involuntary repetitive movements (lip smacking, tongue protrusion, choreiform limb movements) | May be irreversible; valbenazine (Ingrezza) or deutetrabenazine (Austedo) FDA-approved for TD; switch to clozapine or quetiapine |

### Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)
- **Life-threatening emergency** (mortality 5-20%)
- **FALTER mnemonic:** Fever (>104°F/40°C), Autonomic instability, Leukocytosis, Tremor/rigidity (lead-pipe), Elevated CK (rhabdomyolysis), Reduced mental status
- Onset: days to weeks after starting or dose increase
- **Treatment:** STOP antipsychotic immediately; supportive care (cooling, hydration); dantrolene (muscle relaxant); bromocriptine (dopamine agonist); ICU admission
- Distinguish from serotonin syndrome: NMS = lead-pipe rigidity with HYPOreflexia; serotonin syndrome = clonus with HYPERreflexia

### Metabolic Syndrome
- Weight gain, hyperglycemia/diabetes, dyslipidemia
- Highest risk: Clozapine > olanzapine > quetiapine > risperidone > others
- Can develop DKA even without prior diabetes diagnosis (Black Box for all SGAs)
- Monitor: fasting glucose, A1C, lipids, weight, waist circumference
sections.black-box-warnings:
- **Elderly dementia-related psychosis:** Increased mortality (all antipsychotics); NOT FDA-approved for dementia behavioral symptoms
- **Metabolic effects / DKA** (SGAs): Hyperglycemia, diabetes
- **Clozapine:** Agranulocytosis, seizures, myocarditis, cardiovascular effects
sections.contraindications:
- **Haloperidol IV:** QTc > 500 ms
- **Clozapine:** History of clozapine-induced agranulocytosis; uncontrolled seizure disorder; severe neutropenia
- **All antipsychotics:** Parkinson's disease / Lewy body dementia (extreme sensitivity to D2 blockade → severe parkinsonism, NMS); exception: quetiapine and clozapine may be used with extreme caution
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Baseline AIMS exam and repeat q6-12 months (tardive dyskinesia screening)
  - Metabolic panel, fasting glucose, lipids, weight/BMI at baseline
  - Fall risk assessment (orthostatic hypotension, sedation)
  - Temperature monitoring (NMS risk; also impaired thermoregulation in heat)
  - Mental status and symptom assessment using validated tools
- **Administration:**
  - IM olanzapine + IM benzodiazepine: Do NOT give within 1 hour of each other (respiratory depression/death risk)
  - Clozapine: Titrate slowly (risk of orthostatic hypotension, seizures); ensure REMS enrollment and ANC monitoring
  - Long-acting injectables (LAIs): Verify correct injection site and technique per manufacturer; Z-track for oil-based formulations
  - Ziprasidone and lurasidone: MUST be taken with food (significant absorption difference)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Rise slowly from sitting/lying (orthostatic hypotension)
  - Report involuntary movements (tongue, face, limbs) immediately
  - Avoid overheating (antipsychotics impair thermoregulation → heat stroke risk)
  - Metabolic monitoring is essential -- diet, exercise, weight management
  - Do not stop abruptly (withdrawal psychosis, rebound symptoms)
  - Clozapine patients: Report fever, sore throat, flu-like symptoms immediately (agranulocytosis)
sections.related-concepts:
- Nervous System
- Antidepressants
- Mood Stabilizers
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics

clinicalPearls: **Akathisia is frequently misdiagnosed as worsening psychosis.** The patient appears agitated and restless → the clinician increases the antipsychotic → akathisia worsens → vicious cycle. Always assess for akathisia before escalating antipsychotic doses. Propranolol helps.

**NMS vs. Serotonin Syndrome:** Both are emergencies with altered mental status and autonomic instability. **NMS = lead-pipe rigidity + HYPOreflexia + very high CK.** **Serotonin syndrome = clonus + HYPERreflexia + diarrhea.** Treatment is completely different.

**Clozapine is the MOST effective antipsychotic** but used last due to monitoring burden and agranulocytosis risk. Treatment-resistant schizophrenia patients should not be denied clozapine because of perceived complexity -- it saves lives and reduces suicidality.

**Never give antipsychotics in Lewy body dementia** without extreme caution. These patients have profound sensitivity to D2 blockade -- even low doses can cause severe parkinsonism, NMS, or death. Quetiapine is safest if absolutely necessary.
**IM olanzapine + IM lorazepam = respiratory arrest.** The interaction is well-documented. Wait at least 1 hour between these two drugs. This is an ISMP high-alert combination.

**Tardive dyskinesia may be irreversible.** AIMS screening every 6-12 months is the early warning system. Newer VMAT2 inhibitors (valbenazine, deutetrabenazine) offer treatment options, but prevention through lowest effective dose and regular screening is key.
**QTc prolongation with haloperidol IV** can cause Torsades de Pointes. Always check baseline ECG before IV haloperidol. Many facilities now require telemetry monitoring. Have IV magnesium available.

**Metabolic syndrome from SGAs is the leading cause of premature death** in patients with schizophrenia (cardiovascular disease). Metabolic monitoring and lifestyle interventions should be as much a part of treatment as the medication itself.

antivenoms-and-antitoxins.title: Antivenoms and Antitoxins

sections.definition:
Antivenoms and antitoxins are biological products derived from immunized animal sera (equine or ovine) that contain antibodies capable of neutralizing specific venoms or toxins. In the United States, the most commonly encountered envenomations are **pit viper bites** (rattlesnakes, copperheads, water moccasins) and **black widow spider bites**. These agents are time-sensitive — envenomation is a progressive process, and delayed antivenom administration leads to worse outcomes.

Nursing priorities: early recognition of envenomation severity, serial assessment and documentation, preparation for anaphylaxis, and coordination with **Poison Control (1-800-222-1222)**.
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — Antivenom is passive immunization: the antibodies doing the neutralizing were generated by an immunized horse or sheep, not the patient; IgG antibodies (or Fab/F(ab')2 fragments) bind venom antigens with high specificity — this is humoral adaptive immunity harvested from one organism and transferred to another to confer immediate protection*

### Antivenom Antibodies
- Animal (horse or sheep) is immunized with sublethal doses of venom over months → develops antibodies
- Serum is harvested and processed:
  - **Fab fragments** (CroFab): ovine-derived, smaller fragments with shorter half-life; better tissue penetration
  - **F(ab')2 fragments** (Anavip): equine-derived, larger fragments with longer half-life; less frequent dosing
  - **Whole IgG** (older products, Latrodectus antivenom): higher anaphylaxis risk
- Antibodies bind circulating venom components → neutralize enzymatic and cytotoxic activity
- Cannot reverse tissue already destroyed — must be given EARLY before venom distributes into tissues

### Venom Pathophysiology
- **Pit viper venom:** complex mixture of metalloproteinases, phospholipases, serine proteases → causes local tissue destruction, coagulopathy (consumptive), thrombocytopenia, systemic hemorrhage
- **Black widow venom (alpha-latrotoxin):** causes massive neurotransmitter release at nerve terminals → severe muscle spasm, autonomic instability, abdominal rigidity mimicking acute abdomen
- **Coral snake venom:** primarily neurotoxic → progressive descending paralysis, respiratory failure (delayed onset — may appear hours after bite)
sections.key-agents:
| Drug | Dose / Route | Onset / Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **CroFab** (Crotalidae Polyvalent Immune Fab — ovine) | Initial: **4–6 vials** IV over 60 min; repeat 4–6 vials q1h up to 3 times until control achieved; Maintenance: **2 vials q6h × 3 doses** (18h) | Onset 1–4 h; Fab half-life ~15 h | Shorter half-life → recurrence of coagulopathy in 30–50% of cases; recheck coags at 48h and 7d |
| **Anavip** (Crotalidae Immune F(ab')2 — equine) | Initial: **10 vials** IV; repeat 10 vials if not controlled at 1h; Maintenance: **4 vials at 6, 12, 18h** | Onset 1–4 h; F(ab')2 half-life ~130 h | Longer half-life = less coagulopathy recurrence; now preferred by many toxicologists |
| **Latrodectus antivenom** (Black Widow — equine whole IgG) | **1 vial** IV diluted in 50–100 mL NS over 60 min | Onset 30–60 min; duration 24–48 h | Reserved for SEVERE cases (respiratory distress, refractory hypertension, seizures, pregnancy); HIGH anaphylaxis risk — equine whole IgG |
| **North American Coral Snake Antivenom** (equine) | 3–5 vials IV initially; may need 10+ | Onset variable; give early before symptom onset | EXTREMELY limited supply; bite marks often invisible; treat empirically if confirmed coral snake bite |
| **Botulism antitoxin (BAT)** — heptavalent | 1 vial IV; obtain from CDC via state health dept | Does not reverse existing paralysis; prevents progression | Contact CDC 24/7 Emergency Operations: 770-488-7100 |
| **Diphtheria antitoxin (DAT)** | Dose varies by severity; obtain from CDC | Prevents progression of toxin-mediated damage | Equine-derived; skin test for horse serum sensitivity first |
sections.clinical-indications:
### Pit Viper Envenomation — When to Give Antivenom
- **Progressive local swelling** crossing a joint or involving >1 body segment
- **Coagulopathy:** INR >1.5, fibrinogen <150, platelets <150K, elevated D-dimer
- **Systemic signs:** hypotension, altered mental status, respiratory distress
- **Rapidly progressing** symptoms despite observation
- **Note:** Not all pit viper bites envenomate — "dry bites" occur in 20–25% of cases
- ALL pit viper bites require **minimum 8–12 hours of observation** regardless of initial presentation

### Black Widow Spider Envenomation
- Most cases managed with supportive care: opioids for pain, benzodiazepines for muscle spasm
- Antivenom reserved for: **severe uncontrolled hypertension, respiratory compromise, seizures, pregnancy, pediatric patients, elderly with cardiac comorbidities**
- Calcium gluconate is NOT effective (historical myth) — benzodiazepines and opioids are first-line analgesics

### Coral Snake Envenomation
- **Treat empirically** if confirmed coral snake bite — do NOT wait for symptoms
- Neurotoxicity onset is delayed (hours) but may progress rapidly to respiratory failure
- "Red on yellow, kill a fellow" — Eastern coral snake (Micrurus fulvius) identification
- Observe for minimum **24 hours** even if asymptomatic

### Botulism
- Descending symmetric paralysis (cranial nerves first: diplopia, dysphagia, dysarthria)
- Foodborne, wound, or infant botulism
- Antitoxin does NOT reverse existing paralysis — prevents progression; early administration critical
- For infant botulism: use **BabyBIG** (human-derived botulism immune globulin), NOT equine BAT
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### Acute Infusion Reactions / Anaphylaxis
- Risk highest with **whole IgG products** (Latrodectus antivenom): up to 10–15% anaphylaxis rate
- Fab and F(ab')2 products have lower risk (~5–10% mild reactions, <1% severe anaphylaxis)
- Symptoms: urticaria, bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema, cardiovascular collapse
- **Have at bedside BEFORE starting infusion:**
  - Epinephrine 1:1000 (0.3–0.5 mg IM)
  - Diphenhydramine 50 mg IV
  - Methylprednisolone 125 mg IV
  - Albuterol nebulizer
  - Airway equipment
- If reaction occurs: STOP infusion → epinephrine → supportive care → may restart at slower rate once stabilized (risk-benefit with toxicologist)

### Serum Sickness (Delayed Hypersensitivity)
- Occurs **7–21 days** after antivenom administration
- Type III hypersensitivity: immune complex deposition
- Symptoms: fever, arthralgias, urticaria, lymphadenopathy, malaise
- More common with larger doses and equine-derived products
- Treatment: **prednisone 1 mg/kg/day × 5–7 days** + antihistamines (diphenhydramine or cetirizine)
- Counsel ALL patients about serum sickness at discharge — provide written instructions

### Coagulopathy Recurrence (CroFab-specific)
- Fab fragments have short half-life (~15 hours) vs venom components that persist in tissue
- Venom re-enters circulation after Fab cleared → **recurrent coagulopathy** in 30–50%
- Recheck **CBC, INR, fibrinogen at 48 hours and 5–7 days** post-discharge
- Anavip has longer half-life (~130 hours) → significantly less recurrence
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Snakebite Assessment and Documentation
- **Mark the leading edge** of swelling with a pen and **time-stamp** it at presentation and q15–30 min
- Measure **circumference** at consistent anatomical landmarks (e.g., 10 cm proximal to bite) q15–30 min
- Document progression or stabilization — this drives antivenom decisions
- Remove all rings, watches, constricting clothing/jewelry from affected extremity IMMEDIATELY
- Immobilize the affected extremity at or below heart level
- **Do NOT:** apply tourniquet, ice, incision, suction, or electrical stimulation (all harmful and ineffective)

### Pre-Treatment Checklist
- Establish **two large-bore IVs** (at least 18G) — one for antivenom, one for resuscitation
- Draw baseline labs: **CBC, CMP, PT/INR, fibrinogen, D-dimer, type and screen, CK, lactate**
- Ensure **anaphylaxis kit** at bedside (see above)
- Confirm **tetanus** status — update if >5 years since last booster
- Contact **Poison Control: 1-800-222-1222** — they will guide dosing and help coordinate antivenom supply
- Obtain informed consent (if time permits) — explain anaphylaxis risk, serum sickness

### Antivenom Reconstitution and Administration
- **CroFab:** Reconstitute each vial with 25 mL NS; gently swirl (do NOT shake — proteins denature); pool into 250 mL NS; infuse over 60 min; start slowly for first 10 min
- **Anavip:** Reconstitute each vial with 10 mL NS; pool into 250 mL NS; infuse over 60 min
- **Latrodectus antivenom:** Dilute 1 vial in 50–100 mL NS; infuse over 60 min with close monitoring
- ALL antivenoms: **start infusion slowly** (25–50 mL/hr for first 10 min) → if no reaction, increase to complete over 60 min
- Observe patient continuously during infusion; vitals q5 min for first 15 min, then q15 min

### Compartment Syndrome vs Envenomation Swelling
- Pit viper envenomation causes massive subcutaneous edema that mimics compartment syndrome
- **True compartment syndrome from snakebite is RARE** (<1%)
- Measuring compartment pressures before fasciotomy is essential — most "compartment syndrome" is just envenomation edema that responds to antivenom
- Give MORE antivenom before considering fasciotomy
- If pressures truly elevated (>30 mmHg or within 30 of diastolic): consult surgery + toxicology
- Unnecessary fasciotomy in envenomated tissue → catastrophic bleeding due to coagulopathy

### Discharge Planning
- Pit viper: minimum **8–12 hours** observation even if asymptomatic
- If antivenom given: observe until coags stabilized, swelling controlled
- Discharge instructions must include:
  - **Return for:** worsening swelling, new bleeding, bruising, dark urine, fever, joint pain, rash
  - **Serum sickness warning:** fever, joint pain, rash at 7–21 days → return to ED or call PCP
  - **Follow-up labs:** CBC, INR, fibrinogen at **48 hours and 5–7 days** (especially CroFab)
  - Wound care: keep clean and dry, watch for infection, follow up with wound check in 48–72h
  - Activity restrictions: limit use of affected extremity × 1–2 weeks
  - **No NSAIDs or aspirin** until coagulopathy fully resolved
sections.drug-interactions:
| Interaction | Clinical Significance |
|------------|----------------------|
| Anticoagulants (heparin, warfarin, DOACs) | Hold during active envenomation — venom already causes coagulopathy; anticoagulants worsen bleeding |
| Antiplatelets (aspirin, clopidogrel) | Avoid until coagulopathy resolved — increased hemorrhagic risk |
| NSAIDs | Avoid — antiplatelet effect + GI bleeding risk in coagulopathic patient |
| ACE inhibitors | May worsen hypotension from envenomation; hold if BP low |
| Beta-blockers | May blunt tachycardia response to hypovolemia; masks compensatory signs |
| Blood products (FFP, cryo, platelets) | Venom consumes clotting factors faster than replacement — give antivenom FIRST; blood products only as bridge for life-threatening hemorrhage |
sections.keyPoints:
- **Call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222)** for EVERY envenomation — they are the experts and will co-manage in real time
- CroFab and Anavip are both FDA-approved for pit viper envenomation; Anavip has longer half-life with less coagulopathy recurrence
- Black widow antivenom is reserved for severe cases only due to high anaphylaxis risk
- Coral snake bites: treat empirically — do NOT wait for symptoms; neurotoxicity onset is delayed but can be fatal
- Serial swelling assessment (leading edge + circumference) is the most important bedside nursing intervention
- True compartment syndrome from snakebite is extremely rare — antivenom, not fasciotomy, is the treatment
- ALL patients receiving antivenom need serum sickness counseling and follow-up labs

clinicalPearls: **"Antivenom first, blood products never"** — giving FFP or platelets into a patient with circulating venom is like pouring water into a bucket with a hole. The venom will consume whatever you put in. Give antivenom to neutralize the venom, THEN replace factors if still deficient.

**Mark the edge and write the time** — a Sharpie and a clock are your most important snakebite assessment tools. Serial leading-edge markings with timestamps create an objective record of progression or stabilization that drives every treatment decision.

**The copperhead paradox** — copperhead bites are the most common pit viper envenomation in the US but are usually mild. Many toxicologists recommend observation without antivenom for copperhead bites unless there is coagulopathy, progressive swelling past the elbow/knee, or systemic signs. The decision is nuanced — call Poison Control.

**Serum sickness is the price of admission** — patients who receive antivenom need to know that in 1–3 weeks they may develop fever, joint pain, and rash. This is immune-complex mediated, not a new emergency. A short course of prednisone and antihistamines resolves it. Pre-printed discharge instructions save confusion.

**"It's not compartment syndrome until you prove it with pressures"** — the swelling from pit viper envenomation looks terrifying but is almost always subcutaneous edema, not true intracompartmental hypertension. Fasciotomy through envenomated, coagulopathic tissue is a disaster. Give more antivenom and measure pressures before going to the OR.

**Coral snakes are sneaky** — the bite may be painless with minimal local findings. The patient may look fine for hours. Then progressive bulbar weakness begins — ptosis, diplopia, dysphagia, respiratory failure. If a coral snake bite is confirmed, give antivenom immediately and prepare for intubation.

**The dry bite dilemma** — 20–25% of pit viper strikes inject no venom. But you cannot determine this at presentation. Every patient with confirmed pit viper bite gets the full workup and minimum 8–12 hours observation. "Dry bite" is a retrospective diagnosis.

antivirals.title: Antivirals

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — Antivirals exploit the viral replication cycle; nucleoside analogs (acyclovir, tenofovir) act as fraudulent DNA building blocks, terminating chain elongation during viral DNA synthesis*
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — Viral resistance emerges through the same principles as bacterial resistance; HIV reverse transcriptase and protease rapidly acquire resistance mutations under selective drug pressure, which is why ART requires combination therapy targeting multiple steps*

Varies significantly by target virus:
- **Nucleoside/nucleotide analogs (acyclovir, valacyclovir, ganciclovir, tenofovir):** Incorporate into viral DNA during replication → chain termination; require viral thymidine kinase for activation (acyclovir) → selective for infected cells
- **Neuraminidase inhibitors (oseltamivir, zanamivir):** Block neuraminidase enzyme on influenza virus surface → prevent viral release from infected cells → limit spread
- **Protease inhibitors (ritonavir, atazanavir):** Block HIV protease → prevent viral polyprotein cleavage → immature, non-infectious viral particles
- **Integrase inhibitors (dolutegravir, bictegravir):** Block HIV integrase → prevent viral DNA integration into host genome. Backbone of modern HIV regimens.
- **NNRTIs (efavirenz, rilpivirine, doravirine):** Bind directly to HIV reverse transcriptase at non-active site → conformational change → inhibit function
- **Direct-acting antivirals for HCV (sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, glecaprevir/pibrentasvir):** Target specific HCV proteins (NS5B polymerase, NS5A, NS3/4A protease) → cure rates >95%
sections.key-drugs-by-indication:
### Herpes Viruses (HSV, VZV, CMV)
| Drug | Route/Dose | Indication | Key Points |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Acyclovir (Zovirax)** | IV: 10 mg/kg q8h; PO: 400-800 mg 3-5x/day | HSV encephalitis (IV), HSV/VZV infections | Requires adequate hydration (crystalline nephrotoxicity); dose adjust for renal function; IV over 1 hr minimum |
| **Valacyclovir (Valtrex)** | PO: 500 mg-1g BID-TID | HSV (genital, orolabial), VZV (shingles) | Prodrug of acyclovir; better oral bioavailability → less frequent dosing; preferred PO agent |
| **Ganciclovir / Valganciclovir** | IV: 5 mg/kg q12h; PO: 900 mg BID | CMV (retinitis, prophylaxis in transplant) | Major toxicity: myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia); monitor CBC 2x/week; teratogenic |

### Influenza
| Drug | Route/Dose | Indication | Key Points |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Oseltamivir (Tamiflu)** | PO: 75 mg BID x 5 days | Influenza A and B treatment and prophylaxis | Most effective within 48 hr of symptom onset; still give even if >48 hr in hospitalized/high-risk patients; nausea common; renally cleared |
| **Baloxavir (Xofluza)** | PO: 40-80 mg single dose | Influenza A and B | Single-dose convenience; cap-dependent endonuclease inhibitor; avoid with dairy/calcium products |

### HIV (see HIV for comprehensive management)
Modern HIV treatment = ART (antiretroviral therapy) using combination regimens:

| Regimen Component | Common Drugs | Key Points |
|-------------------|-------------|-----------|
| **NRTI backbone** | Tenofovir (TAF or TDF) + emtricitabine (Truvada/Descovy) | TAF = less renal/bone toxicity than TDF; emtricitabine also active against HBV |
| **Integrase inhibitor (preferred 3rd agent)** | Dolutegravir, bictegravir | High barrier to resistance; well tolerated; first-line in most guidelines |
| **Common STRs (single-tablet regimens)** | Biktarvy (bictegravir/TAF/FTC); Triumeq (dolutegravir/abacavir/3TC) | One pill, once daily → maximizes adherence |

> **U=U: Undetectable = Untransmittable.** Patients with sustained viral suppression (<200 copies/mL) have effectively no risk of sexually transmitting HIV.

### Hepatitis B
| Drug | Key Points |
|------|-----------|
| **Tenofovir (TDF or TAF)** | First-line for chronic HBV; also used in HIV (dual activity) |
| **Entecavir** | First-line alternative; high barrier to resistance; dose adjust in renal impairment |

> **Critical:** Stopping HBV antivirals can cause severe hepatitis flares. Monitor closely if discontinuing.

### Hepatitis C
| Regimen | Key Points |
|---------|-----------|
| **Sofosbuvir/Ledipasvir (Harvoni)** | Genotype 1; 8-12 weeks; cure rate >95% |
| **Glecaprevir/Pibrentasvir (Mavyret)** | Pan-genotypic; 8-16 weeks; cure rate >95% |
| **Sofosbuvir/Velpatasvir (Epclusa)** | Pan-genotypic; 12 weeks; cure rate >95% |

> HCV is now a CURABLE disease with DAAs. SVR (sustained virologic response) at 12 weeks = cure.
sections.monitoring:
| Drug/Class | Key Monitoring |
|-----------|---------------|
| Acyclovir IV | BUN/Cr (nephrotoxicity); hydration status; infusion site |
| Ganciclovir | CBC 2x/week (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia); renal function |
| Oseltamivir | Renal function for dose adjustment; GI tolerance |
| HIV ART | HIV viral load + CD4 count (q3-6 months); renal function, LFTs, lipids, HgbA1C; resistance testing at baseline |
| HCV DAAs | HCV viral load at baseline, week 4, end of treatment, and 12 weeks post-treatment (SVR12 = cure); LFTs; HBV coinfection screening |
| HBV antivirals | HBV DNA, LFTs q3-6 months; HBsAg; renal function (tenofovir); do NOT abruptly stop |
sections.adverseEffects:
| Drug | Common | Serious |
|------|--------|---------|
| Acyclovir/Valacyclovir | Nausea, headache | Crystalline nephrotoxicity (IV -- hydrate aggressively); neurotoxicity (confusion, tremors) in renal impairment |
| Ganciclovir | GI upset | Myelosuppression (neutropenia -- may need G-CSF); teratogenic; carcinogenic |
| Oseltamivir | Nausea, vomiting | Rare neuropsychiatric events (more reported in pediatrics) |
| HIV ART (general) | GI upset, headache | Varies by class: TDF → nephrotoxicity, bone loss; abacavir → hypersensitivity (HLA-B*5701 testing required); efavirenz → CNS/psych effects; PIs → metabolic syndrome |
| HCV DAAs | Fatigue, headache, nausea | HBV reactivation (Black Box -- screen ALL patients for HBV before starting HCV treatment); bradycardia with amiodarone (sofosbuvir) |
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Acyclovir IV:**
  - Infuse over minimum 1 hour (rapid infusion → crystalline nephrotoxicity)
  - Ensure adequate hydration: 1-2 L fluid before and during infusion
  - Dose adjust for renal function (common error)
  - Monitor urine output; report oliguria
- **Ganciclovir/Valganciclovir:**
  - Handle with cytotoxic precautions (teratogenic, carcinogenic)
  - Monitor CBC -- hold if ANC < 500 or platelets < 25,000
  - Do NOT substitute valganciclovir for valacyclovir (name confusion → serious medication error)
- **HIV ART:**
  - Adherence is CRITICAL -- missing doses promotes resistance
  - Screen for HLA-B*5701 before abacavir (hypersensitivity reaction can be fatal)
  - Many drug interactions -- always check before adding medications
  - Assess for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) in newly treated patients
  - PrEP (pre-exposure prophylaxis): Truvada or Descovy for HIV-negative individuals at high risk
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Antivirals do NOT cure HSV or HIV -- they suppress viral replication
  - HCV DAAs DO cure hepatitis C (>95% cure rate)
  - Take all medications as prescribed; do not skip doses (especially HIV ART)
  - Report fever, sore throat, unusual bleeding (ganciclovir -- myelosuppression)
  - Oseltamivir: Start within 48 hours of flu symptoms for maximum benefit; take with food to reduce nausea
sections.related-concepts:
- Infection and Infectious Disease
- Inflammation and Immune Response
- Antibiotics Overview
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics

clinicalPearls: **Acyclovir IV nephrotoxicity is preventable.** Aggressive hydration + slow infusion (≥1 hr) + renal dose adjustment. The drug crystallizes in renal tubules when concentrated -- keep them flushed.

**Valganciclovir and valacyclovir are NOT interchangeable.** This is a dangerous name-pair confusion. Valganciclovir is much more myelosuppressive. ISMP has flagged this as a high-risk medication error.

**HLA-B*5701 testing before abacavir is MANDATORY.** Positive patients must NEVER receive abacavir -- the hypersensitivity reaction can be fatal and re-challenge is even more dangerous than first exposure.

**Oseltamivir in hospitalized patients: Give it even if >48 hours from symptom onset.** The 48-hour window is for outpatient benefit. Hospitalized patients benefit from treatment regardless of timing.

**HBV reactivation during HCV treatment is a Black Box warning.** Screen EVERY patient for hepatitis B (HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs) before starting HCV direct-acting antivirals. Reactivation can cause fulminant liver failure.

**U=U changed HIV care.** People living with HIV who achieve and maintain an undetectable viral load cannot sexually transmit HIV. This is one of the most important public health messages in modern medicine.

**HIV ART adherence > 95% is the target** to prevent resistance. A patient taking 1 pill daily who misses even 2 doses/month is below 95%. Simplifying to single-tablet regimens dramatically improves adherence.
**Flu antivirals are underutilized in high-risk patients.** Oseltamivir should be started empirically in hospitalized patients with suspected influenza -- don't wait for confirmatory testing.

arbs-angiotensin-ii-receptor-blockers.title: ARBs (Angiotensin II Receptor Blockers)

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — The AT1 receptor is a Gq-coupled GPCR; blocking it prevents IP3/DAG/Ca2+ signaling that drives vasoconstriction and aldosterone release*

Selectively block angiotensin II type 1 (AT1) receptors, preventing the effects of angiotensin II:
- **Vasodilation** (blocks angiotensin II-mediated vasoconstriction)
- **Decreased aldosterone release** (reduces sodium/water retention)
- **Reduced cardiac and vascular remodeling**
- Unlike ACE Inhibitors, ARBs do NOT increase bradykinin levels, so the dry cough and angioedema risk are significantly lower

Net effect: decreased afterload, decreased preload, decreased BP, cardioprotective and renoprotective effects similar to ACEi.
sections.indications:
- Hypertension - first-line, especially ACEi-intolerant patients
- Heart Failure with reduced EF (valsartan, candesartan, losartan) - when ACEi not tolerated
- Diabetic Nephropathy - renoprotective (irbesartan, losartan have strongest evidence)
- Post-Myocardial Infarction - valsartan as ACEi alternative
- Stroke prevention - losartan (LIFE trial)
- Chronic Kidney Disease with proteinuria
- Marfan Syndrome - losartan for aortic root dilation
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Losartan | PO | 25-100 mg daily | Prodrug; uricosuric effect (lowers uric acid); shortest half-life |
| Valsartan | PO | 80-320 mg daily | Strong HF evidence (Val-HeFT); component of Entresto |
| Candesartan | PO | 4-32 mg daily | Potent; strong HF evidence (CHARM trial) |
| Irbesartan | PO | 150-300 mg daily | Strong evidence for diabetic nephropathy (IDNT trial) |
| Olmesartan | PO | 20-40 mg daily | Potent BP lowering; rare sprue-like enteropathy |
| Telmisartan | PO | 20-80 mg daily | Longest half-life (24h); PPAR-gamma activity |
| Azilsartan | PO | 40-80 mg daily | Newest; may have slightly greater BP reduction |
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Onset:** 1-3 hours
- **Peak:** Losartan 1 hr (active metabolite 3-4 hr); valsartan 2-4 hr; candesartan 3-4 hr
- **Duration:** 24 hours (most); losartan may need BID dosing in some patients
- **Metabolism:** Hepatic (CYP2C9 for losartan; others vary); losartan is a prodrug
- **Elimination:** Primarily hepatic/biliary; some renal
- **Half-life:** Losartan 2 hr (metabolite 6-9 hr); valsartan 6-9 hr; telmisartan 24 hr
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Dizziness
- Hyperkalemia (less than ACEi but still significant)
- Hypotension
- Headache
- Fatigue
- Upper respiratory infection
- **NO dry cough** (or significantly less than ACEi)

### Serious/Black Box
- **BLACK BOX: Fetal toxicity** - contraindicated in pregnancy (same as ACEi)
- Angioedema (rare, but CAN occur; lower risk than ACEi but NOT zero)
- Acute renal failure (bilateral renal artery stenosis)
- Hyperkalemia with cardiac effects
- Rhabdomyolysis (rare)
- Olmesartan: sprue-like enteropathy (chronic diarrhea, weight loss)
sections.contraindications:
- Pregnancy (all trimesters)
- Concurrent use with aliskiren in diabetic patients
- Bilateral renal artery stenosis
- Concurrent use with ACE Inhibitors (dual RAAS blockade)
- Concurrent use with sacubitril within 36 hours (valsartan component of Entresto is the exception)
- Hypersensitivity
sections.drug-interactions:
- **K-sparing diuretics / K supplements** - hyperkalemia risk
- **NSAIDs** - reduce efficacy; increase renal risk
- **Lithium** - increased lithium levels
- **ACEi** - do NOT combine (no benefit, increased harm)
- **Aliskiren** - contraindicated in diabetes/CKD
sections.comparison-arbs-vs-ace-inhibitors:
| Feature | ACE Inhibitors | ARBs |
|---------|---------------|------|
| Mechanism | Block ACE enzyme | Block AT1 receptor |
| Cough | 10-20% | <1% |
| Angioedema | 0.1-0.7% | <0.1% (but NOT zero) |
| Bradykinin effect | Increased (causes cough) | No effect |
| Pregnancy | Contraindicated | Contraindicated |
| Hyperkalemia | Yes | Yes (slightly less) |
| Renal protection | Yes | Yes |
| HF mortality benefit | First-line | Alternative when ACEi not tolerated |
| Cost | Generic available | Generic available |
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Blood pressure (hold if SBP <90-100 mmHg per protocol)
  - BMP: potassium, BUN/creatinine at baseline, 1-2 weeks after initiation, periodically
  - Same creatinine monitoring as ACEi (30% rise threshold)
  - Monitor for angioedema (less common but possible)
  - Urine protein in diabetic patients
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Avoid potassium supplements and salt substitutes
  - Do not take if pregnant; use effective contraception
  - Report face/lip/tongue swelling immediately
  - Rise slowly to prevent orthostatic hypotension
  - Avoid NSAIDs without provider approval
  - Common reason for switch from ACEi: cough should resolve within 1-4 weeks
- **Administration Notes:**
  - Most ARBs: take without regard to meals
  - Can be dosed once daily (except losartan in some patients)
  - Start low, titrate to target dose
  - If switching from ACEi: can start ARB next day (no washout needed unless switching TO Entresto)
  - For HF: titrate to evidence-based target doses
sections.hold-parameters-typical:
- Hold if SBP <90-100 mmHg (per protocol)
- Hold if K+ >5.5 mEq/L
- Hold if creatinine rise >30% from baseline
- Notify MD for angioedema symptoms

clinicalPearls: Primary role is ACEi alternative when cough is intolerable
Some patients who had angioedema on ACEi CAN develop angioedema on ARBs (roughly 2-17% cross-reactivity) - use with caution and close monitoring
Do NOT combine ACEi + ARB - ONTARGET trial showed no benefit and more adverse effects
Losartan has a unique uricosuric effect - may be preferred in patients with [[Gout]]
Valsartan is the ARB component in sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) for HFrEF
Candesartan may be preferred for HF based on CHARM data
Olmesartan: if patient develops chronic diarrhea/weight loss, consider sprue-like enteropathy
Often combined with [[Diuretics]] (e.g., losartan/HCTZ combo products)
In [[Atrial Fibrillation]]: may have modest role in preventing recurrence

beta-blockers-beta-adrenergic-antagonist.title: Beta-Blockers (Beta-Adrenergic Antagonists)

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

Block beta-adrenergic receptors, reducing the effects of catecholamines (epinephrine, norepinephrine):

**Beta-1 receptors (heart):**
- Decreased heart rate (negative chronotropy)
- Decreased contractility (negative inotropy)
- Decreased conduction velocity through AV node (negative dromotropy)
- Decreased renin release from kidneys

**Beta-2 receptors (lungs, peripheral vasculature, liver):**
- Non-selective agents also block beta-2, causing bronchospasm, peripheral vasoconstriction, and masking hypoglycemia symptoms

**Alpha-1 receptors (some agents):**
- Carvedilol and labetalol also block alpha-1 receptors, causing vasodilation
sections.classification:
| Type | Agents | Key Feature |
|------|--------|-------------|
| **Cardioselective (beta-1)** | Metoprolol, atenolol, bisoprolol, nebivolol | Preferred in COPD/asthma (at low doses) |
| **Non-selective (beta-1 + beta-2)** | Propranolol, nadolol, timolol, sotalol | Avoid in reactive airway disease |
| **Alpha + Beta blocking** | Carvedilol, labetalol | Additional vasodilation; preferred in HF (carvedilol) |
| **ISA (intrinsic sympathomimetic)** | Pindolol, acebutolol | Partial agonist activity; NOT used in HF or post-MI |
sections.indications:
- Hypertension (no longer first-line monotherapy except with compelling indications)
- Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction - **mortality benefit** (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol ONLY)
- Post-myocardial infarction - mortality reduction
- Atrial Fibrillation / Atrial Flutter - rate control
- Supraventricular Tachycardia
- Stable Angina - reduces myocardial oxygen demand
- Aortic Dissection - first-line (target HR <60, SBP <120)
- Portal Hypertension / variceal bleeding prophylaxis (propranolol, nadolol)
- Thyrotoxicosis / Thyroid Storm - symptom control (propranolol)
- Migraine prophylaxis (propranolol)
- Essential tremor (propranolol)
- Performance anxiety (propranolol)
- Pheochromocytoma - ONLY after alpha-blockade established
- Glaucoma (timolol ophthalmic)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Metoprolol tartrate | PO/IV | 25-100 mg BID (PO); 5 mg IV q5min x3 | Beta-1 selective; BID dosing; immediate release |
| Metoprolol succinate | PO | 25-200 mg daily | Beta-1 selective; once daily ER; HF-approved (MERIT-HF) |
| Atenolol | PO | 25-100 mg daily | Beta-1 selective; renally cleared; less CNS effects |
| Bisoprolol | PO | 2.5-10 mg daily | Most beta-1 selective; HF-approved (CIBIS-II) |
| Carvedilol | PO | 3.125-25 mg BID | Alpha+beta blocker; HF-approved (COPERNICUS); vasodilating |
| Carvedilol CR | PO | 10-80 mg daily | Once-daily formulation |
| Propranolol | PO/IV | 10-80 mg BID-QID | Non-selective; lipophilic (crosses BBB); many indications |
| Labetalol | PO/IV | 100-400 mg BID (PO); 20 mg IV then infusion | Alpha+beta; preferred in pregnancy HTN; IV for emergencies |
| Esmolol | IV only | 500 mcg/kg bolus, then 50-300 mcg/kg/min | Ultra-short acting (t1/2 = 9 min); easily titrated |
| Sotalol | PO/IV | 80-160 mg BID | Non-selective + Class III antiarrhythmic; QT prolongation |
| Nebivolol | PO | 5-40 mg daily | Beta-1 selective; nitric oxide-mediated vasodilation |
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Onset:** IV esmolol: 1-2 min; IV metoprolol: 5 min; PO agents: 1-2 hours
- **Duration:** Esmolol: 10-30 min; metoprolol tartrate: 12 hr; metoprolol succinate: 24 hr
- **Metabolism:**
  - Hepatic (CYP2D6): metoprolol, propranolol, carvedilol (poor metabolizers have higher levels)
  - Renal: atenolol, nadolol, sotalol (dose adjust in CKD)
- **Lipophilicity:** Propranolol > metoprolol > atenolol (more lipophilic = more CNS effects)
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Bradycardia
- Hypotension
- Fatigue / exercise intolerance
- Dizziness
- Cold extremities (peripheral vasoconstriction)
- Sexual dysfunction (especially non-selective agents)
- Weight gain
- Depression / insomnia (lipophilic agents cross BBB)
- GI: nausea, diarrhea

### Serious/Black Box
- **Severe bradycardia / heart block** (2nd/3rd degree AV block)
- **Acute decompensated heart failure** (in naive HF patients - must be stable and euvolemic before starting)
- **Bronchospasm** (non-selective agents; risk with cardioselective at high doses)
- **Masking hypoglycemia** (blocks tachycardia response; sweating still present)
- **Rebound tachycardia / hypertension** with abrupt discontinuation (especially propranolol)
- **Sotalol: QT prolongation, torsades de pointes**
- Carvedilol: hepatotoxicity (rare)
sections.contraindications:
- Severe bradycardia (HR <50 without pacemaker)
- 2nd or 3rd degree heart block (without pacemaker)
- Decompensated heart failure / cardiogenic shock
- Severe reactive airway disease (absolute for non-selective; relative for selective)
- Pheochromocytoma (without prior alpha-blockade - risk of hypertensive crisis)
- Prinzmetal/variant angina (non-selective agents)
- Sick sinus syndrome (without pacemaker)
- Cocaine-induced chest pain (controversial; unopposed alpha-stimulation concern)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Heart rate and blood pressure before every dose
  - **Hold parameters:** typically hold if HR <60 bpm or SBP <90-100 mmHg (per protocol)
  - ECG: monitor for heart block, QT prolongation (sotalol)
  - Blood glucose in diabetic patients (masking of hypoglycemia)
