  - Sotalol: continuous telemetry for first 3 days of initiation; QTc monitoring
  - Respiratory status in COPD/asthma patients
  - Weight, I&Os, signs of HF exacerbation when initiating in HF patients
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take pulse before each dose; do not take if HR <60 (or per provider instruction)
  - **Do NOT stop abruptly** - taper over 1-2 weeks to avoid rebound tachycardia, HTN, angina, or MI
  - May cause fatigue and exercise intolerance
  - Rise slowly (orthostatic hypotension)
  - Diabetics: monitor blood sugar closely; tachycardia may be masked during hypoglycemia
  - Report: shortness of breath, weight gain >2 lbs/day, dizziness, slow pulse
  - Avoid alcohol (additive hypotension)
- **Administration Notes:**
  - Metoprolol tartrate: take with food (increases absorption)
  - Metoprolol succinate (ER): may take with or without food; do NOT crush/chew
  - Carvedilol: take with food (slows absorption, reduces orthostatic hypotension)
  - IV esmolol: use infusion pump; closely monitor in ICU setting
  - IV labetalol: patient should be supine during administration
  - **HF initiation: start at lowest dose, uptitrate every 2 weeks as tolerated; patient must be euvolemic and on stable diuretic dose**
sections.heart-failure-dosing-evidence-based-targ:
| Drug | Starting Dose | Target Dose |
|------|--------------|-------------|
| Carvedilol | 3.125 mg BID | 25 mg BID (50 mg BID if >85 kg) |
| Metoprolol succinate | 12.5-25 mg daily | 200 mg daily |
| Bisoprolol | 1.25 mg daily | 10 mg daily |

> **NOTE:** Only these three beta-blockers have mortality benefit in HFrEF. Metoprolol TARTRATE is NOT interchangeable with succinate for HF.
sections.key-conditions:
- Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction — carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol have proven mortality benefit
- ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction / Non-ST Elevation Myocardial Infarction — post-MI beta-blockers reduce mortality and reinfarction
- Atrial Fibrillation — rate control with metoprolol, esmolol, or propranolol
- Dysrhythmias — sotalol (Class III antiarrhythmic); esmolol for acute SVT; beta-blockers for rate control
- Graves Disease / thyroid storm — propranolol controls sympathetic symptoms and blocks T4-to-T3 conversion
- Aortic Dissection — first-line treatment to reduce HR and aortic shear force
- Preeclampsia and Eclampsia — labetalol is preferred antihypertensive in pregnancy-related hypertension

---

clinicalPearls: **Metoprolol tartrate vs succinate:** tartrate is BID, immediate release; succinate is daily, extended release - NOT interchangeable, especially in HF
**"Beta-blockers before beta-agonists"** - in a code/emergency, remember that beta-blockers will blunt the response to epinephrine
In [[Aortic Dissection]]: give IV beta-blocker FIRST to reduce HR and shear force, THEN add vasodilator if needed
For [[Thyroid Storm]]: propranolol is preferred because it also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion
Esmolol is the "test dose" beta-blocker - ultra-short acting, useful when unsure if patient will tolerate
In HF: beta-blockers reduce mortality but may temporarily worsen symptoms during uptitration - do NOT start during acute decompensation
Carvedilol may be preferred in HF patients with diabetes (alpha-blockade improves insulin sensitivity slightly)
Labetalol is a preferred agent in [[Pregnancy]]-related hypertension ([[Preeclampsia and Eclampsia]])
Beta-blocker + non-dihydropyridine [[Calcium Channel Blockers]] (diltiazem/verapamil) = risk of severe bradycardia/heart block - avoid combination
Often used with [[ACE Inhibitors]] or [[ARBs]] in HF management
In perioperative setting: continue beta-blocker in patients already taking them; do NOT start de novo before surgery (POISE trial)
relatedNotes: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

blood-products-and-transfusion.title: Blood Products and Transfusion

sections.definition: Blood transfusion is the administration of blood components to treat specific deficiencies. Modern transfusion medicine uses component therapy -- give ONLY what the patient needs, not whole blood. Every transfusion carries risks and requires informed consent, positive patient identification, and monitoring.
sections.blood-products:
### Component Therapy
| Product | Contents | Volume | Indications | Expected Effect |
|---------|----------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **PRBCs (Packed Red Blood Cells)** | RBCs with minimal plasma | ~300 mL/unit | Symptomatic anemia, acute hemorrhage, Hgb < 7 g/dL (most patients) or < 8 g/dL (cardiac/ACS) | ↑ Hgb ~1 g/dL per unit; ↑ Hct ~3% per unit |
| **FFP (Fresh Frozen Plasma)** | All coagulation factors | ~250 mL/unit | Coagulopathy (INR > 1.5-2.0 with bleeding), massive transfusion, warfarin reversal (when PCC unavailable), TTP (plasma exchange) | Replaces clotting factors; ↓ INR |
| **Platelets** | Platelet concentrate | ~300 mL (pooled) or ~200 mL (apheresis) | Thrombocytopenia with bleeding; platelet count < 10,000 (prophylactic); < 50,000 (surgical); < 100,000 (neurosurgery/ocular) | ↑ Platelet count ~30,000-50,000 per unit (apheresis) |
| **Cryoprecipitate** | Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, vWF, Factor XIII | ~15 mL/unit (give as pooled 5-10 units) | Hypofibrinogenemia (fibrinogen < 100-150 mg/dL); DIC; massive transfusion; vWD (when DDAVP/factor concentrate unavailable) | ↑ Fibrinogen ~50-70 mg/dL per 10-unit pool |
| **Albumin** | 5% or 25% human albumin | 250-500 mL (5%); 50-100 mL (25%) | Volume expansion; SBP; hepatorenal syndrome; hypoalbuminemia (burns, nephrotic) | Volume expansion; oncotic pressure support |

> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — FFP replaces all clotting factors across both pathways; cryoprecipitate specifically restores fibrinogen, factor VIII, vWF, and factor XIII; PCC concentrates the vitamin K-dependent factors (II, VII, IX, X); each product targets a different deficit in the cascade*

### Massive Transfusion Protocol (MTP)
- Activated for life-threatening hemorrhage (trauma, GI bleed, obstetric, surgical)
- **Goal ratio:** 1:1:1 (PRBCs : FFP : Platelets) to prevent dilutional coagulopathy
- Tranexamic acid (TXA) within 3 hours of injury (CRASH-2 trial)
- Permissive hypotension until surgical source control (target SBP 80-90 in trauma)
- Monitor: CBC, fibrinogen, INR/PT, ionized calcium, ABG/lactate frequently
- **MultiCare MTP Pack:** 4 RBC : 4 FFP : 1 PLT component; initial 2 RBC released within 10 min; cryo ordered separately (1 pool per 2 MTP sets or fibrinogen <100). See Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy for full MultiCare MTP protocol with activation criteria, nursing responsibilities, goal-directed targets, and discontinuation criteria.
sections.blood-type-compatibility:
| Recipient Type | Can Receive RBCs From | Can Receive Plasma From |
|---------------|----------------------|------------------------|
| **O-** | O- only | O, A, B, AB |
| **O+** | O-, O+ | O, A, B, AB |
| **A-** | A-, O- | A, AB |
| **A+** | A+, A-, O+, O- | A, AB |
| **B-** | B-, O- | B, AB |
| **B+** | B+, B-, O+, O- | B, AB |
| **AB+** | Universal recipient (all types) | AB only |
| **AB-** | AB-, A-, B-, O- | AB only |
| **O-** | Universal RBC donor | |
| **AB** | | Universal plasma donor |

> **Emergency:** When type is unknown, give O-negative PRBCs (universal donor) and AB plasma (universal donor).
sections.transfusion-reactions:
| Reaction | Timing | Signs/Symptoms | Cause | Management |
|----------|--------|---------------|-------|-----------|
| **Acute hemolytic** | Minutes to hours | Fever, chills, flank/back pain, hemoglobinuria (dark/red urine), hypotension, DIC | ABO incompatibility (usually clerical error) | **STOP transfusion immediately**; NS bolus; send blood bank samples; supportive care; may be fatal |
| **Febrile non-hemolytic (FNHTR)** | During or within 4 hr | Fever (≥1°C rise), chills, rigors | WBC antibodies, cytokines | Stop transfusion; rule out hemolytic reaction; acetaminophen; usually benign; use leukoreduced products |
| **Allergic (minor)** | During transfusion | Urticaria, hives, itching | Plasma protein sensitivity | Stop briefly; diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV; restart slowly if only urticaria and vital signs stable |
| **Anaphylactic** | Minutes | Dyspnea, bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema | IgA deficiency with anti-IgA antibodies (most common); plasma protein allergy | **STOP immediately**; epinephrine IM; supportive care; future: washed or IgA-deficient products |
| **TRALI (Transfusion-Related Acute Lung Injury)** | Within 6 hr | Acute respiratory distress, bilateral infiltrates, hypoxemia, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema | Donor antibodies activate recipient neutrophils in lungs | **STOP**; supportive care (O2, possible intubation); NO diuretics (not fluid overload); leading cause of transfusion-related death |
| **TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload)** | During or within 6 hr | Dyspnea, hypertension, JVD, crackles, peripheral edema | Volume overload (especially elderly, HF, renal failure) | **Stop or slow**; upright position; diuretics (furosemide); O2; prevent with slower rate and pre-transfusion diuretic in at-risk patients |
| **Delayed hemolytic** | 3-14 days post-transfusion | Unexplained Hgb drop, jaundice, positive DAT | Anamnestic antibody response | Usually self-limited; send new type and screen; supportive |
| **Infection/Sepsis** | During or shortly after | High fever, rigors, hypotension, shock | Bacterial contamination (highest risk with platelets -- stored at room temp) | **STOP**; blood cultures from patient AND product; broad-spectrum antibiotics; supportive |
sections.monitoring:
| Timepoint | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **Pre-transfusion** | Informed consent; type & screen/crossmatch; baseline vital signs (T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2); two-RN patient ID verification at bedside (compare wristband to blood product label) |
| **First 15 minutes** | Stay with patient; vital signs at 15 min; highest risk period for severe reactions |
| **During transfusion** | Vital signs q30min-1hr per facility protocol; assess for reaction signs |
| **Post-transfusion** | Vital signs within 1 hr of completion; document product info; assess response (post-transfusion labs as ordered) |
sections.administration-guidelines:
| Product | Rate | Max Infusion Time | Special Considerations |
|---------|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| PRBCs | Start slow (~2 mL/min for first 15 min), then increase to complete in 1.5-2 hr per unit | **4 hours maximum** (bacterial growth risk at room temp) | Use 18-gauge IV minimum (20-gauge acceptable but slower); use blood administration tubing with 170-260 micron filter; NS ONLY (no LR, dextrose, or medications through same line) |
| FFP | 200-250 mL over 30-60 min | 4 hours | Must be ABO compatible; thaw before use (takes ~30 min); use within 24 hr of thawing |
| Platelets | 300 mL over 15-30 min | 4 hours | Rapid infusion; ABO compatible preferred; DO NOT refrigerate (room temp storage); gently agitate before infusing |
| Cryoprecipitate | Pool of 5-10 units over 10-30 min | 4 hours (use quickly after thawing) | Give rapidly; primarily for fibrinogen replacement |
sections.contraindications-cautions:
- **Jehovah's Witness patients:** Refuse blood products -- respect autonomy; discuss alternatives (cell salvage, EPO, iron, TXA); document informed refusal
- **Volume overload risk:** CHF, renal failure, elderly → slow infusion rate; pre-medicate with furosemide; consider one unit at a time
- **IgA deficiency:** Risk of anaphylaxis → use washed or IgA-deficient products
- **Autoimmune hemolytic anemia:** Crossmatch difficulties; coordinate closely with blood bank
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **The Two-Person Verification:**
  - BOTH RNs must independently verify at bedside: patient name, DOB, MRN, blood product unit number, ABO/Rh type, expiration date
  - Compare with patient's wristband AND blood bank requisition
  - THIS IS THE #1 SAFETY CHECK. Most fatal hemolytic reactions are caused by clerical/identification errors.
- **IV Access:**
  - Dedicated line or compatible only with NS
  - NEVER add medications to blood products
  - NEVER infuse blood with dextrose (causes hemolysis) or lactated Ringer's (calcium causes clotting)
- **Reaction Protocol:**
  1. STOP the transfusion immediately
  2. Maintain IV access with NS
  3. Notify provider and blood bank
  4. Send post-reaction labs: blood bank specimen (re-type and crossmatch), CBC, urinalysis, DAT, haptoglobin, bilirubin, LDH
  5. Return blood product and tubing to blood bank
  6. Document everything
- **Special Populations:**
  - Neonates/pediatrics: Volume-based dosing (10-15 mL/kg PRBCs)
  - Sickle cell disease: May need phenotypically matched/irradiated products
  - Transplant candidates: Use irradiated and CMV-safe products
  - Chronic transfusion patients: Monitor for iron overload (ferritin); may need chelation therapy (deferasirox)
sections.related-concepts:
- Coagulation and Hemostasis
- Shock
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance
- Anticoagulants

clinicalPearls: **The most dangerous step in transfusion is patient identification.** The majority of fatal hemolytic reactions are caused by giving the wrong unit to the wrong patient -- a preventable clerical error. The bedside two-RN check is non-negotiable.

**TRALI vs. TACO -- know the difference.** Both cause dyspnea during/after transfusion. TRALI = non-cardiogenic (bilateral infiltrates, hypotension, no JVD); treat with supportive care, NOT diuretics. TACO = cardiogenic (hypertension, JVD, edema); treat with diuretics. BNP can help differentiate (elevated in TACO).

**Restrictive transfusion (Hgb < 7) is the standard for most patients.** The TRICC trial showed that transfusing to Hgb > 10 is not beneficial and may be harmful. Exceptions: active ACS (trigger Hgb 8), massive hemorrhage.
**Platelets are stored at room temperature** -- this is why they have the highest bacterial contamination risk of all blood products. High fever + rigors during platelet transfusion = think sepsis.

**Calcium matters in massive transfusion.** Citrate preservative in blood products chelates calcium. Massive transfusion → hypocalcemia → coagulopathy → cardiac dysfunction. Monitor ionized calcium and replace aggressively.
**The 4-hour rule is absolute.** Blood products at room temperature beyond 4 hours = bacterial growth risk. If not infused by 4 hours, it must be discarded. Plan infusion rate accordingly.

**Pre-medication with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine** is common practice for patients with prior febrile/allergic reactions but is NOT evidence-based for routine use in all patients. Many facilities are moving away from routine pre-medication.
**One unit at a time, then reassess.** Especially in elderly or HF patients. Check post-transfusion Hgb before ordering the next unit. Each unit is an independent clinical decision.

bronchodilators.title: Bronchodilators

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

- **Beta-2 agonists (SABA/LABA):** Stimulate beta-2 adrenergic receptors on bronchial smooth muscle, causing relaxation and bronchodilation; also increase mucociliary clearance
- **Anticholinergics (SAMA/LAMA):** Block muscarinic (M3) receptors in airway smooth muscle, preventing acetylcholine-mediated bronchoconstriction; also reduce mucus secretion
sections.classification:
| Category | Abbreviation | Onset | Duration | Examples |
|----------|-------------|-------|----------|----------|
| Short-acting beta-2 agonist | SABA | 5-15 min | 4-6 hr | Albuterol, levalbuterol |
| Long-acting beta-2 agonist | LABA | 15-30 min | 12 hr | Salmeterol, formoterol |
| Ultra-LABA | LABA | 15-30 min | 24 hr | Indacaterol, olodaterol |
| Short-acting muscarinic antagonist | SAMA | 15-30 min | 4-8 hr | Ipratropium |
| Long-acting muscarinic antagonist | LAMA | 30-60 min | 24 hr | Tiotropium, umeclidinium |
sections.indications:
- Asthma -- SABA for rescue; LABA only with ICS (never LABA monotherapy in asthma)
- Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease -- SABA/SAMA for rescue; LABA and/or LAMA for maintenance
- Acute bronchospasm / Status Asthmaticus
- Exercise-induced bronchospasm (SABA pre-treatment)
- Hyperkalemia (albuterol nebulizer as adjunct to shift K intracellularly)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Albuterol | MDI/Neb | MDI: 2 puffs q4-6h PRN; Neb: 2.5 mg q4-6h PRN; acute: q20min x 3 | Gold standard rescue inhaler; tremor and tachycardia common |
| Levalbuterol | MDI/Neb | Neb: 0.63-1.25 mg q6-8h | R-isomer of albuterol; theoretically fewer cardiac side effects; more expensive |
| Ipratropium | MDI/Neb | MDI: 2 puffs QID; Neb: 0.5 mg q6-8h | Anticholinergic; slower onset than albuterol; combine with albuterol in acute exacerbations |
| Salmeterol | DPI | 50 mcg (1 puff) BID | LABA; must use with ICS in asthma (Black Box); never for rescue |
| Formoterol | DPI/Neb | DPI: 12 mcg BID; Neb: 20 mcg BID | LABA; faster onset than salmeterol; still maintenance only |
| Tiotropium | DPI/SMI | 18 mcg (1 cap) daily via HandiHaler; 2.5 mcg (2 puffs) daily via Respimat | LAMA; cornerstone of COPD maintenance; once-daily dosing |
sections.common-combination-products:
- **Albuterol + ipratropium** (DuoNeb/Combivent): SABA + SAMA for acute COPD exacerbation
- **Fluticasone + salmeterol** (Advair): ICS + LABA
- **Budesonide + formoterol** (Symbicort): ICS + LABA
- **Tiotropium + olodaterol** (Stiolto): LAMA + LABA (COPD only)
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Beta-2 agonists: Tremor, tachycardia, palpitations, nervousness, hypokalemia, hyperglycemia
- Anticholinergics: Dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision (if sprayed in eyes)
- Headache (both classes)

### Serious/Black Box
- **LABA (Black Box for asthma):** Increased risk of asthma-related death when used WITHOUT an ICS; NEVER use LABA monotherapy in asthma
- Paradoxical bronchospasm (rare; discontinue and switch agents)
- Cardiac arrhythmias at high doses (beta-2 agonists)
- Anaphylaxis (rare)
sections.contraindications:
- LABA monotherapy in asthma (Black Box)
- Ipratropium/tiotropium: Severe allergy to atropine or soy/peanut (some formulations contain soy lecithin)
- Caution: Narrow-angle glaucoma (anticholinergics); severe cardiovascular disease; hyperthyroidism
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Respiratory status: RR, SpO2, breath sounds, accessory muscle use, peak flow
  - Heart rate (tachycardia with beta-2 agonists)
  - Potassium (albuterol shifts K intracellularly; can worsen hypokalemia)
  - Acute exacerbation: Monitor response q20 min during serial nebulizer treatments
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - SABA is rescue only -- using > 2 days/week suggests uncontrolled asthma
  - LABA/LAMA are maintenance -- NOT for acute rescue
  - Proper MDI technique: Shake, exhale, actuate on slow inhalation, hold breath 10 sec
  - Spacer use with MDI improves drug delivery to lungs (recommended for all MDI use)
  - Rinse mouth after ICS use -- but also good habit after any inhaler to reduce oral side effects
  - Carry rescue inhaler at all times
- **Administration Notes:**
  - MDI vs nebulizer: Equivalent efficacy with proper technique; nebulizer useful if patient unable to coordinate MDI
  - Sequence if using multiple inhalers: Bronchodilator first (open airways), then ICS
  - DPI (dry powder inhaler): Do NOT use spacer; requires adequate inspiratory flow; do not exhale into device
  - Nebulizer: Upright position; deep slow breaths; treatment complete when sputtering (~10 min)
sections.key-conditions:
- Asthma — SABA for rescue; LABA + ICS for maintenance; continuous nebulization for status asthmaticus
- Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease — LAMA and/or LABA for maintenance; SABA/SAMA for acute exacerbations
- Hyperkalemia — high-dose albuterol nebulizer (10-20 mg) shifts potassium intracellularly as adjunct therapy
- Anaphylaxis — bronchospasm component treated with albuterol nebulizer alongside epinephrine
- Cystic Fibrosis — bronchodilators before airway clearance and inhaled antibiotics

---

clinicalPearls: In acute asthma/COPD exacerbation: Albuterol neb q20 min x 3, then q1-4h; add ipratropium for first 3 doses in severe exacerbation
Continuous albuterol nebulization (10-15 mg/hr) for severe/status asthmaticus
If patient uses SABA > 2x/week (excluding exercise pre-treatment), step up controller therapy
Albuterol for hyperkalemia: 10-20 mg nebulized (much higher than respiratory dose); shifts K ~0.5-1.0 mEq/L; onset 15-30 min
**Hold parameters:** Hold SABA if HR > 120-130 (assess clinical context); hold LAMA if urinary retention symptoms worsen
Tiotropium HandiHaler: Capsule is for inhalation only -- never swallow; pierce capsule in device, inhale twice

calcium-channel-blockers.title: Calcium Channel Blockers

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

Block L-type voltage-gated calcium channels, reducing intracellular calcium in:
- **Vascular smooth muscle** - vasodilation (primarily dihydropyridines)
- **Cardiac myocytes** - decreased contractility (primarily non-dihydropyridines)
- **SA and AV node** - decreased automaticity and conduction (non-dihydropyridines)

### Two Subclasses
| Feature | Dihydropyridines (DHPs) | Non-Dihydropyridines (Non-DHPs) |
|---------|------------------------|--------------------------------|
| Primary effect | Vascular smooth muscle relaxation | Cardiac effects (rate, contractility) |
| Vasodilation | +++ | + |
| Heart rate | Reflex tachycardia | Decreased HR |
| Contractility | Minimal effect | Decreased |
| AV conduction | No effect | Slowed |
| Agents | Amlodipine, nifedipine | Diltiazem, verapamil |
sections.indications:
### Dihydropyridines
- Hypertension - first-line, especially in elderly and Black patients
- Stable Angina (amlodipine)
- Raynaud's Phenomenon (nifedipine)
- Vasospastic/Prinzmetal Angina
- Subarachnoid Hemorrhage - nimodipine for cerebral vasospasm prevention

### Non-Dihydropyridines
- Atrial Fibrillation / Atrial Flutter - rate control (diltiazem, verapamil)
- Supraventricular Tachycardia - acute conversion (IV verapamil, IV diltiazem)
- Hypertension
- Stable Angina
- Migraine prophylaxis (verapamil)
- Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (verapamil)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Amlodipine** | PO | 2.5-10 mg daily | Long half-life (30-50 hr); no reflex tachycardia; most prescribed |
| **Nifedipine ER** | PO | 30-90 mg daily | Potent vasodilator; reflex tachycardia with IR form |
| Felodipine | PO | 2.5-10 mg daily | Grapefruit interaction; vascular selective |
| Nicardipine | IV/PO | IV: 5-15 mg/hr | IV for hypertensive emergency; titratable |
| Clevidipine | IV | 1-32 mg/hr | Ultra-short acting; lipid emulsion; ICU use |
| Nimodipine | PO | 60 mg q4h x 21 days | CNS selective; SAH vasospasm ONLY; do NOT give IV |
| **Diltiazem** | PO/IV | PO: 120-360 mg daily; IV: 0.25 mg/kg bolus then 5-15 mg/hr | Rate control + mild vasodilation; versatile |
| **Verapamil** | PO/IV | PO: 120-480 mg daily; IV: 2.5-5 mg over 2 min | Strongest negative inotrope/chronotrope; constipation common |
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Onset:** IV diltiazem/verapamil: 3-5 min; PO amlodipine: gradual over days
- **Peak:** Amlodipine: 6-12 hours; diltiazem IR: 2-4 hours
- **Duration:** Amlodipine: 24+ hours; diltiazem IR: 6-8 hours; diltiazem ER: 24 hours
- **Metabolism:** Hepatic (CYP3A4 for most) - significant drug interactions
- **Elimination:** Hepatic metabolism, renal excretion of metabolites
- **Half-life:** Amlodipine 30-50 hr; nifedipine 2-5 hr (ER formulation extends); diltiazem 3-5 hr; verapamil 6-8 hr
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common - Dihydropyridines
- **Peripheral edema** (dose-dependent; amlodipine up to 10%; due to precapillary vasodilation, NOT fluid retention - diuretics do NOT help)
- Flushing / headache
- Reflex tachycardia (nifedipine IR >> amlodipine)
- Dizziness
- Gingival hyperplasia (nifedipine)

### Common - Non-Dihydropyridines
- **Constipation** (especially verapamil - up to 40%)
- Bradycardia
- Hypotension
- Dizziness
- Peripheral edema (less than DHPs)
- Gingival hyperplasia

### Serious/Black Box
- **Severe hypotension** (especially IR nifedipine; avoid sublingual/bite-and-swallow nifedipine)
- **Heart block** (non-DHPs, especially with concurrent Beta-Blockers)
- **Heart failure exacerbation** (non-DHPs due to negative inotropy; verapamil worst)
- Severe bradycardia (non-DHPs)
- **Nimodipine: fatal hypotension if given IV** (oral capsule contents given IV by error)
sections.contraindications:
- **Non-DHPs:**
  - Severe Heart Failure / left ventricular dysfunction (EF <40%)
  - 2nd/3rd degree heart block (without pacemaker)
  - Sick sinus syndrome (without pacemaker)
  - Concurrent IV beta-blocker use
  - WPW syndrome with atrial fibrillation (verapamil)
- **DHPs:**
  - Severe aortic stenosis (relative)
  - Avoid IR nifedipine in acute MI or unstable angina
- **All CCBs:**
  - Hypersensitivity
  - Severe hypotension
sections.drug-interactions:
- **Beta-blockers + non-DHPs** = severe bradycardia, heart block, HF - avoid combination (DHPs + beta-blockers are generally safe and often used together)
- **CYP3A4 inhibitors** (azole antifungals, macrolides, grapefruit) - increased CCB levels
- **CYP3A4 inducers** (rifampin, phenytoin) - decreased CCB levels
- **Digoxin** - verapamil/diltiazem increase digoxin levels (reduce digoxin dose by ~50%)
- **Simvastatin** - verapamil/diltiazem increase statin levels (simvastatin max 10 mg with verapamil; 20 mg with diltiazem)
- **Dantrolene** - hyperkalemia and CV collapse with verapamil
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Blood pressure and heart rate before each dose
  - **Hold parameters:** HR <60 (non-DHPs); SBP <90-100 mmHg
  - ECG/telemetry for IV diltiazem/verapamil drips
  - Peripheral edema assessment (DHPs)
  - Bowel function (verapamil - may need bowel regimen)
  - IV diltiazem: continuous cardiac monitoring; watch for hypotension/bradycardia
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Swallow ER/SR formulations whole - do NOT crush, chew, or split
  - Report swelling in ankles/feet (peripheral edema)
  - Avoid grapefruit/grapefruit juice (increases drug levels)
  - Do not stop abruptly (rebound angina possible)
  - Verapamil: increase fiber, fluids for constipation; may need stool softener
  - Rise slowly (orthostatic hypotension)
  - Report: slow heartbeat, dizziness, shortness of breath, fainting
- **Administration Notes:**
  - **Nimodipine:** ORAL ONLY - extract from capsule and give via NG if needed; NEVER give IV (has caused death)
  - IV diltiazem: bolus 0.25 mg/kg over 2 min; may repeat 0.35 mg/kg; then infusion 5-15 mg/hr
  - Amlodipine: no food restrictions; long half-life means steady state takes 7-8 days
  - Nifedipine IR: NO LONGER recommended for hypertensive urgency (unpredictable hypotension)
  - IV nicardipine/clevidipine: use infusion pump in ICU; easily titratable for BP control

clinicalPearls: **DHP peripheral edema** is NOT from fluid retention - it is from preferential arteriolar dilation causing capillary leak. Diuretics will NOT help. Adding an [[ACE Inhibitors]] or [[ARBs]] can reduce edema (venodilation balances arteriolar dilation)
**Amlodipine** is the safest CCB in [[Heart Failure]] - does not worsen HF (PRAISE trial)
**Non-DHPs + beta-blockers = dangerous combination** for bradycardia/AV block; DHPs + beta-blockers = complementary (BP reduction without reflex tachycardia)
IR nifedipine should NOT be used sublingually for hypertensive urgency - unpredictable drops, strokes, and MIs reported
**Diltiazem drip** is a workhorse for acute rate control in [[Atrial Fibrillation]] - transition to oral diltiazem or other agent
Conversion from IV to PO diltiazem: multiply IV rate (mg/hr) x 3 + 3 = approximate total daily oral dose in mg
Verapamil is preferred in [[Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy]] (improves diastolic function)
Nimodipine is ONLY for cerebral vasospasm prophylaxis after [[Subarachnoid Hemorrhage]] - started within 96 hours, continued for 21 days
Often combined with [[ACE Inhibitors]], [[ARBs]], or [[Diuretics]] for BP management

coagulation-factor-concentrates.title: Coagulation Factor Concentrates

sections.definition:
Coagulation factor concentrates are purified or recombinant products that replace specific clotting factors to achieve hemostasis in patients with congenital or acquired bleeding disorders. Unlike fresh frozen plasma (FFP), which provides all coagulation factors in dilute form requiring large volumes, factor concentrates deliver targeted replacement in small volumes with rapid effect. This matters critically in the ED: a patient with hemophilia A who is actively bleeding into the brain needs factor VIII NOW -- not 30 minutes from now when FFP has thawed and infused 4 units of volume into an already-compromised patient.

This class also includes adjunctive hemostatic agents like DDAVP (which releases endogenous factor stores) and tranexamic acid (TXA, which prevents clot breakdown). Together, these agents form the pharmacologic backbone of acute hemorrhage management beyond simple blood product transfusion.

**Key principle: Identify the specific factor deficiency and replace it precisely. Shotgun approaches waste time and blood bank resources.**
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — Factor concentrates directly replace specific serine proteases in the cascade; understanding which step is deficient (factor VIII = intrinsic pathway, factor VII = extrinsic, prothrombin complex = common) determines which product to reach for — targeted replacement is faster and more effective than shotgun FFP*

### Factor Replacement (Direct)
- **Cryoprecipitate** -- Concentrated from FFP by slow thawing; contains fibrinogen (150-250 mg/unit), factor VIII (80-100 IU/unit), von Willebrand factor (vWF), factor XIII, and fibronectin. Replaces multiple factors simultaneously.
- **Fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP)** -- Purified human fibrinogen; directly replenishes plasma fibrinogen levels. Unlike cryo, it is pathogen-reduced and does not require ABO matching or thawing.
- **Factor VIII concentrate** -- Recombinant or plasma-derived factor VIII; directly replaces the deficient procoagulant in hemophilia A. Binds vWF in circulation for stabilization.
- **Factor IX concentrate** -- Recombinant or plasma-derived factor IX; replaces the deficient factor in hemophilia B (Christmas disease). Activates factor X in the intrinsic pathway.
- **Recombinant factor VIIa (rFVIIa, NovoSeven)** -- Supraphysiologic doses of activated factor VII bind tissue factor at injury sites, generating a "thrombin burst" that overwhelms the coagulation system. Bypasses the need for factors VIII and IX entirely.
- **Prothrombin complex concentrate (PCC)** -- 4-factor PCC (KCentra) contains factors II, VII, IX, X plus proteins C and S. Primarily used for warfarin reversal but also covers multiple factor deficiencies. See Reversal Agents.

### Endogenous Factor Release
- **DDAVP (desmopressin)** -- Synthetic vasopressin analog that stimulates endothelial cells to release stored vWF and factor VIII from Weibel-Palade bodies. Typically raises factor VIII and vWF levels 2-5x baseline within 30-60 minutes. Does NOT work if stores are depleted (tachyphylaxis after 2-3 doses at 12-24 hour intervals).

### Antifibrinolytic
- **Tranexamic acid (TXA)** -- Synthetic lysine analog that competitively blocks plasminogen binding to fibrin, preventing plasmin-mediated clot dissolution. Stabilizes clots that have already formed. Does NOT create new clots -- it preserves existing ones.
sections.key-agents:
| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Cryoprecipitate** | 10 units (1 pool) IV; repeat as needed for fibrinogen >150 mg/dL | Onset: immediate upon infusion; Duration: 12-24 hr (fibrinogen half-life) | 10-unit pool raises fibrinogen ~50-70 mg/dL in a 70-kg adult |
| **Fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP)** | 70 mg/kg IV (typically 4-5 g in adults) | Onset: immediate; Duration: ~4 days (half-life 78 hr) | No ABO matching, no thawing, pathogen-reduced -- faster to bedside than cryo |
| **Factor VIII concentrate** | Dose = (desired % rise) x (weight in kg) x 0.5; e.g., life-threatening bleed: target 100% activity → 50 IU/kg | Onset: 10-15 min; Duration: 8-12 hr (half-life ~12 hr) | For hemophilia A; half-life shorter in children; extended half-life products (emicizumab) available for prophylaxis |
| **Factor IX concentrate** | Dose = (desired % rise) x (weight in kg) x 1.0; life-threatening bleed: target 100% → ~100 IU/kg | Onset: 10-15 min; Duration: 18-24 hr (half-life ~24 hr) | For hemophilia B; requires HIGHER doses per % rise than factor VIII (recovery ~50%) |
| **DDAVP (desmopressin)** | 0.3 mcg/kg IV over 20-30 min (max ~20 mcg); also intranasal 150 mcg per nostril | Onset: 30-60 min IV; peak 90 min; Duration: 6-12 hr | First-line for mild hemophilia A (baseline factor VIII >5%) and type 1 vWD; test with trial dose before relying on it |
| **Recombinant factor VIIa (NovoSeven)** | 90 mcg/kg IV bolus; repeat q2h PRN | Onset: 10-20 min; Duration: 2-3 hr (short half-life) | Off-label for refractory hemorrhage; VERY expensive ($5,000-10,000/dose); thrombotic risk |
| **Tranexamic acid (TXA)** | 1 g IV over 10 min; may repeat 1 g over 8 hr. Trauma: give within 3 hours of injury | Onset: minutes; Duration: ~8 hr (renal elimination) | CRASH-2 trial: reduced mortality in trauma if given within 3 hr; WOMAN trial: reduced bleeding death in postpartum hemorrhage |
sections.clinical-indications:
### Hemophilia A (Factor VIII Deficiency)
- **Acute bleeding episodes** -- factor VIII concentrate to target level based on severity:
  - Joint/muscle bleed: target 40-50% activity
  - Life-threatening (ICH, retroperitoneal, airway): target 80-100% activity
- **Mild hemophilia A (baseline >5%)** -- DDAVP 0.3 mcg/kg is first-line; avoids factor product exposure
- See Hemophilia for full management

### Hemophilia B (Factor IX Deficiency)
- Factor IX concentrate dosing; same severity-based targets as hemophilia A
- DDAVP is NOT effective for hemophilia B (does not release factor IX)

### Von Willebrand Disease
- **Type 1 (mild, most common)** -- DDAVP is first-line (releases stored vWF)
- **Type 2 and 3** -- May require vWF-containing factor VIII concentrate (Humate-P, Wilate) because DDAVP response is unreliable (type 2) or absent (type 3 -- no vWF to release)
- **TXA** -- excellent adjunct for mucosal bleeding (menorrhagia, dental procedures, epistaxis)
- See Von Willebrand Disease for full classification

### Massive Transfusion and Trauma
- **Cryoprecipitate or fibrinogen concentrate** when fibrinogen <150 mg/dL during massive hemorrhage
- **TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours** of traumatic injury (CRASH-2 protocol) -- standard of care in trauma resuscitation
- rFVIIa as rescue therapy when standard MTP products fail to achieve hemostasis
- See Blood Products and Transfusion for MTP ratios (1:1:1)

### DIC (Disseminated Intravascular Coagulation)
- Cryoprecipitate to maintain fibrinogen >100 mg/dL
- Factor replacement is supportive -- MUST treat underlying cause
- TXA is generally CONTRAINDICATED in DIC (risk of microvascular thrombosis) unless bleeding is dominant and thrombosis is not a concern

### Obstetric Hemorrhage
- TXA 1 g IV (WOMAN trial) for postpartum hemorrhage
- Fibrinogen replacement (cryo or RiaSTAP) when fibrinogen drops below 200 mg/dL (obstetric threshold is higher than general population)
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### Cryoprecipitate
- **Volume overload** -- 10-unit pool = ~150-200 mL; less than FFP but still significant in volume-sensitive patients
- **Transfusion reactions** -- allergic, febrile nonhemolytic (same risks as any blood product)
- **Infection risk** -- NOT pathogen-reduced (unlike fibrinogen concentrate)
- Requires ABO compatibility and thawing (delays administration ~30 min)

### Factor VIII and IX Concentrates
- **Inhibitor development** -- the most significant long-term complication. 20-30% of severe hemophilia A patients develop neutralizing antibodies (inhibitors) against infused factor VIII. Managed with bypassing agents (rFVIIa, FEIBA) or immune tolerance induction.
- Recombinant products carry essentially zero infectious disease risk
- Plasma-derived products undergo viral inactivation but have historical association with HIV/HCV (pre-1985 products)
- Allergic reactions (rare)
- Thrombosis (rare; primarily with PCCs and activated PCCs like FEIBA)

### DDAVP
- **Hyponatremia** -- antidiuretic effect causes free water retention; restrict fluids for 24 hr after dosing; monitor Na+ especially in children and elderly
- **Tachyphylaxis** -- vWF/factor VIII stores deplete after 2-3 doses; subsequent doses produce diminishing response
- Facial flushing, headache (common, benign)
- Mild hypotension during rapid infusion

### Recombinant Factor VIIa (NovoSeven)
- **Thrombotic events** -- arterial and venous thrombosis (MI, stroke, PE, DVT) -- the major safety concern for off-label use
- Extremely expensive
- Short half-life requiring frequent redosing

### Tranexamic Acid (TXA)
- **Seizures** -- dose-dependent risk, especially at high doses (>2 g) or in renal impairment; related to GABA-A receptor antagonism
- Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea
- Hypotension with rapid IV push (infuse over 10 min)
- **Theoretical thrombotic risk** -- not borne out in large trials (CRASH-2, WOMAN) when given appropriately
- MUST give within 3 hours of traumatic injury -- CRASH-2 showed INCREASED mortality when given after 3 hours
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Cryoprecipitate Administration
- Requires ABO compatibility (contains anti-A and anti-B antibodies in residual plasma)
- Must be thawed by blood bank before release (~30 min)
- Once thawed, infuse within 6 hours (preferably within 4 hours)
- Infuse rapidly through standard blood filter tubing (170-260 micron)
- Pooled units (typically 10 units = 1 pool) are pre-combined by blood bank
- **Two-RN verification** required at bedside (same as any blood product) -- see Blood Products and Transfusion
- Monitor for transfusion reactions during and after infusion

### Factor Concentrate Administration
- **Reconstitution** -- most factor concentrates come as lyophilized powder; reconstitute with provided diluent using gentle swirling (do NOT shake)
- Infuse IV over 3-5 minutes (slow push); rate varies by product
- Do NOT mix with other IV solutions
- Check factor activity levels 15-30 min post-infusion and periodically to guide redosing
- **For hemophilia patients: always call hematology** -- these patients know their disease better than most providers. Many carry letters with their factor type, inhibitor status, and usual treatment
- Document product lot numbers (regulatory requirement for traceability)

### DDAVP Administration
- IV: Dilute in 50 mL NS; infuse over 20-30 minutes (rapid infusion causes hypotension and flushing)
- Intranasal (Stimate): 150 mcg per nostril using high-concentration nasal spray (1.5 mg/mL). NOT the same as the diabetes insipidus nasal spray (much lower concentration)
- **Fluid restrict** for 24 hours post-dose to prevent hyponatremia
- Monitor Na+ levels, especially with repeat dosing
- Record pre- and post-DDAVP factor VIII levels and vWF activity to assess response (typically done as a "DDAVP challenge" electively before relying on it emergently)

### TXA Administration
- **1 g IV over 10 minutes** -- NOT IV push (hypotension and seizure risk with rapid administration)
- Can follow with 1 g IV over 8 hours as maintenance
- In trauma: start in the field or as soon as possible upon ED arrival -- the clock is ticking from time of injury
- Also available PO (650 mg TID for 5 days) for menorrhagia and dental procedures
- Topical TXA: soak gauze in 500 mg/5 mL solution and apply to wound -- useful for epistaxis and oral bleeding
- Adjust dose in renal impairment (CrCl <30: reduce dose by 50%)

### Massive Transfusion Nursing Priorities
- Assign one nurse to blood bank communication and product tracking
- Keep fibrinogen level >150 mg/dL (check q30-60 min during active MTP)
- Warm all products through a blood warmer to prevent hypothermia-induced coagulopathy
- Monitor ionized calcium (citrate in blood products chelates calcium → coagulopathy worsens)
- Document all products transfused with times and lot numbers
sections.drug-interactions:
- **DDAVP + NSAIDs/Aspirin** -- Additive antiplatelet effect with DDAVP's vWF-mediated platelet activation; however, NSAIDs are generally avoided in bleeding disorders regardless
- **TXA + Combined oral contraceptives** -- theoretical increased thrombotic risk; use with caution
- **TXA + Factor IX complex concentrates (FEIBA, PCC)** -- increased risk of thrombosis; avoid concurrent use or separate by 12 hours
- **rFVIIa + PCC/FEIBA** -- synergistic thrombotic risk; avoid combination
- **Factor VIII/IX + Emicizumab** -- emicizumab (bispecific antibody mimicking factor VIII function) complicates factor VIII monitoring (artifactually elevated aPTT normalization); coordinate with hematology for accurate assay interpretation
- **DDAVP + Carbamazepine/Chlorpropamide/SSRIs** -- enhanced antidiuretic effect; increased hyponatremia risk
sections.keyPoints:
- Cryoprecipitate is the go-to blood bank product for fibrinogen replacement (10-unit pool raises fibrinogen ~50-70 mg/dL); it also contains factor VIII, vWF, and factor XIII
- Fibrinogen concentrate (RiaSTAP) is faster to administer than cryo (no thawing, no ABO matching) but is significantly more expensive
- DDAVP is first-line for mild hemophilia A and type 1 vWD -- always test with a trial dose before relying on it in emergencies
- DDAVP does NOT work for hemophilia B, type 3 vWD, or severe hemophilia A
- Factor dosing formulas differ: factor VIII uses weight x desired rise x 0.5; factor IX uses weight x desired rise x 1.0 (due to lower recovery)
- TXA must be given within 3 hours of traumatic injury -- after 3 hours, mortality INCREASES (CRASH-2)
- rFVIIa is a salvage therapy for refractory bleeding -- expensive, short-acting, and carries real thrombotic risk
- In the ED, always ask hemophilia patients about their factor type, inhibitor status, usual product, and usual dose -- they are experts in their own disease
sections.related-topics:
- Blood Products and Transfusion
- Coagulation and Hemostasis
- Hemophilia
- Von Willebrand Disease
- Reversal Agents
- Anticoagulants
- Antiplatelet Agents

#pharmacology #hematology #ed-critical #transfusion #coagulation-factors

clinicalPearls: **10 units of cryo = 1 pool = one dose** -- this is the standard adult dose. Each individual unit is only ~15 mL with 150-250 mg fibrinogen. Giving single units is pointless. Always order pooled.

**Fibrinogen is the first factor to become critically depleted in massive hemorrhage** -- it reaches critical levels (<150 mg/dL) before other factors become clinically deficient. Trend fibrinogen early and often during MTP activation.

**DDAVP tachyphylaxis is real and predictable** -- after 2-3 doses at 12-24 hour intervals, endothelial vWF stores are depleted and DDAVP stops working. If bleeding continues beyond 48-72 hours, you MUST switch to factor concentrate.

**TXA over 10 minutes, not 10 seconds** -- rapid IV push causes hypotension and lowers the seizure threshold. Set up a minibag (1 g in 100 mL NS) on a pump. In trauma, time pressure is real, but a 10-minute infusion is still fast.

**Never give TXA after 3 hours post-injury** -- the CRASH-2 trial showed clear mortality benefit within 3 hours but INCREASED mortality when given after 3 hours. Late TXA appears to promote thrombosis without preventing ongoing hemorrhage. Time of injury matters.

**Hemophilia patients with head trauma get factor FIRST, CT second** -- do not wait for imaging to confirm bleeding before treating. In a patient with severe hemophilia A, give 50 IU/kg factor VIII immediately upon arrival, then scan. Intracranial hemorrhage in hemophilia is lethal without immediate replacement.

**Topical TXA is an underused tool** -- soaking gauze or a dental roll in TXA solution and applying directly to a bleeding site (epistaxis, oral laceration, post-extraction socket) provides excellent local hemostasis without systemic effects. Keep TXA ampules accessible in the ED.

**DDAVP causes hyponatremia, not just in theory** -- fluid-restrict for 24 hours after any DDAVP dose. Monitor sodium at 4-6 hours and again at 24 hours, especially in children and elderly patients who are at highest risk for symptomatic hyponatremia.

**rFVIIa is NOT a substitute for surgical hemostasis** -- it is a pharmacologic bridge to allow surgeons to achieve source control in refractory hemorrhage. If the surgeon cannot identify and fix the bleeding source, no amount of factor VIIa will save the patient.

diuretics.title: Diuretics

sections.mechanism:
- **Loop diuretics** (furosemide, bumetanide): Block Na-K-2Cl cotransporter in thick ascending loop of Henle; most potent diuresis
- **Thiazide diuretics** (hydrochlorothiazide): Block Na-Cl cotransporter in distal convoluted tubule; moderate diuresis
- **K-sparing diuretics** (spironolactone): Aldosterone antagonist in collecting duct; weak diuresis but preserves potassium
- **Metolazone** (thiazide-like): Acts on proximal and distal tubule; synergistic with loops
sections.indications:
- Acute Heart Failure / Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction (loop diuretics first-line for volume overload)
- Hypertension (thiazides first-line for HTN)
- Alcoholic Liver Disease / Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure with ascites (spironolactone + furosemide, classic 100:40 ratio)
- Nephrotic Syndrome
- Cerebral edema, pulmonary edema
- Hyperaldosteronism (spironolactone)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Furosemide | PO/IV | 20-80 mg PO daily; 20-40 mg IV | PO bioavailability ~50%; IV:PO ratio 1:2 |
| Bumetanide | PO/IV | 0.5-2 mg PO/IV | 40x more potent than furosemide (1 mg bumetanide = 40 mg furosemide) |
| Hydrochlorothiazide (HCTZ) | PO | 12.5-50 mg daily | Preferred for HTN; loses efficacy if CrCl < 30 |
| Spironolactone | PO | 25-50 mg daily (HF); up to 400 mg (ascites) | Mortality benefit in HFrEF (RALES trial); anti-androgen effects |
| Metolazone | PO | 2.5-10 mg daily | Give 30 min before loop diuretic for synergistic effect in refractory edema |
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Furosemide IV:** Onset 5 min, peak 30 min, duration 2 hr
- **Furosemide PO:** Onset 30-60 min, peak 1-2 hr, duration 6-8 hr
- **Bumetanide:** More predictable PO absorption than furosemide
- **HCTZ:** Onset 2 hr, duration 6-12 hr
- **Spironolactone:** Onset 24-48 hr; full effect takes 2-3 days

> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — diuretic efficacy and toxicity depend heavily on renal function; loop diuretic dosing must be adjusted in CKD*
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Hypokalemia (loops, thiazides) -- monitor K closely
- Hyponatremia (thiazides more than loops)
- Hyperuricemia / gout flares (loops, thiazides)
- Dehydration, orthostatic hypotension
- Hyperkalemia (spironolactone) -- do NOT combine with K supplements without careful monitoring
- Gynecomastia, menstrual irregularities (spironolactone)

### Serious/Black Box
- Severe electrolyte derangement leading to arrhythmias
- Ototoxicity (loop diuretics, especially with rapid IV push or concurrent aminoglycosides)
- Sulfa allergy cross-reactivity (furosemide, HCTZ, bumetanide -- risk is low but document allergy)
sections.contraindications:
- Anuria (loops will not work without some renal function)
- Severe hypokalemia or hyponatremia (correct first)
- Spironolactone: K > 5.0 mEq/L, severe renal failure (CrCl < 10)
- Hepatic encephalopathy with electrolyte imbalance (use cautiously)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Daily weights (most reliable indicator of fluid status; 1 kg = ~1 L fluid)
  - Strict I&Os; assess urine output within 1-2 hr of IV dose
  - BMP (K, Na, Cr, BUN, Mg) at baseline and regularly; Mg often depleted with K
  - Blood pressure, orthostatic vitals
  - Heart rhythm (hypokalemia increases digoxin toxicity)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take morning dose early to avoid nocturia
  - Report dizziness, muscle cramps, hearing changes
  - Eat potassium-rich foods if on loops/thiazides (bananas, oranges, potatoes)
  - Weigh daily; report gain > 2 lbs/day or 5 lbs/week
- **Administration Notes:**
  - IV furosemide: push no faster than 20 mg/min (ototoxicity risk)
  - Bumetanide preferred if inconsistent PO furosemide response
  - Metolazone: administer 30 min BEFORE the loop diuretic
  - HF exacerbation: IV dose should be >= home PO total daily dose
sections.key-conditions:
- Acute Heart Failure — IV loop diuretics are cornerstone of decongestion therapy
- Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction — spironolactone has mortality benefit (RALES trial); loop diuretics for symptom management
- Hyperkalemia — loop diuretics promote renal potassium excretion as adjunct therapy
- Acute Kidney Injury — diuretics manage volume overload but do NOT prevent or treat AKI itself; use cautiously
- Chronic Kidney Disease — loop diuretics preferred (thiazides lose efficacy at low GFR); dose escalation often needed
- Alcoholic Liver Disease — spironolactone + furosemide for ascites management
- Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure — careful diuresis; monitor for hepatorenal syndrome and encephalopathy

---

clinicalPearls: In HF: if patient does not respond to IV furosemide bolus, consider continuous infusion (start 5-10 mg/hr after bolus) or add metolazone
Furosemide drip is useful in ICU for titration; bumetanide drip also an option
**Hold parameters:** Hold if SBP < 90, K < 3.0, or acute kidney injury with rising creatinine (call provider)
Spironolactone in HFrEF (EF <= 35%): proven mortality benefit; keep K < 5.0 and Cr < 2.5 to continue safely
Thiazides can cause **hypercalcemia** (retain calcium); loops cause **hypocalcemia** (waste calcium) -- this is a common test question
Replace Mg with K -- hypomagnesemia makes hypokalemia refractory to replacement
relatedNotes: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

immunobiologics-and-biologics.title: Immunobiologics and Biologics

sections.definition:
Biologics are large, complex molecules produced by living cells that target specific components of the immune system. Unlike traditional small-molecule drugs, biologics are proteins (monoclonal antibodies, fusion proteins, or recombinant cytokines) that modulate immune pathways with high specificity. They have revolutionized the treatment of autoimmune diseases, cancers, and inflammatory conditions — but their immunosuppressive nature carries significant infection risk and requires rigorous screening and monitoring.

**Core principle:** Every biologic that suppresses immunity trades therapeutic benefit for infection susceptibility. The nurse's role is vigilant monitoring for infection in patients who may NOT mount a normal inflammatory response (no fever, no elevated CRP, no leukocytosis).
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — biologics are large proteins; cannot be given orally (destroyed by GI proteases); must be IV or subcutaneous; immunogenicity affects long-term efficacy*

> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — TNF-alpha, IL-1, and IL-6 are the key cytokines produced by macrophages that drive the inflammatory cascade and systemic acute phase response; biologics intercept specific nodes of this pathway to suppress dysregulated inflammation*

### TNF-alpha Inhibitors
- **Tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-a)** is a pro-inflammatory cytokine central to the inflammatory cascade
- TNF inhibitors bind and neutralize soluble and membrane-bound TNF-a → reduce inflammation, tissue destruction, and immune cell recruitment
- Agents: infliximab (chimeric mAb), adalimumab (fully human mAb), etanercept (fusion protein/soluble TNF receptor), golimumab, certolizumab
- Used in: rheumatoid arthritis, Crohn disease, ulcerative colitis, psoriasis, ankylosing spondylitis

### IL-6 Inhibitors
- **Interleukin-6** drives acute phase response (CRP production, fever, neutrophilia) and is a key mediator in cytokine storm
- Tocilizumab and sarilumab block the IL-6 receptor → suppress acute phase response
- Critical implication: **IL-6 blockade masks CRP and fever** — normal CRP and absence of fever do NOT rule out infection
- Used in: cytokine release syndrome (CRS) from CAR-T therapy, COVID-19 cytokine storm, giant cell arteritis, RA

### IL-1 Inhibitors
- **Interleukin-1** (IL-1a and IL-1b) mediates fever, pain, and the inflammasome pathway
- Anakinra (IL-1 receptor antagonist) blocks IL-1 signaling → rapid anti-inflammatory effect
- Short half-life (~4–6 hours) — advantage in acute/critical settings where you want reversibility
- Used in: acute gout flare (when NSAIDs/colchicine/steroids contraindicated), Still disease (adult and juvenile), familial Mediterranean fever, macrophage activation syndrome, ongoing sepsis research

### Anti-CD20 (B-cell Depletion)
- **Rituximab** binds CD20 on B lymphocytes → complement-dependent and antibody-dependent cytotoxicity → B-cell depletion lasting 6–12 months
- Depletes B cells → reduces autoantibody production and B-cell-mediated antigen presentation
- Used in: non-Hodgkin lymphoma, RA refractory to TNF inhibitors, ANCA vasculitis, refractory TTP, pemphigus vulgaris, certain nephrotic syndromes

### Intravenous Immunoglobulin (IVIG)
- Pooled polyclonal IgG from thousands of donors
- Mechanism is multifactorial and not fully understood:
  - Fc receptor blockade on macrophages → reduces antibody-mediated cell destruction
  - Anti-idiotype antibodies → neutralize pathogenic autoantibodies
  - Complement modulation
  - Immune regulation via multiple pathways
- Used in: Guillain-Barre syndrome, Kawasaki disease, ITP, myasthenia gravis crisis, chronic inflammatory demyelinating polyneuropathy (CIDP)

### Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors (ICIs)
- Cancer cells exploit immune checkpoints (PD-1/PD-L1, CTLA-4) to evade T-cell killing
- Checkpoint inhibitors **release the brakes** on T-cell anti-tumor activity:
  - **PD-1 inhibitors:** pembrolizumab, nivolumab
  - **PD-L1 inhibitors:** atezolizumab, durvalumab
  - **CTLA-4 inhibitors:** ipilimumab
- Result: enhanced T-cell recognition and destruction of tumor cells
- Trade-off: unleashed T cells may attack normal tissue → immune-related adverse events (irAEs)
sections.key-agents:
| Drug | Dose / Route | Onset / Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-------------|-----------------|-----------|
| **Infliximab** (Remicade) | 3–10 mg/kg IV at 0, 2, 6 wk then q8wk | Onset 1–2 wk; duration 8 wk | Chimeric (mouse/human) → higher immunogenicity; give with methotrexate to reduce anti-drug antibodies |
| **Adalimumab** (Humira) | 40 mg SubQ q2wk | Onset 2–4 wk; duration 2 wk | Fully human mAb; most prescribed biologic worldwide; now available as biosimilars |
| **Etanercept** (Enbrel) | 50 mg SubQ weekly | Onset 1–2 wk; duration 1 wk | Fusion protein (not true mAb); NOT effective in Crohn disease (unlike infliximab/adalimumab) |
| **Tocilizumab** (Actemira) | 4–8 mg/kg IV q4wk; or 162 mg SubQ weekly | Onset hours (IV); duration 2–4 wk | Masks CRP and fever — infection may present without typical inflammatory markers |
| **Anakinra** (Kineret) | 100 mg SubQ daily; or 2–10 mg/kg IV for critical illness | Onset 4–6 h; half-life 4–6 h | Short half-life = fast reversibility; advantage in critically ill; injection site reactions common (~70%) |
| **Rituximab** (Rituxan) | 375 mg/m2 IV weekly ×4 (lymphoma); 1000 mg IV ×2 doses 2 wk apart (RA) | B-cell depletion in 2–4 wk; lasts 6–12 mo | Premedicate with acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone; high first-dose reaction rate |
| **IVIG** | GBS: 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days; Kawasaki: 2 g/kg single dose; ITP: 1 g/kg × 1–2 days | Onset hours–days; duration 3–4 wk | Infuse slowly; headache/fever common; rare aseptic meningitis, AKI (sucrose-containing formulations), thrombosis |
| **Pembrolizumab** (Keytruda) | 200 mg IV q3wk or 400 mg q6wk | Onset 2–4 wk; duration variable | Anti-PD-1; irAEs can occur any time including months after discontinuation |
| **Nivolumab** (Opdivo) | 240 mg IV q2wk or 480 mg q4wk | Onset 2–4 wk; duration variable | Anti-PD-1; often combined with ipilimumab (higher efficacy but higher irAE rate) |
| **Ipilimumab** (Yervoy) | 3 mg/kg IV q3wk × 4 doses | Onset 2–6 wk; duration months | Anti-CTLA-4; highest irAE rate of all ICIs; colitis is the most dangerous complication |
sections.clinical-indications:
### Autoimmune and Inflammatory
- **Rheumatoid arthritis:** TNF inhibitors (first-line biologic), tocilizumab, rituximab (refractory)
- **Crohn disease / Ulcerative colitis:** infliximab, adalimumab, vedolizumab (gut-selective integrin inhibitor)
- **Psoriasis / Psoriatic arthritis:** TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors (secukinumab), IL-23 inhibitors (guselkumab)
- **Ankylosing spondylitis:** TNF inhibitors, IL-17 inhibitors
- **Giant cell arteritis:** tocilizumab (steroid-sparing)

### Hematologic/Oncologic
- **Non-Hodgkin lymphoma:** rituximab (backbone of R-CHOP regimen)
- **Chronic lymphocytic leukemia:** rituximab, obinutuzumab
- **Multiple myeloma:** daratumumab (anti-CD38)
- **Solid tumors (melanoma, NSCLC, RCC, bladder, etc.):** pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab
- **CAR-T cytokine release syndrome:** tocilizumab (first-line for CRS grade 2+)

### Critical Care / Emergency
- **Kawasaki disease:** IVIG 2 g/kg + aspirin (prevents coronary artery aneurysms)
- **Guillain-Barre syndrome:** IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days (or plasmapheresis)
- **Myasthenic crisis:** IVIG or plasmapheresis as bridge
- **Severe COVID-19 / cytokine storm:** tocilizumab (per NIH guidelines for hospitalized patients on supplemental O2)
- **Refractory TTP:** rituximab (adjunct to plasma exchange)

### Immunodeficiency Replacement
- **Primary immunodeficiency (CVID, XLA):** IVIG or SubQ immunoglobulin replacement every 3–4 weeks (goal trough IgG >500 mg/dL)
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### Infection Risk (ALL Biologics)
- Increased susceptibility to bacterial, viral, fungal, and opportunistic infections
- **TB reactivation** — screen ALL patients before starting TNF inhibitors, tocilizumab, or rituximab:
  - TST (tuberculin skin test) or IGRA (QuantiFERON-Gold / T-SPOT)
  - If latent TB: treat with isoniazid × 9 months BEFORE starting biologic (minimum 1 month lead-in)
- **Hepatitis B reactivation** — check HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs before rituximab and TNF inhibitors
  - If HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+: start antiviral prophylaxis (entecavir) before biologic
  - HBV reactivation on rituximab can be fatal
- **Progressive multifocal leukoencephalopathy (PML)** — JC virus reactivation causing fatal demyelinating brain disease
  - Risk with rituximab, natalizumab (MS drug), prolonged immunosuppression
  - No treatment — prevention and early detection are critical
  - Symptoms: progressive focal neurological deficits, confusion, visual changes
- **Invasive fungal infections** — histoplasmosis, coccidioidomycosis, aspergillosis (especially with TNF inhibitors)

### Immune-Related Adverse Events (irAEs) — Checkpoint Inhibitors
- The immune system "over-activates" and attacks normal tissue
- Can affect ANY organ system; most common:

| irAE | Presentation | Management |
|------|-------------|------------|
| **Colitis** | Diarrhea (>4 stools/day over baseline), abdominal pain, bloody stool | Grade 1–2: loperamide; Grade 3+: hold ICI + high-dose steroids (methylprednisolone 1–2 mg/kg); infliximab if steroid-refractory |
| **Hepatitis** | AST/ALT elevation, jaundice | Hold ICI; steroids; mycophenolate if refractory; do NOT use infliximab (hepatotoxic) |
| **Pneumonitis** | Dyspnea, cough, hypoxia, ground-glass on CT | Hold ICI; high-dose steroids; bronchoscopy to rule out infection |
| **Thyroiditis** | Transient thyrotoxicosis → hypothyroidism | TSH monitoring q6wk; levothyroxine for hypothyroid phase; usually does not require ICI discontinuation |
| **Myocarditis** | Chest pain, arrhythmia, troponin elevation, heart failure | **MEDICAL EMERGENCY** — mortality >40%; hold ICI permanently; high-dose methylprednisolone 1g IV × 3 days; cardiac ICU |
| **Dermatitis** | Maculopapular rash, pruritus, vitiligo | Topical steroids; usually does not require ICI discontinuation |
| **Adrenal insufficiency** | Fatigue, hypotension, hyponatremia, hyperkalemia | Cortisol level; hydrocortisone replacement; may be permanent |

- irAEs can present **weeks to months after discontinuation** — maintain surveillance
- **Ipilimumab + nivolumab combination** has the highest irAE rate (up to 60% grade 3–4)

### Infusion Reactions (All IV Biologics)
- Mild: flushing, pruritus, headache, low-grade fever
- Severe: anaphylaxis, bronchospasm, hypotension, angioedema
- Highest risk: rituximab first dose (up to 77% have some reaction), infliximab
- Prevention: premedicate with acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, +/- corticosteroids
- Management: slow or stop infusion → diphenhydramine, epinephrine if anaphylaxis → may rechallenge at slower rate

### IVIG-Specific Adverse Effects
- **Headache** (most common, up to 50%) — slow infusion rate, premedicate with acetaminophen/ibuprofen
- **Aseptic meningitis** — severe headache, photophobia, stiff neck with negative cultures; self-limited
- **Acute kidney injury** — especially sucrose-containing products; use non-sucrose formulations in renal impairment
- **Thrombotic events** — DVT, PE, stroke, MI; risk increases with high dose, rapid infusion, and prothrombotic states
- **Hemolytic anemia** — anti-A/anti-B antibodies in pooled product; check blood type, monitor for hemolysis in non-O blood types
- **Transfusion-related acute lung injury (TRALI)** — rare but life-threatening
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Pre-Treatment Screening (ALL Biologics)
- **TB screening:** TST or IGRA — document result before first dose
- **Hepatitis panel:** HBsAg, anti-HBc, anti-HBs — especially before rituximab, TNF inhibitors
- **Baseline labs:** CBC with differential, CMP (LFTs, renal function), LDH, quantitative immunoglobulins (before rituximab)
- **Infection assessment:** ask about active infections, fever, recent antibiotic use, sick contacts
- **Vaccination review:** update ALL vaccines BEFORE starting biologics (live vaccines contraindicated once on therapy — MMR, varicella, zoster live, yellow fever, intranasal influenza)
- **Pregnancy test** — many biologics are category X or have limited safety data

### Infusion Monitoring
- Verify premedications given 30–60 min before infusion (per protocol)
- **Vitals:** baseline, then q15 min for first hour, q30 min thereafter (facility-specific)
- Have **emergency equipment** at bedside: epinephrine, diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone, oxygen, suction, bag-valve mask
- **Rituximab first dose:** expect reaction — start at 50 mg/hr, increase by 50 mg/hr q30 min to max 400 mg/hr; hold for ANY reaction
- Monitor for infusion reaction signs: flushing, chills, rigors, dyspnea, chest tightness, rash, hypotension
- If reaction: STOP infusion → assess → administer rescue meds → notify provider → may restart at 50% rate once stable

### Ongoing Monitoring While on Biologics
- **Infection vigilance** — educate patients to report fever, cough, dysuria, wound drainage, malaise IMMEDIATELY
- On IL-6 inhibitors: **CRP and fever are unreliable** — infection may present with only tachycardia, hypotension, or malaise
- Monitor CBC with differential (neutropenia risk with rituximab, tocilizumab)
- Monitor LFTs (hepatotoxicity risk with TNF inhibitors, checkpoint inhibitors)
- Lipid panel on tocilizumab (increases LDL)
- TSH q6 weeks on checkpoint inhibitors (thyroiditis)
- Quantitative immunoglobulins q6–12 months on rituximab (hypogammaglobulinemia → recurrent infections)

### Checkpoint Inhibitor Monitoring
- **Teach patients the irAE red flags:**
  - New or worsening diarrhea (>4 stools/day)
  - New rash or skin changes
  - Shortness of breath or new cough
  - Jaundice, dark urine, or RUQ pain
  - Extreme fatigue, dizziness, or weight loss
  - Chest pain or new palpitations
- **Baseline labs before each cycle:** CBC, CMP (LFTs, creatinine), TSH, glucose, lipase
- **irAEs can occur at ANY time** — including months after the last dose
- If suspected irAE: hold next dose, stat labs, imaging as appropriate, contact oncology immediately

### IVIG Administration
- Use infusion pump — NEVER free-flow
- Start slowly: 0.5–1 mL/kg/hr for first 30 min → titrate up per protocol (max rate varies by product)
- Hydrate before and after infusion (reduces headache and AKI risk)
- Monitor for hemolysis in non-type O patients (check DAT/Coombs, reticulocyte count, haptoglobin if symptomatic)
- Thrombosis prophylaxis: early ambulation, adequate hydration, SCDs; consider anticoagulation in high-risk patients
sections.drug-interactions:
| Interaction | Clinical Significance |
|------------|----------------------|
| Biologics + live vaccines | CONTRAINDICATED — risk of vaccine-strain infection in immunosuppressed patient |
| TNF inhibitors + anakinra | DO NOT combine — excessive immunosuppression, serious infection risk (black box warning) |
| Rituximab + live vaccines | Wait ≥6 months after last rituximab dose before live vaccination; check B-cell recovery |
| Tocilizumab + CYP450 substrates | IL-6 suppresses CYP450; tocilizumab restores CYP activity → may INCREASE metabolism of warfarin, statins, cyclosporine; recheck levels |
| IVIG + live vaccines | Wait ≥3 months after IVIG before MMR or varicella (passive antibodies interfere with vaccine response) |
| Checkpoint inhibitors + corticosteroids | High-dose steroids (>10 mg prednisone equivalent) may reduce ICI efficacy; use only for irAE management, not routine premedication |
| Multiple biologics | Generally avoid combining two biologics — additive immunosuppression → serious/fatal infections |
sections.keyPoints:
- **Screen for TB and Hepatitis B BEFORE starting any biologic** — reactivation can be fatal
- TNF inhibitors are the most widely used biologic class; infliximab requires IV infusion, adalimumab is subcutaneous
- Tocilizumab masks CRP and fever — an afebrile patient with normal CRP on IL-6 blockade may still have sepsis
- Rituximab depletes B cells for 6–12 months; monitor immunoglobulin levels and infection risk
- IVIG has multiple mechanisms and uses; infuse slowly, hydrate well, watch for thrombosis
- Checkpoint inhibitor irAEs can affect any organ, occur at any time, and may be life-threatening (especially myocarditis, colitis, pneumonitis)
- Live vaccines are contraindicated on ALL biologics — update vaccines BEFORE starting therapy

clinicalPearls: **"No fever doesn't mean no infection on tocilizumab"** — IL-6 is THE cytokine that drives fever and CRP production. Block it, and your patient can have raging pneumonia with a temp of 98.6 and a CRP of 0.2. Tachycardia, hypotension, and clinical gestalt are your only clues. This catches people off guard.

**TB screening is not optional — it's medicolegal** — every major biologic has a black box warning about TB reactivation. If a patient reactivates TB on a biologic and there's no documented pre-treatment screening, that's a liability. TST or IGRA, documented, before dose one.

**Rituximab first dose: expect a reaction, plan for it** — up to 77% of patients react to the first rituximab infusion (fevers, rigors, hypotension). This is cytokine release from lysing B cells, not true allergy. Premedicate aggressively, start slowly, and have the rescue meds drawn up. Most patients tolerate subsequent doses fine.

**irAEs can show up months after the last checkpoint inhibitor dose** — the immune activation from pembrolizumab or ipilimumab persists long after the drug is cleared. Patients discharged from oncology can present to the ED with new-onset colitis, hepatitis, or myocarditis weeks to months later. Always ask: "Have you ever received immunotherapy?"

**Myocarditis from checkpoint inhibitors kills fast** — mortality exceeds 40%. Any chest pain, new arrhythmia, or troponin elevation in a patient on (or recently off) a checkpoint inhibitor is an emergency. ICU admission, stat echocardiogram, cardiology and oncology consults, and high-dose methylprednisolone (1g IV daily x3) without waiting for biopsy confirmation.

**IVIG is not just "antibody replacement"** — the immunomodulatory dose (2 g/kg for Kawasaki, 1 g/kg for ITP) works through completely different mechanisms than the replacement dose (0.4 g/kg monthly for immunodeficiency). The high-dose regimen overwhelms Fc receptors and modulates immune function; the low-dose regimen simply provides passive antibodies. Different doses, different goals.

**Biologics and surgery: know the washout** — each biologic has a different half-life and immunosuppressive duration. Elective surgery generally requires holding the biologic for 2–5 half-lives before the procedure. Coordinate with rheumatology/oncology and anesthesia. Rituximab may need 6+ months; adalimumab needs ~2–4 weeks.

inhaled-corticosteroids.title: Inhaled Corticosteroids

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

Suppress airway inflammation by inhibiting multiple inflammatory pathways: reduce cytokine production, decrease eosinophil infiltration, inhibit mast cell mediator release, and reduce airway edema and mucus hypersecretion. Reduce airway hyperresponsiveness over time. Effect is local (topical to airways) with minimal systemic absorption when used correctly.
sections.indications:
- Asthma -- cornerstone of controller therapy (Step 2 and above per GINA)
- COPD -- in combination with LABA for patients with frequent exacerbations and eosinophilia (not monotherapy)
- Eosinophilic Bronchitis
- Croup (nebulized budesonide)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose (Adult) | Key Difference |
|------|-------|---------------------|----------------|
| Fluticasone propionate | MDI | Low: 88 mcg BID; Med: 220 mcg BID; High: 440 mcg BID | Most commonly prescribed; available as MDI and DPI (Flovent HFA / ArmonAir) |
| Budesonide | DPI/Neb | DPI: 180-360 mcg BID; Neb: 0.25-1 mg BID | Only nebulized ICS available; preferred in young children; Category B in pregnancy (safest ICS) |
| Beclomethasone | MDI | 40-320 mcg BID | Older agent; small particle size (reaches small airways well) |
| Fluticasone furoate | DPI | 100-200 mcg daily | Once-daily dosing (Arnuity Ellipta); improved adherence |
| Mometasone | DPI/MDI | 220-440 mcg daily-BID | Once or twice daily; low systemic bioavailability |
sections.asthma-step-therapy-simplified-ginanaepp:
| Step | Controller | Rescue |
|------|-----------|--------|
| Step 1 | PRN low-dose ICS-formoterol or PRN SABA | SABA PRN |
| Step 2 | Low-dose ICS daily | SABA PRN |
| Step 3 | Low-dose ICS + LABA | SABA PRN |
| Step 4 | Medium-dose ICS + LABA | SABA PRN |
| Step 5 | High-dose ICS + LABA +/- add-on (LAMA, biologic) | SABA PRN |

*Current GINA guidelines prefer ICS-formoterol as both maintenance and reliever (MART)*
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- **Onset:** Not immediate; clinical improvement begins within days, full effect in 1-4 weeks
- Deposited in airways; majority swallowed and undergoes first-pass hepatic metabolism
- Systemic absorption is low with proper technique (< 20% reaches lungs, rest deposits in oropharynx)
- Fluticasone propionate: High lipophilicity, longer local residence time
- Budesonide: Moderate lipophilicity, well-studied in pregnancy
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Oropharyngeal candidiasis (thrush) -- mitigate with mouth rinsing
- Dysphonia (hoarseness) -- due to local steroid myopathy of vocal cords
- Cough, throat irritation
- Upper respiratory infections

### Serious/Black Box
- **High-dose/long-term:** Adrenal suppression (rare with standard ICS doses); growth suppression in children (approximately 1 cm reduction in first year, generally not progressive)
- Osteoporosis risk with chronic high-dose ICS (much less than systemic steroids)
- Glaucoma, posterior subcapsular cataracts (high-dose, long-term use)
- Paradoxical bronchospasm (rare)
- Pneumonia risk: Slightly increased with ICS use in COPD (especially fluticasone)
sections.contraindications:
- Primary treatment of acute bronchospasm / status asthmaticus (ICS is NOT a rescue medication)
- Active untreated oral fungal infection
- Severe hypersensitivity to drug or excipients (including milk protein allergy for DPI formulations containing lactose)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Peak flow / FEV1 trends to assess response
  - Oral cavity inspection for candidiasis (white patches on tongue/buccal mucosa)
  - Growth velocity in pediatric patients (plot height at each visit)
  - Asthma control assessment (frequency of rescue inhaler use, nighttime symptoms, activity limitation)
  - Bone density if on high-dose ICS for > 1 year (especially postmenopausal women)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - **Rinse mouth with water and spit after every use** -- most important patient education point; prevents thrush and dysphonia
  - ICS is a controller medication -- use DAILY even when feeling well; does NOT provide immediate relief
  - Proper inhaler technique: MDI with spacer preferred; DPI requires brisk inspiratory effort
  - Do NOT abruptly stop ICS -- may trigger asthma exacerbation; step down only under provider guidance
  - Report persistent sore throat, white patches in mouth, or voice changes
  - Full benefit takes 1-4 weeks; do not abandon therapy prematurely
- **Administration Notes:**
  - If using bronchodilator AND ICS: Use bronchodilator first (opens airways for better ICS deposition), wait 1-5 min, then use ICS
  - MDI: Shake well, use spacer, slow deep inhalation, hold breath 10 sec
  - DPI: Do NOT shake; do NOT use spacer; inhale quickly and deeply
  - Nebulized budesonide: Use jet nebulizer with mouthpiece or tight-fitting mask; wash face after mask use (prevent perioral dermatitis)

clinicalPearls: ICS is the MOST effective anti-inflammatory therapy for asthma -- reduces exacerbations, hospitalizations, and mortality
If stepping up from Step 2, adding a LABA is more effective than doubling ICS dose
**Never use LABA without ICS in asthma** (see [[Bronchodilators]] -- Black Box warning)
Budesonide nebulizer is the ICS of choice for young children (< 4 years) and for acute croup (0.5-2 mg neb)
Budesonide is preferred ICS in pregnancy (most safety data)
Switching from oral steroids to ICS: Taper [[Systemic Corticosteroids]] slowly to avoid adrenal crisis; ICS does NOT provide adequate systemic replacement
Patient using rescue inhaler > 2 days/week or > 2 nights/month needs step-up in controller therapy
ICS does NOT cause the same adverse effects as systemic corticosteroids at standard doses -- reassure patients

insulin.title: Insulin

sections.mechanism:
- Replaces or supplements endogenous insulin produced by pancreatic beta cells
- Binds insulin receptors on cell membranes → facilitates glucose uptake into skeletal muscle and adipose tissue
- Suppresses hepatic glucose production (gluconeogenesis and glycogenolysis)
- Promotes glycogen synthesis, protein synthesis, and lipogenesis
- Inhibits lipolysis and ketogenesis (critical in DKA management)
sections.indications:
- **Type 1 diabetes** (absolute requirement -- no endogenous insulin)
- **Type 2 diabetes** (when oral agents fail to achieve glycemic targets; or as initial therapy if A1C > 10% or symptomatic hyperglycemia)
- **DKA** (regular insulin IV infusion) -- see DKA
- **Hyperglycemic hyperosmolar state (HHS)**
- **Hyperkalemia** (insulin + dextrose drives K+ intracellularly)
- **Hospitalized patients** (basal-bolus preferred; sliding scale alone is NOT recommended)
- **Gestational diabetes** (insulin is safe in pregnancy; most oral agents are not)
- TPN-related hyperglycemia
sections.keyDrugs:
### Insulin Types by Onset/Duration

> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — insulin onset, peak, and duration differ by formulation due to absorption kinetics from subcutaneous tissue*

| Category | Drug | Onset | Peak | Duration | Appearance |
|----------|------|-------|------|----------|-----------|
| **Rapid-acting** | Lispro (Humalog) | 10-15 min | 1-2 hr | 3-5 hr | Clear |
| | Aspart (NovoLog) | 10-15 min | 1-2 hr | 3-5 hr | Clear |
| | Glulisine (Apidra) | 10-15 min | 1-2 hr | 3-5 hr | Clear |
| **Short-acting** | Regular (Humulin R) | 30 min | 2-4 hr | 6-8 hr | Clear |
| **Intermediate** | NPH (Humulin N) | 1-2 hr | 6-10 hr | 12-18 hr | **Cloudy** (must roll gently to mix) |
| **Long-acting** | Glargine (Lantus, Basaglar) | 1-2 hr | **Peakless** | 20-24 hr | Clear |
| | Detemir (Levemir) | 1-2 hr | Relatively flat | 12-24 hr | Clear |
| | Degludec (Tresiba) | 1-2 hr | **Peakless** | >42 hr | Clear |
| **Premixed** | 70/30 (NPH/Regular) | 30 min | Dual peaks | ~12 hr | Cloudy |

### Basal-Bolus Regimen (Physiologic Dosing)
- **Basal:** Glargine or detemir once-twice daily (covers fasting/between-meal glucose)
- **Bolus (prandial):** Rapid-acting before each meal (covers carbohydrate intake)
- **Correction:** Additional rapid-acting units based on pre-meal glucose above target (correction factor)
- Total daily dose (TDD) estimate: 0.5-1 unit/kg/day (type 2); highly variable
sections.monitoring:
| Parameter | Frequency | Goals (General Adult) |
|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Fingerstick blood glucose | AC and HS minimum (before meals + bedtime); q1h on insulin drip | Fasting 80-130 mg/dL; pre-meal 80-130; post-meal peak < 180 |
| A1C | q3 months until at goal, then q6 months | < 7% for most adults; individualize (tighter for young/healthy; looser for elderly/comorbid) |
| Hypoglycemia episodes | Ongoing | Document every episode; < 70 mg/dL = hypoglycemia; < 54 = serious hypoglycemia |
| Injection sites | Each visit | Rotate sites; check for lipohypertrophy |
| Renal function | Annual minimum | Dose adjustment needed as CKD progresses (increased hypoglycemia risk -- insulin clearance decreases) |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- **Hypoglycemia** (most dangerous and most common)
  - Symptoms: tremor, diaphoresis, tachycardia, confusion, irritability, hunger, pallor
  - Severe: seizures, loss of consciousness, death
  - Treatment: Conscious → 15-20g fast-acting carbs (4 oz juice, glucose tabs), recheck in 15 min ("Rule of 15"); Unconscious → glucagon 1 mg IM/SubQ or dextrose 25g (D50 50 mL) IV
- Weight gain (insulin is anabolic)
- Injection site reactions (lipohypertrophy from not rotating sites)
- Peripheral edema (insulin-mediated sodium retention, usually transient)

### Serious
- **Severe hypoglycemia** → seizures, coma, death
- **Hypokalemia** (insulin shifts K+ intracellularly -- monitor K+ especially with IV insulin)
- Lipodystrophy (lipohypertrophy → erratic absorption)
sections.contraindications:
- Hypoglycemia (do not give if BG < 70 mg/dL)
- Known hypersensitivity to insulin type or excipients
- No absolute contraindications to insulin in a patient who needs it -- it is life-sustaining in Type 1 DM
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - ALWAYS check blood glucose before administering insulin
  - Check that patient will be eating before giving prandial insulin (hold if NPO)
  - Assess for signs of hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia
