  - Check potassium before initiating IV insulin (correct K+ < 3.3 before starting insulin in DKA)
- **Administration:**
  - SubQ injection: Abdomen (fastest absorption), outer thigh, upper arm, buttock
  - Rotate injection sites within the same region; avoid lipohypertrophy sites
  - NPH is the ONLY insulin that is cloudy -- roll gently (never shake)
  - When mixing NPH + Regular in same syringe: draw Regular FIRST ("clear before cloudy")
  - Rapid-acting analogs: Give within 15 min of meal start (or immediately after if intake uncertain)
  - Regular insulin: Give 30 min before meals
  - Glargine/Detemir: NEVER mix with other insulins; give at same time daily
  - IV insulin: Only REGULAR insulin can be given IV; use dedicated infusion pump; q1h glucose checks
  - High-alert medication: Independent double-check required per most facility protocols
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Injection technique: 90° angle, rotate sites, pen needle disposal
  - Hypoglycemia treatment: Carry fast-acting glucose at all times; wear MedicAlert ID
  - Sick day rules: Never stop basal insulin (even if not eating); check BG more frequently; call provider if BG > 300 or ketones present
  - Insulin storage: Unopened vials in refrigerator; in-use vials/pens at room temp (28-30 days typically); do not freeze; do not expose to extreme heat
  - Sharps disposal in approved container
sections.related-concepts:
- Oral Hypoglycemics
- Endocrine System
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics

clinicalPearls: **Insulin is a HIGH-ALERT medication** per ISMP. Most facilities require independent double-checks for all insulin doses. Mix-ups between insulin types are a leading cause of medication errors.
**"Clear before cloudy"** -- when drawing up Regular + NPH in the same syringe, always draw the Regular (clear) first to avoid contaminating the Regular vial with NPH.

**Hypoglycemia unawareness** develops in patients with frequent lows -- they lose the adrenergic warning symptoms (tremor, sweating, tachycardia) and progress directly to neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion, seizure). Strict avoidance of lows for 2-3 weeks can restore awareness.
**In DKA, check potassium BEFORE starting insulin.** If K+ < 3.3, replace potassium FIRST. Insulin drives K+ into cells and will worsen hypokalemia → fatal arrhythmia.
**NPO patients still need basal insulin** (usually at 50-80% of home dose). Basal insulin covers hepatic glucose output, not meals. Holding all insulin in an NPO Type 1 diabetic → DKA.
**Sliding scale alone is reactive, not proactive.** Basal-bolus is the standard of care for inpatient glycemic management. Sliding scale as a sole strategy results in glucose roller-coasters.
**Renal failure increases hypoglycemia risk** -- the kidneys clear ~30% of circulating insulin. As GFR declines, insulin requirements often decrease significantly.

**U-100 vs U-500:** U-500 regular insulin is 5x concentrated. It requires special syringes and is only for patients with severe insulin resistance (>200 units/day). Errors between U-100 and U-500 can be fatal.

laxatives-and-bowel-preps.title: Laxatives and Bowel Preps

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — Osmotic laxatives (PEG, lactulose, magnesium citrate) work by the same principle as any osmotic agent: a poorly-absorbed solute in the intestinal lumen retains water osmotically, softening stool and distending the bowel to trigger peristalsis; stimulant laxatives bypass osmosis entirely and directly activate the enteric nervous system (Auerbach's plexus)*

| Category | Mechanism | Onset |
|----------|-----------|-------|
| **Bulk-forming** (psyllium, methylcellulose) | Absorb water → increase stool bulk → stimulate peristalsis | 12-72 hr |
| **Stool softeners** (docusate sodium/calcium) | Surfactant → allows water/fat to penetrate stool → softer stool | 24-72 hr |
| **Osmotic** (PEG 3350, lactulose, magnesium citrate, sorbitol) | Draw water into intestinal lumen via osmotic gradient → distension → peristalsis | PEG: 24-48 hr; Mg citrate: 30 min-6 hr; Lactulose: 24-48 hr |
| **Stimulant** (senna, bisacodyl) | Direct stimulation of intestinal nerve plexus (Auerbach's) → increased motility + fluid/electrolyte secretion | PO: 6-12 hr; PR (bisacodyl suppository): 15-60 min |
| **Lubricant** (mineral oil) | Coats stool and intestinal lining → eases passage | 6-8 hr |
| **Chloride channel activators** (lubiprostone) | Activates ClC-2 chloride channels in intestinal epithelium → chloride-rich fluid secretion | 24 hr |
| **Guanylate cyclase-C agonists** (linaclotide, plecanatide) | Stimulate intestinal fluid secretion and accelerate transit | Variable |
| **Opioid antagonists** (methylnaltrexone, naloxegol) | Block peripheral mu-opioid receptors in GI tract → reverse opioid-induced constipation without affecting analgesia | Methylnaltrexone SubQ: 30-60 min |
sections.indications:
- Constipation (acute and chronic)
- Opioid-induced constipation (OIC) -- see Opioid Analgesics
- Hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose -- see Hepatic Encephalopathy)
- Hyperammonemia (lactulose)
- Bowel preparation before colonoscopy or surgery
- Fecal impaction
- IBS with constipation (linaclotide, lubiprostone)
- Hyperkalemia (sodium polystyrene sulfonate -- technically an exchange resin, often grouped here)
sections.key-drugs:
| Drug | Route/Dose | Category | Key Points |
|------|-----------|----------|-----------|
| **Docusate sodium (Colace)** | PO: 100-200 mg BID | Stool softener | Prevents straining (post-surgical, cardiac); NOT effective alone for established constipation; takes 1-3 days; overused and underwhelming |
| **Senna (Senokot)** | PO: 1-2 tabs HS | Stimulant | Most effective OTC laxative; pairs well with docusate for opioid constipation; safe for chronic use (melanosis coli cosmetic, not harmful); takes 6-12 hr (give at bedtime) |
| **Bisacodyl (Dulcolax)** | PO: 5-10 mg; PR: 10 mg | Stimulant | PO onset 6-12 hr; suppository onset 15-60 min; do not crush enteric-coated tablet; do not take with antacids or milk (premature coating dissolution → stomach cramps) |
| **PEG 3350 (MiraLAX)** | PO: 17 g in 8 oz water daily | Osmotic | OTC; well tolerated; no electrolyte shifts at standard dose; can take several days for full effect; first-line for chronic constipation |
| **PEG-electrolyte solution (GoLYTELY, NuLYTELY)** | PO: 240 mL q10-15 min until 4L consumed or effluent clear | Bowel prep | Colonoscopy/surgical prep; large volume poorly tolerated (nausea); split-dose protocol preferred (half evening, half morning); maintain NPO after prep |
| **Magnesium citrate** | PO: 150-300 mL (full bottle) | Osmotic/Saline | Rapid onset (30 min-6 hr); used for acute constipation and bowel prep; avoid in renal failure (hypermagnesemia); can cause significant fluid shifts |
| **Lactulose** | PO: 15-30 mL BID-QID | Osmotic | Primary use: hepatic encephalopathy (traps ammonia as NH4+ in colon → fecal excretion); goal: 2-3 soft stools/day; titrate to stool output; GI bloating/cramping common |
| **Methylnaltrexone (Relistor)** | SubQ: 8-12 mg PRN; max q24h | Peripheral opioid antagonist | For OIC when conventional laxatives fail; does NOT cross BBB → no reversal of pain relief; contraindicated in known/suspected GI obstruction |
| **Naloxegol (Movantik)** | PO: 25 mg daily on empty stomach | Peripheral opioid antagonist | Oral option for OIC; take on empty stomach; discontinue other laxatives before starting (can restart in 3 days if needed) |
sections.bowel-prep-protocols-colonoscopy:
| Protocol | Volume | Key Points |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| **PEG-ELS (GoLYTELY)** | 4L total | Split-dose preferred: 2L evening before + 2L morning of procedure; finish 4-6 hr before; clear liquid diet day before |
| **Low-volume prep (MiraLAX + Gatorade)** | MiraLAX 238g + 64 oz Gatorade | Better tolerated; split dose; often combined with bisacodyl tabs the evening before |
| **Suprep / Prepopik** | Low volume solutions | Commercial low-volume options; better compliance; still split-dose preferred |

> **Adequate bowel prep = adequate exam.** Poor prep → missed polyps → repeat colonoscopy. Nursing education on prep compliance is critical.
sections.monitoring:
| Parameter | Rationale |
|-----------|----------|
| Stool frequency, consistency (Bristol Scale) | Effectiveness assessment |
| Electrolytes (K+, Mg2+, Na+) | Osmotic laxatives and chronic use can cause imbalances |
| Hydration status | Laxatives draw water into bowel → dehydration risk |
| Bowel sounds, abdominal assessment | Rule out obstruction before giving laxatives (especially stimulants) |
| Ammonia level (lactulose) | Goal: 2-3 soft stools/day for hepatic encephalopathy |
sections.adverseEffects:
| Category | Common | Serious |
|----------|--------|---------|
| **Bulk-forming** | Bloating, gas; must take with adequate water | Esophageal/bowel obstruction if taken without enough fluid |
| **Stool softeners** | GI cramping (minimal) | Essentially none at standard doses |
| **Osmotic** | Bloating, cramping, flatulence, diarrhea | Electrolyte imbalances; magnesium toxicity in renal failure (Mg citrate); dehydration |
| **Stimulant** | Abdominal cramps, diarrhea | Electrolyte depletion with chronic overuse; melanosis coli (benign pigmentation of colon) |
| **Peripheral opioid antagonists** | Abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea | GI perforation (if occult obstruction present) |
sections.contraindications:
- **All laxatives:** Known or suspected bowel obstruction, perforation, toxic megacolon, acute surgical abdomen
- **Stimulant laxatives:** Acute inflammatory bowel conditions
- **Magnesium-containing:** Renal failure (hypermagnesemia → cardiac arrest)
- **Lactulose:** Galactosemia (contains galactose)
- **Peripheral opioid antagonists:** Known or suspected GI obstruction
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Document last BM, consistency, associated symptoms
  - Auscultate bowel sounds -- do NOT give stimulant laxatives if bowel sounds absent
  - Assess for fecal impaction (may need digital disimpaction before oral laxatives)
  - Review medication list for constipation-causing drugs (opioids, anticholinergics, calcium, iron)
- **Administration:**
  - Bulk-forming: MUST be taken with full glass of water (8 oz minimum); can cause obstruction if dry
  - Senna + docusate: Standard opioid constipation regimen; start day 1 of opioid therapy
  - PEG (MiraLAX): Mix in any clear liquid; tasteless/odorless = good compliance
  - Bowel prep: Encourage patient to use straw, drink cold, suck on hard candy between glasses to improve tolerance
  - Bisacodyl: Do not crush; do not give with milk or antacids within 1 hour
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Constipation prevention: Adequate fluids (1.5-2L/day), fiber (25-35 g/day), regular physical activity, respond to urge promptly
  - Opioid patients: "If you take opioids, you WILL get constipated. Take your bowel meds every day, not just when you're constipated."
  - Do not use stimulant laxatives daily long-term without medical guidance
  - Bowel prep: Clear liquids only day before; nothing red or purple (mimics blood on colonoscopy); finish prep as instructed; comply with NPO
sections.related-concepts:
- Gastrointestinal System
- Opioid Analgesics
- Nutrition and Metabolism

clinicalPearls: **Docusate alone is basically useless for opioid-induced constipation.** Opioids paralyze the bowel -- softening the stool doesn't help if peristalsis is gone. You need a stimulant (senna) + softener (docusate) at minimum. Escalate to PEG or peripheral opioid antagonists as needed.

**Opioid constipation prophylaxis starts DAY ONE.** Tolerance NEVER develops to opioid-induced constipation. Every patient started on scheduled opioids should get senna + docusate from the first dose.

**Lactulose for hepatic encephalopathy: Titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day.** More than that → dehydration and electrolyte imbalance. Less than that → ammonia continues to accumulate. This is a nursing-driven titration.

**Split-dose bowel prep improves colonoscopy quality** compared to all-evening prep. The second half taken early morning of the procedure ensures the right colon is clean. This is evidence-based and now standard of care.

**Magnesium citrate in renal failure = hypermagnesemia → respiratory depression → cardiac arrest.** Always check renal function before giving magnesium-containing laxatives. PEG is a safer osmotic choice in kidney disease.
**Methylnaltrexone works fast** (30-60 min SubQ). Make sure the patient is near a bathroom. Also make sure there's no undiagnosed bowel obstruction -- GI perforation is a rare but serious risk.
**Bristol Stool Scale is your documentation friend.** Type 1-2 = constipation. Type 3-4 = ideal. Type 5-7 = loose to watery. Use it for objective, reproducible communication.

local-anesthetics.title: Local Anesthetics

sections.definition:
Local anesthetics are among the most frequently used drugs in emergency medicine. They reversibly block nerve conduction by inhibiting sodium channels, providing regional anesthesia for wound repair, procedural pain, fracture management, and a wide range of nerve blocks. Mastery of local anesthetic pharmacology -- particularly maximum safe doses, onset/duration characteristics, and recognition and management of local anesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) -- is essential for safe ED practice.

Local anesthetics are classified into two chemical families: **amides** (lidocaine, bupivacaine, ropivacaine) and **esters** (procaine, tetracaine, benzocaine). The distinction matters clinically for allergy cross-reactivity, metabolism, and toxicity profiles.

**Memory aid:** Amides have TWO "i"s in the generic name (l**i**doca**i**ne, bup**i**vaca**i**ne, rop**i**vaca**i**ne). Esters have ONE "i" or none (proca**i**ne, tetracaine, benzocaine).
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

> *First principles: Pain Physiology and Management — local anesthetics interrupt pain at the transduction and transmission stages by blocking sodium-channel-dependent action potential propagation in peripheral nociceptors and afferent nerve fibers; differential blockade means small-diameter C fibers (slow pain, burning) are silenced before A-alpha motor fibers*

- All local anesthetics block voltage-gated sodium channels from the intracellular side of the nerve membrane
- They bind the channel in its **inactivated state**, preventing the channel from returning to the resting (activatable) state
- This prevents sodium influx, blocking depolarization and nerve impulse propagation
- **Differential blockade:** Small, unmyelinated C fibers (pain, temperature) are blocked before large, myelinated A-alpha fibers (motor, proprioception). Clinical order of blockade: pain/temperature > touch > pressure > motor
- **pH-dependent:** Local anesthetics exist in equilibrium between ionized (charged, water-soluble) and unionized (uncharged, lipid-soluble) forms. Only the unionized form crosses nerve membranes, but the ionized form binds the sodium channel. Infected/acidic tissue has more ionized drug --> reduced efficacy in infected wounds
- **Epinephrine addition:** Causes local vasoconstriction, reducing systemic absorption, prolonging duration, and decreasing bleeding. Also serves as a marker for inadvertent intravascular injection (tachycardia)
sections.key-agents:
| Drug | Class | Max Dose (plain) | Max Dose (with epi) | Onset | Duration (plain/with epi) | Key Pearl |
|------|-------|------------------|---------------------|-------|--------------------------|-----------|
| **Lidocaine** | Amide | 4.5 mg/kg | 7 mg/kg | 2-5 min | 30-60 min / 60-120 min | Workhorse of ED local anesthesia; fastest onset amide |
| **Bupivacaine** | Amide | 2.5 mg/kg | 3 mg/kg | 5-10 min | 2-4 hours / 3-7 hours | Longest duration; highest cardiotoxicity risk |
| **Ropivacaine** | Amide | 3 mg/kg | N/A (not typically combined) | 5-15 min | 2-6 hours | Less cardiotoxic than bupivacaine; preferentially blocks sensory > motor |
| **Procaine** | Ester | 7 mg/kg | 9 mg/kg | 2-5 min | 15-30 min / 30-60 min | Short-acting; primarily historical interest |
| **Tetracaine** | Ester | 1 mg/kg (topical doses lower) | N/A | 5-10 min | 2-3 hours | Primarily topical use; component of LET gel |
| **Benzocaine** | Ester | Topical only (max 1 spray application) | N/A | <1 min (topical) | 15-30 min | Methemoglobinemia risk -- limit exposure |

### Topical Formulations

| Formulation | Components | Application | Key Pearl |
|-------------|-----------|-------------|-----------|
| **LET gel** | Lidocaine 4% + Epinephrine 0.1% + Tetracaine 0.5% | Apply to wound edges x 20-30 min; look for blanching | Gold standard for pediatric laceration anesthesia; eliminates needle pain |
| **EMLA cream** | Lidocaine 2.5% + Prilocaine 2.5% | Apply under occlusive dressing x 45-60 min | Intact skin only; IV start prep in peds; methemoglobinemia risk in infants (prilocaine) |
| **Lidocaine 4% topical** | Lidocaine 4% cream/solution | Mucosal surfaces, wound irrigation | Useful for nares, oropharynx, urethra |
| **Benzocaine spray** | Benzocaine 20% | Oropharyngeal spray for intubation/procedures | ONE spray only -- methemoglobinemia risk increases with repeat dosing |
sections.clinical-indications:
### Wound Management
- **Local infiltration:** Direct injection around wound edges for laceration repair
- **Field block:** Injection of a wall of anesthetic around (not into) the operative field -- preferred for contaminated wounds (avoids injecting into infected tissue) and distorted anatomy
- **Buffering with bicarbonate:** Add 1 mL 8.4% NaHCO3 per 9 mL lidocaine (10:1 ratio) immediately before injection -- raises pH, increases unionized fraction, dramatically reduces injection pain. Bupivacaine: 30:1 ratio (precipitates more readily)

### Regional Nerve Blocks (ED Applications)
- **Digital block (finger/toe):** 2-4 mL lidocaine or bupivacaine at web space; ring block or single-injection technique. **Epinephrine is SAFE for digital blocks** -- the historical prohibition is based on case reports from the cocaine/procaine era; modern evidence (PHIND study and others) confirms safety with lidocaine + epinephrine
- **Hematoma block:** Direct injection into fracture hematoma (e.g., distal radius fracture) -- 5-10 mL lidocaine; provides rapid analgesia for fracture reduction
- **Femoral nerve block:** For Hip Fracture and femoral shaft fractures; landmark or ultrasound-guided
- **Fascia iliaca block:** For hip fracture analgesia; increasingly replacing femoral nerve block (easier, wider coverage)
- **Intercostal nerve block:** For rib fractures -- see pain management for Rib Fracture
- **Posterior tibial nerve block:** Sole of foot anesthesia
- **Infraorbital/mental nerve blocks:** Facial laceration repair

### Other Applications
- Topical anesthesia for pediatric laceration repair (LET gel)
- Lidocaine IV for refractory Ventricular Tachycardia/VF -- see Antiarrhythmics
- Lidocaine IV for attenuation of intubation response (controversial)
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### Local Reactions
- Injection site pain (mitigated by buffering, slow injection, small-gauge needle, warming solution)
- Local edema and erythema
- Hematoma formation (especially in vascular areas)
- Tissue ischemia from excessive epinephrine (rare; self-limited)

### Allergic Reactions
- **True allergy to amides is exceedingly rare** (<1% of reported "allergies")
- Most reported allergies are vasovagal reactions, epinephrine effects, or preservative (methylparaben) sensitivity
- **Ester allergy** is more common -- esters are metabolized to para-aminobenzoic acid (PABA), a known allergen
- **No cross-reactivity between amides and esters** -- if truly allergic to one class, use the other
- If true amide allergy confirmed: can use diphenhydramine 1% (25 mg/mL diluted to 10 mg/mL with NS) as an alternative local anesthetic

### Local Anesthetic Systemic Toxicity (LAST)
LAST occurs when plasma levels of local anesthetic exceed the toxic threshold, either from inadvertent intravascular injection or excessive dose absorption. This is the most dangerous complication of local anesthetic use.

**Progression of LAST (CNS before cardiac):**

| Stage | Signs/Symptoms |
|-------|---------------|
| **Early CNS excitation** | Circumoral numbness, metallic taste, tinnitus, tongue tingling, lightheadedness, visual disturbances |
| **CNS depression** | Slurred speech, drowsiness, muscle twitching --> tonic-clonic seizures --> loss of consciousness |
| **Cardiovascular toxicity** | Hypertension, tachycardia --> hypotension, bradycardia --> wide-complex arrhythmias --> asystole |

**CRITICAL:** Bupivacaine has the highest risk of fatal cardiotoxicity because it binds cardiac sodium channels with high affinity and slow dissociation ("fast in, slow out"). Bupivacaine-induced cardiac arrest is notoriously resistant to standard ACLS resuscitation.

### LAST Treatment Protocol
1. **Stop injection immediately** -- call for help
2. **Airway management** -- 100% O2, BVM ventilation, intubate if needed
3. **Seizure control** -- benzodiazepines (midazolam 2-5 mg IV); avoid propofol (additional cardiac depression)
4. **LIPID EMULSION 20% (Intralipid):**
   - **Bolus:** 1.5 mL/kg IV over 1 minute (approximately 100 mL for a 70 kg adult)
   - **Infusion:** 0.25 mL/kg/min for 30-60 minutes
   - **May repeat bolus x2** at 5-minute intervals if cardiovascular instability persists
   - **Maximum total dose:** 12 mL/kg
5. **ACLS modifications for LAST cardiac arrest:**
   - Standard ACLS with REDUCED epinephrine doses (1 mcg/kg, not standard 1 mg)
   - **AVOID:** Vasopressin, calcium channel blockers, beta-blockers, lidocaine (if lidocaine was the cause)
   - Continue lipid emulsion throughout resuscitation
   - Consider cardiopulmonary bypass/ECMO for refractory arrest
6. **Monitor for at least 30-60 minutes** after apparent recovery (LAST can recur)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Safe Administration Practices
- **ALWAYS calculate maximum dose before drawing up local anesthetic:**
  - Example: 70 kg patient, lidocaine with epi: 7 mg/kg x 70 kg = 490 mg max
  - 1% lidocaine = 10 mg/mL --> 49 mL maximum
  - 2% lidocaine = 20 mg/mL --> 24.5 mL maximum
- **Concentration matters:** Know the math. Confirm concentration on the vial before drawing up.
  - 0.5% = 5 mg/mL
  - 1% = 10 mg/mL
  - 2% = 20 mg/mL
- **Aspirate before injecting** -- withdraw plunger to check for blood return (intravascular placement)
- **Inject slowly** -- rapid injection increases peak plasma levels and LAST risk
- **Use the lowest effective concentration and volume**
- **Monitor for early LAST symptoms** -- ask patient about metallic taste, tinnitus, lightheadedness during and after injection

### Reducing Injection Pain (Nursing Pearls)
1. **Buffer with bicarbonate** -- 1 mL NaHCO3 per 9 mL lidocaine (must use within 1 week if pre-mixed; best fresh)
2. **Warm the solution** to body temperature (place in warm blanket warmer)
3. **Use 27-30 gauge needle** for infiltration
4. **Inject slowly** (1 mL per 10 seconds)
5. **Inject through wound edges** rather than through intact skin when possible
6. **Distraction techniques** in pediatric patients (vibration device, ice)
7. **Apply topical first** (LET gel for 20-30 minutes before supplemental injection)

### Equipment for LAST Preparedness
- Lipid emulsion 20% (Intralipid) should be stocked in every area where local anesthetics are used
- Know the location of the LAST rescue kit in your ED
- Post the LAST treatment algorithm (ASRA checklist) in procedure rooms
- Have BVM, suction, and advanced airway equipment readily available
sections.drug-interactions:
- **Epinephrine + MAOIs/TCAs:** Exaggerated hypertensive response -- use epinephrine-free local anesthetic or reduce epinephrine concentration
- **Lidocaine IV + Antiarrhythmics (Class I):** Additive sodium channel blockade -- see Antiarrhythmics
- **Lidocaine + CYP1A2 inhibitors (cimetidine, ciprofloxacin, fluvoxamine):** Increased lidocaine levels (hepatic metabolism via CYP1A2/3A4)
- **Bupivacaine + Propofol:** Combined cardiac depression in LAST scenario -- avoid propofol for LAST seizures
- **Local anesthetic + Acidic environment (infection, abscess):** Reduced efficacy due to ion trapping of the charged form; use field block around (not into) infected tissue or increase volume/concentration
sections.keyPoints:
- Maximum doses must be calculated before EVERY procedure -- this is a nursing responsibility
- Lidocaine with epinephrine (7 mg/kg) provides nearly double the safe dose of plain lidocaine (4.5 mg/kg)
- Epinephrine IS safe for digital blocks -- the old prohibition is outdated
- LET gel is the standard of care for pediatric laceration anesthesia before injection
- Buffering with bicarbonate (10:1 ratio) dramatically reduces injection pain
- LAST presents as CNS symptoms first (metallic taste, tinnitus, seizures) then cardiovascular collapse
- Lipid emulsion 20% is the definitive treatment for LAST -- every ED must have it immediately available
- True local anesthetic allergy is rare -- most "allergies" are vasovagal or preservative reactions

clinicalPearls: **Epinephrine in digits is safe -- stop avoiding it** -- the PHIND trial and multiple large studies confirmed zero cases of digital ischemia with commercial lidocaine/epinephrine. The benefit of hemostasis and prolonged anesthesia outweighs the theoretical risk. The old teaching came from procaine + epinephrine era.

**Buffering is the single most effective technique for reducing injection pain** -- add 1 mL of 8.4% NaHCO3 to 9 mL of 1% lidocaine immediately before use. This raises pH from ~6.5 to ~7.2, shifting more drug to the unionized form. Patients consistently rate buffered lidocaine as significantly less painful.
**"Lipid rescue" is not just for anesthesiologists** -- every ED nurse should know the location of Intralipid and the dosing protocol. Post the ASRA LAST checklist (www.asra.com) in procedure rooms.

**Bupivacaine is the most cardiotoxic local anesthetic** -- its high lipid solubility and slow dissociation from cardiac sodium channels make it particularly dangerous. For peripheral nerve blocks where long duration is desired, ropivacaine offers similar duration with a better cardiac safety profile.

**LET gel application technique matters** -- apply generously INTO the wound (not just on surrounding skin), cover with occlusive film, wait a full 20-30 minutes, and look for wound edge blanching as a sign of adequate anesthesia. Many "LET failures" are due to insufficient application time.

**If a patient says "I'm allergic to Novocain"** -- take a thorough allergy history. Novocain is procaine (an ester). True ester allergy does exist (PABA metabolite). But there is NO cross-reactivity with amides. Lidocaine is safe in patients with confirmed ester allergy. Most reported "allergies" are vasovagal reactions, not true hypersensitivity.

**For abscess I&D, do a field block** -- infiltrating directly into the acidic abscess cavity is painful and ineffective (pH-dependent ion trapping). Instead, create a ring of anesthesia in the normal tissue SURROUNDING the abscess.

migraine-and-headache-agents.title: Migraine and Headache Agents

sections.definition:
Migraine is a neurovascular disorder affecting ~15% of the global population, and it is one of the most common chief complaints in emergency medicine. The pharmacology of headache management spans two fundamentally different goals: **acute abortive therapy** (stop this headache now) and **preventive therapy** (reduce frequency and severity of future attacks). The ED focuses almost exclusively on acute treatment, but understanding prevention matters because many ED headache patients have failed or been non-adherent to preventive regimens.

The evolution of migraine pharmacology reflects our growing understanding of pathophysiology: from nonspecific pain relief (opioids, barbiturates -- now discouraged) to serotonin receptor-targeted therapy (triptans) to the newest frontier of calcitonin gene-related peptide (CGRP) pathway-targeted agents (gepants, monoclonal antibodies). Each generation offers more specificity with fewer systemic effects.

**ED reality: The "migraine cocktail" (metoclopramide + diphenhydramine + ketorolac + IV fluids) remains the most effective, evidence-based first-line treatment for acute migraine in the emergency department. Know it, use it, and resist the urge to reach for opioids.**

For cluster headache, the approach is entirely different: high-flow oxygen and subcutaneous sumatriptan are first-line, not the standard migraine cocktail.
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — Triptans are selective 5-HT1B/1D receptor agonists (Gi-coupled GPCRs); 5-HT1B activation reduces cAMP in vascular smooth muscle → vasoconstriction of dilated intracranial vessels; 5-HT1D activation on trigeminal terminals inhibits CGRP/substance P release — the entire migraine pharmacology rests on GPCR biology*

### Triptans (5-HT1B/1D Agonists)
- Selective serotonin receptor agonists targeting 5-HT1B receptors (cause vasoconstriction of dilated intracranial arteries) and 5-HT1D receptors (inhibit release of vasoactive neuropeptides -- CGRP, substance P -- from trigeminal nerve terminals)
- Also inhibit pain signal transmission in the trigeminal nucleus caudalis
- Net effect: reverse the vasodilation, neurogenic inflammation, and central sensitization of migraine

### CGRP Pathway Antagonists
- **Gepants (small molecule CGRP receptor antagonists):** Ubrogepant, rimegepant -- block the CGRP receptor on vascular smooth muscle and trigeminal neurons, preventing CGRP-mediated vasodilation and pain signal transmission. Used for acute treatment.
- **Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies:** Erenumab (targets CGRP receptor), galcanezumab, fremanezumab, eptinezumab (target CGRP ligand) -- bind circulating CGRP or its receptor for weeks to months, preventing CGRP from initiating the migraine cascade. Used for prevention.

### Ergot Alkaloids
- **Dihydroergotamine (DHE)** -- Nonselective serotonin agonist (5-HT1B/1D/1F and others) plus alpha-adrenergic and dopaminergic activity. Produces sustained vasoconstriction and inhibits neurogenic inflammation. Longer duration of action than triptans but more side effects due to nonselective receptor binding.

### Dopamine Antagonists (Antiemetics with Antimigraine Effect)
- **Metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, chlorpromazine** -- Block dopamine D2 receptors in the chemoreceptor trigger zone (antiemetic effect) and in the trigeminovascular pathway (antimigraine effect). Dopamine receptor activation is part of migraine pathophysiology (explaining the nausea, gastroparesis, and yawning of prodrome). Blocking D2 receptors both treats nausea and directly aborts migraine.
- See Antiemetics for full class coverage

### Adjunctive Agents
- **Ketorolac** -- COX-1/COX-2 inhibitor providing anti-inflammatory and analgesic effect. See NSAIDs.
- **Diphenhydramine** -- H1 antihistamine used to prevent akathisia from dopamine antagonists; also provides mild sedation and may have direct analgesic properties.
- **Dexamethasone** -- Glucocorticoid that reduces neurogenic inflammation and prevents migraine recurrence (ED "bounce-back").
- **Magnesium sulfate** -- NMDA receptor antagonist and calcium channel blocker; thought to correct relative magnesium deficiency common in migraineurs. Particularly effective for migraine with aura.
- **Valproate sodium IV** -- Mechanism in migraine unclear; may involve GABA enhancement, sodium channel blockade, and CGRP inhibition. Used for status migrainosus (migraine lasting >72 hours). See Antiepileptics.
- **Oxygen (100% high-flow)** -- Mechanism in cluster headache not fully understood; may involve cerebral vasoconstriction, inhibition of trigeminal nociceptive neurons, or direct effect on parasympathetic outflow.
sections.key-agents:
### Acute Treatment -- ED Migraine Cocktail

| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Metoclopramide** | 10-20 mg IV over 15 min | Onset: 10-15 min; Duration: 4-6 hr | First-line dopamine antagonist for ED migraine; NNT ~4 for pain relief; give with diphenhydramine to prevent akathisia |
| **Diphenhydramine** | 25-50 mg IV | Onset: minutes; Duration: 4-6 hr | Prevents akathisia/dystonia from metoclopramide; contributes sedation and possible direct antimigraine effect |
| **Ketorolac** | 15-30 mg IV (15 mg equally effective as 30 mg per evidence) | Onset: 10-15 min; Duration: 6-8 hr | NSAID component of cocktail; 15 mg IV = 30 mg IV for migraine (ceiling effect); avoid if renal disease, GI bleed risk, pregnancy |
| **IV Normal Saline** | 500-1000 mL bolus | Onset: immediate; Duration: variable | Dehydration common in migraine (vomiting, poor PO intake); improves drug delivery and comfort |
| **Dexamethasone** | 10 mg IV (single dose) | Onset: hours (anti-inflammatory); Duration: prevents recurrence for 48-72 hr | NNT 9 to prevent ED bounce-back within 72 hours; give before discharge; does NOT treat current headache acutely |

### Acute Treatment -- Triptans

| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Sumatriptan (Imitrex)** | 6 mg SQ (fastest); 20 mg intranasal; 50-100 mg PO | SQ onset: 10-15 min; IN: 15-30 min; PO: 30-60 min; Duration: 2-4 hr (may recur) | Prototype triptan; SQ is fastest and most effective route; 6 mg SQ has ~60% response at 1 hr |
| **Rizatriptan (Maxalt)** | 10 mg PO (ODT available) | Onset: 30 min; Duration: 2-4 hr | Fastest-onset oral triptan; ODT dissolves on tongue (useful with nausea); reduce to 5 mg if on propranolol |
| **Zolmitriptan** | 2.5-5 mg PO; 5 mg nasal spray | PO: 30-45 min; IN: 15 min | Nasal spray option; also effective for cluster headache |
| **Eletriptan (Relpax)** | 40 mg PO; may repeat in 2 hr (max 80 mg/day) | Onset: 30-60 min | Highest efficacy among oral triptans per head-to-head data; CYP3A4 substrate |

### Acute Treatment -- Other Agents

| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Dihydroergotamine (DHE-45)** | 1 mg IV/IM/SQ; can repeat x1 in 1 hr; also 4 mg intranasal (Migranal/Trudhesa) | IV onset: minutes; IM: 15-30 min; Duration: much longer than triptans (12+ hr, lower recurrence) | Cannot use within 24 hr of triptans (vasospasm); requires pretreatment with antiemetic (DHE causes nausea); Raskin protocol: 0.5-1 mg IV q8h x 3 days for refractory status migrainosus |
| **Magnesium sulfate** | 2 g IV over 15-20 min | Onset: 15-30 min; Duration: variable | Best evidence for migraine WITH AURA; safe in pregnancy; side effects: flushing, warmth (expected); monitor for hypotension |
| **Valproate sodium IV** | 400-1000 mg IV over 30-60 min | Onset: 30-60 min | For status migrainosus (>72 hr); CONTRAINDICATED in pregnancy (teratogenic); check LFTs; can cause pancreatitis |
| **Prochlorperazine** | 10 mg IV over 2 min | Onset: 10-15 min | Alternative to metoclopramide; may be slightly more effective per some studies; higher akathisia risk |

### Acute Treatment -- CGRP Antagonists (Gepants)

| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Ubrogepant (Ubrelvy)** | 50-100 mg PO; may repeat in 2 hr | Onset: 1-2 hr; Duration: variable | First oral CGRP antagonist for acute migraine; NO vasoconstrictive effects (safe in CAD); CYP3A4 substrate |
| **Rimegepant (Nurtec ODT)** | 75 mg PO (ODT); single dose | Onset: 1-2 hr | FDA-approved for BOTH acute treatment AND prevention (75 mg every other day); no cardiovascular contraindications |

### Preventive Therapy -- CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies

| Drug | Dose/Route | Frequency | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| **Erenumab (Aimovig)** | 70-140 mg SQ | Monthly | Targets the CGRP receptor (not the ligand); constipation is most notable side effect |
| **Galcanezumab (Emgality)** | 240 mg SQ loading, then 120 mg monthly | Monthly | Targets CGRP ligand; also FDA-approved for episodic cluster headache |
| **Fremanezumab (Ajovy)** | 225 mg SQ monthly OR 675 mg SQ quarterly | Monthly or quarterly | Quarterly dosing option offers convenience |
| **Eptinezumab (Vyepti)** | 100-300 mg IV | Quarterly (IV infusion) | Only IV CGRP mAb; fastest onset of preventive effect (some benefit by day 1 post-infusion) |

### Cluster Headache Acute Treatment

| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Oxygen 100%** | 15 L/min via non-rebreather mask x 15-20 min | Onset: 5-10 min; Duration: relief lasts through attack | First-line for cluster; NNT ~3; non-pharmacologic, safe, repeatable; must be HIGH-FLOW (standard 2-6 L nasal cannula is ineffective) |
| **Sumatriptan SQ** | 6 mg SQ | Onset: 5-10 min | First-line pharmacologic for cluster; more effective than any other route; can give with concurrent O2 |
| **Zolmitriptan nasal** | 5-10 mg intranasal | Onset: 15-20 min | Alternative to SQ sumatriptan if patient refuses injection |
sections.clinical-indications:
### ED Migraine Protocol
1. **First-line: Migraine cocktail** -- metoclopramide 10-20 mg IV + diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV + ketorolac 15 mg IV + 1L NS
2. **Add dexamethasone 10 mg IV** before discharge to prevent 72-hour recurrence
3. **If cocktail fails (30-60 min):** Consider DHE 1 mg IV (after pretreatment with antiemetic) OR magnesium 2 g IV (especially if aura)
4. **Status migrainosus (>72 hr):** Valproate 500-1000 mg IV; consider Raskin DHE protocol (admission); magnesium 2 g IV; corticosteroids
5. **AVOID:** Opioids and butalbital -- associated with medication overuse headache (MOH), ED recidivism, and poor long-term outcomes

### Outpatient Acute Treatment
- Triptans are the gold standard for outpatient abortive therapy
- Gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant) for patients who cannot take triptans (cardiovascular disease, uncontrolled HTN) or who have failed triptans
- NSAIDs (naproxen 500 mg + sumatriptan = combination therapy shown superior to either alone)

### Prevention Indications
- >= 4 migraine days/month
- Significant disability despite acute treatment
- Frequent acute medication use (risk of MOH)
- Contraindication to or failure of acute therapies
- Hemiplegic or prolonged-aura migraine
- **Options:** CGRP mAbs (erenumab, galcanezumab, fremanezumab); oral preventives: topiramate, propranolol, amitriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan; onabotulinum toxin A for chronic migraine (>=15 days/month)
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### Triptans
- **Chest tightness/"triptan sensation"** -- occurs in 3-5% of patients; usually benign (esophageal spasm or skeletal muscle), NOT cardiac ischemia; however, cannot be distinguished from angina clinically
- **Vasoconstriction** -- CONTRAINDICATED in: coronary artery disease, prior MI/stroke/TIA, uncontrolled hypertension, peripheral vascular disease, Prinzmetal angina, hemiplegic migraine, basilar migraine
- **Serotonin syndrome** -- theoretical risk with concurrent SSRIs/SNRIs; FDA black box warning exists but clinical risk appears low; use clinical judgment
- Medication overuse headache with use >10 days/month
- Rebound headache if attack returns (recurrence rate 15-40% depending on triptan)

### DHE (Dihydroergotamine)
- **Severe nausea/vomiting** -- pretreat with antiemetic (metoclopramide, ondansetron)
- Vasoconstriction (same cardiovascular contraindications as triptans, potentially more severe)
- **NEVER combine with triptans** -- must wait 24 hr between; combined vasospasm risk
- Contraindicated in pregnancy (uterotonic), severe hepatic/renal disease, CAD, uncontrolled HTN
- Peripheral ischemia with prolonged use (ergotism -- rare with modern short-course use)

### Dopamine Antagonists (Metoclopramide, Prochlorperazine)
- **Akathisia** -- restless, inner agitation; the most common reason patients report the migraine cocktail "made me worse." Prevented by co-administering diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV
- **Dystonic reactions** -- acute cervical/oculogyric dystonia; treated with diphenhydramine 50 mg IV or benztropine 1-2 mg IV
- QT prolongation (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine)
- Sedation (therapeutic for migraine, but warn before discharge)
- Tardive dyskinesia with chronic use (not relevant to single ED doses)
- Black Box Warning on metoclopramide: tardive dyskinesia with >12 weeks of use

### Gepants (CGRP Receptor Antagonists)
- Nausea (most common -- ironic for a migraine drug)
- Somnolence
- Dry mouth
- Generally well-tolerated; NO cardiovascular vasoconstriction (major advantage over triptans)
- CYP3A4 substrate (ubrogepant): avoid with strong CYP3A4 inhibitors (ketoconazole, clarithromycin)
- Hepatotoxicity was an issue with earlier gepants (telcagepant -- withdrawn); current agents have not shown this signal

### Anti-CGRP Monoclonal Antibodies
- Injection site reactions (SQ agents)
- Constipation (erenumab most commonly)
- Hypertension (erenumab -- monitor BP)
- Generally very well tolerated; long-term safety data still accumulating
- Expensive (often requires prior authorization)

### Dexamethasone (Single ED Dose)
- Single dose is generally safe; hyperglycemia (monitor in diabetics)
- Does NOT treat the current headache -- prevents recurrence
- Avoid in active infection if possible (immunosuppressive)

### Magnesium IV
- Flushing, warmth, burning at injection site (expected, not allergic reaction)
- Hypotension if infused too rapidly
- Bradycardia (rare at standard dose)
- Monitor for respiratory depression at high doses (not typical with single 2 g dose)
- Safe in pregnancy (used routinely for preeclampsia)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### ED Migraine Cocktail Administration
- **Environment:** Place patient in a dark, quiet room; offer warm blanket; minimize stimulation. This is free and therapeutic.
- **IV access:** Establish one good peripheral IV; start NS bolus immediately
- **Drug order:** Give metoclopramide + diphenhydramine first (address nausea and prevent akathisia); then ketorolac. Administer metoclopramide slowly over 15 min (reduces akathisia).
- **Reassess at 30 and 60 minutes:** Pain scale, nausea, ability to tolerate PO, functional status
- **Dexamethasone:** Add 10 mg IV before discharge planning; explain to patient this prevents the headache from returning in 2-3 days
- **Discharge readiness:** Patient should be able to tolerate PO fluids, have significant pain improvement, and have a plan for outpatient follow-up

### Sumatriptan (Imitrex) Administration — OCED Guide
> *Source: Clinical Educator (Pam) — Imitrex Quick Tips* `#pam-training`

**⚠️ Do NOT give intravenously!**

**Subcutaneous (preferred in ED — fastest onset):**
- Dose: 6 mg SQ once; may repeat in 1 hour if ordered; max 12 mg/24 hours
- Inject into outer thigh or upper arm (NOT IM)
- Autoinjector for outpatient; in ED, draw from vial using insulin syringe

**Oral:** 25-100 mg PO; slower onset; less useful if patient is vomiting

**Before administration:**
- Full set of vitals
- Assess headache characteristics, neuro status, and **cardiac history**
- Confirm timing of other triptans/ergots (none within 24 hours)
- **If headache is described as "worst of life," explosive, or sudden onset → STOP — may indicate secondary cause requiring imaging (SAH, ICH). Notify provider before giving.**

**After administration (monitor at 30 min and 1 hr):**
- Reassess headache pain
- Monitor for: chest pressure/tightness, SOB, palpitations, dizziness
- Warm/tingling sensations = common and typically benign ("triptan sensation")
- **If chest pain occurs → place on monitor, notify provider STAT**

**Nursing tips:**
- Works best **early** in a migraine, not once nausea/vomiting has progressed — if patient is vomiting, the migraine cocktail IV is a better first-line
- Encourage dim lights and low stimulus while waiting for effect
- Document: headache characteristics pre-treatment, vitals, route/dose, response at follow-up, any adverse effects

### DHE Administration
- **ALWAYS pretreat with antiemetic** 15-30 min before DHE (metoclopramide 10 mg IV or ondansetron 4 mg IV)
- Give DHE 0.5-1 mg IV over 2-3 minutes
- Monitor blood pressure (vasoconstriction)
- Confirm no triptan use in prior 24 hours
- Raskin protocol (inpatient): DHE 0.5-1 mg IV q8h for up to 3 days with continuous antiemetic coverage

### Magnesium IV Administration
- Mix 2 g MgSO4 in 50-100 mL NS; infuse over 15-20 minutes
- Expect patient to report flushing and warmth -- this is normal, reassure
- Monitor BP during infusion
- Check magnesium level if repeat dosing considered
- Safe in pregnancy -- commonly used for Preeclampsia

### Oxygen for Cluster Headache
- Requires non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min minimum (12-15 L/min)
- Duration: 15-20 minutes per attack
- Demand-valve oxygen delivery systems are most effective if available
- Keep patient seated and leaning slightly forward
- Most patients report relief within 5-10 minutes; if no relief by 20 minutes, add sumatriptan SQ

### Medication Overuse Headache (MOH) Screening
- Ask about frequency of ALL acute headache medications: triptans, NSAIDs, acetaminophen, opioids, butalbital, caffeine-containing analgesics
- MOH thresholds: triptans >= 10 days/month, simple analgesics >= 15 days/month, opioids/barbiturates >= 10 days/month
- If MOH suspected: discuss with patient and provider; withdrawal from overused medication is the treatment; bridge with naproxen or corticosteroid taper; referral to neurology/headache specialist
sections.drug-interactions:
| Interaction | Agents Involved | Clinical Significance |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **Triptan + MAOI** | All triptans + phenelzine, tranylcypromine | Contraindicated; MAOIs inhibit triptan metabolism; risk of serotonin syndrome and hypertensive crisis |
| **Triptan + Ergot** | Sumatriptan + DHE/ergotamine | Do NOT use within 24 hr of each other; synergistic vasospasm risk |
| **Triptan + SSRI/SNRI** | All triptans + fluoxetine, sertraline, venlafaxine, duloxetine | FDA serotonin syndrome warning; clinical risk appears LOW but monitor; do NOT withhold triptans reflexively from patients on SSRIs |
| **Ubrogepant + CYP3A4 inhibitors** | Ubrogepant + ketoconazole, itraconazole, clarithromycin, HIV protease inhibitors | Strong inhibitors: AVOID; moderate inhibitors: reduce ubrogepant dose to 50 mg |
| **Ubrogepant + CYP3A4 inducers** | Ubrogepant + rifampin, phenytoin, carbamazepine | Avoid -- reduced efficacy |
| **Metoclopramide + Antipsychotics** | Metoclopramide + haloperidol, risperidone, olanzapine | Additive dopamine blockade; increased EPS/dystonia risk |
| **DHE + CYP3A4 inhibitors** | DHE + macrolide antibiotics, azole antifungals, HIV protease inhibitors | Risk of ergotism (severe peripheral vasoconstriction); CONTRAINDICATED |
| **Rizatriptan + Propranolol** | Rizatriptan + propranolol | Propranolol increases rizatriptan levels; reduce rizatriptan dose to 5 mg |
| **Valproate + Lamotrigine** | Valproate IV + lamotrigine (if on preventive) | Valproate doubles lamotrigine levels; monitor for toxicity |
sections.keyPoints:
- The ED migraine cocktail (metoclopramide + diphenhydramine + ketorolac + IV fluids) is first-line and superior to opioids for acute migraine
- Always add dexamethasone 10 mg IV before discharge to prevent 72-hour recurrence (NNT 9)
- Triptans are contraindicated in CAD, prior stroke/MI, uncontrolled HTN, hemiplegic/basilar migraine
- CGRP antagonists (gepants) are the first non-vasoconstrictive migraine-specific acute treatments -- safe in cardiovascular disease
- DHE offers longer duration of relief than triptans with lower recurrence; requires antiemetic pretreatment
- Magnesium 2 g IV is evidence-based for migraine with aura and safe in pregnancy
- Cluster headache treatment is different from migraine: high-flow O2 (15 L/min NRB x 15 min) + sumatriptan 6 mg SQ
- AVOID opioids and butalbital for migraine -- they cause medication overuse headache, increase ED recidivism, and worsen long-term outcomes
- Valproate IV is a rescue option for status migrainosus (>72 hr)
- Akathisia from dopamine antagonists is preventable with diphenhydramine -- always co-administer
sections.related-topics:
- NSAIDs
- Antiemetics
- Antiepileptics
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics
- Systemic Corticosteroids
- Pain Physiology and Management

#pharmacology #neurological #ed-critical #migraine #headache

clinicalPearls: **Metoclopramide over 15 minutes, not 15 seconds** -- slow infusion rate dramatically reduces akathisia. Giving it as a fast push is the single most common reason patients report the migraine cocktail "made me feel worse." Pair it with diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV and you will see far better tolerance.

**15 mg ketorolac = 30 mg ketorolac for migraine** -- multiple studies show no additional analgesic benefit from the higher dose. Use 15 mg; it has the same ceiling effect with less renal and GI risk. The 30 mg dose persists from habit, not evidence.

**Opioids for migraine are a treatment failure, not a treatment** -- they do not address migraine pathophysiology, they cause medication overuse headache, they increase the probability of ED return within 7 days, and they delay administration of effective therapy. The American Headache Society guidelines explicitly recommend against opioid use for migraine.

**Dexamethasone does not fix the current headache** -- it prevents the headache from coming back. Patients need to understand this. Give it before discharge as an investment in staying out of the ED for the next 48-72 hours.

**Cluster headache patients need oxygen IMMEDIATELY** -- cluster attacks are excruciating and short (15-180 min). Every minute without treatment is a minute of the worst pain a human can experience. Have the RT bring a non-rebreather at 15 L/min while you draw up sumatriptan 6 mg SQ. Do not wait for imaging or a full workup before starting O2.

**"Worst headache of my life" is not migraine until you have ruled out subarachnoid hemorrhage** -- this is a thunderclap headache presentation. CT head within 6 hours has ~99% sensitivity for SAH; after 6 hours, sensitivity drops and LP is needed. Never assume migraine in a first-ever or worst-ever headache without appropriate workup.

**Gepants are a game-changer for the patient with CAD who gets migraines** -- before CGRP antagonists, these patients had no migraine-specific acute therapy (triptans and ergots both contraindicated). Ubrogepant or rimegepant can be used safely without cardiovascular risk. Know these exist and refer patients who need them.

**Magnesium works best for aura, not for pain alone** -- if a patient presents with migraine WITH aura (visual scotoma, sensory symptoms), add 2 g IV MgSO4 to the cocktail. Warn about the flushing sensation so they do not think they are having an allergic reaction.

mood-stabilizers.title: Mood Stabilizers

sections.mechanism:
- **Lithium:** Exact mechanism incompletely understood. Modulates multiple intracellular signaling pathways: inhibits inositol monophosphatase (depletes IP3/DAG pathway), modulates GSK-3β, affects neurotransmitter release, neuroprotective properties. Unique anti-suicidal effect not shared by other mood stabilizers.
- **Valproate:** See Antiepileptics; enhances GABA, modulates sodium channels; anti-manic through unclear additional mechanisms
- **Carbamazepine:** See Antiepileptics; sodium channel blockade; used for acute mania and mixed episodes
- **Lamotrigine:** See Antiepileptics; blocks voltage-gated sodium channels, modulates glutamate release; primarily effective for BIPOLAR DEPRESSION prevention (not acute mania)

> **Note:** Valproate, carbamazepine, and lamotrigine are covered in detail in Antiepileptics. This note focuses on their mood-stabilizing role and emphasizes lithium.
sections.indications:
| Drug | Acute Mania | Maintenance (Mania Prevention) | Bipolar Depression | Other |
|------|------------|-------------------------------|-------------------|-------|
| **Lithium** | ✅ (slower onset -- takes 1-2 weeks) | ✅ (gold standard) | Moderate | Anti-suicidal properties (unique); augmentation for unipolar depression |
| **Valproate** | ✅ (can load for rapid effect) | ✅ | Limited | Mixed episodes; rapid cycling |
| **Carbamazepine** | ✅ | ✅ (second-line) | Limited | Mixed episodes; when lithium/valproate fail |
| **Lamotrigine** | ❌ (NOT effective for acute mania) | ✅ (prevents depressive episodes) | ✅ (prevention, not acute treatment) | Bipolar II depression prevention |

> Many SGAs are also used as mood stabilizers -- see Antipsychotics (olanzapine, quetiapine, aripiprazole, cariprazine, lurasidone).
sections.lithium-the-classic-mood-stabilizer:
### Dosing
| Phase | Typical Dose | Target Level |
|-------|-------------|-------------|
| Acute mania | 300-600 mg TID (titrate to level) | 0.8-1.2 mEq/L |
| Maintenance | 300 mg BID-TID (adjust to level) | 0.6-1.0 mEq/L |
| Elderly | Lower doses; closer to 0.4-0.8 mEq/L | Increased sensitivity |

> **Narrowest therapeutic index of any psychiatric drug.** Therapeutic: 0.6-1.2. Toxic: > 1.5. Lethal: > 2.0. This leaves almost NO margin for error.

> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — lithium has one of the narrowest therapeutic windows in medicine; small changes in sodium balance dramatically affect lithium levels*

### Monitoring
| Parameter | Frequency | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| **Lithium level** | q5-7 days during titration; then q3-6 months when stable | TROUGH level (12 hr after last dose); narrow therapeutic index |
| **Renal function (BUN/Cr, eGFR)** | Baseline, q6 months, then annually | Chronic interstitial nephritis; nephrogenic diabetes insipidus |
| **Thyroid function (TSH, free T4)** | Baseline, q6 months x1 year, then annually | Hypothyroidism (10-20% of patients); goiter |
| **Calcium/PTH** | Baseline and annually | Hyperparathyroidism/hypercalcemia |
| **ECG** | Baseline (patients > 40 or cardiac history) | T-wave flattening/inversion (benign); rare conduction abnormalities |
| **Pregnancy test** | Before starting | Ebstein's anomaly (cardiac defect -- risk lower than historically thought ~0.1-0.2% but still significant) |
| **Weight** | Each visit | Weight gain common |

### Adverse Effects
| Level/Context | Effects |
|--------------|---------|
| **Therapeutic range** | Fine tremor (hands), polyuria/polydipsia (nephrogenic DI), GI upset (nausea, diarrhea), weight gain, cognitive dulling, hypothyroidism, acne, edema |
| **Early toxicity (1.5-2.5)** | Coarse tremor, persistent nausea/vomiting/diarrhea, confusion, slurred speech, ataxia, muscle twitching |
| **Severe toxicity (> 2.5)** | Seizures, coma, renal failure, cardiac arrhythmias, cardiovascular collapse, death |

### Factors That INCREASE Lithium Levels (Risk of Toxicity)
| Factor | Mechanism |
|--------|----------|
| **Dehydration** | Lithium is reabsorbed with sodium in proximal tubule |
| **Low-sodium diet** | Kidneys retain lithium when sodium is scarce |
| **NSAIDs** | Reduce renal prostaglandins → decrease lithium clearance (20-50% increase in level) |
| **ACE inhibitors / ARBs** | Reduce GFR → decrease lithium clearance |
| **Thiazide diuretics** | Increase proximal tubule sodium (and lithium) reabsorption |
| **Renal impairment** | Decreased clearance |
| **Fever / Illness / Heat** | Dehydration + reduced renal perfusion |

> **Loop diuretics (furosemide) are SAFER** with lithium than thiazides (though still require monitoring).

### Lithium Toxicity Management
- **Mild-moderate (1.5-2.5):** Hold lithium, aggressive IV hydration with NS (enhances lithium excretion), check level q2-4 hours, monitor cardiac/neurologic status
- **Severe (> 2.5) or symptomatic with lower levels:** Hemodialysis (lithium is small, water-soluble, no protein binding -- dialyzes well); recheck level 6-8 hours post-dialysis (rebound from tissues)
sections.contraindications:
- **Lithium:** Significant renal impairment; severe dehydration/sodium depletion; Brugada syndrome; first trimester pregnancy if avoidable (Ebstein's anomaly -- risk ~0.1-0.2%); concurrent thiazide diuretics (relative)
- **Valproate:** See Antiepileptics -- pregnancy (neural tube defects), hepatic disease
- **Carbamazepine:** See Antiepileptics -- bone marrow suppression, HLA-B*1502
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Lithium-Specific:**
  - Trough levels only: Draw 12 hours after last dose (usually before AM dose)
  - Educate on hydration: 2-3L fluid daily; maintain consistent salt intake; do NOT start low-sodium diet while on lithium
  - Report excessive thirst/urination (nephrogenic DI), new tremor, GI symptoms, confusion
  - Hold lithium and call provider if: patient vomiting/unable to hydrate, severe diarrhea, starting new NSAID or diuretic, fever
  - Sick day rules: Reduce or hold dose during acute illness with fluid loss (vomiting, diarrhea, fever); rehydrate; check level
  - Summer/heat precautions: Increased sweating → dehydration → toxicity risk
- **Medication Reconciliation:**
  - Any new medication is an opportunity for lithium interaction. Specifically check: NSAIDs (including OTC ibuprofen), ACE/ARBs, thiazide diuretics, metronidazole
  - If patient needs anti-inflammatory: Acetaminophen is SAFE with lithium. Avoid NSAIDs. If NSAID absolutely needed, monitor lithium level closely.
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Carry medical ID (lithium user)
  - Maintain consistent fluid and salt intake
  - Avoid OTC NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen) -- use acetaminophen for pain
  - Report: vomiting, diarrhea, tremor worsening, confusion, muscle weakness, vision changes
  - Do not change salt intake suddenly (going low-sodium → lithium toxicity)
  - Heat precautions: Stay hydrated in hot weather; lithium levels can spike with dehydration from sweating
sections.related-concepts:
- Antiepileptics
- Antidepressants
- Antipsychotics
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance
- Renal System

clinicalPearls: **Lithium is STILL the gold standard for bipolar maintenance** and is the ONLY mood stabilizer proven to reduce suicide risk. It's underprescribed because of monitoring requirements, but it saves lives.

**Lithium + NSAIDs is one of the most common dangerous drug interactions in clinical practice.** Patients buy ibuprofen OTC without thinking. Even a few days of ibuprofen can raise lithium levels 20-50% → toxicity. Acetaminophen is the safe alternative.

**Lithium + thiazide = toxicity waiting to happen.** Thiazides deplete sodium → kidneys compensate by reabsorbing more sodium in the proximal tubule → lithium is reabsorbed along with it → levels rise. If a diuretic is needed, furosemide is safer (acts on loop, not proximal tubule).

**Lamotrigine is for bipolar DEPRESSION prevention, NOT mania.** This is the most commonly confused point. Lamotrigine prevents depressive episodes but does nothing for acute mania. Lithium and valproate are for mania.

**Lithium's narrow therapeutic index means EVERYTHING matters:** hydration, salt intake, kidney function, concurrent medications, temperature, illness. Educate patients that maintaining consistent habits is as important as taking the medication.

**Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus** (polyuria, polydipsia) affects up to 40% of long-term lithium users. Amiloride (potassium-sparing diuretic) is the treatment -- paradoxically, it concentrates urine. Thiazides also work but increase lithium levels (monitor closely).
**Valproate can be loaded for rapid antimanic effect** (20-30 mg/kg/day), unlike lithium which takes 1-2 weeks. This makes valproate preferred for acute, severe mania when rapid control is needed.

**Bipolar depression is the hardest phase to treat.** Antidepressant monotherapy can trigger mania. Quetiapine, lurasidone, and lamotrigine (for prevention) are evidence-based options. Lithium augmentation also helps.

muscle-relaxants.title: Muscle Relaxants

sections.definition:
Skeletal muscle relaxants are a heterogeneous group of drugs used to treat muscle spasm (acute, painful, involuntary contraction associated with musculoskeletal injury) and spasticity (chronic velocity-dependent increase in muscle tone from upper motor neuron lesions such as stroke, MS, spinal cord injury, or cerebral palsy). These are fundamentally different clinical problems requiring different pharmacologic approaches, but the drugs are often lumped together under the same label.

Most "muscle relaxants" used for acute spasm (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol, tizanidine) actually work centrally in the brain and spinal cord -- they are sedatives that happen to reduce muscle tone as a side effect. True peripheral-acting muscle relaxants (dantrolene) are rare and reserved for spasticity and the emergency treatment of Malignant Hyperthermia.

**Key clinical reality: These drugs are prescribed frequently, rarely evidence-based beyond short-term use, carry significant sedation and abuse risk, and should be used as adjuncts to physical therapy -- not as standalone treatment.**

This note covers centrally-acting antispasmodics (for acute spasm), centrally-acting antispasticity agents (baclofen, tizanidine), and the direct-acting peripheral agent (dantrolene). For neuromuscular blocking agents used in intubation and critical care, see Neuromuscular Blocking Agents.
sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — GABA-A is a ligand-gated Cl⁻ channel; baclofen (GABA-B), benzodiazepines, and carisoprodol all enhance GABAergic inhibition — increasing Cl⁻ influx → hyperpolarization → reduced motor neuron excitability → muscle relaxation*
> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability — Skeletal muscle contraction begins with an action potential at the neuromuscular junction; centrally-acting relaxants reduce the frequency of motor neuron firing rather than blocking neuromuscular transmission directly (unlike NMBAs)*

### Centrally-Acting Antispasmodics (Acute Spasm)
- **Cyclobenzaprine** -- Structurally related to tricyclic antidepressants (TCAs); reduces muscle spasm through action in the brainstem, reducing tonic somatic motor activity. Does NOT act at the neuromuscular junction. Strong anticholinergic properties explain most side effects.
- **Methocarbamol** -- Central mechanism poorly understood; thought to produce general CNS depression reducing polysynaptic reflex activity in the spinal cord. Minimal anticholinergic effects compared to cyclobenzaprine.
- **Orphenadrine** -- Centrally-acting with anticholinergic and antihistaminic properties; analgesic effect may be partly NMDA receptor antagonism. Structurally related to diphenhydramine.
- **Metaxalone** -- Centrally-acting; mechanism not well defined. Thought to produce general CNS depression affecting polysynaptic reflexes.
- **Carisoprodol (Soma)** -- Metabolized to meprobamate (a Schedule IV barbiturate-like anxiolytic); acts at GABA-A receptors producing sedation and anxiolysis. HIGH abuse and dependence potential. Schedule IV controlled substance.

### Centrally-Acting Antispasticity Agents
- **Baclofen** -- GABA-B receptor agonist in the spinal cord dorsal horn; inhibits presynaptic release of excitatory neurotransmitters (glutamate, substance P) and hyperpolarizes postsynaptic motor neurons. Reduces both monosynaptic and polysynaptic spinal reflexes. Available PO and intrathecal.
- **Tizanidine** -- Central alpha-2 adrenergic agonist (same mechanism as clonidine); acts in the spinal cord to reduce excitatory interneuron firing and presynaptic inhibition of motor neurons. Produces less weakness than baclofen at equi-effective antispasticity doses.

### Direct-Acting Peripheral Agent
- **Dantrolene** -- Acts directly on skeletal muscle by binding the ryanodine receptor (RyR1) on the sarcoplasmic reticulum, reducing calcium release into the myoplasm. Decreases excitation-contraction coupling WITHOUT affecting nerve conduction or NMJ transmission. This unique peripheral mechanism makes it the definitive treatment for malignant hyperthermia and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

### Benzodiazepines (Adjunct)
- **Diazepam** -- Enhances GABA-A receptor activity in the spinal cord and brainstem; reduces polysynaptic reflex activity. Used for acute muscle spasm, spasticity, and as an adjunct in tetanus. See Sedatives and Anxiolytics for full benzodiazepine coverage.
sections.key-agents:
| Drug | Dose/Route | Onset/Duration | Key Pearl |
|------|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Cyclobenzaprine (Flexeril)** | 5 mg PO TID (start low); max 10 mg TID; avoid >2-3 weeks | Onset: 1-2 hr; Duration: 12-24 hr (long half-life 18 hr; up to 3 days in elderly) | Structurally identical to amitriptyline minus one double bond; carries all TCA side effects; AVOID in elderly (Beers list) |
| **Methocarbamol (Robaxin)** | 1500 mg PO QID (or 1000 mg TID); can give 1-2 g IV/IM for acute spasm | Onset: 30 min PO, immediate IV; Duration: 4-6 hr | Preferred in elderly (fewer anticholinergic effects); IV form available for ED use; may discolor urine brown/black/green (benign) |
| **Tizanidine (Zanaflex)** | 2 mg PO TID initially; titrate to 8 mg TID (max 36 mg/day) | Onset: 1-2 hr; Peak: 1-2 hr; Duration: 3-6 hr | Alpha-2 agonist (like clonidine); causes hypotension and sedation; CYP1A2 substrate -- CONTRAINDICATED with ciprofloxacin/fluvoxamine |
| **Baclofen (Lioresal)** | 5 mg PO TID; titrate by 5 mg q3 days to 20 mg TID (max 80 mg/day); Intrathecal: 50-100 mcg test dose | Onset: 0.5-1 hr PO; Duration: 6-8 hr | GABA-B agonist; intrathecal pump for severe spasticity; NEVER abruptly discontinue -- withdrawal can be FATAL |
| **Dantrolene (Dantrium)** | Spasticity: 25 mg PO daily, titrate to 100 mg QID; MH: **2.5 mg/kg IV** rapid push, repeat q5-10 min to max 10 mg/kg | Onset: 1 week PO (spasticity); Minutes IV (MH); Duration: 6-8 hr | ONLY direct-acting muscle relaxant; definitive treatment for malignant hyperthermia and NMS; hepatotoxicity risk with chronic PO use |
| **Diazepam (Valium)** | 2-10 mg PO/IV q6-8h PRN for acute spasm; 5-10 mg IV for severe spasm/tetanus | Onset: minutes IV, 30-60 min PO; Duration: variable (long-acting metabolites, half-life 20-100 hr) | Most effective for acute severe spasm; long half-life with active metabolites; high abuse potential; avoid prolonged use |
| **Carisoprodol (Soma)** | 250-350 mg PO TID + bedtime; max 2-3 weeks | Onset: 30 min; Duration: 4-6 hr | Metabolized to meprobamate (barbiturate-like); Schedule IV; HIGH abuse/dependence risk; avoid prescribing -- no advantage over safer alternatives |
| **Orphenadrine (Norflex)** | 100 mg PO BID; 60 mg IV/IM q12h | Onset: 30-60 min PO; Duration: 6-8 hr | Anticholinergic + weak NMDA antagonism; avoid in elderly; injectable form available for ED |
| **Metaxalone (Skelaxin)** | 800 mg PO TID-QID | Onset: 1 hr; Duration: 4-6 hr | Fewer sedating effects than cyclobenzaprine; take with food (bioavailability increases); hepatotoxicity rare but monitor LFTs |
sections.clinical-indications:
### Acute Musculoskeletal Spasm
- Low back pain with muscle spasm -- the most common indication in ED and primary care
- Cervical strain/whiplash
- Post-injury muscle guarding
- **First-line choices:** Methocarbamol (especially elderly) or cyclobenzaprine (younger patients, short-term)
- **Evidence reality:** Cochrane reviews show modest short-term benefit over placebo for acute LBP; no evidence supports use beyond 2-3 weeks; physical therapy is superior long-term

### Spasticity (Chronic)
- **Upper motor neuron lesions:** Stroke, MS, spinal cord injury, cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury
- **First-line:** Baclofen (oral or intrathecal) or tizanidine
- **Second-line:** Dantrolene (more side effects), diazepam
- Goal is to reduce tone enough to improve function without eliminating tone needed for ambulation (some spasticity helps patients stand/transfer)

### Malignant Hyperthermia (Emergency)
- **Dantrolene 2.5 mg/kg IV** is the ONLY definitive treatment
- Repeat every 5-10 minutes until symptoms resolve (max 10 mg/kg, though more may be needed)
- Each 20 mg vial requires 60 mL sterile water for reconstitution -- this is labor-intensive (need multiple nurses mixing simultaneously)
- Newer formulation (Ryanodex) = 250 mg/vial, reconstitutes with only 5 mL -- dramatically faster preparation
- MH Hotline: **1-800-644-9737** (MHAUS)

### Neuroleptic Malignant Syndrome (NMS)
- Dantrolene 1-2.5 mg/kg IV used as adjunct (along with stopping offending agent, cooling, bromocriptine)
- Evidence is primarily case series, but dantrolene is standard of care for severe NMS with rigidity and hyperthermia

### Tetanus
- Diazepam or midazolam for muscle spasm control
- Baclofen (intrathecal) for refractory spasm
- NMBAs for severe cases requiring mechanical ventilation
sections.adverse-effects-and-safety:
### CNS Depression (All Centrally-Acting Agents)
- Sedation, drowsiness, dizziness -- the dominant side effect class-wide
- Impaired driving and cognitive function
- Additive with alcohol, opioids, benzodiazepines, and other CNS depressants
- **Elderly are disproportionately affected** -- falls, confusion, oversedation. The Beers Criteria lists cyclobenzaprine, orphenadrine, and carisoprodol as AVOID in patients >=65

### Cyclobenzaprine-Specific
- **Anticholinergic effects:** dry mouth, urinary retention, constipation, blurred vision, tachycardia, confusion
- Serotonin syndrome risk when combined with SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol
- QT prolongation (TCA-like cardiac effects) -- avoid in cardiac disease
- Contraindicated with MAOIs (wait 14 days) and in hyperthyroidism, heart failure, arrhythmias, and acute post-MI

### Tizanidine-Specific
- **Hepatotoxicity** -- monitor LFTs at baseline, 1 month, 3 months, then periodically. Discontinue if ALT >3x ULN or if clinical signs of hepatic injury
- **Hypotension** -- alpha-2 agonist mechanism causes orthostatic hypotension and bradycardia; worse with concomitant antihypertensives
- Rebound hypertension with abrupt discontinuation (taper over 1-2 weeks)
- **CYP1A2 interaction** -- ciprofloxacin and fluvoxamine are CONTRAINDICATED (increase tizanidine levels 10-fold, causing profound sedation and hypotension)

### Baclofen-Specific
- **WITHDRAWAL IS LIFE-THREATENING** -- abrupt discontinuation (especially intrathecal) causes seizures, hyperthermia, rhabdomyolysis, multiorgan failure, and death. Clinically resembles malignant hyperthermia or NMS.
  - Intrathecal pump malfunction/catheter kink = medical emergency
  - Always taper baclofen over 1-2 weeks minimum
  - If intrathecal pump fails: give oral baclofen AND IV benzodiazepines while troubleshooting pump
- CNS depression, sedation, weakness
- Urinary retention
- Lowered seizure threshold

### Dantrolene-Specific
- **Hepatotoxicity** -- the major chronic use concern. Risk increases with doses >400 mg/day, duration >60 days, female sex, age >35, and concurrent hepatotoxic drugs. Monitor LFTs monthly for first 6 months.
- Black Box Warning: Symptomatic hepatitis (fatal and non-fatal) has occurred
- Muscle weakness (expected pharmacologic effect -- reduces strength as it reduces tone)
- Diarrhea (common, dose-related)
- Photosensitivity
- IV form: Tissue necrosis if extravasated (highly alkaline pH ~9.5); use large-bore IV in a large vein

### Carisoprodol-Specific
- **Abuse and dependence** -- metabolized to meprobamate; produces euphoria and anxiolysis; physical dependence develops with regular use
- Withdrawal syndrome (seizures, insomnia, anxiety) resembles benzodiazepine/barbiturate withdrawal
- Commonly combined with opioids and benzodiazepines recreationally ("soma coma" or "Houston cocktail")
- Schedule IV controlled substance; avoid prescribing when safer alternatives exist
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment Before Administration
- Baseline pain assessment using validated scale (0-10, CPOT for nonverbal patients)
- Assess for history of substance abuse (carisoprodol, benzodiazepine risk)
- Baseline vital signs (especially BP for tizanidine, HR for cyclobenzaprine)
- Renal and hepatic function -- most agents require dose adjustment or avoidance in impairment
- Fall risk assessment -- ALL muscle relaxants increase fall risk, especially in elderly and with concurrent CNS depressants
- Current medication review: screen for CYP1A2 inhibitors (tizanidine), MAOIs/serotonergic drugs (cyclobenzaprine), CNS depressants (all)

### Administration Considerations
- **Methocarbamol IV** -- infuse slowly (max rate 300 mg/min, typically 1 g over 3+ min); patient should remain recumbent for 10-15 min after injection (orthostatic hypotension); do NOT give SubQ
- **Dantrolene IV (MH emergency)** -- each 20 mg vial requires 60 mL sterile water; shake vigorously (dissolves slowly); assign multiple nurses to reconstitution simultaneously. Ryanodex (250 mg/vial in 5 mL) is much faster. Use large-bore IV; highly alkaline -- will cause tissue necrosis if extravasated
- **Baclofen intrathecal pump** -- know how to assess pump function; alarm sounds indicate malfunction; if withdrawal suspected, this is a TIME-CRITICAL emergency (call neurosurgery/pain service immediately)
- Most oral agents should be taken with food to reduce GI upset (especially metaxalone, which has increased absorption with food)

### Patient Education
- **Sedation warning** -- do not drive or operate heavy machinery, especially during initial dosing; additive with alcohol
- **Duration limits** -- acute spasm agents (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol) should be used for 2-3 weeks maximum; no evidence supports longer use
- **Baclofen: NEVER stop suddenly** -- always taper; missed doses should be taken ASAP; patients on intrathecal pumps must keep refill appointments (pump runs dry = withdrawal)
- **Tizanidine: avoid ciprofloxacin** -- if prescribed an antibiotic, contact provider to confirm compatibility; also avoid grapefruit juice
- Store medications safely away from children (cyclobenzaprine and carisoprodol are particularly toxic in pediatric ingestion)

### Monitoring
- Sedation level and fall risk q shift for inpatients
- Blood pressure (tizanidine -- orthostatic hypotension; baclofen withdrawal -- hypertension)
- LFTs: baseline and periodic for tizanidine (1, 3, 6 months then periodically), dantrolene (monthly x6 months), metaxalone (baseline)
- Signs of serotonin syndrome with cyclobenzaprine + serotonergic drugs: agitation, clonus, tremor, diaphoresis, hyperthermia, hyperreflexia
- Baclofen pump patients: assess for withdrawal signs (increasing spasticity, fever, altered mental status, autonomic instability)
sections.drug-interactions:
| Interaction | Agents Involved | Clinical Significance |
|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| **CYP1A2 inhibition** | Tizanidine + ciprofloxacin or fluvoxamine | **CONTRAINDICATED** -- tizanidine levels increase 10x → profound hypotension and sedation |
| **CNS depression** | All muscle relaxants + opioids, benzodiazepines, alcohol, antihistamines, gabapentinoids | Additive sedation; respiratory depression risk; falls |
| **Serotonin syndrome** | Cyclobenzaprine + SSRIs, SNRIs, MAOIs, tramadol, triptans | Cyclobenzaprine has TCA structure with serotonergic activity; risk of hyperthermia, clonus, AMS |
| **MAOIs** | Cyclobenzaprine (contraindicated within 14 days of MAOI) | Hypertensive crisis and serotonin syndrome |
| **Anticholinergic burden** | Cyclobenzaprine + other anticholinergics (diphenhydramine, oxybutynin, first-gen antipsychotics) | Additive: urinary retention, constipation, delirium, tachycardia (especially dangerous in elderly) |
| **CYP3A4 inducers** | Dantrolene + carbamazepine, phenytoin, rifampin | May decrease dantrolene efficacy |
| **Hepatotoxic drugs** | Dantrolene + statins, acetaminophen (chronic), methotrexate | Additive hepatotoxicity risk |
| **Antihypertensives** | Tizanidine + beta-blockers, ACE inhibitors, diuretics | Additive hypotension |
| **Meprobamate metabolism** | Carisoprodol + CYP2C19 inhibitors (omeprazole, fluoxetine) | Increased meprobamate levels → enhanced sedation and abuse potential |
sections.keyPoints:
- Muscle relaxants for acute spasm are short-term adjuncts (2-3 weeks max); physical therapy is the definitive treatment
- Methocarbamol is preferred in elderly patients (fewer anticholinergic effects, available IV for ED use)
- Cyclobenzaprine is essentially a TCA -- carries anticholinergic burden, serotonin syndrome risk, QT prolongation, and is on the Beers list for patients >=65
- Tizanidine requires LFT monitoring and is ABSOLUTELY CONTRAINDICATED with ciprofloxacin (10x level increase)
- Baclofen withdrawal (especially intrathecal) is a life-threatening emergency resembling MH/NMS -- NEVER abruptly discontinue
- Dantrolene is the ONLY direct-acting peripheral muscle relaxant and the definitive treatment for malignant hyperthermia (2.5 mg/kg IV, repeat q5-10min)
- Carisoprodol (Soma) has no clinical advantage over safer alternatives and carries significant abuse/dependence risk -- avoid prescribing
- All muscle relaxants cause sedation and increase fall risk; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration
sections.related-topics:
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics
- Pain Physiology and Management
- Neuromuscular Blocking Agents
- Opioid Analgesics
- NSAIDs
- Antidotes and Toxicology Agents

#pharmacology #musculoskeletal #pain #muscle-relaxants

clinicalPearls: **Methocarbamol IV is the ED workhorse for acute back spasm** -- 1-2 g IV over 3-5 min provides rapid relief with a favorable side effect profile. Unlike cyclobenzaprine, it can be given IV and has minimal anticholinergic load. Have the patient lie down for 10-15 min after (orthostatic hypotension).

**Cyclobenzaprine is amitriptyline in disguise** -- they are structurally nearly identical. If you would hesitate to give a TCA, hesitate to give cyclobenzaprine. This means avoiding it in elderly, cardiac patients, patients on serotonergic drugs, and those with urinary retention or glaucoma.

**Tizanidine + ciprofloxacin = one of the most dangerous drug interactions you will encounter in practice** -- CYP1A2 inhibition increases tizanidine AUC by 10-fold. Patients develop profound somnolence and hypotension. If a patient on tizanidine needs a fluoroquinolone, use levofloxacin (not a CYP1A2 inhibitor).

**Baclofen withdrawal kills** -- intrathecal pump malfunction is a neurosurgical emergency. Know the signs: return of spasticity, then fever, then autonomic instability, then rhabdomyolysis and multiorgan failure. Treatment: oral baclofen loading + IV benzodiazepines + ICU admission + urgent pump evaluation.

**Dantrolene reconstitution takes time that you do not have during MH** -- each 20 mg vial needs 60 mL sterile water and vigorous shaking. For a 70-kg patient, the initial dose (2.5 mg/kg = 175 mg) requires ~9 vials. Assign 3-4 people to reconstitute simultaneously. If your facility has Ryanodex (250 mg/vial, 5 mL reconstitution), know where it is BEFORE you need it.

**"Soma coma" is real** -- carisoprodol combined with opioids and benzodiazepines is a well-known recreational combination with high overdose fatality. Recognize the combination on medication reconciliation as a red flag for substance misuse.

**Never treat spasticity to zero** -- patients with upper motor neuron lesions often use their spasticity functionally (standing, transfers, bladder function). The goal is to reduce tone to the point of improved comfort and function, not eliminate it. Ask the patient and PT/OT what functional goals matter.
