**Ketamine emergence reactions are dose-dependent, not inevitable** -- slow IV push (over 1-2 minutes) and a calm, dark recovery environment reduce incidence more than routine midazolam pretreatment. Routine midazolam prolongs recovery without clear benefit.

**The "ketofol" combination (ketamine + propofol mixed 1:1)** is increasingly popular -- ketamine offsets propofol hypotension while propofol attenuates ketamine emergence. Typical dose: 0.5 mg/kg of each.

**Etomidate myoclonus is NOT a seizure** -- warn the proceduralist so they do not abort the procedure. A small dose of midazolam (0.02 mg/kg) or fentanyl (0.5 mcg/kg) before etomidate reduces myoclonus.

**ETCO2 waveform loss = apnea; rising ETCO2 baseline = hypoventilation** -- act on capnography changes BEFORE the SpO2 drops. SpO2 is a lagging indicator, especially in patients pre-oxygenated with high-flow O2.

**Pediatric ketamine IM is the great equalizer** -- for the terrified, uncooperative child who needs a laceration repaired, 4 mg/kg IM in the deltoid provides 20-30 minutes of reliable dissociation. Have suction ready for secretions.
**Nitrous is underutilized in the ED** -- excellent for anxiolysis during painful IV access, abscess I&D, or fracture reduction in cooperative patients. Rapid on/rapid off with minimal recovery time.
**Always document the pre-sedation time-out** -- patient identity, procedure planned, consent obtained, allergies reviewed, NPO status noted, equipment checked, reversal agents available.

proton-pump-inhibitors.title: Proton Pump Inhibitors

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — PPIs work by irreversible enzyme inhibition rather than receptor binding, demonstrating that not all drug targets are traditional receptors*

- Irreversibly inhibit the hydrogen-potassium ATPase (H+/K+ ATPase) proton pump on gastric parietal cells
- This is the FINAL step of acid secretion → most potent acid suppression available
- Reduce basal and stimulated gastric acid production by ~90%
- Prodrugs that require activation in acidic environment of parietal cell canaliculus
- Because they irreversibly bind, acid suppression lasts until new proton pumps are synthesized (~24-48 hr)
sections.indications:
- GERD (first-line for moderate-severe, erosive esophagitis)
- Peptic ulcer disease (gastric and duodenal) -- treatment and healing
- H. pylori eradication (as part of triple or quadruple therapy) -- see H. Pylori
- Stress ulcer prophylaxis (ICU patients with risk factors: mechanical ventilation > 48 hr, coagulopathy, traumatic brain injury, burns, sepsis)
- Zollinger-Ellison syndrome (gastrinoma -- requires high doses)
- NSAID-induced gastroprotection (chronic NSAID use + GI risk factors) -- see NSAIDs
- Eosinophilic esophagitis (emerging indication)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| **Omeprazole (Prilosec)** | PO/OTC | 20-40 mg daily | Most commonly prescribed; OTC available; CYP2C19 interactions (reduces clopidogrel activation) |
| **Esomeprazole (Nexium)** | PO/IV | 20-40 mg daily | S-isomer of omeprazole; IV formulation available; slightly less CYP2C19 interaction |
| **Pantoprazole (Protonix)** | PO/IV | 40 mg daily-BID | Most commonly used IV PPI in hospitals; FEWEST CYP450 interactions (preferred with clopidogrel); IV for stress ulcer prophylaxis |
| **Lansoprazole (Prevacid)** | PO | 15-30 mg daily | Also available as orally disintegrating tablet; can be given via NG tube |
| **Rabeprazole (Aciphex)** | PO | 20 mg daily | Non-enzymatic activation (less affected by CYP2C19 polymorphisms) |

> All PPIs are roughly equivalent in efficacy at equivalent doses. Drug selection is often based on drug interactions, route availability, and formulary.
sections.monitoring:
| Parameter | Frequency | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|----------|
| Symptom response | 4-8 weeks | Assess for adequate acid suppression |
| Magnesium level | Annual if long-term use | Hypomagnesemia (chronic PPI use) |
| Calcium/Vitamin D/Bone density | Consider with prolonged use in high-risk patients | Fracture risk |
| B12 | Consider annually with long-term use | Reduced absorption |
| Renal function | Periodic with chronic use | Acute interstitial nephritis (rare) |
| C. difficile awareness | Ongoing | Increased risk with acid suppression |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Headache, diarrhea, nausea, abdominal pain, flatulence
- Generally very well tolerated short-term

### Long-Term Use Concerns
| Concern | Mechanism | Clinical Significance |
|---------|-----------|---------------------|
| **C. difficile infection** | Gastric acid is a barrier to ingested pathogens; suppression increases risk | Clinically significant; FDA warning |
| **Hypomagnesemia** | Impaired intestinal Mg2+ absorption | Can be severe; presents as muscle cramps, tremor, arrhythmias; may take months-years to develop |
| **B12 deficiency** | Acid required for B12 release from food proteins | Monitor with chronic use; supplement if deficient |
| **Calcium malabsorption / fractures** | Reduced calcium absorption → possible bone density loss | FDA warning for hip, wrist, spine fractures with long-term use; especially postmenopausal women |
| **Fundic gland polyps** | Acid suppression → parietal cell hyperplasia | Usually benign; reversible with PPI discontinuation |
| **Acute interstitial nephritis** | Immune-mediated | Rare but can cause AKI; monitor renal function |
| **Community-acquired pneumonia** | Reduced gastric acid → increased bacterial colonization → aspiration risk | Modest risk increase |
| **Rebound acid hypersecretion** | Chronic suppression → upregulation of gastrin → rebound when stopped | Taper after prolonged use rather than abrupt stop |
sections.contraindications:
- Known hypersensitivity to PPI or benzimidazoles
- Concurrent rilpivirine (HIV NNRTI -- requires acid for absorption)
- Use caution with clopidogrel (omeprazole/esomeprazole reduce clopidogrel activation via CYP2C19; use pantoprazole instead)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Administration:**
  - Take 30-60 minutes BEFORE meals (ideally before breakfast); proton pumps must be actively secreting acid for PPI to bind them
  - Swallow capsules whole -- do not crush or chew (enteric-coated to protect from premature gastric acid activation)
  - For NG tube: Lansoprazole orally disintegrating tablet or omeprazole suspension; alternatively, open capsule and mix granules with applesauce (do not crush granules)
  - IV pantoprazole: Infuse over 15 min (bolus) or continuous infusion for acute GI bleeding; flush line (incompatible with many IV drugs)
- **Assessment:**
  - Evaluate for alarm symptoms that warrant endoscopy: dysphagia, unintentional weight loss, GI bleeding, persistent vomiting, age > 55 with new symptoms
  - Review duration of use -- question chronic PPI use without clear indication
  - Check medication list for interactions (clopidogrel, methotrexate, rilpivirine, tacrolimus)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take before first meal of the day; consistent timing
  - PPIs are for healing and maintenance, not instant relief (take antacid for immediate symptoms)
  - Maximum OTC use: 14 days, up to 3 courses per year (per labeling; see provider if symptoms persist)
  - Report persistent diarrhea (C. diff concern), muscle cramps (Mg2+), bone pain
  - If discontinuing after long-term use, taper gradually (rebound hyperacidity)
sections.related-concepts:
- Gastrointestinal System
- Antibiotics Overview
- NSAIDs
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics

clinicalPearls: **PPIs before meals, not after.** This is the most common patient error. PPIs are activated by acid in parietal cells -- those cells must be actively secreting (stimulated by eating). Taking a PPI after a meal or at bedtime on an empty stomach significantly reduces effectiveness.

**Pantoprazole is the PPI of choice with clopidogrel.** Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, which is the enzyme that activates clopidogrel (a prodrug). This reduces clopidogrel's antiplatelet effect. Pantoprazole has the least CYP2C19 interaction. FDA has a Black Box warning for omeprazole + clopidogrel.

**"PPI deprescribing" is a real initiative.** Many patients are on chronic PPIs without ongoing indication. Regular reassessment and step-down therapy (PPI → H2 blocker → as-needed antacid) is appropriate for many patients.

**Stress ulcer prophylaxis in the ICU is NOT indicated for everyone.** Only patients with risk factors (mechanical ventilation > 48 hr, coagulopathy, TBI, burns > 35% BSA, sepsis) warrant prophylaxis. Unnecessary PPI use in the ICU increases C. diff and pneumonia risk.
**PPIs mask gastric cancer symptoms.** Alarm symptoms (weight loss, dysphagia, bleeding, anemia) require endoscopy before starting long-term PPI, especially in patients > 55 with new onset symptoms.

**Hypomagnesemia from PPIs can be severe and refractory.** Oral and even IV magnesium may not correct levels while the PPI continues. Sometimes the PPI must be discontinued for magnesium to normalize.

**Rebound hyperacidity is real.** After prolonged PPI use (> 8 weeks), chronic gastrin elevation leads to parietal cell hyperplasia. Abrupt discontinuation → surge of acid production → symptoms worse than before starting. Taper over 2-4 weeks.

reversal-agents.title: Reversal Agents

sections.definition:
Reversal agents are medications that counteract the effects of specific drugs — either by competitive antagonism at the receptor, direct binding/neutralization of the drug, or replenishment of depleted clotting factors. These are among the most time-critical medications in emergency and critical care nursing. Knowing which reversal agent matches which drug, the correct dose, and the expected onset is essential for ED and ICU practice.

> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — reversal agents work by competitive antagonism, receptor displacement, or drug sequestration*
sections.key-agents:
### Opioid Reversal

#### Naloxone (Narcan)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Competitive μ-opioid receptor antagonist |
| **Indication** | Opioid overdose (respiratory depression, altered LOC) |
| **IV Dose** | 0.04–0.4 mg IV q2-3min; titrate to respiratory effort (RR ≥12), NOT full alertness |
| **IM/IN Dose** | 0.4 mg IM; 4 mg intranasal (Narcan nasal spray) |
| **Onset** | IV: 1–2 min; IM: 3–5 min; IN: 3–5 min |
| **Duration** | 30–90 minutes (shorter than most opioids!) |
| **Drip** | 0.4–0.8 mg/hr IV infusion for sustained reversal |
| **Key Pearl** | Start LOW (0.04 mg) in opioid-dependent patients to avoid precipitated withdrawal, seizures, pulmonary edema, and severe agitation. Goal = adequate ventilation, NOT full consciousness |
| **Careful!** | Duration shorter than fentanyl, methadone, extended-release opioids → **re-sedation risk**. Observe minimum 4 hours post-naloxone (longer for long-acting opioids). Can precipitate acute withdrawal → vomiting, aspiration risk |

### Benzodiazepine Reversal

#### Flumazenil (Romazicon)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Competitive GABA-A receptor antagonist at benzodiazepine binding site |
| **Indication** | Benzodiazepine overdose; reversal of procedural sedation with benzos |
| **Dose** | 0.2 mg IV over 15 sec → 0.3 mg at 1 min → 0.5 mg q1min; max 3–5 mg total |
| **Onset** | 1–2 minutes |
| **Duration** | 45–90 minutes |
| **Key Pearl** | **RARELY used in ED for unknown overdose** — risk of precipitating seizures in chronic benzo users or mixed ingestions (especially TCAs). Primarily used for reversal of procedural sedation in controlled settings |
| **Careful!** | **Contraindicated in known/suspected chronic benzodiazepine use** (seizure risk), TCA co-ingestion, seizure history. Can cause rebound sedation. NOT recommended as diagnostic tool for altered mental status |

### Direct Oral Anticoagulant (DOAC) Reversal

#### Idarucizumab (Praxbind) — Dabigatran Reversal
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Monoclonal antibody fragment that binds dabigatran with 350× greater affinity than thrombin |
| **Indication** | Life-threatening bleeding or emergent surgery in patients on dabigatran (Pradaxa) |
| **Dose** | 5 g IV (two 2.5 g vials) given as two consecutive infusions or bolus |
| **Onset** | Immediate (within minutes) |
| **Duration** | 24 hours |
| **Key Pearl** | Specific to dabigatran ONLY — does not reverse rivaroxaban, apixaban, or edoxaban. Complete reversal in >98% of patients in RE-VERSE AD trial |
| **Careful!** | Prothrombotic risk. No redosing data. Cost ~$3,500/dose |

#### Andexanet Alfa (Andexxa) — Factor Xa Inhibitor Reversal
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Recombinant modified Factor Xa decoy that binds and sequesters factor Xa inhibitors |
| **Indication** | Life-threatening or uncontrolled bleeding in patients on rivaroxaban (Xarelto) or apixaban (Eliquis) |
| **Dose** | Low-dose: 400 mg bolus → 4 mg/min × 120 min; High-dose: 800 mg bolus → 8 mg/min × 120 min (based on drug/timing/dose) |
| **Onset** | Minutes |
| **Duration** | Requires continuous infusion (anti-Xa activity rebounds after bolus alone) |
| **Key Pearl** | Extremely expensive (~$25,000–$50,000/course). Many institutions use 4-factor PCC as first-line instead due to cost and availability. Check your facility formulary |
| **Careful!** | Thrombotic events reported (18% in ANNEXA-4 trial). Rebound anticoagulation after infusion ends — monitor closely |

### Heparin Reversal

#### Protamine Sulfate
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Positively charged protein that electrostatically binds negatively charged heparin, forming inactive complex |
| **Indication** | Unfractionated heparin (UFH) reversal; partial LMWH reversal |
| **Dose (UFH)** | 1 mg protamine per 100 units heparin given in prior 2–3 hours; max 50 mg single dose; give SLOW IV over 10 min |
| **Dose (LMWH)** | 1 mg per 1 mg enoxaparin (if <8 hrs since dose); only ~60% effective for LMWH |
| **Onset** | 5 minutes |
| **Duration** | ~2 hours |
| **Key Pearl** | Give SLOWLY — rapid administration causes severe hypotension, bradycardia, dyspnea, and anaphylactoid reactions. Have resuscitation equipment at bedside |
| **Careful!** | **Anaphylaxis risk** — higher in patients with fish allergy (protamine derived from salmon sperm), prior protamine exposure, vasectomy, NPH insulin use. Does NOT fully reverse LMWH or fondaparinux |

### Warfarin Reversal

> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — warfarin and DOACs each deplete or block specific cascade factors; PCC and FFP work by directly replenishing those factors (II, VII, IX, X and all factors respectively), restoring the enzymatic amplification chain that produces thrombin and fibrin*

#### Phytonadione (Vitamin K1)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Provides vitamin K substrate for hepatic synthesis of factors II, VII, IX, X |
| **Indication** | Warfarin reversal (elevated INR with or without bleeding) |
| **IV Dose** | 10 mg IV slow infusion over 20–60 min (life-threatening bleeding) |
| **PO Dose** | 2.5–10 mg PO (supratherapeutic INR without major bleeding) |
| **Onset** | IV: 6–8 hours for full effect (factors must be synthesized); PO: 24–48 hours |
| **Key Pearl** | Vitamin K alone is TOO SLOW for acute life-threatening bleeding — must combine with PCC or FFP for immediate factor replacement. IV vitamin K provides sustained reversal; PCC provides the bridge |
| **Careful!** | IV administration can cause anaphylactoid reaction (rare but serious) — must dilute and give slowly. IM route avoided (erratic absorption, hematoma risk in anticoagulated patients) |

#### 4-Factor Prothrombin Complex Concentrate (4F-PCC: Kcentra)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Contains factors II, VII, IX, X + protein C and S — directly replaces depleted clotting factors |
| **Indication** | Acute major bleeding on warfarin; increasingly used off-label for DOAC reversal |
| **Dose** | INR-based: INR 2–4: 25 units/kg; INR 4–6: 35 units/kg; INR >6: 50 units/kg; max 2,500–5,000 units |
| **Onset** | 10–15 minutes |
| **Duration** | 6–8 hours (give vitamin K concurrently for sustained reversal) |
| **Key Pearl** | First-line for warfarin-associated life-threatening bleeding — faster, lower volume, more predictable than FFP. Many facilities also use for DOAC reversal when andexanet alfa unavailable |
| **Careful!** | Thrombotic risk (DVT, PE, DIC). Contraindicated in HIT. Small volume advantage over FFP (doesn't require blood type matching if using Kcentra). Always co-administer vitamin K |

#### 3-Factor PCC (Profilnine, Bebulin)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Contains factors II, IX, X (minimal factor VII) |
| **Indication** | Warfarin reversal when 4F-PCC unavailable |
| **Key Pearl** | Less effective than 4F-PCC due to low factor VII — may need supplemental FFP or rFVIIa |

### Neuromuscular Blockade Reversal

#### Sugammadex (Bridion)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Modified γ-cyclodextrin that encapsulates rocuronium and vecuronium molecules in a 1:1 complex |
| **Indication** | Reversal of rocuronium or vecuronium-induced neuromuscular blockade |
| **Dose** | Moderate block: 2 mg/kg; Deep block: 4 mg/kg; Immediate reversal (RSI): 16 mg/kg |
| **Onset** | 1–3 minutes |
| **Key Pearl** | Revolutionary — can reverse even deep neuromuscular blockade within minutes. Does NOT work for succinylcholine or cisatracurium. May interfere with hormonal contraceptives for 7 days |
| **Careful!** | Expensive (~$100–200/dose). Can cause bradycardia, anaphylaxis (rare). Renal dosing not established for CrCl <30 |

#### Neostigmine + Glycopyrrolate (Traditional Reversal)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Neostigmine inhibits acetylcholinesterase → increases ACh at neuromuscular junction; glycopyrrolate blocks muscarinic side effects |
| **Dose** | Neostigmine 0.03–0.07 mg/kg + glycopyrrolate 0.007–0.014 mg/kg IV |
| **Onset** | 7–10 minutes |
| **Key Pearl** | Only effective at shallow-moderate block (TOF count ≥2). Must give glycopyrrolate to prevent bradycardia, excessive secretions. Being largely replaced by sugammadex |

### Digitalis Reversal

#### Digoxin Immune Fab (DigiFab/Digibind)
| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Mechanism** | Antibody fragments that bind free digoxin, preventing tissue binding and accelerating renal elimination |
| **Indication** | Life-threatening digoxin toxicity (hemodynamically unstable dysrhythmias, hyperkalemia >5.5 with dig toxicity) |
| **Dose** | Acute ingestion: vials = (amount ingested in mg × 0.8) / 0.5; Chronic toxicity: empiric 3–5 vials; may calculate from serum level |
| **Onset** | 20–30 minutes |
| **Key Pearl** | Digoxin level becomes unreliable after DigiFab (assay measures bound + unbound). Monitor clinical response (rhythm, potassium), NOT levels. Expect potassium to drop rapidly as K+ shifts back intracellularly |
| **Careful!** | Allergic reactions possible (derived from sheep antibodies). Worsening heart failure possible as digoxin effect removed. Hypokalemia can develop quickly — monitor K+ closely |
sections.clinical-decision-guide-which-reversal-a:
| Drug to Reverse | Reversal Agent | Key Point |
|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Opioids** (fentanyl, morphine, hydromorphone) | Naloxone | Titrate to respiratory effort, not consciousness |
| **Benzodiazepines** (midazolam, lorazepam) | Flumazenil | Rarely used in ED — seizure risk in chronic users |
| **Dabigatran** (Pradaxa) | Idarucizumab (Praxbind) | Dabigatran-specific; 5 g IV |
| **Rivaroxaban/Apixaban** (Xarelto/Eliquis) | Andexanet alfa OR 4F-PCC | Cost drives institutional choice |
| **UFH** (heparin drip) | Protamine sulfate | 1 mg per 100 units; give slowly |
| **LMWH** (enoxaparin) | Protamine (partial) | Only ~60% effective |
| **Warfarin** (Coumadin) — acute bleed | 4F-PCC + IV Vitamin K | PCC for immediate effect; Vit K for sustained |
| **Warfarin** — elevated INR, no bleeding | PO Vitamin K 2.5–10 mg | Recheck INR in 24 hours |
| **Rocuronium/Vecuronium** | Sugammadex | Works even in deep block |
| **Digoxin** | DigiFab | Monitor K+ closely; dig levels unreliable after |
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment Before Administration
- **Identify the causative drug** — incorrect reversal agent = no effect and wasted time
- **Document time of last dose** of the drug being reversed (affects dosing for protamine, andexanet)
- **Baseline vitals and neurological status** — especially respiratory rate for opioid reversal
- **Assess bleeding severity** — for anticoagulant reversal: active bleed site, hemodynamic stability, hemoglobin trend
- **Allergy history** — fish allergy (protamine), sheep products (DigiFab)

### Administration Pearls
- **Naloxone**: Start with 0.04 mg in opioid-dependent patients. The goal is breathing, not talking. Expect vomiting with full reversal → position for aspiration prevention
- **Protamine**: NEVER push fast — dedicated line, slow infusion over 10+ minutes. Monitor BP and HR continuously
- **PCC (Kcentra)**: Reconstitute per package insert; infuse at 0.12 mL/kg/min, max 8.4 mL/min. Small volume = can go through peripheral IV
- **Idarucizumab**: Two vials given back-to-back as bolus or short infusion. No reconstitution needed. Room temperature stable
- **Vitamin K IV**: Must dilute in 50 mL NS and infuse over 20+ minutes. Keep anaphylaxis kit available
- **DigiFab**: Infuse over 30 minutes. Monitor potassium q1-2h — can drop precipitously

### Monitoring After Administration
| Agent | Monitor | Duration | Watch For |
|-------|---------|----------|-----------|
| Naloxone | RR, SpO2, LOC | **≥4 hours** (longer for long-acting opioids) | Re-sedation, withdrawal |
| Flumazenil | LOC, seizure activity | ≥2 hours | Rebound sedation, seizures |
| Idarucizumab | Bleeding, coags | 24 hours | Rebleeding (can redose) |
| Andexanet alfa | Anti-Xa, bleeding | During and post-infusion | Rebound anticoagulation, thrombosis |
| Protamine | BP, HR, aPTT | 2–3 hours | Hypotension, rebound heparinization |
| PCC + Vit K | INR, bleeding | INR at 30 min, 6h, 24h | Thrombosis, INR rebound |
| Sugammadex | TOF, respiratory effort | Until full recovery | Recurarization (rare), bradycardia |
| DigiFab | K+, rhythm, dig level | Continuous × 24h | Hypokalemia, CHF exacerbation |
sections.drug-interactions:
- **Naloxone + long-acting opioids** → reversal wears off before opioid; redose or start drip
- **Protamine + NPH insulin** → increased anaphylaxis risk (protamine component in NPH)
- **Sugammadex + hormonal contraceptives** → may reduce efficacy × 7 days; advise backup contraception
- **PCC + antifibrinolytics (TXA)** → additive thrombotic risk; use cautiously together
- **DigiFab + serum digoxin assays** → total dig level falsely elevated for days; free dig unmeasurable at most labs
sections.keyPoints:
- **Know your reversal pairs** — the wrong agent wastes critical time in emergencies
- **Naloxone duration < most opioid duration** — always plan for re-sedation monitoring
- **4F-PCC is first-line for warfarin-associated life-threatening bleeding** — faster and more predictable than FFP
- **Flumazenil is NOT a go-to for unknown overdose** in the ED — seizure risk too high
- **Vitamin K alone is too slow** for acute life-threatening warfarin bleeding — need PCC for bridging
- **Protamine only partially reverses LMWH** and does NOT reverse fondaparinux
sections.key-conditions:
- Opioid Overdose — naloxone is the definitive reversal; titrate to respiratory effort
- Intracerebral Hemorrhage — emergent anticoagulant reversal (PCC, idarucizumab, andexanet) to limit hematoma expansion
- Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy — reversal of anticoagulants in hemorrhaging trauma patients
- Atrial Fibrillation — most common reason patients are anticoagulated and thus need reversal for emergent surgery or bleeding
- Hyperkalemia — digoxin toxicity causes hyperkalemia; DigiFab reversal drops K rapidly
sections.references:
- Anticoagulants — full anticoagulant class details
- Opioid Analgesics — opioid pharmacology and toxicity
- Sedatives and Anxiolytics — benzodiazepine pharmacology
- Blood Products and Transfusion — FFP, cryoprecipitate, factor replacement
- Coagulation and Hemostasis — coagulation cascade physiology
- Neuromuscular Blocking Agents — NMB pharmacology and monitoring

clinicalPearls: **"Titrate naloxone to respiratory effort, not consciousness"** — Full reversal in opioid-dependent patients causes projectile vomiting, severe agitation, sympathetic surge, and potentially pulmonary edema. Start at 0.04 mg and go up slowly. The patient who is breathing at RR 14 but sleepy is a WIN

**"PCC is the new FFP"** — For warfarin reversal with active bleeding, 4F-PCC (Kcentra) has largely replaced FFP: faster onset, smaller volume, no blood type needed, more predictable INR correction. Always co-administer IV vitamin K for sustained effect
**"Protamine slow = patient alive"** — Rapid protamine administration causes catastrophic hypotension. 10 minutes minimum for the infusion. This is one medication where slower truly is better

**"Know your facility formulary"** — Andexanet alfa vs PCC for DOAC reversal is often an institutional decision based on cost and availability. Know what's stocked in YOUR pharmacy and blood bank before the emergency

**"Post-naloxone = post-observation"** — Never discharge a patient immediately after naloxone reversal. Minimum 4-hour observation for short-acting opioids, longer for fentanyl patches, methadone, or extended-release formulations. Re-sedation kills

sedatives-and-anxiolytics.title: Sedatives and Anxiolytics

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

- **Benzodiazepines:** Enhance GABA-A receptor activity by increasing chloride channel opening FREQUENCY → neuronal hyperpolarization → CNS depression. Require GABA to be present (not direct agonists).
- **Propofol:** GABA-A receptor agonist; also modulates NMDA and sodium channels. Direct CNS depression.
- **Dexmedetomidine (Precedex):** Selective alpha-2 adrenergic agonist in locus coeruleus → sedation WITHOUT respiratory depression. Also provides analgesia.
- **Ketamine:** NMDA receptor antagonist → dissociative anesthesia, analgesia, amnesia. Sympathomimetic properties.
sections.indications:
- Procedural sedation (midazolam, propofol, ketamine)
- ICU sedation (propofol, dexmedetomidine, midazolam)
- Anxiety disorders (lorazepam, alprazolam, diazepam -- short-term only)
- Insomnia (temazepam, zolpidem -- short-term)
- Status epilepticus (lorazepam first-line, midazolam IM if no IV access) -- see Antiepileptics
- Alcohol withdrawal (lorazepam, diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) -- see Alcohol Use Disorder
- Pre-operative anxiolysis (midazolam)
- Rapid sequence intubation (propofol, ketamine, midazolam)
sections.keyDrugs:
### Benzodiazepines
| Drug | Route | Onset | Duration | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------|----------|----------------|
| **Midazolam (Versed)** | IV/IM/IN/PO | IV: 1-3 min; IM: 5-15 min | 1-2 hr | Short-acting; amnestic; intranasal for pediatric/seizure rescue; CYP3A4 metabolized |
| **Lorazepam (Ativan)** | IV/PO/IM | IV: 1-3 min; PO: 30-60 min | 6-8 hr | Intermediate-acting; NO active metabolites (preferred in liver disease, elderly); first-line for status epilepticus IV |
| **Diazepam (Valium)** | IV/PO/Rectal | IV: 1-3 min; PO: 15-30 min | Long (active metabolites 20-100 hr) | Long-acting; active metabolite (desmethyldiazepam); good for muscle spasm, alcohol withdrawal (self-tapering); rectal gel for seizure rescue |
| **Alprazolam (Xanax)** | PO | 15-30 min | 4-6 hr | Short-acting; HIGH dependence potential; difficult to discontinue; panic disorder |
| **Chlordiazepoxide (Librium)** | PO | 30-60 min | Long (active metabolites) | Classic alcohol withdrawal drug; self-tapering due to long half-life |

### Non-Benzodiazepine Sedatives
| Drug | Route | Key Features |
|------|-------|-------------|
| **Propofol (Diprivan)** | IV only | Onset 30-60 sec; ultra-short acting (wears off in minutes); lipid emulsion (egg/soy allergy -- use caution); NO analgesia; respiratory depression; hypotension; count calories from lipid (1.1 kcal/mL); monitor triglycerides; propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS) with prolonged high-dose use |
| **Dexmedetomidine (Precedex)** | IV | Unique: sedation WITHOUT respiratory depression; allows "cooperative sedation" (patient arousable); bradycardia and hypotension main risks; no loading dose if hemodynamically unstable; ideal for ventilator weaning and delirium-prone patients |
| **Ketamine** | IV/IM/IN | Dissociative anesthesia; maintains airway reflexes and respiratory drive; bronchodilation; emergence reactions (hallucinations); contraindicated in elevated ICP (debated); sympathomimetic → increases HR/BP |
sections.monitoring:
### ICU Sedation Scales
| Scale | Use |
|-------|-----|
| **RASS (Richmond Agitation-Sedation Scale)** | -5 (unarousable) to +4 (combative); target varies by clinical situation; light sedation (RASS 0 to -2) preferred |
| **SAS (Riker Sedation-Agitation Scale)** | 1 (unarousable) to 7 (dangerous agitation) |
| **CAM-ICU** | Delirium screening tool; use with RASS; daily assessment |

| Parameter | Monitoring |
|-----------|-----------|
| Respiratory rate/SpO2 | Continuous during IV sedation |
| Blood pressure/HR | Continuous; propofol → hypotension; dexmedetomidine → bradycardia |
| Level of sedation (RASS) | q1-2h; target to lightest effective level |
| Triglycerides | q48-72h with propofol infusion |
| Daily sedation interruption | Per ABCDEF bundle; turn off sedation and assess |
| Delirium (CAM-ICU) | Every shift minimum |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Benzodiazepines
- Sedation, confusion, anterograde amnesia
- Respiratory depression (potentiated by opioids -- Black Box)
- Paradoxical agitation (especially elderly, brain-injured)
- Physical dependence and withdrawal (seizures, delirium -- can be fatal)
- Falls risk (elderly)

### Propofol
- Hypotension (dose-dependent vasodilation + myocardial depression)
- Respiratory depression/apnea
- Pain on injection (mitigate with lidocaine co-administration)
- Hypertriglyceridemia
- **Propofol infusion syndrome (PRIS):** Rare but fatal -- metabolic acidosis, rhabdomyolysis, hyperkalemia, cardiac failure; risk with >48 hr at >80 mcg/kg/min

### Dexmedetomidine
- Bradycardia (can be severe)
- Hypotension
- Minimal respiratory depression (key advantage)
sections.reversal-agent:
| Drug | Reversal | Dose | Notes |
|------|----------|------|-------|
| Benzodiazepines | **Flumazenil (Romazicon)** | 0.2 mg IV q1min (max 3-5 mg) | Risk of seizures in chronic benzo users or mixed OD with TCAs; short half-life (45-90 min) -- resedation risk; use cautiously |

> **No reversal agent exists for propofol or dexmedetomidine.** Manage supportively (airway, fluids, vasopressors).
sections.contraindications:
- **Benzodiazepines:** Acute narrow-angle glaucoma; severe respiratory depression; concurrent opioids (relative -- Black Box warning)
- **Propofol:** Egg/soy allergy (controversial but listed); should not be used for ICU sedation in pediatrics (PRIS risk)
- **Dexmedetomidine:** Advanced heart block without pacemaker; severe bradycardia
- **Ketamine:** Historically cited as contraindicated in elevated ICP (now debated); severe hypertension; psychotic disorders; eclampsia
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Baseline respiratory status, mental status, hemodynamics
  - Fall risk assessment (benzodiazepines significantly increase fall risk)
  - Assess for delirium risk factors before choosing sedative (dexmedetomidine preferred if delirium-prone)
  - Check for chronic benzo use before using flumazenil (withdrawal seizures)
- **Administration:**
  - Propofol: Strict aseptic technique (lipid emulsion = bacterial growth medium); change tubing q12h per CDC; dedicated line preferred
  - Midazolam IV: Titrate in 0.5-1 mg increments; wait 2-3 min between doses
  - All IV sedatives: Continuous monitoring (SpO2, HR, BP, RR); suction and airway equipment at bedside
  - ABCDEF bundle: Daily sedation interruption + spontaneous breathing trial + delirium assessment
- **Patient Teaching (outpatient benzos):**
  - Do not combine with alcohol or opioids (fatal respiratory depression)
  - Do not drive until response to medication is known
  - Do not stop abruptly after regular use > 2 weeks (seizure risk)
  - Benzodiazepines are for SHORT-TERM use; discuss long-term alternatives with provider
  - Store securely (high misuse potential)
sections.key-conditions:
- Alcohol Use Disorder — benzodiazepines (lorazepam, diazepam, chlordiazepoxide) are first-line for alcohol withdrawal; CIWA-guided dosing
- Anxiety Disorders and Panic — benzodiazepines for acute panic; short-term only due to dependence risk
- Epilepsy — lorazepam IV is first-line for status epilepticus; midazolam IM if no IV access
- Acute Psychosis — benzodiazepines for acute agitation; often combined with antipsychotics
- Delirium — dexmedetomidine preferred in delirium-prone ICU patients (less delirium than benzos/propofol)
- Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome — deep sedation required for proning and neuromuscular blockade; propofol or midazolam infusions
- Stimulant Intoxication and Use Disorder — benzodiazepines first-line for cocaine/meth agitation and seizures
- Opioid Withdrawal — benzodiazepines as adjunct for anxiety/agitation during withdrawal
sections.related-concepts:
- Pain Physiology and Management
- Opioid Analgesics
- Antiepileptics
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics
- Nervous System
sections.clinical-applications-seen:
*Real clinical scenarios where you've seen these drugs used. Links to Clinical Encounters Log.*

| Drug | Clinical Context | Encounter |
|------|-----------------|-----------|
| Diazepam (Valium) 2 mg | Opioid withdrawal agitation — GABA-A calming of CNS hyperexcitability | 2026-02-09 |

---

clinicalPearls: **Lorazepam is the benzo of choice in liver disease and the elderly** -- no active metabolites, no CYP450 metabolism (glucuronidation only). "LOT" rule: Lorazepam, Oxazepam, Temazepam are safe in liver failure.

**ABCDEF bundle saves lives.** Daily sedation vacation + spontaneous breathing trial + delirium screening reduces ventilator days, ICU stay, and mortality. Light sedation (RASS 0 to -2) is almost always the target.
**Propofol is NOT an analgesic.** Patients on propofol infusions still need pain management. A sedated patient who can't express pain is NOT a pain-free patient.

**Dexmedetomidine is the only ICU sedative that doesn't cause respiratory depression.** This makes it ideal for ventilator weaning, extubation, non-intubated procedures, and delirium-prone patients. But watch for bradycardia.
**Paradoxical agitation from benzos** is most common in elderly, traumatic brain injury, and pediatric patients. More benzo makes it WORSE. Switch agents.

**Flumazenil is dangerous in chronic benzo users.** Reversing years of benzodiazepine tolerance acutely → seizures, arrhythmias, death. Know benzo history before using. It's mainly for isolated acute ingestion in benzo-naive patients.
**Propofol infusion syndrome:** Think of it when a patient on high-dose propofol develops unexplained metabolic acidosis + rising CK + bradycardia. Stop propofol immediately and switch sedatives.
**Ketamine is a game-changer for procedural sedation** because it maintains respiratory drive and airway reflexes. Pre-treat with midazolam or ondansetron to reduce emergence reactions.

statins.title: Statins

sections.mechanism: Competitively inhibit HMG-CoA reductase, the rate-limiting enzyme in hepatic cholesterol synthesis. This upregulates LDL receptors on hepatocytes, increasing LDL clearance from blood. Also modestly raise HDL and lower triglycerides. Pleiotropic effects include anti-inflammatory and plaque-stabilizing properties.
sections.indications:
- Hyperlipidemia / Dyslipidemia
- Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease (ASCVD) -- primary and secondary prevention
- Acute Coronary Syndromes (high-intensity statin initiated in hospital)
- Diabetes (age 40-75: at least moderate intensity)
- LDL >= 190 mg/dL (high-intensity regardless of risk score)
- 10-year ASCVD risk >= 7.5% with LDL 70-189 (moderate-to-high intensity)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Atorvastatin | PO | 10-80 mg daily | High-intensity at 40-80 mg; no renal dose adjustment; can take any time of day |
| Rosuvastatin | PO | 5-40 mg daily | Most potent LDL lowering; high-intensity at 20-40 mg; can take any time of day |
| Simvastatin | PO | 10-40 mg daily (max 40 mg per FDA) | Take in evening (short half-life); 80 mg dose restricted (myopathy risk); many drug interactions |
| Pravastatin | PO | 10-80 mg daily | Least drug interactions (not CYP3A4 metabolized); good for transplant patients on cyclosporine |
| Lovastatin | PO | 10-80 mg daily | Take with evening meal (food enhances absorption) |
sections.statin-intensity-classification:
| Intensity | Expected LDL Reduction | Drugs/Doses |
|-----------|----------------------|-------------|
| **High** | >= 50% | Atorvastatin 40-80 mg; Rosuvastatin 20-40 mg |
| **Moderate** | 30-49% | Atorvastatin 10-20 mg; Rosuvastatin 5-10 mg; Simvastatin 20-40 mg; Pravastatin 40-80 mg |
| **Low** | < 30% | Simvastatin 10 mg; Pravastatin 10-20 mg; Lovastatin 20 mg |
sections.pharmacokinetics:
- Short half-life statins (simvastatin, lovastatin, pravastatin, fluvastatin): Take in evening (cholesterol synthesis peaks at night)
- Long half-life statins (atorvastatin, rosuvastatin): Can take any time of day
- Hepatic metabolism: Atorvastatin, simvastatin, lovastatin via CYP3A4 (interaction risk with azole antifungals, macrolides, grapefruit)
- Rosuvastatin/pravastatin: Minimal CYP metabolism (fewer interactions)

> *First principles: Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics — many statins are CYP3A4 substrates; co-administration with inhibitors (azoles, macrolides, amiodarone) increases statin levels and myopathy risk*
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Myalgia (muscle aches without CK elevation; 5-10% of patients)
- GI upset, nausea, constipation, flatulence
- Headache
- Elevated transaminases (usually mild, transient)

### Serious/Black Box
- **Rhabdomyolysis:** Severe myopathy with CK > 10x ULN; muscle breakdown leading to myoglobinuria and Acute Kidney Injury; risk increased with high doses, drug interactions, renal impairment, hypothyroidism
- **Hepatotoxicity:** Rare; check LFTs if symptoms (jaundice, dark urine, fatigue)
- **New-onset diabetes:** Small increased risk, especially with high-intensity; CV benefit still outweighs risk
- Memory complaints (rare, reversible)
sections.contraindications:
- Active liver disease or unexplained persistent LFT elevation (> 3x ULN)
- Pregnancy and breastfeeding (Category X -- teratogenic)
- Concurrent use of strong CYP3A4 inhibitors with simvastatin/lovastatin (e.g., itraconazole, HIV protease inhibitors, clarithromycin)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Lipid panel: Baseline, 4-12 weeks after initiation or dose change, then annually
  - LFTs: Baseline; recheck only if hepatotoxicity symptoms develop (routine monitoring no longer required per guidelines)
  - CK: Check if myalgia symptoms develop; NOT required routinely
  - Report muscle pain, tenderness, weakness, dark urine (rhabdomyolysis)
  - Blood glucose / HgbA1c (new-onset diabetes risk)
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take simvastatin in the evening; atorvastatin and rosuvastatin can be taken any time
  - Report unexplained muscle pain, weakness, or dark brown urine immediately
  - Avoid grapefruit juice with atorvastatin, simvastatin, lovastatin (CYP3A4 inhibition)
  - Statins are long-term therapy; do not stop without discussing with provider
  - Maintain diet and exercise (statins are adjunct, not replacement)
- **Administration Notes:**
  - Can be taken without regard to food (except lovastatin -- take with food)
  - No dose adjustment needed for atorvastatin in renal impairment
  - Rosuvastatin: Start at 5 mg in Asian patients (higher drug levels)

clinicalPearls: Post-ACS: Start high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg) before discharge regardless of baseline LDL
Myalgia management: Try dose reduction, alternate-day dosing, switch to different statin (pravastatin/rosuvastatin have lower myalgia rates), or consider coenzyme Q10 supplementation
Simvastatin 80 mg is restricted by FDA -- only continue if patient has been on it > 12 months without myopathy; do not initiate new patients at 80 mg
**Drug interactions to watch:** Amiodarone + simvastatin (max 20 mg simvastatin); diltiazem + simvastatin (max 20 mg); cyclosporine + any statin (increased myopathy risk)
Statins do NOT need to be held pre-surgery (continue perioperatively)
If LDL goal not met on max statin, add ezetimibe or PCSK9 inhibitor

systemic-corticosteroids.title: Systemic Corticosteroids

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

Bind intracellular glucocorticoid receptors, modulating gene transcription. Produce broad anti-inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects: suppress cytokine production, reduce leukocyte migration, inhibit phospholipase A2 (reducing prostaglandins and leukotrienes), and stabilize lysosomal membranes. Also have mineralocorticoid effects (Na retention, K wasting) -- varies by agent.

> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — corticosteroids suppress the inflammatory cascade at multiple levels: blocking phospholipase A2 halts arachidonic acid release (cutting off both COX and LOX pathways), while cytokine suppression (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-alpha) attenuates the systemic acute phase response*
sections.glucocorticoid-equivalency-table:
| Drug | Equivalent Dose | Anti-inflammatory Potency | Mineralocorticoid Potency | Duration |
|------|----------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|----------|
| Hydrocortisone | 20 mg | 1 | ++ | Short (8-12 hr) |
| Prednisone | 5 mg | 4 | + | Intermediate (12-36 hr) |
| Prednisolone | 5 mg | 4 | + | Intermediate (12-36 hr) |
| Methylprednisolone | 4 mg | 5 | +/- | Intermediate (12-36 hr) |
| Dexamethasone | 0.75 mg | 25-30 | 0 | Long (36-72 hr) |

*Key conversion: Prednisone 5 mg = Methylprednisolone 4 mg = Dexamethasone 0.75 mg = Hydrocortisone 20 mg*
sections.indications:
- Asthma exacerbation (prednisone burst or IV methylprednisolone)
- Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease exacerbation
- Anaphylaxis (adjunct after epinephrine)
- Autoimmune/inflammatory diseases (Lupus, Rheumatoid Arthritis, Crohn's Disease, Ulcerative Colitis, Multiple Sclerosis)
- Cerebral edema (dexamethasone -- vasogenic edema from tumors)
- Adrenal insufficiency / Adrenal Crisis (hydrocortisone)
- Organ transplant rejection (methylprednisolone pulse)
- Croup (dexamethasone)
- Septic shock refractory to vasopressors (stress-dose hydrocortisone)
- COVID-19 with hypoxemia (dexamethasone 6 mg x 10 days)
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Prednisone | PO | Asthma burst: 40-60 mg/day x 5-7 days; chronic: 5-60 mg/day | Oral workhorse; converted to prednisolone in liver; avoid in severe liver failure |
| Methylprednisolone | IV/PO | Acute asthma/COPD: 40-125 mg IV q6-8h; MS pulse: 1 g IV daily x 3-5 days | IV formulation (Solu-Medrol); minimal mineralocorticoid effect |
| Dexamethasone | IV/PO/IM | Croup: 0.6 mg/kg x 1 (max 16 mg); cerebral edema: 10 mg IV then 4 mg q6h; COVID: 6 mg daily x 10 days | Most potent; longest duration; NO mineralocorticoid effect; best for cerebral edema |
| Hydrocortisone | IV/PO | Adrenal crisis: 100 mg IV bolus, then 50 mg q8h; stress dosing: 50-100 mg IV q8h; physiologic replacement: 15-25 mg/day PO | Most similar to endogenous cortisol; HAS mineralocorticoid effect; drug of choice for adrenal insufficiency |
sections.taper-protocols-general-guidance:
- **Short course (< 7 days):** Usually no taper needed
- **7-14 days:** May not need taper, but consider brief taper if high dose
- **> 2-3 weeks at supraphysiologic doses:** MUST taper to prevent adrenal crisis
  - Example: Reduce by ~10-20% every 1-2 weeks
  - Or reduce prednisone by 5-10 mg every 1-2 weeks until 10 mg, then by 2.5 mg increments
  - Final taper: Switch to hydrocortisone physiologic dose and assess AM cortisol
- **HPA axis suppression** occurs with > 20 mg prednisone equivalent daily for > 3 weeks
- NEVER abruptly discontinue chronic steroids
sections.stress-dosing:
- Patients on chronic steroids with suppressed HPA axis need increased doses during physiologic stress (surgery, sepsis, critical illness)
- **Minor stress** (e.g., minor procedure): Hydrocortisone 25-50 mg IV x 1
- **Moderate stress** (e.g., moderate surgery): Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q8h x 24-48 hr
- **Major stress/septic shock:** Hydrocortisone 50 mg IV q6h (200 mg/day) or 100 mg IV q8h
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common (especially with chronic use)
- Hyperglycemia (steroid-induced diabetes) -- check blood glucose frequently
- Insomnia, mood changes, psychosis (especially high-dose)
- Weight gain, Cushingoid appearance (moon face, buffalo hump, central adiposity)
- Increased appetite
- Fluid retention, hypertension (mineralocorticoid effect)
- GI upset, peptic ulcer risk (add PPI if concurrent NSAID use)
- Impaired wound healing, thin skin, easy bruising
- Immunosuppression / increased infection risk

### Serious/Black Box
- **Adrenal suppression/crisis** with abrupt discontinuation after chronic use
- Osteoporosis, vertebral compression fractures (start Ca/vitamin D; consider bisphosphonate if long-term use)
- Avascular necrosis (especially femoral head)
- GI bleeding/perforation
- Posterior subcapsular cataracts, glaucoma
- Psychosis, severe mood disturbance
- Opportunistic infections (PJP, fungal, TB reactivation)
- Growth suppression in children
- Myopathy (proximal muscle weakness with chronic use)
sections.contraindications:
- Systemic fungal infections (relative; hydrocortisone OK for adrenal crisis)
- Live vaccines during immunosuppressive doses
- Caution: Diabetes, active PUD, osteoporosis, psychosis history, HF, HTN
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Blood glucose: Check BG q4-6h when initiating or on high doses; many patients need insulin sliding scale or adjustment
  - I&Os, daily weights (fluid retention)
  - Blood pressure (hypertension exacerbation)
  - Electrolytes: Na (retention), K (wasting)
  - Signs of infection (steroids mask fever and WBC elevation -- WBC will be elevated from steroids, not necessarily infection)
  - Mental status, mood changes, sleep pattern
  - Stool guaiac if prolonged use or concurrent NSAIDs
- **Patient Teaching:**
  - Take with food (reduces GI upset)
  - Morning dosing preferred (mimics physiologic cortisol peak; reduces insomnia)
  - Do NOT abruptly stop -- taper as directed
  - Carry steroid card/MedicAlert if on chronic therapy
  - Report: Signs of infection, vision changes, bone pain, mood changes, black/tarry stools
  - No live vaccines while on immunosuppressive doses (>= 20 mg prednisone equivalent for >= 2 weeks)
- **Administration Notes:**
  - IV methylprednisolone (Solu-Medrol): Can push over 1-2 min for standard doses; infuse pulse doses (500 mg-1 g) over 30-60 min (rapid push can cause arrhythmia, hypotension)
  - Dexamethasone: IV or PO (well absorbed orally, bioavailability ~80%)
  - Hydrocortisone IV: For adrenal crisis, give bolus immediately; do NOT delay for cortisol level
sections.key-conditions:
- Asthma — prednisone burst for exacerbations; IV methylprednisolone for status asthmaticus
- Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease — short course for acute exacerbations (prednisone 40 mg x 5 days)
- Sepsis — stress-dose hydrocortisone (200 mg/day) for vasopressor-refractory septic shock
- Anaphylaxis — adjunct to epinephrine; prevents biphasic reaction
- Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome — dexamethasone in COVID-related ARDS; role in other ARDS evolving
- Contact Dermatitis — moderate-severe cases may require systemic steroids
- Urticaria — short course for severe or refractory urticaria
- Crohn's Disease / Ulcerative Colitis — acute flare management; bridge to steroid-sparing agents

---

clinicalPearls: In septic shock: Stress-dose hydrocortisone (200 mg/day) if refractory to fluids + vasopressors; do NOT use dexamethasone for adrenal crisis (no mineralocorticoid effect)
Steroid-induced hyperglycemia typically peaks in afternoon/evening with morning dosing; anticipate need for PM insulin coverage
**Dexamethasone for COVID:** Only beneficial if patient requires O2; do NOT give to non-hypoxemic patients (may worsen outcomes)
Leukocytosis from steroids (demargination of WBCs) -- WBC may be 15-20K from steroids alone; do not automatically assume infection
Dexamethasone 6 mg = prednisone ~40 mg = methylprednisolone ~32 mg
PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) indicated if >= 20 mg prednisone daily for >= 1 month in immunosuppressed patients
Chronic steroid patients undergoing surgery: ALWAYS ask about home steroid dose and provide stress dosing

thyroid-medications.title: Thyroid Medications
thyroid-medications.drugClass: Endocrine / Thyroid Hormone Replacement and Antithyroid Agents

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

### Thyroid Hormone Replacement
- **Levothyroxine (T4):** Synthetic thyroxine; converted to active T3 in peripheral tissues; replaces deficient endogenous thyroid hormone
- **Liothyronine (T3):** Synthetic triiodothyronine; directly active form; faster onset but shorter duration

### Antithyroid Agents
- **Methimazole (MMI) and Propylthiouracil (PTU):** Inhibit thyroid peroxidase (TPO) enzyme → block iodination of tyrosine residues and coupling of MIT/DIT → decrease thyroid hormone synthesis
- PTU additionally blocks peripheral T4 → T3 conversion (unique advantage in thyroid storm)
sections.indications:
- **Levothyroxine:** Hypothyroidism (primary, secondary); TSH suppression in thyroid cancer; myxedema coma (IV levothyroxine + liothyronine)
- **Methimazole:** Hyperthyroidism / Graves Disease (first-line antithyroid in most cases)
- **PTU:** Hyperthyroidism (reserved for first trimester pregnancy and thyroid storm due to hepatotoxicity risk)
- **Radioactive iodine (I-131):** Definitive treatment for Graves disease; destroys thyroid tissue
- **Beta-blockers:** Symptom control of hyperthyroidism (tachycardia, tremor, anxiety) -- see Beta-Blockers; propranolol preferred (also blocks T4→T3 conversion)
sections.key-drugs:
| Drug | Route/Dose | Key Points |
|------|-----------|-----------|
| **Levothyroxine (Synthroid, Levoxyl)** | PO: Start 1.6 mcg/kg/day (full replacement); start low (12.5-25 mcg/day) in elderly/cardiac patients; IV = 50-75% of oral dose | Take on empty stomach, 30-60 min before breakfast or at bedtime; consistent timing critical; many drug/food interactions affect absorption |
| **Liothyronine (Cytomel)** | PO: 5-25 mcg BID-TID | Rapid onset (hours vs days); used in myxedema coma; T3 levels fluctuate -- not ideal for chronic replacement; generally reserved for specialists |
| **Methimazole (Tapazole)** | PO: Start 10-30 mg daily (dose depends on severity); maintenance 5-10 mg daily | First-line antithyroid agent; once daily dosing; fewer side effects than PTU; teratogenic in first trimester (aplasia cutis) |
| **Propylthiouracil (PTU)** | PO: 100-150 mg TID | Reserved for: (1) First trimester pregnancy, (2) Thyroid storm (blocks peripheral T4→T3), (3) Methimazole intolerance; Black Box hepatotoxicity |
| **Radioactive Iodine (I-131)** | PO (single dose) | Definitive therapy for Graves; results in permanent hypothyroidism (patient will need levothyroxine lifelong); contraindicated in pregnancy; radiation precautions for 1-3 days |
sections.monitoring:
| Medication | Lab Monitoring | Frequency | Goals |
|-----------|---------------|-----------|-------|
| Levothyroxine | TSH (primary); free T4 | TSH q6-8 weeks after dose change; then q6-12 months when stable | TSH 0.5-4.0 mIU/L (general); 0.1-0.5 for thyroid cancer suppression |
| Methimazole/PTU | TSH, free T4 | q4-6 weeks initially | Normalize free T4 first (TSH may remain suppressed for months) |
| Methimazole/PTU | CBC with differential | Baseline; if fever/sore throat develop | Agranulocytosis screening (WBC, ANC) |
| PTU | LFTs | Baseline and periodically | Hepatotoxicity (Black Box) |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Levothyroxine
- **Overreplacement symptoms** (iatrogenic hyperthyroidism): Tachycardia, palpitations, tremor, anxiety, insomnia, weight loss, heat intolerance, diarrhea, angina
- Bone loss with long-term excess (especially postmenopausal women)
- Risk of precipitating angina or arrhythmia in cardiac patients (start low, go slow)

### Antithyroid Agents
| Effect | Methimazole | PTU |
|--------|------------|-----|
| Agranulocytosis | 0.2-0.5%; usually first 3 months | Similar rate |
| Hepatotoxicity | Cholestatic (mild, reversible) | Fulminant hepatic failure (Black Box) -- reason PTU is second-line |
| Rash | Common (minor) | Common |
| Teratogenicity | Aplasia cutis, choanal atresia (first trimester) | Safer in first trimester (switch to MMI in 2nd trimester) |
| ANCA-positive vasculitis | Rare | More common than MMI |
sections.contraindications:
- **Levothyroxine:** Untreated adrenal insufficiency (replace cortisol FIRST or trigger adrenal crisis); acute MI (relative -- start cautiously)
- **Methimazole:** First trimester pregnancy (teratogenic); prior hypersensitivity
- **PTU:** Avoid unless first trimester or thyroid storm (hepatotoxicity risk)
- **I-131:** Pregnancy and breastfeeding (absolute); pregnancy should be avoided for 6 months after treatment
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Levothyroxine:**
  - Timing is everything: Empty stomach, 30-60 min before food, OR at bedtime (≥3 hr after last meal). Consistency matters more than which time.
  - Separate from: calcium, iron, antacids, PPIs, sucralfate (all reduce absorption) -- give levothyroxine 4 hours apart from these
  - Brand consistency: FDA considers levothyroxine narrow therapeutic index. Some patients are sensitive to brand changes -- recheck TSH 6 weeks after any formulation switch
  - Elderly/cardiac patients: Start 12.5-25 mcg/day and increase q4-6 weeks (prevent angina/arrhythmia)
  - Myxedema coma: Medical emergency -- IV levothyroxine + IV liothyronine + IV hydrocortisone (check adrenals); ICU management
- **Antithyroid agents:**
  - Educate patient: **Report fever, sore throat, mouth sores immediately** (agranulocytosis -- need STAT CBC with differential)
  - PTU: Report dark urine, jaundice, RUQ pain, unusual fatigue (hepatotoxicity)
  - Pregnancy: PTU in first trimester → switch to methimazole in second trimester
  - Monitor thyroid function closely during titration
- **I-131:**
  - Pregnancy test before administration (mandatory)
  - Radiation safety: Limit close contact with others (especially children and pregnant women) for 1-3 days
  - Expect hypothyroidism to develop weeks to months after treatment
sections.related-concepts:
- Endocrine System
- Beta-Blockers
- Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics

clinicalPearls: **"Start low, go slow" in elderly and cardiac patients.** Levothyroxine increases metabolic rate → increases myocardial oxygen demand. Starting at full replacement dose in an elderly patient with CAD can trigger angina, MI, or arrhythmia.

**Replace cortisol BEFORE thyroid.** If a patient has concomitant adrenal insufficiency (or pituitary failure), giving levothyroxine before addressing cortisol deficiency can precipitate adrenal crisis. Always check morning cortisol/cosyntropin stimulation test if pituitary disease is suspected.

**Levothyroxine absorption is affected by everything.** Coffee, calcium, iron, PPIs, soy, fiber supplements, and even grapefruit juice all reduce absorption. The safest approach: take on a completely empty stomach with water only.
**TSH takes 6-8 weeks to reflect a dose change.** Do not recheck TSH 2 weeks after adjustment -- it won't be accurate. The long half-life of T4 (~7 days) means steady state requires ~5 half-lives.
**Agranulocytosis from antithyroid drugs can be fatal.** Teach patients that fever + sore throat = emergency. They need a STAT CBC -- do not wait for the next office visit.

**PTU's Black Box for hepatotoxicity is real.** Fulminant liver failure requiring transplant has occurred. This is why methimazole is first-line for everyone except first-trimester pregnant women and thyroid storm patients.

**Thyroid storm is a clinical diagnosis and a medical emergency.** High fever, extreme tachycardia, altered mental status, GI symptoms. Treatment: PTU (blocks synthesis + peripheral conversion) + beta-blocker + iodine solution (given ≥1 hr after PTU) + hydrocortisone + cooling measures. ICU admission.
**Subclinical hypothyroidism** (elevated TSH, normal free T4) is common. Not all patients need treatment -- weigh symptoms, TSH level, pregnancy status, and CV risk factors.

vasopressors-and-inotropes.title: Vasopressors and Inotropes

sections.mechanism:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology · Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology*

- **Vasopressors:** Increase systemic vascular resistance (SVR) via alpha-1 adrenergic or V1 receptor stimulation, raising MAP
- **Inotropes:** Increase cardiac contractility via beta-1 stimulation or phosphodiesterase inhibition, raising cardiac output
- Most agents have mixed receptor profiles -- clinical effect depends on dose and predominant receptor activity
sections.receptorProfiles:
| Drug | Alpha-1 | Beta-1 | Beta-2 | Dopaminergic | V1 |
|------|---------|--------|--------|-------------|-----|
| Norepinephrine | +++ | ++ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Epinephrine | +++ | +++ | ++ | 0 | 0 |
| Phenylephrine | +++ | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Vasopressin | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | +++ |
| Dopamine | ++ (high) | ++ (mid) | 0 | ++ (low) | 0 |
| Dobutamine | + | +++ | ++ | 0 | 0 |
| Milrinone | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 |

*Milrinone = PDE-3 inhibitor (inodilator); does not act on adrenergic receptors*
sections.indications:
- Septic Shock -- norepinephrine first-line, add vasopressin second
- Cardiogenic Shock -- dobutamine or milrinone for pump failure; norepinephrine if hypotensive
- Anaphylaxis -- epinephrine first-line
- Cardiac Arrest -- epinephrine per ACLS
- Neurogenic shock -- norepinephrine or phenylephrine
- Post-cardiotomy / acute heart failure decompensation -- dobutamine, milrinone
- Medication-induced hypotension (e.g., post-anesthesia) -- phenylephrine
sections.keyDrugs:
| Drug | Route | Typical Dose Range | Key Difference |
|------|-------|-------------------|----------------|
| Norepinephrine | IV only | 0.01-0.5 mcg/kg/min (typical start: 2-5 mcg/min) | First-line for septic shock (Surviving Sepsis); potent vasoconstriction + some inotropy |
| Vasopressin | IV only | 0.03-0.04 units/min (fixed, NOT titrated) | Adjunct to norepinephrine; catecholamine-sparing; does not increase HR |
| Epinephrine | IV/IM | Drip: 0.01-0.5 mcg/kg/min; Anaphylaxis: 0.3-0.5 mg IM (1:1000); Cardiac arrest: 1 mg IV q3-5 min (1:10,000) | Most potent combined alpha + beta; increases HR, contractility, SVR; causes hyperglycemia and lactic acidosis |
| Dobutamine | IV only | 2.5-20 mcg/kg/min | Primary inotrope; increases CO but may decrease BP (beta-2 vasodilation); can increase HR |
| Milrinone | IV only | Load: 50 mcg/kg over 10 min (often omitted); Drip: 0.125-0.75 mcg/kg/min | Inodilator (PDE-3 inhibitor); increases CO, decreases SVR and PVR; useful in RV failure and pulmonary HTN; renally cleared -- reduce dose in AKI |
| Phenylephrine | IV only | 0.5-5 mcg/kg/min (bolus: 50-200 mcg IV) | Pure alpha-1; raises SVR without increasing HR; can cause reflex bradycardia; useful in SVT with hypotension |
| Dopamine | IV only | Low: 1-5 mcg/kg/min; Mid: 5-10; High: 10-20 | Dose-dependent effects; associated with more arrhythmias than norepinephrine; largely fallen out of favor for shock |
sections.adverseEffects:
### Common
- Tachycardia (beta-1 agents: dobutamine, epinephrine, dopamine)
- Hypertension (alpha agents at high doses)
- Arrhythmias (tachyarrhythmias, especially dopamine and epinephrine)
- Peripheral vasoconstriction / digital ischemia (norepinephrine, phenylephrine, high-dose vasopressin)
- Hyperglycemia (epinephrine)

### Serious/Black Box
- **Extravasation injury:** Severe tissue necrosis with all vasopressors if peripheral IV infiltrates; treat with phentolamine (5-10 mg in 10 mL NS injected into affected area)
- Limb ischemia, mesenteric ischemia (prolonged high-dose vasopressors)
- Tachyarrhythmias, myocardial ischemia (increased myocardial oxygen demand)
- Milrinone: Hypotension (vasodilation), especially with loading dose; thrombocytopenia (rare)
sections.contraindications:
- Vasopressors via peripheral IV: Acceptable only temporarily in emergencies; central line required ASAP
- Dobutamine: Idiopathic hypertrophic subaortic stenosis (IHSS); not effective without adequate volume resuscitation
- Milrinone: Severe aortic/pulmonic stenosis
- Relative: Tachyarrhythmias for beta-agonists
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitoring:**
  - Continuous telemetry and arterial line (A-line) for MAP monitoring (goal MAP >= 65 mmHg in sepsis)
  - Assess perfusion: Urine output (>= 0.5 mL/kg/hr), lactate trends, capillary refill, mental status
  - Check IV site every 1-2 hours for signs of extravasation (swelling, blanching, pain)
  - Strict I&Os; CVP, ScvO2 if available
  - Blood glucose q1-2h on epinephrine (hyperglycemia)
  - BMP for electrolytes; lactate q2-4h
- **Patient Teaching:** (primarily family/surrogate education in ICU setting)
  - Explain purpose of medications and monitoring equipment
  - Vasopressors support blood pressure while treating underlying cause
- **Administration Notes:**
  - **Central line strongly preferred** for all vasopressors (especially norepinephrine, phenylephrine, dopamine)
  - If peripheral IV must be used emergently: Use large-bore IV in antecubital fossa or above; limit to < 12 hours; monitor site closely
  - Use dedicated lumen for vasopressors; do NOT infuse with other medications on same line
  - Use infusion pump with standard concentrations per institutional protocol
  - Titrate per MAP goal; wean gradually (do not abruptly stop)
  - Vasopressin: Fixed dose (do NOT titrate); add to norepinephrine (do not replace)
sections.key-conditions:
- Sepsis — norepinephrine first-line for septic shock; vasopressin second-line; epinephrine third-line
- Distributive Shock — vasopressors restore SVR in all distributive shock subtypes
- Cardiogenic Shock — dobutamine/milrinone for inotropy; norepinephrine for MAP support
- Hypovolemic Shock — vasopressors as bridge while volume resuscitating (not a substitute for fluids)
- Obstructive Shock — treat underlying cause; vasopressors for hemodynamic support
- Cardiac Arrest — epinephrine 1 mg IV q3-5 min per ACLS
- Anaphylaxis — epinephrine IM first-line; IV drip for refractory cases
- Acute Heart Failure — dobutamine or milrinone for acute decompensation with low output

---

### MHS Vasopressor and Antihypertensive Drip Reference `#pam-training`

> **Source:** MHS Levophed Drip Guide + Antihypertensive Med Infusions reference via Clinical Educator (Pam)

#### Norepinephrine (Levophed) — MHS Titration Protocol

| Concentration | Standard Bag |
|--------------|-------------|
| **8 mg in 250 mL NS** | = 32 mcg/mL |

| MAP Goal | Starting Rate | Titration |
|---------|--------------|-----------|
| MAP ≥65 mmHg | 4 mcg/min (7.5 mL/hr) | Titrate by 2 mcg/min (3.75 mL/hr) q5 min |
| Max recommended | 40 mcg/min | Notify provider; consider adding vasopressin |

**Nursing reminders:**
- Continuous arterial line (A-line) preferred for MAP monitoring; if no A-line, use NIBP q5 min during titration
- Central line required for sustained infusion; peripheral OK as a bridge (<4 hr, proximal site, monitor site q1h)
- Document MAP and rate at every titration change in MAR BP field
- Wean by 2 mcg/min q15–30 min once MAP consistently ≥65 without fluid boluses

#### Antihypertensive Infusions — MHS Quick Reference

| Drug | Indication | Standard Concentration | Starting Rate | Titration | Max | Key Notes |
|------|-----------|----------------------|--------------|-----------|-----|-----------|
| **Nicardipine** | Hypertensive urgency/emergency, post-stroke BP control, SAH | 20 mg/200 mL NS (0.1 mg/mL) | 5 mg/hr | ↑ 2.5 mg/hr q5 min | 15 mg/hr | CCB; does not affect HR; preferred for stroke/neuro patients. Peripheral OK |
| **Labetalol** | Hypertensive emergency, stroke (ICH), eclampsia | Intermittent bolus: 20 mg IV over 2 min; or drip 2 mg/min | Bolus first; drip if needed | Repeat bolus q10 min (max 300 mg total); drip: titrate 1–2 mg/min | 300 mg total | Alpha + beta blocker; reduces HR and BP; avoid in asthma, bradycardia, decompensated CHF |
| **Clevidipine** | Perioperative hypertension, acute HTN when precise control needed | 0.5 mg/mL (premixed) | 1–2 mg/hr | Double dose q90 sec until approaching goal, then titrate by 1–2 mg/hr | 32 mg/hr (most patients 4–6 mg/hr) | Dihydropyridine CCB; ultrashort-acting (t½ 1 min); lipid-based — contraindicated in egg/soy allergy |
| **Esmolol** | Acute rate/BP control, aortic dissection, thyroid storm | 10 mg/mL (premixed) | 500 mcg/kg bolus over 1 min, then 50 mcg/kg/min | ↑ 50 mcg/kg/min q4 min | 300 mcg/kg/min | Cardioselective beta-1 blocker; ultrashort t½ (9 min); avoid in asthma, bradycardia, decompensated CHF |
| **Nitroprusside** | Hypertensive emergency, acute heart failure with high SVR | 50 mg/250 mL D5W (200 mcg/mL) | 0.3 mcg/kg/min | ↑ 0.5 mcg/kg/min q5 min | 10 mcg/kg/min | Wrap bag in foil (light-sensitive); cyanide toxicity risk >3 mcg/kg/min or >48 hr — monitor with thiocyanate levels; use with caution in renal failure |

**All antihypertensive drips:** Document BP before each rate change and after each titration per MAR BP field (MHS updated requirement). Goal is SBP reduction of 10–15% over first hour (avoid precipitous drops → cerebral ischemia).

---

clinicalPearls: **[[Sepsis|Septic shock]] protocol:** Norepinephrine first-line --> add vasopressin (catecholamine-sparing) if MAP still < 65 --> add epinephrine third-line; consider stress-dose [[Systemic Corticosteroids|hydrocortisone]] if refractory
**Cardiogenic shock:** Norepinephrine for BP support + dobutamine for inotropy; milrinone if beta-blocker on board (works independently of beta receptors)
**Wean vasopressors in reverse order** of addition; wean vasopressin LAST (rebound hypotension common)
Dopamine's "renal dose" (low dose) has been debunked -- no evidence of renal protection
**Extravasation antidote:** Phentolamine 5-10 mg diluted in 10-15 mL NS, injected subcutaneously around the site within 12 hours
Milrinone has a 2-3 hour half-life (much longer than dobutamine); slow to titrate and slow to wear off; extend monitoring after stopping
Tachycardia from dobutamine limits its use in patients with active ischemia

abdominal-aortic-aneurysm.title: Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm

sections.definition: A permanent, irreversible localized dilatation of the infrarenal aorta involving all three vessel wall layers (intima, media, adventitia), defined as an aortic diameter >=30 mm or infrarenal-to-suprarenal diameter ratio >=1.5. Classified as small (<55 mm, surveillance) or large (>=55 mm, consider repair).
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** 1-2% in 65-year-old men; 0.5% in 70-year-old women; declining in developed countries over past two decades
- **Risk factors:**
  - **Strongest:** Smoking (most important modifiable risk factor), male sex, positive family history
  - **Other:** Age >65, coronary heart disease, hyperlipidaemia, Hypertension, COPD, white ethnicity
  - **Protective:** Diabetes (lower prevalence, slower growth -- possibly related to metformin or glycation crosslinking of ECM)
  - **Genetic:** 10-20% have at least one affected relative; heritability estimated at 70-80%; 9 genome-wide significant loci identified (including LDLR, IL6R, MMP9, ERG)
  - **Connective tissue disorders:** Ehlers-Danlos syndrome type IV, Marfan Syndrome, Loeys-Dietz syndrome
- **Demographics:** Men affected 4-6x more than women; 12th-15th leading cause of death in persons >55 years in US/UK; 50% of ruptured AAA patients die before hospital admission
- **Mortality:** Ruptured AAA mortality is 65-85%; SMR 1.71 for treated AAA patients (excess cardiovascular death)
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — AAA is driven by chronic transmural inflammation: macrophage infiltration → MMP secretion → degradation of elastin and collagen → progressive wall weakening. Hemodynamics — as aortic diameter increases, wall tension rises (Laplace: T = P × r); larger aneurysms generate progressively greater wall stress with each heartbeat, accelerating expansion.*

AAA results from progressive degenerative changes in the aortic wall involving multiple interconnected mechanisms:

1. **ECM Degradation:** Proteolytic fragmentation of elastin (promotes dilatation) and collagen (promotes rupture) by matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9) and serine proteases (plasmin, leukocyte elastase)
2. **Intraluminal Thrombus (ILT):** Present in most AAAs; highly proteolytic and oxidative environment that recruits neutrophils, platelets, and RBCs; enzymes are outwardly convected through the wall, degrading ECM
3. **VSMC Loss:** Smooth muscle cell death from proteolytic detachment (plasmin, elastase) and oxidative stress (ceroids, ROS); thinning of the media
4. **Inflammation:** Innate immunity (neutrophil infiltration of ILT, mast cells in adventitia) and adaptive immunity (tertiary lymphoid organs in adventitia producing antibodies against neo-antigens)
5. **Oxidative Stress:** Sources include PMN leukocyte oxidases (NADPH, myeloperoxidase) and redox-active ferrous iron from trapped RBCs; smoking increases oxidative stress
6. **Hemodynamics:** Pressure-reflective waves at iliac bifurcation promote outward radial transport of plasma molecules through the wall; ILT outward convection contributes to progressive dilatation

**Growth rate:** Mean 2.5 mm/year for AAAs 39-49 mm; 20-25% faster in current smokers; 25% slower in diabetics
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Intact AAA (most common presentation):**
- Usually **asymptomatic** -- most diagnosed incidentally on imaging or via screening
- Occasionally: pulsatile abdominal mass on palpation (large AAAs)
- Rarely: compression of adjacent organs

**Ruptured AAA (surgical emergency):**
- **Classic triad** (present in only 25-50% of cases):
  1. Sudden-onset abdominal or back pain
  2. Hypotension/hemorrhagic shock
  3. Pulsatile abdominal mass
- Severity of shock depends on location/extent of rupture (intraperitoneal vs retroperitoneal)
- Contained rupture: pain without hemodynamic instability (rare)

### Complications
- **Rupture** -- near 100% mortality if untreated
- Distal embolization from ILT
- Consumptive coagulopathy (decreased platelets, fibrinogen; increased D-dimers)
- Aortoenteric fistula (rare, associated with mycotic AAA)
- Consumptive anaemia from RBC trapping in ILT

**Annual rupture risk by diameter:**
| Diameter | Risk per 100 person-years |
|----------|--------------------------|
| <40 mm | Negligible |
| 40-54 mm | 0.7-1.0 |
| 55-70 mm | ~5.3 |
| >70 mm | ~6.3 |
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **Abdominal ultrasound:** Gold standard for screening and surveillance; nearly 100% diagnostic accuracy; non-invasive, low-cost
- **CT angiography:** Standard of reference for AAA; evaluates morphology for surgical planning; ~85% specificity for rupture detection; <0.5-1 mm slice thickness preferred
- **MRI:** Adjunct imaging; useful with contraindications to iodinated contrast (renal insufficiency, allergy); cannot be used in emergencies
- **PET (18F-FDG):** Functional imaging -- uptake associated with inflammation, proteolytic activity; may predict rupture site
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Computational model from CT; estimates wall stress; may better predict rupture than diameter alone
- **Labs:** D-dimers (fibrinolysis marker), plasma MMP-9, CBC (possible consumptive anaemia)

### Diagnostic Criteria
- Infrarenal aortic diameter >=30 mm, OR
- Infrarenal-to-suprarenal diameter ratio >=1.5
- Note: women have smaller baseline aortic diameters (normal infrarenal: 1.80 +/- 0.24 cm men, 1.55 +/- 0.19 cm women)

### Differential Diagnosis
- Acute Myocardial Infarction
- Renal colic / kidney stone
- Back pain (musculoskeletal)
- Perforated peptic ulcer
- Other GI emergencies
sections.management:
### Screening
- **Recommended:** One-time abdominal ultrasound for all men at age 65
- Participation rate 75-85% in UK/Sweden programs
- Associated with reduction in AAA-specific mortality (4.0%/year decline)
- No current evidence for population-based screening in women

### Surveillance Protocol
| Diameter | Men | Women |
|----------|-----|-------|
| 2.5-3.0 cm | Consider follow-up | Consider follow-up |
| 3.0-3.9 cm | Every 2-3 years | Every 2-3 years |
| 4.0-4.4 cm | Every 6-12 months | Every 6-12 months |
| 4.5-4.9 cm | Every 6 months | Every 3 months |
| 5.0-5.4 cm | Every 3-6 months | **Consider surgery** |
| >=5.5 cm | **Consider surgery** | **Consider surgery** |

*Consider earlier intervention for: rapid growth (>10 mm/year), saccular AAA, mycotic AAA, inflammatory AAA, family history of rupture*

### Acute (Ruptured AAA)
- **Surgical emergency** -- immediate repair required
- Options: EVAR or open repair surgery
- Hemodynamic stabilization, permissive hypotension
- EVAR shows lower 3-year mortality vs open repair for ruptured AAA (42% vs 54%)

### Elective Repair
**Open Repair Surgery:**
- Replacement of aneurysmal segment with synthetic vascular graft via laparotomy
- Infrarenal aortic cross-clamping (suprarenal if AAA extends above renals -- higher renal risk)
- Better long-term durability; reduced need for surveillance/reintervention
- 30-day mortality: ~2.8% men, ~5.4% women
- Indicated when: anatomy unsuitable for EVAR, young/healthy patients, failed EVAR, mycotic AAA

**Endovascular Aortic Repair (EVAR):**
- Bifurcated stent graft placed via femoral/iliac arteries
- Requires adequate proximal and distal sealing zones
- 30-day mortality: ~1.4% men, ~2.3% women
- Lower perioperative morbidity; shorter hospital stay (6 vs 13 days)
- Early survival advantage not sustained long-term (15-year data: open repair has better survival)
- Requires lifelong surveillance for endoleaks
- Advanced techniques: fenestrated grafts, chimney technique for juxtarenal AAAs

**Key EVAR Complications:**
- Endoleak Types I-IV (20-50% overall)
- Stent graft migration (~8%)
- Graft limb occlusion (1.7-3.7%)
- Sac expansion and secondary rupture (up to 41% at 5 years)
- Stent graft infection (<1%)

### Pharmacology
- **No proven pharmacotherapy to reduce AAA growth in humans**
- Failed trials: Beta-Blockers (propranolol -- not tolerated), doxycycline (MMP inhibitor -- no effect), pemirolast (mast cell inhibitor), perindopril (ACE Inhibitors)
- Statins: Small observational studies suggest decreased growth, not confirmed in larger trials
- Metformin: Under investigation; may slow growth in diabetic patients
- Cardiovascular risk factor management: antihypertensives, lipid-lowering therapy, smoking cessation
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Pre-operative assessment:**
  - Baseline vital signs, peripheral pulses (bilateral pedal, femoral)
  - Cardiac and pulmonary risk assessment (high prevalence of comorbid CAD, COPD)
  - Renal function baseline (Cr, BUN) -- especially for contrast exposure and cross-clamping
  - Coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer)
- **Post-operative monitoring (EVAR and Open):**
  - Frequent neurovascular checks of lower extremities (pulses, sensation, color, temperature)
  - Hemodynamic monitoring -- watch for hypotension, tachycardia (hemorrhage)
  - Urine output (risk of renal ischemia, especially with suprarenal clamping)
  - Abdominal girth measurements
  - Monitor for signs of bowel ischemia (abdominal pain, bloody stool, lactic acidosis)
  - Incision site/groin access site assessment (hematoma, pseudoaneurysm)
  - Spinal cord ischemia assessment (0.21-0.9% incidence -- check lower extremity motor/sensory function)
- **Patient education:**
  - Smoking cessation (most critical modifiable risk factor)
  - Surveillance imaging schedule adherence (especially post-EVAR)
  - Signs of rupture: sudden severe abdominal/back pain, dizziness, syncope -- call 911 immediately
  - Activity restrictions post-surgery
  - Cardiovascular risk factor modification (BP control, lipids, diet)
sections.keyPoints:
- AAA is usually asymptomatic and diagnosed incidentally or via screening; a one-time ultrasound for men at age 65 is recommended
- Smoking is the most important modifiable risk factor; diabetes is paradoxically protective
- Rupture mortality is 65-85%; risk increases with diameter but can occur at any size (women at higher risk with smaller AAAs)
- Elective repair threshold is >=55 mm for men (>=50 mm may be considered for women due to smaller baseline aorta)
- EVAR has lower early mortality but inferior long-term durability compared to open repair; both require post-operative surveillance
- No pharmacotherapy has been proven to reduce AAA growth in humans

clinicalPearls: *Add from daily practice*

signs: Usually **asymptomatic** -- most diagnosed incidentally on imaging or via screening
Occasionally: pulsatile abdominal mass on palpation (large AAAs)
Rarely: compression of adjacent organs
**Classic triad** (present in only 25-50% of cases):
Severity of shock depends on location/extent of rupture (intraperitoneal vs retroperitoneal)
Contained rupture: pain without hemodynamic instability (rare)
**Rupture** -- near 100% mortality if untreated
Distal embolization from ILT
Consumptive coagulopathy (decreased platelets, fibrinogen; increased D-dimers)
Aortoenteric fistula (rare, associated with mycotic AAA)
Consumptive anaemia from RBC trapping in ILT

acute-heart-failure.title: Acute Heart Failure

sections.definition: Acute heart failure is the rapid onset or worsening of HF signs and symptoms requiring urgent evaluation and treatment. May be new-onset (de novo) or, more commonly, acute decompensation of chronic HF (ADHF). Presentations range from gradual worsening congestion to cardiogenic shock to flash pulmonary edema. It is the most common cause of hospitalization in adults > 65 and carries 30-day readmission rates of ~25% and 1-year mortality of ~20-30%.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~1 million HF hospitalizations/year in the US; ~80% are acute-on-chronic decompensations; 30-day readmission rate ~25% (CMS quality metric)
- **Risk factors/Precipitants:**
  - **Dietary/medication non-adherence** (most common — excess salt/fluid, missed diuretics or GDMT)
  - Acute coronary syndrome / MI
  - Uncontrolled Hypertension (hypertensive emergency → flash pulmonary edema)
  - New Atrial Fibrillation or uncontrolled tachyarrhythmia
  - Infection (pneumonia, UTI, sepsis)
  - Renal failure / volume overload
  - Anemia
  - Pulmonary embolism
  - Medication effects (NSAIDs, CCBs [non-DHP], thiazolidinediones)
  - Thyroid disease (hyper or hypo)
  - Pregnancy
  - Alcohol binge, cocaine use
