**Hepatorenal syndrome** (most common cause of kidney failure)
**Acute respiratory distress syndrome (ARDS)**
**Cerebral edema** (less common than in acute liver failure but possible in grade IV HE)
**Variceal hemorrhage**
**Death:** 28-day mortality: ACLF-1 (23%), ACLF-2 (31%), ACLF-3 (74%)

alcohol-use-disorder.title: Alcohol Use Disorder

sections.definition:
Alcohol use disorder is a chronic, relapsing brain disease characterized by impaired control over alcohol use despite negative consequences. In the ED, AUD presents along a wide spectrum: acute intoxication, withdrawal (from mild tremors to life-threatening delirium tremens), medical complications of chronic use, trauma, and patients seeking help for their addiction.

Alcohol withdrawal is the clinical emergency. Unlike opioid withdrawal (which is miserable but rarely fatal), **alcohol withdrawal can kill** — seizures and delirium tremens carry significant mortality if untreated.

**Related notes:** Delirium · Sedatives and Anxiolytics · Acute Psychosis · Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm · Opioid Use Disorder

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **AUD prevalence:** ~14.5 million US adults (5.6%) meet criteria; only ~7% receive treatment
- **Alcohol-related ED visits increased 61% from 2006-2014** — outpacing overall ED visit growth
- **Alcohol withdrawal:** ~50% of people with AUD will develop some withdrawal symptoms upon cessation; ~5% progress to severe withdrawal (DTs)
- **Delirium tremens mortality:** 1-4% with treatment; up to 35% untreated
- **Alcohol-related deaths:** ~95,000/year in the US (3rd leading preventable cause of death after tobacco and diet/physical inactivity)
- **Co-occurring disorders:** Depression (30-40%), anxiety (20-40%), other substance use disorders (high), PTSD
- **ED social work context:** Per the MultiCare SW presentation — patients present voluntarily seeking treatment, but publicly funded detox beds are extremely limited (10 male, 6 female beds for all of Pierce County at Tacoma Detox, full 9/10 times). Benzodiazepine use is a rule-out for admission to most detox facilities

---
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — alcohol enhances GABA-A (inhibitory) and blocks NMDA (excitatory) receptors via allosteric modulation; chronic exposure drives compensatory receptor downregulation (GABA-A) and upregulation (NMDA) — the brain recalibrates to function with alcohol present; this is the receptor biology of tolerance and physical dependence*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — neuroadaptation IS homeostasis: the brain uses negative feedback to maintain normal excitatory/inhibitory balance despite constant alcohol-induced CNS depression; when alcohol is abruptly removed, the adapted system is suddenly unbalanced — unopposed excitation = withdrawal syndrome; this is a positive feedback emergency loop*

### Normal Alcohol Effects

Alcohol is a central nervous system depressant that works primarily through two mechanisms:
1. **Enhances GABA-A receptor activity** — GABA is the brain's primary inhibitory neurotransmitter. Alcohol potentiates GABA → sedation, anxiolysis, muscle relaxation
2. **Inhibits NMDA glutamate receptor activity** — Glutamate is the brain's primary excitatory neurotransmitter. Alcohol blocks excitation → further CNS depression

### Neuroadaptation in Chronic Use

With chronic alcohol use, the brain adapts to maintain homeostasis (see Homeostasis and Feedback Loops):
- **GABA-A receptors downregulate** (fewer receptors, less sensitive) — the brain needs more alcohol to achieve the same inhibitory effect (tolerance)
- **NMDA glutamate receptors upregulate** (more receptors, more sensitive) — the brain increases excitatory tone to compensate for chronic suppression

The brain has now recalibrated to function WITH alcohol present.

### Withdrawal: The Unmasking

When alcohol is suddenly removed:
- **Reduced GABA inhibition** (downregulated receptors without their usual agonist) → loss of inhibitory brake
- **Unopposed glutamate excitation** (upregulated receptors now fully active without alcohol blocking them) → excitotoxicity

**Result:** Sympathetic nervous system hyperactivation, neuronal hyperexcitability, seizure risk, autonomic instability → the withdrawal syndrome

This is the SAME mechanism as benzodiazepine withdrawal (both work on GABA) — which is why benzos are the treatment for alcohol withdrawal.

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Alcohol Withdrawal Timeline

| Phase | Onset After Last Drink | Symptoms | Severity |
|-------|----------------------|----------|----------|
| **Minor withdrawal** | 6-12 hours | Tremor (especially hands), anxiety, insomnia, nausea/vomiting, diaphoresis, headache, tachycardia | Mild-Moderate |
| **Alcoholic hallucinosis** | 12-24 hours | Visual, auditory, or tactile hallucinations WITH preserved sensorium (patient knows hallucinations aren't real — unlike DTs). Classically: "bugs crawling" on skin | Moderate |
| **Withdrawal seizures** | 12-48 hours (peak at 24 hr) | Generalized tonic-clonic seizures, often brief and self-limited. May cluster (multiple seizures). Risk of status epilepticus | Severe |
| **Delirium tremens (DTs)** | 48-96 hours (can occur up to 7 days) | Profound confusion, agitation, fever, tachycardia, hypertension, diaphoresis, vivid hallucinations, fluctuating consciousness, tremor | Life-threatening |

> **Critical concept:** These phases CAN overlap and progression is NOT guaranteed. Many patients have mild withdrawal that resolves. Some skip straight to seizures. DTs represent the most severe end of the spectrum and occur in only ~5% of patients with withdrawal — but those 5% can die.

### CIWA-Ar Scale (Clinical Institute Withdrawal Assessment for Alcohol — Revised)

The standard tool for monitoring withdrawal severity. Scored 0-67:

| Domain | What You Assess | Score Range |
|--------|----------------|-------------|
| Nausea/vomiting | Severity | 0-7 |
| Tremor | Arms extended, fingers spread | 0-7 |
| Paroxysmal sweats | Observation | 0-7 |
| Anxiety | Patient report + observation | 0-7 |
| Agitation | Observation | 0-7 |
| Tactile disturbances | Itching, pins/needles, bugs, numbness | 0-7 |
| Auditory disturbances | Harshness of sounds, hallucinations | 0-7 |
| Visual disturbances | Light sensitivity, hallucinations | 0-7 |
| Headache | Severity | 0-7 |
| Orientation/clouding | Person, place, time, date | 0-4 |

**Scoring interpretation:**
- **<10:** Mild withdrawal — may not need medication
- **10-18:** Moderate withdrawal — typically requires pharmacotherapy
- **19-30:** Severe withdrawal — aggressive treatment needed
- **>30:** Very severe — ICU-level care, high risk for DTs/seizures

### Delirium Tremens — The Life Threat

DTs are a medical emergency, not a behavioral problem:

**Features:**
- **Profound delirium** — disorientation, confusion, inability to recognize familiar people
- **Vivid hallucinations** — typically visual (classically "pink elephants" but really any formed visual hallucination), also tactile and auditory
- **Severe autonomic instability** — tachycardia (HR >120), hypertension, fever (>38°C), profuse diaphoresis
- **Extreme agitation** — thrashing, combative, attempting to get out of bed
- **Tremor** — coarse, whole-body
- **Seizures** may occur during or preceding DTs

**Risk factors for DTs:**
- Prior history of DTs or withdrawal seizures (strongest predictor)
- Prolonged heavy drinking
- Prior detoxification episodes (kindling — each withdrawal is worse than the last)
- Concurrent medical illness (infection, trauma, surgery)
- Age >40
- Elevated BAL at presentation with withdrawal symptoms (suggests very high baseline consumption)

---
sections.ed-management:
### Acute Intoxication

- **Most intoxicated patients need observation, not treatment** — time is the treatment
- **ABCs** — protect airway (aspiration risk), monitor breathing
- **Fingerstick glucose** — hypoglycemia from alcohol-induced hepatic gluconeogenesis impairment
- **Thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE glucose** — if giving dextrose to an alcoholic patient, thiamine first to prevent precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy
- **Assess for trauma** — intoxicated patients fall, fight, and get hit. Low threshold for CT head, cervical spine imaging
- **Monitor for transition to withdrawal** — as BAL drops, withdrawal may begin while the patient is still in the ED
- **Do not discharge intoxicated patients** — they lack capacity and are fall/aspiration/exposure risks

### Alcohol Withdrawal — Pharmacotherapy

#### Benzodiazepines (First-Line)

**Symptom-triggered therapy** (using CIWA) is preferred over fixed-schedule dosing — results in less medication, shorter treatment, and fewer complications:

| Medication | Route | Dose | Onset | Duration | Best For |
|------------|-------|------|-------|----------|----------|
| **Diazepam** | IV/PO | 5-20 mg | 2-5 min IV, 15-30 min PO | Long (active metabolite 40-100 hr) | Self-tapering due to long half-life. Preferred if liver function adequate |
| **Lorazepam** | IV/IM/PO | 1-4 mg | 5 min IV, 15-30 min IM | Intermediate (10-20 hr) | **Hepatic impairment** — no active metabolites, does not require hepatic oxidation (glucuronidation only). Also preferred in elderly |
| **Chlordiazepoxide** | PO | 25-100 mg | 30-60 min | Long | Outpatient detox or mild withdrawal. Long half-life provides smooth taper |
| **Midazolam** | IV | 2-5 mg | 1-2 min | Short (1.5-2.5 hr) | Severe DTs not responding to other benzos. Requires continuous monitoring |

**CIWA-based dosing protocol (typical):**
- CIWA <10: Reassess q4-8h, no medication
- CIWA 10-18: Lorazepam 1-2 mg or diazepam 5-10 mg q1h PRN. Reassess q1-2h
- CIWA 19-30: Lorazepam 2-4 mg or diazepam 10-20 mg q30-60min. Reassess q30min-1h
- CIWA >30 or not responding: Consider ICU, benzo infusion, or adjunct agents

#### Phenobarbital (Increasingly Used — ED-Friendly)

- **Single-dose phenobarbital protocol:** 10-15 mg/kg IV (typically 260-390 mg for adults) as a loading dose
- Long half-life (80-120 hours) → self-tapering, no repeated dosing needed
- Works on GABA-A (like benzos) but at a DIFFERENT binding site → can work even in benzo-tolerant patients
- **Advantages:** One dose in the ED, patient can be discharged or observed without repeated CIWA scoring
- **Risk:** Respiratory depression, especially combined with alcohol or benzos. Monitor closely for 2-4 hours
- Growing evidence supports phenobarbital as an alternative or adjunct to benzodiazepines, especially for patients with severe withdrawal or DTs refractory to benzos

#### Adjunctive Agents

| Agent | Role | Notes |
|-------|------|-------|
| **Thiamine** (B1) | Prevent/treat Wernicke encephalopathy | **100 mg IV x 3 days** (high-dose) for any patient with suspected malnutrition or chronic alcoholism. Give BEFORE glucose. Oral absorption is unreliable in alcoholics |
| **Folate** | Replace chronic deficiency | 1 mg PO/IV daily |
| **Magnesium** | Replace chronic deficiency (depleted by alcohol) | 2g IV if hypomagnesemic. Low Mg worsens seizure risk |
| **Multivitamin** | General nutritional repletion | Most alcoholic patients are malnourished |
| **Gabapentin** | Mild-moderate withdrawal, post-acute cravings | NOT adequate for severe withdrawal or seizure prevention. Useful for patients being discharged to reduce post-acute symptoms and cravings |
| **Haloperidol/Droperidol** | Agitation in DTs (adjunct to benzos) | Do NOT use antipsychotics alone — they lower seizure threshold. Always combine with adequate benzodiazepine |

### Wernicke Encephalopathy — Don't Miss This

**Classic triad** (only 10-20% have all three):
1. **Encephalopathy** (confusion, disorientation)
2. **Oculomotor dysfunction** (nystagmus, ophthalmoplegia, conjugate gaze palsy)
3. **Ataxia** (gait instability)

**Treatment:** High-dose IV thiamine (200-500 mg IV TID for 3-5 days). Oral thiamine is NOT adequate for treatment.

If untreated → progresses to **Korsakoff syndrome** (irreversible anterograde amnesia, confabulation) — permanent brain damage.

> **Give thiamine to every chronic alcoholic patient in the ED.** It is cheap, safe, and prevents a devastating, irreversible condition. There is no downside.

---
sections.disposition-and-treatment-resources:
### ED Disposition

| Scenario | Disposition |
|----------|------------|
| **DTs or seizures** | ICU admission |
| **Severe withdrawal (CIWA >20) not responding to initial treatment** | Admission (ICU or monitored bed) |
| **Moderate withdrawal responding to treatment** | Observation → possible discharge if symptoms controlled and safe environment |
| **Mild withdrawal** | Symptom-triggered treatment → discharge with follow-up if resolving |
| **Patient requesting addiction treatment** | SW referral for SUD assessment and placement |

### Treatment Landscape (Pierce County — Per MultiCare SW)

- **Tacoma Detox:** 10 male beds, 6 female beds for the entire county. Full 90% of the time. Must have Medicaid AND live in Pierce County
- **Fairfax / Cascade:** Accepting Medicaid for detox only (no ongoing treatment after detox)
- **Inpatient SUD treatment:** Requires complete SUD assessment by qualified CDP through a local agency → 4-6 week wait for a bed
- **Benzos are a rule-out at most detox facilities** — patients on benzodiazepines cannot be admitted to detox (too medically complex for their license level)
- **Barriers:** Co-occurring psychiatric illness, medical complexity (oxygen, DME), active suicidality
- **Reality:** Many patients will be discharged back to the same environment that drove them to drink. Connect with SW, provide crisis numbers, and document the attempt to connect to treatment

### Medications for AUD (Outpatient — Know They Exist)

| Medication | Mechanism | Notes |
|------------|-----------|-------|
| **Naltrexone** | Opioid receptor antagonist — reduces the rewarding effects of alcohol | PO daily or IM monthly (Vivitrol). Cannot start until opioid-free. Reduces heavy drinking days |
| **Acamprosate** | NMDA antagonist / GABA agonist — reduces cravings | Renally dosed. Good for maintaining abstinence |
| **Disulfiram** (Antabuse) | Inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase → violently sick if patient drinks | Aversive conditioning. Patient must be highly motivated. Risk of hepatotoxicity |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **CIWA scoring is only reliable in an awake, communicative patient.** If the patient is obtunded, seizing, or in DTs, they are beyond CIWA — treat aggressively and don't waste time trying to score a scale
- **Thiamine before glucose — always.** Giving glucose to a thiamine-depleted patient can precipitate acute Wernicke encephalopathy by consuming the last remaining thiamine stores. This is one of the few order-of-operations rules that can cause immediate, permanent harm
- **Monitor for aspiration.** The intoxicated patient who is vomiting is at high risk. Position on their side (recovery position) if obtunded. Suction at bedside. Do NOT leave flat and supine
- **Withdrawal can begin while the patient is still intoxicated.** A patient with a BAL of 300 who normally drinks to a BAL of 400 is already in relative withdrawal. Don't wait for BAL to reach zero before starting CIWA scoring and treatment
- **Kindling is real.** Each withdrawal episode is physiologically worse than the last due to neuronal sensitization. A patient on their fifth detox is at much higher risk for seizures and DTs than a first-time withdrawal — even if the drinking pattern is similar
- **The patient requesting help is at a moment of readiness — don't waste it.** If someone in the throes of withdrawal asks "can you help me get into treatment?" — activate SW immediately. Motivation for treatment is fragile and time-limited. Getting an assessment started while they're motivated can change outcomes
- **Don't use "just an alcoholic" language.** AUD is a chronic brain disease with the same neurobiological basis as other chronic diseases. The stigma attached to alcohol addiction is one of the largest barriers to treatment. Your language matters — to the patient and to the colleagues listening

---
sections.references:
- Wolf C, et al: Management of Alcohol Withdrawal in the Emergency Department: Current Perspectives. Open Access Emergency Medicine 2020 (OHSU NRS313 course material)
- ASAM Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management (2020)
- Long D, et al: Phenobarbital vs Benzodiazepines for Alcohol Withdrawal. Annals of Emergency Medicine 2023
- MultiCare Social Work in the ED — Lindsay Franklin, LICSW (residency presentation)

---

*See also: Delirium · Sedatives and Anxiolytics · Opioid Use Disorder · Stimulant Intoxication and Use Disorder · Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm · Acute Psychosis · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops*

clinicalPearls: **Alcohol withdrawal is one of the few substance withdrawal syndromes that can be directly fatal.** Opioid withdrawal is miserable but almost never lethal. Alcohol (and benzodiazepine) withdrawal can cause seizures, DTs, autonomic collapse, and death. Treat it with the urgency it deserves.
**If in doubt, treat.** Undertreating alcohol withdrawal causes seizures, DTs, and death. Overtreating causes sedation (which you can manage). The risk-benefit overwhelmingly favors treatment.

**Phenobarbital is making a comeback in the ED.** A single loading dose of phenobarbital (10-15 mg/kg) has a half-life of 80-120 hours and effectively self-tapers. It eliminates the need for repeated CIWA scoring and benzo dosing. Multiple ED studies show equivalent or superior outcomes to traditional benzo protocols, with a more streamlined workflow.

**The "frequent flyer" alcoholic is a human being in crisis, not a problem to manage.** These patients die 15-20 years earlier than the general population from cardiovascular disease, liver failure, cancer, suicide, and trauma. Every ED visit is a potential intervention point. Brief motivational interviewing, SW referral, and simple human dignity make a difference — even if you don't see the result.
### MHS Alcohol Withdrawal Order Set (AWS) `#pam-training`
**Source:** MultiCare Health System AWS Order Set via Clinical Educator (Pam)
**CIWA-Ar scoring:** q1 hour while symptomatic; notify provider if CIWA ≥20 or if patient seizes
Vitals with each CIWA assessment
Continuous pulse oximetry for severe withdrawal
#### Standard MHS Medication Protocol

| CIWA Score | Medication | Dose/Route | Frequency | |-----------|-----------|-----------|-----------| | **8–15** (mild-moderate) | Diazepam | 5 mg IV | q1h PRN | | **>15** (severe) | Diazepam | 10 mg IV | q1h PRN; notify provider | | **Seizure** | Lorazepam | 4 mg IV | Once; call provider immediately |
#### Standing Orders (All AWS Patients)
**Thiamine** 100 mg IV × 3 days (give BEFORE any dextrose)
**Folic acid** 1 mg IV/PO daily
**Multivitamin** PO daily
**Magnesium sulfate** 2 g IV if Mg <1.8 or hypokalemia present
**NS IV** at maintenance rate for NPO or dehydrated patients
#### Phenobarbital Protocol (Alternative/Adjunct)
**Phenobarbital loading dose:** 260 mg IV (≈10 mg/kg for 60 kg patient) — single dose
Indicated for: CIWA >20 not responding to benzos, prior DTs or withdrawal seizures, benzo-tolerant patients
Monitor for respiratory depression for 2–4 hours post-load
Long half-life (80–120 hr) → self-tapering, no repeated dosing required
Seizure precautions in place for all CIWA ≥10
Fall precautions active
Quiet, low-stimulation environment when possible
Oral care and skin assessment q shift (diaphoresis, incontinence risk)
Document CIWA score, medication given, and patient response each assessment

signs: **<10:** Mild withdrawal — may not need medication
**10-18:** Moderate withdrawal — typically requires pharmacotherapy
**19-30:** Severe withdrawal — aggressive treatment needed
**>30:** Very severe — ICU-level care, high risk for DTs/seizures
**Profound delirium** — disorientation, confusion, inability to recognize familiar people
**Vivid hallucinations** — typically visual (classically "pink elephants" but really any formed visual hallucination), also tactile and auditory
**Severe autonomic instability** — tachycardia (HR >120), hypertension, fever (>38°C), profuse diaphoresis
**Extreme agitation** — thrashing, combative, attempting to get out of bed
**Tremor** — coarse, whole-body
**Seizures** may occur during or preceding DTs
Prior history of DTs or withdrawal seizures (strongest predictor)
Prolonged heavy drinking
Prior detoxification episodes (kindling — each withdrawal is worse than the last)
Concurrent medical illness (infection, trauma, surgery)
Age >40
Elevated BAL at presentation with withdrawal symptoms (suggests very high baseline consumption)

alcoholic-liver-disease.title: Alcoholic Liver Disease

sections.definition: Alcoholic liver disease (ALD) encompasses the spectrum of liver injury caused by chronic and/or heavy alcohol consumption, ranging from alcoholic fatty liver (steatosis) to alcoholic steatohepatitis to progressive fibrosis, Cirrhosis, and hepatocellular carcinoma. **Severe alcoholic hepatitis (AH)** is an acute-on-chronic presentation with liver failure, systemic inflammation, and high short-term mortality.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** Most prevalent type of chronic liver disease worldwide; affects approximately 90-100% of individuals consuming >40g alcohol daily (develops fatty liver), but only 10-35% progress to advanced fibrosis/cirrhosis
- **Risk factors:** Quantity and duration of alcohol consumption (>40g/day for women, >60g/day for men increases risk significantly), genetic susceptibility (PNPLA3, TM6SF2 polymorphisms), female sex (women develop ALD at lower alcohol doses and shorter duration), obesity, metabolic syndrome, concurrent viral hepatitis (HCV, HBV), malnutrition, pattern of drinking (binge drinking increases risk)
- **Demographics:** Male predominance historically, but gap narrowing due to increased female alcohol consumption; onset typically 40-60 years; racial/ethnic disparities (Native Americans, Hispanics have higher prevalence)

**Natural history:**
- Most individuals with chronic heavy alcohol use develop alcoholic fatty liver (AFL)
- 20-40% of AFL patients progress to alcoholic steatohepatitis (ASH)
- 10-20% of ASH patients develop cirrhosis over 10-20 years
- Cirrhotic patients have 3-10% annual risk of hepatocellular carcinoma
- Severe alcoholic hepatitis carries 30-50% 28-day mortality without treatment
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Nutrition and Metabolism — ethanol metabolism via ADH produces acetaldehyde (directly hepatotoxic); the NADH/NAD+ ratio shifts dramatically, blocking beta-oxidation and gluconeogenesis while promoting lipogenesis; CYP2E1 induction by chronic alcohol generates ROS and produces more acetaldehyde, compounding mitochondrial dysfunction*
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — acetaldehyde is a direct hepatotoxin that forms protein adducts and triggers immune responses; alcohol-induced gut dysbiosis and increased intestinal permeability allow LPS from gram-negative bacteria to flood the portal circulation, activating Kupffer cells (hepatic innate immune macrophages) → TNF-α surge → neutrophilic hepatitis characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis*

ALD results from **direct ethanol toxicity, metabolic derangements, oxidative stress, and inflammatory responses**:

**Ethanol metabolism:**
- Hepatic metabolism via alcohol dehydrogenase (ADH) and cytochrome P450 2E1 (CYP2E1) produces acetaldehyde (toxic metabolite)
- Acetaldehyde forms protein adducts, triggers inflammatory responses, impairs mitochondrial function, promotes collagen synthesis (fibrosis)
- NAD+/NADH ratio disruption impairs fatty acid oxidation and promotes lipogenesis

**Mechanisms of injury:**
1. **Hepatic steatosis:** Increased fatty acid synthesis, decreased fatty acid oxidation, impaired VLDL secretion → triglyceride accumulation in hepatocytes
2. **Oxidative stress:** CYP2E1 activity generates reactive oxygen species (ROS); depletes glutathione; causes lipid peroxidation and membrane damage
3. **Acetaldehyde toxicity:** Forms adducts with proteins (triggers immune responses); impairs microtubular function; promotes collagen synthesis
4. **Inflammation:** Gut-derived endotoxins (lipopolysaccharide) activate Kupffer cells → cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6); neutrophil infiltration characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis
5. **Gut-liver axis dysregulation:** Alcohol increases intestinal permeability ("leaky gut"), allowing bacterial translocation and endotoxemia
6. **Fibrosis:** Stellate cell activation by acetaldehyde, oxidative stress, and cytokines → collagen deposition → progressive fibrosis and cirrhosis

**Genetic factors:**
- **PNPLA3 I148M variant:** Strongest genetic risk factor; impairs fat mobilization from hepatocytes; increases risk of steatosis, fibrosis, cirrhosis, HCC
- **TM6SF2 variant:** Impairs VLDL secretion; increases steatosis and fibrosis risk
- Polymorphisms in ADH and ALDH (acetaldehyde dehydrogenase) affect alcohol metabolism rates
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Alcoholic fatty liver (AFL):**
- Usually asymptomatic
- Hepatomegaly (smooth, non-tender liver edge)
- Mild RUQ discomfort
- Normal or mildly elevated transaminases (AST/ALT ratio often >1)

**Alcoholic steatohepatitis / Chronic ALD:**
- Fatigue, malaise
- Anorexia, weight loss
- RUQ pain or discomfort
- Hepatomegaly
- Mild jaundice (late finding)
- Signs of chronic liver disease (spider angiomata, palmar erythema, gynecomastia, testicular atrophy)

**Severe alcoholic hepatitis (acute presentation):**
- Rapid onset jaundice (bilirubin often >5 mg/dL, may exceed 20 mg/dL)
- Fever (38-39°C)
- RUQ pain and tender hepatomegaly
- Anorexia, nausea, vomiting
- Ascites
- Hepatic Encephalopathy (in severe cases)
- Coagulopathy
- Systemic inflammatory response (tachycardia, leukocytosis)

**Cirrhosis (end-stage ALD):**
- Ascites, peripheral edema
- Variceal hemorrhage
- Hepatic Encephalopathy
- Jaundice
- Coagulopathy
- Sarcopenia and muscle wasting
- Signs of portal hypertension (splenomegaly, caput medusae)

### Complications

- **Acute-on-chronic liver failure:** See Acute-on-Chronic Liver Failure
- **Portal hypertension complications:** Variceal bleeding, ascites, Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome, hepatopulmonary syndrome
- **Hepatocellular carcinoma:** 3-10% annual incidence in cirrhotic ALD
- **Infections:** Increased susceptibility (pneumonia, SBP, UTI, skin/soft tissue infections)
- **Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, thiamine deficiency (Wernicke encephalopathy), folate deficiency, vitamin deficiencies
- **Extrahepatic complications:** Alcoholic cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis, neuropathy, myopathy
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies

**Laboratory findings:**
- **Transaminases:** AST/ALT ratio >2 highly suggestive of ALD (in contrast to most other liver diseases where ALT > AST); both typically <300 U/L (vs. >1000 in viral hepatitis or ischemic hepatitis)
- **GGT (gamma-glutamyl transferase):** Elevated; induced by alcohol; useful marker of ongoing alcohol consumption
- **Bilirubin:** Elevated in alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis; total bilirubin >12 mg/dL indicates severe disease
- **Alkaline phosphatase:** Mildly elevated (usually <3x ULN)
- **INR:** Elevated in severe alcoholic hepatitis and cirrhosis; reflects synthetic dysfunction
- **Albumin:** Decreased in advanced disease
- **CBC:** Macrocytic anemia (elevated MCV from folate deficiency and direct ethanol effect), thrombocytopenia (from portal hypertension and direct marrow suppression), leukocytosis (in alcoholic hepatitis)
- **Creatinine:** Elevated in hepatorenal syndrome or acute kidney injury

**Alcohol use markers:**
- **Ethanol level:** Detects recent use but not chronic consumption
- **Carbohydrate-deficient transferrin (CDT):** Sensitive marker of heavy alcohol consumption (>50-80g/day for ≥2 weeks)
- **Phosphatidylethanol (PEth):** Highly sensitive and specific biomarker; detects alcohol use for up to 3-4 weeks

**Imaging:**
- **Ultrasound:** Detects steatosis (increased echogenicity), cirrhosis (nodular contour, coarse echotexture), ascites; limited sensitivity for fibrosis
- **Transient elastography (FibroScan):** Non-invasive assessment of liver stiffness (correlates with fibrosis stage); LSM >12 kPa suggests cirrhosis
- **CT/MRI abdomen:** Assess for hepatic steatosis, cirrhosis, HCC, portal hypertension; MRI superior for detecting HCC

**Liver biopsy:**
- Gold standard for diagnosis and staging but not always necessary
- Histology: macrovesicular steatosis, hepatocyte ballooning, Mallory-Denk bodies, neutrophilic infiltration (characteristic of alcoholic hepatitis), perisinusoidal fibrosis (chicken-wire pattern), perivenular fibrosis
- Indicated when: diagnosis uncertain, evaluate for concurrent liver disease, assess disease severity for treatment decisions

### Diagnostic Criteria

**Clinical diagnosis** based on:
1. History of heavy alcohol consumption (>40g/day women, >60g/day men, for ≥10 years for cirrhosis; recent binge for acute alcoholic hepatitis)
2. Compatible clinical and laboratory findings (AST/ALT >2, elevated GGT, elevated bilirubin in AH)
3. Exclusion of other liver diseases (viral hepatitis, autoimmune hepatitis, hemochromatosis, Wilson disease, NAFLD)

**Severe alcoholic hepatitis criteria:**
- **Maddrey Discriminant Function (DF) ≥32:** DF = 4.6 × (PT patient - PT control) + bilirubin (mg/dL)
  - DF ≥32 indicates severe disease with high mortality; identifies patients who may benefit from corticosteroids
- **MELD score ≥20:** Alternative threshold for severe disease
sections.management:
### Acute

**Severe alcoholic hepatitis (Maddrey DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20):**

1. **Corticosteroids (prednisolone):**
   - **Prednisolone 40mg PO daily × 28 days** (oral preferred over IV methylprednisolone)
   - Assess response at **day 7** using **Lille score:** combines bilirubin change, albumin, creatinine, PT, age
     - Lille <0.45 = responder → complete 28-day course
     - Lille ≥0.45 = non-responder → stop steroids (no benefit, increased infection risk)
   - Reduces 28-day mortality from 35% to 20-25% in responders
   - **Contraindications:** Active GI bleeding, uncontrolled infection, renal failure, pancreatitis

2. **Pentoxifylline:**
   - **Pentoxifylline 400mg PO TID × 28 days**
   - Alternative to prednisolone if contraindications exist
   - Phosphodiesterase inhibitor; reduces TNF-α production
   - Benefit controversial; recent meta-analyses show modest or no survival benefit

3. **Supportive care:**
   - **Nutrition:** Aggressive nutritional support (enteral preferred); goal 35-40 kcal/kg/day, protein 1.5 g/kg/day; thiamine 100mg IV daily × 3-5 days, folate, multivitamins
   - **Infection surveillance:** High infection risk; low threshold for cultures and antibiotics
   - **Treat complications:** Ascites (diuretics, therapeutic paracentesis), Hepatic Encephalopathy (lactulose, rifaximin), coagulopathy (vitamin K, FFP/platelets only if bleeding)
   - **Alcohol withdrawal prophylaxis:** Benzodiazepines (lorazepam preferred due to lack of hepatic metabolism) using CIWA protocol

4. **Liver transplantation:**
   - Early liver transplant for highly selected patients with severe AH not responding to medical therapy
   - Requires careful patient selection (psychosocial evaluation, demonstrated commitment to abstinence)
   - 6-month sobriety rule relaxed at some centers for severe, first-episode AH
   - 1-year survival post-transplant 90% in select AH patients

### Chronic/Maintenance

**ABSTINENCE is the single most effective treatment at all stages of ALD.**

**Alcohol cessation:**
- Counseling and behavioral therapy (motivational interviewing, cognitive-behavioral therapy)
- **Pharmacotherapy:**
  - Naltrexone 50mg daily (opioid antagonist; reduces craving and reward)
  - Acamprosate 666mg TID (modulates glutamatergic system; reduces withdrawal symptoms)
  - Disulfiram 250mg daily (aversive therapy; causes severe illness if alcohol consumed; requires high motivation and supervision)
- Alcohol cessation programs (Alcoholics Anonymous, SMART Recovery)
- Addiction psychiatry referral

**Benefits of abstinence:**
- Alcoholic fatty liver: complete resolution with abstinence
- Alcoholic hepatitis: significant improvement in liver tests and histology
- Compensated cirrhosis: halts progression; reduces decompensation and HCC risk
- Decompensated cirrhosis: improves survival; may allow delisting from transplant in select cases

**Management of cirrhosis complications:** See Cirrhosis
- Portal hypertension: beta-blockers, variceal surveillance and prophylaxis
- Ascites: sodium restriction, diuretics (spironolactone + furosemide)
- Hepatic Encephalopathy: lactulose, rifaximin
- HCC surveillance: ultrasound + AFP every 6 months

**Nutritional rehabilitation:**
- Protein 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day (do NOT restrict protein)
- Thiamine 100mg daily, folate 1mg daily, multivitamin
- Zinc supplementation (if deficient; improves HE)
- Branched-chain amino acids (may improve outcomes in advanced cirrhosis)

**Liver transplantation:**
- ALD is second most common indication for transplant (after NAFLD)
- Traditional 6-month sobriety requirement (to assess commitment and allow for liver recovery)
- Post-transplant alcohol relapse rates 20-30%; comprehensive psychosocial evaluation critical
- 5-year survival 70-80%

### Pharmacology
- **Systemic Corticosteroids** — prednisolone 40 mg daily × 28 days for severe alcoholic hepatitis (Maddrey DF ≥ 32)
- **Diuretics** — spironolactone + furosemide for ascites management
- **Beta-Blockers** — non-selective (propranolol, nadolol) for portal hypertension / variceal prophylaxis
- **Antibiotics Overview** — for SBP prophylaxis and treatment; norfloxacin, ceftriaxone
- **PPIs** — stress ulcer prophylaxis; caution with SBP risk in cirrhosis
- **NSAIDs** — AVOID (nephrotoxicity, GI bleeding, impaired hemostasis)
- **Opioid Analgesics** — AVOID or use extreme caution (hepatic metabolism impaired; precipitates encephalopathy)
- **Sedatives and Anxiolytics** — lorazepam preferred for alcohol withdrawal in liver disease (glucuronidation, not CYP metabolism)
- See also: Naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram for AUD treatment; lactulose, rifaximin for hepatic encephalopathy
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Alcohol cessation support:** Establish trust and non-judgmental therapeutic relationship; use motivational interviewing techniques; connect with addiction services, social work, AA/SMART Recovery; address barriers (transportation, housing, mental health)
- **Nutritional rehabilitation:** ALD patients are severely malnourished; ensure high-protein intake 1.5 g/kg/day (do NOT restrict protein); administer thiamine IV before any dextrose-containing fluids (prevents Wernicke encephalopathy); encourage small frequent meals; involve dietitian early
- **Thiamine replacement:** **Thiamine 100mg IV daily × 3-5 days BEFORE any glucose administration** (glucose metabolism depletes thiamine, precipitating Wernicke); then transition to oral thiamine 100mg daily
- **Alcohol withdrawal monitoring:** Use CIWA-Ar protocol; administer lorazepam (preferred in liver disease due to lack of hepatic metabolism); monitor for tremor, agitation, hallucinations, seizures; severe withdrawal can mimic or worsen hepatic encephalopathy
- **Maddrey and MELD scoring:** Calculate on admission to identify severe alcoholic hepatitis requiring corticosteroids; Maddrey DF = 4.6 × (PT patient - PT control in seconds) + total bilirubin (mg/dL); DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20 = severe disease
- **Lille score assessment:** On day 7 of prednisolone therapy, recalculate bilirubin; if Lille ≥0.45 (non-responder), notify provider to discontinue steroids (no further benefit, increased infection risk)
- **Steroid side effects:** Monitor glucose (hyperglycemia common), blood pressure (hypertension), mood changes, infection risk; maintain stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPI)
- **Infection surveillance:** ALD patients have high infection risk (impaired immunity, gut translocation); monitor for fever, leukocytosis, mental status changes; low threshold for cultures (blood, urine, ascites); early antibiotics
- **Medication teaching:** Naltrexone (take daily, reduces craving, avoid opioid pain meds); acamprosate (take TID, continue even if relapse); disulfiram (severe illness if any alcohol consumed, including mouthwash, hand sanitizer, cooking wine)
- **Ascites management:** Daily weights, strict I&O, low-sodium diet (<2g/day), fluid restriction if hyponatremic (<1500 mL/day if Na <125); monitor electrolytes (hypokalemia, hyponatremia common with diuretics)
- **Fall prevention:** High fall risk due to hepatic encephalopathy, weakness, sarcopenia; bed alarm, assist with ambulation
- **Transplant education:** For eligible candidates, explain evaluation process, sobriety requirements, psychosocial assessment; emphasize that transplant is not a "cure" for alcoholism and requires lifelong abstinence
sections.keyPoints:
- ALD is the most prevalent chronic liver disease worldwide
- Spectrum: alcoholic fatty liver → steatohepatitis → fibrosis → cirrhosis → HCC
- Most individuals consuming >40g alcohol daily develop fatty liver; only 10-35% progress to cirrhosis
- AST/ALT ratio >2 highly suggestive of ALD
- Severe alcoholic hepatitis defined by Maddrey DF ≥32 or MELD ≥20; 28-day mortality 30-50%
- Prednisolone 40mg × 28 days for severe AH; Lille score at day 7 guides continuation
- **Abstinence is the most effective treatment at all disease stages**
- Nutritional rehabilitation critical: protein 1.5 g/kg/day, thiamine, folate, multivitamins
- Pharmacotherapy for alcohol cessation: naltrexone, acamprosate, disulfiram
- Liver transplantation for end-stage disease; traditionally requires 6-month sobriety

clinicalPearls: **AST/ALT ratio >2 is the signature of alcoholic liver disease** — if you see ALT > AST, think viral hepatitis, NAFLD, or drug-induced liver injury, not alcohol; the "2:1 rule" (AST:ALT >2:1) has 90% specificity for ALD.

**Thiamine BEFORE glucose or you risk precipitating Wernicke encephalopathy** — malnourished alcoholics are thiamine-deficient; glucose administration increases metabolic demand and depletes remaining thiamine stores; give 100mg IV thiamine before hanging any dextrose-containing fluids.

**"I only have 2 beers a day" often means 6+ drinks when you dig deeper** — patients underreport alcohol consumption by 50% on average; use non-judgmental questions ("How many days per week do you drink? How many drinks on a typical day? What size are the drinks?") and collateral history from family.

**Stopping steroids at day 7 in non-responders (Lille ≥0.45) is not giving up — it's evidence-based medicine** — continuing prednisolone beyond day 7 in non-responders provides zero survival benefit and dramatically increases infection risk; stopping is the correct clinical decision, not "giving up."

**Protein restriction in alcoholic liver disease is medical malpractice, not best practice** — these patients are severely protein-calorie malnourished with sarcopenia; restricting protein worsens outcomes, increases infection risk, and does NOT improve hepatic encephalopathy; they need 1.5 g/kg/day, not 0.5 g/kg/day.

**Alcohol withdrawal can look identical to hepatic encephalopathy — but the treatment is opposite** — both cause confusion, tremor, agitation; HE needs lactulose, withdrawal needs benzodiazepines; giving benzos for HE worsens encephalopathy, and giving lactulose for withdrawal does nothing; use CIWA scoring and careful history to distinguish.

**Portal hypertension complications:** Variceal bleeding, ascites, Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis, hepatorenal syndrome, hepatopulmonary syndrome
**Hepatocellular carcinoma:** 3-10% annual incidence in cirrhotic ALD
**Infections:** Increased susceptibility (pneumonia, SBP, UTI, skin/soft tissue infections)
**Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, thiamine deficiency (Wernicke encephalopathy), folate deficiency, vitamin deficiencies
**Extrahepatic complications:** Alcoholic cardiomyopathy, pancreatitis, neuropathy, myopathy

altitude-illnesses.title: Altitude Illnesses

sections.physics-of-altitude-from-diffusion-and-g:
> *First principles: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — at altitude, reduced atmospheric PO₂ → reduced PaO₂ → reduced SaO₂ → reduced DO2 despite normal Hgb and CO; the entire altitude illness spectrum is the body's response to insufficient DO2. Oxygenation and Ventilation — hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HAPE) is a pathologic V/Q response: hypoxia triggers global pulmonary vasoconstriction → pulmonary hypertension → non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema.*

### Why Oxygen Drops at Altitude
- **Atmospheric pressure decreases** with altitude — the fraction of O₂ (FiO₂) stays 21%, but the **partial pressure of O₂ (PO₂)** drops
- At sea level: PB = 760 mmHg → PiO₂ = 150 mmHg → PAO₂ ≈ 100 mmHg
- At 18,000 ft: PB = 380 mmHg → PiO₂ = 75 mmHg → PAO₂ ≈ 40 mmHg (equivalent to breathing 10% O₂ at sea level)

| Altitude | Barometric Pressure | PiO₂ (approx) | Clinical Zone |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sea level | 760 mmHg | 150 mmHg | Normal |
| 5,000-8,000 ft | 632-565 mmHg | 125-112 mmHg | Moderate — AMS possible |
| 8,000-14,000 ft | 565-446 mmHg | 112-85 mmHg | High — AMS common, HAPE/HACE possible |
| 14,000-18,000 ft | 446-380 mmHg | 85-75 mmHg | Very high — significant risk |
| >18,000 ft | <380 mmHg | <75 mmHg | Extreme — acclimatization limited |

### Normal Acclimatization Response
When the body DOES acclimatize successfully:
1. **Hypoxic ventilatory response (HVR)** — carotid body chemoreceptors sense low PaO₂ → hyperventilation → increases PAO₂ (but causes respiratory alkalosis)
2. **Renal compensation** — kidneys excrete bicarbonate to correct respiratory alkalosis (takes 2-3 days)
3. **Increased erythropoietin (EPO)** → increased RBC production (takes weeks)
4. **Increased 2,3-DPG** → rightward shift of oxyhemoglobin dissociation curve → improved O₂ offloading to tissues
5. **Pulmonary vasoconstriction** (HPV) → redirects blood to better-ventilated alveoli

Altitude illness occurs when this acclimatization process **fails or is overwhelmed** by too-rapid ascent.

---
sections.acute-mountain-sickness-ams:
### Pathophysiology
- Mild cerebral vasodilation and increased cerebral blood flow in response to hypoxia
- Mild blood-brain barrier disruption → subclinical cerebral edema
- Essentially a **mild form of the HACE spectrum**

### Clinical Features
- **Headache** (required for diagnosis) PLUS at least one of:
  - Nausea/vomiting
  - Fatigue/weakness
  - Dizziness/lightheadedness
  - Difficulty sleeping
- Onset: 6-12 hours after ascent to >8,000 ft
- **Lake Louise Score** ≥3 with headache = AMS diagnosis

### Management
- **Stop ascending** — do not go higher until symptoms resolve
- **Descend if symptoms worsen** — even 500-1,000 ft can help
- **Acetazolamide** (Diamox) 250 mg PO BID — accelerates acclimatization
  - Mechanism: carbonic anhydrase inhibitor → renal bicarbonate wasting → metabolic acidosis → stimulates ventilation
  - Side effects: paresthesias (classic tingling fingers/toes), altered taste of carbonated beverages, polyuria
  - **Sulfa allergy cross-reactivity** — historically contraindicated; current evidence suggests risk is very low, but discuss with patient
- **Dexamethasone** 4 mg PO/IM q6h — for moderate-severe AMS or when acetazolamide contraindicated
  - Does NOT help acclimatization — masks symptoms only; rebound risk if stopped at altitude
- **Ibuprofen** 600 mg PO TID — effective for AMS headache and mild prevention
- Supplemental O₂ if available
- Most cases self-resolve with rest and time at same altitude (1-2 days)

---
sections.high-altitude-cerebral-edema-hace:
### Pathophysiology
- **End-stage of the AMS spectrum** — progressive vasogenic cerebral edema
- Hypoxia → cerebral vasodilation → increased capillary pressure → blood-brain barrier breakdown → extracellular fluid accumulation
- Cytotoxic edema also contributes — direct hypoxic injury to neurons and glia
- **Primarily affects white matter** — especially posterior fossa (cerebellar symptoms prominent)

### Clinical Features
- **Ataxia** — the hallmark; truncal ataxia (can't walk straight) is the earliest reliable sign
  - **Tandem gait test:** Can the patient walk heel-to-toe? If not → HACE until proven otherwise
- Altered mental status — confusion, bizarre behavior, drowsiness → coma
- Severe headache unresponsive to analgesics
- Vomiting
- Papilledema (if able to examine)
- Seizures, focal neurologic deficits (late)
- **Progression from AMS to HACE can occur over hours**

> [!critical]
> **HACE is fatal without descent.** Mortality is near 100% if untreated. The treatment is DESCENT — everything else buys time.

### Management
1. **IMMEDIATE DESCENT** — the definitive treatment; descend at least 1,000-3,000 ft
2. **Dexamethasone** 8 mg IV/IM/PO × 1, then 4 mg q6h — reduces cerebral edema; buys time for descent
3. **Supplemental O₂** — high flow if available; target SpO₂ >90%
4. **Portable hyperbaric chamber (Gamow bag)** — simulates descent of ~5,000 ft; temporizing measure when descent is impossible (e.g., storm, darkness)
5. Keep patient warm, hydrated
6. **Do NOT let the patient descend alone** — ataxia + altered mental status = fall/death risk

---
sections.high-altitude-pulmonary-edema-hape:
### Pathophysiology — Non-Cardiogenic Pulmonary Edema
HAPE has a **unique mechanism** — it is NOT the same as cardiogenic pulmonary edema:
1. **Hypoxia → exaggerated hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction (HPV)** — the key
   - HPV is a normal response (redirects blood from hypoxic to normoxic alveoli)
   - In HAPE-susceptible individuals, HPV is **excessive and non-uniform**
2. Non-uniform vasoconstriction → some capillary beds are vasoconstricted, others are exposed to **very high pulmonary arterial pressures**
3. High pressure in unprotected capillaries → **stress failure of pulmonary capillaries** → leak
4. Protein-rich fluid and red blood cells flood alveoli → impaired gas exchange
5. **This creates a vicious cycle:** edema → worse hypoxia → more vasoconstriction → more edema

Key distinguishing features from cardiogenic edema:
- **Normal cardiac function** — normal LV, normal filling pressures
- **Non-uniform edema** — often **patchy** on imaging (vs bilateral symmetric in cardiogenic)
- **Pink frothy sputum** from hemorrhagic edema fluid (capillary stress failure)

### Clinical Features
- **Dyspnea at rest** — initially with exertion, progressing to rest
- Dry cough → productive cough → **pink frothy sputum** (late)
- Tachypnea, tachycardia
- **Crackles** on auscultation (often asymmetric/patchy — unlike cardiogenic)
- Cyanosis
- **Low-grade fever** — common (do not mistake for pneumonia)
- Onset: typically 2-4 days after ascent; can be as early as day 1 in rapid ascent
- **HAPE can occur WITHOUT preceding AMS** — a patient can develop pulmonary edema without ever having a headache

> [!warning]
> **HAPE is the most common cause of death from altitude illness.** It can be misdiagnosed as pneumonia (fever + cough + crackles + infiltrate on CXR). The history of recent ascent is the key differentiator.

### Management
1. **DESCENT** — the definitive treatment; even 1,000-2,000 ft dramatically improves oxygenation
2. **Supplemental O₂** — high flow; target SpO₂ >90%; often rapidly effective
3. **Nifedipine** 30 mg extended-release PO q12h — reduces pulmonary artery pressure
   - Mechanism: calcium channel blocker → pulmonary vasodilation → reduces the driving force behind capillary stress failure
4. **Portable hyperbaric chamber** — temporizing if descent impossible
5. **CPAP/PEEP** if intubated — improves oxygenation by recruiting flooded alveoli
6. **Tadalafil or sildenafil** — PDE-5 inhibitors cause pulmonary vasodilation; used for prevention in susceptible individuals
7. **Do NOT give diuretics** — these patients are often intravascularly depleted already; diuretics worsen hypovolemia
8. **Bed rest at altitude with O₂** can be sufficient for mild cases if descent is impossible

---
sections.prevention:
### Ascent Guidelines
- **Above 10,000 ft:** Increase sleeping altitude by no more than **1,000-1,500 ft/day**
- **Rest day** every 3,000 ft of altitude gained
- "Climb high, sleep low" — day hikes to higher altitude with return to lower sleeping altitude
- **Adequate hydration** — dehydration worsens symptoms
- Avoid alcohol and sedatives at altitude (depress ventilatory drive)

### Pharmacologic Prophylaxis
| Drug | Dose | Indication | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Acetazolamide** | 125-250 mg PO BID | AMS prevention (all altitude travelers) | Carbonic anhydrase inhibition → metabolic acidosis → stimulates ventilation |
| **Dexamethasone** | 2-4 mg PO q6h | AMS prevention when acetazolamide contraindicated | Anti-inflammatory, reduces cerebral edema |
| **Nifedipine** | 30 mg ER PO q12h | HAPE prevention in susceptible individuals | Pulmonary vasodilation |
| **Tadalafil** | 10 mg PO BID | HAPE prevention | PDE-5 inhibition → pulmonary vasodilation |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### ED Assessment
- **Ascent history** — altitude reached, rate of ascent, onset of symptoms relative to ascent
- **SpO₂ with altitude context** — an SpO₂ of 85% at 14,000 ft has different significance than at sea level
- **Neuro assessment** — GCS, ataxia (tandem gait test), orientation, pupil exam
- **Respiratory assessment** — auscultate for crackles (especially asymmetric), work of breathing, sputum character
- **Concurrent injuries** — altitude illness often co-occurs with hypothermia, falls, dehydration, frostbite
- **CXR** — HAPE shows patchy infiltrates (often right middle lobe predominant); rule out pneumothorax from climbing falls

### Monitoring
- Continuous SpO₂ and cardiac monitoring
- Frequent neuro checks (HACE can deteriorate rapidly)
- I&Os — avoid both dehydration and over-hydration
- Temperature — hypothermia common in rescue/descent scenarios

### Patient Education (Prevention)
- Know the ascent rate guidelines — most altitude illness is **preventable with proper ascent rate**
- Recognize early symptoms — headache + nausea at altitude = stop ascending
- **"Never ascend with symptoms"** — the golden rule of altitude medicine
- Carry acetazolamide if going above 10,000 ft
- Report for Rainier/Cascades climbs: discuss prophylaxis with PCP before trip

---
sections.references:
- Luks et al., "Wilderness Medical Society Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Prevention and Treatment of Acute Altitude Illness," Wilderness & Environmental Medicine, 2019
- Bärtsch & Swenson, "Acute High-Altitude Illnesses," New England Journal of Medicine, 2013
- Imray et al., "Acute Mountain Sickness: Pathophysiology, Prevention, and Treatment," Progress in Cardiovascular Diseases, 2010

clinicalPearls: **Ataxia at altitude = HACE = descend NOW** — the tandem gait test is the single most useful bedside test; if a climber can't walk a straight line at altitude, they need to come down immediately
**HAPE is NOT cardiogenic pulmonary edema** — the mechanism is exaggerated pulmonary vasoconstriction → capillary stress failure → leak; diuretics make it worse, not better
**HAPE can occur without AMS** — don't wait for a headache; someone can develop pulmonary edema as their first altitude symptom
**Fever in HAPE mimics pneumonia** — low-grade fever with cough, crackles, and CXR infiltrate at altitude is HAPE until proven otherwise; the ascent history is your differentiator
**Dexamethasone masks but doesn't fix** — it buys time for HACE but does NOT promote acclimatization; if you stop it at altitude, rebound cerebral edema can occur
**Acetazolamide's tingling fingers are a feature, not a bug** — paresthesias mean the drug is working (carbonic anhydrase inhibition); counsel patients so they don't stop taking it

**Mt. Rainier climbers come to YOUR ED** — Tacoma General is a natural destination for Rainier evacuations; HAPE and HACE are in your differential for any climber presenting with dyspnea or altered mental status

anaphylaxis.title: Anaphylaxis
anaphylaxis.edTriage: ESI Level 1 — Immediate life threat requiring resuscitation; airway loss and cardiovascular collapse can occur within minutes

sections.definition:
Anaphylaxis is an acute, potentially fatal systemic allergic reaction involving multiple organ systems. It is characterized by rapid onset (seconds to minutes) of life-threatening airway, breathing, and circulatory compromise following exposure to an allergen. The hallmark is cardiovascular collapse and/or airway obstruction caused by massive immune-mediated release of vasoactive mediators.

**Anaphylactic shock** specifically refers to anaphylaxis with hemodynamic compromise — distributive shock from profound vasodilation and capillary leak.

**Anaphylactoid (non-IgE) reactions** produce an identical clinical syndrome through direct mast cell degranulation without prior sensitization (e.g., radiocontrast, opioids, vancomycin "red man syndrome"). The clinical management is the same — the distinction is immunologic, not clinical.

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Incidence:** Estimated 50-112 episodes per 100,000 person-years in the US; true incidence is likely underreported
- **Mortality:** ~1,500 deaths/year in the US; case fatality rate ~0.3-1% (higher in medication-triggered and older adults)
- **Demographics:** All ages; food triggers predominate in children/adolescents, medication and insect venom triggers predominate in adults
- **Risk Factors:**
  - Prior anaphylactic episode (strongest predictor of recurrence)
  - Atopy/asthma (especially poorly controlled asthma — dramatically increases fatal anaphylaxis risk)
  - Beta-blocker or ACE inhibitor use (impairs compensatory response to anaphylaxis)
  - Mast cell disorders (mastocytosis, elevated baseline tryptase)
  - Age >50 (cardiovascular comorbidities reduce compensatory reserve)
  - Exercise in proximity to allergen exposure (exercise-induced anaphylaxis)
- **Trends:** Incidence rising over past two decades, particularly food-related anaphylaxis in children and drug-related anaphylaxis in adults

---
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — IgE-mediated mast cell degranulation → massive histamine/tryptase/prostaglandin release → type I hypersensitivity*
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — distributive shock mechanism: histamine causes profound vasodilation (↓SVR) + increased capillary permeability → BP = CO × SVR collapses*

### IgE-Mediated (Classic Anaphylaxis)

1. **Sensitization phase** — initial allergen exposure generates allergen-specific IgE antibodies that bind to high-affinity FcεRI receptors on mast cells and basophils (no reaction on first exposure)
2. **Re-exposure** — allergen cross-links surface-bound IgE on mast cells/basophils → receptor aggregation → cell activation
3. **Degranulation** — within seconds, activated mast cells release preformed mediators from cytoplasmic granules:
   - **Histamine** — vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, bronchospasm, pruritus, tachycardia
   - **Tryptase** — protease marker of mast cell degranulation (diagnostic lab value)
   - **Heparin** — anticoagulant effects
4. **Newly synthesized mediators** (minutes to hours):
   - **Leukotrienes** (LTC4, LTD4, LTE4) — potent bronchoconstrictors, 1000x more potent than histamine at bronchospasm; mucus hypersecretion
   - **Prostaglandins** (PGD2) — vasodilation, bronchoconstriction
   - **Platelet-activating factor (PAF)** — vascular permeability, platelet aggregation, hypotension
   - **Cytokines** (TNF-α, IL-4, IL-13) — amplify and sustain inflammatory cascade

### Systemic Effects

| Organ System | Mediator Effect | Clinical Manifestation |
|---|---|---|
| **Vascular** | Vasodilation + capillary leak | Hypotension, distributive shock, third-spacing |
| **Airway (upper)** | Mucosal edema | Laryngeal edema, tongue swelling, stridor |
| **Airway (lower)** | Bronchospasm + mucus secretion | Wheezing, dyspnea, respiratory failure |
| **Skin** | Vasodilation + permeability | Urticaria, flushing, angioedema |
| **GI** | Smooth muscle contraction + edema | Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, cramping |
| **Cardiac** | Direct histamine effect + hypovolemia | Tachycardia, arrhythmias, Kounis syndrome (allergic coronary spasm) |

### Non-IgE Mediated (Anaphylactoid)

Same clinical picture via direct mast cell activation without IgE involvement:
- **Radiocontrast media** — direct mast cell activation via osmotic mechanisms
- **Opioids** — direct mast cell degranulation (morphine > fentanyl)
- **Vancomycin** — "red man syndrome" from histamine release (infusion rate-dependent)
- **NSAIDs** — cyclooxygenase inhibition shunts arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway
- **Exercise** — mechanism incompletely understood; often food-dependent

**Key concept:** Non-IgE anaphylaxis can occur on FIRST exposure (no prior sensitization needed). Treat identically — epinephrine is still first-line.

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Onset:** Usually within 5-30 minutes of exposure; can be immediate (seconds) with IV medications or insect stings. Delayed onset (hours) is possible with oral allergens.

**Cutaneous (80-90% of cases):**
- Urticaria (hives) — raised, pruritic, erythematous wheals
- Flushing — diffuse erythema
- Angioedema — deep tissue swelling of lips, tongue, eyelids, face
- Pruritus — palms, soles, scalp, and groin often affected first

**Respiratory (70%):**
- Upper airway: hoarseness, stridor, throat tightness, sensation of throat closing, tongue swelling
- Lower airway: wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, cough, hypoxia
- Rhinitis: sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion

**Cardiovascular (45%):**
- Tachycardia (compensatory, or direct histamine effect)
- Hypotension (SBP <90 or >30% decrease from baseline)
- Dizziness, syncope, altered mental status
- Cardiac arrest (PEA most common arrest rhythm in anaphylaxis)

**Gastrointestinal (45%):**
- Nausea, vomiting
- Abdominal cramping, diarrhea
- Dysphagia

**Neurologic:**
- Sense of impending doom (classic and reliable early symptom)
- Confusion, altered mental status
- Loss of consciousness

### Red Flags — Severe or Fatal Anaphylaxis

- **Stridor or inability to speak** — laryngeal edema, airway loss imminent
- **Hypotension or altered mental status** — cardiovascular collapse
- **History of asthma** (especially poorly controlled) — dramatically increases risk of fatal bronchospasm
- **Patient on beta-blockers** — refractory to epinephrine, paradoxical bradycardia
- **Absence of skin findings** — 10-20% of anaphylaxis has NO cutaneous signs (especially hypotensive presentations); do NOT rule out anaphylaxis because there are no hives
- **Rapid progression** — the faster symptoms develop, the more severe the reaction is likely to be

### Complications

- **Fatal airway obstruction** from laryngeal edema
- **Cardiovascular collapse** and cardiac arrest
- **Biphasic reaction** — recurrence of symptoms after initial resolution (see below)
- **Anoxic brain injury** from prolonged hypotension or hypoxia
- **Kounis syndrome** — allergic coronary artery spasm → acute MI
- **Aspiration** from vomiting with altered mental status

---
sections.diagnosis:
### NIAID/FAAN Diagnostic Criteria

Anaphylaxis is a **clinical diagnosis** — there is no single lab test that rules it in or out in real time. ANY ONE of the following three scenarios = anaphylaxis:

**Scenario 1: Acute onset with skin/mucosal involvement PLUS one of:**
- Respiratory compromise (dyspnea, wheeze, stridor, hypoxia, reduced PEF)
- Hypotension or associated symptoms (syncope, incontinence, collapse)

**Scenario 2: Two or more of the following occurring rapidly after exposure to a LIKELY allergen:**
- Skin/mucosal involvement (urticaria, flushing, angioedema)
- Respiratory compromise
- Hypotension or associated symptoms
- Persistent GI symptoms (cramping, vomiting)

**Scenario 3: Reduced blood pressure after exposure to a KNOWN allergen for that patient:**
- Adults: SBP <90 mmHg or >30% decrease from baseline

**Clinical pearl:** Most anaphylaxis is diagnosed at the bedside before any labs return. If it looks like anaphylaxis, treat it like anaphylaxis.

### Key Labs/Studies

| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **Serum tryptase** | Peaks 60-90 min after onset, returns to normal by 6h. Elevated tryptase (>11.4 ng/mL) supports mast cell degranulation. Draw during event AND at baseline (24h+ later). NORMAL tryptase does NOT rule out anaphylaxis — often normal in food-triggered anaphylaxis |
| **Histamine** | Peaks within 5-10 min, very short half-life (~1h). Rarely useful clinically; primarily research |
| **CBC** | Assess for hemoconcentration (capillary leak), eosinophilia |
| **BMP** | Baseline renal function, electrolytes |
| **ECG** | ST changes (Kounis syndrome), arrhythmias, tachycardia |
| **Chest X-ray** | If respiratory distress — hyperinflation, pulmonary edema |
| **ABG/VBG** | If significant respiratory compromise — assess oxygenation, ventilation, acid-base |

### Differential Diagnosis

- Severe asthma exacerbation (no skin findings, no hypotension)
- Vasovagal syncope (bradycardia, not tachycardia; no urticaria; rapid recovery)
- Panic attack/anxiety (no objective signs, normal vitals)
- Angioedema without anaphylaxis (ACE inhibitor-related — no urticaria, no hypotension)
- Carcinoid syndrome (flushing, diarrhea, wheezing — but chronic)
- Mastocytosis (recurrent flushing, elevated baseline tryptase)
- Septic shock (fever, infectious source, no urticaria)
- Scombroid fish poisoning (histamine from spoiled fish — looks identical, self-limited)

---
sections.management:
### Immediate ED Management (First 5 Minutes)

**1. IM EPINEPHRINE — FIRST AND ALWAYS FIRST**
- **Dose:** 0.3-0.5 mg of 1:1,000 (1 mg/mL) concentration IM
- **Site:** Anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) — best absorption
- **Repeat:** Every 5-15 minutes as needed — there is NO contraindication to epinephrine in anaphylaxis
- **Mechanism:** Alpha-1 → vasoconstriction (reverses hypotension, reduces mucosal edema). Beta-1 → increased HR and contractility. Beta-2 → bronchodilation, inhibits further mast cell degranulation
- **Do NOT use subcutaneous** — absorption is unreliable in hypotensive/vasoconstricted patients
- **Do NOT use IV push of 1:1,000** — this is cardiac-dose epinephrine and can cause fatal arrhythmia

**2. Position the patient**
- **Supine with legs elevated** — maximizes venous return in distributive shock
- If respiratory distress predominates → **semi-upright/seated** position
- **Do NOT sit up or stand a hypotensive anaphylaxis patient** — this can cause cardiac arrest from empty ventricle syndrome (loss of venous return)

**3. Airway management**
- High-flow oxygen 15L via non-rebreather mask
- Prepare for rapid airway deterioration — have intubation equipment at bedside
- If laryngeal edema → early intubation before complete obstruction (the airway only gets worse, not better)
- If unable to intubate → surgical cricothyrotomy

**4. IV access**
- Two large-bore IVs (18G or larger)
- **Normal saline bolus 1-2 liters** for hypotension — may need 5-10 liters in severe anaphylactic shock (massive capillary leak)
- Rapid infusion with pressure bags

### Adjunctive Medications

| Medication | Dose | Purpose | Timing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Diphenhydramine** (H1 blocker) | 50 mg IV | Urticaria, pruritus, flushing | After epinephrine |
| **Famotidine** (H2 blocker) | 20 mg IV | Synergistic with H1 blocker for histamine blockade | After epinephrine |
| **Methylprednisolone** | 125 mg IV | Prevents biphasic reaction; anti-inflammatory | After epinephrine |
| **Albuterol** nebulizer | 2.5-5 mg | Bronchospasm not resolving with epinephrine alone | Concurrent with above |

**Important:** Antihistamines and steroids are ADJUNCTS. They do NOT treat anaphylaxis — they treat symptoms. **Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment.**

### Refractory Anaphylaxis

If hypotension or respiratory compromise persists despite 2-3 doses of IM epinephrine:

- **Epinephrine infusion:** 1-10 mcg/min IV (0.1-0.5 mcg/kg/min), titrate to effect — use 1:10,000 concentration (0.1 mg/mL) or dilute 1 mg in 250 mL NS
- **Glucagon 1-5 mg IV** over 5 minutes for **beta-blocked patients** — bypasses beta-receptor blockade via direct cAMP stimulation; may cause vomiting, position patient to prevent aspiration
- **Vasopressin** — if refractory hypotension despite epinephrine drip
- **Methylene blue** 1-2 mg/kg IV — emerging rescue therapy for refractory vasoplegia via nitric oxide pathway inhibition

### Observation Period

- **Minimum 4-6 hours** after resolution of symptoms for all anaphylaxis patients
- **Extended observation 12-24 hours** for:
  - Severe initial reaction (required epinephrine drip, intubation, or ICU care)
  - Patients on beta-blockers (blunted response, harder to resuscitate)
  - History of biphasic reactions
  - Late-presenting reactions (symptoms developed slowly)
  - Poor access to emergency care (rural, remote)
  - Poorly controlled asthma
- **Admit to ICU** if: required epinephrine infusion, intubation, persistent hypotension, multi-organ involvement

### Biphasic Reactions

- Occur in up to **20%** of anaphylaxis cases
- Recurrence of symptoms after complete resolution, typically **1-72 hours** after initial reaction (most within 8 hours)
- Can be as severe or MORE severe than the initial reaction
- **Risk factors for biphasic reaction:** severe initial presentation, delayed epinephrine administration, need for multiple epinephrine doses, unknown trigger
- **Steroids may reduce risk** — this is the primary rationale for methylprednisolone in anaphylaxis management
- Educate patient to return to ED immediately if symptoms recur

---
sections.discharge-planning:
### Every anaphylaxis patient must leave with:

1. **EpiPen prescription** — two auto-injectors (they may need a second dose before EMS arrives)
2. **EpiPen training** — demonstrate proper technique before discharge:
   - Remove blue safety cap
   - Press orange tip firmly into anterolateral thigh (through clothing is fine)
   - Hold 10 seconds
   - Call 911 immediately after use
   - Common error: pressing wrong end → needle into thumb
3. **Allergen identification** — document confirmed or suspected trigger; if unknown, stress allergen avoidance counseling
4. **Allergy referral** — outpatient allergist/immunologist for skin testing, specific IgE testing, and ongoing management
5. **Anaphylaxis action plan** — written instructions for patient and family on recognizing early symptoms and when to use EpiPen
6. **Medical alert identification** — recommend bracelet or necklace
7. **Diphenhydramine and/or prednisone taper** — oral antihistamine and possibly a 3-5 day steroid taper for ongoing symptom control
8. **Return precautions** — return immediately for ANY recurrence of symptoms (biphasic reaction risk)

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### ED Assessment Priorities

1. **Recognize the pattern** — anaphylaxis is a clinical diagnosis. Urticaria + dyspnea + hypotension = anaphylaxis. A "sense of impending doom" is a real and reliable symptom, not anxiety
2. **Give epinephrine IMMEDIATELY** — do not wait for a physician order if the patient is in anaphylaxis with airway compromise or hemodynamic instability. Many facilities have standing orders or NISO for IM epinephrine in anaphylaxis. Know your facility's protocol
3. **Time matters as much as in STEMI or stroke** — delayed epinephrine is the number one cause of fatal anaphylaxis. Every minute of delay allows mediator cascade to amplify
4. **Assess airway continuously** — laryngeal edema can progress rapidly. Ask the patient to speak — hoarseness or voice change indicates worsening edema. Have suction and intubation equipment at bedside
5. **Two large-bore IVs early** — you will need rapid fluid resuscitation and multiple IV medications

### Medication Administration Pearls

- **IM epinephrine goes in the THIGH** — deltoid absorption is significantly inferior. Anterolateral vastus lateralis, 90-degree angle, deep IM
- **Know the concentrations:** IM = 1:1,000 (1 mg/mL). IV infusion = 1:10,000 (0.1 mg/mL) or diluted. NEVER give 1:1,000 IV push
- **Diphenhydramine IV push** — give slowly over 1-2 minutes; rapid push causes hypotension (paradoxically worsening the problem)
- **Albuterol** is for lower airway bronchospasm only — it will not help laryngeal edema (that is epinephrine's job via alpha-1 vasoconstriction)
- **Steroids take 4-6 hours to work** — they are not for the acute reaction; they are for preventing the biphasic reaction

### Monitoring Parameters

- **Continuous cardiac monitoring** — watch for arrhythmias, especially in patients receiving epinephrine infusions
- **Continuous SpO2** — target >94%
- **Vital signs q5 minutes** during acute resuscitation, then q15 minutes during observation
- **Respiratory assessment** — stridor, wheezing, work of breathing, voice quality
- **Skin assessment** — progression or resolution of urticaria/angioedema
- **Mental status** — confusion or somnolence suggests worsening hypoperfusion
- **Urine output** — if Foley placed, target >0.5 mL/kg/hr

### Special Populations

**Beta-blocked patients:**
- May present with paradoxical bradycardia instead of tachycardia
- Epinephrine may be ineffective (beta-receptors blocked)
- **Glucagon is the rescue** — 1-5 mg IV, bypasses beta-receptor blockade
- These patients require extended observation (12-24 hours minimum)
- Are at highest risk for refractory, fatal anaphylaxis

**Pregnant patients:**
- Epinephrine is safe and indicated — fetal hypoxia from untreated anaphylaxis is far more dangerous than epinephrine
- Position in left lateral tilt to prevent aortocaval compression
- Continuous fetal monitoring if viable gestational age

**Pediatric patients:**
- Epinephrine dose: 0.01 mg/kg IM (max 0.3 mg for <30 kg, 0.5 mg for >30 kg)
- EpiPen Jr (0.15 mg) for children 15-30 kg; standard EpiPen (0.3 mg) for >30 kg

### Patient Education Before Discharge

- Demonstrate EpiPen technique — have the patient and family member return-demonstrate with a trainer device
- Emphasize: **use the EpiPen FIRST, then call 911** — do not drive to the hospital instead of using the EpiPen
- Explain biphasic reaction risk — "your symptoms can come back even after you feel better; return to the ED immediately if anything recurs within the next 3 days"
- Review allergen avoidance strategies specific to their trigger
- Emphasize carrying EpiPen at ALL times — not in the car, not at home, ON their person

---
sections.keyPoints:
- **Epinephrine IM is the ONLY first-line treatment** — antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts, not treatments
- **Give epinephrine early and repeat as needed** — there is no maximum dose and no absolute contraindication in anaphylaxis
- **Anterolateral thigh, not the deltoid** — vastus lateralis provides superior and more reliable absorption
- **Absence of skin findings does NOT rule out anaphylaxis** — 10-20% of cases present without urticaria or angioedema
- **Beta-blocked patients are the highest risk** — may not respond to epinephrine; glucagon is the rescue; plan for extended observation
- **Biphasic reactions occur in up to 20%** — observe minimum 4-6 hours; longer for severe reactions and high-risk patients
- **Tryptase is confirmatory, not diagnostic** — a normal tryptase does not exclude anaphylaxis
- **Every patient goes home with two EpiPens, an action plan, and an allergy referral**
- **Patient positioning matters** — supine with legs elevated for hypotension; do NOT make a hypotensive patient sit up

---

clinicalPearls: **"Epi first, questions later"** — the single most common cause of fatal anaphylaxis is delayed epinephrine administration. If you are thinking about whether this might be anaphylaxis, the answer is to give epinephrine. The risk of unnecessary epinephrine (transient tachycardia, tremor, anxiety) is trivially small compared to the risk of untreated anaphylaxis (death). You will never be wrong for giving epi to a patient in suspected anaphylaxis.

**"The thigh, not the arm"** — IM epinephrine in the anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) achieves higher and more reliable peak plasma levels than the deltoid. This is especially true in patients who are already vasoconstricted and hypotensive. Auto-injector needle length is designed for thigh injection. This is not optional — it is the standard of care.

**"Beta-blocked patients are the scariest anaphylaxis"** — patients on beta-blockers lose their primary compensatory mechanism (tachycardia and increased cardiac output) AND may be resistant to epinephrine's beta-mediated effects. They present with paradoxical bradycardia, refractory hypotension, and severe bronchospasm that does not respond to standard doses. Have glucagon ready. These patients may need an epinephrine drip and ICU admission. Extended observation is mandatory.

**"No hives? Still anaphylaxis"** — the most dangerous anaphylaxis presentations are cardiovascular collapse WITHOUT skin findings. Do not anchor on the absence of urticaria. Isolated hypotension after a known allergen exposure meets diagnostic criteria. The skin may not show findings because all the blood is pooling in the splanchnic circulation — perfusion to the skin is too poor to manifest hives.

**"Sense of impending doom is real"** — when a patient tells you they feel like they are going to die, take it seriously. This is a well-documented symptom of anaphylaxis caused by the massive catecholamine and mediator surge. It often precedes cardiovascular collapse by minutes. It is not anxiety — it is a clinical warning sign.

**"Dead patients don't have biphasic reactions"** — the observation period exists because biphasic reactions are real and can be fatal. But the observation period is meaningless if you did not treat the initial reaction aggressively. Focus on the acute resuscitation first. Steroids are given to reduce biphasic risk, but the evidence is not definitive.

Flushing — diffuse erythema

Pruritus — palms, soles, scalp, and groin often affected first

Lower airway: wheezing, dyspnea, chest tightness, cough, hypoxia
Rhinitis: sneezing, rhinorrhea, nasal congestion

Hypotension (SBP <90 or >30% decrease from baseline)
Dizziness, syncope, altered mental status

Abdominal cramping, diarrhea
Dysphagia

Confusion, altered mental status

**Stridor or inability to speak** — laryngeal edema, airway loss imminent
**Hypotension or altered mental status** — cardiovascular collapse
**History of asthma** (especially poorly controlled) — dramatically increases risk of fatal bronchospasm
**Patient on beta-blockers** — refractory to epinephrine, paradoxical bradycardia
**Absence of skin findings** — 10-20% of anaphylaxis has NO cutaneous signs (especially hypotensive presentations); do NOT rule out anaphylaxis because there are no hives
**Rapid progression** — the faster symptoms develop, the more severe the reaction is likely to be
**Fatal airway obstruction** from laryngeal edema
**Cardiovascular collapse** and cardiac arrest
**Biphasic reaction** — recurrence of symptoms after initial resolution (see below)
**Anoxic brain injury** from prolonged hypotension or hypoxia
**Kounis syndrome** — allergic coronary artery spasm → acute MI
**Aspiration** from vomiting with altered mental status
redFlags: **Stridor or inability to speak** — laryngeal edema, airway loss imminent
**Hypotension or altered mental status** — cardiovascular collapse
**History of asthma** (especially poorly controlled) — dramatically increases risk of fatal bronchospasm
**Patient on beta-blockers** — refractory to epinephrine, paradoxical bradycardia
**Absence of skin findings** — 10-20% of anaphylaxis has NO cutaneous signs (especially hypotensive presentations); do NOT rule out anaphylaxis because there are no hives
**Rapid progression** — the faster symptoms develop, the more severe the reaction is likely to be

anxiety-disorders-and-panic.title: Anxiety Disorders and Panic

sections.definition:
Anxiety disorders are the most common class of psychiatric illness, and panic attacks are one of the most common reasons for ED visits. The challenge for the ED is not diagnosing anxiety — it is **ruling out the dangerous medical conditions that anxiety mimics** before reassuring the patient.

A panic attack produces real physiologic symptoms (tachycardia, chest pain, dyspnea, diaphoresis, tremor) that are indistinguishable from MI, PE, anaphylaxis, or thyroid storm on initial presentation. The ED nurse's job is to take the symptoms seriously, perform the appropriate workup, and — once medical causes are excluded — provide compassionate care for a genuinely distressing condition.

**Related notes:** Major Depressive Disorder · Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm · Bipolar Disorder · Sedatives and Anxiolytics · Antidepressants

---
sections.epidemiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — GABA-A (ligand-gated Cl⁻ channel) is the key inhibitory brake; anxiolytics (benzodiazepines) enhance GABA-A. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the threat-response (amygdala → SNS → cortisol) is a normal feedback loop; in anxiety disorders, the set point is dysregulated and the response is disproportionate/persistent.*

- **Anxiety disorders:** Lifetime prevalence ~30% (most common psychiatric conditions)
- **Panic disorder:** Lifetime prevalence ~5%; mean onset mid-20s; women 2x more likely
- **ED visits:** Anxiety/panic account for ~2-5% of all ED visits. Up to 25% of patients presenting with chest pain have panic disorder
- **Comorbidity:** 60-70% comorbid with depression; high rates of substance use (self-medication with alcohol, benzodiazepines); other anxiety disorders commonly co-occur
- **Suicide risk:** Panic disorder alone modestly increases suicide risk; comorbid depression + panic significantly increases risk. Always screen for SI

---
sections.types-of-anxiety-disorders:
| Disorder | Key Feature | ED Relevance |
|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Panic Disorder** | Recurrent unexpected panic attacks + persistent worry about future attacks | Chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, dizziness — mimics cardiac/pulmonary emergency |
| **Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD)** | Excessive worry about multiple topics for ≥6 months | Chronic somatic complaints (headache, GI distress, insomnia, muscle tension) |
| **Social Anxiety Disorder** | Intense fear of social/performance situations | Rarely presents to ED specifically for this |
| **Specific Phobias** | Fear of specific objects/situations | May present to ED after avoidant behavior causes injury or crisis |
| **Agoraphobia** | Fear of situations where escape may be difficult | May refuse to come to ED; may panic in ED environment itself |
| **PTSD** | Re-experiencing, avoidance, hyperarousal after trauma | Flashbacks, hypervigilance, dissociation; triggered by ED environment (especially trauma patients). High comorbidity with SUD |

---
sections.panic-attack-clinical-features:
A panic attack is a **discrete episode of intense fear/discomfort** that peaks within minutes and includes ≥4 of the following (DSM-5):

| Symptom | Medical Mimic |
|---------|---------------|
| **Palpitations / racing heart** | SVT, atrial fibrillation, thyrotoxicosis |
| **Chest pain / tightness** | **MI, PE, aortic dissection, pneumothorax** |
| **Shortness of breath** | PE, asthma, pneumothorax, anaphylaxis |
| **Dizziness / lightheadedness** | Arrhythmia, hypoglycemia, stroke, orthostatic hypotension |
| **Trembling / shaking** | Thyroid storm, withdrawal, hypoglycemia |
