Recurrent pneumonia and progressive lung damage
Chronic *Pseudomonas aeruginosa* colonization (associated with worse outcomes)
Respiratory failure
Pulmonary hypertension and cor pulmonale (late)
Nontuberculous mycobacterial (NTM) infection

burns.title: Burns

sections.skin-anatomy-review-from-integumentary-s:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — thermal injury triggers the most dramatic inflammatory response in medicine: massive cytokine release (TNF, IL-1, IL-6) → systemic capillary leak → fluid shifts comparable to septic shock in large burns. Wound Healing — burn wound healing is depth-dependent: superficial (epidermis regenerates from keratinocytes), partial-thickness (heal from dermal appendages), full-thickness (require grafting — no epithelial source remains).*

Understanding burn depth requires knowing skin layers:
- **Epidermis** — avascular outer layer; barrier function; regenerates from hair follicles and wound edges
- **Dermis** — contains blood vessels, nerves, hair follicles, sweat glands; provides structural strength (collagen)
  - **Papillary dermis** (superficial) — thin, highly vascular
  - **Reticular dermis** (deep) — thick, dense collagen
- **Hypodermis/subcutaneous** — fat, larger vessels, fascia
- **Below:** Muscle, bone

---
sections.burn-depth-classification:
| Classification | Old Term | Depth | Appearance | Sensation | Healing |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Superficial** | 1st degree | Epidermis only | Red, dry, no blisters (sunburn) | **Painful** | 3-5 days, no scarring |
| **Superficial partial-thickness** | 2nd degree (superficial) | Epidermis + papillary dermis | Pink/red, moist, **blisters**, blanches briskly | **Very painful** (exposed nerve endings) | 7-14 days, minimal scarring |
| **Deep partial-thickness** | 2nd degree (deep) | Epidermis + reticular dermis | Pale, mottled, less moist, may have blisters, sluggish blanching | **Decreased** — nerve damage | 14-35 days, **significant scarring**, may need grafting |
| **Full-thickness** | 3rd degree | Through entire dermis | White, waxy, leathery, brown/black (eschar), dry, no blanching | **Absent** — nerves destroyed | Cannot self-heal — requires skin grafting |
| **4th degree** | — | Into fat, muscle, bone | Charred, exposed deep structures | Absent | Requires excision, reconstruction, possible amputation |

> [!important]
> **The most painful burns are superficial partial-thickness** — nerve endings are exposed but intact. Full-thickness burns are paradoxically painless in the burned area (nerves destroyed) but painful at the margins where partial-thickness injury transitions.

### Zone of Injury Model (Jackson's Burn Model)
| Zone | Description | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Zone of coagulation** | Central area; irreversible cell death | Cannot be salvaged |
| **Zone of stasis** | Surrounding area; decreased perfusion, potentially salvageable | **This is where resuscitation matters** — inadequate perfusion converts this to coagulation (deepening) |
| **Zone of hyperemia** | Outermost; inflammatory response, increased blood flow | Will recover unless complicated by infection or hypoperfusion |

---
sections.tbsa-estimation:
### Rule of Nines (Adults)
| Body Region | % TBSA |
|---|---|
| Head/neck | 9% |
| Each upper extremity | 9% |
| Anterior trunk | 18% |
| Posterior trunk | 18% |
| Each lower extremity | 18% |
| Perineum/genitalia | 1% |

### Lund-Browder Chart
- More accurate, especially for **pediatrics** (children have proportionally larger heads)
- Age-adjusted percentages — gold standard for TBSA estimation

### Palm Method
- Patient's palm (fingers together) ≈ **1% TBSA** — useful for scattered or irregular burns

> [!note]
> **Only count partial-thickness and full-thickness burns** in TBSA calculation. Superficial (1st degree) burns are NOT included in TBSA for resuscitation decisions.

---
sections.burn-etiology-and-special-considerations:
### Thermal Burns
- Most common type — flames, scalds (liquids/steam), contact (hot surface)
- **Scald burns in children** — consider non-accidental trauma (NAT):
  - Stocking/glove distribution, uniform depth, clear demarcation = immersion burn = abuse until proven otherwise
  - **Mandatory reporting**

### Inhalation Injury
- **Most common cause of death in fire-related burns**
- Three components:
  1. **Upper airway thermal injury** — heat damages supraglottic structures → edema → obstruction
  2. **Lower airway chemical injury** — products of combustion (not heat) damage bronchial epithelium → bronchospasm, cast formation
  3. **Systemic toxicity** — carbon monoxide (CO) and cyanide (HCN) poisoning

**Red flags for inhalation injury:**
- Enclosed space fire
- Facial burns, singed nasal hairs, singed eyebrows
- Soot in mouth/nose/sputum (carbonaceous sputum)
- Hoarseness, stridor, dyspnea
- Altered mental status

> [!critical]
> **Intubate EARLY if inhalation injury suspected** — airway edema progresses rapidly with fluid resuscitation. A patient who looks fine at presentation may have a completely obstructed airway 2-4 hours later. **If in doubt, intubate before the swelling makes it impossible.**

### Chemical Burns
- **Alkali burns** (lye, cement, drain cleaners) — worse than acid; cause **liquefactive necrosis** (ongoing tissue destruction)
- **Acid burns** — cause coagulative necrosis (self-limiting, protein coagulation creates barrier)
- **Treatment:** Immediate, copious water irrigation (minimum 20-30 minutes); do NOT try to neutralize
- **Hydrofluoric acid (HF)** — special case: fluoride ions penetrate deeply, bind calcium → **hypocalcemia, cardiac arrest**
  - Treatment: **topical calcium gluconate gel** + possible IV calcium; cardiac monitoring
  - Even small HF burns can be lethal

### Electrical Burns
- **Low voltage** (<1000V, household): typically local thermal injury at contact points
- **High voltage** (>1000V): devastating internal injury along current pathway
  - **Iceberg injury** — surface burns may look minor while massive deep tissue destruction occurs along current path
  - Current travels through **lowest resistance path:** nerves → blood vessels → muscle → skin → bone
  - Complications: rhabdomyolysis, compartment syndrome, cardiac arrhythmias, spinal cord injury
  - **ECG mandatory** for all significant electrical injuries
  - Monitor CK, myoglobin; aggressive hydration for rhabdomyolysis prevention
- **Lightning** — unique pattern; often cardiac arrest (asystole); "flashover" phenomenon means some survive with minimal external injury

---
sections.systemic-response-to-major-burns:
### Burn Shock (First 24-48 Hours)
Burns >20% TBSA trigger a **massive systemic inflammatory response**:
1. **Capillary leak** — inflammatory mediators (histamine, prostaglandins, complement) increase vascular permeability systemically (not just at burn site)
2. **Third-spacing** — massive fluid shifts from intravascular to interstitial space
3. **Hypovolemia** — despite no external blood loss, effective circulating volume drops dramatically
4. **Decreased cardiac output** — hypovolemia + myocardial depressant factors
5. **Tissue edema** — generalized, including non-burned tissue; worst at 12-24 hours

This is **distributive + hypovolemic shock** — and it's why burn resuscitation requires enormous fluid volumes.

### Hypermetabolic Response (Days to Months)
- Metabolic rate increases **1.5-2× normal** for large burns
- Catabolism → muscle wasting, hyperglycemia, immunosuppression
- Nutritional support is critical — early enteral feeding, high-protein/high-calorie diet
- Lasts until wounds are closed

---
sections.ed-management:
### Primary Survey (ATLS/ABCDE)
Burns patients are **trauma patients** — follow ATLS:
- **A — Airway:** Assess for inhalation injury; intubate early if ANY concern
- **B — Breathing:** 100% O₂ via NRB; assess for circumferential chest burns (may need escharotomy)
- **C — Circulation:** Two large-bore IVs (through burned skin if necessary); begin fluid resuscitation
- **D — Disability:** GCS, neuro exam; altered mental status → think CO/cyanide
- **E — Exposure:** Remove all clothing, jewelry, watches; stop the burning process; prevent hypothermia (burned patients lose thermoregulation)

### Fluid Resuscitation — Parkland Formula
**For burns ≥20% TBSA:**

$$4 \text{ mL} \times \text{kg body weight} \times \text{% TBSA} = \text{total crystalloid in first 24 hours}$$

- **First half** in the first 8 hours (from time of injury, not arrival)
- **Second half** over the next 16 hours
- Use **lactated Ringer's** (preferred over NS — less hyperchloremic acidosis)
- **Titrate to urine output:**
  - Adults: **0.5-1 mL/kg/hour**
  - Children: **1-1.5 mL/kg/hour**
  - Electrical burns/rhabdomyolysis: **1-2 mL/kg/hour** (prevent myoglobin precipitation)

> [!warning]
> **Parkland is a STARTING POINT, not a prescription.** Titrate to urine output. Over-resuscitation causes abdominal compartment syndrome, pulmonary edema, and orbital compartment syndrome ("fluid creep"). Under-resuscitation causes organ failure.

### Carbon Monoxide Poisoning
- **CO binds hemoglobin with 200-250× affinity of O₂** → carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) → tissue hypoxia
- **SpO₂ is unreliable** — pulse oximetry cannot distinguish COHb from oxyhemoglobin; reads falsely normal
- Diagnosis: **COHb level on co-oximetry** (arterial or venous blood gas)
- Treatment: **100% O₂ via NRB** (half-life of COHb: 4-5 hours on room air → 60-90 min on 100% O₂)
- Consider **hyperbaric oxygen** for: COHb >25%, loss of consciousness, pregnancy, persistent neurologic symptoms

### Cyanide Poisoning
- Generated from burning synthetics (plastics, wool, silk, polyurethane)
- Inhibits **cytochrome c oxidase** → blocks aerobic metabolism → lactic acidosis despite adequate O₂
- Clue: **persistent lactic acidosis despite adequate oxygenation and resuscitation**
- Treatment: **Hydroxocobalamin (Cyanokit)** 5 g IV — binds cyanide to form cyanocobalamin (vitamin B12)

### Escharotomy
- **Circumferential full-thickness burns** of extremities or chest → eschar is non-elastic → as edema develops, acts as a tourniquet
- **Extremity:** Compromised distal perfusion (absent pulses, increasing pain, paresthesias)
- **Chest:** Restricted ventilation (elevated peak pressures, inadequate tidal volumes)
- **Procedure:** Bedside incision through eschar down to subcutaneous fat along lateral aspects
- **This is a nursing-observed emergency** — if a circumferentially burned extremity loses pulses, escalate immediately

---
sections.wound-management:
### Initial Care
- **Cool running water** for thermal burns (10-20 minutes, within first 3 hours) — do NOT use ice
- Remove clothing, jewelry, constrictive items
- Cover with clean, dry dressings
- **Do NOT apply butter, toothpaste, or home remedies**
- **Do NOT debride blisters in the ED** (controversial, but generally leave intact blisters initially)

### Topical Agents
| Agent | Use | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Silver sulfadiazine (Silvadene)** | Traditional burn cream; broad-spectrum antimicrobial | Do NOT use on face; may impede re-epithelialization; sulfa allergy concern |
| **Bacitracin** | Superficial burns, face burns | Less antimicrobial potency; well-tolerated |
| **Mafenide acetate (Sulfamylon)** | Deep burns, cartilage (ears, nose) | Penetrates eschar; **painful on application**; carbonic anhydrase inhibitor (metabolic acidosis) |
| **Silver-impregnated dressings** | Modern alternative to Silvadene | Less frequent dressing changes; evidence-based |
| **Honey (medical-grade)** | Superficial partial-thickness | Antimicrobial, moist wound healing; growing evidence base |

### Burn Center Referral Criteria (ABA)
Transfer to a verified burn center for:
- Partial-thickness burns >10% TBSA
- Burns involving face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum, major joints
- Full-thickness (3rd degree) burns of any size
- Electrical burns (including lightning)
- Chemical burns
- Inhalation injury
- Burns with significant comorbidities
- Burns with associated trauma (if burn is the greater injury)
- Burns in children (if facility lacks pediatric expertise)
- Circumferential burns

---
sections.pain-management:
- Burns are among the **most painful injuries** — aggressive pain management is essential
- **Opioids** are first-line for moderate-severe burns: morphine, fentanyl, hydromorphone
- **Ketamine** — excellent for procedural pain (dressing changes, debridement); dissociative doses for major procedures
- **Anxiolytics** — midazolam for procedural anxiety
- **Acetaminophen/NSAIDs** — adjuncts for mild burns
- **Wound-specific:** adequate topical coverage reduces pain significantly
- Anticipate **breakthrough pain with dressing changes** — pre-medicate 30 minutes before

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment
- **Airway first** — voice changes, stridor, facial burns, soot → alert provider immediately
- **Accurate TBSA estimation** — directly drives resuscitation volumes; use Lund-Browder when possible
- **Burn depth assessment** — document appearance, blanching, sensation in each area
- **Neurovascular checks** — hourly for circumferential burns; pulses, cap refill, sensation, pain
- **Urine output monitoring** — Foley catheter for ≥20% TBSA; titrate fluids to target UO
- **Weight** — obtain early (needed for Parkland calculation)
- **Tetanus status** — update if needed

### Fluid Resuscitation Monitoring
- **Record I&Os meticulously** — this is the primary resuscitation endpoint
- Communicate UO trends to provider hourly
- Watch for over-resuscitation signs: increasing ventilator pressures, worsening edema, abdominal distension
- **Time zero = time of burn, NOT time of arrival** — calculate accordingly

### Wound Care
- Use sterile technique for all dressing changes
- Pre-medicate for pain before dressing changes
- Elevate burned extremities to reduce edema
- Maintain warm environment — **hypothermia is a major risk** (loss of skin barrier = loss of thermoregulation)
- Document wound appearance at each dressing change with photos when possible

### Psychosocial
- Burns are traumatic — both physically and psychologically
- Acute stress responses are normal
- Body image concerns, especially with visible burns
- Social work/chaplain involvement for major burns
- Screen for non-accidental trauma in children and vulnerable adults

---
sections.references:
- American Burn Association, "Advanced Burn Life Support (ABLS) Course," 2018
- Jeschke et al., "Burn Injury," Nature Reviews Disease Primers, 2020
- Cancio, "Initial Assessment and Fluid Resuscitation of Burn Patients," Surgical Clinics of North America, 2014
- Greenhalgh, "Management of Burns," New England Journal of Medicine, 2019

clinicalPearls: **Intubate early for inhalation injury** — airway edema worsens with resuscitation; the 2-hour window between "looks fine" and "can't intubate" is real and lethal
**SpO₂ lies in CO poisoning** — pulse ox reads COHb as oxyhemoglobin; a patient with 40% COHb will show SpO₂ of 99%; get co-oximetry
**Parkland is a GUIDE, urine output is the TARGET** — calculate Parkland, start the infusion, then adjust based on UO every hour; don't blindly run the calculated volume
**Circumferential burn + losing pulses = escharotomy NOW** — do not wait; this is a bedside procedure that saves limbs
**Electrical burns: the surface lies** — what you see on the skin is the tip of the iceberg; massive internal destruction follows the current path through muscle and nerves
**Stocking/glove burn in a child = abuse until proven otherwise** — clear demarcation lines, uniform depth, sparing of flexion creases = forced immersion; mandatory reporting
**Remove the rings before the swelling** — this takes 5 seconds at triage and prevents digit ischemia; burned hands swell enormously
**The zone of stasis is your resuscitation target** — adequate perfusion preserves this potentially salvageable tissue; poor resuscitation converts it to full-thickness injury (burn deepening)

cancer-associated-venous-thromboembolism.title: Cancer-Associated Venous Thromboembolism

sections.definition: Cancer-associated venous thromboembolism (VTE) encompasses deep vein thrombosis (DVT) and pulmonary embolism (PE) occurring in the setting of active malignancy. Cancer creates a hypercoagulable state through multiple mechanisms, making VTE one of the most common and serious complications of malignancy. VTE is the second leading cause of death in cancer patients (after the cancer itself) and affects ~4-20% of cancer patients during their disease course. The relationship is bidirectional: cancer causes VTE, and unprovoked VTE may be the first sign of occult malignancy.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** Cancer patients have 4-7x increased VTE risk (age-stratified RR 14.91 vs non-cancer population); VTE prevalence 4.6% in cancer patients vs 0.4% in non-cancer (Austrian population data); ~20% of all VTE events occur in cancer patients; VTE incidence varies by cancer type (highest in pancreatic, brain, gastric cancers); VTE is the #2 cause of death in ambulatory cancer patients; 12-month VTE incidence has risen from 1% (1997) to 3.4% (2017) in Danish registry data
- **Risk factors:**
  - **Cancer-specific:**
    - Highest risk cancers: Pancreatic (~15-20% VTE rate), stomach, brain (glioma), lung, ovarian, kidney, myeloma, lymphoma
    - Advanced stage > early stage
    - First 3-6 months after diagnosis (highest risk period)
    - Active/progressive disease > remission
  - **Treatment-related:**
    - Chemotherapy (cisplatin, thalidomide/lenalidomide, L-asparaginase, tamoxifen, bevacizumab)
    - Surgery (cancer surgery carries higher VTE risk than non-cancer surgery)
    - Central venous catheters (upper extremity DVT)
    - Radiation therapy
    - Hormonal therapy
    - Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESAs)
    - Immunotherapy (checkpoint inhibitors — cumulative VTE incidence ~12.9% over 8.5 months; up to 24% over ~14 months; VTE on immunotherapy associated with markedly worse survival: 11.6 vs 25.5 months median OS)
  - **Patient-related:**
    - Prior VTE
    - Immobility/hospitalization
    - Obesity
    - Older age
    - Poor performance status
- **Demographics:** Incidence increasing (better cancer survival → more time at risk; more aggressive chemotherapy regimens); thrombosis is a leading cause of emergency department visits in cancer patients

### Khorana Score — VTE Risk Prediction in Ambulatory Cancer Patients Starting Chemotherapy
| Risk Factor | Points |
|------------|--------|
| Very high-risk cancer site (stomach, pancreas) | 2 |
| High-risk cancer site (lung, lymphoma, gynecologic, bladder, testicular) | 1 |
| Pre-chemotherapy platelets ≥ 350,000 | 1 |
| Hemoglobin < 10 g/dL or use of ESAs | 1 |
| Pre-chemotherapy WBC > 11,000 | 1 |
| BMI ≥ 35 | 1 |

- Score 0 = Low risk (~0.3-1.5%); Score 1-2 = Intermediate (~1.8-4.7%); Score ≥ 3 = High (~6.7-12.9%)
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — cancer cells express tissue factor, cancer procoagulants, and PAI-1; tumor-derived microparticles activate the coagulation cascade, creating a hypercoagulable state (Virchow's triad: hypercoagulability + stasis + endothelial injury).*

Cancer promotes thrombosis through ALL elements of Virchow's Triad:

1. **Hypercoagulability (most important):**
   - Tumor cells produce **tissue factor (TF)** → directly activates extrinsic coagulation cascade → thrombin generation
   - Cancer procoagulant (CP) → directly activates Factor X
   - Tumor-derived extracellular vesicles with TF circulate systemically → widespread thrombotic potential; polyphosphate-positive vesicles activate Factor XII and platelets
   - **Podoplanin (PDPN):** Tumor-expressed protein released on extracellular vesicles → directly activates platelets (particularly relevant in brain tumors)
   - Mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas (pancreatic, GI) produce mucins that activate platelets and selectins → particularly high VTE risk
   - Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) formation promoted by tumor → additional prothrombotic mechanism
   - **PAI-1** (plasminogen activator inhibitor 1) released by tumors → inhibits fibrinolysis
   - Inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) from tumor and host → activate endothelium → pro-thrombotic surface
   - Decreased natural anticoagulants (protein C, protein S, antithrombin) in cancer

2. **Stasis:**
   - Tumor compression of vessels (pelvic tumors → iliac veins)
   - Immobility (hospitalization, poor functional status, debilitation)
   - Extrinsic compression from lymphadenopathy

3. **Endothelial injury:**
   - Central venous catheters (port-a-cath, PICC lines)
   - Chemotherapy-induced endothelial damage
   - Tumor invasion of vessel walls
   - Radiation-induced vascular injury

### Trousseau Syndrome
Migratory superficial thrombophlebitis classically associated with pancreatic and other mucin-secreting adenocarcinomas. May present as recurrent thrombosis in unusual locations (upper extremity, mesenteric, portal, cerebral veins) and may be refractory to anticoagulation. Can be the presenting sign of occult malignancy.
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Same as non-cancer VTE:**
  - DVT: Unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, erythema, Homan's sign (unreliable)
  - PE: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, tachypnea, hemoptysis, syncope, hypotension (massive PE)
  - See Pulmonary Embolism for detailed PE presentation
- **Cancer-specific presentations:**
  - **Unusual site thrombosis:** Upper extremity DVT (from central lines), portal/hepatic vein thrombosis, mesenteric vein thrombosis, cerebral sinus thrombosis — should prompt cancer workup if unprovoked
  - **Recurrent VTE despite anticoagulation** — hallmark of cancer-associated hypercoagulability
  - **Migratory thrombophlebitis** (Trousseau) — superficial veins; shifts locations
  - **Incidental PE** — found on staging CT scans in ~3-5% of cancer patients; clinically significant and requires treatment
  - **Catheter-related thrombosis** — upper extremity DVT at PICC or port site

### Complications
- Death (VTE is #2 cause of cancer death)
- Recurrent VTE (higher recurrence rate than non-cancer VTE even on anticoagulation)
- Post-thrombotic syndrome (chronic leg swelling, skin changes, ulceration)
- Anticoagulation-related bleeding (cancer patients bleed MORE AND clot MORE simultaneously — major management challenge)
- Treatment delays (VTE may delay/interrupt chemotherapy)
- Reduced quality of life
- Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **D-dimer** | Almost always elevated in cancer (inflammation, tumor activity) → LOW specificity; still useful as a negative predictive tool in low-risk scenarios |
| **CTPA** | Gold standard for PE diagnosis; same as non-cancer population |
| **Lower extremity duplex US** | First-line for DVT diagnosis |
| **CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast** | May reveal DVT in unusual locations (portal, mesenteric, IVC); staging CT may incidentally find PE |
| **CBC** | Thrombocytopenia (from chemo, DIC, marrow infiltration) complicates anticoagulation decisions |
| **Coagulation studies** | PT/INR, aPTT — baseline before anticoagulation; evaluate for DIC |
| **Liver function** | Hepatic metastases affect drug metabolism and bleeding risk |
| **Renal function** | Affects DOAC dosing and LMWH clearance |

### Cancer Screening in Unprovoked VTE
- An unprovoked VTE in a patient > 40-50 should raise concern for occult malignancy (~5-10% will be diagnosed with cancer within 1 year)
- **Recommended:** Age-appropriate cancer screening (colonoscopy, mammogram, PSA, etc.), CBC, BMP, LFTs, chest X-ray, urinalysis
- **Controversial:** Extensive CT screening (CT abdomen/pelvis) for occult malignancy — SOME-DVT trial showed earlier cancer detection but no mortality benefit
- Clinical judgment: Higher suspicion with bilateral DVT, unusual site thrombosis, recurrent VTE, or Trousseau syndrome
sections.management:
### Anticoagulation — Cancer-Specific Considerations

**Initial Treatment (First 5-10 days):**
| Agent | Details |
|-------|---------|
| LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg BID) | Traditional first-line for cancer VTE; reliable, injectable, no food interactions |
| Apixaban 10 mg BID × 7 days | Can be used as initial monotherapy (no heparin bridge needed); ADAM-VTE trial |
| Rivaroxaban 15 mg BID × 21 days | Can be used as initial monotherapy; SELECT-D, CALLISTO trials |
| UFH (if CrCl < 30 or need for procedural reversal) | aPTT-guided; more predictable offset for peri-procedural management |

**Long-term Treatment (3-6+ months):**
| Agent | Evidence | Key Considerations |
|-------|---------|-------------------|
| **LMWH (enoxaparin, dalteparin)** | CLOT trial (dalteparin); traditional standard of care; 50% VTE reduction vs. warfarin | Daily injections (adherence challenge); dose-adjustable; safe with most chemo |
| **Apixaban 5 mg BID** | Caravaggio trial: Non-inferior to dalteparin; NO increase in GI bleeding | Preferred DOAC for cancer VTE; avoids GI bleeding concern |
| **Edoxaban 60 mg daily** | Hokusai-VTE Cancer: Non-inferior to dalteparin; INCREASED GI bleeding in GI/GU cancers | Effective but AVOID in upper GI malignancies |
| **Rivaroxaban 20 mg daily** | SELECT-D: Non-inferior; increased GI bleeding in GI cancers | Same GI bleeding concern as edoxaban |
| **Warfarin** | INFERIOR to LMWH in cancer VTE (CLOT trial); difficult INR management with chemo | Generally AVOID; use only if LMWH and DOACs not feasible |

**KEY DECISION: LMWH vs. DOAC**
| Favor DOAC (apixaban preferred) | Favor LMWH |
|--------------------------------|------------|
| No active GI/GU malignancy | Active GI or GU cancer (upper GI especially) |
| Patient prefers oral medication | Drug interactions with chemotherapy |
| Good oral intake/absorption | GI issues (N/V, mucositis, malabsorption) |
| Low bleeding risk | Thrombocytopenia (dose-adjustable) |
| | Renal impairment (CrCl < 30 for most DOACs) |
| | Need for procedural interruption (predictable offset) |

**Duration:**
- **Minimum 3-6 months**; continue as long as cancer is active or being treated
- Extended/indefinite anticoagulation for patients with active malignancy, ongoing chemotherapy, or metastatic disease
- Reassess periodically (every 3-6 months) for risk-benefit of continued anticoagulation

### Special Situations
| Situation | Management |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Thrombocytopenia (platelets < 50,000)** | Hold or reduce-dose anticoagulation; therapeutic dose if platelets > 50,000; prophylactic dose if platelets 25-50,000; hold if < 25,000; IVC filter considered if acute VTE and cannot anticoagulate |
| **Brain metastases** | Anticoagulation generally safe (intracranial hemorrhage risk is low — ~3%); LMWH often preferred; melanoma and renal cell carcinoma brain mets have higher bleed risk |
| **Catheter-related DVT** | Anticoagulate × minimum 3 months; catheter can remain if functional and needed; remove if no longer needed, infected, or symptoms not improving |
| **Incidental PE on staging CT** | Treat same as symptomatic PE (same mortality risk); full anticoagulation |
| **Recurrent VTE on anticoagulation** | If on DOAC → switch to LMWH; if on LMWH → increase dose by 25%; consider IVC filter if truly refractory |
| **IVC filter** | Only if anticoagulation absolutely contraindicated (active hemorrhage, severe thrombocytopenia); retrievable filter preferred; resume anticoagulation as soon as safely possible |

### VTE Prophylaxis in Cancer Patients
| Setting | Prophylaxis |
|---------|------------|
| **Hospitalized cancer patients** | Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg daily or UFH) + mechanical (SCDs); continue until discharge |
| **Post-cancer surgery** | Extended prophylaxis × 4 weeks post-abdominal/pelvic cancer surgery (LMWH) — higher VTE risk than non-cancer surgery |
| **Ambulatory chemotherapy** | Consider prophylaxis if Khorana score ≥ 2 (rivaroxaban 10 mg daily or apixaban 2.5 mg BID — CASSINI, AVERT trials); discuss with oncologist |
| **Multiple myeloma on IMiDs** | Aspirin (low-risk) or LMWH/DOAC (high-risk) with lenalidomide/thalidomide regimens |

### Pharmacology
- **Anticoagulants** — LMWH (enoxaparin, dalteparin) or DOACs (rivaroxaban, apixaban, edoxaban) for treatment and secondary prevention; DOACs avoid need for injections but higher GI bleeding in GI/GU cancers
- **Antiplatelet Agents** — aspirin for low-risk myeloma prophylaxis; NOT a substitute for anticoagulation in VTE treatment
- **Reversal Agents** — andexanet alfa (DOAC reversal), protamine (heparin reversal) for life-threatening bleeding
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - VTE screening at every visit/admission: Leg circumference, swelling, pain, Homan's sign (low sensitivity but still assessed)
  - Respiratory: New dyspnea, tachycardia, pleuritic chest pain → think PE
  - Catheter sites: Swelling, pain, difficulty flushing PICC/port (catheter-related thrombosis)
  - Bleeding assessment (concurrent risk): Stool guaiac, urine dipstick, gum bleeding, bruising, hemoptysis
  - Platelet count trending: Chemotherapy-induced thrombocytopenia affects anticoagulation dosing
  - Renal function: Affects LMWH clearance and DOAC dosing
- **Medication administration:**
  - LMWH: SubQ injection technique; rotate injection sites (abdomen preferred); do NOT expel air bubble from prefilled syringe; do NOT rub site after injection
  - DOACs: Rivaroxaban MUST be taken with food (bioavailability drops ~40% without); apixaban can be taken with or without food; educate on missed dose protocols
  - Monitor for drug interactions with chemotherapy (CYP3A4/P-gp interactions with DOACs)
- **Patient teaching:**
  - Signs of DVT/PE: When to go to ED immediately (sudden dyspnea, chest pain, new severe leg swelling)
  - Anticoagulation compliance is critical — missed doses in cancer VTE have higher recurrence risk than non-cancer VTE
  - Self-injection teaching for LMWH (many patients/caregivers do this at home for months)
  - Bleeding precautions: Soft toothbrush, electric razor, avoid contact sports, fall prevention
  - Medical alert information: Carry anticoagulant card; inform all healthcare providers
  - When to hold medication: Surgery, procedures, active bleeding → contact oncology team
  - VTE can recur even ON anticoagulation — report new symptoms immediately
- **Coordination of care:**
  - Communicate with oncology team about anticoagulation management around chemotherapy cycles
  - Thrombocytopenia management: Know the institution's protocol for dose adjustment/holding
  - Peri-procedural bridging: Coordinate with interventional teams for procedures/biopsies
  - Pharmacy collaboration: DOAC-chemotherapy interaction screening

clinicalPearls: **Cancer patients simultaneously have the highest clotting AND highest bleeding risk of any patient population.** This makes every anticoagulation decision a careful balance. There is no risk-free option — both treating and not treating VTE carry significant danger.

**Apixaban is emerging as the preferred DOAC for cancer-associated VTE.** The Caravaggio trial showed it was non-inferior to dalteparin WITHOUT the increased GI bleeding seen with rivaroxaban and edoxaban. For patients without GI/GU malignancy, apixaban offers the convenience of oral dosing with excellent safety.

**AVOID DOACs in active upper GI or GU cancers.** Rivaroxaban and edoxaban showed significantly higher GI bleeding rates in patients with luminal GI cancers (SELECT-D, Hokusai-VTE Cancer trials). Use LMWH for these patients. Apixaban appears safer but caution is still warranted.

**Warfarin is the WORST choice for cancer VTE.** The CLOT trial definitively showed LMWH was superior. Warfarin INR is nearly impossible to manage with chemotherapy (N/V, drug interactions, liver dysfunction, variable vitamin K intake). It should only be used when LMWH and DOACs are truly not options.

**An unprovoked VTE may be the first sign of cancer.** About 5-10% of patients with unprovoked VTE will be diagnosed with cancer within 1 year. Age-appropriate cancer screening is recommended. The cancers most commonly found: lung, GI, pancreatic, GU, hematologic.

**Autopsy data reveal hidden PE burden:** Among cancer patients who died, PE prevalence on autopsy was 12.4% (highest in pancreatic cancer at 19.3%); PE was the primary cause of death in 37.6% and a contributing factor in 29.3% of these cases. The true thrombotic burden of cancer is likely underestimated clinically.

**VTE is an independent marker of aggressive disease.** The strong association between VTE and decreased survival persists even after adjusting for disease stage and treatment — suggesting that VTE may reflect a more aggressive tumor biology rather than just a complication.

**Incidental PE found on staging CT scans should be TREATED.** ~3-5% of cancer staging CTs incidentally find PE. These carry similar morbidity and mortality to symptomatic PE and require full anticoagulation. Don't ignore them.

**SCDs and VTE prophylaxis in hospitalized cancer patients save lives.** The thromboprophylaxis rate in hospitalized cancer patients remains suboptimal. Ensure SCDs are applied and functioning AND pharmacologic prophylaxis is ordered. This is a core nursing responsibility.

signs: **Same as non-cancer VTE:**
DVT: Unilateral leg swelling, pain, warmth, erythema, Homan's sign (unreliable)
PE: Sudden dyspnea, pleuritic chest pain, tachycardia, tachypnea, hemoptysis, syncope, hypotension (massive PE)
See Pulmonary Embolism for detailed PE presentation
**Cancer-specific presentations:**

**Unusual site thrombosis:** Upper extremity DVT (from central lines), portal/hepatic vein thrombosis, mesenteric vein thrombosis, cerebral sinus thrombosis — should prompt cancer workup if unprovoked
**Recurrent VTE despite anticoagulation** — hallmark of cancer-associated hypercoagulability
**Migratory thrombophlebitis** (Trousseau) — superficial veins; shifts locations
**Incidental PE** — found on staging CT scans in ~3-5% of cancer patients; clinically significant and requires treatment
**Catheter-related thrombosis** — upper extremity DVT at PICC or port site
Death (VTE is #2 cause of cancer death)
Recurrent VTE (higher recurrence rate than non-cancer VTE even on anticoagulation)
Post-thrombotic syndrome (chronic leg swelling, skin changes, ulceration)
Anticoagulation-related bleeding (cancer patients bleed MORE AND clot MORE simultaneously — major management challenge)
Treatment delays (VTE may delay/interrupt chemotherapy)
Reduced quality of life
Chronic thromboembolic pulmonary hypertension (CTEPH)

cardiac-arrest.title: Cardiac Arrest

sections.definition:
Cardiac arrest is the abrupt cessation of effective cardiac mechanical activity, resulting in the absence of a palpable pulse and loss of consciousness. Without immediate intervention — high-quality CPR and defibrillation when indicated — death follows within minutes.

Cardiac arrest is NOT a diagnosis. It is the **final common pathway** of many diseases. The arrest rhythm tells you WHAT the heart is doing; the H's and T's help you figure out WHY.

**Related notes:** Code Blue Management · Electricity and Excitability · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Antiarrhythmics · Vasopressors and Inotropes

> *First principles: Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability · Electricity and Excitability*

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Out-of-hospital cardiac arrest (OHCA):** ~350,000/year in the US; survival to hospital discharge ~10%
- **In-hospital cardiac arrest (IHCA):** ~290,000/year; survival to discharge ~25%
- **Peak incidence:** Males > females; increases with age; peak 60-80 years
- **Most common initial rhythm (OHCA):** Asystole/PEA (~75%), VF/pVT (~25%) — VF/pVT proportion has DECREASED as bystander AED use catches early VF before EMS arrival
- **Most common initial rhythm (IHCA):** PEA/asystole (~80%), VF/pVT (~20%)
- **Survival factors:** Bystander CPR doubles survival; AED use within 3-5 minutes → survival >50%; every minute without CPR/defib decreases survival 7-10%

---
sections.arrest-rhythms-the-four-presentations:
### Shockable Rhythms

| Rhythm | ECG | Mechanism | Prognosis |
|--------|-----|-----------|-----------|
| **Ventricular Fibrillation (VF)** | Chaotic, irregular, no discernible QRS complexes. Coarse (high amplitude) → fine (low amplitude) over time | Multiple disorganized re-entry circuits in ventricles → no coordinated contraction → no cardiac output | Best prognosis IF defibrillated early. Coarse VF > fine VF in terms of defibrillation success |
| **Pulseless Ventricular Tachycardia (pVT)** | Wide-complex, regular, >100 bpm, no pulse | Organized ventricular re-entry circuit → ineffective cardiac output at very high rates | Good prognosis if defibrillated promptly. Often degenerates to VF if untreated |

### Non-Shockable Rhythms

| Rhythm | ECG | Mechanism | Prognosis |
|--------|-----|-----------|-----------|
| **Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA)** | Organized electrical activity on monitor but NO palpable pulse | Heart has electrical activity but can't generate mechanical contraction — ALWAYS has an underlying cause | Variable — depends on identifying and treating the cause. Better if narrow-complex, slower rate |
| **Asystole** | Flat line (confirm in 2 leads — rule out fine VF and lead disconnect) | No electrical activity. Complete cardiac standstill | Worst prognosis. Usually represents prolonged arrest or end-stage deterioration from other rhythms |

> **Key distinction:** Shockable rhythms (VF/pVT) = defibrillation can work because organized rhythm can be restored. Non-shockable (PEA/asystole) = electrical shock won't help because the problem isn't a disorganized rhythm — it's either no rhythm or a rhythm that can't generate output. Find and fix the CAUSE.

---
sections.the-hs-and-ts-reversible-causes:
For EVERY cardiac arrest, especially PEA and asystole, systematically consider reversible causes:

### The H's

| Cause | How to Identify | How to Treat |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| **Hypovolemia** | History (trauma, GI bleed, ruptured AAA), flat neck veins, narrow-complex PEA | Volume resuscitation — crystalloid, then blood products. Activate MTP for hemorrhagic causes |
| **Hypoxia** | History (respiratory failure, drowning, airway obstruction), cyanosis | Secure airway, ventilate with 100% O2, confirm ET tube placement with waveform capnography |
| **Hydrogen ion (acidosis)** | ABG: pH <7.1, known DKA/renal failure/toxic ingestion | Sodium bicarbonate 1 mEq/kg IV; treat underlying cause (Diabetic Ketoacidosis, Acute Kidney Injury) |
| **Hypo/Hyperkalemia** | Labs (if available), ECG clues (peaked T waves = hyperK; U waves = hypoK), renal failure history | HyperK: Calcium chloride 1g IV, insulin + glucose, bicarb. HypoK: KCl 40 mEq IV (faster than usual) |
| **Hypothermia** | Core temp <30°C, cold exposure history, cold skin | Active rewarming. **Continue CPR until rewarmed to 32°C** — "not dead until warm and dead." Modified ACLS intervals |

### The T's

| Cause | How to Identify | How to Treat |
|-------|----------------|-------------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** | Unilateral absent breath sounds, tracheal deviation, distended neck veins, history of trauma/ventilation | Needle decompression (2nd ICS, MCL or 5th ICS, AAL) → chest tube |
| **Tamponade (cardiac)** | Beck's triad (muffled hearts, JVD, hypotension), narrow pulse pressure, electrical alternans on ECG, trauma history | Pericardiocentesis or ED thoracotomy (trauma) |
| **Toxins** | History of ingestion, toxidrome, pill bottles at scene | Specific antidotes: naloxone (opioids), sodium bicarb (TCAs/Na+ channel blockers), glucagon (beta-blockers/CCBs), intralipid (local anesthetic toxicity), digoxin Fab fragments |
| **Thrombosis — Coronary** | History (chest pain before arrest), ST changes on monitor, known CAD | Consider emergent PCI if ROSC with STEMI. During arrest: standard ACLS; no role for thrombolytics empirically for suspected coronary |
| **Thrombosis — Pulmonary** | History (immobilization, DVT, recent surgery), RV dilation on bedside echo, distended neck veins with clear lungs | Systemic tPA (alteplase 50 mg IV bolus) during CPR if high clinical suspicion. Continue CPR for 15-30 min after tPA |

> **PEA = think H's and T's.** PEA almost always has a treatable cause. If you can identify and reverse it, the patient can survive. The most common reversible causes: hypovolemia, hypoxia, tension pneumothorax, tamponade, PE, hyperkalemia.

---
sections.acls-algorithms:
### Cardiac Arrest Algorithm — Universal

```
Unresponsive, no pulse
        ↓
   BEGIN CPR
   Attach monitor/defibrillator
        ↓
   ┌─────────────────────────────────┐
   │        RHYTHM CHECK             │
   └────────┬────────────────┬───────┘
            ↓                ↓
      VF / pVT          PEA / Asystole
    (SHOCKABLE)        (NON-SHOCKABLE)
            ↓                ↓
       SHOCK              CPR 2 min
     (200J biphasic)      Epinephrine 1mg IV
            ↓              q3-5 min ASAP
       CPR 2 min               ↓
            ↓              RHYTHM CHECK
       RHYTHM CHECK             ↓
            ↓              Still PEA/Asystole?
     Still VF/pVT?         → CPR 2 min
       → SHOCK              → Epi q3-5 min
       → CPR 2 min          → Treat H's & T's
       → Epi 1mg IV         → Consider advanced
       → Consider             airway
         Amiodarone
         300mg IV
            ↓
       RHYTHM CHECK
            ↓
     Still VF/pVT?
       → SHOCK
       → CPR 2 min
       → Amiodarone
         150mg IV
       → Treat H's & T's
```

### Key Medications During Arrest

| Drug | Dose | When | Mechanism |
|------|------|------|-----------|
| **Epinephrine** | 1 mg IV/IO q3-5 min | All arrest rhythms. Give ASAP for PEA/asystole. After 2nd shock for VF/pVT | Alpha-1 → vasoconstriction → ↑ coronary perfusion pressure during CPR |
| **Amiodarone** | 300 mg IV first dose, 150 mg IV second dose | Refractory VF/pVT (after 3rd shock) | Class III antiarrhythmic — prolongs refractory period |
| **Lidocaine** | 1-1.5 mg/kg IV first dose, 0.5-0.75 mg/kg subsequent | Alternative to amiodarone for refractory VF/pVT | Class IB antiarrhythmic — Na+ channel blockade |
| **Calcium chloride** | 1 g (10 mL of 10%) IV | Suspected hyperkalemia, hypocalcemia, Ca2+ channel blocker OD | Membrane stabilization (see Electricity and Excitability) |
| **Sodium bicarbonate** | 1 mEq/kg IV | Known/suspected severe acidosis, hyperkalemia, TCA OD | Buffers H+; alkalinizes blood to shift K+ intracellularly |
| **Magnesium sulfate** | 1-2 g IV | Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT with prolonged QT) | Stabilizes myocardial membranes |

> **No vasopressin, no atropine in current ACLS.** Vasopressin was removed as an alternative to epinephrine (no benefit). Atropine was removed from the cardiac arrest algorithm (no evidence of benefit in asystole/PEA). Atropine IS still used for symptomatic bradycardia (not arrest).

---
sections.high-quality-cpr-the-foundation:
The SINGLE most important intervention in cardiac arrest is **high-quality chest compressions.** Everything else is secondary.

| Parameter | Target |
|-----------|--------|
| **Rate** | 100-120 compressions/min |
| **Depth** | At least 2 inches (5 cm), no more than 2.4 inches (6 cm) |
| **Recoil** | Allow FULL chest recoil between compressions — don't lean |
| **Interruptions** | Minimize ALL pauses. <10 seconds for rhythm checks. Pre-charge defibrillator during compressions |
| **Rotation** | Switch compressors every 2 minutes (fatigue degrades quality within 1-2 min even if the compressor doesn't feel tired) |
| **Ventilation ratio** | 30:2 (no advanced airway) or continuous compressions with 1 breath q6 sec (with advanced airway) |
| **ETCO2 target** | >10 mmHg during CPR indicates adequate compressions and some cardiac output. <10 = poor perfusion, consider compression quality |

> **Waveform capnography during CPR is the single best real-time quality indicator.** ETCO2 correlates with cardiac output during CPR. If ETCO2 drops → compression quality has degraded → switch compressors. A sudden rise in ETCO2 to >40 mmHg → strongly suggests ROSC before a pulse check.

---
sections.post-cardiac-arrest-care-post-rosc:
When a pulse returns, the work is NOT over. Post-arrest care directly impacts neurological outcome.

### Immediate Post-ROSC Priorities

| Priority | Action | Rationale |
|----------|--------|-----------|
| **Airway** | Secure airway if not already intubated. Confirm ET tube position. Waveform capnography | Maintain oxygenation/ventilation |
| **Oxygenation** | Target SpO2 92-98%. Titrate FiO2 DOWN from 100% once ROSC confirmed | Hyperoxia causes oxidative injury to vulnerable brain tissue (ischemia-reperfusion injury) |
| **Ventilation** | Target PaCO2 35-45 mmHg. Avoid hyperventilation | Hypocapnia → cerebral vasoconstriction → worsened brain ischemia |
| **Hemodynamics** | Target MAP ≥65 mmHg (consider ≥80 for neurological benefit). Vasopressors/fluids as needed | Adequate cerebral perfusion pressure for brain recovery |
| **12-lead ECG** | Obtain immediately post-ROSC | Identify STEMI → emergent cath lab |
| **Targeted Temperature Management** | Cool to 32-36°C for ≥24 hours if patient remains comatose | Neuroprotection — slows ischemia-reperfusion injury, reduces cerebral O2 demand |
| **Glucose** | Avoid hypoglycemia and marked hyperglycemia. Target 140-180 mg/dL | Both hypo- and hyperglycemia worsen neurological outcomes |
| **Seizure monitoring** | Continuous EEG if available; treat seizures aggressively | Post-arrest seizures are common and worsen brain injury |

### Targeted Temperature Management (TTM)

- **Who:** All comatose patients after ROSC (GCS motor <6) regardless of initial rhythm
- **Target:** 32-36°C for at least 24 hours (TTM2 trial showed no difference between 33°C and 36°C; avoid fever >37.5°C)
- **Methods:** Surface cooling (Arctic Sun), intravascular cooling (Thermogard), cold saline infusion (adjunct, not sole method)
- **Avoid:** Shivering (counterproductive — increases O2 demand). Treat with sedation, buspirone, meperidine, or neuromuscular blockade if refractory
- **Rewarming:** Slow — 0.25°C/hr. Rapid rewarming causes rebound cerebral edema

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### During the Code
- See Code Blue Management for detailed team roles and workflow
- **Focus on compression quality** — this is the ONE thing that saves lives
- **Minimize interruptions** — pre-charge the defibrillator, have pads in place, keep pauses <10 sec
- **Track time** — announce 2-minute cycles, drug timing, total code duration
- **Push epi early for PEA/asystole** — don't wait for a rhythm check cycle
- **Think H's and T's** — if the rhythm isn't converting, the team needs to broaden the differential
- **Communicate clearly** — closed-loop communication for every order and intervention

### Post-ROSC
- **Continuous monitoring** — arterial line if available (most accurate BP), waveform capnography, continuous SpO2
- **Re-arrest readiness** — keep pads on, defibrillator at bedside, drugs drawn up. 30-50% of ROSC patients re-arrest
- **Temperature monitoring** — esophageal or bladder probe. Avoid fever aggressively even if not doing active cooling
- **Neuroprognostication** — DO NOT prognosticate within 72 hours post-arrest (minimum). Brain recovery takes time. Premature withdrawal of care based on early neuro exam is a leading cause of preventable death post-arrest
- **Family communication** — social work, chaplain. Honest but hopeful framing: "We got their heart back. The next 72 hours will tell us about brain recovery."

---
sections.special-situations:
### Pregnancy (Perimortem C-Section)
- Left uterine displacement throughout resuscitation (manual or with wedge)
- If no ROSC by **4-5 minutes** of CPR in a visibly pregnant patient (>20 weeks) → **perimortem cesarean delivery** at bedside
- Goal: delivery within 5 minutes of arrest onset — improves both maternal and fetal survival
- Maternal resuscitation continues during and after delivery

### Drowning
- Prioritize oxygenation and ventilation (primary problem is hypoxia)
- 5 rescue breaths before compressions (modification from standard ACLS)
- Hypothermia is common → continue CPR until rewarmed
- C-spine precautions if diving/unknown mechanism

### Hypothermia
- Continue CPR until core temp ≥32°C — "not dead until warm and dead"
- Withhold epinephrine until temp >30°C (medications ineffective in severe hypothermia)
- Space medications to q6-10 min intervals between 30-35°C
- Consider cardiopulmonary bypass/ECMO for rewarming in refractory arrest

### Opioid-Associated Arrest
- Standard ACLS takes priority — CPR + airway management
- Give naloxone 2 mg IV/IO (or IN if no access) — but naloxone does NOT replace CPR
- Primary mechanism of death is respiratory arrest → hypoxia → cardiac arrest. Address the airway.
- See Opioid Overdose

---
sections.termination-of-resuscitation:
No universal protocol, but consider termination when:
- Prolonged arrest (>30 min of ACLS) without ROSC
- No shockable rhythm at any point
- No reversible cause identified
- ETCO2 persistently <10 mmHg despite high-quality CPR (indicates no cardiac output despite interventions)
- Patient/family has documented goals of care limiting resuscitation

> **The decision to stop is a physician decision made in consultation with the team.** As the nurse, document meticulously — total CPR time, all interventions, all rhythm checks, the final discussion, and time of death.

---
sections.references:
- AHA ACLS Guidelines (2020 update)
- Panchal AR, et al: 2020 AHA Guidelines for CPR and ECC. Circulation. 2020;142(suppl 2)
- Dankiewicz J, et al: Hypothermia versus Normothermia after Out-of-Hospital Cardiac Arrest (TTM2). NEJM 2021
- Soar J, et al: European Resuscitation Council Guidelines 2021: Adult Advanced Life Support

---

*See also: Code Blue Management · Electricity and Excitability · Antiarrhythmics · Vasopressors and Inotropes · Cardiogenic Shock · Hypovolemic Shock · Obstructive Shock · Distributive Shock · Opioid Overdose · Hyperkalemia · Pulmonary Embolism · Cardiac Tamponade*

clinicalPearls: **Push hard, push fast, don't stop.** Compression quality is the single biggest determinant of survival. Everything else — drugs, intubation, IV access — is secondary. If you're not compressing, the patient is dying.

**ETCO2 is your CPR report card.** Waveform capnography during CPR tells you if compressions are generating cardiac output (ETCO2 >10). A sudden spike to >40 mmHg = check for ROSC. This is more reliable than pulse checks.

**PEA is not a rhythm — it's a symptom.** PEA means "something is preventing the heart from generating output despite electrical activity." Your job is to figure out WHAT. Run through H's and T's every cycle.
**VF doesn't stay VF forever.** Coarse VF degenerates to fine VF, then to asystole over minutes. Early defibrillation catches VF when it's most treatable. This is why public-access AEDs save lives.

**Epi is for coronary perfusion, not for "restarting the heart."** During CPR, epinephrine's alpha-1 effect constricts peripheral vessels → redirects blood to the coronary arteries → improves the chance that defibrillation will restore a perfusing rhythm. It doesn't restart anything.

**Don't prognosticate at the bedside during the code.** And don't prognosticate for 72 hours after ROSC. Patients have meaningful recovery days after arrest. Premature withdrawal of care is an iatrogenic cause of death.

cardiac-tamponade.title: Cardiac Tamponade

sections.definition: Cardiac tamponade is a life-threatening condition in which fluid accumulation in the pericardial space compresses the heart, impairing diastolic filling and reducing cardiac output. The pericardium normally contains 15-50 mL of fluid. Tamponade physiology depends not just on volume but on the RATE of accumulation — rapid accumulation of even 100-200 mL can cause tamponade, while slow accumulation (malignancy) can tolerate 1-2 L before becoming hemodynamically significant.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** Incidence ~0.05% of emergency admissions in the US (115,638 cases among 216 million admissions, 2008-2014); ~2% of penetrating chest trauma; ~15% of large pericardial effusions progress to tamponade; annual pericardial effusion incidence ~20/100,000; in-hospital mortality 14.3%
- **Etiological breakdown (US data):** CKD (18.4%), cancer (17.0%), sepsis (10.6%), connective tissue diseases (4.0%), idiopathic pericarditis (3.7%), chest trauma (2.1%); no significant sex difference (46.5% female)
- **Mortality predictors:** Chest trauma, acute kidney injury, metastatic cancer, and sepsis
- **Risk factors/Causes:**
  - **Traumatic:** Penetrating chest trauma (stab wound, GSW), blunt trauma, cardiac surgery, catheter-based procedures (perforation), post-CPR
  - **Medical:** Malignancy (lung, breast, lymphoma — most common cause of large effusions), pericarditis (viral, uremic, autoimmune, TB), acute MI (free wall rupture), aortic dissection (type A extending into pericardium), hypothyroidism (myxedema), post-cardiac surgery (Dressler syndrome)
  - **Iatrogenic:** Central line placement, pacemaker insertion, cardiac catheterization, ablation procedures
- **Demographics:** Any age (mean ~62 in US data); traumatic more common in young adults; malignant effusions in older adults; racial distribution: white 63.4%, Black 12.6%, Hispanic 7.4%
  - **Autoimmune:** SLE-related pericardial effusion in ~16.4% of SLE patients; tamponade in ~1.5-5.9% of SLE patients; higher mortality with effusion (30.1% vs 11.3%)
  - **Iatrogenic:** AF ablation → tamponade in ~0.84% (higher risk in women); risk lower at high-volume centers
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — pericardial pressure exceeds ventricular filling pressure → preload falls → SV falls → CO falls; this is obstructive shock from mechanical restriction. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — Beck's triad (hypotension, JVD, muffled heart sounds) reflects compensatory mechanisms: tachycardia and venoconstriction maintain BP temporarily until decompensation. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — pulsus paradoxus is the hemodynamic signature of tamponade: exaggerated drop in SBP with inspiration as RV fills at LV's expense in the constrained pericardial space.*

1. **Pericardial fluid accumulation** → increased intrapericardial pressure
2. **When intrapericardial pressure exceeds intracardiac filling pressures** → compression of cardiac chambers (right-sided chambers compress first due to lower pressures)
3. **Impaired diastolic filling** → decreased ventricular preload → decreased stroke volume → decreased cardiac output
4. **Compensatory mechanisms:** Tachycardia (to maintain CO), peripheral vasoconstriction (to maintain BP), increased venous pressure (to overcome pericardial pressure)
5. **Decompensation:** When compensatory mechanisms are exhausted → profound hypotension → cardiogenic shock → PEA arrest → death
6. **Pulsus paradoxus:** Exaggerated inspiratory decrease in systolic BP (> 10 mmHg); mechanism: inspiration increases venous return to RV → interventricular septum bows leftward (due to limited pericardial space) → reduces LV filling → drops SBP
7. **"Last-drop" phenomenon:** Cardiac tamponade follows an exponential pressure-volume curve — the last increment of pericardial fluid causes critical compression, and the first decrement during drainage produces the greatest relative decompression; the right ventricle collapses before the left (thinner wall, lower intracavitary pressure)

### Rate vs. Volume
| Accumulation | Volume Tolerated | Why |
|-------------|-----------------|-----|
| **Rapid** (minutes-hours) | 100-200 mL causes tamponade | Pericardium is non-distensible acutely; no time to stretch |
| **Slow** (weeks-months) | 1-2 L may be tolerated | Pericardium gradually stretches to accommodate |
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms — Beck's Triad (Classic but NOT Always Present)
1. **Hypotension** (low cardiac output)
2. **Jugular venous distension (JVD)** (impaired venous return to compressed heart)
3. **Muffled/distant heart sounds** (fluid insulates sounds)

> Beck's triad is present in < 50% of cases. Don't wait for all three.

**Other findings:**
- Tachycardia (compensatory — most reliable early sign)
- **Pulsus paradoxus** > 10 mmHg (SBP drops > 10 mmHg during inspiration); measure with manual BP cuff or arterial line
- Dyspnea, tachypnea
- Anxiety, restlessness
- Cool, clammy extremities
- Narrowed pulse pressure
- Kussmaul sign (paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration) — more specific for constrictive pericarditis but can occur
- **Electrical alternans on ECG** (alternating QRS amplitude — swinging heart in fluid)
- PEA arrest (tamponade is a reversible cause of PEA — always consider in cardiac arrest)

### Complications
- Cardiogenic Shock
- PEA cardiac arrest
- Death (rapidly fatal if untreated)
- Recurrence (especially malignant effusions)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Findings |
|------|---------|
| **Echocardiogram** (DIAGNOSTIC — do immediately if suspected) | Pericardial effusion; RA/RV diastolic collapse (most specific sign); IVC plethora (dilated, no inspiratory collapse); respiratory variation in mitral/tricuspid inflow velocities |
| **POCUS (bedside echo)** | First-line in ED/ICU; can diagnose in < 2 minutes; subxiphoid view |
| **ECG** | Low voltage QRS; electrical alternans; sinus tachycardia; diffuse ST elevation if concurrent pericarditis |
| **Chest X-ray** | "Water bottle" silhouette (enlarged cardiac silhouette if large effusion); may be normal in acute tamponade |
| **CT chest** | Not first-line for acute tamponade; useful for identifying cause (malignancy, aortic dissection) |
| **Pericardial fluid analysis** | After pericardiocentesis: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, gram stain/culture, cytology, adenosine deaminase (TB) |

> **Clinical diagnosis in hemodynamic instability.** Do NOT delay treatment to obtain imaging in a crashing patient — bedside echo → pericardiocentesis.

### ESC Triage Scoring System (Score ≥ 6 → Immediate Drainage)
| Category | Criteria (Points) |
|----------|------------------|
| **Etiology** | Iatrogenic hemopericardium (2), malignancy (2), TB (2), recent radiotherapy (1), recent viral infection (1), recurrent effusion/prior pericardiocentesis (1), CKD (1), immunodeficiency (1), type A aortic dissection with hemopericardium (6), free wall rupture post-MI (3), severe trauma with hemopericardium (3) |
| **Clinical** | Orthopnea without rales (3), pulsus paradoxus >10 mmHg (2), rapid worsening symptoms (2), hypotension SBP <95 (1), dyspnea/tachypnea (1), distended jugular veins (1), sinus tachycardia (1), oliguria (1) |
| **Imaging** | Large effusion >2 cm (3), RV diastolic collapse (3), RA collapse (2), IVC plethora (1), electrical alternans (1), low QRS voltage (1) |
sections.management:
### Acute — This Is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY

**Temporizing Measures (while preparing for drainage):**
- IV fluid bolus (500-1000 mL NS) — increases preload to overcome pericardial pressure; buys time
- Avoid vasodilators (nitroglycerin), diuretics, and positive pressure ventilation (all reduce preload → worsens tamponade)
- Keep patient upright (reduces pericardial pressure on heart)
- Prepare for emergent pericardiocentesis

**Definitive Treatment:**
| Procedure | Indication | Details |
|-----------|-----------|---------|
| **Pericardiocentesis** (needle drainage) | First-line in medical tamponade | Subxiphoid approach under echo guidance (or fluoroscopy); aspirate fluid; send for analysis; can leave pigtail catheter for continued drainage |
| **Surgical pericardial window** | Recurrent effusions (malignancy), traumatic tamponade, loculated effusions | Creates permanent drainage pathway into pleural space or peritoneum |
| **Emergent thoracotomy** | Traumatic tamponade (penetrating injury) with cardiac arrest or hemodynamic collapse | ED thoracotomy if penetrating trauma + loss of vital signs within 10-15 min |

### Post-Drainage
- Monitor for reaccumulation (serial echo)
- Treat underlying cause (malignancy → oncology, chemo, radiation; infection → antibiotics; uremia → dialysis; autoimmune → immunosuppression)
- If malignant: Consider pericardial window, intrapericardial chemotherapy, or sclerotherapy for recurrent effusions
- **Pharmacological prevention of recurrence:** Colchicine and NSAIDs (ibuprofen) reduce pericardial effusion recurrence in pericarditis-related tamponade; IL-1 receptor antagonists (anakinra, rilonacept) reduce both effusion AND pericardial thickening (emerging evidence)
- **Effusive-constrictive pericarditis:** Some patients develop constrictive physiology even after drainage — monitor for persistent hemodynamic compromise post-pericardiocentesis
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Continuous telemetry and hemodynamic monitoring
  - Recognize Beck's triad: Hypotension + JVD + muffled heart sounds
  - Check pulsus paradoxus: Inflate BP cuff above systolic → slowly deflate → note pressure where sounds first heard only during expiration → continue deflating until heard throughout → difference > 10 mmHg = positive pulsus paradoxus
  - Monitor for PEA arrest — tamponade is on the "H's and T's" reversible causes list
  - Trending vitals: Worsening tachycardia + narrowing pulse pressure + rising JVP = deteriorating
- **Pericardiocentesis assistance:**
  - Emergency equipment at bedside (crash cart, intubation supplies)
  - Position patient at 30-45 degrees (pools fluid inferiorly near needle insertion)
  - Continuous ECG monitoring during procedure (ST elevation on ECG = needle touching myocardium → withdraw slightly)
  - Monitor for complications: dysrhythmias, pneumothorax, myocardial laceration, coronary artery injury
  - Post-procedure: Monitor output from pigtail catheter; report sudden cessation (clot) or bloody drainage; serial vitals and echo
- **Critical teaching points:**
  - **DO NOT give diuretics** for JVD in tamponade (reduces preload → fatal)
  - **DO NOT initiate positive pressure ventilation** unless absolutely necessary (reduces venous return → worsens tamponade)
  - **DO give IV fluids** even though the patient looks "volume overloaded" (JVD, edema) — the problem is external compression, not true volume overload

clinicalPearls: **Tamponade is a CLINICAL diagnosis, not an echocardiographic one.** A large pericardial effusion without hemodynamic compromise is not tamponade. A small effusion in a hypotensive tachycardic patient IS tamponade. Treat the patient, not the ultrasound.
**Pulsus paradoxus is the most sensitive bedside finding** (~80% sensitivity). If you suspect tamponade, check pulsus paradoxus before anything else. > 10 mmHg drop = significant; > 20 mmHg = severe.

**"Give fluids, NOT diuretics" is counterintuitive but critical.** The JVD and edema make it look like volume overload. But the problem is external compression. IV fluids increase intracardiac pressures enough to overcome pericardial pressure and maintain filling. Diuretics in tamponade can be fatal.

**Positive pressure ventilation (including BiPAP) worsens tamponade** by reducing venous return. Avoid intubation if possible. If the patient must be intubated, be prepared for hemodynamic collapse at induction — have pericardiocentesis ready.

**Tamponade is a reversible cause of PEA arrest.** During cardiac arrest, if the rhythm is PEA → perform bedside echo → if pericardial effusion present → emergent pericardiocentesis may restore circulation. This is lifesaving.

**Electrical alternans is specific but not sensitive.** When you see alternating QRS heights on ECG, think tamponade. But most tamponade patients do NOT have electrical alternans. Don't rule it out based on ECG.

**In trauma, tamponade = immediate surgery, not pericardiocentesis.** Traumatic tamponade (especially penetrating) requires surgical repair of the cardiac injury. Pericardiocentesis is a temporizing bridge, not definitive treatment in trauma.

**Medications can precipitate tamponade in patients with pre-existing effusions.** IV loop diuretics can unmask "low-pressure tamponade"; beta-blockers impair compensatory tachycardia; anticoagulants/thrombolytics can increase effusion size. Review medication lists carefully in any patient with known pericardial effusion.

**The ESC triage score ≥ 6 = immediate drainage.** This validated scoring system (etiology + clinical + imaging) helps structure the decision-making process. Type A aortic dissection with hemopericardium alone scores 6 (emergent). Know the high-yield triggers.

signs: Tachycardia (compensatory — most reliable early sign)
**Pulsus paradoxus** > 10 mmHg (SBP drops > 10 mmHg during inspiration); measure with manual BP cuff or arterial line
Dyspnea, tachypnea
Anxiety, restlessness
Cool, clammy extremities
Narrowed pulse pressure
Kussmaul sign (paradoxical rise in JVP with inspiration) — more specific for constrictive pericarditis but can occur
**Electrical alternans on ECG** (alternating QRS amplitude — swinging heart in fluid)
PEA arrest (tamponade is a reversible cause of PEA — always consider in cardiac arrest)
Cardiogenic Shock
PEA cardiac arrest
Death (rapidly fatal if untreated)
Recurrence (especially malignant effusions)

cardiogenic-shock.title: Cardiogenic Shock

sections.definition:
Cardiogenic shock is a state of critical end-organ hypoperfusion caused by **primary cardiac pump failure** — the heart cannot generate sufficient cardiac output to meet tissue metabolic demands. It is the most lethal form of shock, with mortality of 40-50% even with modern treatment.

The DO2 problem: CO is critically low because the heart can't squeeze (systolic failure), can't fill (diastolic failure/mechanical obstruction), or has a catastrophic structural problem (acute valvular rupture, VSD).

**Related notes:** Shock · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops · Vasopressors and Inotropes · Cardiac Arrest

---
sections.etiology:
| Cause | Mechanism | Frequency |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Acute MI (most common)** | ≥40% of LV myocardium infarcted or stunned → pump failure. Usually anterior STEMI (LAD territory) | ~70% of cases |
| **Acute decompensated HF** | Chronic HF with acute trigger (infection, medication non-compliance, arrhythmia, dietary indiscretion) | ~15% |
| **Acute valvular catastrophe** | Acute MR (papillary muscle rupture post-MI), acute AR (endocarditis, aortic dissection) | ~5% |
| **Ventricular septal rupture** | Post-MI complication — septal necrosis → left-to-right shunt → pulmonary overcirculation + decreased systemic output | Rare but lethal |
| **Myocarditis** | Inflammatory cardiomyopathy (viral, autoimmune) → acute pump failure | ~3% |
| **Stress cardiomyopathy (Takotsubo)** | Catecholamine-mediated LV dysfunction (apical ballooning) — often triggered by severe emotional/physical stress | Uncommon; usually reversible |
| **Arrhythmia** | Sustained VT, rapid AF with very poor LV function, complete heart block with no escape | Variable |
| **PE (massive)** | RV failure from acute pressure overload → ↓ LV preload → ↓ CO (overlaps with obstructive shock) | See Obstructive Shock |

---
sections.pathophysiology:
Through the Oxygen Delivery and Consumption lens:

1. **Primary insult** → myocardial damage → ↓ contractility → ↓ stroke volume → ↓ cardiac output
2. **↓ CO → ↓ DO2** → tissue hypoperfusion → rising lactate → organ dysfunction
> *First principles: Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — cardiogenic shock breaks Lever 1 (cardiac output); when CO collapses, DO2 drops regardless of Hgb or SaO2. Treatment restores CO via inotropes and MAP via vasopressors — always rebuilding DO2 to meet VO2.*

3. **Compensatory response** (see Homeostasis and Feedback Loops):
   - ↑ HR (sympathetic activation) — but ↑ HR → ↑ myocardial O2 demand on an already failing heart
   - ↑ SVR (vasoconstriction) — but ↑ afterload → harder for the failing ventricle to eject
   - ↑ Preload (RAAS, ADH) — but ↑ preload → pulmonary congestion → worsened oxygenation
4. **Vicious cycle:** Every compensatory mechanism worsens the underlying problem → **downward spiral** → death without intervention

> **This is the central tragedy of cardiogenic shock:** the body's normal compensatory responses to low CO (tachycardia, vasoconstriction, fluid retention) are EXACTLY the wrong things for a failing heart. Treatment is aimed at breaking these maladaptive compensations.

### Hemodynamic Profile

| Parameter | Expected Finding | Why |
|-----------|-----------------|-----|
| **CO/CI** | ↓↓ (CI <2.2 L/min/m²) | Pump failure — the defining feature |
| **SVR** | ↑↑ | Compensatory vasoconstriction (sympathetic) |
| **PCWP** | ↑↑ (>18 mmHg) | Blood backing up behind failing LV |
| **CVP/JVP** | ↑ | Volume overload / RV failure |
| **MAP** | ↓ (<65 mmHg) or maintained by extreme vasoconstriction | Inadequate CO despite high SVR |
| **SvO2/ScvO2** | ↓↓ (<65%) | Tissues extracting maximally because delivery is so low |

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Presentation
- **Hypotension:** SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg (or >30 mmHg drop from baseline for >30 min)
- **Tachycardia:** Compensatory (but may be bradycardic if AV nodal disease or inferior MI)
- **Cool, clammy, mottled skin** — vasoconstriction redirecting blood to vital organs
- **Altered mental status** — confusion, agitation, obtundation (cerebral hypoperfusion)
- **Oliguria** — urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr (renal hypoperfusion)
- **Pulmonary edema** — dyspnea, crackles, pink frothy sputum (LV failure → pulmonary congestion)
- **JVD** — elevated right-sided pressures
- **Diaphoresis** — sympathetic activation
- **Narrow pulse pressure** — ↓ SV → ↓ systolic BP with maintained diastolic (vasoconstriction)

### Key Distinction from Other Shock Types

| Finding | Cardiogenic | Hypovolemic | Distributive |
|---------|-------------|-------------|--------------|
| **JVD** | Present | Absent | Variable |
| **Lungs** | Wet (crackles, edema) | Clear | Usually clear |
| **Skin** | Cold, clammy | Cold, clammy | Warm, flushed (early) |
| **ScvO2** | ↓↓ (<65%) | ↓ | ↑ or normal (>70%) |
| **PCWP** | ↑↑ | ↓↓ | ↓ or normal |

---
sections.diagnosis:
### Essential Workup
- **12-lead ECG** — STEMI? Arrhythmia? Right-sided leads if inferior MI (RV involvement)
- **Troponin** — serial trending. Rise pattern suggests acute MI
- **Lactate** — severity marker and resuscitation guide
- **BNP/NT-proBNP** — elevated in heart failure (may help distinguish from other causes if diagnosis unclear)
- **CXR** — pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly
- **ABG/VBG** — acidosis assessment
- **BMP** — electrolytes (K+, Mg2+), renal function (Cr, BUN)
- **CBC** — anemia worsens DO2
- **Bedside echocardiography** — THE most important diagnostic tool:
  - EF estimation (severely reduced in pump failure)
  - Wall motion abnormalities (regional = MI, global = cardiomyopathy)
  - Valvular catastrophe (flail leaflet, severe regurgitation)
  - VSD (color Doppler showing left-to-right shunt)
  - Pericardial effusion/tamponade (→ obstructive shock, not cardiogenic)
  - RV dilation (→ consider PE as cause)

---
sections.management:
### Initial Stabilization
1. **Airway/breathing** — high-flow O2; BiPAP for pulmonary edema if patient can tolerate (reduces preload AND afterload); intubate if respiratory failure
2. **IV access** — two large-bore IVs; avoid excessive fluid boluses (this is NOT hypovolemic shock — fluid worsens pulmonary edema)
3. **Monitoring** — continuous telemetry, arterial line for beat-to-beat BP, Foley for urine output, central line for CVP/ScvO2 if available

### Vasopressors and Inotropes

| Agent | Receptor | Effect | When to Use |
|-------|----------|--------|-------------|
| **Norepinephrine** | Alpha-1 >> Beta-1 | ↑ SVR (vasoconstriction) + mild ↑ contractility | **First-line vasopressor** (SOAP II trial). Use when MAP <65 despite inotropes |
| **Dobutamine** | Beta-1 > Beta-2 | ↑ Contractility, ↑ HR, mild ↓ SVR (vasodilation) | **First-line inotrope** — directly addresses the pump failure. Watch for hypotension from vasodilation |
| **Milrinone** | PDE-3 inhibitor | ↑ Contractility + ↓ SVR (vasodilation) | Alternative/adjunct to dobutamine. Longer half-life; greater hypotension risk. Useful if on chronic beta-blockers (works independently of beta receptors) |
| **Dopamine** | Dose-dependent | Low: D1 (renal vasodilation). Moderate: Beta-1 (inotropy). High: Alpha-1 (vasoconstriction) | **Second-line** — higher arrhythmia risk than norepinephrine (SOAP II) |
| **Epinephrine** | Alpha + Beta | ↑ Contractility + ↑ SVR + ↑ HR | Refractory shock — when norepinephrine + dobutamine insufficient |

> **Typical combination:** Norepinephrine for MAP support + dobutamine for inotropy. Start norepinephrine first if MAP <65, then add dobutamine to improve CO once MAP is supported.

### Treat the Cause

| Cause | Definitive Treatment |
|-------|---------------------|
| **STEMI** | Emergent PCI (percutaneous coronary intervention) — door-to-balloon. Cardiogenic shock is an indication for emergent cath even in NSTEMI |
| **Acute valvular catastrophe** | Emergent cardiac surgery (valve repair/replacement) |
| **VSD** | Surgical repair (extremely high surgical mortality — IABP/Impella as bridge) |
| **Arrhythmia-driven** | Cardioversion (tachyarrhythmia) or pacing (bradyarrhythmia) |
| **Myocarditis** | Supportive care, immunosuppression if appropriate, mechanical support if refractory |

### Mechanical Circulatory Support

| Device | Mechanism | CO Support | When |
|--------|-----------|-----------|------|
| **IABP (Intra-Aortic Balloon Pump)** | Inflates in diastole (↑ coronary perfusion), deflates in systole (↓ afterload) | Modest (~0.5 L/min) | Bridge to definitive therapy. IABP-SHOCK II showed no mortality benefit — declining use |
| **Impella** | Axial flow pump across aortic valve — directly unloads LV | 2.5-5.5 L/min depending on model | Increasing use. Better hemodynamic support than IABP |
| **ECMO (VA-ECMO)** | Blood drained from venous system, oxygenated externally, returned to arterial system | Full cardiac and respiratory support | Refractory cardiogenic shock as bridge to recovery, decision, or transplant |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Volume is the enemy in cardiogenic shock.** Unlike hypovolemic shock, fluid boluses WORSEN pulmonary edema and increase myocardial workload. If unsure of shock type → bedside echo before volume loading
- **Titrate vasopressors to MAP ≥65** (or higher per provider) — use arterial line for accuracy
- **Monitor ScvO2 if central line available** — goal >65%. Rising ScvO2 = improving CO. Falling ScvO2 = deteriorating
- **Strict I&O** — urine output is your real-time renal perfusion indicator. Target >0.5 mL/kg/hr
- **Watch for arrhythmias** — ischemic myocardium is electrically unstable. Have defibrillator at bedside
- **Trending lactate** — q2-4h. Clearance >10%/2hr = responding to treatment. Rising lactate = failing
- **BiPAP can be a game-changer** — reduces preload (↓ venous return) AND afterload (↓ intrathoracic pressure improves LV ejection) while improving oxygenation. But patient must be alert enough to protect airway
- **Prepare for rapid escalation** — cardiogenic shock can deteriorate to cardiac arrest quickly. Keep crash cart at bedside, ACLS drugs drawn

---
sections.references:
- Thiele H, et al: Intraaortic Balloon Support for Myocardial Infarction with Cardiogenic Shock (IABP-SHOCK II). NEJM 2012
- De Backer D, et al: Comparison of Dopamine and Norepinephrine in Shock (SOAP II). NEJM 2010
- van Diepen S, et al: Contemporary Management of Cardiogenic Shock. Circulation 2017
- AHA/ACC Guidelines for STEMI Management

---

*See also: Shock · Cardiac Arrest · ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction · Acute Heart Failure · Vasopressors and Inotropes · Oxygen Delivery and Consumption · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops*

clinicalPearls: **Cardiogenic shock is a spiral, not a state.** Without intervention, every compensatory response (tachycardia, vasoconstriction, fluid retention) worsens the failing heart. Treatment breaks the cycle: inotropes restore CO, vasopressors maintain MAP, diuretics offload congestion.

**Cold and wet = cardiogenic shock.** Cool extremities (↑ SVR) + pulmonary crackles/edema (↑ PCWP). Warm and wet = sepsis with pulmonary edema. Cold and dry = hypovolemia. This bedside assessment takes 30 seconds and guides initial management.

**ScvO2 <65% with elevated lactate = inadequate DO2.** In cardiogenic shock, this means the pump isn't delivering enough. Add inotrope support. In contrast, septic patients often have ScvO2 >70% because the problem is extraction, not delivery.

**Avoid fluids unless CLEARLY hypovolemic.** If JVD is present and lungs are wet, more fluid will push the patient toward intubation or arrest. A "small fluid challenge" in cardiogenic shock is NOT benign.
**Door-to-balloon time saves myocardium in MI-driven cardiogenic shock.** The more myocardium you reperfuse, the more pump function you salvage. Time is muscle.

signs: **Hypotension:** SBP <90 mmHg or MAP <65 mmHg (or >30 mmHg drop from baseline for >30 min)
**Tachycardia:** Compensatory (but may be bradycardic if AV nodal disease or inferior MI)
**Cool, clammy, mottled skin** — vasoconstriction redirecting blood to vital organs
**Altered mental status** — confusion, agitation, obtundation (cerebral hypoperfusion)
**Oliguria** — urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr (renal hypoperfusion)
**Pulmonary edema** — dyspnea, crackles, pink frothy sputum (LV failure → pulmonary congestion)
**JVD** — elevated right-sided pressures
**Diaphoresis** — sympathetic activation
**Narrow pulse pressure** — ↓ SV → ↓ systolic BP with maintained diastolic (vasoconstriction)

catatonia.title: Catatonia

sections.definition:
Catatonia is a neuropsychiatric syndrome of motor dysregulation — the patient's ability to move, speak, and interact with the environment is profoundly disrupted despite an alert consciousness. It is NOT simply "a patient who won't move or talk." It is a distinct syndrome with specific motor signs, a diagnostic test (the lorazepam challenge), and a specific treatment (benzodiazepines and ECT).

The biggest clinical danger of catatonia is **not recognizing it.** It is underdiagnosed because it looks like many other things — depression, psychosis, delirium, stroke, NMS, or "just being difficult." Missing it means missing the treatment, and untreated catatonia can progress to the malignant form (fever, autonomic instability, rhabdomyolysis, death).

**Related notes:** Acute Psychosis · Schizophrenia · Bipolar Disorder · Delirium · Sedatives and Anxiolytics

---
sections.epidemiology:
- Prevalence among hospitalized psychiatric patients: **10-15%** (far more common than most clinicians think)
- Most commonly associated with **mood disorders** (bipolar depression, MDD), NOT schizophrenia (despite historical association)
- Can be caused by medical conditions in **20-30%** of cases (autoimmune encephalitis, metabolic, drugs)
- All ages, including pediatric
- Estimated mortality for malignant catatonia: **10-20%** without treatment

---
sections.etiology:
### Psychiatric Causes (~70%)
- **Bipolar disorder** (most common psychiatric cause — especially depressive or mixed episodes)
- **Major depressive disorder** (especially with psychotic features)
- Schizophrenia (historically the classic association, but actually less common than mood disorders)
- Autism spectrum disorder (catatonic features can develop)
- PTSD / conversion disorder

### Medical Causes (~20-30%)
