gastroparesis.title: Gastroparesis

sections.definition: Gastroparesis is a chronic disorder of gastric neuromuscular function characterized by delayed gastric emptying of solids in the absence of mechanical obstruction. It is part of a broader spectrum of gastric neuromuscular dysfunction that includes impaired gastric accommodation. Cardinal symptoms include early satiety, postprandial fullness, nausea, vomiting, bloating, and belching.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** Estimated 1.8% of general population; likely underdiagnosed; higher prevalence in women (4:1 female predominance)
- **Risk factors:**
  - Diabetes Mellitus Type 1 and Diabetes Mellitus Type 2 (30% of cases; duration and poor glycemic control increase risk)
  - Post-surgical (especially post-vagotomy, gastric resection, fundoplication, bariatric surgery; ~13% of cases)
  - Post-viral infection (viral gastroenteritis; acute onset; ~15% of cases)
  - Medications (opioids, anticholinergics, GLP-1 agonists, pramlintide, tricyclic antidepressants, calcium channel blockers)
  - Neurologic disorders (Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, autonomic neuropathy)
  - Connective tissue disorders (scleroderma, systemic lupus erythematosus)
  - Idiopathic (most common, 35-40%; often young to middle-aged women; may follow viral illness)
- **Demographics:** Female predominance (80% of idiopathic cases); peak diagnosis 20-40 years for idiopathic; any age for diabetic
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — gastric emptying is regulated by the enteric nervous system (ENS) working in concert with vagal parasympathetic input (M3 receptors on smooth muscle promote motility); diabetic gastroparesis = loss of vagal input from autonomic neuropathy + depletion of interstitial cells of Cajal (gastric pacemaker cells that generate slow waves via gap junctions); without the pacemaker signal and cholinergic drive, coordinated antral contractions fail*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — normal gastric emptying rate is a tightly regulated feedback process: antral mechanoreceptors, CCK (from fat/protein in duodenum), and GLP-1 all feed back to slow or accelerate emptying to match absorptive capacity; gastroparesis = disruption of the effector arm (gastric smooth muscle + ENS); the sensor and control center still receive normal input but cannot produce the motor response*

Gastroparesis results from impaired coordination of gastric neuromuscular function:
- **Normal gastric motility requires:**
  - Intact vagal innervation
  - Functional enteric nervous system
  - Interstitial cells of Cajal (ICC; gastric pacemaker cells)
  - Smooth muscle integrity
  - Coordinated pyloric relaxation
- **Mechanisms of delayed gastric emptying:**
  - **Diabetic gastroparesis:**
    - Vagal autonomic neuropathy (chronic hyperglycemia causes nerve damage)
    - Loss of interstitial cells of Cajal (ICC depletion disrupts pacemaker activity)
    - Hyperglycemia acutely impairs gastric motility
    - Oxidative stress, advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
  - **Post-surgical:**
    - Vagal nerve injury during surgery (vagotomy, fundoplication)
    - Disruption of gastric pacemaker mechanisms
  - **Idiopathic/post-viral:**
    - ICC loss
    - Enteric nervous system dysfunction
    - Low-grade inflammation
    - Possible viral injury to vagal nerve or enteric neurons
- **Pathologic findings:**
  - Reduced ICC density
  - Enteric neuronal loss
  - Macrophage infiltration
  - Fibrosis of gastric smooth muscle
- **Impaired gastric accommodation:** Fundus fails to relax after eating; contributes to early satiety and postprandial fullness
- **Pyloric dysfunction:** Pylorospasm or impaired pyloric relaxation may contribute
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Cardinal symptoms (gastroparesis cardinal symptom index - GCSI):**
  - **Nausea:** Chronic, often worse postprandially
  - **Vomiting:** May contain undigested food eaten hours earlier
  - **Early satiety:** Inability to finish normal-sized meal
  - **Postprandial fullness:** Prolonged sensation of fullness after eating
  - **Bloating and abdominal distension**
  - **Upper abdominal pain/discomfort:** Epigastric pain (though not typical of gastroparesis; suggests overlap with functional dyspepsia)
- **Belching, burping**
- **Weight loss:** Typically mild; severe weight loss should prompt evaluation for alternative diagnoses
- **Malnutrition and dehydration:** In severe cases
- **Erratic blood glucose control (diabetic patients):** Unpredictable postprandial glucose due to delayed nutrient absorption
- **Bezoar formation:** Accumulation of undigested food material

### Complications
- **Severe dehydration and electrolyte disturbances:** From intractable vomiting
- **Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies
- **Bezoars:** Gastric concretions (phytobezoars from undigested plant material); may cause obstruction or ulceration
- **Erratic glycemic control:** Difficult diabetes management with hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia
- **Esophagitis and GERD:** From vomiting and delayed gastric emptying
- **Reduced quality of life:** Significant impact on daily activities, work, social functioning
- **Hospitalization:** Frequent admissions for dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities, symptom exacerbations
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **Basic metabolic panel:** Assess electrolytes (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypochloremia from vomiting), kidney function
- **Complete blood count:** Rule out anemia
- **Hemoglobin A1c:** Assess glycemic control in diabetic patients
- **Thyroid function tests:** Rule out hypothyroidism (can cause delayed gastric emptying)
- **Thiamine level:** Deficiency can mimic gastroparesis symptoms

**Gastric emptying studies:**
- **Gastric scintigraphy (gold standard):**
  - 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying study using radiolabeled egg sandwich meal
  - Normal: <10% retention at 4 hours
  - Delayed emptying: >10% retention at 4 hours (diagnostic for gastroparesis)
  - Grading: Mild (10-15% retention), moderate (15-35%), severe (>35%)
  - Must discontinue prokinetic agents and other medications affecting gastric motility 48-72 hours before test
  - Should be performed with blood glucose <275 mg/dL (hyperglycemia delays emptying)
- **Gastric emptying breath test (GEBT):**
  - Non-radioactive alternative; measures 13CO2 in breath after ingesting 13C-labeled substrate
  - Less standardized than scintigraphy
- **Wireless motility capsule:**
  - Measures pH, temperature, pressure as capsule transits GI tract
  - Provides gastric emptying time, small bowel transit, colonic transit
  - Contraindicated if obstructive symptoms or dysphagia

**Upper endoscopy (EGD):**
- Essential to rule out mechanical obstruction (peptic ulcer, tumor, stricture)
- May reveal retained food/bezoar
- Evaluate for mucosal disease (celiac disease, eosinophilic gastroenteritis)

**Other studies:**
- **Gastric accommodation testing:** Barostat study (research setting)
- **Antroduodenal manometry:** Assess gastric and small bowel motility patterns; rarely performed
- **Electrogastrography (EGG):** Records gastric electrical activity; limited clinical utility

### Diagnostic Criteria
- **Symptoms:** Cardinal symptoms (nausea, vomiting, early satiety, postprandial fullness) present for ≥3 months
- **Delayed gastric emptying:** Documented by gastric scintigraphy (>10% retention at 4 hours)
- **Absence of mechanical obstruction:** Normal upper endoscopy
- **Rome IV criteria for gastroparesis-like syndrome:** Symptoms present but normal gastric emptying (overlap with functional dyspepsia)

**Differential diagnosis:**
- Functional Dyspepsia (normal gastric emptying; symptom overlap)
- Mechanical obstruction (peptic ulcer disease, malignancy, stricture)
- Cyclic Vomiting Syndrome
- Rumination Syndrome
- Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome
- Small intestinal bacterial overgrowth (SIBO)
- Eating disorders (anorexia nervosa, bulimia)
sections.management:
### Acute
- **Correct fluid, electrolyte, and nutritional deficiencies:**
  - IV hydration for dehydration
  - Electrolyte repletion (potassium, magnesium)
  - Nutritional assessment and supplementation
- **Optimize glycemic control (diabetic gastroparesis):**
  - Target blood glucose <180 mg/dL
  - Avoid hypoglycemia
  - Continuous glucose monitoring
  - Insulin adjustments (may need prandial insulin delayed or given after meals)
- **Discontinue medications that delay gastric emptying:**
  - Opioids, anticholinergics, GLP-1 agonists, pramlintide

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Dietary modifications (first-line):**
- Small, frequent meals (4-6 per day)
- Low-fat diet (<40 g/day; fat delays gastric emptying)
- Low-fiber diet (avoid insoluble fiber, raw vegetables, skins, seeds)
- Well-cooked, soft foods; puree or blend if needed
- Avoid carbonated beverages and alcohol
- Liquid calories (nutritional supplements, soups, smoothies) better tolerated than solids
- Adequate hydration
- Ginger, peppermint tea may help nausea

**Pharmacologic therapy:**
- **Prokinetic agents (improve gastric emptying):**
  - **Metoclopramide:**
    - Dopamine D2 receptor antagonist; central antiemetic; peripheral prokinetic
    - 5-10 mg PO 30 minutes before meals and at bedtime (maximum 40 mg/day)
    - Most evidence-based prokinetic
    - FDA black box warning: tardive dyskinesia (5% risk with long-term use, especially >3 months)
    - Other side effects: extrapyramidal symptoms (dystonia, akathisia), drowsiness, diarrhea
    - Use lowest effective dose for shortest duration
  - **Domperidone:**
    - Dopamine D2 antagonist; does not cross blood-brain barrier (fewer CNS side effects than metoclopramide)
    - 10-20 mg PO 3-4 times daily before meals
    - NOT FDA approved in US (available through compassionate use); available in Canada, Europe
    - Risk of QT prolongation and cardiac arrhythmias
  - **Erythromycin:**
    - Motilin receptor agonist; prokinetic at low doses
    - 40-250 mg PO/IV 3-4 times daily before meals
    - Effective acutely; tachyphylaxis develops (loss of efficacy over weeks to months)
    - Use as short-term bridge or rescue therapy
    - Side effects: QT prolongation, nausea, diarrhea, antibiotic resistance
- **Antiemetic agents:**
  - **5-HT3 antagonists:** Ondansetron 4-8 mg PO/IV every 8 hours; granisetron
  - **NK-1 antagonist:** Aprepitant
  - **Phenothiazines:** Prochlorperazine, promethazine (anticholinergic effects may worsen gastroparesis)
  - **Antihistamines:** Meclizine, diphenhydramine (anticholinergic; use with caution)
- **Pain management:** Tricyclic antidepressants (amitriptyline, nortriptyline) for visceral pain; avoid opioids (worsen gastroparesis)

**Novel and investigational therapies:**
- **Prucalopride:** Serotonin 5-HT4 receptor agonist; prokinetic; approved for chronic constipation; some evidence in gastroparesis
- **Relamorelin:** Ghrelin receptor agonist; investigational
- **Tradipitant:** NK-1 receptor antagonist; investigational

**Endoscopic therapies:**
- **Pyloric botulinum toxin injection:** Relax pyloric sphincter; conflicting evidence; not routinely recommended
- **Gastric electrical stimulation (GES, "gastric pacemaker"):**
  - Surgically implanted device delivers high-frequency, low-energy electrical stimulation to stomach
  - FDA approved under Humanitarian Device Exemption for refractory diabetic and idiopathic gastroparesis
  - Improves symptoms (especially nausea, vomiting) but does NOT improve gastric emptying
  - Mechanism unclear (neuromodulation?)
  - Consider in severe, refractory cases
- **Transpyloric stent:** Temporary stent across pylorus; limited evidence
- **Gastric peroral endoscopic myotomy (G-POEM):** Endoscopic pyloromyotomy; emerging technique; early evidence promising

**Surgical therapies (rarely used; for refractory cases):**
- **Pyloroplasty:** Surgical widening of pylorus
- **Gastric bypass:** May improve symptoms in select patients
- **Gastrectomy:** Last resort; subtotal or total gastrectomy with Roux-en-Y reconstruction; high morbidity
- **Venting gastrostomy tube:** For intractable nausea/vomiting to decompress stomach; does not improve nutrition

**Nutritional support (severe cases):**
- **Enteral nutrition:**
  - Nasojejunal (NJ) tube or percutaneous endoscopic gastrojejunostomy (PEG-J) tube for post-pyloric feeding
  - Bypasses stomach; delivers nutrition directly to small bowel
  - Used when oral intake insufficient
- **Parenteral nutrition (TPN):**
  - Reserved for most severe cases when enteral nutrition fails or not tolerated
  - Central venous access; risks include infection, thrombosis, liver disease
  - Goal: bridge to improved oral/enteral intake

### Pharmacology
- **Antiemetics** — ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist, potent antiemetic, no prokinetic), promethazine, prochlorperazine; for symptom relief
- **Insulin** — diabetes is the most common cause; tight glycemic control improves gastric motility (hyperglycemia directly slows emptying)
- Metoclopramide: Dopamine D2 antagonist + 5-HT4 agonist; prokinetic + antiemetic; tardive dyskinesia risk (FDA black box)
- Domperidone: Dopamine D2 antagonist; peripheral only (no CNS effects); QT prolongation risk
- Erythromycin: Motilin agonist; prokinetic; tachyphylaxis limits long-term use
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Nutritional assessment:**
  - Monitor weight, BMI, albumin, prealbumin
  - Assess oral intake (food diary helpful)
  - Screen for vitamin/mineral deficiencies
  - Dietitian referral for individualized meal planning
- **Dietary education:**
  - Small, frequent meals (4-6 per day)
  - Low-fat, low-fiber, well-cooked soft foods
  - Liquid calories when solid foods not tolerated
  - Adequate hydration between meals
  - Identify and avoid trigger foods
  - Explain rationale: fat and fiber delay gastric emptying
- **Blood glucose monitoring (diabetic gastroparesis):**
  - Erratic glucose due to delayed nutrient absorption
  - Continuous glucose monitoring helpful
  - Educate on hypoglycemia risk (insulin given before meal but food absorption delayed)
  - Insulin timing adjustments: may need to give prandial insulin after eating or split dose
  - Target glucose <180 mg/dL to optimize gastric motility
- **Medication timing:**
  - Prokinetics: take 30 minutes before meals (metoclopramide, domperidone, erythromycin)
  - Antiemetics: take before meals or as needed for nausea
  - Hold medications that worsen gastroparesis (opioids, anticholinergics)
- **Medication education:**
  - **Metoclopramide:** Explain black box warning about tardive dyskinesia risk; teach to report involuntary movements (facial grimacing, tongue protrusion, lip smacking); use lowest dose for shortest duration
  - Monitor for extrapyramidal symptoms: restlessness, dystonia
- **Symptom management:**
  - Nausea/vomiting: Antiemetics; cold foods may be better tolerated; avoid strong odors
  - Bloating: Avoid carbonated beverages, chewing gum, drinking through straws
  - Early satiety: Emphasize small portions; liquid nutrition
- **Enteral feeding tube care (NJ, PEG-J):**
  - Teach tube care, flushing, administration of feeds
  - Troubleshooting: clogging, dislodgement
  - Monitor for complications: aspiration, site infection, tube migration
- **Monitoring:**
  - Vitals: orthostatic hypotension (dehydration)
  - Intake/output
  - Daily weights
  - Electrolytes (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia)
  - Signs of dehydration (dry mucous membranes, decreased skin turgor, concentrated urine)
- **Quality of life impact:**
  - Screen for depression, anxiety (high prevalence)
  - Social isolation (difficulty eating with others, unpredictable symptoms)
  - Impact on work, relationships
  - Refer for counseling, support groups
- **Pre-procedure preparation:**
  - Gastric emptying study: Discontinue prokinetics 48-72 hours before; blood glucose <275 mg/dL; NPO per protocol
- **Patient advocacy:**
  - Gastroparesis is a chronic, often debilitating condition
  - Validate patient symptoms and experiences
  - Coordinate multidisciplinary care (gastroenterology, endocrinology, nutrition, pain management)
sections.keyPoints:
- Gastroparesis is delayed gastric emptying without mechanical obstruction; cardinal symptoms include nausea, vomiting, early satiety, postprandial fullness
- Idiopathic (35-40%) and diabetic (30%) are the most common etiologies
- Diagnosis requires gastric scintigraphy showing >10% retention at 4 hours; must rule out mechanical obstruction with upper endoscopy
- Management: dietary modifications (small frequent meals, low-fat, low-fiber), prokinetics (metoclopramide first-line), antiemetics, glycemic control (diabetic gastroparesis)
- Metoclopramide has FDA black box warning for tardive dyskinesia; use lowest dose for shortest duration
- Erythromycin effective acutely but tachyphylaxis limits long-term use
- Gastric electrical stimulation for refractory cases; improves symptoms but not gastric emptying
- Severe cases may require enteral nutrition (post-pyloric tube feeding) or parenteral nutrition (TPN)

clinicalPearls: **Metoclopramide's black box warning is serious: counsel every patient** — tardive dyskinesia risk is ~5% with long-term use (>3 months), higher in elderly and women; characterized by irreversible involuntary movements (lip smacking, tongue protrusion, facial grimacing); teach patients to report these symptoms immediately; use the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.

**Glucose control affects gastric motility: check the blood sugar** — acute hyperglycemia (>180-200 mg/dL) directly impairs gastric emptying; a diabetic patient with worsening gastroparesis symptoms may simply have poor glucose control; optimize glycemic control before escalating other therapies.

**Gastric scintigraphy must be done off prokinetics** — patients must discontinue metoclopramide, domperidone, erythromycin, and other motility agents 48-72 hours before the test; otherwise, test results are invalid; also ensure blood glucose <275 mg/dL during the test.

**Erythromycin works great initially, then stops working** — tachyphylaxis (loss of prokinetic effect) develops within weeks to months due to motilin receptor downregulation; best used as short-term rescue therapy or bridge to other treatments, not long-term maintenance.

**Post-viral gastroparesis can improve over time** — unlike diabetic gastroparesis (which is progressive), post-viral gastroparesis may improve spontaneously over months to years; counsel patients that symptoms may gradually resolve, especially within the first 12-18 months.

**Liquid calories are your friend when solids fail** — when patients cannot tolerate solid foods, liquid nutrition (nutritional supplement drinks, smoothies, soups, broths) is often better tolerated because liquids empty from the stomach more easily than solids; prioritize caloric intake over food texture preferences.

**Not all upper GI symptoms are gastroparesis** — significant symptom overlap with [[functional dyspepsia]]; many patients with typical gastroparesis symptoms have normal gastric emptying studies (gastroparesis-like syndrome); conversely, some patients with delayed gastric emptying are asymptomatic; correlation between symptoms and emptying is imperfect.

**Opioids are the enemy of gastroparesis** — opioids directly delay gastric emptying through mu-receptor effects on GI motility; avoid opioids for gastroparesis-related pain; use alternatives (TCAs, gabapentin, pregabalin); if opioids unavoidable (e.g., post-surgery), use lowest dose and shortest duration.

**Gastroparesis can cause erratic diabetes control, and diabetes causes gastroparesis** — bidirectional relationship creates vicious cycle; delayed nutrient absorption makes postprandial glucose unpredictable; hyperglycemia further impairs gastric motility; insulin timing becomes challenging (may need to delay prandial insulin until after meal to avoid hypoglycemia).

signs: **Cardinal symptoms (gastroparesis cardinal symptom index - GCSI):**
**Nausea:** Chronic, often worse postprandially
**Vomiting:** May contain undigested food eaten hours earlier
**Early satiety:** Inability to finish normal-sized meal
**Postprandial fullness:** Prolonged sensation of fullness after eating
**Bloating and abdominal distension**
**Upper abdominal pain/discomfort:** Epigastric pain (though not typical of gastroparesis; suggests overlap with functional dyspepsia)
**Belching, burping**
**Weight loss:** Typically mild; severe weight loss should prompt evaluation for alternative diagnoses
**Malnutrition and dehydration:** In severe cases
**Erratic blood glucose control (diabetic patients):** Unpredictable postprandial glucose due to delayed nutrient absorption
**Bezoar formation:** Accumulation of undigested food material
**Severe dehydration and electrolyte disturbances:** From intractable vomiting
**Malnutrition:** Protein-calorie malnutrition, vitamin deficiencies
**Bezoars:** Gastric concretions (phytobezoars from undigested plant material); may cause obstruction or ulceration
**Erratic glycemic control:** Difficult diabetes management with hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia
**Esophagitis and GERD:** From vomiting and delayed gastric emptying
**Reduced quality of life:** Significant impact on daily activities, work, social functioning
**Hospitalization:** Frequent admissions for dehydration, electrolyte abnormalities, symptom exacerbations

glioma.title: Glioma

sections.definition: Primary brain tumors arising from glial cells (astrocytes, oligodendrocytes, or their precursors). Gliomas represent the most common primary malignant brain tumors in adults. Classification is based on both histology and molecular markers (WHO 2021 CNS classification), with IDH mutation status and 1p/19q codeletion being critical determinants of diagnosis, treatment, and prognosis.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~100,000 people living with malignant brain tumors in the US
- **Incidence:** Gliomas: ~6 per 100,000/year; GBM: ~3-4 per 100,000/year (most common malignant primary brain tumor)
- **Risk factors:** Prior ionizing radiation (only established environmental risk factor), rare genetic syndromes (Li-Fraumeni/TP53, neurofibromatosis type 1, Turcot syndrome), family history (small increased risk)
- **Demographics:**
  - GBM (grade 4): Median age 64; male > female (1.6:1); median survival 14-16 months with treatment
  - IDH-mutant astrocytoma (grade 2-3): Younger adults (30-45); better prognosis
  - Oligodendroglioma (IDH-mutant, 1p/19q codeleted): Young-middle adults; best prognosis among diffuse gliomas; median survival 12-20 years for grade 2
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — IDH mutation disrupts normal metabolic regulation → 2-HG production → epigenetic reprogramming → glial cell dedifferentiation and uncontrolled proliferation. Genetics and Protein Expression — IDH1/2, TERT, EGFR, and 1p/19q codeletion status define molecular subtypes and predict prognosis.*

**WHO 2021 Classification (molecular-based):**
- **Astrocytoma, IDH-mutant (grades 2, 3, 4):** IDH mutation + ATRX loss + p53 mutation; no 1p/19q codeletion; grade 4 has homozygous CDKN2A/B deletion
- **Oligodendroglioma, IDH-mutant, 1p/19q codeleted (grades 2, 3):** IDH mutation + 1p/19q codeletion; better chemosensitivity (especially to PCV)
- **Glioblastoma, IDH-wildtype (grade 4):** No IDH mutation; TERT promoter mutation, EGFR amplification, +7/-10 chromosome changes; most aggressive

**Key molecular markers:**
- **IDH mutation (IDH1 R132H most common):** Better prognosis; younger patients; associated with 2-HG production (oncometabolite) → epigenetic reprogramming
- **1p/19q codeletion:** Defines oligodendrogliomas; predicts chemosensitivity to PCV and longer survival
- **MGMT promoter methylation:** Silences DNA repair gene → tumor cells cannot repair temozolomide damage → better response to alkylating chemotherapy; present in ~40% of GBM
- **TERT promoter mutation, EGFR amplification, +7/-10:** Define GBM even in absence of high-grade histology

**Tumor biology:**
- Diffuse infiltration of surrounding brain parenchyma (cannot achieve complete resection)
- Angiogenesis (especially GBM — neovascularization with leaky BBB → vasogenic edema → ring enhancement on MRI)
- GBM may have extensive necrosis and hemorrhage
- Glioma stem cells contribute to treatment resistance and recurrence
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Presentation depends on tumor location, size, and growth rate:**

- **Headache (50-60%):** Classically worse in morning (increased ICP from recumbent position), progressive, may be positional; often nonspecific early
- **Seizures (20-40% at presentation; 60-80% during disease course):** Most common presenting symptom for low-grade gliomas; focal seizures reflect tumor location
- **Focal neurological deficits:** Motor weakness, sensory changes, visual field cuts, aphasia — depend on tumor location
- **Cognitive/personality changes:** Especially frontal lobe tumors; may be subtle initially (mistaken for depression or dementia)
- **Signs of raised ICP:** Progressive headache, nausea/vomiting (especially morning), papilledema, altered consciousness, CN VI palsy (false localizing)
- **Language dysfunction:** Tumors near Broca's or Wernicke's areas
- **Low-grade gliomas:** Often present with isolated seizures in a young adult with otherwise normal exam

### Complications
- Increased intracranial pressure → herniation (medical emergency)
- Tumor hemorrhage
- Venous thromboembolism (20-30% — gliomas are highly prothrombotic)
- Treatment toxicity: radiation necrosis, chemotherapy myelosuppression, steroid side effects
- Seizures (ongoing)
- Cognitive decline (from tumor, edema, treatment, or all three)
- Mood disorders, fatigue
- Tumor recurrence (universal in high-grade gliomas)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **MRI brain with and without gadolinium (gold standard):**
  - GBM: Ring-enhancing mass with central necrosis, surrounding vasogenic edema, mass effect; often crosses midline ("butterfly glioma" if bihemispheric)
  - Low-grade glioma: T2/FLAIR hyperintensity without enhancement (though enhancement doesn't always mean high grade)
  - DWI, perfusion imaging, MR spectroscopy provide additional characterization
- **Advanced MRI sequences:**
  - Functional MRI (fMRI): Maps eloquent cortex (language, motor) for surgical planning
  - Diffusion tensor imaging (DTI): Maps white matter tracts to preserve during surgery
  - MR spectroscopy: Elevated choline/NAA ratio suggests tumor; helps distinguish from radiation necrosis
  - MR perfusion: Elevated cerebral blood volume suggests high-grade
- **Tissue diagnosis (required for definitive classification):**
  - Stereotactic biopsy or surgical resection
  - Molecular profiling: IDH mutation, 1p/19q codeletion, MGMT methylation, ATRX, p53, TERT, EGFR
- **CT head:** Less sensitive than MRI; often initial imaging in ED for acute presentation; may show mass, edema, hemorrhage, calcification (oligodendroglioma)
- **Labs:** CBC, BMP, coagulation (pre-operative); hepatic function (chemotherapy planning)

### Diagnostic Criteria
1. Clinical suspicion based on symptoms + imaging
2. MRI with characteristic findings
3. Histopathological and molecular confirmation via tissue sampling
4. Integrated diagnosis per WHO 2021: histology + molecular markers = final classification and grade
sections.management:
### Acute
- **Vasogenic edema/raised ICP:**
  - Dexamethasone 4-16 mg/day (potent for vasogenic edema; start with 10 mg IV loading dose if severe); dramatic symptom improvement within hours
  - PPI for GI prophylaxis with steroids
  - Mannitol or hypertonic saline for acute herniation risk
  - Elevate HOB 30 degrees
- **Seizures:** Start antiseizure medication if seizures have occurred; levetiracetam first-line (no enzyme induction); prophylactic AEDs NOT recommended without prior seizure
- **DVT prophylaxis:** Mechanical + pharmacologic (LMWH) starting 24-48h post-operatively; glioma patients are high VTE risk

### Chronic/Maintenance
**GBM (grade 4, IDH-wildtype) — Stupp Protocol:**
1. **Maximal safe surgical resection:** Extent of resection correlates with survival; awake craniotomy with cortical mapping for tumors near eloquent areas; goal: gross total resection while preserving function
2. **Concurrent chemoradiation:** Temozolomide (75 mg/m²/day) + focal radiation (60 Gy in 30 fractions over 6 weeks)
3. **Adjuvant temozolomide:** 150-200 mg/m²/day x 5 days q28 days x 6 cycles
4. **Tumor-treating fields (TTFields/Optune):** Alternating electric fields via scalp electrodes; added to adjuvant TMZ improves survival; requires wearing device ≥18 hours/day
- **MGMT-methylated GBM:** Better temozolomide response; median survival ~21-23 months
- **MGMT-unmethylated GBM:** Poorer response; consider clinical trials; median survival ~12-15 months
- **Elderly/poor performance status:** Hypofractionated RT (40 Gy/15 fractions) ± temozolomide; TMZ monotherapy if MGMT methylated

**IDH-mutant astrocytoma (grades 2-3):**
- Surgical resection (maximal safe)
- Grade 2: Observation if favorable prognostic factors (age <40, GTR, no deficit) OR radiation + chemotherapy
- Grade 3: Radiation + PCV or temozolomide
- Vorasidenib (IDH inhibitor): Recently approved for grade 2 IDH-mutant gliomas; significantly prolongs progression-free survival

**Oligodendroglioma (IDH-mutant, 1p/19q codeleted):**
- Surgical resection
- Grade 2: Radiation + PCV (especially if high-risk features)
- Grade 3: Radiation + PCV (PCV superior to temozolomide for 1p/19q codeleted tumors)
- Best long-term prognosis among diffuse gliomas

**Recurrence:**
- Re-resection if feasible
- Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF; improves symptoms and PFS but not OS for GBM)
- Re-irradiation (selected cases)
- Clinical trials strongly encouraged

### Pharmacology
- Temozolomide — oral alkylating agent; monitor CBC (lymphopenia, thrombocytopenia); PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX during concurrent phase
- Systemic Corticosteroids — dexamethasone for edema; taper as soon as possible (significant side effects with chronic use)
- Antiepileptics — levetiracetam preferred (no CYP interactions with chemotherapy); avoid enzyme-inducing AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine — interact with TMZ)
- Anticoagulants — LMWH for VTE treatment/prophylaxis
- Bevacizumab — anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody; for recurrent GBM
- Vorasidenib — IDH inhibitor for grade 2 IDH-mutant glioma
- PCV regimen: Procarbazine + CCNU (lomustine) + vincristine
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Neurological assessment is your early warning system:** Perform focused neuro checks (GCS, pupil reactivity, motor strength, speech, orientation) every 2-4 hours post-operatively; report ANY decline immediately — could indicate hemorrhage, edema, or herniation
- **Post-craniotomy care:**
  - HOB elevated 30 degrees (reduce ICP, promote venous drainage)
  - Avoid Valsalva maneuvers (straining, coughing — increase ICP)
  - Surgical site assessment: monitor for CSF leak, wound infection, excessive swelling
  - Pain management (acetaminophen + low-dose opioid; avoid excessive sedation that masks neuro changes)
- **Steroid management:** Monitor blood glucose closely (dexamethasone causes hyperglycemia in most patients); PPI for GI prophylaxis; document taper schedule; teach patients NOT to abruptly stop steroids
- **Temozolomide teaching:**
  - Take on empty stomach to reduce nausea (1 hour before or 2 hours after meals)
  - Anti-emetics (ondansetron) 30 min before dose
  - CBC monitoring (especially lymphocyte count — severe lymphopenia common; PCP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX)
  - Contraception required (teratogenic)
- **TTFields (Optune) support:** Help patients manage scalp care (shave head, rotate array placement), skin irritation, device troubleshooting; emotional support for visible device wear; adherence counseling (≥18h/day)
- **Seizure precautions:** Keep padded side rails, suction, O2 at bedside; teach family seizure first aid; medication adherence counseling
- **VTE prevention:** Early mobilization, SCDs, LMWH administration; assess for calf tenderness, swelling; educate patient on signs of DVT/PE
- **Psychosocial support:**
  - GBM diagnosis is devastating — median survival is measured in months; early palliative care and goals-of-care conversations
  - Cognitive changes affect personality, relationships, independence
  - Support caregiver; refer to social work, chaplain, Neuro-oncology support groups
- **Advance care planning:** Initiate early while patient retains capacity; discuss preferences for aggressive treatment, clinical trials, hospice
sections.keyPoints:
- WHO 2021 classification is molecular-first: IDH mutation status + 1p/19q codeletion define the tumor type, not just histology
- GBM (IDH-wildtype, grade 4) is the most common and most aggressive — median survival 14-16 months with maximal treatment
- MGMT methylation predicts response to temozolomide — the most actionable biomarker in GBM
- Stupp protocol (surgery → concurrent TMZ/RT → adjuvant TMZ) remains standard of care for GBM
- 1p/19q codeletion defines oligodendrogliomas and predicts excellent chemosensitivity (PCV regimen)
- Dexamethasone is dramatically effective for tumor-associated edema but carries significant side effects — taper aggressively
- All glioma patients need VTE prophylaxis — highly prothrombotic tumors

clinicalPearls: **A new seizure in a previously healthy adult is a brain tumor until proven otherwise** — especially if focal; get MRI, not just EEG; low-grade gliomas often present with an isolated seizure and a normal neurological exam
**Ring enhancement + central necrosis on MRI in an adult = GBM until proven otherwise** — but always get tissue; brain abscess and lymphoma can mimic this appearance

**Dexamethasone is the most effective drug you'll give these patients** — a patient who comes in obtunded from vasogenic edema can be sitting up and talking within 12-24 hours of starting steroids; but it's a double-edged sword — steroid myopathy, hyperglycemia, immunosuppression, and insomnia are nearly universal with prolonged use

**Levetiracetam is the seizure medication for brain tumor patients** — enzyme-inducing AEDs (phenytoin, carbamazepine) accelerate the metabolism of temozolomide and other chemotherapeutics; this interaction matters

**Goals of care conversations need to happen at diagnosis, not at the end** — GBM patients lose cognitive capacity as the disease progresses; the window for meaningful advance care planning is at diagnosis, when the patient can still participate

signs: **Headache (50-60%):** Classically worse in morning (increased ICP from recumbent position), progressive, may be positional; often nonspecific early
**Seizures (20-40% at presentation; 60-80% during disease course):** Most common presenting symptom for low-grade gliomas; focal seizures reflect tumor location
**Focal neurological deficits:** Motor weakness, sensory changes, visual field cuts, aphasia — depend on tumor location
**Cognitive/personality changes:** Especially frontal lobe tumors; may be subtle initially (mistaken for depression or dementia)
**Signs of raised ICP:** Progressive headache, nausea/vomiting (especially morning), papilledema, altered consciousness, CN VI palsy (false localizing)
**Language dysfunction:** Tumors near Broca's or Wernicke's areas
**Low-grade gliomas:** Often present with isolated seizures in a young adult with otherwise normal exam
Increased intracranial pressure → herniation (medical emergency)
Tumor hemorrhage
Venous thromboembolism (20-30% — gliomas are highly prothrombotic)
Treatment toxicity: radiation necrosis, chemotherapy myelosuppression, steroid side effects
Seizures (ongoing)
Cognitive decline (from tumor, edema, treatment, or all three)
Mood disorders, fatigue
Tumor recurrence (universal in high-grade gliomas)

graves-disease.title: Graves Disease

sections.definition:
Graves disease is an autoimmune disorder caused by stimulating autoantibodies directed against the thyroid-stimulating hormone receptor (TSHR), resulting in unregulated thyroid hormone synthesis and secretion. It is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions, accounting for 60-80% of all cases. The disease may also involve extrathyroidal manifestations, particularly Graves orbitopathy (thyroid eye disease) and, rarely, pretibial myxedema and thyroid acropachy.

Unlike other causes of hyperthyroidism (toxic nodular goiter, thyroiditis), Graves disease produces sustained and often severe thyroid hormone excess driven by autoimmune stimulation rather than autonomous or inflammatory release.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** Affects approximately 2% of women and 0.2% of men (10:1 female-to-male ratio). Annual incidence is 20-40 per 100,000 population. Lifetime risk for women is approximately 3%.
- **Risk factors:** Female sex, family history of autoimmune thyroid disease, other autoimmune conditions (Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus, rheumatoid arthritis, celiac disease, vitiligo), smoking (especially for Graves orbitopathy — 7.7x risk), high iodine intake, psychological stress, postpartum period, HLA-DR3 genotype
- **Demographics:** Peak incidence in women aged 30-50. Can occur at any age including childhood. Slight increase in incidence observed in recent decades. More common in White and Asian populations compared to Black populations.
- **Mortality:** Untreated Graves disease carries significant cardiovascular morbidity. Thyroid storm has mortality of 10-30% even with aggressive treatment. Long-term excess thyroid hormone increases risk of Atrial Fibrillation (6-10x), osteoporotic fractures, and heart failure.
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — TSH receptor is a Gs-coupled GPCR; thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) are IgG antibodies that bind and permanently activate the TSH receptor, bypassing normal feedback suppression. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the HPT axis (hypothalamus→TRH→pituitary→TSH→thyroid→T3/T4→feedback suppression) is hijacked; TSI activates the receptor independently of TSH, making the feedback loop nonfunctional → unrestrained thyroid hormone production.*

Graves disease is driven by a breakdown in immune tolerance to thyroid antigens:

1. **Loss of immune tolerance:** Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3, CTLA-4, PTPN22 polymorphisms) combined with environmental triggers leads to activation of autoreactive B and T lymphocytes against the TSHR.
2. **TSHR autoantibody production:** Thyroid-stimulating immunoglobulins (TSI) bind to and activate the TSHR on thyroid follicular cells, mimicking the action of TSH. These antibodies are agonists — they stimulate cyclic AMP-mediated thyroid hormone synthesis without negative feedback control.
3. **Thyroid gland hyperplasia:** TSHR stimulation causes diffuse enlargement of the thyroid (goiter) with increased vascularity. Follicular cells become columnar, and colloid is depleted as hormone synthesis and secretion accelerate.
4. **Unregulated thyroid hormone excess:** T4 and T3 production increases dramatically. Elevated circulating thyroid hormones suppress pituitary TSH (TSH <0.01 mIU/L), but this has no effect because the gland is driven by autoantibodies, not TSH.
5. **Systemic hypermetabolic state:** Excess T3 increases basal metabolic rate, oxygen consumption, heat production, catecholamine sensitivity, and cardiac output. Effects include tachycardia, weight loss, tremor, anxiety, and heat intolerance.

### Graves Orbitopathy
- Orbital fibroblasts express TSHR and insulin-like growth factor 1 receptor (IGF1R)
- TSHR autoantibodies activate orbital fibroblasts, causing production of glycosaminoglycans (hyaluronic acid) and differentiation into adipocytes
- Expansion of orbital fat and extraocular muscles within the bony orbit causes proptosis, diplopia, and compression
- Clinically apparent in approximately 5% of Graves patients (subclinical orbital involvement detectable by imaging in 50-70%)

### Thyroid Storm
- Life-threatening exacerbation triggered by surgery, infection, trauma, iodine load, or abrupt discontinuation of antithyroid drugs
- Mechanism: acute surge in free thyroid hormones combined with catecholamine hypersensitivity
- Presents with high fever (>104degF/40degC), extreme tachycardia, delirium/psychosis, cardiac failure, hepatic dysfunction
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Hyperthyroidism (systemic):**
- Tachycardia, palpitations, widened pulse pressure
- Heat intolerance, excessive sweating, warm moist skin
- Unintentional weight loss despite increased appetite
- Tremor (fine distal), hyperreflexia, proximal muscle weakness
- Anxiety, irritability, insomnia, emotional lability
- Increased bowel frequency (not true diarrhea)
- Menstrual irregularities — oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea
- Diffuse non-tender goiter with audible bruit (increased vascularity)

**Graves Orbitopathy:**
- Proptosis (exophthalmos) — may be unilateral or asymmetric
- Lid retraction ("stare"), lid lag on downward gaze
- Periorbital edema, conjunctival injection, chemosis
- Diplopia from extraocular muscle enlargement
- Exposure keratitis from incomplete lid closure
- Optic neuropathy (severe cases — vision threatening)

**Other Graves-Specific Features:**
- Pretibial myxedema — waxy, indurated plaques on anterior shins (uncommon)
- Thyroid acropachy — clubbing and periosteal new bone formation (rare)

### Complications
- Atrial Fibrillation — occurs in 10-15% of hyperthyroid patients; can be presenting feature in elderly
- Heart failure — high-output initially, may progress to dilated cardiomyopathy
- Thyroid storm — medical emergency with 10-30% mortality
- Osteoporosis — accelerated bone resorption from thyroid hormone excess
- Ophthalmopathy progression — corneal ulceration, optic nerve compression, vision loss
- Pregnancy complications — miscarriage, preeclampsia, neonatal thyrotoxicosis (transplacental TSI passage)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **TSH:** Suppressed, typically <0.01 mIU/L (most sensitive screening test)
- **Free T4:** Elevated (confirms overt hyperthyroidism)
- **Free T3 or total T3:** May be disproportionately elevated ("T3 thyrotoxicosis"); check if TSH suppressed with normal free T4
- **TSHR antibodies (TRAb/TSI):** Positive in >95% of Graves disease. TSI is specific for stimulating activity. Useful for diagnosis without radioactive iodine uptake, in pregnancy (predicts neonatal thyrotoxicosis), and for monitoring disease activity.
- **Radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU):** Diffusely increased uptake (>35% at 24 hours) distinguishes Graves from thyroiditis (low/absent uptake). Contraindicated in pregnancy and breastfeeding.
- **Thyroid ultrasound with Doppler:** Diffuse enlargement with increased vascularity ("thyroid inferno" pattern). Used when RAIU is contraindicated.
- **CBC:** Monitor for baseline WBC before starting antithyroid drugs (agranulocytosis risk)
- **LFTs:** Baseline before treatment (antithyroid drugs can cause hepatotoxicity)

### Diagnostic Criteria
Diagnosis is clinical + biochemical:
1. Suppressed TSH with elevated free T4 and/or T3
2. Plus one of: positive TSHR antibodies (TRAb/TSI), diffusely increased RAIU, or diffuse goiter with Graves orbitopathy

**Burch-Wartofsky Score for Thyroid Storm:** Point-based system using temperature, heart rate, CNS effects, GI/hepatic dysfunction, heart failure status, and precipitant. Score ≥45 = thyroid storm; 25-44 = impending storm.
sections.management:
### Acute
**Thyroid Storm (medical emergency):**
1. **Beta-Blockers** — Propranolol 60-80 mg PO q4h (preferred — also blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion) or esmolol drip for severe cases. Controls tachycardia and sympathetic symptoms.
2. **Antithyroid drugs** — Propylthiouracil (PTU) 200-300 mg PO/NG q6h (preferred in storm — blocks T4-to-T3 conversion). Methimazole is alternative.
3. **Iodine** — Lugol's solution or saturated potassium iodide (SSKI) given 1 hour AFTER first PTU dose (Wolff-Chaikoff effect blocks new hormone synthesis). If given before antithyroid drug, iodine will fuel new hormone production.
4. **Glucocorticoids** — Hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h (blocks T4-to-T3 conversion, treats potential adrenal insufficiency)
5. **Supportive** — Cooling blankets (avoid aspirin — displaces T4 from binding proteins), IV fluids, treat precipitant (antibiotics for infection)

### Chronic/Maintenance
**Three definitive treatment options:**

**1. Antithyroid Drugs (ATDs):**
- Methimazole is first-line (better side effect profile, once-daily dosing, higher remission rate vs. PTU)
- Starting dose: methimazole 10-30 mg/day depending on severity
- Titrate to maintain euthyroidism (free T4, TSH normalization)
- 12-18 month course recommended; ~40-50% achieve lasting remission after course completion
- PTU reserved for first trimester pregnancy (methimazole is teratogenic — aplasia cutis, choanal atresia) and thyroid storm

**2. Radioactive Iodine (RAI — I-131):**
- Definitive therapy; destroys thyroid tissue over 6-18 weeks
- Single oral dose in most cases
- Results in hypothyroidism in >80% of patients (lifelong levothyroxine required)
- Contraindicated in pregnancy, breastfeeding, and moderate-severe active orbitopathy (can worsen eye disease)
- Avoid close contact with children/pregnant individuals for several days post-dose

**3. Total Thyroidectomy:**
- Definitive and immediate
- Indications: large goiter with compressive symptoms, coexisting thyroid nodule suspicious for malignancy, moderate-severe orbitopathy, patient preference
- Risks: hypoparathyroidism (1-2%), recurrent laryngeal nerve injury (<1%)
- Results in permanent hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine

**Graves Orbitopathy Management:**
- Smoking cessation (single most important modifiable factor)
- Selenium supplementation (200 mcg/day) for mild disease
- IV glucocorticoid pulses for moderate-severe active disease
- Teprotumumab (anti-IGF1R monoclonal antibody) — reduces proptosis and diplopia; FDA-approved for thyroid eye disease
- Orbital decompression surgery for sight-threatening disease or disfiguring proptosis

### Pharmacology
- Methimazole — First-line antithyroid drug; inhibits thyroid peroxidase. Risk: agranulocytosis (~0.3%), hepatotoxicity, teratogenic in first trimester
- Propylthiouracil (PTU) — Second-line; additionally blocks peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion. Risk: agranulocytosis, hepatic failure (FDA black box warning), vasculitis
- Propranolol — Symptomatic relief of tachycardia, tremor, anxiety; also inhibits T4-to-T3 conversion at high doses
- Radioactive iodine (I-131) — Definitive ablation therapy
- Teprotumumab — Anti-IGF1R for Graves orbitopathy
- Levothyroxine — Lifelong replacement after RAI or thyroidectomy
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Monitor for agranulocytosis in any patient on methimazole or PTU.** Teach patients to report sore throat, fever, or mouth ulcers immediately — these can be the first signs. If WBC drops below 1,000/mm3 or absolute neutrophil count <500, the drug must be stopped immediately and the patient needs urgent evaluation. This is a rare (0.3%) but life-threatening adverse effect.
- **Know the order of medications in thyroid storm: antithyroid drug FIRST, then iodine.** If iodine is given before PTU or methimazole, it provides substrate for new thyroid hormone synthesis (Jod-Basedow effect), worsening the storm. Iodine must be given at least 1 hour after the first antithyroid drug dose.
- **Weight and nutritional assessment are nursing priorities.** Hyperthyroid patients are in a hypermetabolic state and may lose significant weight. Ensure high-calorie, high-protein diet. Monitor daily weights. Patients may need 3,000+ calories/day to maintain weight until euthyroid.
- **Do not use aspirin for fever in thyroid storm.** Aspirin displaces T4 from thyroid-binding globulin, increasing free T4 levels and potentially worsening the storm. Use acetaminophen and cooling blankets instead.
- **Eye care for Graves orbitopathy patients:** Artificial tears during the day, lubricating ointment at night, elevate head of bed 30 degrees (reduces periorbital edema), sunglasses for photosensitivity. Assess visual acuity and report any changes — optic neuropathy is an emergency.
- **Cardiac monitoring during initial treatment.** Hyperthyroid patients are at risk for atrial fibrillation, tachyarrhythmias, and heart failure. Continuous telemetry is appropriate for severe thyrotoxicosis. Assess for signs of new-onset Atrial Fibrillation.
- **Medication adherence counseling is critical.** ATD courses last 12-18 months. Patients who feel better may stop medication prematurely, precipitating relapse or storm. Emphasize the importance of completing the full course and attending follow-up labs.
- **Pregnancy counseling:** Methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester. Women of childbearing age on methimazole need reliable contraception. If pregnancy occurs, switch to PTU for the first trimester, then back to methimazole for the second and third trimesters (lower hepatotoxicity risk).
sections.keyPoints:
- Graves disease is the most common cause of hyperthyroidism, affecting ~2% of women and ~0.2% of men (10:1 female ratio)
- Caused by TSHR-stimulating autoantibodies (TSI) that drive unregulated thyroid hormone production
- Diagnosis: suppressed TSH, elevated free T4/T3, positive TSHR antibodies or diffusely increased RAIU
- Three treatment options: antithyroid drugs (methimazole first-line, 12-18 month course, ~40-50% lasting remission), radioactive iodine, or total thyroidectomy
- Methimazole is teratogenic in the first trimester; PTU is used during early pregnancy
- Agranulocytosis is a rare but life-threatening risk of all antithyroid drugs — patients must report fever and sore throat immediately
- Thyroid storm is a medical emergency (10-30% mortality) requiring beta-blockers, antithyroid drugs, iodine (after ATD), and glucocorticoids
- Teprotumumab (anti-IGF1R) is a breakthrough therapy for Graves orbitopathy, reducing proptosis and diplopia

clinicalPearls: **An elderly patient with new-onset atrial fibrillation and unexplained weight loss needs a TSH checked.** Apathetic thyrotoxicosis in the elderly can present without classic hyperadrenergic symptoms — just AFib, fatigue, and weight loss. A suppressed TSH may be the only clue.

**The sequence matters in thyroid storm: ATD first, iodine second.** Giving iodine before blocking new hormone synthesis is like throwing fuel on a fire. PTU goes first, wait one hour, then give iodine. Reverse the order and you can make a dying patient worse.

**Radioactive iodine can flare Graves orbitopathy.** Patients with moderate-severe active eye disease should NOT receive RAI. If RAI is chosen in patients with mild eye disease, concurrent low-dose prednisone (0.3-0.5 mg/kg for 6-8 weeks) reduces the risk of orbitopathy worsening.

**A Graves patient who develops a sore throat on methimazole is a medical emergency until proven otherwise.** Do not dismiss it as a viral URI. Check a CBC with differential immediately. Agranulocytosis peaks in the first 90 days of therapy and can progress to fatal sepsis within hours.

**After RAI or thyroidectomy, Graves is not "cured" — it is exchanged for a different chronic disease.** The patient will be hypothyroid for life and needs lifelong levothyroxine, regular TSH monitoring, and education that medication adherence is permanent. Ensure this is clearly communicated before definitive treatment.

signs: Tachycardia, palpitations, widened pulse pressure
Heat intolerance, excessive sweating, warm moist skin
Unintentional weight loss despite increased appetite
Tremor (fine distal), hyperreflexia, proximal muscle weakness
Anxiety, irritability, insomnia, emotional lability
Increased bowel frequency (not true diarrhea)
Menstrual irregularities — oligomenorrhea, amenorrhea
Diffuse non-tender goiter with audible bruit (increased vascularity)
Proptosis (exophthalmos) — may be unilateral or asymmetric
Lid retraction ("stare"), lid lag on downward gaze
Periorbital edema, conjunctival injection, chemosis
Diplopia from extraocular muscle enlargement
Exposure keratitis from incomplete lid closure
Optic neuropathy (severe cases — vision threatening)
Pretibial myxedema — waxy, indurated plaques on anterior shins (uncommon)
Thyroid acropachy — clubbing and periosteal new bone formation (rare)
Atrial Fibrillation — occurs in 10-15% of hyperthyroid patients; can be presenting feature in elderly
Heart failure — high-output initially, may progress to dilated cardiomyopathy
Thyroid storm — medical emergency with 10-30% mortality
Osteoporosis — accelerated bone resorption from thyroid hormone excess
Ophthalmopathy progression — corneal ulceration, optic nerve compression, vision loss
Pregnancy complications — miscarriage, preeclampsia, neonatal thyrotoxicosis (transplacental TSI passage)

heart-failure-with-preserved-ejection-fr.title: Heart Failure with Preserved Ejection Fraction

sections.definition: Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction (HFpEF) is a clinical syndrome of heart failure symptoms (dyspnea, exercise intolerance, fluid retention) with LVEF ≥ 50% and evidence of diastolic dysfunction (impaired ventricular relaxation and/or increased stiffness). The ventricle contracts normally but cannot fill adequately, leading to elevated filling pressures and congestion. HFpEF accounts for ~50% of all HF cases and is the predominant form in elderly women. Unlike HFrEF, effective treatments have been limited until recently.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~50% of all heart failure; prevalence increasing faster than HFrEF (aging population, obesity epidemic); ~3-4 million in the US
- **Risk factors:** Advanced age (strongest), female sex, Hypertension (most common comorbidity; ~60-80%), obesity, diabetes, Atrial Fibrillation, coronary artery disease, chronic kidney disease, obstructive sleep apnea, physical inactivity
- **Demographics:** Predominantly affects older women (>65); 5-year mortality ~35-40% (slightly better than HFrEF but still substantial); hospitalization rates similar to HFrEF; quality of life often worse than HFrEF
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — HFpEF = impaired relaxation (diastolic dysfunction); the ventricle cannot fill adequately at normal pressures, so filling pressures must rise to maintain SV — explaining the pulmonary congestion despite preserved EF. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — compensatory mechanisms (RAAS, SNS) increase preload to maintain CO, but in a stiff ventricle this drives filling pressures up and causes pulmonary congestion rather than helping.*

HFpEF is increasingly understood as a systemic disease, not just a cardiac problem:

1. **Comorbidity-driven systemic inflammation:** Obesity, diabetes, HTN, CKD → systemic microvascular endothelial inflammation → reduced nitric oxide bioavailability → decreased cyclic GMP and protein kinase G activity → coronary microvascular dysfunction → myocardial fibrosis and stiffening
   - **Sarcomere dysfunction:** Hypophosphorylation of titin (the macromolecular "spring" protein) → stiffer cardiomyocytes with increased passive force at the same sarcomere length; microtubule abnormalities (increased detyrosination) also contribute
2. **Impaired ventricular relaxation:** Active process requiring ATP (calcium reuptake into SR); impaired by ischemia, hypertrophy, fibrosis → slowed early diastolic filling
3. **Increased ventricular stiffness (decreased compliance):** LVH (concentric), myocardial fibrosis, advanced glycation end products (diabetes) → steep pressure-volume curve → small changes in volume → large increases in filling pressure
4. **Elevated filling pressures:** ↑ LVEDP → transmitted to left atrium → pulmonary veins → pulmonary congestion (dyspnea, crackles)
5. **Impaired cardiac reserve:** At rest, patients may compensate; with exercise, inability to augment diastolic filling → disproportionate rise in filling pressures → exercise intolerance (hallmark symptom)
   - **Chronotropic incompetence:** Inability to adequately increase heart rate with exercise; further limits cardiac output augmentation; particularly common in obese HFpEF phenotype; also associated with renal dysfunction
6. **Left atrial dysfunction:** LA pressure overload → LA dilation → Atrial Fibrillation → loss of atrial kick → further worsens diastolic filling (vicious cycle)
7. **Pulmonary hypertension:** Chronic elevated LA pressure → reactive pulmonary vasoconstriction → RV pressure overload → RV failure → systemic congestion

### HFpEF vs. HFrEF
| Feature | HFrEF | HFpEF |
|---------|-------|-------|
| EF | ≤ 40% | ≥ 50% |
| Problem | Impaired contraction (systolic) | Impaired filling (diastolic) |
| LV size | Dilated | Normal or concentric LVH |
| Typical patient | Younger male, post-MI | Older female, hypertensive, obese |
| Proven drug therapies | Extensive (ARNI, BB, MRA, SGLT2i) | Limited (SGLT2i, MRA, diuretics) |

> **HFmrEF (EF 41-49%):** "Mildly reduced EF" — intermediate phenotype; increasingly treated like HFrEF
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Exercise intolerance** (hallmark — patients are often fine at rest but become severely dyspneic with activity)
- Dyspnea on exertion
- Orthopnea, PND (same as HFrEF — cannot distinguish clinically)
- Peripheral edema, weight gain
- Fatigue
- JVD, pulmonary crackles (congestion)
- S4 gallop (atrial contraction against stiff ventricle — classic but not always present)
- Often presents during acute triggers: AFib onset, uncontrolled HTN, dietary indiscretion, infection, anemia

### Complications
- Acute decompensated HF (frequently triggered by AFib, HTN crisis, or volume overload)
- Atrial fibrillation (both cause and consequence)
- Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure
- Reduced quality of life and functional capacity
- Hospitalizations (as frequent as HFrEF)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **BNP/NT-proBNP** | Elevated but often LOWER than in HFrEF with equivalent symptoms; may be normal at rest (exercise provocation unmasks); obesity lowers BNP (adipose tissue clears it) |
| **Echocardiogram** | EF ≥ 50%; LVH (concentric); impaired diastolic filling parameters (E/e' ratio > 14, elevated LA volume index > 34 mL/m², elevated TR velocity > 2.8 m/s); LA dilation |
| **Exercise echo or invasive hemodynamics** | May be needed when resting echo is non-diagnostic; exercise unmasks elevated filling pressures (PAWP > 25 mmHg with exercise = diagnostic) |
| **ECG** | LVH, AFib, left atrial enlargement |
| **BMP, CBC, TSH, iron studies** | Same workup as HFrEF; identify comorbidities |

### H2FPEF Score (0-9 points; AUC 0.84 vs invasive testing)
| Component | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **H**eavy (BMI > 30) | 2 points |
| **H**ypertension (≥2 antihypertensive meds) | 1 point |
| **F**ibrillation (atrial, paroxysmal or persistent) | 3 points |
| **P**ulmonary HTN (PASP > 35 on echo) | 1 point |
| **E**lder (age > 60) | 1 point |
| **F**illing pressure (E/e' > 9) | 1 point |

- Score 0 → HFpEF excluded; Score ≥ 5 → HFpEF diagnosed; 1-4 → indeterminate (consider exercise RHC)

### HFA-PEFF Score (ESC)
Incorporates natriuretic peptides + echocardiographic features (LA size, diastolic parameters, LV mass, TR velocity, GLS). Score < 2 excludes HFpEF; score ≥ 6 is diagnostic.

### HFpEF Phenogroups (Phenomapping)
Three major phenogroups identified across studies:
1. **Older, vascular aging** — predominant arterial stiffness and diastolic dysfunction
2. **Metabolic, obese** — obesity-driven inflammation, epicardial fat, ventricular interdependence from RV dilation in constrained pericardial space
3. **Younger, natriuretic peptide deficiency** — often with lower BNP despite congestion
sections.management:
### Cornerstone: Treat Congestion and Comorbidities

| Intervention | Details |
|-------------|---------|
| **SGLT2 inhibitors** | **First disease-modifying therapy with proven benefit in HFpEF!** Empagliflozin (EMPEROR-Preserved) and dapagliflozin (DELIVER) reduced HF hospitalization; now guideline-recommended for ALL HFpEF |
| **Diuretics** | Loop diuretics for congestion; titrate to euvolemia; no mortality benefit but essential for symptoms |
| **MRA (spironolactone)** | TOPCAT trial showed benefit in subset (Americas); reduces HF hospitalization; guideline-recommended; monitor K+ |
| **Blood pressure control** | Target < 130/80; particularly important as HTN drives LVH and diastolic dysfunction |
| **Rate/rhythm control for AFib** | Uncontrolled AFib is a major destabilizer; rate control or rhythm control per clinical picture |
| **Weight loss** | Obesity is a primary driver; weight loss improves exercise capacity, filling pressures, and QoL; GLP-1 agonists under active study |
| **Exercise training** | BEST proven intervention for improving exercise capacity and QoL in HFpEF; cardiac rehab should be prescribed for ALL patients |
| **Treat sleep apnea** | CPAP if OSA present; improves diastolic function and BP |

### NOT Proven in HFpEF (Unlike HFrEF)
- ACE inhibitors/ARBs: No mortality benefit (PEP-CHF, CHARM-Preserved trials negative)
- ARNI (sacubitril/valsartan): PARAGON-HF narrowly missed primary endpoint; possible benefit in lower EF range and women
- Beta-blockers: No mortality benefit in HFpEF (may worsen exercise intolerance by limiting chronotropic response)
- Digoxin: No proven benefit
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Same as HFrEF: Daily weights, I&O, lung sounds, JVD, edema, orthostatic vitals
  - Pay attention to exercise tolerance — HFpEF patients may look fine at rest
  - Monitor for AFib (very common trigger for decompensation)
  - BP monitoring (HTN control is critical)
- **Key differences from HFrEF nursing:**
  - These patients are more sensitive to volume changes — small fluid shifts cause big symptom changes
  - Over-diuresis is a real risk: Stiff ventricle needs adequate preload to fill → too dry = low CO and hypotension
  - AFib with loss of atrial kick is particularly devastating (stiff ventricle relies heavily on atrial contraction for filling)
- **Patient teaching:**
  - Daily weights; fluid and sodium restriction
  - EXERCISE is a prescription — cardiac rehab referral
  - Weight management (obesity is a primary driver)
  - BP medication adherence
  - Report: Increasing dyspnea, weight gain, new palpitations (AFib)
  - Avoid NSAIDs (fluid retention)

clinicalPearls: **HFpEF is NOT "mild heart failure."** Morbidity, hospitalization rates, and quality of life impact are comparable to HFrEF. The only difference was the lack of effective drugs — and SGLT2 inhibitors have changed that.

**SGLT2 inhibitors are now the FIRST proven disease-modifying therapy for HFpEF.** EMPEROR-Preserved and DELIVER are landmark trials. Every HFpEF patient should be on empagliflozin or dapagliflozin unless contraindicated.

**Exercise is the best medicine for HFpEF.** Exercise training consistently improves peak VO2, exercise duration, and quality of life in HFpEF trials. It's better evidence than any drug except SGLT2 inhibitors. Prescribe cardiac rehab.

**Over-diuresis in HFpEF is dangerous.** The stiff ventricle needs adequate preload. If you make a HFpEF patient too dry, they become hypotensive and symptomatic from low cardiac output. It's a narrower "sweet spot" than HFrEF.

**BNP can be falsely low in obese HFpEF patients.** Adipose tissue produces neprilysin which degrades BNP. An obese patient with HFpEF symptoms and a BNP of 90 may actually have significant HF. Don't be falsely reassured.

**New AFib in a HFpEF patient often triggers acute decompensation.** The stiff ventricle depends on the atrial kick for 25-30% of filling. Lose the atrial kick + rapid rate = acute pulmonary edema. Rate and/or rhythm control is urgent.

**HFpEF is a systemic disease.** It's not just the heart — it's obesity, diabetes, HTN, CKD, inflammation, and deconditioning working together. Treating any ONE comorbidity in isolation is insufficient. A multifaceted approach is required.

**~75% of HFpEF patients have hypertension.** HTN induces myocardial overload and systemic inflammation → dysfunction and remodeling. Target SBP < 130 mmHg per guidelines. Note: diastolic BP ≥90 OR <60 mmHg both associated with worse outcomes (TOPCAT data).

**The H2FPEF score is a practical bedside diagnostic tool.** Only 6 variables, all easily available (BMI, HTN meds, AFib, age, PASP, E/e'). Score ≥5 = diagnostic. Score 0 = excluded. Use it when the diagnosis is uncertain.

signs: **Exercise intolerance** (hallmark — patients are often fine at rest but become severely dyspneic with activity)
Dyspnea on exertion
Orthopnea, PND (same as HFrEF — cannot distinguish clinically)
Peripheral edema, weight gain
Fatigue
JVD, pulmonary crackles (congestion)
S4 gallop (atrial contraction against stiff ventricle — classic but not always present)
Often presents during acute triggers: AFib onset, uncontrolled HTN, dietary indiscretion, infection, anemia
Acute decompensated HF (frequently triggered by AFib, HTN crisis, or volume overload)
Atrial fibrillation (both cause and consequence)
Pulmonary hypertension → right heart failure
Reduced quality of life and functional capacity
Hospitalizations (as frequent as HFrEF)

heart-failure-with-reduced-ejection-frac.title: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

sections.definition: Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) is a clinical syndrome in which the heart is unable to pump sufficient blood to meet metabolic demands due to impaired systolic (contractile) function. Defined by LVEF ≤ 40%. Previously called "systolic heart failure." Characterized by symptoms of congestion (dyspnea, edema) and/or low cardiac output (fatigue, exercise intolerance). HFrEF carries significant morbidity and mortality despite advances in treatment.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~6.7 million adults in the US have HF; ~50% are HFrEF; ~1 million new cases/year; prevalence increasing (aging population, improved MI survival)
- **Risk factors:** Prior MI (most common cause), Hypertension, coronary artery disease, valvular heart disease, dilated cardiomyopathy, myocarditis, alcohol abuse, cardiotoxic chemotherapy (doxorubicin, trastuzumab), familial/genetic cardiomyopathy, tachycardia-mediated (chronic Atrial Fibrillation)
- **Demographics:** 5-year mortality ~50% (worse than many cancers); leading cause of hospitalization in adults > 65; Black patients have highest incidence and worst outcomes; men slightly more than women for HFrEF
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — in HFrEF the Starling curve is flattened and shifted right; higher filling pressures produce less output. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — RAAS and SNS activation are initially compensatory but become maladaptive, driving remodeling and worsening the cycle. Oxygen Delivery and Consumption — reduced CO directly reduces DO2; compensatory mechanisms (↑HR, ↑extraction) eventually fail.*

1. **Initial myocardial injury** (MI, chronic HTN, valvular disease, toxin, genetic) → reduced contractile function → decreased stroke volume and cardiac output
2. **Neurohormonal activation** (compensatory but ultimately maladaptive):
   - **Sympathetic nervous system:** ↑ HR, ↑ contractility, vasoconstriction → increased afterload → increased myocardial oxygen demand
   - **RAAS activation:** Angiotensin II → vasoconstriction + aldosterone → Na+/water retention → volume overload → increased preload (see ACE Inhibitors, ARBs)
   - **ADH release:** Water retention → dilutional hyponatremia
   - **Natriuretic peptide release** (BNP/NT-proBNP): Counterregulatory → vasodilation, natriuresis; overwhelmed in advanced HF; used as biomarker
3. **Ventricular remodeling:** Chronic neurohormonal activation → myocyte hypertrophy, fibrosis, chamber dilation → further reduces EF → worsening HF (vicious cycle)
4. **Forward failure:** Low cardiac output → fatigue, exercise intolerance, renal hypoperfusion (cardiorenal syndrome), cool extremities
5. **Backward failure:** Elevated filling pressures → pulmonary congestion (left-sided: dyspnea, crackles, orthopnea, PND) and systemic congestion (right-sided: JVD, hepatomegaly, peripheral edema, ascites)

### NYHA Functional Classification
| Class | Symptoms |
|-------|---------|
| **I** | No limitation of physical activity |
| **II** | Slight limitation; comfortable at rest; ordinary activity causes symptoms |
| **III** | Marked limitation; comfortable at rest; less than ordinary activity causes symptoms |
| **IV** | Unable to perform any physical activity without symptoms; symptoms at rest |

### ACC/AHA Stages
| Stage | Description | Intervention Focus |
|-------|------------|-------------------|
| **A** | At risk (HTN, DM, CAD) but no structural disease or symptoms | Risk factor management |
| **B** | Structural heart disease (LVH, low EF, prior MI) but no symptoms | Neurohormonal blockade (ACE-I/ARB, beta-blocker) |
| **C** | Structural disease + current or prior symptoms | Full GDMT + device therapy |
| **D** | Refractory HF requiring advanced therapies | Transplant, LVAD, palliative care |
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Left-sided (pulmonary congestion):**
- Dyspnea on exertion (most common early symptom)
- Orthopnea (dyspnea lying flat — ask "how many pillows?")
- Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND — wakes up gasping 1-2 hr after lying down)
- Crackles/rales on auscultation (pulmonary edema)
- S3 gallop (pathologic in adults; indicates volume overload/ventricular dysfunction)
- Tachycardia, tachypnea
- Cough (especially nocturnal; may be pink-tinged frothy sputum in severe pulmonary edema)

**Right-sided (systemic congestion):**
- Jugular venous distension (JVD)
- Peripheral edema (dependent: ankles upright, sacral if bedbound)
- Hepatomegaly, hepatojugular reflux
- Ascites (advanced)
- Weight gain (fluid retention)

**Low output:**
- Fatigue, exercise intolerance
- Cool extremities, weak pulses
- Altered mental status (decreased cerebral perfusion)
- Oliguria (decreased renal perfusion)

### Complications
- Acute decompensated heart failure (see Acute Heart Failure)
- Cardiogenic Shock
- Arrhythmias (AFib, VT/VF — leading cause of death in mild-moderate HF)
- Cardiorenal syndrome
- Thromboembolism (low EF → stasis → LV thrombus)
- Cardiac cachexia
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **BNP** | > 100 pg/mL suggestive of HF; > 400 pg/mL highly likely |
| **NT-proBNP** | > 300 pg/mL suggestive; age-stratified cutoffs for acute HF (> 450 if < 50 yr; > 900 if 50-75; > 1800 if > 75) |
| **Echocardiogram** | CORNERSTONE: LVEF ≤ 40% = HFrEF; wall motion abnormalities; chamber dimensions; valvular assessment; diastolic function |
| **BMP** | Renal function (cardiorenal), electrolytes (K+ on ACE/ARB/MRA; Na+ — hyponatremia = poor prognosis) |
| **CBC** | Anemia worsens HF |
| **TSH** | Thyroid disease as cause/contributor |
| **Iron studies** | Iron deficiency common and treatable (IV iron improves symptoms/QoL) |
| **Chest X-ray** | Cardiomegaly, pulmonary edema (cephalization, Kerley B lines, pleural effusions, bat-wing pattern) |
| **ECG** | LVH, bundle branch block (LBBB → CRT candidacy), arrhythmias, Q waves (prior MI) |
sections.management:
### Guideline-Directed Medical Therapy (GDMT) — The "Fantastic Four"

Modern HFrEF management centers on 4 drug classes initiated as early as possible:

| Drug Class | Agent | Target Dose | Mechanism | Mortality Benefit |
|-----------|-------|------------|-----------|------------------|
| **ACE-I or ARB** (or ARNI) | Sacubitril/valsartan (Entresto) preferred; enalapril, lisinopril, losartan | Entresto 97/103 mg BID | Neurohormonal blockade; ARNI adds neprilysin inhibition → ↑ natriuretic peptides | Yes (PARADIGM-HF) |
| **Beta-blocker** (evidence-based only) | Carvedilol, metoprolol succinate (XL), bisoprolol | Carvedilol 25 mg BID; metoprolol XL 200 mg daily | ↓ HR, ↓ remodeling, anti-arrhythmic | Yes (COPERNICUS, MERIT-HF) |
| **MRA** (mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist) | Spironolactone, eplerenone | Spironolactone 25-50 mg daily | ↓ Aldosterone effects (fibrosis, Na retention, K wasting) | Yes (RALES, EMPHASIS-HF) |
| **SGLT2 inhibitor** | Dapagliflozin (Farxiga), empagliflozin (Jardiance) | Dapa 10 mg; empa 10 mg daily | Osmotic diuresis, cardioprotective mechanisms (multiple); benefit even WITHOUT diabetes | Yes (DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced) |

**Additional medications:**
- **Diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide):** For congestion; symptom relief; NO mortality benefit; titrate to euvolemia (see Diuretics)
- **Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate:** Alternative if ACE/ARB/ARNI not tolerated; particularly beneficial in Black patients (A-HeFT trial)
- **Digoxin:** Reduces HF hospitalizations; no mortality benefit; narrow TI (0.5-0.9 ng/mL target)
- **IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose):** If iron deficient (ferritin < 100, or 100-300 with TSAT < 20%); improves symptoms and exercise capacity (CONFIRM-HF, AFFIRM-AHF)
- **Ivabradine (Corlanor):** If sinus rhythm with HR ≥ 70 on max beta-blocker; funny channel (If) blocker; reduces HF hospitalization

### Device Therapy
| Device | Indication | Benefit |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| **ICD** (implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) | EF ≤ 35% despite ≥ 3 months of GDMT; NYHA II-III; expected survival > 1 year | Prevents sudden cardiac death (VT/VF) |
| **CRT** (cardiac resynchronization therapy) | EF ≤ 35% + LBBB + QRS ≥ 150 ms; NYHA II-IV despite GDMT | Improves synchrony, remodeling, symptoms; mortality benefit |
| **CRT-D** | Meets criteria for both CRT and ICD | Combined benefits |

### Advanced Therapies (Stage D)
- LVAD (left ventricular assist device) — bridge to transplant or destination therapy
- Heart transplant — for refractory Stage D HF
- Palliative care — concurrent with all stages; essential for symptom management and goals of care discussions

### Pharmacology
- **ACE Inhibitors** / **ARBs** — GDMT pillar; reduce afterload, prevent remodeling; sacubitril/valsartan (ARNI) superior to ACEi alone
- **Beta-Blockers** — carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol; reduce mortality 30-35%; start low, titrate slow
- **Diuretics** — loop diuretics for volume management (symptom relief, not mortality benefit); thiazide for resistance
- **Vasopressors and Inotropes** — dobutamine, milrinone for acute decompensation with low output
- **Anticoagulants** — warfarin or DOAC if concurrent AFib or LV thrombus
- **Antiarrhythmics** — amiodarone for AF rate/rhythm control; avoid class IC agents in structural heart disease
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - **Daily weights** (same time, same scale, same clothing) — most reliable indicator of fluid status; report gain > 2 lbs/day or 5 lbs/week
  - I&O strictly (fluid restriction typically 1.5-2 L/day in advanced HF)
  - Lung sounds (crackles = congestion), JVD, peripheral edema (pitting scale), hepatojugular reflux
  - Hemodynamics if PA catheter: elevated PAWP (> 18 mmHg), decreased CI (< 2.2 L/min/m²)
  - Monitor K+ closely (ACE/ARB/MRA all raise K+; diuretics lower K+; net effect varies)
  - BNP/NT-proBNP trending (decreasing = responding to treatment)
  - Fall risk (orthostatic hypotension from vasodilators/diuretics)
- **Medication management:**
  - Titrate GDMT to target doses (most patients are undertreated — doses too low)
  - Beta-blockers: Start low, go slow; do NOT start during acute decompensation (start when euvolemic)
  - ACE-I/ARNI: Monitor BP, K+, creatinine; 36-hour washout when switching ACE-I → ARNI (angioedema risk)
  - MRA: Monitor K+ within 1 week of starting and after dose changes; hold if K+ > 5.5
  - SGLT2i: Monitor for volume depletion, UTI/yeast; hold during acute illness
- **Patient teaching:**
  - Daily weights — call provider if gain > 2 lbs in a day or 5 lbs in a week
  - Fluid restriction (1.5-2 L/day); sodium restriction (< 2000 mg/day)
  - Take medications as prescribed — "feeling better" doesn't mean stop meds
  - Report: Increasing dyspnea, new or worsening edema, waking up short of breath, persistent cough, dizziness/lightheadedness, decreased urine output
  - Activity: Stay active as tolerated; cardiac rehab is beneficial; rest when symptomatic
  - Avoid NSAIDs (fluid retention, worsen renal function; see NSAIDs)
  - Get influenza and pneumococcal vaccines annually
  - Advance care planning discussions (especially Stage C-D)

clinicalPearls: **The "Fantastic Four" should be started in ALL HFrEF patients unless contraindicated:** ARNI (or ACE/ARB) + evidence-based beta-blocker + MRA + SGLT2 inhibitor. Each has independent mortality benefit. Together they reduce mortality by ~60-70% compared to no treatment. Most patients are UNDER-dosed.
**SGLT2 inhibitors benefit HFrEF patients even WITHOUT diabetes.** DAPA-HF and EMPEROR-Reduced proved this definitively. If your HFrEF patient isn't on dapagliflozin or empagliflozin, ask why.

**Don't start beta-blockers during acute decompensation.** They can worsen acute HF by reducing contractility when the patient is already in crisis. Stabilize first (diuresis, vasodilators), then start low-dose beta-blocker when euvolemic.
**S3 gallop in an adult is pathologic until proven otherwise.** It indicates elevated LV filling pressure and ventricular dysfunction. If you hear an S3, think HF.
**Hyponatremia in HF is a BAD prognostic sign.** It reflects severe neurohormonal activation (excess ADH). Fluid restriction is the treatment — not sodium supplementation.

**Iron deficiency is present in ~50% of HFrEF patients** and independently worsens symptoms, exercise capacity, and outcomes. IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) improves QoL even without anemia. Check iron studies on every HF patient.

**Metoprolol tartrate is NOT the same as metoprolol succinate.** Only metoprolol succinate (XL/ER) has mortality data in HFrEF (MERIT-HF). Tartrate is IR and not evidence-based for HFrEF. Carvedilol and bisoprolol are the other evidence-based options.

**Weight gain precedes clinical congestion by days.** Daily weights catch fluid retention BEFORE the patient becomes symptomatic. A 2-lb gain today means crackles and edema in 2-3 days if not addressed.

signs: Dyspnea on exertion (most common early symptom)
Orthopnea (dyspnea lying flat — ask "how many pillows?")
Paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea (PND — wakes up gasping 1-2 hr after lying down)
Crackles/rales on auscultation (pulmonary edema)
S3 gallop (pathologic in adults; indicates volume overload/ventricular dysfunction)
Tachycardia, tachypnea
Cough (especially nocturnal; may be pink-tinged frothy sputum in severe pulmonary edema)
Jugular venous distension (JVD)
Peripheral edema (dependent: ankles upright, sacral if bedbound)

Ascites (advanced)
Weight gain (fluid retention)

Altered mental status (decreased cerebral perfusion)
Oliguria (decreased renal perfusion)
