sections.keyPoints:
- ICH is the deadliest stroke subtype with 40% 30-day mortality and high morbidity; yet early pessimism and care withdrawal contribute to poor outcomes
- Hypertension is the most important cause and most important target for both acute management and secondary prevention
- Hematoma expansion in the first hours is the major driver of early deterioration; the "spot sign" on CTA predicts expansion
- Acute SBP lowering to <140 mmHg is safe and may improve outcomes (INTERACT2)
- Rapid coagulopathy reversal is critical in anticoagulant-related ICH (4F-PCC for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet alfa for Xa inhibitors)
- Cerebellar hemorrhage >= 3 cm with deterioration is a neurosurgical emergency requiring evacuation
- Anticoagulant restart decisions require careful balancing of thrombotic vs hemorrhagic risk on an individual basis

clinicalPearls: **ICH has surpassed many conditions to become the 4th leading cause of premature death globally (2019).** The ~70 million DALYs lost from ICH actually exceed those from ischemic stroke. Despite this, most research and funding have focused on ischemic stroke therapies.

**Deep perforating vasculopathy (hypertensive arteriopathy) is the main cause of ICH.** Lipohyalinosis, arteriolosclerosis, and fibrinoid necrosis of small penetrating arteries (lenticulostriate, thalamoperforating) lead to both rupture AND occlusive events — same vessel pathology, two different stroke types.

**Minimally invasive surgery is an evolving frontier.** While craniotomy (STICH I, STICH II) has not shown clear functional benefit over conservative management, minimally invasive approaches (MISTIE III, ENRICH) show promise, especially for lobar hemorrhages. Watch this space.

**ICP monitoring prevalence and utility remain uncertain in ICH.** One meta-analysis showed 67% prevalence of intracranial hypertension episodes and 50% associated mortality. Thresholds are borrowed from traumatic brain injury literature (GCS ≤ 8), but ICH-specific evidence is lacking.

**Time to treatment matters for hematoma expansion, just as it does for ischemic stroke.** Intensive BP management and hemostatic therapies reduce hematoma expansion, but translation to functional benefit has been elusive — potentially because intervention timing hasn't been fast enough. Door-to-treatment time monitoring may improve outcomes.

signs: Sudden onset of focal neurological deficit (similar to Ischemic Stroke but not reliably distinguishable clinically)
Headache (40-50%; more common than in ischemic stroke)
Nausea and vomiting (common with elevated ICP)
Decreased level of consciousness (50% at presentation)
Rapid deterioration over minutes to hours (hematoma expansion)
**Putamen/basal ganglia (~35%):** Contralateral hemiparesis, hemisensory loss, gaze deviation toward lesion, aphasia (dominant hemisphere)
**Thalamus (~15%):** Contralateral hemisensory loss, hemiparesis, vertical gaze palsy (upgaze), small reactive pupils
**Lobar (~30%):** Deficits corresponding to lobe: frontal (personality change, motor deficit), parietal (sensory loss), occipital (hemianopia), temporal (aphasia)
**Cerebellum (~10%):** Ataxia, vertigo, nystagmus, headache, vomiting; risk of rapid deterioration from brainstem compression and obstructive hydrocephalus
**Pons (~5%):** Quadriparesis, pinpoint pupils, coma, locked-in syndrome; high mortality
Hematoma expansion (30-40% in first 6 hours; major cause of early deterioration)
Intraventricular hemorrhage and obstructive hydrocephalus
Cerebral edema and herniation
Seizures (5-10% early; 8% within 30 days; higher in lobar ICH)
Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism
Aspiration pneumonia
Cardiac arrhythmias
Hyponatremia (cerebral salt wasting or SIADH)
Long-term cognitive impairment and depression

invasive-candidiasis.title: Invasive Candidiasis

sections.mycology:
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — Candida is a commensal organism; invasive disease requires breach of mucosal/skin barriers (central lines, gut translocation) plus impaired host immunity; the "portal of entry" is the key link in the chain. Inflammation and Immune Response — neutropenia is the dominant risk factor because neutrophils are the primary defense against Candida; T-cell deficiency drives mucocutaneous disease; complement deficiency increases dissemination risk.*

### Candida Biology
- **Kingdom:** Fungi — fundamentally different from bacteria
- **Morphology:** Dimorphic — exists as both **yeast** (round budding cells) and **hyphae/pseudohyphae** (elongated filaments)
- **Cell wall composition:**
  - **β-1,3-glucan** — target of echinocandins
  - **Ergosterol** in cell membrane — target of azoles and amphotericin B
  - **Mannan and mannoprotein** — outer layer, involved in adhesion and immune evasion
- Eukaryotic organism — shares cellular machinery with human cells, making antifungal development difficult (narrow therapeutic window)

### Morphologic Switching — The Virulence Switch
*C. albicans* can switch between yeast and hyphal forms — this is a key virulence mechanism:
- **Yeast form:** Dissemination through bloodstream, surface colonization
- **Hyphal form:** Tissue invasion — hyphae physically penetrate epithelial and endothelial barriers
- **Biofilm formation:** Both forms contribute to biofilms on medical devices (catheters, prosthetics)
- The transition is triggered by: body temperature (37°C), serum, neutral pH, CO₂

### Virulence Factors
| Factor | Mechanism | Clinical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Morphologic switching** | Yeast ↔ hyphae transition | Tissue invasion vs dissemination |
| **Biofilm formation** | Polysaccharide matrix on devices | Catheter-related candidemia, antifungal resistance within biofilm |
| **Secreted aspartyl proteases (SAPs)** | Degrade host proteins (IgA, complement, extracellular matrix) | Tissue destruction, immune evasion |
| **Phospholipases** | Damage host cell membranes | Tissue invasion |
| **Adhesins (Als proteins)** | Bind host extracellular matrix | Colonization, biofilm initiation |
| **Phenotypic switching** | Colony morphology changes | Antigenic variation, immune evasion |

### Clinically Important Candida Species
| Species | Frequency | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **C. albicans** | ~50% of candidemia | Most common; usually azole-susceptible; germ tube positive |
| **C. glabrata** | ~25% | **Intrinsically less susceptible to fluconazole**; increasing echinocandin resistance; common in elderly, prior azole exposure |
| **C. parapsilosis** | ~15% | Associated with **central lines and TPN**; forms biofilms on devices; usually azole-susceptible |
| **C. tropicalis** | ~5-10% | More virulent in neutropenic patients; usually azole-susceptible |
| **C. krusei** | ~2-3% | **Intrinsically resistant to fluconazole**; associated with prior fluconazole prophylaxis |
| **C. auris** | Emerging | **Multidrug-resistant** — resistant to azoles, amphotericin B, and sometimes echinocandins; environmental persistence; healthcare-associated outbreaks |

> [!warning]
> **Candida auris is a global emerging threat.** It is often misidentified by conventional lab methods (requires MALDI-TOF or molecular ID), can persist on surfaces for weeks, and has caused hospital outbreaks with high mortality. CDC considers it an urgent antimicrobial resistance threat.

---
sections.risk-factors:
Understanding why Candida turns invasive:

### Host Defense Breaches
| Category | Risk Factors | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **Mucosal barrier disruption** | Abdominal surgery, GI perforation, mucositis (chemotherapy), broad-spectrum antibiotics | Loss of commensal competition + direct portal entry |
| **Neutropenia** | Chemotherapy, hematologic malignancy, aplastic anemia | Neutrophils are primary defense against Candida |
| **Central venous catheters** | ICU, TPN, hemodialysis | Biofilm formation on foreign material |
| **Broad-spectrum antibiotics** | Especially prolonged courses | Eliminates competing bacteria → Candida overgrowth |
| **TPN** | Glucose-rich IV nutrition | Candida thrives on glucose; catheter biofilm |
| **ICU stay** | Multiple risk factors converge | 5-10% of ICU bloodstream infections are Candida |
| **Immunosuppression** | Corticosteroids, transplant, HIV with low CD4 | Impaired cellular immunity |
| **Renal replacement therapy** | Dialysis catheters, uremia-related immune dysfunction | Foreign material + impaired immunity |
| **Diabetes mellitus** | Hyperglycemia | Impaired neutrophil function, glucose-rich environment |

### The "Rule of Threes" (Candida Colonization → Infection)
Colonization at ≥3 body sites (e.g., urine, sputum, wound, stool) in an ICU patient significantly increases candidemia risk — this concept underlies the **Candida Score** used for empiric treatment decisions.

---
sections.clinical-presentations:
### Candidemia
- **Fever unresponsive to broad-spectrum antibacterials** — the classic presentation
- Often indistinguishable from bacterial sepsis clinically
- May present with sepsis or septic shock
- **Skin lesions** — painless erythematous papules or nodules (10-15% of cases); if present, biopsy shows organisms
- Endophthalmitis — Candida seeds the eye in ~15% of candidemia cases

### Deep-Seated Organ Involvement
| Organ | Presentation | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Eye (endophthalmitis)** | Blurry vision, floaters, eye pain | **Fundoscopic exam required for ALL candidemia** |
| **Heart (endocarditis)** | Valve vegetations, emboli | Large vegetations; usually requires surgical intervention |
| **Kidney** | Renal abscess, fungal balls | Common seeding site |
| **Liver/spleen (hepatosplenic)** | Persistent fever after neutrophil recovery | "Bulls-eye" lesions on CT; seen in hematologic malignancy patients |
| **Bone/joint** | Osteomyelitis, septic arthritis | Often indolent, delayed presentation |
| **CNS (meningitis)** | Headache, altered mental status | Rare; CSF may show lymphocytic pleocytosis |
| **Peritoneum** | Peritonitis in surgical/dialysis patients | Post-operative or PD-related |

---
sections.diagnosis:
### Blood Cultures
- **Positive blood culture for Candida = ALWAYS clinically significant** — never a contaminant
- Sensitivity only ~50% — blood cultures miss half of invasive candidiasis cases
- Time to positivity: usually 2-5 days (slower than bacteria)
- **Species identification is critical** — determines antifungal choice and resistance risk
- **ALWAYS obtain susceptibility testing** — resistance patterns vary by species

### Non-Culture Diagnostics
| Test | Target | Sensitivity/Specificity | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **β-1,3-D-glucan (BDG)** | Cell wall component of most fungi | Sensitive (~80%) but not specific to Candida | Screening in high-risk ICU patients; negative BDG has good NPV |
| **T2Candida panel** | Candida DNA directly from blood | Very sensitive; results in 3-5 hours | Rapid identification; detects 5 common species |
| **MALDI-TOF** | Protein fingerprint from culture | Rapid species ID from positive culture | Gold standard for species ID once culture grows |
| **Mannan/anti-mannan** | Candida cell wall antigen + antibody | Variable | Used in combination; less available in US |

### The Candidemia Workup (After Positive Blood Culture)
1. **Repeat blood cultures** daily until negative (document clearance)
2. **Dilated fundoscopic exam** by ophthalmology (within 1 week of diagnosis)
3. **Echocardiography** if sustained candidemia, prosthetic valve, or clinical suspicion
4. **Remove/replace central venous catheters** when feasible
5. **Species identification and susceptibility testing**
6. **CT imaging** of abdomen if hepatosplenic candidiasis suspected

---
sections.management:
### Empiric Therapy — Start Before Species Identification
| Clinical Scenario | First-Line | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-neutropenic, hemodynamically stable** | **Echinocandin** (micafungin 100 mg IV daily, caspofungin 70 mg load → 50 mg daily, or anidulafungin 200 mg load → 100 mg daily) | Covers C. glabrata and C. krusei; fungicidal against Candida |
| **Neutropenic** | **Echinocandin** | Same rationale + excellent safety profile |
| **C. parapsilosis identified** | **Fluconazole 800 mg load → 400 mg daily** | Echinocandins have higher MICs against C. parapsilosis |
| **Step-down (stable, susceptible species)** | **Fluconazole** PO | Once species susceptible, clinically improved, cultures clearing |

### Antifungal Classes and Mechanisms
| Class | Drugs | Target | Mechanism | Key Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Echinocandins** | Micafungin, caspofungin, anidulafungin | β-1,3-glucan synthase | Inhibits cell wall synthesis | **IV only**; higher MICs for C. parapsilosis; no CNS penetration |
| **Azoles** | Fluconazole, voriconazole | Lanosterol 14-α-demethylase (CYP51) | Inhibits ergosterol synthesis → leaky fungal membrane | C. krusei intrinsically resistant; C. glabrata often resistant; many drug interactions (CYP450) |
| **Polyenes** | Amphotericin B (liposomal) | Ergosterol | Binds ergosterol → pore formation → cell lysis | Nephrotoxicity (less with liposomal); infusion reactions; broad-spectrum including resistant species |

### Duration of Treatment
- **Candidemia without metastatic complications:** 14 days from first negative blood culture
- **Endocarditis:** 6 weeks after valve replacement (medical treatment rarely sufficient)
- **Endophthalmitis:** 4-6 weeks with intravitreal antifungal
- **Osteomyelitis:** 6-12 months

### Source Control
- **Remove/replace central venous catheters** — this is the most important intervention for catheter-related candidemia
- Early line removal is associated with faster clearance and reduced mortality
- If the line cannot be removed (e.g., tunneled dialysis catheter with no alternative access), antifungal lock therapy may be considered

---
sections.candida-auris-the-emerging-superbug:
### Why C. auris Is Different
- **Multidrug resistance:** Often resistant to fluconazole (90%), amphotericin B (30%), echinocandins (5-10%)
  - Some isolates are **pan-resistant** — resistant to all three antifungal classes
- **Environmental persistence:** Survives on surfaces for weeks (unlike other Candida species)
- **Difficult identification:** Misidentified as C. haemulonii by standard lab methods; requires MALDI-TOF or molecular testing
- **Person-to-person transmission:** Healthcare-associated outbreaks (unlike typical Candida infections)
- **High mortality:** 30-60% in reported outbreaks

### Infection Prevention for C. auris
- **Contact precautions** with gown and gloves
- **Single-patient room** (ideally private bathroom)
- **Enhanced environmental cleaning** — standard hospital disinfectants often inadequate; EPA-registered products effective against C. auris required
- **Screening** — axilla and groin surveillance cultures for exposed patients
- **Notification** — alert infection prevention immediately if identified

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Central Line Management
- **Meticulous line care** — the single most preventable risk factor for candidemia
- CHG-impregnated dressings, proper hub disinfection ("scrub the hub")
- Daily assessment of line necessity — advocate for removal when possible
- **If candidemia suspected:** obtain peripheral blood cultures AND catheter cultures to assess differential time to positivity

### Antifungal Administration
- **Echinocandins:** IV infusion over 1 hour; generally well-tolerated; minimal drug interactions
  - Watch for histamine-related reactions (flushing, pruritus) — slow infusion
- **Amphotericin B (liposomal):** Test dose may be ordered; premedicate with acetaminophen + diphenhydramine ± meperidine (for rigors)
  - **Hydrate aggressively** — NS 500-1000 mL before and after infusion to protect kidneys
  - Monitor renal function, potassium, magnesium (amphotericin causes renal K+ and Mg²+ wasting)
- **Fluconazole:** Good bioavailability PO = IV; drug interactions (CYP2C9/2C19/3A4 inhibitor)
  - Watch for QTc prolongation, especially with other QT-prolonging drugs

### Assessment Priorities
- **Daily eye symptom assessment** — ask about vision changes, floaters, eye pain (endophthalmitis)
- **Temperature trends** — persistent fever despite antibacterials is a red flag for candidiasis
- **Skin assessment** — erythematous papules/nodules suggestive of disseminated candidiasis
- Monitor for line complications — line removal timing and replacement

---
sections.references:
- Pappas et al., "Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Candidiasis: 2016 Update by IDSA," Clinical Infectious Diseases, 2016
- McCarty & Pappas, "Invasive Candidiasis," Infectious Disease Clinics of North America, 2016
- CDC, "Candida auris: Antifungal Resistance and Clinical Updates," 2023
- Kullberg & Arendrup, "Invasive Candidiasis," New England Journal of Medicine, 2015

clinicalPearls: **Candida in blood cultures is NEVER a contaminant** — treat immediately with echinocandin and pull the line; this is not like coag-negative staph where you might watch and wait
**Fever on antibacterials in an ICU patient with a central line = think Candida** — especially with TPN, recent abdominal surgery, or broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure

**The species matters enormously for treatment:** C. albicans = fluconazole fine; C. glabrata = echinocandin; C. krusei = echinocandin (intrinsically fluconazole-resistant); C. parapsilosis = fluconazole (echinocandins have higher MICs)
**Every candidemia patient needs an eye exam** — endophthalmitis in ~15% of cases; can cause permanent vision loss if missed
**Pull the line, pull the line, pull the line** — source control with line removal is as important as antifungals; mortality increases with delayed line removal
**β-D-glucan negative in a low-risk patient = good news** — high NPV makes it useful for ruling out invasive candidiasis in the ICU

**C. auris is the pathogen infection preventionists lose sleep over** — multidrug-resistant, environmentally persistent, transmissible between patients; if your lab identifies it, escalate immediately
**14 days from first NEGATIVE culture** — not from first positive; keep drawing daily blood cultures until they clear

ischemic-stroke.title: Ischemic Stroke

sections.definition: Acute neurological deficit caused by focal cerebral, spinal, or retinal infarction resulting from interruption of blood supply to the brain. Ischemic stroke accounts for approximately 87% of all strokes. Defined by the presence of permanent brain tissue injury (infarction) attributable to ischemia, confirmed either by neuroimaging or by clinical symptoms persisting >= 24 hours. Transient ischemic attack (TIA) represents ischemia without infarction.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~7.6 million people in the US have had a stroke; approximately 795,000 new or recurrent strokes per year in the US (~610,000 first events); global incidence ~12.2 million new strokes per year (2019 GBD data)
- **Risk factors:**
  - **Non-modifiable:** Age (doubles each decade after 55), male sex, race/ethnicity (Black and Hispanic populations at higher risk), family history, prior stroke/TIA
  - **Modifiable:** Hypertension (most important modifiable risk factor; PAR ~48%), Atrial Fibrillation, Diabetes, smoking, hyperlipidemia, physical inactivity, obesity, excessive alcohol use, Obstructive Sleep Apnea
  - **Cardioembolic sources:** Atrial Fibrillation, valvular heart disease, patent foramen ovale (PFO), left ventricular thrombus, infective endocarditis
  - **Other:** Carotid artery stenosis, hypercoagulable states, sickle cell disease, oral contraceptive use, illicit drug use (cocaine, amphetamines)
- **Demographics:** Second leading cause of death worldwide; leading cause of long-term adult disability; age-adjusted mortality declining in high-income countries but increasing in low/middle-income countries; women have higher lifetime risk due to longer life expectancy
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — cerebral perfusion pressure depends on MAP; occlusion drops local perfusion pressure below autoregulatory threshold, triggering the ischemic cascade*
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — neurons in the core undergo irreversible necrosis within 4–6 minutes; penumbra neurons shift from reversible injury to apoptosis over hours, defining the therapeutic window*

Ischemic stroke results from occlusion of a cerebral artery by thrombosis or embolism, leading to a cascade of ischemic injury:

1. **Vascular Occlusion:** Large artery atherosclerosis (carotid, vertebral, intracranial arteries), cardioembolism (Atrial Fibrillation, valvular disease), small vessel disease (lacunar infarcts), or other/undetermined etiologies (TOAST classification)
2. **Ischemic Core and Penumbra:** Within seconds of occlusion, the core territory undergoes irreversible injury; the surrounding penumbra has compromised but viable tissue with residual perfusion from collateral circulation -- this is the therapeutic target
3. **Excitotoxicity:** Energy failure leads to membrane depolarization, glutamate release, NMDA/AMPA receptor activation, and massive calcium influx causing neuronal death
4. **Oxidative Stress:** Reperfusion generates reactive oxygen species (ROS), exacerbating mitochondrial dysfunction and cell membrane damage
5. **Inflammation:** Microglial activation, leukocyte infiltration, and cytokine release (IL-1beta, TNF-alpha) contribute to secondary injury over hours to days
6. **Blood-Brain Barrier Disruption:** Leads to vasogenic edema, increased intracranial pressure, and potential hemorrhagic transformation
7. **Collateral Circulation:** Quality of leptomeningeal collaterals determines penumbral survival and response to reperfusion therapy -- varies greatly between individuals

**TOAST Classification of Ischemic Stroke:**
| Subtype | Frequency |
|---------|-----------|
| Large artery atherosclerosis | ~20% |
| Cardioembolism | ~25% |
| Small vessel occlusion (lacunar) | ~20% |
| Other determined etiology | ~5% |
| Cryptogenic/undetermined | ~30% |
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Presentation depends on vascular territory:**

**Anterior circulation (carotid/MCA):**
- Contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg in MCA)
- Aphasia (dominant hemisphere -- typically left): Broca's (expressive), Wernicke's (receptive), global
- Hemineglect/inattention (non-dominant hemisphere -- typically right)
- Contralateral homonymous hemianopia
- Gaze deviation toward the side of lesion

**Posterior circulation (vertebrobasilar):**
- Vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia
- Ataxia, nystagmus
- Crossed deficits (ipsilateral cranial nerve, contralateral motor/sensory)
- Bilateral motor/sensory deficits (basilar occlusion)
- Decreased level of consciousness (brainstem involvement)

**Lacunar syndromes (small vessel):**
- Pure motor hemiparesis
- Pure sensory stroke
- Ataxic hemiparesis
- Dysarthria-clumsy hand syndrome

**BE-FAST screening:** Balance, Eyes, Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call 911

### Complications
- Cerebral edema (peaks 3-5 days; malignant MCA infarction can cause herniation)
- Hemorrhagic transformation (2-10%; higher with reperfusion therapy, large infarct, anticoagulation)
- Seizures (5-10% within first 2 weeks)
- Dysphagia and aspiration pneumonia
- Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism
- Depression (post-stroke, ~30%)
- Delirium and cognitive impairment
- Spasticity
- Falls and fractures
- Urinary tract infections
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **Non-contrast CT head:** First-line emergency imaging; rules out hemorrhage; early ischemic changes (hyperdense vessel sign, loss of gray-white differentiation, sulcal effacement)
- **CT Angiography (CTA):** Identifies large vessel occlusion (LVO) for thrombectomy candidacy; evaluates carotid/vertebral arteries
- **CT Perfusion (CTP):** Identifies ischemic core vs salvageable penumbra; guides extended-window treatment decisions (using mismatch ratios)
- **MRI with DWI/FLAIR:** Most sensitive for acute infarction; DWI positive within minutes; DWI-FLAIR mismatch can estimate stroke onset for wake-up strokes
- **NIHSS (National Institutes of Health Stroke Scale):** Standardized severity assessment (0-42); guides treatment decisions
- **Labs:** CBC, BMP, coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT), troponin, glucose (hypo/hyperglycemia mimics), lipid panel, HbA1c
- **Cardiac evaluation:** ECG (arrhythmia, Atrial Fibrillation), echocardiography (TTE/TEE), prolonged cardiac monitoring (14-30 days for cryptogenic stroke)
- **Vascular imaging:** Carotid duplex ultrasound, MRA, or CTA of head/neck

### Diagnostic Criteria
- Acute neurological deficit with imaging evidence of cerebral infarction, OR
- Clinical syndrome lasting >= 24 hours without alternative explanation
- TOAST classification used for etiological subtyping
- ASCOD phenotyping: Atherosclerosis, Small vessel disease, Cardiac pathology, Other causes, Dissection
sections.management:
### Acute
**Thrombolysis (IV alteplase/tenecteplase):**
- IV alteplase (0.9 mg/kg, max 90 mg; 10% bolus, remainder over 60 min) within 4.5 hours of symptom onset
- Tenecteplase (0.25 mg/kg) increasingly used; single bolus, non-inferior for LVO
- Extended window (4.5-9 hours or wake-up stroke): guided by perfusion imaging mismatch or DWI-FLAIR mismatch
- Key exclusions: active bleeding, platelet count <100,000, INR >1.7, recent major surgery, BP >185/110 despite treatment

**Mechanical thrombectomy:**
- For large vessel occlusion (ICA, M1 MCA, basilar artery)
- Within 24 hours of symptom onset (guided by perfusion imaging in extended window, per DAWN/DEFUSE 3 trials)
- NNT of ~2.6 to reduce disability by at least one mRS level
- Should be performed at comprehensive stroke centers

**Supportive care:**
- BP management: permissive hypertension (do not lower unless >220/120 or >185/110 if thrombolysis candidate); post-thrombolysis maintain <180/105
- Blood glucose: maintain 140-180 mg/dL; avoid hypoglycemia
- Temperature management: treat fever aggressively (target normothermia)
- NPO until swallowing screen completed (aspiration risk)
- DVT prophylaxis: intermittent pneumatic compression; pharmacological prophylaxis after 24-48 hours if no hemorrhage
- Decompressive craniectomy: consider for malignant MCA syndrome (age <60, deteriorating consciousness)

### MultiCare Code Neuro/Stroke Protocol

> [!institution]
> **Source:** MultiCare Health System — PSR Adult Acute Stroke Response (2023). Applies to Tacoma General/Allenmore, Auburn Medical Center, Good Samaritan, Covington Medical Center, Capital Medical Center.

#### BE-FAST Screening
Any single factor or combination = positive screen:
- **B**alance: Loss of balance or coordination
- **E**yes: Double vision, blurry vision, or vision loss
- **F**ace: Facial droop
- **A**rm/Leg: Unequal strength or numbness/tingling
- **S**peech: Slurred, unable to speak or find the right words
- **T**ime: Activate Code Neuro/Stroke if last known well (LKW) < 24 hours

#### Code Neuro/Stroke Activation Criteria
- Positive BE-FAST OR high suspicion of stroke AND symptom onset within 24 hours
- Stroke/rule-out stroke patient with sudden decline in neuro status
- Inpatient admitted for stroke with sudden neuro decline
- **Any staff member can activate** — Rapid Response does NOT need to be called first

#### Treatment Windows
| Window | Eligibility |
|--------|------------|
| **LKW < 4.5 hours** | IV thrombolytics AND/OR thrombectomy |
| **LKW 4.5-24 hours** | Thrombectomy only (with imaging selection) |
| **LKW > 24 hours** | Medical management only |

#### Code Neuro/Stroke Timeline Goals

**≤ 5 Minutes — Provider at Bedside:**
- Activate Code Neuro/Stroke
- Obtain Code Neuro/Stroke packet (inpatient: located on crash cart)
- Locate Code Neuro/Stroke Checklist within packet
- Provider performs rapid neuro assessment at bedside
- CT department notified to hold table

**≤ 10 Minutes — Assessment Complete:**
- ABCD assessment, vitals, pulse oximetry, POCT glucose
  - If glucose < 50 → notify provider and treat immediately (hypoglycemia mimics stroke)
  - Maintain O₂ sat > 94%
- Document patient weight
- Document specific LKW in military time (NOT "2 hours ago" — exact time)
- Provider orders **STAT CT Head w/o contrast** ± **STAT CT-Angio Head & Neck**
  - Use "ED STROKE ORDER SET [769]" or "INPATIENT STROKE ACTIVATION/CODE NEURO ORDER SET [11419]"
- Place 20-gauge IV or larger for CTA — do NOT delay non-contrast CT; place after if needed
- If LKW < 4.5 hours → Provider and Pharmacist review Inclusion/Exclusion Criteria
- Draw STAT labs (rainbow + green top) — do NOT delay CT for labs
- Transport to CT with RN if patient stable

**≤ 20 Minutes — CT Complete:**
- Initial CT scan completed
- Document vital signs
  - If thrombolytic candidate and BP > 185/110 → notify Provider, treat per orders
- Document initial **full NIHSS** + pupil assessment
- Complete **RN Swallow Screen** before ANY PO meds or food
  - FAIL if patient has ANY dysphagia risk factors or if intubated
  - Failed screen → order Speech Therapy consult, keep patient **strict NPO**

**≤ 35 Minutes — Treatment Plan Determined:**
- Provider receives non-contrast CT results from radiologist
- Provider determines thrombolytic eligibility and treatment plan
- If large vessel occlusion identified → Provider contacts NIR (neuro-interventional radiology): 1-800-200-9022
- Provider discusses and documents risks/benefits/alternatives with patient/family
- Initiate appropriate stroke order set

#### IV Thrombolytic Administration (Goal: ≤ 45 Minutes from Activation)

**Pre-Administration:**
- Verify vitals and neuro assessment within 15 minutes BEFORE administering
- **BP must be < 185/110** before administration
  - If BP > 185/110 → treat and recheck before giving thrombolytic

**Post-Administration Monitoring:**
| Timeframe (from BOLUS start) | Assessment Interval |
|------------------------------|-------------------|
| First 2 hours | Vitals + neuro assessment **every 15 minutes** |
| Next 6 hours | **Every 30 minutes** |
| Remaining 16 hours | **Every 1 hour** |

- **1:1 RN care for 6 hours** post-thrombolytic start time
- If BP > 180/105, hypotension, or vitals out of parameter → recheck within ~5 min; if persistent → treat per thrombolytic orders, notify Provider, document response
- **Adverse reactions requiring IMMEDIATE Provider notification:**
  - Bleeding (any site)
  - Angioedema
  - New or worsening headache
  - Vomiting
  - Neurological decline
- Document full NIHSS at 2 hours post-thrombolytic and with any neuro changes
- Provide patient/family education handouts from Code Neuro/Stroke Packet

#### Emergent Thrombectomy/NIR (Neuro-Interventional Radiology)

**TGH (Tacoma General) patients:**
- Goal: ED arrival/symptom recognition to device ≤ **90 minutes**
- Preparation: 2 IVs, Foley catheter, remove personal belongings, patient in gown
- Call report to TGH NIR

**Transfer patients (to TGH):**
- Goal: ED door/symptom recognition to transfer out ≤ **120 minutes**
- Emergent transfer (lights/sirens) to TGH NIR Department
- No ICU bed needed for emergent NIR transfer — patient can go directly to ED if no ICU bed available after procedure
- Complete "Emergent Transfer of Stroke Patient" form from Code Neuro/Stroke packet, send with transport staff
- Preparation: 2 IVs, Foley catheter, personal belongings removed, gown, document if thrombolytic given
- Call report to TGH NIR

#### Suspected Stroke — Admit (No Transfer/No Emergent Procedure)
- Admit to designated Stroke Unit; recommend ≥ 72 hours for frequent monitoring
- Goal: ED door to monitored bed ≤ **3 hours**
- Provider utilizes Inpatient Stroke Order Set
- NIHSS and vital sign parameters/frequency per order

#### Required Documentation (Quality Metrics)
| Data Point | Responsible Staff |
|-----------|------------------|
| Time of Code Neuro/Stroke activation | RN/Provider/Pharmacist |
| Time of Last Known Well (military time) | RN/Provider/Pharmacist |
| Initial full NIHSS | Provider/RN |
| Thrombolytic contraindications reviewed | Provider/Pharmacist |
| Thrombectomy contraindications reviewed (if LVO + NIR consult) | Provider |
| Risk/benefit discussion with patient/family documented | Provider |

#### NIHSS Documentation
- **Initial assessment:** Full NIHSS (0-42, all domains)
- **Ongoing monitoring:** Modified NIHSS (0-31) acceptable for frequent assessments
  - Modified NIHSS includes: 1b, 1c, 2, 3, 5a, 5b, 6a, 6b, 8, 9, 11
  - Modified NIHSS components indicated by "+" in Epic fields
- **Full NIHSS required:** At initial assessment, 2 hours post-thrombolytic, and with any neurological change

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Antiplatelet therapy:** Aspirin 81-325 mg (initiated within 24-48 hours if no thrombolysis); dual antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) for 21 days in minor stroke/high-risk TIA, then single agent
- **Anticoagulation:** Anticoagulants (DOACs preferred over warfarin) for Atrial Fibrillation-related stroke; typically started 4-14 days post-stroke depending on infarct size
- **Statin therapy:** High-intensity Statins (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20 mg) regardless of baseline LDL
- **Blood pressure management:** Target <130/80 mmHg long-term; ACE Inhibitors, ARBs, Calcium Channel Blockers, Diuretics
- **Carotid revascularization:** Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) or carotid artery stenting (CAS) for symptomatic carotid stenosis >= 50% (NASCET criteria)
- **Cardiac rhythm monitoring:** Extended monitoring for cryptogenic stroke to detect occult Atrial Fibrillation
- **PFO closure:** Consider for cryptogenic stroke with high-risk PFO features in patients < 60 years
- **Rehabilitation:** Early mobilization (within 24-48 hours if stable); PT, OT, speech therapy; inpatient rehab for moderate-severe deficits

### Pharmacology
- Anticoagulants -- DOACs (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) for AF-related stroke; warfarin as alternative
- Antiplatelet Agents -- aspirin, clopidogrel, aspirin-dipyridamole
- Statins -- atorvastatin, rosuvastatin (high-intensity)
- ACE Inhibitors / ARBs -- for long-term BP management
- Thrombolytics -- alteplase (tPA), tenecteplase
- Antihypertensives -- labetalol, nicardipine, clevidipine (acute BP management)
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Acute phase:**
  - Perform and document serial NIHSS assessments (baseline, q1h during thrombolysis, q4-6h thereafter)
  - Monitor for signs of hemorrhagic transformation: worsening neuro status, headache, nausea/vomiting, acute hypertension
  - Strict BP monitoring per protocol (q15min during/after tPA for first 2 hours, then q30min x 6h, then q1h x 16h)
  - Maintain IV access; no arterial punctures or invasive procedures for 24 hours post-tPA
  - Swallowing screen before any PO intake (water swallow test, bedside assessment, SLP evaluation)
  - HOB flat or per MD order (flat may improve cerebral perfusion; elevate if edema/aspiration risk)
  - Continuous cardiac monitoring for arrhythmia detection
  - Blood glucose monitoring q6h; treat hyperglycemia (>180 mg/dL) with insulin; prevent hypoglycemia
  - Temperature monitoring q4h; acetaminophen and cooling measures for fever
  - Fall prevention measures (impaired mobility, hemineglect, visual field deficits)
- **Post-acute/rehabilitation:**
  - Facilitate early mobilization when hemodynamically stable
  - Screen for depression (PHQ-9) starting at 2-4 weeks
  - Patient and family education: stroke warning signs (BE-FAST), medication compliance, risk factor modification
  - Discharge planning: home safety assessment, outpatient rehabilitation referral, follow-up appointments
  - Smoking cessation counseling and resources
  - Education on anticoagulant/antiplatelet adherence and signs of bleeding
sections.keyPoints:
- Ischemic stroke is a medical emergency; "time is brain" -- approximately 1.9 million neurons lost per minute of untreated large vessel occlusion
- IV thrombolysis (alteplase/tenecteplase) within 4.5 hours and mechanical thrombectomy up to 24 hours (with imaging selection) are the cornerstones of acute treatment
- Hypertension is the single most important modifiable risk factor (PAR ~48%)
- Atrial Fibrillation is the most common cardioembolic source; all cryptogenic strokes warrant prolonged cardiac monitoring
- Secondary prevention with antiplatelet or anticoagulant therapy, high-intensity statins, and blood pressure control is essential to reduce recurrence (5-year recurrence rate ~25% without intervention)
- Swallowing assessment before oral intake is a critical nursing safety measure to prevent aspiration pneumonia

clinicalPearls: **"Time is brain" = 1.9 million neurons per minute** — every minute of untreated large vessel occlusion destroys nearly 2 million neurons. Your speed in activating Code Neuro directly determines how much brain your patient keeps. Know the timeline: 5 min to provider, 10 min to assessment, 20 min to CT, 35 min to treatment plan, 45 min to thrombolytic.

**Last Known Well is NOT "when symptoms started"** — LKW is the last time the patient was seen at their neurological BASELINE. For wake-up strokes, LKW = bedtime. Get a specific military time, not "a couple hours ago." This determines the entire treatment window.

**Check glucose before anything else** — hypoglycemia perfectly mimics stroke and is instantly treatable. POCT glucose is part of your ≤10 minute goals. A BG of 40 presenting as left-sided weakness → dextrose, not tPA.

**BP > 185/110 blocks thrombolytic administration** — you cannot give tPA until BP is below this threshold. Have IV labetalol or nicardipine drip ready. But don't aggressively lower BP in non-thrombolytic candidates (permissive hypertension protects the penumbra).

**1:1 nursing for 6 hours post-tPA is not optional** — the highest risk for hemorrhagic transformation is in the first hours. Q15 min vitals and neuro checks for 2 hours, then q30 min for 6 hours. New headache, vomiting, or any neuro decline → stop infusion, STAT CT, call provider.

**Swallow screen before ANYTHING by mouth** — aspiration pneumonia is the most common preventable complication of stroke. If the patient fails or has ANY dysphagia risk factors → strict NPO, order SLP consult. This includes medications — use IV route.

**Any staff member can activate Code Neuro** — you do not need to call Rapid Response first and you do not need physician permission. If your BE-FAST is positive and LKW is within 24 hours, activate immediately. Seconds count.

**The Modified NIHSS is your friend for frequent monitoring** — you don't need to do the full 42-point scale every time. The modified version (11 components) is validated for serial assessments and much faster at the bedside.
### MHS Updated Stroke Pathways and Blood Pressure Panels (Oct 2025) `#pam-training`
**Source:** MultiCare Neuroscience Institute Practice Alert — NEW ED Stroke Pathways and Orders (Rev. 10/7/25)
#### Blood Pressure Targets by Stroke Type

| Stroke Type | BP Goal | Key Details | |------------|---------|-------------| | **Ischemic (no thrombolysis/thrombectomy)** | SBP < 220 AND DBP < 120 | Permissive hypertension × 24 hrs. After 24 hours, reduce BP by 15% | | **TIA** | SBP < 220 AND DBP < 120 | Permissive hypertension × 24 hrs (or until stroke ruled out). After 24 hrs, goal SBP < 130 AND DBP < 80 | | **Intracerebral Hemorrhage (ICH)** | Target SBP 140–160 | Maintain as close to 140 as possible without dropping below. Labetalol IV 1st line, nicardipine IV 2nd line | | **Subarachnoid Hemorrhage (SAH)** | SBP < 140, MAP > 65 | Avoid hypotension. Consider early nicardipine initiation |
**Critical timing:** Target < 60 minutes from stroke diagnosis to initiate BP intervention.
#### Updated ED Stroke/TIA Order Set
Ordered for ALL patients with confirmed/suspected TIA, ischemic, or hemorrhagic stroke
**Updated nurse monitoring:** Defined neurological assessment (GCS, LOC, pupils, visual field, motor strength, sensation, speech)
**Removed** Modified NIHSS as an assessment option — use full NIHSS
**Vitals/neuro assessment frequency:** Q15 min × 2, then Q1 hour until new orders received
**New prechecked orders:**
Lie flat (zero degrees LVO) — only for confirmed LVO on imaging awaiting emergent thrombectomy
Consult telehealth neurology where available
CT Perfusion per stroke protocol

**MAR BP Documentation:** New BP field in MAR for documenting/confirming BP at every rate change and new bag/bottle/cartridge for antihypertensive drips (nicardipine, esmolol, labetalol, nitroglycerin, nitroprusside). Validate BP pre and post titration.
#### TIA Pathway (Canadian TIA Score)
**For patients presenting with TIA or stroke symptoms that have completely resolved:**

1. **Nursing:** Vital signs + neurologic assessment (Q15 min × 2, then Q1 hr). Full NIHSS + pupils 2. **Provider:** Initiate ED Stroke/TIA Order Set → select TIA Blood Pressure Management Panel 3. **Nursing:** Complete CT/CTA head/neck and prechecked labs STAT 4. **Goals:** Temperature < 37.5°C, blood glucose 140–180 mg/dL, O₂ sat ≥ 94% 5. **Provider:** Complete Canadian TIA Score (Epic tools)

**Risk Stratification:** | Risk Level | Canadian TIA Score | Action | |-----------|-------------------|--------| | **Low Risk** | -3 to 3 | May discharge. MRI outpatient. Enter ED TIA Discharge Smart Set. Refer to ED/TIA Stroke Rapid Clinic (REF513). PCP follow-up | | **Medium Risk** | 4–8 | Shared decision-making. Consider neurology consult before discharge without MRI | | **High Risk** | ≥ 9 or abnormal findings | **Admit.** Consult neurology. Use Stroke/TIA Order Set with IP Admission OS |
**High-risk findings requiring admission:** Stenosis or acute vascular abnormality, new-onset atrial fibrillation, carotid/vertebral artery dissection, hypercoagulable state, crescendo/recurrent TIA.
**TIA Discharge Medications:**
DAPT × 21 days: Plavix 300 mg load → 75 mg daily + ASA 81 mg daily (continue ASA beyond 21 days)
Statin: High dose (age > 75 → moderate or high dose)
Nursing: Add AVS MHS Stroke/TIA discharge instructions smart phrase: **.STROKEDC**
#### ICH Pathway (Non-Traumatic Intracerebral Hemorrhage)
Initiate ED Stroke/TIA Order Set + ICH BP Management Panel
Labetalol IV 1st line, nicardipine IV 2nd line
Initiate neurosurgery consultation
ICH Severity Score documented within 6 hours by neurosurgery or attending
**Emergent anticoagulation reversal:** Consult pharmacy, goal < 60 min from arrival
**Seizure management:** Avoid antiseizure drugs (ASD) unless confirmed/suspected seizure; unexplained AMS → neurology + pharmacy consult, consider EEG/Ceribell
Complete initial NIHSS, neuro assessment, vital signs
Q15 min × 2, then Q1 hr (if surgical intervention planned: Q30 min)
**BP goal:** SBP 140–160, initiate BP lowering < 60 min of arrival, avoid fluctuations
Temperature q4h, maintain < 37.5°C
Glucose: notify provider if > 180 or < 70 mg/dL
O₂ sat ≥ 94%; monitor and initiate pain management
**Disposition:** Admit to critical care. Consider transfer to comprehensive stroke center. Door-to-transfer goal: < 2 hours from ED arrival
#### SAH Pathway (Non-Traumatic Subarachnoid Hemorrhage)
Initiate ED Stroke/TIA Order Set + SAH BP Management Panel
Initiate consultation with endovascular and/or neurosurgical specialist
Consider Hunt & Hess Grade (aSAH only) for severity/prognosis
**Emergent anticoagulation reversal:** Goal < 60 min from arrival
**Seizure management:** Initiate neurology consult if new-onset; prophylactic ASD not beneficial without seizure features
Complete initial NIHSS + neuro assessment + vital signs
Q15 min × 2, then Q1 hr
**BP goal:** SBP < 140, MAP > 65
Initiate BP lowering < 60 min of arrival; avoid fluctuations
Prior to aSAH coiling/embolization/clipping: bedrest, quiet/dim environment, avoid overstimulation
Temperature q4h < 37.5°C; glucose monitoring; O₂ sat ≥ 94%
**Disposition:** Admit to ICU. Strongly consider air transport. Door-to-transfer goal: < 2 hours

**Endovascular/Neurosurgical Resources by MHS Region:** | Region | Endovascular | Neurosurgical | |--------|-------------|---------------| | Inland Northwest | Sacred Heart Neuro Intervention | Deaconess Neurosurgery | | Puget Sound/Yakima | Neurointerventional (NIR) | MHS Neurosurgery |
### Right Brain vs Left Brain Stroke — Clinical Presentation `#pam-training`

| Feature | Right Brain Stroke | Left Brain Stroke | |---------|-------------------|-------------------| | **Motor deficit** | Left-sided weakness | Right-sided weakness | | **Sensory deficit** | Left-sided numbness/loss | Right-sided numbness/loss | | **Key cognitive deficit** | **Left-sided neglect** (unaware of left side of world) | **Aphasia** (expressive, receptive, or global) | | **Behavioral changes** | Impulsive, poor judgment, safety risks | Slow, cautious, depressed | | **Visual deficit** | Left homonymous hemianopia | Right homonymous hemianopia | | **Other features** | Difficulty recognizing faces (prosopagnosia), anosognosia (denying deficits) | Difficulty with reading, writing, calculations | | **Nursing safety concern** | Patient doesn't recognize their own deficits — **high fall risk** | Patient aware of deficits — **high depression/frustration risk** |

**Clinical Pearl:** Left-sided neglect in right brain stroke means the patient may not eat food from the left side of the tray, may not acknowledge people on their left, and may not even recognize their own left arm. **Always approach from the affected side** to stimulate awareness.

signs: Contralateral hemiparesis and hemisensory loss (face and arm > leg in MCA)
Aphasia (dominant hemisphere -- typically left): Broca's (expressive), Wernicke's (receptive), global
Hemineglect/inattention (non-dominant hemisphere -- typically right)
Contralateral homonymous hemianopia
Gaze deviation toward the side of lesion
Vertigo, diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia
Ataxia, nystagmus
Crossed deficits (ipsilateral cranial nerve, contralateral motor/sensory)
Bilateral motor/sensory deficits (basilar occlusion)
Decreased level of consciousness (brainstem involvement)
Pure motor hemiparesis
Pure sensory stroke
Ataxic hemiparesis
Dysarthria-clumsy hand syndrome
Cerebral edema (peaks 3-5 days; malignant MCA infarction can cause herniation)
Hemorrhagic transformation (2-10%; higher with reperfusion therapy, large infarct, anticoagulation)
Seizures (5-10% within first 2 weeks)
Dysphagia and aspiration pneumonia
Deep Vein Thrombosis and Pulmonary Embolism
Depression (post-stroke, ~30%)
Delirium and cognitive impairment
Spasticity
Falls and fractures
Urinary tract infections

large-vessel-vasculitis.title: Large-Vessel Vasculitis

sections.giant-cell-arteritis-gca-temporal-arteri:
### Pathophysiology

> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — GCA/Takayasu = granulomatous transmural inflammation of large vessel walls; activated CD4+ T cells and macrophages form granulomas → giant cells → vessel wall destruction → stenosis or aneurysm formation. Cell Proliferation and Death — vessel wall ischemia from luminal occlusion causes tissue death: optic nerve ischemia (GCA) → irreversible blindness if untreated.*

1. **Dendritic cells** in the adventitia (outer vessel wall layer) become activated — likely by an unknown antigen
2. Activated dendritic cells recruit **CD4+ T cells** → Th1 and Th17 responses
3. T cells produce **IFN-γ** → activate macrophages → form **multinucleated giant cells** (hence the name)
4. **Granulomatous inflammation** develops in the vessel wall, centered on the **internal elastic lamina**
5. Inflammation causes:
   - **Intimal hyperplasia** → progressive luminal narrowing → ischemia
   - **Destruction of elastic lamina** → vessel wall weakening → aneurysm (especially aortic)
   - **Vasa vasorum inflammation** → vessel wall ischemia → further damage
6. **Skip lesions** — inflammation is not continuous; some segments are affected, others spared → this explains why temporal artery biopsy can be falsely negative

### Vessels Affected
- **Temporal arteries** — most clinically apparent (headache, tender scalp arteries)
- **Ophthalmic and posterior ciliary arteries** → **anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION)** → **BLINDNESS**
- **Aorta and branches** — aortitis in ~50% of GCA; often subclinical
- **Vertebral arteries** → posterior circulation stroke
- External carotid branches → jaw claudication

### Epidemiology
- Almost exclusively **age >50** (mean onset ~72 years)
- Women > men (2-3:1)
- Northern European descent (highest prevalence in Scandinavians)
- Strong association with **polymyalgia rheumatica (PMR)** — 40-60% of GCA patients have PMR; 15-20% of PMR patients develop GCA

### Clinical Features

#### Classic Triad (Not Always All Present)
1. **New-onset headache** — temporal, often unilateral, throbbing or boring; different from any prior headache pattern
2. **Jaw claudication** — pain/fatigue in jaw muscles with chewing (ischemia of masseter muscle) — **most specific symptom for GCA**
3. **Visual symptoms** — transient visual loss (amaurosis fugax), diplopia, partial or complete vision loss

#### Other Features
| Finding | Mechanism |
|---|---|
| **Scalp tenderness** | Temporal artery inflammation; pain brushing hair |
| **Temporal artery abnormality** | Thickened, nodular, tender, diminished pulse |
| **Polymyalgia rheumatica symptoms** | Bilateral shoulder/hip girdle pain and stiffness; morning stiffness >30 min |
| **Constitutional symptoms** | Fever, weight loss, fatigue, malaise (can present as FUO) |
| **Tongue claudication** | Rare but specific — pain with chewing |
| **Aortic involvement** | Often silent initially; aortic aneurysm (especially thoracic), aortic dissection |

> [!critical]
> **Visual loss from GCA is a MEDICAL EMERGENCY.** Anterior ischemic optic neuropathy (AION) causes sudden, painless, monocular visual loss. Without immediate treatment, the other eye will follow in **days to weeks** (bilateral blindness in ~25-50% if untreated). **Start steroids IMMEDIATELY on clinical suspicion — do not wait for biopsy results.**

### Diagnosis

#### Labs
| Test | Expected | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **ESR** | Markedly elevated (often >50, frequently >100 mm/hr) | Very sensitive but not specific; **normal ESR does not rule out GCA** (~5% have normal ESR) |
| **CRP** | Elevated | More sensitive than ESR; use both |
| **Platelets** | Often elevated (reactive thrombocytosis) | Common but non-specific |
| **Anemia** | Mild normocytic anemia of chronic disease | Common |
| **Alkaline phosphatase** | May be elevated | Hepatic involvement |

#### Temporal Artery Biopsy (Gold Standard)
- **Unilateral biopsy** — take ≥1 cm of artery (skip lesions can cause false negatives)
- Should be performed within **2 weeks of starting steroids** — inflammation persists long enough for biopsy to be diagnostic
- Pathology: granulomatous inflammation, multinucleated giant cells, intimal hyperplasia, fragmented internal elastic lamina
- **False negative rate ~15-40%** due to skip lesions → negative biopsy does NOT rule out GCA if clinical suspicion is high
- Some centers now use **temporal artery ultrasound** first — "halo sign" (hypoechoic wall thickening) is suggestive

#### Imaging for Aortic Involvement
- CTA or MRA of aorta if GCA confirmed — screening for aortitis/aneurysm
- Long-term surveillance — aortic aneurysm can develop years after GCA diagnosis

### Management

#### Acute Treatment — START IMMEDIATELY
**Do not wait for biopsy to treat if clinical suspicion is high:**

| Scenario | Treatment |
|---|---|
| **GCA without visual symptoms** | **Prednisone 40-60 mg PO daily** |
| **GCA with visual symptoms or vision loss** | **Methylprednisolone 1 g IV daily × 3 days**, then prednisone 60 mg PO daily |
| **Threatened vision (amaurosis fugax)** | Same as vision loss — treat as impending AION |

#### Steroid Taper
- Begin taper after 2-4 weeks at full dose
- Very gradual — typically over **1-2 years** (faster tapers → relapse)
- Monitor symptoms, ESR, CRP monthly during taper
- Relapse rate: ~50% during taper or after discontinuation

#### Steroid-Sparing Agents
- **Tocilizumab** (IL-6 receptor inhibitor) — FDA-approved for GCA; enables faster steroid taper; increasingly used as first-line adjunct
- **Methotrexate** — modest benefit; used when tocilizumab not available

#### Complications of Long-Term Steroids
- Osteoporosis → start calcium/vitamin D + consider bisphosphonate
- Hyperglycemia → monitor glucose
- Adrenal suppression → do not stop abruptly
- Cataracts, glaucoma
- Immunosuppression → infection risk
- Weight gain, cushingoid features
- Avascular necrosis

---
sections.takayasu-arteritis-brief-overview:
| Feature | Takayasu | GCA |
|---|---|---|
| **Age** | <40 (typically 15-30) | >50 |
| **Sex** | Female >> male (9:1) | Female > male (2-3:1) |
| **Vessels** | Aorta and primary branches | Temporal, ophthalmic, aorta |
| **Geography** | Asia > other regions | Northern European descent |
| **Presentation** | Limb claudication, absent pulses ("pulseless disease"), BP discrepancy between arms, bruits | Headache, jaw claudication, visual loss |
| **Treatment** | Glucocorticoids ± methotrexate/tocilizumab | Same |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment
- **Visual assessment** — ask about vision changes at EVERY encounter; document acuity
- **Temporal artery exam** — palpate for tenderness, thickening, nodularity, diminished pulse
- **Scalp tenderness** — ask specifically about pain brushing hair or wearing hats
- **Jaw claudication** — ask about jaw pain while chewing (very specific)
- **Blood pressure in BOTH arms** — discrepancy >15 mmHg systemic suggests large-vessel involvement
- **Constitutional symptoms** — weight loss, fever, fatigue

### Steroid Administration and Monitoring
- **IV methylprednisolone** for vision-threatening GCA — infuse over 30-60 minutes
- Monitor blood glucose — steroids cause hyperglycemia, especially in diabetics
- **GI prophylaxis** — PPI while on high-dose steroids
- **Bone protection** — calcium 1200 mg + vitamin D 800 IU daily from the start
- Watch for steroid psychosis (agitation, insomnia, mood changes) — especially in elderly

### Patient Education
- **Report ANY vision changes immediately** — even brief episodes of blurring or vision loss in either eye; these may herald permanent blindness
- **Do not stop steroids abruptly** — adrenal crisis risk; always taper per provider
- **Steroid side effects are expected** — weight gain, moon face, insomnia, mood changes; these improve with taper
- **This is a long treatment course** — typically 1-2 years; adherence is critical
- **Ophthalmology follow-up** — baseline and regular eye exams

---
sections.references:
- Dejaco et al., "Giant Cell Arteritis and Polymyalgia Rheumatica," Lancet, 2017
- Hellmich et al., "EULAR Recommendations for the Management of Large Vessel Vasculitis," Annals of the Rheumatic Diseases, 2020
- Weyand & Goronzy, "Giant-Cell Arteritis and Polymyalgia Rheumatica," New England Journal of Medicine, 2014

clinicalPearls: **New headache in a patient >50 + elevated ESR = GCA until proven otherwise** — start steroids BEFORE biopsy; the biopsy can wait 2 weeks; the vision cannot
**Jaw claudication is the most specific symptom** — it has ~95% specificity for GCA; always ask about jaw pain with chewing
**Normal ESR does not rule out GCA** — ~5% of biopsy-proven GCA has normal ESR; use CRP as well; if clinical suspicion is high, treat regardless of labs
**Visual loss from GCA is preventable but NOT reversible** — once the optic nerve infarcts, that vision is gone; the urgency is protecting the remaining vision

**GCA and PMR are a spectrum** — bilateral shoulder/hip girdle stiffness + morning stiffness + elevated inflammatory markers in an elderly patient = PMR; add headache or visual symptoms = GCA; always screen PMR patients for GCA symptoms
**Steroids work FAST** — dramatic symptom improvement within 24-48 hours is actually a diagnostic clue; if the patient doesn't improve on high-dose steroids, reconsider the diagnosis
**Negative biopsy ≠ no GCA** — skip lesions cause 15-40% false negative rate; if clinical picture is compelling, keep treating

major-depressive-disorder.title: Major Depressive Disorder

sections.definition:
Major depressive disorder is the most common psychiatric condition, characterized by persistent depressed mood and/or loss of interest in activities, accompanied by neurovegetative symptoms that significantly impair daily functioning. In the ED, depression most often presents in two scenarios: the patient in crisis (suicidal ideation or attempt — see Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm) or the patient with somatic complaints that are actually depression in disguise.

The critical ED role is not to treat chronic depression — it is to **assess safety, rule out medical mimics, identify suicidality, and connect to appropriate follow-up**.

**Related notes:** Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm · Bipolar Disorder · Anxiety Disorders and Panic · Antidepressants · Alcohol Use Disorder

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Lifetime prevalence:** 16-20% (most common psychiatric disorder)
- **12-month prevalence:** ~7% of US adults
- **Gender:** Women 2x more likely than men (hormonal, psychosocial, and reporting factors)
- **Peak onset:** 20s-30s, but can occur at any age
- **Recurrence:** >50% will have another episode; risk increases with each episode
- **Comorbidity:** Anxiety disorders (60-70% comorbidity), substance use (30-40%), chronic pain, cardiovascular disease, diabetes
- **Suicide:** ~4% of people with MDD die by suicide; risk is 20x the general population

---
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — serotonin (5-HT), norepinephrine, and dopamine signal via GPCRs; monoamine hypothesis = insufficient synaptic signaling at these receptors. Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the HPA axis stress-response feedback loop is disrupted in depression; cortisol hypersecretion feeds back to suppress hippocampal neurogenesis.*

### Monoamine Hypothesis (Classic Model)

Depression = deficiency of serotonin (5-HT), norepinephrine (NE), and/or dopamine (DA) in critical brain circuits. This model explains why SSRIs, SNRIs, and other monoamine-acting drugs work — but it's incomplete because:
- Monoamine levels increase within hours of starting an antidepressant, but clinical improvement takes 2-6 weeks
- Not all patients respond to monoamine drugs
- Ketamine works via glutamate (not monoamines) and takes effect within hours

### Neuroplasticity / Neurotrophic Model

- Depression involves impaired neuroplasticity — reduced BDNF (brain-derived neurotrophic factor), hippocampal atrophy, reduced synaptic connections in prefrontal cortex
- Chronic stress → elevated cortisol → hippocampal neurotoxicity → impaired neurogenesis
- Antidepressants (and ketamine) work by restoring BDNF and promoting synaptogenesis — this takes weeks (explaining the therapeutic delay)
- See Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology for receptor and second messenger details

### HPA Axis Dysregulation

- Hyperactivity of the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis → chronically elevated cortisol
- Impaired negative feedback (dexamethasone suppression test often abnormal)
- Links depression to cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and immunosuppression

### Inflammatory Model

- Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, CRP) in depressed patients
- "Sickness behavior" (fatigue, anhedonia, social withdrawal, sleep changes) resembles depression
- Anti-inflammatory treatments show antidepressant effects in some populations
- Bidirectional: chronic medical illness → inflammation → depression → worsened medical outcomes

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### DSM-5 Criteria — SIG E CAPS Mnemonic

Five or more of the following for ≥2 weeks (must include depressed mood OR anhedonia):

| Criterion | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **S**leep changes | Insomnia (early morning awakening is classic) or hypersomnia |
| **I**nterest loss (anhedonia) | Loss of pleasure in previously enjoyed activities — one of two REQUIRED symptoms |
| **G**uilt / worthlessness | Excessive, inappropriate guilt; feelings of worthlessness |
| **E**nergy loss / fatigue | Persistent fatigue even with adequate rest |
| **C**oncentration impaired | Difficulty focusing, making decisions, "brain fog" |
| **A**ppetite changes | Decreased or increased appetite with weight change (≥5% in a month) |
| **P**sychomotor changes | Retardation (slowed movements, speech) or agitation — observable by others |
| **S**uicidal ideation | Recurrent thoughts of death, SI with or without plan |
| **Depressed mood** | Sad, empty, hopeless — one of two REQUIRED symptoms. May be reported by patient or observed by others |

### Depression in the ED — How It Actually Presents

Patients rarely walk in saying "I'm depressed." More commonly:

- **Somatic complaints:** Headache, back pain, chest pain, fatigue, GI symptoms, dizziness — with negative medical workup after negative medical workup. "Frequent flyer" with no identifiable organic cause
- **Vague complaints:** "I just don't feel right." "Something is wrong but I can't explain it."
- **Suicidal crisis:** Post-attempt, active SI with plan, or SI disclosed during medical visit for another reason
- **Functional decline:** Brought in by family because they "stopped eating," "won't get out of bed," "stopped taking medications"
- **Substance use:** Self-medicating with alcohol or drugs — the depression is the underlying driver
- **Elderly:** Depression presents atypically — cognitive impairment ("pseudodementia"), somatic complaints, social withdrawal, weight loss. Often mistaken for dementia or "just aging"

### Red Flags — Is This Really Depression, or Something Else?

| Finding | Consider Instead |
|---------|-----------------|
| **First episode after age 50** with no prior history | Medical cause: hypothyroidism, cancer (especially pancreatic), stroke, B12 deficiency, Parkinson's disease |
| **Mania or hypomania history** | Bipolar Disorder — antidepressant monotherapy contraindicated |
| **Psychotic features** (delusions, hallucinations) | MDD with psychotic features (nihilistic delusions, somatic delusions, guilt-congruent hallucinations) OR Schizophrenia |
| **Postpartum onset** (within 4 weeks of delivery) | Postpartum depression (screen with Edinburgh Postnatal Depression Scale) — also rule out thyroid dysfunction |
| **Seasonal pattern** | Seasonal affective disorder — light therapy may be first-line |
| **Cognitive decline predominant** in elderly | Pseudodementia (depression) vs true dementia — key difference: pseudodementia patients are distressed by their cognitive symptoms; dementia patients are often unaware |

---
sections.medical-mimics-of-depression:
Always consider — especially in new-onset or treatment-resistant depression:

| Cause | Screening |
|-------|-----------|
| **Hypothyroidism** | TSH (most common medical mimic) |
| **Anemia** | CBC |
| **B12 / folate deficiency** | B12, folate levels |
| **Chronic infection** (HIV, hepatitis, Lyme) | Based on risk factors |
| **Sleep apnea** | Ask about snoring, daytime somnolence |
| **Medication-induced** | Beta-blockers, corticosteroids, interferon, hormonal contraceptives, isotretinoin, benzodiazepines (paradoxical), opioids |
| **Substance use** | Alcohol (CNS depressant), cannabis, stimulant withdrawal |
| **Chronic pain** | Pain and depression are bidirectional |
| **Neurologic** | Parkinson's (depression may precede motor symptoms), MS, stroke, TBI |
| **Endocrine** | Cushing's, Addison's, hyperparathyroidism, testosterone deficiency |
| **Cancer** | Especially pancreatic (classically presents with depression before other symptoms) |

---
sections.ed-management:
### Safety Assessment (Priority #1)

- **Screen every depressed patient for suicidality** — ask directly: "Are you having thoughts of killing yourself?" Direct questions do NOT increase suicide risk (this is a myth)
- Use structured screening: Columbia Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) or ASQ (Ask Suicide-Screening Questions)
- See Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm for complete risk assessment and management

### Medical Workup for New or Worsening Depression

| Test | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| TSH | Hypothyroidism |
| CBC | Anemia |
| BMP | Electrolytes, renal function |
| Glucose | Hypo/hyperglycemia |
| B12 | Deficiency (especially elderly, vegans) |
| UDS, BAL | Co-occurring substance use |
| Pregnancy test | Women of childbearing age (changes management, postpartum risk) |

### Disposition

| Scenario | Disposition |
|----------|------------|
| Active SI with plan/intent or recent attempt | Psychiatric admission (voluntary or involuntary) |
| SI without plan, stable, with safety plan and follow-up | May discharge with safety plan + 24-48 hour outpatient follow-up. SW involvement essential |
| Depression without SI, functional | Discharge with outpatient referral. Do NOT start antidepressants in ED unless clear plan for follow-up |
| Post-attempt, medically cleared | Psychiatric evaluation (SW → psychiatry → admission vs RRC vs discharge with intensive outpatient) |

> **Do NOT prescribe benzodiazepines to treat depression.** They are CNS depressants, increase suicide risk, are addictive, and do not treat the underlying disorder. If the patient has comorbid anxiety, refer to outpatient for appropriate treatment initiation.

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Somatic complaints with negative workups should trigger a depression screen.** The patient who has been to the ED 6 times in 3 months for headaches with normal CT, normal labs, and no red flags may be depressed. A simple "How have you been feeling emotionally?" can open the door
- **Ask about suicidality directly.** "Are you thinking about hurting yourself?" is not intrusive — it is the standard of care. Patients are often relieved to be asked because they didn't know how to bring it up. See Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm
- **Depression in men presents differently.** Men are less likely to report sadness and more likely to present with irritability, anger, risk-taking, substance use, or somatic complaints. Ask about sleep, energy, and interest — not just "are you sad?"
- **Medication reconciliation matters.** If the patient is on an antidepressant, ask: Are they taking it? Did they recently stop? Was it recently changed? Abrupt SSRI/SNRI discontinuation can cause discontinuation syndrome (dizziness, nausea, "brain zaps," irritability, flu-like symptoms) — which itself can bring a patient to the ED
- **Elderly depression is underdiagnosed and underreated.** It is NOT a normal part of aging. Screen elderly patients with persistent fatigue, weight loss, cognitive complaints, or social withdrawal. PHQ-2 ("over the past 2 weeks, have you felt down/hopeless? Lost interest in things?") takes 30 seconds
- **The patient who says "I don't want to be here" may mean more than one thing.** Clarify: "Do you mean you don't want to be in the ED, or do you mean you don't want to be alive?" This distinction matters enormously

---
sections.references:
- APA Practice Guidelines for the Treatment of Major Depressive Disorder, 3rd Edition
- Malhi GS, Mann JJ: Depression. Lancet 2018
- PHQ-9 Validation Studies (Kroenke et al, Journal of General Internal Medicine 2001)
- Duman RS, Aghajanian GK: Synaptic Dysfunction in Depression: Potential Therapeutic Targets. Science 2012

---

*See also: Suicidal Ideation and Self-Harm · Bipolar Disorder · Anxiety Disorders and Panic · Antidepressants · Alcohol Use Disorder · Schizophrenia*

clinicalPearls: **Depression is the leading cause of disability worldwide.** It is not a character weakness, a choice, or something the patient can "snap out of." It is a brain disease with identifiable neurobiological changes. Approach with the same clinical seriousness as diabetes or heart failure.

**Pancreatic cancer classically presents with depression before any GI symptoms.** New-onset depression in a middle-aged or older adult with no psychiatric history and unexplained weight loss warrants further investigation. This is low-frequency but high-impact knowledge.

**PHQ-2 is your fastest screening tool.** Two questions: (1) Little interest or pleasure in doing things? (2) Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless? If positive, follow up with the PHQ-9 for severity. Takes 30 seconds and can identify patients who would never have mentioned their depression.

**Serotonin syndrome is a life-threatening complication of serotonergic medications.** Classic triad: altered mental status + neuromuscular hyperactivity (clonus, hyperreflexia, rigidity) + autonomic instability (hyperthermia, tachycardia, diaphoresis). Risk increases with multiple serotonergic agents or overdose. Treatment: stop offending agents, benzodiazepines, cyproheptadine, cooling.

signs: **Somatic complaints:** Headache, back pain, chest pain, fatigue, GI symptoms, dizziness — with negative medical workup after negative medical workup. "Frequent flyer" with no identifiable organic cause
**Vague complaints:** "I just don't feel right." "Something is wrong but I can't explain it."
**Suicidal crisis:** Post-attempt, active SI with plan, or SI disclosed during medical visit for another reason
**Functional decline:** Brought in by family because they "stopped eating," "won't get out of bed," "stopped taking medications"
**Substance use:** Self-medicating with alcohol or drugs — the depression is the underlying driver
**Elderly:** Depression presents atypically — cognitive impairment ("pseudodementia"), somatic complaints, social withdrawal, weight loss. Often mistaken for dementia or "just aging"

mdr-tb-multidrug-resistant-tuberculosis.title: MDR-TB (Multidrug-Resistant Tuberculosis)

sections.microbiology-of-mycobacterium-tuberculos:
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — MDR-TB = resistance to isoniazid + rifampin via chromosomal mutations (katG, rpoB, inhA); resistance emerges from incomplete/inadequate treatment allowing natural selection of resistant mutants — a direct consequence of Darwinian selection pressure. Genetics and Protein Expression — TB resistance is mutation-driven (not plasmid-mediated like gram-negatives); each drug selects for pre-existing spontaneous mutants; combination therapy is required to prevent selection of multiply-resistant organisms.*

### Unique Bacterial Features
- **Acid-fast bacillus (AFB)** — retains carbol fuchsin stain despite acid-alcohol wash (Ziehl-Neelsen stain)
- **Mycolic acid cell wall** — the defining feature:
  - ~60% of cell wall is lipid (vs ~2% in gram-negatives)
  - Creates a **waxy, hydrophobic barrier** that is:
    - Impermeable to most antibiotics (intrinsic resistance)
    - Resistant to desiccation (survives in aerosol droplets)
    - Resistant to many disinfectants
    - Resistant to complement and oxidative killing by macrophages
- **Extremely slow growth:** Doubling time ~24 hours (vs 20 minutes for E. coli)
  - This means cultures take **2-8 weeks** to grow
  - Drug susceptibility testing adds additional weeks
  - Total time from specimen to resistance results: potentially 2-3 months with conventional methods
- **Obligate aerobe** — preferentially infects lung apices (highest PO₂)
- **Intracellular pathogen** — survives and replicates inside macrophages

### Why the Mycolic Acid Wall Matters Clinically
The waxy cell wall explains almost everything about TB:
1. **Slow growth** — nutrients cross the wall slowly
2. **Intrinsic drug resistance** — most antibiotics can't penetrate
3. **Long treatment courses** — drugs reach bacteria slowly; dormant bacilli require prolonged exposure
4. **Acid-fast staining** — mycolic acids prevent decolorization
5. **Airborne transmission** — resistant to desiccation, survives as droplet nuclei
6. **Granuloma formation** — immune system walls off what it can't kill

---
sections.tb-pathogenesis-the-immune-battle:
### Initial Infection
1. **Inhalation** of droplet nuclei (1-5 μm) containing M. tuberculosis
2. Bacilli reach **terminal alveoli** (lung apices preferred — highest O₂)
3. **Alveolar macrophages** engulf the bacilli via phagocytosis
4. **M. tuberculosis BLOCKS phagosome-lysosome fusion** — this is the central immune evasion strategy
   - Normally: phagosome fuses with lysosome → acidic environment kills pathogen
   - TB: the bacterium prevents this fusion → survives and replicates inside the macrophage
5. Infected macrophages release cytokines → recruit more macrophages and T cells

### Granuloma Formation
6. **CD4+ T cells** (Th1 response) produce **IFN-γ** → activate macrophages
7. Activated macrophages form **epithelioid cells** and fuse into **multinucleated giant cells (Langhans cells)**
8. These organize into a **granuloma** — a structured immune barricade:
   - Center: infected macrophages, sometimes **caseous necrosis** (cheese-like)
   - Surrounding: epithelioid cells, giant cells, lymphocytes, fibroblasts
9. Granuloma **contains but does not eliminate** the bacteria
10. Bacilli enter a **dormant state** within the granuloma — metabolically inactive, resistant to most drugs

> [!critical]
> **Latent TB infection (LTBI)** = viable but dormant M. tuberculosis contained within granulomas. The person is infected but **not infectious** and has **no symptoms**. LTBI can reactivate years to decades later if immune function declines (HIV, immunosuppression, aging, malnutrition).

### Reactivation
- When immune surveillance weakens → granuloma loses integrity → bacilli reactivate
- Reactivation typically occurs in **lung apices** (highest O₂ tension)
- Caseous center liquefies → communicates with airways → **cavitary disease** → patient becomes infectious
- Cough aerosolizes bacilli → airborne transmission to others

---
sections.drug-resistance-mechanisms:
### How Resistance Develops in TB
- TB drug resistance is **chromosomally encoded** — NOT plasmid-mediated (unlike most bacteria)
- Resistance arises from **spontaneous mutations** at predictable rates:
  - Isoniazid resistance: ~1 in 10⁶ bacilli
  - Rifampin resistance: ~1 in 10⁸ bacilli
  - Probability of spontaneous dual resistance: ~1 in 10¹⁴ (essentially impossible naturally)
- **This is why TB is ALWAYS treated with multiple drugs** — any single drug will select for pre-existing resistant mutants in the large bacterial population (cavitary TB lesions contain 10⁷-10⁹ organisms)
- MDR-TB arises from **sequential acquisition of resistance** — typically due to:
  - Incomplete treatment (non-adherence, interrupted supply)
  - Inadequate regimens (wrong drugs, wrong doses)
  - Malabsorption
  - Direct transmission of already-resistant strains

### Specific Resistance Mutations
| Drug | Target | Gene Mutated | Resistance Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Isoniazid** | Mycolic acid synthesis (InhA/enoyl-ACP reductase) | **katG** (most common), inhA promoter | katG mutation → loss of catalase-peroxidase that activates the prodrug INH; inhA mutation → target modification |
| **Rifampin** | RNA polymerase β subunit | **rpoB** | Altered binding site → rifampin cannot inhibit transcription |
| **Ethambutol** | Arabinosyltransferase (cell wall) | **embB** | Altered target |
| **Pyrazinamide** | Disrupts membrane energetics | **pncA** | Loss of pyrazinamidase that activates the prodrug |
| **Fluoroquinolones** | DNA gyrase | **gyrA, gyrB** | Altered binding site |
| **Aminoglycosides** | 16S rRNA | **rrs** | Altered ribosomal binding |

> [!important]
> **Isoniazid is a prodrug** — it must be activated by the bacterial enzyme **KatG** (catalase-peroxidase). The most common resistance mechanism is KatG mutation → enzyme no longer activates INH → INH is useless. This is why INH resistance is common — the bacteria just need to break their own activating enzyme.

---
sections.resistance-categories:
| Category | Definition | Global Burden |
|---|---|---|
| **Drug-susceptible TB** | Susceptible to all first-line drugs | Majority of cases |
| **INH-resistant TB** | Resistant to isoniazid, susceptible to rifampin | ~8% of new TB cases globally |
| **MDR-TB** | Resistant to at least **INH + rifampin** | ~3-4% of new, 18-21% of previously treated TB cases |
| **Pre-XDR-TB** | MDR + resistance to any fluoroquinolone | Subset of MDR |
| **XDR-TB** | MDR + fluoroquinolone resistance + bedaquiline or linezolid resistance | Most difficult to treat |

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Global:** ~10.6 million new TB cases/year; ~500,000 MDR/RR-TB cases
- **Highest burden:** India, China, Russian Federation, South Africa, Philippines
- **US:** Low overall TB incidence (~8,000 cases/year), but:
  - ~1-2% are MDR-TB
  - Most US MDR-TB in **foreign-born individuals** from high-burden countries
  - **Pacific Northwest:** WA state sees TB in immigrant/refugee communities (Somalia, Myanmar, Vietnam, Philippines)
- **HIV-TB co-infection:** Dramatically increases TB risk (20-30× higher) and complicates treatment
- **Risk factors for drug resistance:**
  - Previous TB treatment (strongest risk factor)
  - Contact with known MDR-TB case
  - Birth in high-burden country
  - HIV co-infection
  - Incarceration (especially in former Soviet countries)

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Active Pulmonary TB (Same for Drug-Susceptible and MDR)
- **Cough >2-3 weeks** — the cardinal symptom
- Hemoptysis (cavitary disease)
- Night sweats, fevers (often low-grade)
- Weight loss, anorexia
- Fatigue, malaise

### Red Flags for TB in the ED
- Chronic cough + weight loss + night sweats in a high-risk patient
- Upper lobe infiltrate or cavity on CXR
- Immigrant from high-burden country
- HIV-positive with any respiratory symptoms
- Known TB contact
- Incarceration history
- Homelessness

### Extrapulmonary TB (~20% of cases)
- **Lymph nodes** (most common extrapulmonary site — cervical lymphadenopathy)
- **Pleural** (effusion, usually lymphocytic)
- **Skeletal** (Pott disease = vertebral TB)
- **CNS** (TB meningitis — medical emergency, high mortality)
- **Genitourinary, peritoneal, pericardial**
- **Miliary** (disseminated — diffuse millet seed-sized nodules on CXR)

---
sections.diagnosis:
### Microbiologic Diagnosis
| Test | Speed | Sensitivity | Key Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **AFB smear** (Ziehl-Neelsen or auramine-rhodamine) | Hours | 50-60% (need ~5,000 organisms/mL) | Rapid screen; positive = highly infectious |
| **GeneXpert MTB/RIF** (Xpert) | **2 hours** | ~88% (comparable to culture for smear+) | **Simultaneously detects M. tuberculosis AND rifampin resistance** |
| **Liquid culture** (MGIT/BACTEC) | 1-4 weeks | Gold standard (~98%) | Definitive diagnosis; enables full DST |
| **Solid culture** (Lowenstein-Jensen) | 3-8 weeks | High but slower | Traditional method |
| **Drug susceptibility testing (DST)** | Additional 1-4 weeks after culture | N/A | Required for all isolates; determines treatment |

> [!important]
> **GeneXpert MTB/RIF is a game-changer** — it detects TB AND rifampin resistance in 2 hours from sputum. Rifampin resistance is a reliable surrogate marker for MDR-TB (95% of rifampin-resistant TB is also INH-resistant). If Xpert shows rifampin resistance → treat as MDR-TB while awaiting full DST.

### Molecular DST
- **Line probe assays (GenoType MTBDRplus)** — detect INH + rifampin resistance mutations directly from smear-positive sputum or culture
- **Whole genome sequencing** — emerging gold standard; identifies all known resistance mutations in one test; results in days once available

### Latent TB Testing
| Test | Mechanism | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|---|
| **TST (tuberculin skin test)** | Intradermal PPD → delayed hypersensitivity (48-72h read) | Inexpensive, widely available | BCG cross-reactivity, reader variability, requires return visit |
