| **IGRA (QuantiFERON, T-SPOT)** | Blood test measuring IFN-γ from TB-specific antigens | Single visit, no BCG interference | Cost, indeterminate results in immunocompromised |

---
sections.management:
### Drug-Susceptible TB (Standard Regimen — For Context)
**RIPE × 2 months → IR × 4 months (total 6 months):**
| Drug | Mechanism | Major Toxicity |
|---|---|---|
| **Rifampin (R)** | Inhibits RNA polymerase | Hepatotoxicity, orange discoloration, drug interactions (CYP450 inducer) |
| **Isoniazid (I)** | Inhibits mycolic acid synthesis | Hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy (give B6/pyridoxine) |
| **Pyrazinamide (P)** | Disrupts membrane energetics (acidic pH) | Hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia |
| **Ethambutol (E)** | Inhibits arabinosyltransferase (cell wall) | **Optic neuritis** (red-green color discrimination) |

### MDR-TB Treatment — Dramatically Different
- **Longer:** 9-20 months (vs 6 months for drug-susceptible)
- **More drugs:** Typically 4-5 active agents
- **More toxic:** Second-line drugs have worse side effect profiles
- **Requires specialist management** — ID and/or pulmonology with TB expertise
- **Directly observed therapy (DOT)** mandatory

### MDR-TB Regimen Components
| Priority | Drugs | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Group A (include all 3)** | Bedaquiline, linezolid, levofloxacin/moxifloxacin | Form the backbone of all MDR-TB regimens |
| **Group B (add 1-2)** | Clofazimine, cycloserine/terizidone | Added to strengthen regimen |
| **Group C (if needed)** | Ethambutol, delamanid, pyrazinamide, imipenem-cilastatin + amoxicillin-clavulanate, ethionamide/prothionamide, p-aminosalicylic acid | Added if Groups A/B insufficient |

### Key MDR-TB Drugs
| Drug | Mechanism | Critical Toxicity | Monitoring |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bedaquiline** | Inhibits ATP synthase (mycobacterial-specific) | **QTc prolongation** | ECG at baseline, 2, 12, 24 weeks; hold if QTc >500 ms |
| **Linezolid** | Inhibits 50S ribosomal subunit (protein synthesis) | **Myelosuppression** (anemia, thrombocytopenia), **peripheral/optic neuropathy**, lactic acidosis | CBC weekly × 2 months then monthly; report vision changes or paresthesias |
| **Fluoroquinolones** (levo/moxi) | Inhibit DNA gyrase | QTc prolongation, tendinopathy, C. diff, dysglycemia | ECG monitoring; avoid concurrent QT-prolonging drugs |
| **Clofazimine** | Multiple mechanisms (exact unknown) | **Skin discoloration** (red-brown, reversible), QTc prolongation | Counsel patient about cosmetic effect; ECG |
| **Delamanid** | Inhibits mycolic acid synthesis (different mechanism than INH) | QTc prolongation, hepatotoxicity | ECG monthly; LFTs |

> [!warning]
> **QTc prolongation is the recurring theme in MDR-TB treatment** — bedaquiline, fluoroquinolones, clofazimine, and delamanid ALL prolong QTc. Combining these requires serial ECG monitoring and avoiding other QT-prolonging medications.

### Treatment Outcomes
| Category | Drug-Susceptible TB | MDR-TB | XDR-TB |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Treatment success** | ~85% | ~60% | ~40% |
| **Duration** | 6 months | 9-20 months | 18-24+ months |
| **Side effects** | Generally tolerable | Significant, often treatment-limiting | Severe |
| **Cost** | ~$500 | ~$100,000-250,000 | Higher |

---
sections.infection-prevention-the-priority-in-the:
### Airborne Precautions
- **N95 respirator** (fit-tested) for all healthcare workers entering the room
- **Negative pressure room (AIIR)** — air exchanges to outside; door must remain closed
- Patient wears **surgical mask** when outside the room (during transport)
- **NEVER use a regular surgical mask** for provider TB protection — droplet nuclei (1-5 μm) pass through surgical masks

### ED Workflow for Suspected TB
1. **Triage screening** — ask about cough duration, risk factors (birth country, HIV, incarceration, homelessness, contacts)
2. **Immediate masking** — surgical mask on the patient at triage
3. **Place in AIIR** as soon as available — do NOT leave in waiting room
4. **Three sputum specimens** — ideally 8-24 hours apart; at least one early morning specimen
   - In the ED: collect first specimen and arrange inpatient collection of remaining two
5. **GeneXpert** on first specimen if available
6. **AFB smear** — if positive, patient is highly infectious
7. **Notify infection prevention and public health**

### When to Discontinue Airborne Precautions
Standard: **3 consecutive negative AFB smears** collected 8-24 hours apart PLUS:
- Clinical improvement on treatment
- Alternative diagnosis confirmed
- Low clinical suspicion

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### N95 Fit Testing
- **Annual fit testing required** for all ED nurses
- Know your N95 size and model
- **Facial hair compromises seal** — this is a real issue to address professionally
- If fit test fails → try different model or use PAPR (powered air-purifying respirator)

### Specimen Collection
- **Sputum induction** if patient cannot produce spontaneous specimen — nebulized hypertonic saline (3%)
  - This is an **aerosol-generating procedure** — perform in AIIR with N95
- Label specimens correctly: "AFB culture and smear" + "GeneXpert MTB/RIF" if available
- Handle specimens with standard precautions (TB is an airborne risk primarily during aerosolization, not surface contact)

### Medication Administration (MDR-TB Inpatient)
- **Bedaquiline/clofazimine** — administer with food (improves absorption)
- **Linezolid** — monitor for drug interactions (serotonergic drugs → serotonin syndrome; tyramine-containing foods)
- **Pyridoxine (B6)** 25-50 mg/day with any regimen containing INH, cycloserine, or linezolid (prevents neuropathy)
- **ECG monitoring** — obtain baseline and serial ECGs; know your unit's QTc threshold for holding doses
- **Directly Observed Therapy (DOT)** — observe medication ingestion; this is standard of care for TB

### Patient Education
- **TB is curable** — even MDR-TB, with proper treatment and adherence
- Treatment is long but essential — stopping early causes resistance and relapse
- Infectious until proven otherwise — avoid contact with immunocompromised persons until cleared
- Side effects should be reported immediately (vision changes, numbness, hearing changes)
- Public health will be involved — this is required by law, not optional

### Occupational Exposure
- **Baseline and annual TST or IGRA** for all ED healthcare workers
- If new conversion: evaluate for LTBI treatment
- **Exposure incident** (unprotected contact with confirmed TB): notify employee health, begin monitoring

---
sections.references:
- WHO, "WHO Consolidated Guidelines on Tuberculosis: Module 4 — Drug-Resistant Tuberculosis Treatment," 2022
- Nahid et al., "Treatment of Drug-Resistant Tuberculosis: ATS/CDC/IDSA Practice Guidelines," American Journal of Respiratory and Critical Care Medicine, 2019
- Behr et al., "Tuberculosis: Past, Present, and Future," New England Journal of Medicine, 2018
- Lange et al., "Management of Drug-Resistant Tuberculosis," Lancet, 2019

clinicalPearls: **Chronic cough + upper lobe cavity + night sweats = TB until proven otherwise** — put the mask on the patient and get them into an AIIR before anything else
**Rifampin resistance on GeneXpert = treat as MDR-TB** — 95% of rifampin-resistant TB is also INH-resistant; don't wait weeks for full DST to start appropriate therapy
**Multi-drug therapy prevents resistance; inadequate therapy creates it** — TB is treated with 4 drugs because a single drug will select for resistant mutants from the 10⁸ bacteria in a cavity

**Isoniazid is a prodrug activated by KatG** — understanding this explains why INH resistance is so common (bacteria just break the activating enzyme) and why resistance doesn't cross over to other drug classes
**N95, not surgical mask** — droplet nuclei are 1-5 μm and pass right through surgical masks; this is the difference between airborne and droplet precautions
**QTc is the MDR-TB nurse's constant companion** — bedaquiline + moxifloxacin + clofazimine all prolong QTc; know your thresholds and escalate when QTc >500 ms
**Foreign-born patient with unilateral upper lobe infiltrate on CXR in the ED = isolate first, diagnose second** — the infection control intervention is more urgent than the diagnostic workup
**Linezolid revolutionized MDR-TB** — but it comes with a price; myelosuppression and neuropathy are dose-dependent and time-dependent; weekly CBCs are not optional

signs: **Cough >2-3 weeks** — the cardinal symptom
Hemoptysis (cavitary disease)
Night sweats, fevers (often low-grade)
Weight loss, anorexia
Fatigue, malaise
Chronic cough + weight loss + night sweats in a high-risk patient
Upper lobe infiltrate or cavity on CXR
Immigrant from high-burden country
HIV-positive with any respiratory symptoms
Known TB contact
Incarceration history
Homelessness
**Lymph nodes** (most common extrapulmonary site — cervical lymphadenopathy)
**Pleural** (effusion, usually lymphocytic)
**Skeletal** (Pott disease = vertebral TB)
**CNS** (TB meningitis — medical emergency, high mortality)
**Genitourinary, peritoneal, pericardial**
**Miliary** (disseminated — diffuse millet seed-sized nodules on CXR)
redFlags: Chronic cough + weight loss + night sweats in a high-risk patient
Upper lobe infiltrate or cavity on CXR
Immigrant from high-burden country
HIV-positive with any respiratory symptoms
Known TB contact
Incarceration history

measles.title: Measles

sections.definition:
Measles is a **highly contagious, vaccine-preventable viral illness** caused by the measles virus (paramyxovirus). It spreads via **airborne transmission** (aerosolized droplets that remain suspended in air for up to 2 hours after the infected person leaves). A single measles case in an ED waiting room can expose dozens of patients and staff.

Measles is resurging in the US due to declining vaccination rates. It is a **public health emergency** — every suspected case triggers immediate isolation, health department notification, and contact tracing.

**Related notes:** Infection and Infectious Disease · Inflammation and Immune Response

---
sections.epidemiology:
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — measles has an R₀ of 12–18, the highest of any respiratory pathogen; airborne transmission (droplet nuclei persist suspended for 2 hours) combined with pre-symptomatic contagiousness (4 days before rash) makes containment dependent entirely on herd immunity (>95% vaccination required)*
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — measles virus induces profound immune suppression for 2–3 years post-infection (measles immune amnesia), during which the adaptive immune system loses memory cells and children become susceptible to infections they were previously immune to, explaining the spike in childhood mortality from other infections following measles outbreaks*

- **Transmission:** Airborne (one of the most contagious diseases known — R₀ of 12-18, meaning one case can infect 12-18 unvaccinated contacts)
- **Incubation:** 10-14 days from exposure to symptom onset; rash appears ~14 days post-exposure
- **Contagious period:** 4 days before rash onset through 4 days after rash appears
- **Risk factors for severe disease:** Immunocompromised, malnourished, pregnant, unvaccinated children <5, adults >20
- **Complications:** Pneumonia (most common cause of measles death), encephalitis (1 in 1,000 cases), subacute sclerosing panencephalitis (SSPE — rare, fatal, years later), otitis media, diarrhea/dehydration
- **Mortality:** 1-2 per 1,000 cases in developed countries; much higher in immunocompromised and malnourished populations

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Classic Presentation (The 3 C's + Rash)
1. **Cough** — dry, persistent
2. **Coryza** — runny nose, nasal congestion
3. **Conjunctivitis** — bilateral, non-purulent ("red eyes")
4. **High fever** — often 104°F+ (40°C+)
5. **Koplik spots** — pathognomonic; tiny white-blue spots on buccal mucosa (inside cheeks opposite molars); appear 2-3 days before rash
6. **Maculopapular rash** — starts at hairline/face, spreads cephalocaudally (downward to trunk → extremities); appears ~day 4 of illness; lasts 5-6 days

### Complications
- **Pneumonia** — primary viral or secondary bacterial; leading cause of measles mortality
- **Encephalitis** — 1 in 1,000; presents with seizures, AMS, focal deficits; high mortality/morbidity
- **SSPE** — subacute sclerosing panencephalitis; rare (1 in 10,000); occurs 7-10 years post-infection; progressive, fatal
- **Otitis media** — common in children
- **Severe dehydration** — from fever, poor intake, diarrhea
- **Pregnancy:** Premature labor, low birth weight, fetal death

---
sections.when-to-suspect-measles-at-triage:
> **Source: Clinical Educator (Pam) — Measles Triage Guide** `#pam-training`

Suspect measles if **ALL** of these apply:
- ✅ Fever ≥101°F (38.3°C)
- ✅ Maculopapular rash
- ✅ At least ONE of the "3 C's" (cough, coryza, conjunctivitis)

**High-risk clues that increase suspicion:**
- Unvaccinated or unknown MMR status
- Recent international travel or exposure at public sites (past 21 days)
- Exposure to someone with fever + rash illness
- Young children, infants, pregnant, or immunocompromised patients

> **With rising measles nationwide and active cases in Washington state (2025-2026), any patient with fever + rash must be rapidly isolated at triage.** When in doubt — isolate first, ask questions second.

---
sections.ed-measles-workflow-multicare:
> **Source: MultiCare ED Measles Workflow (12/22/2025) via Clinical Educator (Pam)** `#pam-training`

### Immediately Upon Arrival

1. **Place a mask on the patient AND family members**
2. **Escort directly to a room** — **negative pressure room preferred**
   - If negative pressure room unavailable → single room with door closed
   - **BYPASS the waiting area** — do NOT let patient/family wait in the waiting room
   - If no room available → determine an alternative private waiting space
3. If pre-arrival notification → **prepare the room BEFORE patient arrives**

### Prepare and Protect the Team

1. **Place Airborne isolation precautions signage** on the room
2. **Designate which staff will provide care:**
   - Must be **non-pregnant**
   - Must be **up-to-date on vaccinations OR have positive measles titers**
3. **PPE for all room entry:** N95 respirator + eye protection + gown + gloves
4. **Log ALL persons entering the room** (for contact tracing)

### Is Measles Suspected?

**If YES → Immediately notify:**
- **Local Health Jurisdiction** (for guidance on testing, room/lobby closures if needed)
- **MHS Infection Prevention Team**

**If NO (measles not suspected but exposure suspected):**
- If exposed to measles but asymptomatic and without evidence of immunity:
- **Order and administer MMR vaccine (IM)** or **IV immunoglobulin (IG)** per WA State DOH guidelines
- IV IG available in ALL MHS Emergency Departments
- **IM IG available at:** Deaconess, Yakima, Mary Bridge
- **MMR vaccine:** Refer to PCP or community pharmacy

### Orders and Testing

1. **Collect and HOLD urine specimen**
2. **Collect NP swab:**
   - Order: **Misc Test (L00044)**
   - In comments specify: **"NP swab for measles testing"**
   - **Specimen collection procedure:**
     - Don PPE (gown, N95, eye protection, gloves)
     - Confirm 2-patient ID; label specimen container with initials, time/date
     - Collect NP swab → place in viral transport media → place in lab bag
     - Doff PPE
     - Place labeled specimen in **refrigerator** — courier will pick up
     - **Document in chart** — indicate all PPE that was worn

### Disposition

| Decision | Key Actions |
|----------|-------------|
| **Admit** | Pre-notify receiving unit; coordinate shortest path to inpatient bed to reduce exposure risk |
| **Transfer** | Coordinate with transport team to ensure reduced exposure risk during transport |
| **Discharge** | AVS must emphasize: **quarantine for at least 4 days after rash appearance**. Unvaccinated family members → refer for post-exposure prophylaxis (direct them to wear a mask) |

### After Patient Departs the Room
- **Keep the room EMPTY for 2 hours with the door closed** (airborne particles remain suspended)
- After 2 hours → clean with standard disinfectant products

### Exposure Management
- If exposure is confirmed → refer patient to PCP or DOH
- **If IG given:** quarantine up to **28 days**
- **If MMR given:** quarantine still needed; avoid non-immune visitors and all public settings for **21 days**

---
sections.washington-state-exposure-tracking:
- WA DOH Measles Dashboard: Available through WA DOH Arcgis dashboard
- WA State DOH Measles Cases page: doh.wa.gov
- WA DOH Healthcare Facility Post-Exposure Prophylaxis Guideline (for IG/MMR decision-making)

---
sections.diagnosis:
| Test | Purpose | Notes |
|------|---------|-------|
| **NP swab for measles PCR** | Confirmatory | Ordered as Misc Test (L00044) at MHS |
| **Urine for measles PCR** | Additional specimen | Collect and hold |
| **Measles IgM serology** | Acute infection | May be negative in first 3 days of rash; sensitivity increases after day 3 |
| **Measles IgG serology** | Prior immunity | Positive = immune (prior vaccination or infection) |

### Clinical Diagnosis
- **Koplik spots + 3 C's + fever + rash** = clinically diagnostic
- Do not wait for lab confirmation to initiate isolation and public health notification

---
sections.management:
### Treatment
- **Supportive care** — no specific antiviral therapy exists for measles
- **Fever management** — acetaminophen or ibuprofen
- **Hydration** — oral or IV fluids as needed
- **Vitamin A** — WHO recommends for all children with measles (reduces mortality and complication rates):
  - <6 months: 50,000 IU × 2 days
  - 6-11 months: 100,000 IU × 2 days
  - ≥12 months: 200,000 IU × 2 days
- **Treat secondary bacterial infections** (pneumonia, otitis) if present — antibiotics as indicated
- **Monitor for complications** — pneumonia, encephalitis, dehydration

### Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP)
| Population | PEP | Timing |
|-----------|-----|--------|
| Unvaccinated immunocompetent contacts ≥6 months | **MMR vaccine** | Within 72 hours of exposure |
| Immunocompromised, pregnant, infants <6 months | **Immunoglobulin (IG)** | Within 6 days of exposure |
| Unvaccinated contacts (72 hr-6 days post-exposure) | **IG** (MMR less effective after 72 hr) | Within 6 days |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Isolation is your #1 priority** — measles in a waiting room is a public health disaster. Every minute an infectious patient sits in a shared space, more people are exposed. Mask and isolate IMMEDIATELY
- **Know your own immune status** — you should know your measles titer. If non-immune and not pregnant, you should not be caring for these patients
- **N95 is mandatory** — surgical masks are NOT sufficient for airborne precautions
- **Log everyone who enters** — this is not optional. Contact tracing depends on this documentation
- **2-hour room closure** — after the patient leaves, that room is still infectious. Enforce the 2-hour empty period with the door closed
- **Communicate early with infection prevention** — they will guide you on facility-wide exposure assessment and whether the waiting room needs to be closed

---

clinicalPearls: **Measles is the most contagious disease you'll encounter in the ED.** One patient in a waiting room for 30 minutes can infect every unvaccinated person in that space — and the virus lingers in the air for 2 hours after they leave. Speed of isolation is everything.

**Koplik spots are pathognomonic but fleeting.** They appear 2-3 days before the rash and disappear within 1-2 days of rash onset. If you see white-blue spots on the buccal mucosa in a febrile patient with cough/coryza/conjunctivitis — that's measles until proven otherwise.

**The rash starts at the head and moves down.** This cephalocaudal spread pattern distinguishes measles from many other viral exanthems. If the rash started on the trunk or extremities, think of other diagnoses.

**"Looks like a cold" can be measles.** The prodrome (cough, runny nose, red eyes, fever) looks like any viral URI. The distinguishing feature is severity of fever (often >104°F) and the progression to rash. In the context of known community cases or travel history, maintain high suspicion.

**Ask about vaccination status and travel.** Two questions that take 10 seconds and can completely change your triage decision. Unvaccinated + recent travel or known exposure + febrile illness = isolate first, ask questions second.

signs: **Pneumonia** — primary viral or secondary bacterial; leading cause of measles mortality
**Encephalitis** — 1 in 1,000; presents with seizures, AMS, focal deficits; high mortality/morbidity
**SSPE** — subacute sclerosing panencephalitis; rare (1 in 10,000); occurs 7-10 years post-infection; progressive, fatal
**Otitis media** — common in children
**Severe dehydration** — from fever, poor intake, diarrhea
**Pregnancy:** Premature labor, low birth weight, fetal death

membranous-nephropathy.title: Membranous Nephropathy

sections.definition:
Membranous nephropathy is an autoimmune glomerular disease characterized by immune complex deposition on the subepithelial surface of the glomerular basement membrane (GBM), causing diffuse thickening of the capillary walls and heavy proteinuria. It is the most frequent cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults.

Primary (idiopathic) MN accounts for approximately 75-80% of cases and is driven by circulating autoantibodies against podocyte antigens, most notably the M-type phospholipase A2 receptor (PLA2R). Secondary MN (~20-25%) is associated with autoimmune diseases (systemic lupus erythematosus), infections (hepatitis B, hepatitis C), drugs (NSAIDs, gold, penicillamine), and malignancies (solid organ tumors, especially lung and gastrointestinal). The natural history classically follows a "rule of thirds" — approximately one-third of patients achieve spontaneous remission, one-third maintain stable but persistent proteinuria, and one-third progress to kidney failure.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** Annual incidence approximately 10-12 per million population in North America. Accounts for 20-30% of nephrotic syndrome cases in adults and is the leading cause of primary nephrotic syndrome in Caucasian adults.
- **Risk factors:** Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DQA1 and PLA2R1 risk alleles), environmental exposures (air pollution — PM2.5 associated with increased MN incidence in China), hepatitis B infection, autoimmune diseases (Systemic Lupus Erythematosus), malignancy (especially in patients >60 years), certain medications
- **Demographics:** Mean age at diagnosis 50-60 years. Male-to-female ratio approximately 2:1. More common in Caucasian populations. In children, MN is rare and secondary causes (especially lupus and hepatitis B) are more common. In patients >60 years, malignancy screening is essential.
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — in primary MN, IgG4 autoantibodies against podocyte PLA2R antigen form subepithelial immune complexes that activate complement (C5b-9 membrane attack complex), causing sublytic podocyte injury; this sequence — autoantibody → immune complex → complement activation → target cell damage — is the classic type III hypersensitivity pattern*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — massive proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) depletes intravascular albumin → drops plasma oncotic pressure → Starling forces shift fluid into interstitium → edema, while the liver upregulates lipoprotein synthesis in a compensatory but counterproductive response that causes nephrotic hyperlipidemia*

### Primary MN
1. **Autoantibody formation:** The immune system produces IgG4-dominant antibodies against PLA2R (phospholipase A2 receptor), a transmembrane protein on podocyte foot processes. PLA2R autoantibodies are found in approximately 70-80% of primary MN cases.
2. **Immune complex formation in situ:** Circulating anti-PLA2R antibodies cross the GBM and bind to PLA2R antigen on the subepithelial surface of podocytes, forming immune complexes directly on the outer aspect of the GBM
3. **Complement activation:** Subepithelial immune deposits activate the complement cascade (primarily membrane attack complex C5b-9), which inserts into the podocyte membrane
4. **Podocyte injury:** C5b-9 causes sublytic podocyte damage — not killing the cell but disrupting its cytoskeleton, leading to foot process effacement and loss of the slit diaphragm filtration barrier
5. **GBM remodeling:** Podocytes lay down new basement membrane material around and over the immune deposits, creating the characteristic "spike and dome" pattern visible on silver stain
6. **Massive proteinuria:** Loss of podocyte filtration barrier integrity allows large-scale albumin and protein leakage into the urine, producing nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/day)

### Other Target Antigens
- **THSD7A** (thrombospondin type-1 domain-containing 7A): Found in ~3-5% of PLA2R-negative primary MN; associated with malignancy
- **NELL-1, Semaphorin 3B, PCDH7, NCAM1:** Recently discovered antigens in PLA2R/THSD7A-negative cases

### Secondary MN
- Immune complexes form differently (circulating immune complexes deposit in subepithelial space)
- Usually IgG1/IgG3-dominant (not IgG4)
- Full-house immunofluorescence pattern in lupus-associated MN

### Genetic Basis
- Strong genetic associations: HLA-DQA1 allele (antigen presentation) and PLA2R1 gene polymorphisms (target antigen expression)
- Combined risk alleles increase MN susceptibility dramatically
- Environmental triggers (infections, pollution) may initiate autoimmunity in genetically predisposed individuals
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Nephrotic Presentation (most common):**
- Heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) — foamy urine is often the first noticed symptom
- Peripheral edema (lower extremities, periorbital, scrotal/vulvar)
- Hypoalbuminemia (serum albumin often <2.5 g/dL)
- Hyperlipidemia (compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis)
- Lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese crosses on urinalysis)

**Sub-nephrotic Presentation (~20%):**
- Incidental proteinuria on routine urinalysis
- Mild edema or no edema
- Normal albumin levels

**General Features:**
- Typically no hypertension or hematuria at presentation (unlike proliferative glomerulonephritides)
- Microscopic hematuria in ~30% but gross hematuria is rare
- Fatigue, weight gain from fluid retention
- No systemic inflammatory symptoms (no fever, arthralgia, rash) unless secondary MN

### Complications
- **Thromboembolic events:** Significantly elevated risk — renal vein thrombosis (up to 30%), deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism. Risk correlates with degree of hypoalbuminemia (antithrombin III lost in urine)
- **Infection:** Loss of immunoglobulins in urine plus immunosuppressive therapy increases infection risk; spontaneous bacterial peritonitis possible with ascites
- **Acute Kidney Injury:** From renal vein thrombosis, intravascular volume depletion, or nephrotoxic drug exposure
- **Hyperlipidemia and accelerated atherosclerosis**
- **Progression to ESKD** in approximately one-third of untreated patients over 10-15 years
- **Malignancy:** In secondary MN and THSD7A-positive cases, the underlying malignancy itself is a major complication
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **24-hour urine protein or spot urine protein-to-creatinine ratio (UPCR):** Nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) in most cases
- **Serum albumin:** Low (<3.0 g/dL), often <2.5 g/dL
- **Serum anti-PLA2R antibodies:** Positive in ~70-80% of primary MN; titer correlates with disease activity and predicts treatment response. A rising titer predicts relapse; declining titer predicts remission.
- **Complement levels (C3, C4):** Normal in primary MN; low in lupus MN
- **Lipid panel:** Elevated total cholesterol, LDL, triglycerides
- **Renal function:** Serum creatinine and eGFR — often normal at presentation
- **Serologies to rule out secondary causes:** Hepatitis B surface antigen, hepatitis C antibody, ANA, anti-dsDNA, serum free light chains, SPEP/UPEP
- **Age-appropriate cancer screening:** Especially in patients >60 years and THSD7A-positive patients

### Kidney Biopsy (Gold Standard)
- **Light microscopy:** Diffuse GBM thickening; silver stain shows "spikes" (new BM projecting around deposits)
- **Immunofluorescence:** Granular IgG4 and C3 deposits along capillary walls (IgG4-dominant in primary; full-house in lupus MN)
- **Electron microscopy:** Subepithelial electron-dense deposits; staged by Ehrenreich-Churg classification (Stage I-IV)
- **PLA2R staining on biopsy:** Confirms primary MN even when serum antibodies are negative

### Diagnostic Criteria
1. Nephrotic-range proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) or significant sub-nephrotic proteinuria with other nephrotic features
2. Kidney biopsy showing characteristic subepithelial deposits and GBM thickening
3. Positive anti-PLA2R antibodies (can support diagnosis even before biopsy in appropriate clinical context)
4. Exclusion of secondary causes (lupus, hepatitis B/C, malignancy, drugs)
sections.management:
### Acute
- **Edema management:** Sodium restriction (<2 g/day) and Diuretics (loop diuretics; often need high doses due to tubular resistance and albumin binding). IV albumin + furosemide for severe refractory edema.
- **Thromboprophylaxis:** Anticoagulation with warfarin or direct oral Anticoagulants if serum albumin <2.5 g/dL (some guidelines <2.0 g/dL). Prophylaxis is critical — thromboembolic risk is among the highest of any glomerular disease.
- **Treat secondary causes:** Antiviral therapy for hepatitis B-associated MN, treat underlying malignancy, discontinue offending drug

### Observation Period (3-6 months)
- Given the rule of thirds (1/3 spontaneous remission), patients with preserved renal function and sub-nephrotic proteinuria or declining PLA2R titers may be observed with supportive care before initiating immunosuppression
- Maximum RAAS blockade during observation: ACE Inhibitors or ARBs titrated to maximum tolerated dose to reduce proteinuria

### Immunosuppressive Therapy (for persistent nephrotic-range proteinuria >6 months or declining GFR)
- **Rituximab:** First-line in most current guidelines. Anti-CD20 B-cell depletion. Well-tolerated but achieves remission in 60-70% (partial or complete). May take 12-18 months for full effect. Monitor PLA2R titers as immunological response marker.
- **Cyclophosphamide + corticosteroids (modified Ponticelli regimen):** Most effective regimen (complete remission ~60-70%) with alternating months of IV methylprednisolone/oral prednisone and oral cyclophosphamide for 6 months. However, significant toxicity: infection risk, bone marrow suppression, hemorrhagic cystitis, gonadal toxicity, and carcinogenic risk (bladder cancer). Reserve for severe or rituximab-refractory cases.
- **Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus):** High remission rates (~70-80%) but high relapse rate (~40-50%) upon discontinuation. Used when cyclophosphamide and rituximab are contraindicated.

### Chronic/Maintenance
- RAAS blockade long-term (ACE Inhibitors or ARBs) for proteinuria reduction and renoprotection
- Statins for hyperlipidemia management
- Monitor anti-PLA2R titers every 3-6 months (rising titer predicts relapse and may warrant preemptive treatment)
- Continue anticoagulation as long as albumin remains critically low
- Screen for malignancy periodically, especially in THSD7A-positive and older patients

### Pharmacology
- ACE Inhibitors/ARBs — Maximum-dose RAAS blockade for proteinuria reduction; cornerstone of supportive care
- Diuretics — Loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide) for edema management; may need combination therapy with metolazone
- Anticoagulants — Warfarin or DOACs for thromboprophylaxis when albumin <2.5 g/dL
- Statins — Hyperlipidemia management and cardiovascular risk reduction
- Rituximab — Anti-CD20 monoclonal antibody; first-line immunosuppression
- Cyclophosphamide — Alkylating agent; most effective but highest toxicity
- Calcineurin inhibitors (cyclosporine, tacrolimus) — Alternative immunosuppression; high relapse rate
- NSAIDs — Avoid; can worsen renal function and cause secondary MN
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Edema assessment and management** is a primary nursing focus. Measure abdominal girth daily for ascites. Weigh daily. Elevate lower extremities. Meticulous skin care for edematous areas to prevent skin breakdown — thin, stretched skin over massive edema tears easily and heals poorly.
- **Thromboembolism vigilance** is critical. Assess for unilateral leg swelling (DVT), sudden flank pain with hematuria (renal vein thrombosis), dyspnea and chest pain (PE). Serum albumin <2.5 g/dL is a red flag for thromboembolic risk. Ensure anticoagulation orders are in place and administered on time.
- **Immunosuppression education and monitoring:** Patients on rituximab, cyclophosphamide, or calcineurin inhibitors are significantly immunocompromised. Teach infection prevention (hand hygiene, avoiding crowds, recognizing early signs of infection). Monitor CBC for leukopenia. Ensure prophylactic medications are ordered (PJP prophylaxis with TMP-SMX if on cyclophosphamide or prolonged corticosteroids).
- **Urine protein monitoring:** Teach patients to report changes in urine foaminess as a practical self-monitoring tool. Collect timed urine specimens accurately — incomplete collections lead to inaccurate proteinuria quantification.
- **Dietary education:** Sodium restriction is essential for edema control. High-protein diets are NOT indicated despite urinary protein losses (excessive protein intake increases glomerular hyperfiltration and proteinuria). Target 0.8-1.0 g/kg/day protein.
- **Medication administration:** IV rituximab infusions require premedication (acetaminophen, diphenhydramine, methylprednisolone) and monitoring for infusion reactions. First infusion rate is slow; monitor vital signs every 30 minutes.
- **Psychosocial support:** MN is a chronic, unpredictable disease. The uncertainty of the "rule of thirds" prognosis is anxiety-provoking. Connect patients with nephrology support groups and counseling resources.
sections.keyPoints:
- MN is the most frequent cause of nephrotic syndrome in non-diabetic adults, with annual incidence of 10-12 per million
- Anti-PLA2R autoantibodies are found in ~70-80% of primary MN and serve as a diagnostic biomarker and disease activity marker
- Natural history follows the "rule of thirds": 1/3 spontaneous remission, 1/3 persistent proteinuria, 1/3 progression to kidney failure
- Thromboembolic risk is significantly elevated — anticoagulation indicated when serum albumin <2.5 g/dL
- Rituximab is now first-line immunosuppressive therapy; cyclophosphamide is most effective but carries carcinogenic risk
- An observation period of 3-6 months with supportive care is appropriate for patients with preserved renal function, as spontaneous remission is possible
- Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DQA1, PLA2R1 alleles) interacts with environmental triggers (infections, air pollution) to initiate disease
- Always screen for secondary causes, especially malignancy in patients >60 years

clinicalPearls: **Anti-PLA2R antibody titers are the immunological GPS of MN** — a declining titer after treatment predicts remission months before proteinuria improves, and a rising titer predicts relapse before clinical recurrence. Monitor titers to guide treatment decisions rather than waiting for proteinuria changes alone.

**The nephrotic patient with sudden flank pain and gross hematuria has renal vein thrombosis until proven otherwise** — MN carries the highest thromboembolic risk of any glomerular disease. Up to 30% develop renal vein thrombosis. A low albumin is your warning sign; anticoagulation should not be delayed.

**Not all MN needs immunosuppression** — the rule of thirds means one-third of patients will remit spontaneously. A period of 3-6 months of supportive care (RAAS blockade, anticoagulation, diuretics) is appropriate before committing to rituximab or cyclophosphamide, unless kidney function is deteriorating.

**New-onset nephrotic syndrome in a patient over 60 warrants a malignancy workup before labeling it primary MN** — secondary MN from occult malignancy (lung, colon, prostate, breast) is common in this age group. Age-appropriate cancer screening is not optional.

**Foamy urine is the patient's own bedside urinalysis** — teach patients that increased foaminess indicates worsening proteinuria and should prompt earlier follow-up. This simple self-monitoring tool empowers patients and may detect relapse before the next lab draw.

signs: Heavy proteinuria (>3.5 g/day) — foamy urine is often the first noticed symptom
Peripheral edema (lower extremities, periorbital, scrotal/vulvar)
Hypoalbuminemia (serum albumin often <2.5 g/dL)
Hyperlipidemia (compensatory hepatic lipoprotein synthesis)
Lipiduria (oval fat bodies, Maltese crosses on urinalysis)

Mild edema or no edema
Normal albumin levels

Microscopic hematuria in ~30% but gross hematuria is rare
Fatigue, weight gain from fluid retention
No systemic inflammatory symptoms (no fever, arthralgia, rash) unless secondary MN

**Infection:** Loss of immunoglobulins in urine plus immunosuppressive therapy increases infection risk; spontaneous bacterial peritonitis possible with ascites
**Acute Kidney Injury:** From renal vein thrombosis, intravascular volume depletion, or nephrotoxic drug exposure
