**NEVER compress an arterial ulcer** — compression wraps are for venous insufficiency; applying them to PAD ulcers worsens ischemia and can cause gangrene
**Remove shoes on every diabetic** — you will find things the patient doesn't know about; this takes 10 seconds and can prevent an amputation
**Supervised exercise is as effective as angioplasty for claudication** — consistently underutilized; "go walk more" is not the same as a structured program with proven protocols
**Acute limb ischemia = vascular STEMI** — time is tissue; heparinize immediately and call vascular surgery; a cold, pulseless, pale leg with sudden onset is as urgent as a STEMI
**The PAD + neuropathy combination is how people lose legs** — neuropathy creates wounds; PAD prevents healing; this is the #1 pathway to non-traumatic amputation in the US

signs: **Reproducible muscle pain with exertion, relieved by rest** — the hallmark
Calf claudication is most common (SFA disease)
Hip/buttock/thigh claudication = aortoiliac disease (Leriche syndrome if bilateral + erectile dysfunction)
**Key distinction from neurogenic claudication (spinal stenosis):**
Severe, burning pain in forefoot/toes at rest
**Worse at night** when supine (gravity no longer assists perfusion)
**Better when dangling legs off bed** (gravity augments arterial pressure)
Patients often sleep in a chair — classic history clue
Indicates perfusion pressure is critically inadequate even at rest
**Arterial ulcers:**
Location: **toes, pressure points (metatarsal heads, heel), between toes** — distal and at pressure points
Appearance: **pale/dusky base, punched-out borders, minimal granulation tissue**
**PAINFUL** (unless concurrent neuropathy masks it)
Surrounding skin: shiny, atrophic, hairless, cool
Compare to venous ulcers (medial malleolus, shallow, weepy, surrounded by stasis dermatitis)
**Gangrene:**
Dry gangrene = mummified, black, dry tissue (autoamputation may occur)
Wet gangrene = infected necrotic tissue — **surgical emergency** (sepsis risk)

pertussis.title: Pertussis

sections.definition:
Pertussis is a **highly contagious respiratory illness** caused by *Bordetella pertussis*, a gram-negative coccobacillus. It produces a toxin that paralyzes airway cilia and stimulates excessive mucus production, leading to the classic prolonged paroxysmal cough. Despite widespread vaccination, pertussis resurges in cycles every 3–5 years and remains endemic — immunity wanes over time after both vaccination and infection.

**Related notes:** Infection and Infectious Disease · Respiratory System

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Transmission:** Respiratory droplets; highly contagious (R₀ ~5.5); close contact (within 3–6 feet)
- **Incubation:** 7–10 days (range 4–21 days)
- **Contagious period:** From symptom onset through 3 weeks of cough (or 5 days after starting appropriate antibiotics)
- **High-risk populations:** Unvaccinated infants (<1 year — highest mortality), pregnant individuals (can transmit to newborn), adolescents/adults with waning immunity (often the source of infant infections)
- **Vaccine:** DTaP (children), Tdap (adolescents/adults). Immunity wanes — **Tdap booster recommended every 10 years and in every pregnancy (27–36 weeks)**

---
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Infection and Infectious Disease — pertussis toxin is a bacterial ADP-ribosyltransferase that inactivates Gi proteins in host cells, paralyzing ciliary function and triggering lymphocytosis; the organism’s R₀ of ~5.5 reflects its highly efficient droplet transmission during the pre-symptomatic catarrhal phase when patients appear to “just have a cold”*
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — adenylate cyclase toxin impairs neutrophil and macrophage oxidative burst, allowing B. pertussis to evade innate immune clearance; the resulting lymphocytosis (WBC >30k, >60% lymphocytes) is a direct immune response signature of pertussis toxin activity*

1. *B. pertussis* colonizes the respiratory epithelium via **filamentous hemagglutinin and pertactin** adhesins
2. **Pertussis toxin** inhibits G-protein signaling, paralyzes cilia, promotes lymphocytosis
3. **Tracheal cytotoxin** destroys ciliated respiratory cells → mucus accumulates → cough reflex overwhelmed
4. **Adenylate cyclase toxin** impairs neutrophil/macrophage function → prolonged infection
5. Result: Paroxysmal coughing spells as the only mechanism left to clear mucus

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### The 3 Stages

| Stage | Duration | Key Features |
|-------|---------|--------------|
| **Catarrhal** | 1–2 weeks | Mild URI symptoms (runny nose, low-grade fever, mild cough) — looks like a cold; MOST CONTAGIOUS phase |
| **Paroxysmal** | 1–6 weeks | Paroxysmal coughing spells (5–10+ rapid coughs in succession), inspiratory "whoop" (classic but absent in infants and adults), post-tussive vomiting, cyanosis during spells, apnea in infants |
| **Convalescent** | Weeks–months | Gradually decreasing cough; may persist for months ("the 100-day cough") |

### Presentation by Age

| Age Group | Typical Presentation | Key Danger |
|-----------|---------------------|------------|
| **Infants <6 months** | **Often NO whoop** — presents as apnea, gasping, cyanosis, or sudden color change | Highest mortality; respiratory failure; pertussis kills by apnea in infants |
| **Children 1–9** | Classic paroxysmal cough with whoop; post-tussive vomiting | Generally manageable; watch for pneumonia |
| **Adolescents/Adults** | Prolonged "hacking" cough, exhaustion; often NO classic whoop; frequently misdiagnosed as bronchitis | Source of transmission to infants; rarely life-threatening |

### Complications

- **Pneumonia** — most common cause of pertussis-related death (primary viral or secondary bacterial)
- **Apnea** — especially in infants <2 months; may be the presenting symptom
- **Pulmonary hypertension** — severe pertussis in young infants → fatal pulmonary hypertension
- **Encephalopathy** — rare; from hypoxia or toxin
- **Subconjunctival hemorrhage, rib fractures, urinary incontinence** — from forceful coughing
- **Post-tussive syncope**

---
sections.ed-isolation-mhs-protocol-pam-training:
> **Source:** MHS Pertussis PPE and Isolation Guide via Clinical Educator (Pam)

### Isolation Precautions

**Droplet precautions** (NOT airborne) until the patient has received ≥5 days of appropriate antibiotic therapy OR pertussis is ruled out.

| Element | Requirements |
|---------|-------------|
| **Transmission** | Respiratory droplets (large droplets; not airborne) |
| **Room selection** | Standard single patient room. For cohorting, contact your Infection Preventionist |
| **Staff PPE** | Surgical mask + eye protection + gown + gloves |
| **Room cleaning** | Standard cleaning. Pay special attention to hand-touch areas (knobs, rails). Staff performing cleaning: hand hygiene → gloves + gown |
| **Privacy curtain** | Remove and bag for stays ≥24 hours unless visibly soiled; send to Environmental Services |
| **Room reuse** | Use room as soon as it has been cleaned — no waiting period required (unlike airborne precautions) |

> **Key difference from measles:** Pertussis = Droplet (surgical mask is adequate). Measles = Airborne (N95 required). Know this distinction for your PPE donning.

### When to Initiate Isolation

**Place patient on Droplet precautions if ANY of the following:**
- Paroxysmal cough + epidemiologic risk (unvaccinated, known exposure, outbreak setting)
- Infant with apnea, gasping, or cyanosis especially during feeding
- Prolonged cough (>2 weeks) without identified cause, especially if associated with post-tussive emesis or whoop
- Known community pertussis outbreak (check local health department reports)

**Maintain precautions until:**
- Patient has received ≥5 days of appropriate antibiotics AND clinically improved, OR
- Pertussis has been definitively ruled out by NP PCR

---
sections.diagnosis:
| Test | Timing/Notes | Significance |
|------|-------------|-------------|
| **Nasopharyngeal PCR** | Best in first 3 weeks of illness; swab must reach posterior nasopharynx | Gold standard; most sensitive; can be positive even after antibiotics started |
| **NP culture** | Requires specific media (Bordet-Gengou or Regan-Lowe); takes 7–10 days | Gold standard historically; replaced by PCR in clinical practice |
| **Serology (anti-PT IgG)** | Useful in convalescent phase (>2 weeks into illness); not useful acutely | May support diagnosis in adults with prolonged cough |
| **CBC** | Classic finding: **marked lymphocytosis** (often WBC >30k with 60–90% lymphocytes) — especially in young infants | Highly suggestive; "white-out" on differential is a clue |
| **CXR** | "Shaggy heart sign" (perihilar infiltrates) in pneumonia; may be normal | Rule out pneumonia, aspiration |

---
sections.management:
### Antibiotics (Treatment and Post-Exposure Prophylaxis)

**Antibiotics shorten duration and contagiousness if started in catarrhal or early paroxysmal phase. In late paroxysmal/convalescent phase, treatment is primarily to reduce transmission (patient still coughs).**

| Antibiotic | Dose | Notes |
|-----------|------|-------|
| **Azithromycin (Z-pack)** | Adults: 500 mg day 1, then 250 mg days 2–5. Infants <6 months: 10 mg/kg/day × 5 days | **First-line for all ages.** Preferred in infants (avoid erythromycin in <1 month — pyloric stenosis risk) |
| **Clarithromycin** | Adults: 500 mg BID × 7 days; children: 15 mg/kg/day divided BID × 7 days | Alternative to azithromycin |
| **TMP-SMX** | Adults: 1 DS tablet BID × 14 days; children: 8 mg/kg/day TMP divided BID × 14 days | For patients allergic to macrolides; NOT for infants <2 months |

**Post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP):** Same antibiotic regimen; offered to close contacts (household, HCW with face-to-face exposure without mask) within 21 days of exposure onset.

### Supportive Care

- **Airway management:** Bulb suction for infants with thick secretions; positioning (upright); suction at bedside
- **Supplemental O2:** Target SpO2 ≥92%; may need HFNC in severe infant cases
- **Antipyretics:** Acetaminophen or ibuprofen for fever
- **IV hydration:** If unable to feed adequately (post-tussive vomiting)
- **Avoid cough suppressants** — not effective and potentially harmful (mask worsening)
- **Albuterol:** Not evidence-based for pertussis-specific cough; consider only if known reactive airway component

### ICU Criteria (Infants)

- Apnea requiring stimulation or rescue breathing
- Bradycardia with coughing spells
- SpO2 <90% despite O2
- Pulmonary hypertension (echocardiogram)
- Severe lymphocytosis (WBC >30k in infant = high risk for fatal pertussis)

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Mask the patient immediately** — pertussis is most contagious before the whoop appears; the patient who "just has a bad cough" may be in peak contagious catarrhal phase
- **Apnea monitoring in infants** — any infant admitted for pertussis or rule-out pertussis needs continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring; apnea may occur suddenly during or after a coughing spell
- **NP swab technique matters** — the swab must reach the **posterior nasopharynx** (not just the anterior nares). Insert perpendicular to the face along the floor of the nasal cavity until resistance felt; hold 5–10 seconds; withdraw. Inadequate depth = false negative
- **Post-tussive vomiting management** — observe during coughing spells; have emesis basin ready; assess hydration; position on side if there's any aspiration concern
- **Count coughing paroxysms** — document number of coughing spells per shift, duration, associated color change or apnea, recovery time. This is the key clinical progress parameter
- **Contact HCW exposed without PPE** — report to employee health; they need PEP if unvaccinated or Tdap not current

---
sections.public-health-reporting:
Pertussis is a **notifiable disease** in Washington State. Report all confirmed and probable cases to the local health jurisdiction within **24 hours** of diagnosis. The health department will coordinate contact tracing and ensure household contacts receive PEP.

---

clinicalPearls: **The "whoop" is absent in infants and adults** — and those are the two groups most likely to present to your ED. Infants cough until they run out of air and then gasp silently or stop breathing; adults just cough endlessly for weeks. If you only look for the classic whoop, you'll miss most cases.
**A marked lymphocytosis is pertussis until proven otherwise** — WBC of 35k with 80% lymphocytes in a coughing infant is highly suggestive. Pull up the CBC differential, not just the total WBC.

**Treatment in the late paroxysmal phase is still worth it** — even if antibiotics won't change the cough at this point, they will dramatically reduce contagiousness. This matters for protecting household contacts, especially unvaccinated siblings.

**Your Tdap status is your professional responsibility** — nurses who work in pediatric or ED settings and aren't current on Tdap are a vector for infant transmission. If you're not sure of your last booster, check with employee health.

signs: **Pneumonia** — most common cause of pertussis-related death (primary viral or secondary bacterial)
**Apnea** — especially in infants <2 months; may be the presenting symptom
**Pulmonary hypertension** — severe pertussis in young infants → fatal pulmonary hypertension
**Encephalopathy** — rare; from hypoxia or toxin
**Subconjunctival hemorrhage, rib fractures, urinary incontinence** — from forceful coughing
**Post-tussive syncope**

pneumonia.title: Pneumonia

sections.definition: Pneumonia is an acute infection of the lung parenchyma causing inflammation and consolidation of the alveoli. It is classified by setting of acquisition (community vs. hospital), causative organism, and anatomic pattern (lobar vs. bronchopneumonia vs. interstitial). Pneumonia remains the leading infectious cause of death worldwide and the most common cause of sepsis.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence:** ~1.5 million ED visits/year in the US; ~50,000 deaths/year; **leading infectious cause of death worldwide** — >2.49 million LRTI deaths globally (2019), exceeding tuberculosis (1.18M) and HIV (864K); #1 cause of sepsis; 30-day mortality: outpatient CAP ~1%; hospitalized CAP ~5-15%; ICU-admitted CAP ~20-30%; HAP/VAP ~15-30%; mortality in adults >70 INCREASED 33.6% between 2007-2017 (aging frail population + comorbidities); mortality in children <5 DECREASED 36.4% (vaccines, antibiotics, improved sanitation)
- **Risk factors:**
  - **General:** Age > 65, chronic lung disease (COPD, asthma), smoking, immunosuppression (HIV, transplant, chemotherapy, steroids), diabetes, heart failure, chronic liver/renal disease, malnutrition
  - **Aspiration:** Altered consciousness (stroke, seizure, anesthesia, alcohol intoxication), dysphagia, GERD, poor dentition, NG/OG tube, mechanical ventilation
  - **HAP/VAP:** Mechanical ventilation (greatest risk factor), ICU stay > 5 days, prior antibiotics, supine positioning, sedation
- **Demographics:** Bimodal age distribution (very young and elderly); men slightly > women; higher incidence and mortality in Black and Indigenous populations; seasonal peaks (winter for viral and streptococcal)

### Classification
| Type | Definition |
|------|-----------|
| **CAP** | Acquired outside hospital (or < 48h after admission) |
| **HAP** | Develops ≥ 48 hours after hospital admission, not intubated |
| **VAP** | Develops ≥ 48 hours after endotracheal intubation |
| **Aspiration pneumonia** | Infection from aspiration of oropharyngeal or gastric contents; often polymicrobial including anaerobes |

### Common Organisms
| Setting | Likely Pathogens |
|---------|-----------------|
| **CAP — Outpatient** | Streptococcus pneumoniae (most common bacterial), Mycoplasma pneumoniae, Chlamydophila pneumoniae, respiratory viruses (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, RSV) |
| **CAP — Inpatient** | S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, Legionella, Staph aureus, gram-negatives; respiratory viruses |
| **CAP — ICU** | S. pneumoniae, Legionella, S. aureus (including MRSA), Pseudomonas (if risk factors), gram-negatives |
| **HAP/VAP** | MRSA, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, Klebsiella, Acinetobacter, other MDR gram-negatives |
| **Aspiration** | Oral anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus, Fusobacterium), gram-negatives, S. aureus |
| **Immunocompromised** | All of above + PCP (Pneumocystis jirovecii), fungi (Aspergillus, Cryptococcus), CMV, Nocardia, TB |

**Aspiration pneumonia mortality:** RR 3.62 for in-hospital mortality and RR 3.57 for 30-day mortality vs non-aspiration CAP.
**COPD + pneumonia:** Annual CAP incidence in COPD patients is 93.6/1,000 vs 5.09/1,000 in non-COPD (~18x higher risk).
sections.pathophysiology:
1. **Pathogen entry:** Micro-aspiration of oropharyngeal secretions (most common), inhalation of aerosolized droplets, hematogenous spread (rare), direct extension; colonization of the nasopharynx precedes most bacterial pneumonias — bacteria translocate from nasopharynx to lower respiratory tract
2. **Lung microbiome:** Healthy lungs are NOT sterile — they harbor ~100 taxa (Prevotella, Streptococcus, Veillonella, Fusobacterium, Haemophilus). Pneumonia involves a shift from homeostatic microbiome to dysbiosis (low diversity, high microbial burden)
3. **Overwhelmed local defenses:** Normal defenses (cough reflex, mucociliary clearance, alveolar macrophages, IgA) fail due to large inoculum, virulent organism, or impaired host immunity; alveolar macrophage death via necroptosis (vs apoptosis) impairs antibacterial defense
3. **Alveolar infection and inflammation:**
   - Bacteria multiply in alveoli → inflammatory cytokine release (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6) → neutrophil recruitment
   - Alveoli fill with neutrophils, bacteria, fluid, fibrin, and cellular debris (= consolidation)
> *First principles: Oxygenation and Ventilation — consolidated alveoli are perfused but unventilated (V/Q=0, or shunt); blood traverses fluid-filled alveoli without picking up O2, producing hypoxemia that only partially responds to supplemental O2 (because shunted blood is never exposed to higher FiO2).*

4. **Impaired gas exchange:** Consolidated alveoli cannot participate in gas exchange → V/Q mismatch → hypoxemia
5. **Systemic response:** Inflammatory mediators enter bloodstream → fever, leukocytosis → sepsis if severe (pneumonia is the #1 cause of sepsis)

### Anatomic Patterns
| Pattern | Description | Typical Organisms |
|---------|------------|-------------------|
| **Lobar** | Entire lobe consolidated; sharp borders | S. pneumoniae (classic), Klebsiella |
| **Bronchopneumonia** | Patchy, multifocal consolidation around bronchioles | S. aureus, gram-negatives, H. influenzae |
| **Interstitial** | Diffuse interstitial infiltrates (ground-glass) | Viruses, Mycoplasma, PCP |
| **Cavitary** | Necrosis forming cavities | S. aureus, Klebsiella, TB, anaerobes, fungi |
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms

**Typical pneumonia (bacterial):**
- Acute onset productive cough (purulent/rusty sputum)
- High fever with rigors/chills
- Pleuritic chest pain
- Dyspnea, tachypnea
- Signs of consolidation: Bronchial breath sounds, egophony (E→A change), increased tactile fremitus, dullness to percussion
- Crackles/rales over affected area

**Atypical pneumonia (Mycoplasma, Chlamydophila, Legionella, viruses):**
- Gradual onset
- Dry/nonproductive cough
- Lower fever, headache, malaise, myalgias
- Exam may be relatively benign despite significant imaging findings ("walking pneumonia")
- Legionella: GI symptoms (diarrhea), confusion, hyponatremia

**Special populations:**
- **Elderly:** May present with confusion, falls, or functional decline WITHOUT classic fever/cough; hypothermia possible
- **Immunocompromised:** Atypical presentations; opportunistic organisms; may lack typical inflammatory response

### Severity Assessment — CURB-65 / PSI
**CURB-65 (simple bedside tool):**
| Criteria | Points |
|----------|--------|
| Confusion (new) | 1 |
| Urea (BUN) > 20 mg/dL | 1 |
| Respiratory rate ≥ 30 | 1 |
| Blood pressure (SBP < 90 or DBP ≤ 60) | 1 |
| Age ≥ 65 | 1 |

- 0-1: Outpatient treatment
- 2: Consider short admission or close outpatient follow-up
- 3-5: Inpatient (≥4 consider ICU)

### Complications
- Parapneumonic effusion / empyema (infected pleural fluid → requires drainage)
- Lung abscess (especially anaerobes, Staph aureus, Klebsiella)
- Sepsis / septic shock (pneumonia = #1 cause)
- Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
- Respiratory failure (requiring intubation)
- Bacteremia (~25% of pneumococcal CAP)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **Chest X-ray** | Confirms diagnosis; identifies consolidation pattern; detects effusions, cavitation; lobar vs. multilobar (severity indicator) |
| **CT chest** | More sensitive; consider when CXR equivocal, suspect complication (abscess, empyema), or immunocompromised |
| **Blood cultures (×2 sets)** | Before antibiotics in hospitalized patients; positive in ~5-14% of CAP; identifies organism and sensitivities |
| **Sputum culture and Gram stain** | Before antibiotics if obtainable; most useful in hospitalized patients; Gram stain guides initial therapy |
| **CBC** | Leukocytosis (bacterial); lymphopenia (viral); leukopenia (poor prognosis/overwhelming infection) |
| **BMP** | BUN (CURB-65 component); electrolytes; renal function for antibiotic dosing |
| **Procalcitonin** | Elevated in bacterial infection; helps distinguish bacterial vs. viral; can guide antibiotic duration (stop when < 0.25) |
| **Lactate** | If sepsis suspected; elevated = tissue hypoperfusion |
| **Legionella urinary antigen** | Detects L. pneumophila serogroup 1 (most common); positive in ~70-80% of Legionella cases |
| **Pneumococcal urinary antigen** | Detects S. pneumoniae; sensitivity ~70%; remains positive on antibiotics |
| **ABG** | Severe pneumonia: Assess oxygenation and acid-base status |
| **Respiratory viral panel** | Influenza, COVID-19, RSV — guides antiviral therapy and isolation |
| **Thoracentesis** | If significant pleural effusion → analyze for empyema (pH < 7.2, glucose < 40, positive culture/Gram stain → drain) |
sections.management:
### CAP — Outpatient
| Patient | Regimen |
|---------|---------|
| Previously healthy, no comorbidities | Amoxicillin 1g TID × 5 days (preferred); OR doxycycline 100 mg BID; OR azithromycin 500 mg then 250 mg daily (only if local resistance < 25%) |
| Comorbidities (COPD, DM, heart/liver/renal disease, alcoholism, immunosuppression) | Amoxicillin-clavulanate 875 mg BID or cephalosporin (cefpodoxime) PLUS macrolide or doxycycline; OR respiratory fluoroquinolone alone (levofloxacin 750 mg daily or moxifloxacin) |

### CAP — Inpatient (Non-ICU)
| Regimen | Details |
|---------|---------|
| **Beta-lactam + macrolide** | Ceftriaxone 1-2g IV daily + azithromycin 500 mg IV/PO daily (PREFERRED) |
| **Respiratory fluoroquinolone** | Levofloxacin 750 mg IV/PO daily (monotherapy alternative) |
| Duration | Minimum 5 days; afebrile ≥ 48h; clinically improving before stopping |

### CAP — ICU (Severe)
| Regimen | Details |
|---------|---------|
| Standard | Beta-lactam (ceftriaxone or ampicillin-sulbactam) + macrolide (azithromycin) OR beta-lactam + fluoroquinolone |
| If MRSA risk factors | Add vancomycin or linezolid |
| If Pseudomonas risk factors | Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, meropenem) + fluoroquinolone |

### HAP/VAP
| Regimen | Details |
|---------|---------|
| Empiric (broad) | Anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (pip-tazo, cefepime, meropenem) + MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid) ± aminoglycoside or fluoroquinolone for double gram-negative coverage if MDR risk |
| Narrow based on cultures | De-escalate as soon as culture data available (antibiotic stewardship) |
| Duration | 7 days for most HAP/VAP (shorter courses = less resistance) |

### Aspiration Pneumonia
- **Witnessed aspiration without infection signs:** Observation, supportive care; antibiotics NOT automatically indicated
- **Aspiration with pneumonia (fever, infiltrate, leukocytosis):** Amoxicillin-clavulanate or ampicillin-sulbactam (cover anaerobes); clindamycin alternative; piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem for severe

### MDR Risk Assessment — DRIP Score
**Drug Resistance in Pneumonia (DRIP) score** helps identify patients at risk for MDR organisms:
- **Major risk factors (2 points each):** Prior antibiotic use, residence in long-term care facility, tube feeding, prior MDR pathogen infection (within 1 year)
- **Minor risk factors (1 point each):** Hospitalization within 60 days, chronic pulmonary disease, poor functional status, gastric acid suppression, wound care, MRSA colonization (within 1 year)

### Adjunctive Therapies
- Supplemental O2 (target SpO2 ≥ 94%; lower in COPD)
- IV fluids for sepsis/dehydration
- Corticosteroids: Consider in severe CAP/septic shock (dexamethasone — CAPE COD trial showed reduced mortality)
- Chest PT and early mobilization
- Influenza → oseltamivir within 48 hours; COVID-19 → antivirals per current guidelines

### Pharmacology
- **Antibiotics Overview** — empiric regimen based on CAP vs HAP/VAP, severity, and local resistance patterns; de-escalate based on cultures
- **Antivirals** — oseltamivir for influenza; remdesivir/nirmatrelvir for COVID-19
- **Systemic Corticosteroids** — dexamethasone in severe CAP/septic shock (CAPE COD trial); standard in COVID-19 with hypoxia
- **Bronchodilators** — for bronchospasm or COPD exacerbation complicating pneumonia
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Assessment:**
  - Respiratory: RR, SpO2, work of breathing, lung sounds (consolidation vs. clearing), cough character (productive? blood-tinged?)
  - Vital signs: Fever curve trending (improving on antibiotics?); hemodynamics (sepsis screening)
  - Sputum: Color, consistency, volume; collect specimen BEFORE first antibiotic dose
  - Mental status: Confusion is a CURB-65 criterion and sign of severity; elderly may present primarily with AMS
  - Fluid status: I&O; signs of dehydration; sepsis-related fluid needs
- **Antibiotic stewardship (nursing role):**
  - First dose within 1 hour of order (time-to-antibiotic matters)
  - Obtain cultures BEFORE antibiotics (blood cultures, sputum if possible)
  - Monitor for switch from IV to PO (when tolerating oral, improving, afebrile)
  - Track antibiotic duration — shorter courses (5-7 days) are guideline-recommended; advocate for de-escalation
- **Prevention (VAP bundle — CRITICAL):**
  - HOB ≥ 30-45 degrees (reduces aspiration risk)
  - Oral care with chlorhexidine q4-6h (some institutions moving away from CHG — check local protocol)
  - Daily sedation interruption + daily assessment for extubation readiness
  - DVT prophylaxis
  - Stress ulcer prophylaxis (PPI or H2 blocker if high risk)
  - Subglottic suctioning (if available on ETT design)
  - Early mobilization
- **Aspiration prevention:**
  - Swallow evaluation before oral intake in stroke, neurologic impairment, prolonged intubation
  - Upright positioning during and 30-60 min after meals
  - Thickened liquids if dysphagia identified
  - Oral hygiene (poor dentition = higher aspiration pneumonia risk)
- **Patient teaching (discharge):**
  - Complete full antibiotic course
  - Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination if not up to date
  - Follow-up CXR in 6-8 weeks (especially > 50 years — to confirm resolution and rule out underlying malignancy)
  - Return precautions: Worsening dyspnea, high fever, hemoptysis, confusion

clinicalPearls: **Pneumonia is the #1 cause of sepsis.** Every pneumonia patient should be screened for sepsis (qSOFA, SIRS, NEWS). Time-to-antibiotic within 1 hour of sepsis recognition is a quality metric that saves lives. Don't wait for imaging confirmation to start antibiotics in a septic-appearing patient.

**Elderly pneumonia is sneaky.** An 82-year-old with new confusion and no fever, cough, or leukocytosis may have pneumonia. Atypical presentations are the RULE, not the exception, in older adults. Low threshold for chest X-ray in elderly with any acute change.

**CAP antibiotics should be given for 5 days — not 7, not 10, not 14.** ATS/IDSA guidelines recommend minimum 5 days of treatment provided the patient is afebrile ≥48 hours and clinically improving. Longer courses increase resistance, C. difficile risk, and side effects with no benefit.

**The VAP bundle works — but ONLY when every element is done consistently.** Each individual intervention provides modest benefit; the BUNDLE provides substantial benefit. The most commonly missed element? HOB elevation and oral care. These are nursing-driven interventions that reduce VAP rates by 40-70%.

**Not every aspiration needs antibiotics.** Witnessed aspiration in a healthy patient often causes chemical pneumonitis (inflammatory response to gastric acid), NOT bacterial infection. Observe and treat with antibiotics only if infectious signs develop (fever, leukocytosis, purulent sputum, new infiltrate). Prophylactic antibiotics for aspiration events are NOT recommended.

**Procalcitonin helps guide antibiotic duration, not diagnosis.** A falling procalcitonin (< 0.25 or 80% decrease from peak) supports safe discontinuation of antibiotics. This is a tool for antibiotic stewardship, which is a shared nursing-pharmacy-physician responsibility.

**Follow-up chest X-ray matters.** A new lung infiltrate in a smoker > 50 that persists at 6-8 weeks despite antibiotics may be lung cancer with post-obstructive pneumonia. Always arrange follow-up imaging to document resolution.

**The lungs are NOT sterile.** Healthy lungs harbor ~100 bacterial taxa. Pneumonia represents a shift from homeostatic microbiome to dysbiosis — it's an ecological disruption, not just a simple "infection." This concept is reshaping how we think about antibiotic stewardship and host-pathogen interactions.

**Oropharyngeal colonization drives most pneumonia.** In long-term care facility residents, 38% are colonized with antibiotic-resistant pathogens (Acinetobacter, Enterobacterales, Pseudomonas). Oral care isn't just about comfort — it's pneumonia prevention.

**Regional mortality disparities are staggering.** In Central African Republic, child pneumonia mortality is 460/100,000; compare this to single digits in high-income countries. Access to vaccines (pneumococcal, Hib), antibiotics, and basic sanitation drives the difference.

signs: Acute onset productive cough (purulent/rusty sputum)
High fever with rigors/chills
Pleuritic chest pain
Dyspnea, tachypnea
Signs of consolidation: Bronchial breath sounds, egophony (E→A change), increased tactile fremitus, dullness to percussion
Crackles/rales over affected area
Gradual onset
Dry/nonproductive cough
Lower fever, headache, malaise, myalgias
Exam may be relatively benign despite significant imaging findings ("walking pneumonia")
Legionella: GI symptoms (diarrhea), confusion, hyponatremia
**Elderly:** May present with confusion, falls, or functional decline WITHOUT classic fever/cough; hypothermia possible
**Immunocompromised:** Atypical presentations; opportunistic organisms; may lack typical inflammatory response
0-1: Outpatient treatment
2: Consider short admission or close outpatient follow-up
3-5: Inpatient (≥4 consider ICU)
Parapneumonic effusion / empyema (infected pleural fluid → requires drainage)
Lung abscess (especially anaerobes, Staph aureus, Klebsiella)
Sepsis / septic shock (pneumonia = #1 cause)
Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome (ARDS)
Respiratory failure (requiring intubation)
Bacteremia (~25% of pneumococcal CAP)

pneumothorax.title: Pneumothorax

sections.definition:
Pneumothorax is the presence of **air in the pleural space** — the potential space between the visceral pleura (covering the lung) and the parietal pleura (lining the chest wall). This disrupts the normal negative intrapleural pressure that maintains lung expansion, causing partial or complete lung collapse. Severity ranges from a small, clinically insignificant air collection to **tension pneumothorax**, a life-threatening emergency causing obstructive shock and cardiac arrest.

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Primary spontaneous pneumothorax (PSP):** 7-18 per 100,000 males; 1-6 per 100,000 females annually
- **Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP):** 6 per 100,000 males; 2 per 100,000 females annually
- **Traumatic pneumothorax:** Present in up to 20% of significant blunt chest trauma and 60-80% of penetrating chest injuries
- **Iatrogenic pneumothorax:** Complicates 1-6% of central line placements (subclavian > IJ), 5-15% of transthoracic lung biopsies
- **Recurrence:** PSP has 30% recurrence after first episode; 50%+ after second episode
- **Mortality:** Low for PSP (<1%); SSP mortality 3-10% (underlying disease); tension pneumothorax rapidly fatal if untreated

---
sections.classification-and-etiology:
### Types of Pneumothorax

| Type | Mechanism | Key Features |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| **Primary Spontaneous (PSP)** | Rupture of apical subpleural blebs in otherwise healthy lung | Tall, thin, young males (15-35 years); smokers at higher risk; NO underlying lung disease by definition |
| **Secondary Spontaneous (SSP)** | Rupture from underlying pulmonary disease | COPD (most common cause), cystic fibrosis, bullous emphysema, Pneumocystis pneumonia, lung cancer, Marfan syndrome, catamenial (menstruation-related, endometriosis) |
| **Traumatic** | Penetrating injury, blunt chest trauma, rib fractures | Stab wounds, GSW, MVC, falls; rib fractures lacerate visceral pleura; often accompanied by hemothorax (hemopneumothorax) |
| **Iatrogenic** | Complication of medical procedures | Central venous catheter insertion (especially subclavian), thoracentesis, transthoracic needle biopsy, mechanical ventilation (barotrauma), post-CPR |
| **Tension** | One-way valve mechanism allowing air IN but not OUT | **Any type can progress to tension**; most common with traumatic and ventilator-associated; life-threatening emergency |

### Tension Pneumothorax — Detailed Pathophysiology

This is the **most critical subtype** and must be recognized and treated immediately:

1. **One-way valve mechanism:** Air enters the pleural space during inspiration but cannot escape during expiration
2. **Progressive air accumulation:** With each breath, more air is trapped; intrapleural pressure rises relentlessly
3. **Ipsilateral lung collapse:** Affected lung completely collapses
4. **Mediastinal shift:** Rising pressure pushes the mediastinum (heart, great vessels, trachea) toward the contralateral side
5. **Decreased venous return:** Mediastinal shift kinks the IVC/SVC and compresses the heart, dramatically reducing preload
6. **Obstructive shock:** Cardiac output plummets from impaired venous return and cardiac compression
7. **Contralateral lung compression:** Shifting mediastinum compresses the opposite lung, worsening hypoxemia
8. **PEA/Cardiac arrest:** Without intervention, tension pneumothorax progresses to pulseless electrical activity and death within minutes

> **TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX IS A CLINICAL DIAGNOSIS. DO NOT DELAY TREATMENT TO OBTAIN IMAGING.**

---
sections.pathophysiology-general:
> *First principles: Oxygenation and Ventilation — pneumothorax collapses the affected lung → that lung is perfused but not ventilated → intrapulmonary shunt (V/Q = 0) → hypoxemia proportional to collapse size. Hemodynamics — tension PTX: progressive air accumulation → mediastinal shift → kinking of SVC/IVC → impaired venous return → reduced preload → obstructive shock; BP = CO × SVR collapses as CO falls.*

### Normal Pleural Physiology
- The pleural space normally contains a thin layer of serous fluid (~5 mL)
- Intrapleural pressure is normally **negative** (approximately -5 cmH2O at rest)
- This negative pressure keeps the lung expanded against the chest wall
- The lung's natural elastic recoil would cause collapse without this negative pressure

### When Air Enters the Pleural Space
1. Negative intrapleural pressure is lost
2. The lung's elastic recoil causes it to collapse toward the hilum
3. Degree of collapse depends on volume of air and lung compliance
4. Ventilation-perfusion mismatch develops: blood continues to flow to non-ventilated collapsed lung → right-to-left shunt → hypoxemia
5. Reduced tidal volume from lung collapse → hypoventilation → hypercarbia (in large pneumothorax)

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Presentation by Type

**Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax**
- Sudden onset of unilateral pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worsens with inspiration)
- Dyspnea (often mild in young, healthy patients)
- Often occurs at rest; may follow Valsalva maneuver
- Patients are typically tall, thin males aged 15-35

**Secondary Spontaneous Pneumothorax**
- Similar symptoms but often MORE severe (less respiratory reserve)
- Even a small pneumothorax can cause significant distress in patients with underlying lung disease
- Hypoxemia more pronounced due to baseline V/Q mismatch

**Traumatic Pneumothorax**
- Chest pain and dyspnea following trauma
- Look for chest wall injuries: rib fractures, subcutaneous emphysema, penetrating wounds
- May be accompanied by hemothorax (dullness to percussion at base + absent breath sounds)

**Tension Pneumothorax**
- **Severe respiratory distress** — rapidly progressive
- **Hypotension / obstructive shock**
- **Tracheal deviation** away from affected side (late finding, unreliable)
- **Distended neck veins** (JVD) — from impaired venous return (may be absent if hypovolemic)
- **Absent breath sounds** on affected side
- **Hyperresonance** to percussion on affected side
- **Tachycardia** — severe, progressive
- **Cyanosis** — late, ominous
- **Agitation/altered mental status** — from hypoxia and hypoperfusion

### Physical Examination Findings (All Types)

| Finding | Significance |
|---------|-------------|
| **Decreased/absent breath sounds** | Ipsilateral to pneumothorax; most reliable sign |
| **Hyperresonance to percussion** | Air-filled pleural space transmits sound differently than normal lung |
| **Decreased chest wall movement** | Affected side moves less with respiration |
| **Subcutaneous emphysema** | Crepitus on palpation — air tracking into soft tissues; common with traumatic PTX |
| **Tachypnea** | Compensatory increase in respiratory rate |
| **Tachycardia** | Hypoxia, pain, or hemodynamic compromise |
| **Oxygen desaturation** | Degree correlates with pneumothorax size and underlying lung function |

---
sections.diagnosis:
### Chest X-Ray
- **Standard upright PA CXR** — first-line imaging for stable patients
- **Findings:**
  - **Visceral pleural line:** White line visible at the edge of the collapsed lung, with absence of lung markings peripheral to it
  - **Absence of lung markings** beyond the pleural edge
  - **Deep sulcus sign:** Abnormally deep, lucent costophrenic angle — **KEY finding on supine CXR** (trauma patients who cannot sit upright)
  - Mediastinal shift toward contralateral side (large pneumothorax or tension)
  - Subcutaneous emphysema
- **Expiratory films** were traditionally obtained but have NOT been shown to improve detection; routine use is no longer recommended

### Size Estimation (CXR)
| Classification | Measurement | Management Implication |
|---------------|-------------|----------------------|
| **Small** | <2 cm from apex to cupola (BTS) or <20% lung volume | May be observed |
| **Large** | >2 cm or >20% lung volume | Typically requires intervention |

> **Note:** CXR can underestimate pneumothorax size. CT is more accurate but not needed for straightforward cases.

### CT Chest
- Most sensitive imaging modality — detects small pneumothoraces missed on CXR ("occult pneumothorax")
- Identifies blebs and bullae (relevant for recurrence risk and surgical planning)
- Essential in trauma (already being obtained as part of trauma CT)
- Differentiates pneumothorax from large bullae (critical distinction — draining a bulla is catastrophic)

### Point-of-Care Ultrasound (POCUS)
- **Increasingly used in ED** — more sensitive than CXR, faster than CT
- **Normal lung:** Lung sliding (shimmering/gliding of visceral against parietal pleura) visible at the pleural interface; comet tail artifacts (B-lines)
- **Pneumothorax:** **Absent lung sliding**, absent B-lines, **lung point** (transition point where sliding starts — pathognomonic, specificity ~100%)
- **Barcode sign** (M-mode) replaces normal "seashore sign"
- Sensitivity 86-98%; specificity 97-100%
- Limitations: operator-dependent, difficult in obese or subcutaneous emphysema

### Arterial Blood Gas
- Hypoxemia (PaO2 decreased)
- May show respiratory alkalosis (hyperventilation from dyspnea) or respiratory acidosis (large PTX with hypoventilation)
- Not needed for diagnosis but helpful for assessing severity in SSP

---
sections.management:
### Management by Type and Severity

#### Small Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax (<2 cm, Minimal Symptoms)
- **Observation:** 3-6 hour observation period in ED with repeat CXR
- **High-flow supplemental O2:** Accelerates pleural air reabsorption (nitrogen washout effect); increases reabsorption rate from 1.25%/day to approximately 4-5%/day
- **Discharge criteria:** Stable or improving on repeat CXR, reliable patient with follow-up
- **Follow-up CXR** in 24-48 hours
- **Activity restrictions:** No flying or diving until confirmed resolution

#### Large or Symptomatic Primary Spontaneous Pneumothorax
- **Needle aspiration** (first-line for PSP per BTS guidelines):
  - 16-18g IV cannula, 2nd intercostal space (ICS) midclavicular line (MCL)
  - Aspirate with 50 mL syringe via three-way stopcock
  - Success if <2 cm residual and symptoms improve; if fails, proceed to chest tube
- **Small-bore chest tube (pigtail catheter, 12-14 Fr):**
  - Less painful, equally effective as large-bore for PSP
  - Inserted via Seldinger technique
  - Connected to Heimlich valve (one-way flutter valve allowing air out but not in) or water seal
- **Large-bore chest tube (24-28 Fr):** Reserved for hemopneumothorax or failure of small-bore

#### Secondary Spontaneous Pneumothorax
- **Almost always requires chest tube** (these patients have less pulmonary reserve)
- Small SSP (< 1-2 cm, minimal symptoms) may be aspirated, but admission and close monitoring are mandatory
- Large SSP: chest tube (14-24 Fr) with water seal or suction
- **All SSP patients should be admitted** — even small pneumothoraces are poorly tolerated
- Cardiothoracic/pulmonary consultation for recurrence prevention

#### Traumatic Pneumothorax
- **Chest tube (28-32 Fr):** Standard management for traumatic pneumothorax
  - Larger bore to accommodate potential blood (hemopneumothorax)
  - Inserted at 4th-5th ICS, anterior axillary line (AAL), "safe triangle"
- **Occult pneumothorax** (seen only on CT, not CXR): May be observed if patient is NOT on mechanical ventilation; if ventilated, chest tube is recommended (risk of tension)
- **Open pneumothorax (sucking chest wound):** Cover with three-sided occlusive dressing (allows air to escape but prevents entry), then chest tube placement remote from wound

#### Iatrogenic Pneumothorax
- Management mirrors spontaneous: observe if small and stable; aspirate or tube if symptomatic or large
- Prevention: ultrasound-guided central line placement significantly reduces risk
- Post-procedure CXR is standard after subclavian/IJ central line placement and thoracentesis

#### TENSION PNEUMOTHORAX — Emergency Management

**This is a clinical diagnosis. Treat immediately upon recognition.**

**Step 1: Needle Decompression**
- **Site options:**
  - **2nd ICS, MCL (midclavicular line):** Traditional site; use long (8 cm) needle — standard 5 cm angiocath may be insufficient due to chest wall thickness
  - **4th-5th ICS, AAL (anterior axillary line):** Preferred by many trauma guidelines — thinner chest wall, higher success rate
- **Equipment:** 14-gauge angiocatheter (angiocath), at least 5 cm long (8 cm preferred)
- **Technique:** Insert perpendicular to chest wall, over the top of the rib (avoid neurovascular bundle on inferior rib margin); rush of air confirms diagnosis
- **Leave catheter in place** — this is a temporizing measure only

**Step 2: Chest Tube Placement**
- **28-32 Fr** tube thoracostomy at 4th-5th ICS, AAL
- Connect to water seal or Pleur-evac system with -20 cmH2O suction
- Obtain post-procedure CXR to confirm lung re-expansion and tube position
- Needle decompression alone is NOT definitive treatment — always follow with chest tube

### Chest Tube Management (Nursing Focus)

**Setup and System**
- **Water seal chamber:** Fluid should fluctuate (tidaling) with respiration — this confirms tube patency and pleural space communication
  - Tidaling absent = tube may be kinked, clamped, or lung may be fully re-expanded
- **Suction:** Typically -20 cmH2O; continuous bubbling in suction control chamber confirms suction is applied
- **Air leak assessment:** Continuous bubbling in the water seal chamber during expiration indicates ongoing air leak from the lung
  - Large, persistent air leak may indicate bronchopleural fistula
  - Document: "Air leak present/absent, continuous/intermittent, with cough only"

**Ongoing Monitoring**
| Parameter | Frequency | Action |
|-----------|-----------|--------|
| Tidaling | Every 2-4 hours | Should be present; absence suggests tube malfunction or re-expansion |
| Air leak | Every 2-4 hours | Document size and timing; notify provider if new or worsening |
| Drainage output | Every 1-2 hours initially | Document volume, color, character; >200 mL/hr = possible hemorrhage |
| Chest tube site | Every shift | Dressing intact, no air leak at insertion site, no subcutaneous emphysema |
| Respiratory status | Continuous + every 2-4 hours | SpO2, RR, breath sounds, work of breathing |
| CXR | Post-insertion, then daily or as ordered | Confirm tube position and lung re-expansion |

**Critical Chest Tube Rules**
- **NEVER clamp a chest tube** unless specifically ordered by a physician for a planned trial (risk of tension pneumothorax)
- Keep drainage system **below the level of the chest** at all times
- Ensure all connections are **taped and secure** to prevent accidental disconnection
- If the system is accidentally disconnected: submerge the tube end in sterile water (creates a water seal) and reconnect immediately
- If the tube is accidentally pulled out: cover the site with an occlusive dressing (petroleum gauze) taped on three sides; notify provider emergently
- **Dependent loops in tubing trap drainage** and impair function — keep tubing straight from patient to collection system
- Patient may sit up, ambulate with system below chest; encourage coughing and deep breathing
- **Transport:** Always keep below chest level; NEVER clamp during transport (risk of tension)

### Recurrence Prevention
- **Chemical pleurodesis:** Talc or doxycycline instilled via chest tube; creates inflammation and adhesion between pleural layers; 90-95% effective
- **Video-assisted thoracoscopic surgery (VATS):** Bleb resection + mechanical pleurodesis; gold standard for recurrence prevention; recurrence <5%
- **Indications for definitive intervention:** Second ipsilateral episode, first contralateral episode, bilateral, persistent air leak (>5-7 days), high-risk occupation (pilot, diver)

---
sections.complications:
| Complication | Context |
|-------------|---------|
| **Tension pneumothorax** | Any pneumothorax can progress to tension, especially with positive-pressure ventilation |
| **Re-expansion pulmonary edema** | Rare but serious; occurs after rapid re-expansion of a lung collapsed for >3 days; unilateral pulmonary edema on the re-expanded side; mechanism: increased capillary permeability from reperfusion injury; management is supportive (oxygen, diuretics, possible mechanical ventilation) |
| **Hemopneumothorax** | Blood + air; especially traumatic; may require large-bore tube and surgical exploration |
| **Persistent air leak** | >5-7 days; suggests bronchopleural fistula; may require surgery |
| **Recurrence** | 30% after first PSP; 50%+ after second; higher in smokers and SSP |
| **Empyema** | Infection of pleural space; complication of chest tube or pneumonia |
| **Subcutaneous emphysema** | Air tracking into soft tissues; usually self-limited but can be massive and distressing |
| **Tube malposition** | Intraparenchymal, intrafissural, or extrapleural placement — confirm position on CXR |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment Priorities
- **Respiratory status:** Continuous pulse oximetry, respiratory rate, work of breathing, bilateral breath sounds
- **Hemodynamic monitoring:** Heart rate, blood pressure — tachycardia and hypotension may indicate tension physiology or hemothorax
- **Pain assessment:** Pleuritic chest pain; adequate analgesia improves respiratory effort and coughing
- **Chest tube function:** Tidaling, air leak, drainage volume and character, system integrity
- **Subcutaneous emphysema:** Palpate chest, neck, and face; document extent; mark borders with skin marker to track progression

### Patient Education
- **Activity restrictions:** No flying until confirmed radiographic resolution (minimum 1-2 weeks after resolution); no scuba diving (permanent restriction unless pleurodesis performed); avoid heavy lifting and Valsalva maneuvers during recovery
- **Recurrence recognition:** Sudden chest pain, dyspnea — seek immediate medical attention
- **Smoking cessation:** Single most important modifiable risk factor; reduces recurrence risk significantly
- **Follow-up:** CXR in 1-2 weeks after discharge to confirm resolution

### Special Situations

**Pneumothorax in Mechanically Ventilated Patients**
- Positive-pressure ventilation dramatically increases risk of tension physiology
- Occult pneumothorax on CT in a ventilated patient generally warrants prophylactic chest tube
- Sudden desaturation + hypotension + rising peak airway pressures in a ventilated patient = tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise
- **Auto-PEEP** can mimic or cause pneumothorax — briefly disconnect from ventilator to differentiate

**Bilateral Pneumothorax**
- Rare, life-threatening; typically traumatic or iatrogenic
- Requires bilateral chest tubes
- Do NOT decompress both sides simultaneously if one is under tension — prioritize the tension side

---
sections.differential-diagnosis:
- Acute coronary syndrome / MI — chest pain, but typically substernal pressure without pleuritic quality
- Pulmonary embolism — pleuritic pain with dyspnea; risk factors, D-dimer, CTPA
- Pericardial tamponade — JVD and hypotension like tension PTX, but muffled heart sounds (Beck's triad) and no absent breath sounds
- Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome — bilateral infiltrates, not unilateral absent breath sounds
- COPD exacerbation — may coexist with SSP; CXR differentiates
- Rib fracture — pleuritic pain after trauma; CXR may or may not show pneumothorax
- Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave) — left pneumothorax/hydropneumothorax after vomiting; mediastinal air
- Large pulmonary bulla — can mimic pneumothorax on CXR; **CT differentiates — critical before chest tube placement** (draining a bulla is catastrophic)

---
sections.key-cross-references:
- Acute Respiratory Distress Syndrome — Differential; patients with ARDS on ventilators are at high risk for barotrauma-induced pneumothorax
- Cystic Fibrosis — Secondary spontaneous pneumothorax is a common and serious complication
- Respiratory System — Normal lung anatomy, pleural physiology, respiratory mechanics
- Oxygenation and Ventilation — V/Q mismatch, shunt physiology
- Shock — Tension pneumothorax causes obstructive shock
- Hemodynamics — Preload, venous return, and cardiac output in tension physiology
- COPD — Most common cause of secondary spontaneous pneumothorax

clinicalPearls: **Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis — do NOT wait for CXR.** Absent breath sounds + hypotension + respiratory distress = decompress NOW. A chest X-ray in a crashing patient is a delay that can kill.

**Absent breath sounds + hypotension = decompress now.** If the patient is in extremis, needle decompression at the 4th-5th ICS AAL or 2nd ICS MCL buys you time for definitive chest tube placement. Do not hesitate.

**Standard angiocaths may be too short.** Chest wall thickness (especially in obese patients) can exceed 4.5 cm. Use the longest available 14-gauge catheter. Failure of needle decompression is often a length problem, not a location problem.

**POCUS is transforming pneumothorax diagnosis.** Absent lung sliding is faster and more sensitive than CXR. If your ED has ultrasound and you are trained, it should be your first-line assessment tool for dyspnea and chest trauma.

**Every pneumothorax on a ventilator is a potential tension pneumothorax.** Positive-pressure ventilation forces air through the defect. If a ventilated patient suddenly desaturates with rising peak pressures and dropping blood pressure, think tension first.

**Never clamp a chest tube for transport.** This is a persistent myth in nursing practice. A clamped tube in a patient with an ongoing air leak will create a tension pneumothorax. The system travels with the patient, below the chest.

**Re-expansion pulmonary edema is rare but dangerous.** After re-expanding a lung that has been collapsed for more than 72 hours, watch for worsening oxygenation, cough, and frothy sputum on the re-expanded side. This is not infection — it is reperfusion injury.
**Smoking is the biggest modifiable risk.** In PSP, smoking increases risk 20-fold in men and 9-fold in women. Cessation counseling is a critical nursing intervention before discharge.
**Check the other lung.** Bilateral pneumothorax (especially in trauma) is uncommon but devastating. Always auscultate and image both sides.

signs: Sudden onset of unilateral pleuritic chest pain (sharp, worsens with inspiration)
Dyspnea (often mild in young, healthy patients)
Often occurs at rest; may follow Valsalva maneuver
Patients are typically tall, thin males aged 15-35
Similar symptoms but often MORE severe (less respiratory reserve)
Even a small pneumothorax can cause significant distress in patients with underlying lung disease
Hypoxemia more pronounced due to baseline V/Q mismatch
Chest pain and dyspnea following trauma
Look for chest wall injuries: rib fractures, subcutaneous emphysema, penetrating wounds
May be accompanied by hemothorax (dullness to percussion at base + absent breath sounds)
**Severe respiratory distress** — rapidly progressive
**Hypotension / obstructive shock**
**Tracheal deviation** away from affected side (late finding, unreliable)
**Distended neck veins** (JVD) — from impaired venous return (may be absent if hypovolemic)
**Absent breath sounds** on affected side
**Hyperresonance** to percussion on affected side
**Tachycardia** — severe, progressive
**Cyanosis** — late, ominous
**Agitation/altered mental status** — from hypoxia and hypoperfusion

polycystic-ovary-syndrome.title: Polycystic Ovary Syndrome

sections.definition:
Polycystic ovary syndrome is a heterogeneous endocrine-metabolic disorder characterized by hyperandrogenism, ovulatory dysfunction, and polycystic ovarian morphology. It is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women and the leading cause of anovulatory infertility. PCOS is not simply a reproductive disorder — it is a lifelong metabolic condition with significant cardiovascular, metabolic, and psychological consequences that extend well beyond the reproductive years.

The name "polycystic" is a misnomer: the ovarian "cysts" are actually arrested follicles (antral follicles 2-9 mm) that failed to achieve dominance and ovulation, not true cysts.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** 6-13% of reproductive-age women depending on diagnostic criteria used; up to 20% using Rotterdam criteria (the broadest); most common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age; estimated 5-6 million women affected in the US
- **Risk factors:** Family history of PCOS (strong genetic component, heritability ~70%), insulin resistance, obesity (amplifies symptoms but is not required — 20-30% of PCOS patients are lean), family history of type 2 diabetes, exposure to excess androgens in utero, low birth weight, premature adrenarche
- **Demographics:** Onset typically around menarche or in the early twenties; affects all races and ethnicities; often diagnosed late (average delay 2+ years, multiple providers); Hispanic women may have more severe metabolic features; Southeast Asian women may have higher prevalence; symptoms often worsen with weight gain and may improve with menopause (androgen levels decline) but metabolic risks persist lifelong
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — GnRH pulses regulate LH/FSH secretion via GPCR signaling; in PCOS, increased GnRH pulse frequency preferentially drives LH over FSH, and LH receptors on theca cells (also GPCRs) are overactivated, producing excess androgens that disrupt folliculogenesis*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — the hypothalamic-pituitary-ovarian (HPO) axis normally operates on estrogen/progesterone negative feedback; in PCOS this feedback is disrupted by chronic anovulation (no progesterone from corpus luteum) and hyperinsulinemia (suppresses SHBG, amplifying free androgen) — creating a self-reinforcing cycle of androgen excess and anovulation*

1. **Hypothalamic-pituitary dysregulation:** Increased GnRH pulse frequency → preferential LH secretion over FSH → elevated LH:FSH ratio (classically >2:1, though not required for diagnosis) → LH drives ovarian theca cell androgen production
2. **Insulin resistance and hyperinsulinemia:** Present in 50-70% of women with PCOS regardless of BMI → insulin acts synergistically with LH on theca cells → amplifies ovarian androgen synthesis → insulin also decreases hepatic production of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG) → increases free (bioavailable) testosterone
3. **Ovarian androgen excess:** Excess androgens disrupt normal folliculogenesis → multiple small antral follicles are recruited but none achieves dominance → follicular arrest → anovulation → "polycystic" ovarian morphology on ultrasound
4. **Adipose tissue amplification:** In obese PCOS patients, adipose tissue converts androgens to estrogens (via aromatase) → chronic unopposed estrogen exposure to endometrium (no progesterone from ovulation) → endometrial hyperplasia and cancer risk; adipose tissue also produces inflammatory cytokines → worsens insulin resistance → vicious cycle
5. **Chronic low-grade inflammation:** Elevated CRP, IL-6, TNF-alpha → contributes to insulin resistance, endothelial dysfunction, and cardiovascular risk independent of obesity
6. **Genetic and epigenetic factors:** Highly heritable (~70%); polygenic with variants in genes involved in androgen synthesis (CYP11A, CYP17), insulin signaling, and gonadotropin action; possible epigenetic programming via in utero androgen exposure
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Menstrual irregularity:** Oligomenorrhea (<8 cycles/year) or amenorrhea; irregular, unpredictable cycles; onset typically at menarche (may be masked by OCPs started for "irregular periods")
- **Hyperandrogenism — clinical:**
  - Hirsutism (60-70%) — excess terminal hair in male pattern (upper lip, chin, chest, abdomen, back); assessed by modified Ferriman-Gallwey score (≥4-6 depending on ethnicity)
  - Acne — inflammatory, often along jawline and chin; persistent beyond adolescence
  - Androgenic alopecia — frontal/vertex hair thinning (female pattern)
- **Hyperandrogenism — biochemical:** Elevated total testosterone, free testosterone, or DHEA-S; free testosterone is most sensitive (often elevated when total T is normal due to low SHBG)
- **Polycystic ovarian morphology:** ≥12 antral follicles (2-9 mm) per ovary OR ovarian volume >10 mL on transvaginal ultrasound (2003 threshold; 2018 international guideline raised threshold to ≥20 follicles with modern high-resolution probes)
- **Metabolic features:** Central obesity, acanthosis nigricans (marker of insulin resistance — velvety hyperpigmented patches on neck, axillae, groin), skin tags
- **Infertility:** Due to chronic anovulation; PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory infertility
- **Psychological:** Increased rates of anxiety (40%), depression (30-40%), eating disorders, and reduced quality of life — often underrecognized

### Complications
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: 5-10x increased risk; up to 40% develop impaired glucose tolerance by age 40
- Gestational diabetes (2-3x increased risk)
- Endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer (2-6x increased risk from chronic unopposed estrogen)
- Cardiovascular disease: dyslipidemia (low HDL, high triglycerides, elevated LDL), hypertension, subclinical atherosclerosis
- Obstructive sleep apnea (5-30x increased risk, even in non-obese PCOS)
- Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
- Obesity: present in 40-80% of PCOS patients; amplifies all metabolic risks
- Pregnancy complications: miscarriage (increased rate), preeclampsia, preterm birth
sections.diagnosis:
### Rotterdam Criteria (2003) — International Consensus
Diagnosis requires **at least 2 of the following 3 criteria**, after exclusion of other causes:

| Criterion | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **1. Oligo-ovulation or anovulation** | Oligomenorrhea (<8 cycles/year), amenorrhea, or irregular cycles; may be confirmed by low mid-luteal progesterone |
| **2. Hyperandrogenism (clinical or biochemical)** | Clinical: hirsutism, acne, androgenic alopecia; Biochemical: elevated free testosterone, total testosterone, or DHEA-S |
| **3. Polycystic ovaries on ultrasound** | ≥12 follicles (2-9 mm) per ovary OR ovarian volume >10 mL; at least one ovary meeting criteria is sufficient |

### Phenotypes (Rotterdam)
- **Phenotype A (classic):** All 3 criteria — most severe metabolic profile
- **Phenotype B (classic):** Hyperandrogenism + ovulatory dysfunction, no polycystic morphology
- **Phenotype C (ovulatory):** Hyperandrogenism + polycystic morphology, regular cycles
- **Phenotype D (non-hyperandrogenic):** Ovulatory dysfunction + polycystic morphology, no hyperandrogenism — mildest metabolic profile

### Must Exclude
| Condition | How to Exclude |
|-----------|---------------|
| Thyroid dysfunction | TSH |
| Hyperprolactinemia | Prolactin level |
| Non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia (21-hydroxylase deficiency) | Early morning 17-hydroxyprogesterone (<200 ng/dL rules out) |
| Cushing syndrome | 24-hour urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test, or late-night salivary cortisol (if clinical suspicion) |
| Androgen-secreting tumor | Total testosterone >200 ng/dL or DHEA-S >700 mcg/dL — rapid virilization raises suspicion |

### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **Total and free testosterone** | Free T most sensitive; elevated in 60-80% |
| **DHEA-S** | Mildly elevated (adrenal androgen contribution); markedly elevated suggests adrenal tumor |
| **SHBG** | Low (due to hyperinsulinemia); inversely correlates with insulin resistance |
| **LH, FSH** | LH:FSH ratio often >2:1 (but not required for diagnosis and not routinely measured for diagnosis) |
| **17-hydroxyprogesterone** | To exclude non-classic CAH |
| **TSH, prolactin** | Rule out thyroid disease and hyperprolactinemia |
| **Fasting glucose + insulin or OGTT** | Screen for insulin resistance and glucose intolerance; 2-hour 75g OGTT preferred (fasting glucose alone misses 50% of IGT in PCOS) |
| **Lipid panel** | Dyslipidemia screening (high TG, low HDL pattern) |
| **Transvaginal ultrasound** | Polycystic ovarian morphology; also assess endometrial thickness if prolonged amenorrhea (>3 months without withdrawal bleed) |
sections.management:
### Lifestyle Modification — First-Line for All Patients
- **Weight loss:** 5-10% body weight reduction improves ovulatory function, reduces androgens, improves insulin sensitivity, and may restore fertility — this is the single most effective intervention for overweight/obese PCOS patients
- **Diet:** No single best diet; Mediterranean, low-glycemic-index, or anti-inflammatory diets show benefit; caloric deficit of 500-750 kcal/day for weight loss; avoid highly processed foods and refined carbohydrates
- **Exercise:** 150 minutes/week of moderate-intensity activity; improves insulin sensitivity independent of weight loss; resistance training plus aerobic exercise is optimal
- **Behavioral counseling:** Address emotional eating, body image, and psychological comorbidities

### Menstrual Regulation and Endometrial Protection
- **Combined oral contraceptives (OCPs):** First-line for menstrual irregularity and hyperandrogenism in patients not seeking pregnancy; regulate cycles, provide endometrial protection (oppose estrogen with progestin), reduce androgens (increase SHBG, suppress ovarian androgen production); prefer pills with anti-androgenic progestins (drospirenone, cyproterone acetate) when available; avoid in patients with migraine with aura, VTE history, or uncontrolled HTN
- **Cyclic progestin:** Alternative if OCPs contraindicated; medroxyprogesterone acetate 10 mg x 10-14 days per month to induce withdrawal bleed and protect endometrium
- **Hormonal IUD (levonorgestrel):** Provides endometrial protection; does not address hyperandrogenism or regulate cycles

### Hyperandrogenism Treatment
- **Spironolactone:** 50-200 mg/day; anti-androgen — blocks androgen receptor and inhibits 5-alpha-reductase; effective for hirsutism (takes 6-12 months for full effect) and acne; MUST be combined with reliable contraception (teratogenic — feminizes male fetus); monitor potassium
- **Topical treatments:** Eflornithine cream (slows facial hair growth); minoxidil for androgenic alopecia; topical retinoids and benzoyl peroxide for acne
- **Finasteride:** 5-alpha-reductase inhibitor; off-label for hirsutism; also teratogenic — requires contraception

### Insulin Resistance and Metabolic Management
- **Metformin:** 1500-2000 mg/day (titrate slowly to reduce GI side effects); improves insulin sensitivity, may modestly reduce androgens, may improve ovulatory function; NOT first-line for menstrual regulation (OCPs are superior) but useful adjunct in patients with glucose intolerance, T2DM, or significant insulin resistance; useful in patients who cannot take OCPs; see Oral Hypoglycemics
- **GLP-1 receptor agonists:** Emerging evidence for PCOS with obesity; semaglutide, liraglutide — significant weight loss + metabolic improvement; not yet standard of care for PCOS specifically but increasingly used off-label
- **Glucose monitoring:** 2-hour 75g OGTT at diagnosis and every 1-3 years (annual if BMI >30 or other risk factors)

### Fertility — Ovulation Induction
- **Letrozole:** First-line for ovulation induction (aromatase inhibitor — reduces estrogen → releases hypothalamic-pituitary axis from negative feedback → FSH surge → follicular maturation); superior to clomiphene for live birth rates in PCOS; 2.5-7.5 mg/day on cycle days 3-7
- **Clomiphene citrate:** Second-line (selective estrogen receptor modulator); 50-150 mg/day on cycle days 5-9; historically first-line but letrozole now preferred per evidence
- **Gonadotropins (FSH injections):** Second/third-line; low-dose step-up protocol to minimize ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) risk — PCOS patients are high-risk for OHSS
- **Ovarian drilling:** Laparoscopic ovarian drilling — rarely used; reserved for clomiphene/letrozole-resistant anovulation when gonadotropins are not available
- **IVF:** For patients who fail ovulation induction; requires careful stimulation protocols (high OHSS risk)
- **Metformin as adjunct:** May improve ovulation induction response when combined with letrozole or clomiphene

### Endometrial Surveillance
- Transvaginal ultrasound to assess endometrial thickness if amenorrhea >3 months without withdrawal bleed
- Endometrial biopsy if endometrial thickness >10 mm, abnormal uterine bleeding pattern, or age >35 with prolonged anovulation

### Pharmacology
- **Oral Hypoglycemics** — metformin for insulin resistance (improves ovulation, reduces androgen levels); inositol as adjunct
- **Insulin** — insulin resistance is the central metabolic derangement; hyperinsulinemia drives ovarian androgen production
- **Statins** — for dyslipidemia management in metabolic syndrome
- Hormonal contraceptives: First-line for menstrual regulation, acne, and hirsutism; estrogen-progestin combination pills suppress ovarian androgens
- Spironolactone: Anti-androgen; for hirsutism and acne (requires contraception — teratogenic)
- Letrozole/clomiphene: Ovulation induction for fertility
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Holistic assessment:**
  - PCOS affects multiple body systems — screen for depression, anxiety, disordered eating, and body image concerns at every visit
  - Many patients have been dismissed or told to "just lose weight" — validate their experience and recognize PCOS as a real medical condition
  - Hirsutism and acne significantly impact self-esteem and quality of life; acknowledge this and discuss treatment options
- **Metabolic screening coordination:**
  - Ensure OGTT (not just fasting glucose) is ordered at diagnosis and repeated every 1-3 years
  - Lipid panel, blood pressure monitoring at regular intervals
  - Screen for OSA symptoms (snoring, daytime sleepiness) especially if BMI >30
- **Medication education:**
  - OCPs: review VTE risk factors, when to seek emergency care (leg swelling, chest pain, sudden headache)
  - Spironolactone: MUST use contraception (pregnancy category X); monitor potassium; may cause dizziness, breast tenderness
  - Metformin: start low, titrate slowly; take with food; GI side effects (nausea, diarrhea) usually improve over 2-4 weeks; extended-release formulation better tolerated
  - Letrozole: educate about timing in menstrual cycle, monitoring requirements (ultrasound for follicular development), signs of OHSS (rapid weight gain, abdominal distension, dyspnea)
- **Lifestyle counseling:**
  - Frame as empowerment, not blame — 5-10% weight loss has measurable clinical benefit
  - Exercise improves insulin sensitivity even without weight loss — emphasize this for lean PCOS patients
  - Refer to dietitian for individualized nutrition counseling
  - Address barriers to lifestyle modification (cost, time, mental health)
- **Fertility support:**
  - Provide emotional support and realistic expectations during ovulation induction
  - Educate about OHSS warning signs (PCOS patients are highest-risk group)
  - Connect with reproductive endocrinology early if first-line agents fail
sections.keyPoints:
- PCOS is the most common endocrine disorder in reproductive-age women, affecting 6-13%, and is diagnosed using the Rotterdam criteria (2 of 3: oligo/anovulation, hyperandrogenism, polycystic ovaries on ultrasound)
- Insulin resistance is the central metabolic driver, present in 50-70% of patients regardless of BMI, and amplifies ovarian androgen production via synergy with LH
- PCOS confers significantly increased lifetime risk of type 2 diabetes (5-10x), cardiovascular disease, endometrial cancer, and obstructive sleep apnea
- Lifestyle modification (5-10% weight loss) is the most effective first-line intervention for metabolic and reproductive outcomes
- OCPs are first-line for menstrual regulation and hyperandrogenism; spironolactone is the primary anti-androgen for hirsutism/acne (requires contraception)
- Letrozole has replaced clomiphene as first-line for ovulation induction based on superior live birth rates
- Metformin is a useful adjunct for insulin resistance and glucose intolerance but is not first-line for menstrual regulation
- PCOS is a lifelong condition — metabolic risks persist beyond menopause and require ongoing surveillance

clinicalPearls: **PCOS is a diagnosis of exclusion — always rule out the mimics.** Non-classic congenital adrenal hyperplasia, thyroid dysfunction, hyperprolactinemia, and androgen-secreting tumors can all present identically. A 17-hydroxyprogesterone, TSH, and prolactin level are minimum workup before diagnosing PCOS. If total testosterone exceeds 200 ng/dL or virilization is rapid, image the ovaries and adrenals urgently.

**Fasting glucose alone misses half of glucose intolerance in PCOS — use the OGTT.** Women with PCOS have a unique pattern of post-load hyperglycemia with relatively normal fasting glucose. The 2-hour 75g oral glucose tolerance test is the only reliable screening tool. A normal fasting glucose does not rule out significant insulin resistance or impaired glucose tolerance.

**Lean PCOS is real and often missed.** About 20-30% of PCOS patients have a normal BMI. They still have insulin resistance, hyperandrogenism, and metabolic risk. Do not dismiss PCOS symptoms because the patient "doesn't look like she has PCOS." Lean patients benefit from exercise, metformin, and the same screening protocols.

**Unopposed estrogen is the silent threat.** Every anovulatory cycle means estrogen without progesterone opposition to the endometrium. Prolonged amenorrhea (>3 months) without progestin withdrawal raises endometrial cancer risk. Always ensure endometrial protection — OCP, cyclic progestin, or hormonal IUD.

**Spironolactone takes 6-12 months for hirsutism — set expectations early.** Patients often expect rapid improvement. Spironolactone prevents new terminal hair growth but does not reverse existing hair. Combination with mechanical removal (laser, electrolysis) gives best cosmetic results. Warn about the long timeline upfront to prevent premature discontinuation.

signs: **Menstrual irregularity:** Oligomenorrhea (<8 cycles/year) or amenorrhea; irregular, unpredictable cycles; onset typically at menarche (may be masked by OCPs started for "irregular periods")
**Hyperandrogenism — clinical:**
Hirsutism (60-70%) — excess terminal hair in male pattern (upper lip, chin, chest, abdomen, back); assessed by modified Ferriman-Gallwey score (≥4-6 depending on ethnicity)
Acne — inflammatory, often along jawline and chin; persistent beyond adolescence
Androgenic alopecia — frontal/vertex hair thinning (female pattern)
**Hyperandrogenism — biochemical:** Elevated total testosterone, free testosterone, or DHEA-S; free testosterone is most sensitive (often elevated when total T is normal due to low SHBG)

**Polycystic ovarian morphology:** ≥12 antral follicles (2-9 mm) per ovary OR ovarian volume >10 mL on transvaginal ultrasound (2003 threshold; 2018 international guideline raised threshold to ≥20 follicles with modern high-resolution probes)
**Metabolic features:** Central obesity, acanthosis nigricans (marker of insulin resistance — velvety hyperpigmented patches on neck, axillae, groin), skin tags
**Infertility:** Due to chronic anovulation; PCOS is the most common cause of anovulatory infertility
**Psychological:** Increased rates of anxiety (40%), depression (30-40%), eating disorders, and reduced quality of life — often underrecognized
Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus: 5-10x increased risk; up to 40% develop impaired glucose tolerance by age 40
Gestational diabetes (2-3x increased risk)
Endometrial hyperplasia and endometrial cancer (2-6x increased risk from chronic unopposed estrogen)
Cardiovascular disease: dyslipidemia (low HDL, high triglycerides, elevated LDL), hypertension, subclinical atherosclerosis
Obstructive sleep apnea (5-30x increased risk, even in non-obese PCOS)
Nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD)
Obesity: present in 40-80% of PCOS patients; amplifies all metabolic risks
Pregnancy complications: miscarriage (increased rate), preeclampsia, preterm birth

preeclampsia-and-eclampsia.title: Preeclampsia and Eclampsia

sections.definition:
**Preeclampsia** is a pregnancy-specific hypertensive disorder characterized by new-onset hypertension (≥140/90 mmHg) AND proteinuria or end-organ dysfunction after 20 weeks gestation (or postpartum). It is a systemic endothelial disease driven by abnormal placentation.

**Eclampsia** is preeclampsia with **new-onset generalized tonic-clonic seizures** not explained by another cause — a life-threatening emergency.

**HELLP Syndrome** is a severe variant: **H**emolysis, **E**levated **L**iver enzymes, **L**ow **P**latelets. Can occur with or without classic preeclampsia findings.

**Related notes:** Hypertension · Homeostasis and Feedback Loops · Epilepsy · Acute Kidney Injury

---
sections.epidemiology:
- **Incidence:** 2-8% of all pregnancies worldwide; ~5% in the US
- **Eclampsia:** ~0.1% of pregnancies (1 in 2000-3000)
- **Maternal mortality:** Leading cause of maternal death worldwide. Responsible for ~15% of maternal deaths
- **Risk factors:**
  - Nulliparity (first pregnancy — highest risk)
  - Prior history of preeclampsia
  - Chronic hypertension, chronic kidney disease, diabetes
  - Multiple gestation (twins, triplets)
  - Age extremes (<20 or >35)
  - Obesity (BMI >30)
  - Autoimmune disease (SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome)
  - Family history (mother or sister with preeclampsia)
  - Interpregnancy interval >10 years

---
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Hemodynamics — placental ischemia → antiangiogenic factors (sFlt-1) → endothelial dysfunction → vasoconstriction → HTN + proteinuria; BP = CO × SVR (↑SVR from vasoconstriction)*
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — eclampsia = decompensation: endothelial dysfunction triggers cerebral edema, seizures, HELLP syndrome, and multi-organ failure*

### The Root Cause: Abnormal Placentation

1. **Normal pregnancy:** Spiral arteries in the uterine wall are remodeled by trophoblasts → they become wide, low-resistance vessels → high blood flow to the placenta
2. **In preeclampsia:** Trophoblast invasion is shallow and incomplete → spiral arteries remain narrow, high-resistance → **placental ischemia**
3. **Ischemic placenta releases factors** into maternal circulation:
   - sFlt-1 (anti-angiogenic) — blocks VEGF and PlGF → endothelial dysfunction
   - Inflammatory cytokines
   - Reactive oxygen species
4. **Systemic endothelial dysfunction** → the disease becomes multi-organ:
   - **Vasospasm** → hypertension
   - **Capillary leak** → edema, proteinuria
   - **Platelet activation + consumption** → thrombocytopenia (HELLP)
   - **Hepatic ischemia** → elevated AST/ALT, subcapsular hematoma, liver rupture
   - **Renal endotheliosis** → glomerular injury → proteinuria, ↓ GFR
   - **Cerebral vasospasm + edema** → headache, visual changes, seizures (eclampsia)
   - **Uteroplacental insufficiency** → fetal growth restriction, oligohydramnios, placental abruption

> **The cure is delivery of the placenta.** Since the placenta drives the disease, removing it is the only definitive treatment. Everything else (antihypertensives, magnesium, steroids) is temporizing to stabilize the mother and optimize fetal maturity.

---
sections.diagnostic-criteria:
### Preeclampsia (ACOG 2013/2020)

**Hypertension** (after 20 weeks in a previously normotensive patient):
- SBP ≥140 mmHg OR DBP ≥90 mmHg on two occasions ≥4 hours apart
- OR SBP ≥160 OR DBP ≥110 on ONE occasion (severe range — treat immediately)

**PLUS one of the following:**
- Proteinuria: ≥300 mg/24h, protein/creatinine ratio ≥0.3, or dipstick ≥2+
- OR end-organ dysfunction (proteinuria is no longer required if any of these are present):
  - Thrombocytopenia (<100,000/μL)
  - Renal insufficiency (Cr >1.1 mg/dL or doubling of baseline)
  - Elevated liver transaminases (≥2x upper limit of normal)
  - Pulmonary edema
  - New-onset headache unresponsive to medication (cerebral)
  - Visual disturbances (scotomata, blurred vision, cortical blindness)
