**Watch the sodium on both carbamazepine and oxcarbazepine** — hyponatremia is common, especially in elderly patients; check levels within the first month and with any dose changes
**Agranulocytosis from carbamazepine is rare but deadly** — teach every patient to report sore throat, fever, or unusual bruising immediately; this is not a side effect to learn about after the fact

signs: **Paroxysmal lancinating pain:** Sudden, severe, electric shock-like or stabbing pain lasting seconds to 2 minutes
**Unilateral:** Almost always one side; bilateral should prompt workup for MS or other secondary cause
**Trigger zones:** Light touch to specific areas triggers attacks — perioral, nasal ala, cheek, gums; patients may avoid eating, talking, shaving, brushing teeth, or facing wind
**Triggered by:** Chewing, talking, brushing teeth, light touch to face, cold air, smiling, drinking
**Refractory periods:** Brief period after a paroxysm during which another cannot be triggered
**Clusters:** Pain attacks may cluster in bouts lasting weeks to months, followed by pain-free remissions (may shorten over time)
**Between attacks:** Neurological exam is typically NORMAL in classic TN; any persistent sensory deficit suggests secondary cause
**Continuous background pain:** May develop over time, especially in TN Type 2 (atypical)
Severe weight loss and dehydration (avoidance of eating and drinking due to pain triggers)
Depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation (one of the most debilitating chronic pain conditions)
Social isolation (fear of triggering attacks)
Medication side effects from chronic anticonvulsant use
Dental complications from avoidance of oral hygiene

tumor-lysis-syndrome.title: Tumor Lysis Syndrome

sections.definition:
Tumor lysis syndrome (TLS) is an **oncologic emergency** caused by the rapid release of intracellular contents into the bloodstream following massive tumor cell death (lysis). The dying cells release potassium, phosphate, and nucleic acids in quantities that overwhelm the body's homeostatic and renal excretory capacity, producing a constellation of potentially fatal metabolic derangements:

- **Hyperuricemia** (from nucleic acid breakdown)
- **Hyperkalemia** (from intracellular potassium release)
- **Hyperphosphatemia** (from intracellular phosphate release)
- **Hypocalcemia** (secondary to calcium-phosphate binding)

TLS most commonly occurs 12-72 hours after initiation of cytotoxic chemotherapy but can occur **spontaneously** in tumors with very high proliferation rates. Left untreated, TLS leads to acute kidney injury, fatal cardiac arrhythmias, seizures, and death.

---
sections.epidemiology:
- Most common with **hematologic malignancies**, particularly:
  - **Burkitt lymphoma** — highest risk (TLS incidence up to 25-30%)
  - **Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL)** — especially with WBC >100,000
  - **Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL)** — bulky disease
  - Acute myeloid leukemia (AML) with high WBC
- Can occur with **solid tumors** (less common): small cell lung cancer, hepatoblastoma, neuroblastoma, bulky germ cell tumors
- Overall incidence varies widely by tumor type: 3-25% in high-risk malignancies
- Mortality from established clinical TLS ranges from **20-50%** even with treatment
- Spontaneous TLS (without chemotherapy) accounts for a small but significant subset, often presenting to the ED as the initial cancer diagnosis

### Risk Factors for TLS

| Factor | Why It Increases Risk |
|---|---|
| **Bulky disease** (tumor >10 cm or high tumor burden) | More cells available to lyse |
| **High WBC** (>25,000-50,000, especially >100,000) | Large circulating tumor cell mass |
| **Elevated LDH** (>2x upper limit of normal) | Marker of high cell turnover |
| **Pre-existing renal dysfunction** (elevated Cr, low GFR) | Reduced capacity to clear uric acid, potassium, phosphate |
| **Dehydration / low urine output** | Reduced renal clearance |
| **Hyperuricemia at baseline** | Already at threshold for urate crystal precipitation |
| **High tumor sensitivity to treatment** | More rapid cell kill = more rapid lysis |

---
sections.cairo-bishop-classification:
The standard diagnostic framework distinguishes laboratory TLS from clinical TLS:

### Laboratory TLS (LTLS)

Requires **2 or more** of the following within 3 days before or 7 days after initiation of therapy:

| Metabolic Abnormality | Threshold |
|---|---|
| Uric acid | >8 mg/dL (or 25% increase from baseline) |
| Potassium | >6.0 mEq/L (or 25% increase from baseline) |
| Phosphate | >4.5 mg/dL (or 25% increase from baseline) |
| Calcium | <7.0 mg/dL (or 25% decrease from baseline) |

### Clinical TLS (CTLS)

**Laboratory TLS PLUS one or more** of the following:
- **Acute kidney injury** (creatinine >=1.5x upper limit of normal for age)
- **Cardiac dysrhythmia** or sudden death
- **Seizures**

Clinical TLS carries significantly higher mortality than laboratory TLS alone.

---
sections.risk-stratification:
| Risk Level | Examples | Prophylaxis Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| **High** | Burkitt lymphoma, ALL with WBC >100K, bulky DLBCL, any cancer with baseline elevated uric acid + LDH + renal dysfunction | Aggressive IV hydration + **rasburicase** + cardiac monitoring + q4-6h labs |
| **Intermediate** | ALL with WBC 25-100K, AML with WBC >50K, DLBCL with elevated LDH, other aggressive lymphomas | Aggressive IV hydration + **allopurinol** + q8-12h labs |
| **Low** | Most solid tumors, CLL, indolent lymphomas, low LDH, normal renal function | Standard hydration + monitoring, consider allopurinol |

---
sections.pathophysiology-the-four-metabolic-deran:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — rapid cancer cell death (from chemotherapy) releases intracellular contents en masse: potassium, phosphate, uric acid, and nucleic acids flood into the bloodstream simultaneously. Acid-Base Balance — TLS causes metabolic acidosis (lactic acid from dying cells), hyperkalemia (cardiac risk), and hypocalcemia (from hyperphosphatemia) simultaneously.*

### 1. Hyperuricemia

**Mechanism:** Tumor cells contain large amounts of nucleic acids (DNA and RNA). When cells lyse, purines are released and metabolized:

```
Purines (adenine, guanine)
    ↓ (purine catabolism)
Hypoxanthine
    ↓ xanthine oxidase
Xanthine
    ↓ xanthine oxidase
URIC ACID ← the problem
    ↓ (humans lack uricase)
Cannot be further broken down
```

- Uric acid is poorly soluble, especially in acidic urine
- At high concentrations, uric acid **crystallizes in renal tubules** (urate nephropathy)
- Crystal deposition causes tubular obstruction and direct tubular injury
- This is the primary mechanism of TLS-related acute kidney injury
- AKI then impairs excretion of potassium and phosphate, compounding all other derangements

**Key point:** Humans lack the enzyme uricase that would convert uric acid to the highly soluble allantoin. This is why rasburicase (recombinant uricase) is so effective.

### 2. Hyperkalemia

**Mechanism:** Intracellular potassium concentration is ~150 mEq/L versus ~4 mEq/L in plasma. Massive cell lysis releases enormous quantities of potassium that exceed renal excretory capacity.

**Clinical consequences:**
- Peaked T waves, widened QRS, sine wave pattern on ECG
- Ventricular tachycardia, ventricular fibrillation
- **Cardiac arrest** — the most immediately life-threatening component of TLS
- Risk compounded by concurrent AKI (reduced renal potassium excretion)

### 3. Hyperphosphatemia

**Mechanism:** Tumor cells (especially lymphoblasts) contain up to 4x more intracellular phosphate than normal cells. Rapid lysis releases massive phosphate loads.

**Clinical consequences:**
- Phosphate binds ionized calcium in the blood, forming **calcium-phosphate crystals**
- These crystals deposit in renal tubules (nephrocalcinosis), worsening AKI
- Crystals also deposit in soft tissues (joints, skin, vasculature)
- The calcium-phosphate binding directly causes secondary hypocalcemia

### 4. Hypocalcemia

**Mechanism:** Secondary to hyperphosphatemia. As serum phosphate rises, it binds free (ionized) calcium, precipitating as calcium-phosphate complexes. The calcium-phosphate product (Ca x PO4) exceeding 60-70 mg2/dL2 significantly increases precipitation risk.

**Clinical consequences:**
- Neuromuscular irritability: tetany, Chvostek sign, Trousseau sign
- **Seizures** — a defining feature of clinical TLS
- **QTc prolongation** — risk for torsades de pointes
- Muscle cramps, paresthesias, laryngospasm
- Cardiac dysfunction (calcium is essential for myocardial contractility)

---
sections.prevention:
Prevention is the cornerstone of TLS management. Once clinical TLS is established, mortality is high despite aggressive treatment.

### Aggressive IV Hydration

- **3 L/m2/day** (pediatric dosing) or **200 mL/hr** (adult dosing) of isotonic crystalloid (NS or D5W-based)
- Begin **24-48 hours before chemotherapy** when possible
- Goal: maintain **urine output >2 mL/kg/hr** (or >80-100 mL/hr in adults)
- High urine flow dilutes uric acid and phosphate in the tubules, preventing crystal formation
- Avoid potassium in all IV fluids (no LR, no KCl additives)
- Avoid bicarbonate alkalinization (previously standard, now controversial — may worsen calcium-phosphate precipitation)
- Fluid balance must be monitored carefully: TLS patients may develop oliguria from AKI, and fluid overload is a real risk

### Rasburicase (Elitek)

- **Recombinant urate oxidase** — converts uric acid to allantoin (highly soluble, easily excreted)
- Dose: **0.2 mg/kg IV** as a single dose (some protocols use 0.05-0.2 mg/kg)
- Onset: reduces uric acid within **4 hours** (often dramatically, from >20 to <1 mg/dL)
- Indicated for **high-risk patients** and those with established hyperuricemia
- Far superior to allopurinol for active TLS (allopurinol only prevents NEW uric acid; rasburicase breaks down EXISTING uric acid)

**CRITICAL CONTRAINDICATION: G6PD deficiency**
- Rasburicase generates hydrogen peroxide as a byproduct of uric acid conversion
- In G6PD-deficient patients, the red blood cell cannot neutralize hydrogen peroxide
- Result: **methemoglobinemia and severe hemolytic anemia**
- **Screen for G6PD before administering rasburicase** (especially in patients of African, Mediterranean, or Southeast Asian descent)
- If G6PD status is unknown and the situation is emergent, weigh risks vs. benefits with the oncology team

**Lab handling note:** Rasburicase continues to break down uric acid in the blood tube at room temperature. Uric acid samples must be placed **on ice immediately** and processed within 4 hours to get an accurate level.

### Allopurinol

- **Xanthine oxidase inhibitor** — blocks the conversion of hypoxanthine/xanthine to uric acid
- Dose: **300-800 mg/day PO** (or IV formulation available at 200-400 mg/m2/day)
- Begin **24-48 hours before chemotherapy**
- Appropriate for **intermediate-risk** patients as prophylaxis
- **Limitations:** Only prevents new uric acid formation; does not reduce existing uric acid levels. May cause xanthine to accumulate and crystallize (xanthine nephropathy). Multiple drug interactions (especially with 6-mercaptopurine and azathioprine — dose reduce by 50-75%)

---
sections.treatment-of-established-tls:
### General Principles

- **Aggressive IV hydration** — same as prevention; may need higher rates if oliguric
- **Continuous cardiac monitoring** — ECG changes from hyperkalemia or hypocalcemia require immediate intervention
- **Frequent lab monitoring** — BMP, uric acid, phosphate, LDH, calcium q4-6h during active TLS
- **Rasburicase** if not already given and uric acid is elevated
- **Nephrology consultation** early — dialysis may be needed

### Hyperkalemia Management

Follow standard hyperkalemia protocol with urgency proportional to ECG changes:

| Intervention | Purpose | Details |
|---|---|---|
| **Calcium gluconate 1-3g IV** | Cardiac membrane stabilization | Does NOT lower K+; buys time. Give if ECG changes present |
| **Regular insulin 10 units + D50 1 amp IV** | Intracellular K+ shift | Onset 15-30 min, lasts 4-6 hours |
| **Sodium bicarbonate 50 mEq IV** | Intracellular K+ shift | Less effective alone, useful if concurrent acidosis |
| **Albuterol 10-20 mg nebulized** | Intracellular K+ shift | Additive with insulin |
| **Sodium polystyrene sulfonate (Kayexalate)** or **sodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma)** | GI potassium removal | Lokelma preferred (faster onset, better tolerated) |
| **Hemodialysis** | Definitive removal | Most effective; indicated for refractory hyperkalemia or K+ >7 with ECG changes |

### Hyperphosphatemia Management

- **Phosphate binders** with meals and snacks: sevelamer (Renagel) 800-1600 mg TID, aluminum hydroxide short-term, calcium-free binders preferred
- **Low-phosphate diet** (if patient is eating)
- **Aggressive hydration** to promote renal phosphate excretion
- **Dialysis** for severe or refractory hyperphosphatemia (phosphate >10 mg/dL with AKI)

### Hypocalcemia Management

- **Calcium replacement is nuanced in TLS:**
  - Only treat **symptomatic** hypocalcemia (seizures, tetany, significant QTc prolongation, hemodynamic compromise)
  - **Calcium gluconate 1-2g IV over 10-20 minutes** for symptomatic patients
  - **Use with extreme caution** — supplemental calcium in the setting of hyperphosphatemia increases the calcium-phosphate product, potentially worsening renal calcium-phosphate crystal deposition
  - Correct the hyperphosphatemia first when possible; calcium will normalize as phosphate falls
- Asymptomatic hypocalcemia in TLS: **monitor closely but do not aggressively replace**

### Acute Kidney Injury / Dialysis

Indications for renal replacement therapy in TLS:
- Refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >7 with ECG changes despite medical management)
- Severe hyperphosphatemia (>10 mg/dL) not responding to binders
- Volume overload unresponsive to diuretics
- Severe symptomatic uremia
- Oliguric or anuric AKI with rising potassium/phosphate

Continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) may be preferred over intermittent hemodialysis in hemodynamically unstable patients.

---
sections.nursing-assessment-and-management:
### Monitoring

- **Continuous cardiac monitoring** — watch for peaked T waves, widened QRS (hyperkalemia), prolonged QTc (hypocalcemia), dysrhythmias
- **Strict intake and output** — hourly urine output measurement via Foley catheter
- **Target UOP >2 mL/kg/hr** (or >80-100 mL/hr in adults) — the single most important nursing metric
- **Serial metabolic panels** — BMP q4-6h during active TLS (potassium, calcium, phosphate, creatinine, BUN)
- **Uric acid levels** — q6-12h (on ice if rasburicase given)
- **LDH** — marker of ongoing cell lysis
- **Daily weights** — fluid balance assessment
- **Neurological checks** — assess for altered mental status, seizure activity, neuromuscular irritability (Chvostek sign: facial twitch with tapping; Trousseau sign: carpal spasm with BP cuff inflation)

### IV Fluid Management

- Ensure patent, large-bore IV access (18g or larger preferred)
- Run maintenance fluids at 200 mL/hr (or per oncology protocol) — typically NS or D5 1/2NS
- **NO potassium** in ANY IV fluids — check every bag before hanging
- No lactated Ringer's (contains potassium)
- Monitor for fluid overload: lung sounds, JVD, peripheral edema, oxygen saturation
- May need diuretics (furosemide) to maintain UOP in the setting of aggressive hydration — but only after ensuring adequate intravascular volume
- Diuretics should NOT be used if patient is hypovolemic or obstructed

### Medication Administration

- **Rasburicase:** Infuse over 30 minutes. Do NOT give through the same line as other medications. Place uric acid labs on ice immediately after draw.
- **Allopurinol:** Verify dose reductions with concomitant 6-MP or azathioprine. Monitor for rash (rare but can be severe: Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
- **Phosphate binders:** Give WITH meals or snacks for maximum efficacy. Separate from other oral medications by 1-2 hours (binders interfere with drug absorption).
- **Calcium gluconate:** Give slowly over 10-20 minutes via peripheral or central line. Calcium chloride is 3x more concentrated but requires central access due to tissue necrosis risk with extravasation.

### Seizure Precautions

- Pad side rails, keep suction at bedside
- Low bed position with bed alarm
- Assess for prodromal signs: muscle twitching, confusion, visual changes, paresthesias
- If seizure occurs: protect airway, time the event, administer benzodiazepines per protocol
- Seizures in TLS are typically from hypocalcemia — check ionized calcium immediately

### Documentation

- Record all fluid intake (type, rate, total volume) and output (urine, drains, emesis) hourly
- Document serial lab trends in flow sheet format for easy visualization
- Note time of chemotherapy initiation relative to TLS onset
- Record all blood product or electrolyte replacements with times
- Document patient and family education about signs/symptoms to report

---
sections.complications:
- **Acute kidney injury** — most common serious complication; may require temporary or prolonged dialysis
- **Fatal cardiac arrhythmia** — from hyperkalemia or hypocalcemia; most immediately life-threatening
- **Seizures** — from hypocalcemia; may be the presenting symptom
- **Multiorgan failure** — from combined metabolic insults and AKI
- **DIC** — can co-occur with TLS, especially in acute leukemias
- **Delayed chemotherapy** — TLS may force treatment interruption, potentially compromising cancer outcomes
- **Fluid overload / pulmonary edema** — from aggressive hydration in the setting of AKI
- **Calcium-phosphate deposition** — nephrocalcinosis, soft tissue calcification

---
sections.key-cross-references:
- Hyperkalemia — Detailed management of hyperkalemia including ECG recognition and treatment algorithm
- Acute Kidney Injury — AKI classification, workup, and management including dialysis indications
- Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia — Highest-risk malignancy for TLS; treatment protocols that trigger TLS
- Multiple Myeloma — Hematologic malignancy with TLS risk, especially with high tumor burden
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — Principles of electrolyte homeostasis and renal handling
- Inflammation and Immune Response — Cytokine release and systemic inflammatory response in TLS
- Coagulation and Hemostasis — DIC as concurrent or complicating process

---

*Last updated: 2026-02-07*

clinicalPearls: **The cancer that kills before you can treat the cancer** — TLS can be fatal within hours of chemotherapy initiation. In spontaneous TLS, patients may present to the ED in extremis from a cancer they didn't even know they had. A young patient with a mediastinal mass, sky-high LDH, and hyperkalemia on their first BMP is TLS until proven otherwise.

**Hydrate aggressively -- urine output is your best friend** — The kidneys are the only exit route for uric acid, potassium, and phosphate. Maintaining UOP >2 mL/kg/hr is the single most important intervention. If urine output drops, escalate immediately.

**G6PD check before rasburicase** — Giving rasburicase to a G6PD-deficient patient can cause life-threatening hemolysis and methemoglobinemia. In emergencies where G6PD status is unknown, this must be a conscious risk-benefit discussion with oncology. In elective settings, screen first. Always.

**Don't chase the calcium unless the patient is symptomatic** — The instinct to replace low calcium is strong, but in TLS, supplemental calcium can worsen calcium-phosphate precipitation in the kidneys. Focus on lowering phosphate first. Only give calcium for seizures, significant QTc prolongation, or hemodynamic instability.

**Ice the uric acid sample** — After rasburicase administration, urate oxidase continues to work in the blood tube at room temperature. A uric acid level drawn into a room-temperature tube will be falsely low. Always place the sample on ice and send to the lab immediately.
**No potassium in the IV, period** — This seems obvious but is one of the most common near-miss events. Check every IV bag, every additive. LR contains 4 mEq/L of potassium and should not be used.

**Watch for spontaneous TLS** — Not all TLS follows chemotherapy. Spontaneous TLS from rapidly proliferating tumors can present as the initial emergency, before any cancer diagnosis is made. Have a high index of suspicion in any patient with unexplained hyperkalemia + hyperuricemia + renal failure.

type-1-diabetes-mellitus.title: Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus

sections.definition:
Type 1 diabetes mellitus is a chronic autoimmune disease characterized by T-cell-mediated destruction of pancreatic beta cells, resulting in absolute insulin deficiency. Without exogenous Insulin, patients cannot metabolize glucose and develop life-threatening Diabetic Ketoacidosis. Unlike type 2 diabetes, T1DM is not primarily driven by insulin resistance but by irreversible loss of insulin-producing capacity.

The disease progresses through three defined stages: Stage 1 (autoantibody-positive with normoglycemia), Stage 2 (autoantibody-positive with dysglycemia but asymptomatic), and Stage 3 (clinical diabetes with symptomatic hyperglycemia). This staging framework enables earlier identification and intervention before metabolic crisis.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** T1DM accounts for 10-15% of all diabetes cases worldwide. More than 500,000 children are living with T1DM globally, with approximately 90,000 new diagnoses per year in children. Incidence has been increasing at 2-3% annually over the past several decades.
- **Risk factors:** HLA-DR3-DQ2 and HLA-DR4-DQ8 haplotypes confer major genetic risk (~40-50% of genetic susceptibility). First-degree relatives have a 5-6% lifetime risk compared to 0.3% in the general population. Environmental triggers under investigation include enteroviral infections, early cow's milk exposure, and vitamin D deficiency.
- **Demographics:** Highest incidence in Scandinavia (Finland leads globally at ~60/100,000/year) and Sardinia. Bimodal age of onset with peaks at 4-6 years and 10-14 years. Increasingly recognized in adults — up to 50% of T1DM is diagnosed after age 20 (latent autoimmune diabetes of adults, LADA). Slight male predominance in populations with high incidence.
- **Mortality:** Life expectancy reduced by 8-13 years compared to general population. Leading causes of death are cardiovascular disease and diabetic kidney disease.
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Homeostasis and Feedback Loops — glucose sensing and insulin secretion form a classic negative feedback loop; T1DM destroys the sensor (beta cells). Cell Signaling and Receptor Biology — insulin receptor signaling (tyrosine kinase) is intact in T1DM; the problem is upstream: no insulin to bind.*

T1DM pathogenesis involves a complex interplay of genetic susceptibility, environmental triggers, and autoimmune destruction:

1. **Genetic predisposition:** HLA class II genes (particularly HLA-DR3-DQ2 and HLA-DR4-DQ8) account for approximately 50% of genetic susceptibility. Over 60 additional non-HLA loci contribute, including INS, PTPN22, and CTLA4.
2. **Environmental trigger:** A precipitating event (likely viral infection, especially Coxsackievirus B) initiates autoimmune activation in genetically susceptible individuals.
3. **Autoimmune activation:** Autoreactive CD4+ and CD8+ T cells infiltrate pancreatic islets (insulitis). Autoantibodies appear against key beta-cell antigens: anti-insulin (IAA), anti-GAD65, anti-IA2 (islet antigen 2), and anti-ZnT8 (zinc transporter 8). These autoantibodies are present months to years before clinical symptoms.
4. **Progressive beta-cell destruction:** Ongoing T-cell-mediated killing progressively depletes beta-cell mass. Clinical symptoms appear when approximately 80-90% of beta cells are destroyed.
5. **Absolute insulin deficiency:** Loss of insulin production eliminates glucose uptake by insulin-dependent tissues. Counter-regulatory hormones (glucagon, cortisol, catecholamines) are unopposed, driving hepatic gluconeogenesis and lipolysis. Free fatty acid oxidation produces ketone bodies, leading to Diabetic Ketoacidosis if untreated.
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
**Classic Presentation (Stage 3):**
- Polyuria (osmotic diuresis from glycosuria)
- Polydipsia (compensatory for fluid losses)
- Polyphagia with unintentional weight loss (catabolic state)
- Fatigue, blurred vision
- Onset typically acute over days to weeks

**At Diagnosis:**
- Approximately 30% of children present in Diabetic Ketoacidosis
- Blood glucose usually >250 mg/dL at presentation
- Dehydration, fruity breath (ketone odor), Kussmaul respirations

**Chronic Disease Features:**
- Glycemic variability — wide swings between hypo- and hyperglycemia
- Hypoglycemia unawareness (develops after years of disease due to autonomic neuropathy)
- "Honeymoon period" — partial remission lasting weeks to months after diagnosis when residual beta cells temporarily recover function

### Complications
**Acute:**
- Diabetic Ketoacidosis — most dangerous acute complication, mortality 0.15-0.3% in developed countries
- Severe hypoglycemia — seizures, loss of consciousness, cognitive impairment
- Cerebral edema during DKA treatment (primarily pediatric patients)

**Chronic Microvascular:**
- Diabetic retinopathy — leading cause of blindness in working-age adults
- Diabetic Kidney Disease — occurs in 20-40% of patients, major driver of ESRD
- Diabetic neuropathy — peripheral (symmetric distal polyneuropathy) and autonomic (gastroparesis, orthostatic hypotension, hypoglycemia unawareness)

**Chronic Macrovascular:**
- Cardiovascular disease — 2-4x increased risk of MI and stroke
- Peripheral arterial disease — contributes to diabetic foot ulcers and amputation
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
- **Fasting blood glucose:** ≥126 mg/dL
- **Random blood glucose:** ≥200 mg/dL with classic symptoms
- **HbA1c:** ≥6.5% (though may be normal early in rapid-onset disease)
- **C-peptide:** Low or undetectable (reflects endogenous insulin production; distinguishes T1DM from T2DM)
- **Autoantibodies:** Anti-GAD65 (most sensitive in adults), anti-IA2, anti-insulin (most useful in young children), anti-ZnT8. Presence of ≥2 autoantibodies confers nearly 100% lifetime risk of progression to clinical diabetes.
- **Basic metabolic panel:** Assess for DKA — glucose, bicarbonate, anion gap, potassium
- **Beta-hydroxybutyrate:** Elevated in ketosis/DKA (>3 mmol/L in DKA)
- **Urinalysis:** Glycosuria, ketonuria

### Diagnostic Criteria
**Staging (TrialNet/ADA):**
| Stage | Autoantibodies | Glycemia | Symptoms |
|-------|---------------|----------|----------|
| 1 | ≥2 autoantibodies | Normoglycemia | None |
| 2 | ≥2 autoantibodies | Dysglycemia (impaired fasting/tolerance) | None |
| 3 | ± autoantibodies | Hyperglycemia meeting diabetes criteria | Present |

**Distinguishing from T2DM:** Young age, lean body habitus, acute onset, presence of autoantibodies, low C-peptide, ketosis-prone. In adults, suspect LADA if autoantibody-positive, initially insulin-independent, progresses to insulin dependence within 6 years.
sections.management:
### Acute
- **DKA management:** See Diabetic Ketoacidosis — IV fluids, IV insulin drip, potassium replacement, monitoring
- **New diagnosis stabilization:** Start Insulin therapy immediately at diagnosis. Initial total daily dose ~0.5 units/kg/day (reduce if significant residual beta-cell function during honeymoon)
- **Hypoglycemia treatment:** Rule of 15 — 15g fast-acting carbs, recheck in 15 minutes. Glucagon IM/IN for severe hypoglycemia with altered consciousness

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Intensive insulin therapy:** Basal-bolus regimen via multiple daily injections (MDI) or continuous subcutaneous insulin infusion (CSII/insulin pump). Basal: long-acting insulin (glargine, detemir, degludec). Bolus: rapid-acting insulin (lispro, aspart, glulisine) with meals based on carbohydrate counting and correction factors.
- **Glucose monitoring:** Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) is standard of care — reduces HbA1c and hypoglycemia compared to fingerstick alone. Target time-in-range (70-180 mg/dL) >70%.
- **Hybrid closed-loop systems:** Automated insulin delivery (AID) systems integrate CGM with pump, adjusting basal rates algorithmically. Shown to improve time-in-range and reduce hypoglycemia.
- **HbA1c targets:** <7% for most adults (ADA). <7.5% for pediatric patients. Individualize based on hypoglycemia risk and life expectancy.
- **Screening for complications:** Annual dilated eye exam (retinopathy), annual urine albumin-to-creatinine ratio (nephropathy), annual foot exam (neuropathy), lipid panel, blood pressure monitoring
- **Cardiovascular risk reduction:** Statins for patients ≥40 years or with additional risk factors. ACE Inhibitors or ARBs for albuminuria or hypertension.

### Pharmacology
- Insulin — Absolute requirement for survival. Basal-bolus therapy or pump. See insulin note for detailed pharmacokinetics.
- **Teplizumab (anti-CD3 monoclonal antibody)** — FDA-approved to delay onset of Stage 3 T1DM by approximately 2 years in Stage 2 patients. First disease-modifying therapy for T1DM.
- ACE Inhibitors/ARBs — Renoprotective in Diabetic Kidney Disease, first-line for hypertension
- Statins — Cardiovascular risk reduction, indicated for most adults with T1DM
- **Pramlintide (amylin analog)** — Adjunct to insulin, reduces postprandial glucose excursions and promotes satiety. Limited use due to injection burden and GI side effects.
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Blood glucose monitoring is the foundation of T1DM care.** Teach patients CGM use, calibration, and alarm management. For inpatient settings, check glucose every 4-6 hours at minimum, before meals and at bedtime. Report BG <70 or >300 mg/dL immediately.
- **Never hold basal insulin in a T1DM patient.** Unlike T2DM patients who can tolerate NPO without insulin, T1DM patients have zero endogenous insulin production. Holding basal insulin will precipitate DKA within hours. Reduce dose (typically by 20-30%) for NPO status but never discontinue entirely.
- **Sick day management education:** Patients must continue insulin during illness even if not eating. Increase glucose monitoring frequency to every 2-4 hours. Check ketones (blood or urine) when BG >250 mg/dL. Push fluids. Establish "sick day rules" before discharge.
- **Insulin pump troubleshooting:** Know how to identify pump malfunction (unexplained hyperglycemia, site issues). If pump fails, immediately start subcutaneous injections — a pump patient without insulin delivery is in a DKA-prone state. Keep backup insulin pens available.
- **Hypoglycemia preparedness:** Ensure glucose tablets or juice at bedside at all times. Verify glucagon emergency kit is prescribed, available, and caregivers know how to administer. Recognize that hypoglycemia unawareness (no adrenergic symptoms) develops after years of disease.
- **Psychosocial support:** T1DM is a relentless, 24/7 disease. Screen for diabetes distress, burnout, depression (2-3x increased prevalence), and eating disorders (especially in adolescent females — insulin restriction for weight loss, "diabulimia"). Refer to diabetes educator and mental health resources.
- **Inpatient insulin management:** Do not use sliding-scale-only regimens for T1DM patients. They require scheduled basal-bolus insulin. Coordinate with endocrinology for patients on pumps or complex regimens.
sections.keyPoints:
- T1DM results from autoimmune destruction of pancreatic beta cells, causing absolute insulin deficiency requiring lifelong exogenous Insulin
- Accounts for 10-15% of all diabetes; >500,000 children living with T1DM globally with ~90,000 new diagnoses per year
- Four autoantibodies (anti-GAD65, anti-IA2, anti-insulin, anti-ZnT8) appear months to years before clinical disease; ≥2 autoantibodies confers near-100% lifetime risk
- Three-stage model enables pre-symptomatic identification: Stage 1 (autoantibodies, normoglycemia), Stage 2 (dysglycemia), Stage 3 (clinical diabetes)
- Teplizumab (anti-CD3) is the first disease-modifying therapy, delaying Stage 3 onset by approximately 2 years
- Intensive insulin therapy (MDI or pump) with CGM and hybrid closed-loop systems is the standard of care
- DKA remains the most dangerous acute complication; approximately 30% of children present in DKA at diagnosis
- Chronic microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) are driven by cumulative hyperglycemic exposure

clinicalPearls: **Never stop basal insulin in a T1DM patient, even if they are NPO or vomiting.** The number one cause of inpatient DKA is inappropriate discontinuation of basal insulin. These patients make zero insulin. Reduce the dose, but never hold it entirely.

**A T1DM patient with "normal" blood glucose can still be developing DKA** — SGLT2 inhibitor use (increasingly off-label in T1DM) can cause euglycemic DKA with glucose <250 mg/dL. If the patient has metabolic acidosis and ketonemia, it is DKA regardless of the glucose number.

**The honeymoon period is a trap for complacency.** Newly diagnosed patients who enter partial remission with minimal insulin needs will inevitably lose remaining beta-cell function. Use this window to solidify self-management skills, not to relax monitoring.

**Hypoglycemia unawareness is an underrecognized danger.** After years of T1DM, patients lose counter-regulatory hormone responses and no longer feel low blood sugars. A patient who says "I don't really feel my lows anymore" needs CGM with alarms, a glucagon kit, and education of household contacts.

**Know the difference between the insulin pump patient who is hyperglycemic and the one who is acidotic.** Hyperglycemia alone may be a site issue — change the infusion set. Hyperglycemia with ketones means the pump has been failing long enough for ketogenesis to begin. That patient needs subcutaneous injection of rapid-acting insulin immediately, not a pump troubleshoot.
