urticaria-hives.title: Urticaria (Hives)

sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Inflammation and Immune Response — IgE-mediated urticaria: antigen cross-links IgE on mast cells → degranulation → histamine, tryptase, leukotrienes → transient dermal edema (wheal) and surrounding erythema (flare); resolves within 24 hours as mediators are cleared.*

### Mast Cell Degranulation — The Central Event
1. **Trigger** activates cutaneous mast cells (IgE-mediated, direct, or autoimmune)
2. Mast cells release **preformed mediators** from granules:
   - **Histamine** — the dominant mediator → vasodilation, increased vascular permeability, pruritus, smooth muscle contraction
   - Tryptase, chymase, heparin
3. Mast cells also synthesize **new mediators:**
   - Prostaglandins (PGD₂), leukotrienes (LTC₄, LTD₄)
   - Cytokines (TNF-α, IL-4, IL-5)
4. Result in superficial dermis: **vasodilation + plasma leakage → wheal** (raised, erythematous, edematous)
5. Result in deep dermis/subcutis: **angioedema** (deeper, diffuse, non-pitting swelling)

### Histamine Receptors in Urticaria
| Receptor | Location | Effect | Clinical Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **H1** | Skin, smooth muscle, endothelium | Pruritus, vasodilation, bronchoconstriction | First-line antihistamines target this |
| **H2** | Gastric parietal cells, vascular smooth muscle | Vasodilation, gastric acid | H2 blockers as adjunct for refractory urticaria |
| **H4** | Immune cells (eosinophils, mast cells) | Chemotaxis, immune regulation | Emerging therapeutic target |

---
sections.classification:
### Acute Urticaria (<6 weeks)
- Most common form; affects ~20% of people at some point in life
- **Identifiable trigger found in ~50%:**
  - **Foods:** Shellfish, nuts, eggs, milk, soy, wheat
  - **Medications:** Antibiotics (penicillins, cephalosporins), NSAIDs, opioids (direct mast cell degranulation), ACE inhibitors (bradykinin-mediated angioedema)
  - **Infections:** Viral URIs (very common trigger in children), hepatitis, EBV
  - **Insect stings:** Hymenoptera (bees, wasps)
  - **Latex, physical stimuli**
- **Other 50%:** Idiopathic — no trigger identified
- Self-limited; usually resolves within days to weeks

### Chronic Urticaria (≥6 weeks)
- **Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU)** — daily or near-daily hives with no identifiable external trigger
  - **~40-50% are autoimmune** — IgG autoantibodies against IgE or the high-affinity IgE receptor (FcεRI) on mast cells → chronic activation
  - Frustrating for patients — often years-long course
- **Chronic inducible urticaria** — triggered by specific physical stimuli:
  - **Dermatographism** — wheals from skin scratching/pressure (most common)
  - **Cold urticaria** — hives from cold exposure (swimming in cold water can trigger anaphylaxis)
  - **Cholinergic urticaria** — tiny punctate wheals from sweating/heat/exercise
  - **Pressure urticaria** — delayed swelling from sustained pressure (tight clothing, prolonged sitting)
  - **Solar urticaria** — sun exposure

---
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Wheals (Hives)
- Raised, erythematous, well-circumscribed plaques
- **Intensely pruritic** (not painful — if painful, think urticarial vasculitis)
- **Blanch with pressure** (unlike purpura)
- Variable size — from small papules to large plaques
- **Each individual wheal lasts <24 hours** — the single most important diagnostic feature
  - If a lesion lasts >24 hours in the same location → biopsy to rule out **urticarial vasculitis**
- Can occur anywhere on the body
- Often migratory — appear in one location, resolve, appear elsewhere

### Angioedema
- Non-pitting swelling of subcutaneous/submucosal tissue
- **Lips, eyelids, tongue, genitalia** most common
- Usually asymmetric
- Not typically pruritic — more often described as burning or pressure
- Resolves over 24-72 hours
- Can coexist with urticaria (50% of urticaria patients also have angioedema)

### Angioedema WITHOUT Urticaria — Special Consideration
If angioedema occurs **without hives**, consider:
| Type | Mechanism | Key Features |
|---|---|---|
| **ACE inhibitor angioedema** | Bradykinin accumulation (ACE normally degrades bradykinin) | Can occur months-years into therapy; does NOT respond to epinephrine/antihistamines; higher incidence in Black patients |
| **Hereditary angioedema (HAE)** | C1-esterase inhibitor deficiency → uncontrolled bradykinin | Family history; recurrent episodes; NO urticaria ever; abdominal attacks; C4 level low (screening test) |

> [!critical]
> **ACE inhibitor angioedema does NOT respond to antihistamines, steroids, or epinephrine** — it is bradykinin-mediated, not histamine-mediated. Treatment: stop the ACE inhibitor permanently; for acute attacks, icatibant (bradykinin receptor antagonist) or C1-INH concentrate. Epinephrine may help mechanically via vasoconstriction but does not address the cause.

---
sections.when-to-worry-urticaria-vs-anaphylaxis:
**Urticaria alone** (hives only, no systemic symptoms) → NOT anaphylaxis

**Urticaria + ANY of the following = possible anaphylaxis** → see Anaphylaxis:
- Respiratory symptoms (dyspnea, wheezing, stridor, throat tightness)
- Cardiovascular symptoms (hypotension, syncope, tachycardia)
- GI symptoms (severe abdominal pain, vomiting, diarrhea)
- Altered mental status

---
sections.diagnosis:
- **Clinical diagnosis** — history and exam are usually sufficient
- No routine lab workup needed for acute urticaria
- **Chronic urticaria** basic workup: CBC with differential, ESR/CRP, TSH (autoimmune thyroid disease association), hepatitis panel if risk factors
- **Allergy testing** (skin prick, specific IgE) — only if clear trigger suspected; NOT useful for chronic spontaneous urticaria
- **Biopsy** — only if wheals last >24 hours, leave bruising, or are painful (rule out urticarial vasculitis)
- **C4 level** — screening test for hereditary angioedema (C4 is low even between attacks)

---
sections.management:
### Acute Urticaria (ED Treatment)
| Severity | Treatment |
|---|---|
| **Mild** (localized hives, no angioedema) | **Cetirizine 10 mg** or **diphenhydramine 25-50 mg** PO; discharge with 2nd-gen antihistamine daily × 5-7 days |
| **Moderate** (widespread hives ± mild angioedema) | **Diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV** + **famotidine 20 mg IV** (H2 adjunct); consider **methylprednisolone 125 mg IV** or prednisone 40-60 mg PO |
| **Severe** (significant angioedema, throat involvement) | See Anaphylaxis pathway; **epinephrine** if any airway concern |

### Second-Generation Antihistamines (First-Line for All Urticaria)
| Drug | Dose | Advantages |
|---|---|---|
| **Cetirizine** (Zyrtec) | 10 mg PO daily (can updose to 20-40 mg for chronic) | Fast onset; most effective H1 blocker; mildly sedating |
| **Loratadine** (Claritin) | 10 mg PO daily | Non-sedating |
| **Fexofenadine** (Allegra) | 180 mg PO daily | Non-sedating; no cardiac effects |

> [!note]
> **High-dose 2nd-gen antihistamines** (up to 4× standard dose) are recommended for chronic urticaria before adding other agents. Cetirizine 40 mg/day is safe and effective for many patients who don't respond to 10 mg.

### Chronic Urticaria Step Therapy
1. **2nd-gen H1 antihistamine** at standard dose
2. **Updose to 4× standard** (e.g., cetirizine 40 mg/day)
3. **Add H2 blocker** (famotidine) + **leukotriene receptor antagonist** (montelukast)
4. **Omalizumab** (Xolair) — anti-IgE monoclonal antibody; highly effective for CSU; administered SC monthly
5. **Cyclosporine** — for refractory cases; immunosuppressive; significant side effect monitoring

### Medications to AVOID in Urticaria
- **NSAIDs** — can worsen urticaria in ~30% of chronic urticaria patients (COX-1 inhibition shifts arachidonic acid to leukotriene pathway)
- **Opioids** — direct mast cell degranulators; avoid if possible
- **ACE inhibitors** — if angioedema is present, discontinue and switch to ARB (although ~10% cross-reactivity reported)

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Assessment
- **Full skin exam** — document distribution, individual lesion size, presence of excoriations
- **Check for angioedema** — examine lips, tongue, uvula, hands, feet, genitalia
- **Vital signs** — tachycardia and hypotension suggest anaphylaxis, not simple urticaria
- **Airway assessment** — voice changes, drooling, difficulty swallowing → emergent
- **Medication reconciliation** — new medications? ACE inhibitors? NSAIDs? Antibiotics?
- **Recent exposures** — foods (within 2-4 hours), insects, new products

### Patient Education
- **Antihistamines work best taken regularly, not PRN** — for chronic urticaria, take daily regardless of symptoms
- **Avoid identified triggers** — food diary if food-related
- **Avoid aspirin/NSAIDs** if they worsen hives
- **When to return:** throat tightness, difficulty breathing, lip/tongue swelling, dizziness, fainting → call 911
- **Expect unpredictability** — chronic urticaria waxes and wanes; resolution may take months to years
- Cool compresses and loose clothing for comfort

---
sections.references:
- Zuberbier et al., "The International EAACI/GA²LEN/EuroGuiDerm/APAAACI Guideline for the Definition, Classification, Diagnosis, and Management of Urticaria," Allergy, 2022
- Bernstein et al., "The Diagnosis and Management of Acute and Chronic Urticaria: 2014 Update," Journal of Allergy and Clinical Immunology, 2014
- Maurer et al., "Omalizumab for the Treatment of Chronic Idiopathic or Spontaneous Urticaria," New England Journal of Medicine, 2013

clinicalPearls: **Each wheal lasts <24 hours — if a lesion stays >24 hours, it's not simple urticaria** — biopsy for urticarial vasculitis; this is the single most important historical question
**Angioedema without hives + ACE inhibitor = stop the ACE inhibitor forever** — bradykinin-mediated; antihistamines and steroids won't help; can recur even years after starting the drug
**Urticaria alone is NOT anaphylaxis** — hives without respiratory, cardiovascular, or GI symptoms are uncomfortable but not life-threatening; don't over-triage
**NSAIDs make ~30% of chronic urticaria worse** — COX-1 inhibition diverts arachidonic acid metabolism toward leukotrienes; switch to acetaminophen
**Updose before you add** — cetirizine 40 mg/day (4× standard) is the recommended step before adding H2 blockers, montelukast, or omalizumab; most patients never need escalation

**Cold urticaria + cold water immersion = anaphylaxis risk** — a patient with cold urticaria who jumps into a cold lake can have massive mast cell degranulation → cardiovascular collapse; counsel about swimming safety
**C4 is your HAE screening test** — if angioedema recurs without hives and there's no ACE inhibitor, check C4; it's low even between attacks in hereditary angioedema

signs: Raised, erythematous, well-circumscribed plaques
**Intensely pruritic** (not painful — if painful, think urticarial vasculitis)
**Blanch with pressure** (unlike purpura)
Variable size — from small papules to large plaques
**Each individual wheal lasts <24 hours** — the single most important diagnostic feature
If a lesion lasts >24 hours in the same location → biopsy to rule out **urticarial vasculitis**
Can occur anywhere on the body
Often migratory — appear in one location, resolve, appear elsewhere
Non-pitting swelling of subcutaneous/submucosal tissue
**Lips, eyelids, tongue, genitalia** most common
Usually asymmetric
Not typically pruritic — more often described as burning or pressure
Resolves over 24-72 hours
Can coexist with urticaria (50% of urticaria patients also have angioedema)

vitamin-b12-deficiency.title: Vitamin B12 Deficiency
vitamin-b12-deficiency.system: Lymphatic and Immune System, Nervous System

sections.definition:
Vitamin B12 (cobalamin) deficiency is a common and potentially serious nutritional and metabolic disorder resulting from inadequate B12 intake, impaired absorption, or increased utilization. B12 is an essential cofactor for two critical enzymatic reactions: DNA synthesis (thymidylate pathway) and myelin maintenance (methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA conversion). Deficiency produces a characteristic combination of hematologic (megaloblastic anemia) and neurologic (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord) manifestations that can become irreversible if not recognized and treated promptly.

The most important clinical principle: neurologic damage from B12 deficiency can progress silently and irreversibly, even while the hematologic picture appears normal — especially if folate is supplemented without B12 correction.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** Affects approximately 6% of adults under 60 and nearly 20% of adults over 60 in the US and UK; subclinical deficiency (low-normal B12 with elevated MMA) is far more common, affecting up to 40% of older adults; pernicious anemia affects ~2% of adults over 60; prevalence is higher in developing countries where dietary B12 sources are limited
- **Risk factors:** Age >60 (decreased gastric acid → impaired B12 liberation from food), pernicious anemia (autoimmune), strict vegan or vegetarian diet (B12 found almost exclusively in animal products), chronic proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use (>2 years), chronic metformin use (10-30% develop deficiency), H. pylori infection (destroys parietal cells), chronic alcoholism, prior gastric surgery (gastrectomy, bariatric surgery — especially Roux-en-Y), terminal ileum disease or resection (Crohn's disease), pancreatic insufficiency, tapeworm infection (Diphyllobothrium latum), nitrous oxide abuse (oxidizes B12 → inactivation)
- **Demographics:** Pernicious anemia more common in Northern European ancestry, women > men, and associated with other autoimmune disorders (autoimmune thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes, vitiligo, Addison disease); dietary deficiency most common in South Asian, African, and Central American populations where meat consumption is limited; increasing prevalence in elderly due to widespread PPI and metformin use
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Nutrition and Metabolism — B12 is a coenzyme for methionine synthase (homocysteine → methionine; regenerates tetrahydrofolate for DNA synthesis) and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (odd-chain fatty acid metabolism; essential for myelin maintenance); these two enzymatic roles explain why deficiency causes both megaloblastic anemia and subacute combined spinal cord degeneration independently*
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — B12 deficiency traps folate as methyltetrahydrofolate, blocking thymidylate synthesis; rapidly dividing cells (erythroid precursors, GI mucosa) fail S-phase — producing megaloblasts with RNA-rich cytoplasm but impaired nuclear maturation; folate supplementation bypasses the block and corrects anemia but leaves the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway (myelin) uncorrected*

### B12 Absorption (Normal)
1. Dietary B12 (bound to animal proteins) enters stomach → gastric acid and pepsin liberate B12 from food proteins
2. Free B12 binds to R-protein (haptocorrin) secreted by salivary glands
3. In duodenum, pancreatic proteases degrade R-protein → release B12
4. Free B12 binds to intrinsic factor (IF), produced by gastric parietal cells
5. B12-IF complex travels to terminal ileum → absorbed by cubam receptor (cubilin-amnionless complex)
6. Absorbed B12 enters portal circulation bound to transcobalamin II → delivered to tissues
7. Body stores 2-5 mg of B12 (primarily in liver) → sufficient for 3-5 years even with complete cessation of intake → deficiency develops insidiously

### How Deficiency Causes Disease
**Pathway 1 — DNA Synthesis (Hematologic Effects):**
- B12 is a cofactor for methionine synthase → converts homocysteine → methionine + regenerates tetrahydrofolate (THF)
- Without B12 → THF is "trapped" as methyltetrahydrofolate (methyl-folate trap) → unavailable for thymidylate synthesis → impaired DNA synthesis
- Result: cells cannot complete S phase → nuclear maturation lags behind cytoplasmic maturation → megaloblastic change (large immature cells with immature nuclei)
- Affects all rapidly dividing cells: erythroid precursors (megaloblastic anemia, macro-ovalocytes), granulocyte precursors (hypersegmented neutrophils), and GI epithelium (glossitis, malabsorption)
- This pathway is shared with folate → folate supplementation can bypass the block and correct anemia → BUT does NOT correct the neurologic damage → this is the "masking" danger

**Pathway 2 — Myelin Maintenance (Neurologic Effects):**
- B12 is a cofactor for methylmalonyl-CoA mutase → converts methylmalonyl-CoA → succinyl-CoA (enters TCA cycle)
- Without B12 → methylmalonyl-CoA accumulates → converted to abnormal odd-chain fatty acids → incorporated into myelin → destabilizes myelin sheaths → demyelination
- Also: homocysteine accumulation → direct neurotoxicity, impaired methylation of myelin basic protein
- Result: subacute combined degeneration — demyelination of posterior columns (proprioception, vibration) and lateral corticospinal tracts (motor) of the spinal cord
- This pathway is INDEPENDENT of folate → folate cannot compensate → neurologic damage progresses even if anemia is corrected with folate alone

### Causes of Deficiency
| Mechanism | Specific Causes |
|-----------|----------------|
| **Decreased intake** | Strict vegan/vegetarian diet (no meat, dairy, eggs); malnutrition; alcoholism |
| **Impaired liberation from food** | Achlorhydria (aging, PPI use >2 years, H2 blockers); H. pylori gastritis |
| **Impaired IF production** | Pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells — most common cause of severe B12 deficiency); atrophic gastritis; gastrectomy |
| **Impaired ileal absorption** | Crohn's disease (terminal ileum involvement); ileal resection; bacterial overgrowth (SIBO — bacteria consume B12); tropical sprue; celiac disease |
| **Increased utilization/loss** | Diphyllobothrium latum (fish tapeworm — competes for B12 in gut lumen); pregnancy; hyperthyroidism |
| **Drug-induced** | Metformin (impairs calcium-dependent B12-IF absorption in ileum; 10-30% develop deficiency); PPIs (reduce acid → impaired food-B12 liberation); colchicine; neomycin |
| **B12 inactivation** | Nitrous oxide (oxidizes cobalt in B12 → irreversible inactivation; recreational "whippet" abuse or repeated surgical anesthesia in deficient patients can precipitate acute neurologic crisis) |
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Hematologic Manifestations
- **Megaloblastic anemia:** Macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL, often >110 fL); macro-ovalocytes on peripheral smear; fatigue, weakness, dyspnea on exertion, pallor
- **Hypersegmented neutrophils:** ≥5 lobes in ≥5% of neutrophils, or any neutrophil with ≥6 lobes — earliest and most specific morphologic finding of megaloblastic anemia; appears before anemia develops
- **Pancytopenia:** In severe cases — ineffective hematopoiesis affects all cell lines; thrombocytopenia (usually mild), leukopenia
- **Ineffective erythropoiesis:** Intramedullary hemolysis of megaloblastic precursors → elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin (can be markedly elevated, mimicking hemolytic anemia); low haptoglobin; reticulocyte count inappropriately low for degree of anemia
- **Glossitis:** Smooth, beefy red tongue (loss of filiform papillae) — classic but not always present
- **Angular cheilitis**

### Neurologic Manifestations
- **Subacute combined degeneration (SCD) of spinal cord:**
  - **Posterior column dysfunction:** Loss of vibration sense and proprioception → sensory ataxia (positive Romberg sign), wide-based unsteady gait; diminished joint position sense in toes; typically starts in lower extremities and ascends
  - **Lateral corticospinal tract dysfunction:** Upper motor neuron signs — spasticity, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski sign, weakness
  - The combination of absent ankle reflexes (peripheral neuropathy) with positive Babinski sign (upper motor neuron lesion) is a classic clue to B12 myelopathy
- **Peripheral neuropathy:** Symmetric, distal, "stocking-glove" pattern; paresthesias (tingling, numbness, "pins and needles") — often the earliest neurologic symptom; begins in feet → hands
- **Cognitive and psychiatric manifestations:**
  - Memory impairment, difficulty concentrating, confusion
  - Dementia (reversible if caught early, irreversible if prolonged)
  - Depression, irritability, personality changes
  - Psychosis ("megaloblastic madness") — rare but well-described
- **Autonomic dysfunction:** Orthostatic hypotension, bladder dysfunction (urinary retention or incontinence), impotence
- **Optic neuropathy:** Bilateral visual loss — rare

### Critical Clinical Point
Neurologic manifestations can occur WITHOUT anemia — up to 25-30% of patients with B12-related neurologic disease have a normal MCV and normal hemoglobin. Absence of anemia does NOT rule out clinically significant B12 deficiency. Always check B12/MMA in patients with unexplained neuropathy, myelopathy, or cognitive decline regardless of CBC findings.
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **Serum B12 level** | <200 pg/mL (148 pmol/L) = definitive deficiency; 200-300 pg/mL = borderline/indeterminate (need further workup); >300 pg/mL generally rules out deficiency; can be falsely normal in liver disease, myeloproliferative disorders |
| **Methylmalonic acid (MMA)** | ELEVATED in B12 deficiency (>0.4 umol/L); MORE SPECIFIC than serum B12; the single best confirmatory test; normal MMA essentially rules out tissue-level B12 deficiency; can be elevated in renal insufficiency (false positive) |
| **Homocysteine** | ELEVATED in B12 deficiency; LESS SPECIFIC — also elevated in folate deficiency, vitamin B6 deficiency, renal insufficiency, hypothyroidism; useful when combined with MMA |
| **CBC with differential** | Macrocytic anemia (MCV >100 fL); may show pancytopenia in severe cases; reticulocyte count low or inappropriately normal |
| **Peripheral blood smear** | Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes), macro-ovalocytes, anisocytosis, poikilocytosis; hypersegmented neutrophils are the EARLIEST morphologic finding |
| **Reticulocyte count** | Low or inappropriately normal (ineffective erythropoiesis); rises briskly after B12 replacement (reticulocyte peak at days 5-7 confirms diagnosis) |
| **LDH and indirect bilirubin** | Markedly elevated from intramedullary hemolysis (LDH can be >1000 U/L, sometimes extremely high — mimicking TTP or hemolysis) |
| **Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies** | Specific for pernicious anemia (sensitivity 50-70%, specificity >95%); positive result is diagnostic; negative does not exclude PA |
| **Anti-parietal cell antibodies** | Sensitive (~90%) but not specific for pernicious anemia (also positive in atrophic gastritis, other autoimmune conditions) |
| **Holotranscobalamin (holoTC)** | Active form of B12; earliest marker of deficiency; not widely available; may replace serum B12 as first-line test in future |

### Diagnostic Algorithm
1. **High clinical suspicion** (macrocytic anemia, neuropathy, cognitive decline, risk factors) → check serum B12
2. **B12 <200 pg/mL:** Deficiency confirmed → treat and determine cause
3. **B12 200-300 pg/mL (borderline):** Check MMA (and homocysteine) → elevated MMA confirms tissue-level deficiency → treat
4. **Determine etiology:** Anti-IF antibodies (pernicious anemia); dietary history; medication review (PPI, metformin); evaluate for malabsorption (Crohn's, celiac, SIBO)

### Distinguishing B12 from Folate Deficiency
| Feature | B12 Deficiency | Folate Deficiency |
|---------|---------------|------------------|
| MMA | ELEVATED | Normal |
| Homocysteine | Elevated | Elevated |
| Neurologic findings | YES (can be severe) | NO (or very rarely) |
| Peripheral smear | Identical (hypersegmented neutrophils, macro-ovalocytes) | Identical |
| Serum B12 | Low | Normal |
| Serum/RBC folate | Normal or elevated (methyl-folate trap) | Low |
sections.management:
### Acute Replacement
- **Pernicious anemia or severe deficiency with neurologic symptoms:**
  - IM cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg daily for 7 days → weekly for 4 weeks → monthly for life (indefinite)
  - Neurologic recovery depends on duration before treatment: symptoms present <6 months → good recovery expected; symptoms >6-12 months → partial or no recovery; irreversible damage if severely prolonged
  - Expect reticulocyte peak at days 5-7 (confirms diagnosis and response); hemoglobin normalization over 6-8 weeks

- **Dietary deficiency (no malabsorption):**
  - High-dose oral cyanocobalamin 1000-2000 mcg daily — evidence supports oral route is effective even in pernicious anemia (1% absorbed via passive diffusion independent of IF), though IM route ensures compliance and rapid repletion
  - Dietary counseling: fortified cereals, nutritional yeast, B12 supplements for vegans/vegetarians
  - Duration: lifelong supplementation if cause is not correctable

- **Drug-induced (metformin, PPI):**
  - Oral B12 1000 mcg daily while continuing the causative medication if indicated
  - Annual B12 monitoring for all patients on chronic metformin or PPI therapy
  - Consider switching from PPI to H2 blocker if clinically appropriate

### Monitoring Response to Treatment
| Timepoint | Expected Response |
|-----------|------------------|
| **Days 3-5** | Improvement in well-being, appetite |
| **Days 5-7** | Reticulocyte peak (reticulocytosis — confirms diagnosis) |
| **Week 2** | Hemoglobin begins to rise; hypersegmented neutrophils disappear |
| **Week 8** | Hemoglobin normalized; MCV normalizes |
| **Months 3-6** | Neurologic symptoms begin to improve (if recoverable) |
| **Months 6-12** | Maximum neurologic recovery achieved; residual deficits are likely permanent |

### Critical Management Points
- **ALWAYS check and correct B12 BEFORE starting folate supplementation.** Folate corrects the megaloblastic anemia (bypasses the methyl-folate trap) → hematologic parameters normalize → clinician assumes problem is solved → BUT neurologic damage continues to progress silently because folate does NOT participate in the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway. This is one of the most dangerous masking effects in medicine.
- **Hypokalemia risk during treatment:** Rapid correction of megaloblastic anemia → vigorous erythropoiesis → potassium shifts into new red blood cells → potentially severe hypokalemia within first 48 hours of treatment. Monitor potassium daily for the first week, especially in patients with severe anemia (Hb <7 g/dL). Supplement potassium as needed.
- **Do not transfuse reflexively for severe megaloblastic anemia.** These patients have chronically adapted to low hemoglobin. Transfusion may precipitate volume overload and heart failure. If transfusion is necessary (hemodynamic instability, Hb <5 g/dL with symptoms), give slowly (1 unit over 3-4 hours) with concurrent furosemide.

### Pharmacology
- **PPIs** — chronic PPI use (>2 years) reduces gastric acid, impairing liberation of B12 from food proteins; a major and increasingly common cause of B12 deficiency; consider annual B12 monitoring in all chronic PPI users
- **Blood Products and Transfusion** — for severe symptomatic megaloblastic anemia (Hb <5 g/dL with hemodynamic instability); transfuse cautiously (1 unit over 3-4 hours with furosemide) as chronically adapted patients risk volume overload
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Injection teaching:**
  - IM cyanocobalamin: teach self-injection technique for lifelong monthly maintenance (deltoid or vastus lateralis)
  - Rotate injection sites; proper disposal of sharps
  - For patients unable to self-inject: arrange home health nursing or clinic visits
  - Document site, lot number, and patient tolerance
- **Neurologic assessment and safety:**
  - Baseline and ongoing neurologic assessment: test vibration sense (tuning fork on great toe), proprioception (Romberg test), gait, deep tendon reflexes
  - Fall risk assessment — patients with posterior column disease have impaired proprioception and balance, especially in dark environments
  - Implement fall prevention: non-skid footwear, bed alarm, clear pathways, nightlights, assist with ambulation
  - Educate patient that neurologic recovery is slow (months) and may be incomplete — set realistic expectations
- **Monitoring during acute treatment:**
  - Daily potassium for first week (hypokalemia from reticulocytosis)
  - CBC with reticulocyte count at day 5-7 (confirm response), then weekly until hemoglobin normalizes
  - Monitor for fluid overload if severely anemic patient is receiving IV fluids or transfusion
- **Medication review:**
  - Identify contributing medications: metformin, PPIs, H2 blockers, colchicine
  - Advocate for annual B12 screening in all patients on chronic metformin (per ADA guidelines) or chronic PPI therapy
  - Verify patient is NOT receiving folate alone without B12 — check medication reconciliation carefully
- **Dietary education:**
  - B12 sources: meat, fish, poultry, eggs, dairy, fortified cereals, nutritional yeast
  - Strict vegans require lifelong supplementation — dietary sources alone are insufficient
  - B12 is heat-stable but may be reduced by prolonged cooking
  - Elderly patients: food-bound B12 may not be absorbed due to achlorhydria → crystalline B12 (supplements) is absorbed normally → supplement even if diet seems adequate
- **Pernicious anemia education:**
  - Lifelong monthly injections are required — stopping leads to recurrence within 1-3 years as stores deplete
  - Associated autoimmune conditions: screen for hypothyroidism (TSH annually), type 1 diabetes, vitiligo
  - Increased risk of gastric carcinoid tumors and gastric adenocarcinoma — some guidelines recommend endoscopic surveillance, though this remains debated
  - Genetic counseling: family members of PA patients have increased risk
sections.keyPoints:
- B12 deficiency is common, insidious (3-5 year body stores delay presentation), and potentially devastating if neurologic damage becomes irreversible
- Pernicious anemia (autoimmune destruction of parietal cells) is the most common cause of severe B12 deficiency; other major causes include dietary insufficiency (vegans), chronic PPI/metformin use, gastric surgery, and terminal ileum disease (Crohn's)
- B12 is required for two enzymatic pathways: DNA synthesis (shared with folate → megaloblastic anemia) and myelin maintenance (independent of folate → neurologic damage)
- Hematologic findings include macrocytic anemia (MCV >100), hypersegmented neutrophils, pancytopenia, and markedly elevated LDH from ineffective erythropoiesis
- Neurologic findings include subacute combined degeneration (posterior columns + lateral corticospinal tracts), peripheral neuropathy, cognitive decline, and psychiatric disturbances — can occur WITHOUT anemia in 25-30% of cases
- MMA is the most specific confirmatory test; serum B12 200-300 pg/mL is indeterminate and requires MMA for definitive assessment
- Folate supplementation WITHOUT B12 correction masks the anemia while neurologic damage silently progresses — always correct B12 first
- Treatment is IM cyanocobalamin for pernicious anemia or severe deficiency; high-dose oral B12 is effective for dietary deficiency; neurologic recovery depends on duration of deficiency before treatment

clinicalPearls: **Folate masks, B12 kills — always check B12 before giving folate.** This is one of the most dangerous drug interactions in medicine. Folate bypasses the methyl-folate trap and corrects the megaloblastic anemia, making the CBC look normal. Meanwhile, the methylmalonyl-CoA pathway remains blocked, and demyelination of the posterior columns and corticospinal tracts continues unchecked. By the time the neurologic damage becomes clinically apparent, it may be irreversible. Any patient started on folate supplementation should have B12 checked first.

**Hypersegmented neutrophils appear before the anemia does — look at the smear.** A single neutrophil with 6 or more lobes, or ≥5% with 5 lobes, is the earliest morphologic clue to megaloblastic change. If you see this on a peripheral smear, check B12 and folate even if the MCV and hemoglobin are still normal. Early detection prevents irreversible neurologic damage.

**A sky-high LDH with anemia is not always TTP — think megaloblastic anemia.** B12 deficiency causes such severe ineffective erythropoiesis that LDH can exceed 1000-5000 U/L, mimicking thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura (TTP) or other hemolytic emergencies. The peripheral smear distinguishes them: macro-ovalocytes and hypersegmented neutrophils (megaloblastic) vs schistocytes (TTP). Before ordering plasma exchange, look at the smear.

**Nitrous oxide can precipitate acute neurologic crisis in subclinically deficient patients.** N2O irreversibly oxidizes the cobalt in B12, inactivating it completely. A patient with borderline B12 stores who undergoes surgery with N2O anesthesia — or a recreational "whippet" user — can develop fulminant subacute combined degeneration within days. Ask about N2O exposure in any patient presenting with acute-onset myelopathy or neuropathy.

**Watch the potassium during B12 repletion — reticulocytosis eats it up.** When megaloblastic anemia is corrected, the bone marrow rapidly produces new red blood cells. Potassium shifts intracellularly as new cells form, and severe hypokalemia can develop within 48 hours. This is analogous to refeeding syndrome. Monitor potassium daily for the first week of treatment and replace aggressively.

**Monthly B12 injections for pernicious anemia are truly lifelong — there are no breaks.** Patients sometimes stop injections when they feel better, thinking they are "cured." Pernicious anemia is a permanent autoimmune condition. Body stores will deplete within 1-3 years of stopping injections, and neurologic damage will recur. Reinforce this at every visit.

**Hypersegmented neutrophils:** ≥5 lobes in ≥5% of neutrophils, or any neutrophil with ≥6 lobes — earliest and most specific morphologic finding of megaloblastic anemia; appears before anemia develops
**Pancytopenia:** In severe cases — ineffective hematopoiesis affects all cell lines; thrombocytopenia (usually mild), leukopenia

**Ineffective erythropoiesis:** Intramedullary hemolysis of megaloblastic precursors → elevated LDH and indirect bilirubin (can be markedly elevated, mimicking hemolytic anemia); low haptoglobin; reticulocyte count inappropriately low for degree of anemia
**Glossitis:** Smooth, beefy red tongue (loss of filiform papillae) — classic but not always present
**Angular cheilitis**
**Subacute combined degeneration (SCD) of spinal cord:**

**Posterior column dysfunction:** Loss of vibration sense and proprioception → sensory ataxia (positive Romberg sign), wide-based unsteady gait; diminished joint position sense in toes; typically starts in lower extremities and ascends
**Lateral corticospinal tract dysfunction:** Upper motor neuron signs — spasticity, hyperreflexia, positive Babinski sign, weakness
The combination of absent ankle reflexes (peripheral neuropathy) with positive Babinski sign (upper motor neuron lesion) is a classic clue to B12 myelopathy
**Peripheral neuropathy:** Symmetric, distal, "stocking-glove" pattern; paresthesias (tingling, numbness, "pins and needles") — often the earliest neurologic symptom; begins in feet → hands
**Cognitive and psychiatric manifestations:**
Memory impairment, difficulty concentrating, confusion
Dementia (reversible if caught early, irreversible if prolonged)
Depression, irritability, personality changes
Psychosis ("megaloblastic madness") — rare but well-described
**Autonomic dysfunction:** Orthostatic hypotension, bladder dysfunction (urinary retention or incontinence), impotence
**Optic neuropathy:** Bilateral visual loss — rare

von-willebrand-disease.title: Von Willebrand Disease
von-willebrand-disease.system: Lymphatic and Immune System, Cardiovascular System

sections.definition:
Von Willebrand disease is the most common inherited bleeding disorder, caused by quantitative deficiency or qualitative dysfunction of von Willebrand factor (VWF). VWF serves two critical hemostatic roles: it mediates platelet adhesion to damaged endothelium (primary hemostasis) and acts as the carrier protein for coagulation factor VIII, protecting it from premature proteolytic degradation (secondary hemostasis). VWD therefore impairs both primary and secondary hemostasis to varying degrees depending on the type and severity.

VWD is classified into three main types: Type 1 (partial quantitative deficiency, most common), Type 2 (qualitative defects with four subtypes), and Type 3 (complete or near-complete absence of VWF, most severe). The clinical hallmark is mucocutaneous bleeding — menorrhagia, epistaxis, easy bruising, prolonged bleeding from dental and surgical procedures, and GI bleeding.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Prevalence/Incidence:** Prevalence of VWF mutations ~1% of the general population; clinically symptomatic VWD ~0.01% (1 in 10,000); Type 1 accounts for 60-80% of diagnosed cases; Type 2 accounts for 20-30%; Type 3 is rare (~1 in 500,000 to 1 in 1,000,000); most common inherited bleeding disorder worldwide; significantly underdiagnosed, especially in women
- **Risk factors:** Autosomal inheritance (affects males and females equally, unlike Hemophilia); Type 1 and most Type 2: autosomal dominant (one affected parent = 50% transmission risk); Type 3 and Type 2N: autosomal recessive; blood type O associated with ~25% lower VWF levels (contributes to Type 1 phenotype in borderline cases)
- **Demographics:** All ethnicities equally affected; affects both sexes, but women are more frequently diagnosed due to menorrhagia and obstetric bleeding bringing them to clinical attention; average delay from symptom onset to diagnosis is 10+ years; many mild Type 1 patients never diagnosed
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Coagulation and Hemostasis — vWF bridges platelet GP1b receptors to exposed subendothelial collagen; without vWF, the initial platelet plug cannot anchor — the primary hemostatic plug never forms.*

### VWF Functions
1. **Platelet adhesion (primary hemostasis):** VWF is released from endothelial Weibel-Palade bodies and platelet alpha-granules → binds exposed subendothelial collagen at sites of vascular injury → unfolds under shear stress → GPIb receptors on platelets bind to VWF A1 domain → tethers platelets to vessel wall → initiates platelet plug formation
2. **FVIII carrier protein (secondary hemostasis):** VWF binds and stabilizes FVIII in circulation → extends FVIII half-life from ~2 hours (free) to ~12 hours (VWF-bound); VWF deficiency → secondary FVIII deficiency

### Type-Specific Mechanisms
| Type | Mechanism | VWF Level | FVIII Level | Multimer Pattern |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------------|------------------|
| **Type 1** | Partial quantitative deficiency of structurally normal VWF | Reduced (15-40%) | Mildly reduced | Normal distribution, reduced quantity |
| **Type 2A** | Loss of high-molecular-weight (HMW) multimers → impaired platelet binding | Variable | Variable | Absent HMW multimers |
| **Type 2B** | Gain-of-function → VWF binds platelets spontaneously in circulation → clears VWF and platelets | Reduced | Variable | Absent HMW multimers + thrombocytopenia |
| **Type 2M** | Decreased platelet binding without loss of HMW multimers | Reduced | Variable | Normal multimer pattern |
| **Type 2N** | Decreased binding to FVIII → FVIII cleared rapidly → mimics mild hemophilia A | Normal | Markedly reduced (5-15%) | Normal |
| **Type 3** | Complete absence of VWF | Undetectable (<3%) | Very low (1-10%) | Absent |

### ADAMTS13 Regulation
- ADAMTS13 metalloprotease cleaves ultra-large VWF multimers on endothelial surface → regulates VWF size → prevents spontaneous platelet aggregation (ADAMTS13 deficiency → Thrombotic Thrombocytopenic Purpura)
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Mucocutaneous bleeding (hallmark):**
  - Menorrhagia — most common symptom in women; present in ~80% of women with VWD; often the presenting complaint; leads to iron deficiency anemia
  - Epistaxis — recurrent, often bilateral, prolonged (>10 minutes); common in children
  - Easy bruising — disproportionate to injury; large, palpable bruises
  - Prolonged bleeding from dental extractions, dental cleaning, tonsillectomy
  - GI bleeding — especially in Type 2 and Type 3 (may involve angiodysplasia — acquired vascular malformations associated with VWD)
  - Postpartum hemorrhage — despite VWF levels rising during pregnancy (Type 1), levels drop rapidly postpartum → delayed PPH at 1-3 weeks
- **Type 3 (severe) features:** Hemarthrosis and deep tissue bleeding resembling severe Hemophilia (due to very low FVIII levels); less common than in hemophilia but can occur
- **Bleeding score tools:** ISTH Bleeding Assessment Tool (BAT) standardizes bleeding history; score ≥4 in adult women, ≥3 in adult men, ≥2 in children suggests further evaluation

### Complications
- Iron deficiency anemia (from chronic mucocutaneous blood loss, especially menorrhagia)
- Postoperative hemorrhage (especially after tonsillectomy, dental surgery, obstetric procedures)
- Postpartum hemorrhage (primary and delayed)
- GI bleeding from angiodysplasia (especially in Type 2A and Type 3)
- Hemarthrosis and arthropathy (Type 3 predominantly)
- Severe hemorrhage in Type 3 (including intracranial hemorrhage, though rarer than in hemophilia)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs/Studies
| Test | Significance |
|------|-------------|
| **VWF antigen (VWF:Ag)** | Quantitative VWF level; reduced in Types 1, 2A, 2B, 2M; normal in Type 2N; undetectable in Type 3; normal range 50-200%; blood type O baseline is ~25% lower |
| **VWF activity (VWF:RCo or VWF:GPIbM)** | Measures VWF ability to bind platelets; VWF:RCo (ristocetin cofactor activity) is traditional assay; reduced disproportionately to antigen in Type 2 qualitative defects |
| **VWF:RCo / VWF:Ag ratio** | Ratio <0.7 suggests qualitative (Type 2) defect; ratio ~1.0 in Type 1 |
| **Factor VIII activity** | Reduced in proportion to VWF reduction (Type 1/3) or markedly reduced in Type 2N; helps differentiate 2N from hemophilia A |
| **VWF multimer analysis** | Gel electrophoresis separates multimer sizes; loss of HMW multimers → Type 2A or 2B; normal pattern → Type 1, 2M, or 2N |
| **Ristocetin-induced platelet aggregation (RIPA)** | Enhanced (low-dose) aggregation in Type 2B — diagnostic; absent/reduced in other types |
| **VWF:FVIII binding assay** | Specifically diagnoses Type 2N (reduced VWF binding to FVIII) |
| **Platelet count** | Normal in most types; THROMBOCYTOPENIA in Type 2B (spontaneous VWF-platelet binding → clearance) |
| **aPTT** | May be mildly prolonged (FVIII reduction) or normal; NOT sensitive for VWD diagnosis |
| **PT** | Normal |
| **CBC** | May show microcytic anemia (iron deficiency from chronic blood loss) |

### Diagnostic Criteria
- Clinical bleeding history (ISTH BAT score above threshold) PLUS laboratory confirmation
- VWF:Ag and/or VWF activity <30% on at least two occasions → definitive VWD diagnosis
- VWF:Ag 30-50% = "low VWF" (not diagnostic VWD but may still have clinical bleeding)
- Type classification requires VWF activity/antigen ratio, multimer analysis, RIPA, and FVIII binding
- Repeat testing may be necessary — VWF is an acute-phase reactant and rises with stress, inflammation, pregnancy, OCP use, exercise; a single normal result does not exclude VWD
sections.management:
### Acute — Bleeding Episodes
- **Desmopressin (DDAVP) — first-line for Type 1 and some Type 2 variants:**
  - Synthetic vasopressin analogue → stimulates release of stored VWF and FVIII from endothelial Weibel-Palade bodies
  - IV: 0.3 mcg/kg in 50 mL NS over 30 min; intranasal (Stimate): 150 mcg/spray, 1 spray (<50 kg) or 2 sprays (>50 kg)
  - Raises VWF and FVIII 3-5x baseline within 30-60 minutes; effect lasts 6-12 hours
  - **Desmopressin challenge test:** Perform BEFORE clinical need to document individual response (measure VWF/FVIII at baseline, 1 hr, and 4 hr post-dose)
  - **Tachyphylaxis:** Repeated doses (>2-3 doses in 48 hrs) → depleted stores → diminishing response
  - **CONTRAINDICATED in Type 2B** (releases more abnormal VWF → worsens thrombocytopenia) and **ineffective in Type 3** (no VWF stores to release)
  - Monitor for hyponatremia (antidiuretic effect) — restrict fluids; especially in children and elderly
- **VWF-containing concentrates (when DDAVP inadequate, contraindicated, or Type 3):**
  - Plasma-derived VWF/FVIII concentrates: Humate-P (VWF:RCo/FVIII ratio ~2.4:1), Alphanate, Wilate
  - Recombinant VWF: Vonvendi (vonicog alfa) — recombinant VWF without FVIII; may need supplemental FVIII for initial dose
  - Dose based on VWF:RCo units; target depends on clinical situation (minor bleed: 30-50 IU/dL; major surgery: 80-100 IU/dL)
- **Tranexamic acid (adjunctive — highly effective for mucosal bleeding):**
  - Oral: 1000-1300 mg TID; IV: 10 mg/kg; stabilizes clot by inhibiting fibrinolysis
  - Excellent for dental procedures, epistaxis, menorrhagia, GI bleeding
  - Can be used alone for minor mucosal bleeding or combined with DDAVP/VWF concentrates for major procedures
- **Topical hemostatic agents:** Topical thrombin, fibrin sealant — useful for dental extractions and minor procedures

### Chronic/Maintenance
- **Menorrhagia management (critical in women with VWD):**
  - Combined oral contraceptives (raise VWF/FVIII levels + reduce menstrual flow)
  - Levonorgestrel IUD (Mirena) — highly effective; reduces menstrual blood loss by >90%
  - Tranexamic acid 1300 mg TID during menses
  - DDAVP intranasal at onset of menses (if Type 1 with documented response)
  - Iron supplementation for iron deficiency anemia
- **Prophylaxis for Type 3 and severe Type 2:** Regular VWF concentrate infusions (similar to hemophilia prophylaxis) for patients with recurrent hemarthrosis or severe mucosal bleeding
- **GI angiodysplasia bleeding:** Challenging to manage; tranexamic acid, VWF concentrate prophylaxis; thalidomide or octreotide for refractory cases; endoscopic treatment of visible lesions

### Pregnancy and Delivery in VWD
- VWF and FVIII levels rise during pregnancy (2-3x by third trimester) in Type 1 → may normalize
- Type 2 and Type 3: levels do NOT adequately correct → plan VWF replacement for delivery
- Check VWF/FVIII levels at 34-36 weeks → plan for delivery
- Target VWF activity ≥50 IU/dL for vaginal delivery, ≥80 IU/dL for cesarean section
- Maintain levels for ≥3-5 days postpartum (risk of delayed PPH as VWF drops rapidly)
- Neuraxial anesthesia (epidural): generally safe if FVIII >50 IU/dL and no severe VWD
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **Bleeding assessment:**
  - Ask specifically about menstrual history, easy bruising pattern, dental procedure bleeding, family bleeding history — VWD symptoms are often normalized by patients ("everyone in my family bruises easily")
  - Use ISTH Bleeding Assessment Tool for standardized evaluation
  - Monitor for signs of acute hemorrhage: mucosal bleeding, menorrhagia, GI bleeding, postoperative oozing
  - Track hemoglobin trends — chronic mucosal blood loss causes insidious iron deficiency
- **DDAVP administration:**
  - IV route: dilute in 50 mL NS, infuse over 30 minutes; monitor BP (can cause hypotension or hypertension)
  - Intranasal (Stimate): NOT the same as regular DDAVP nasal spray (different concentration); ensure patient has correct product
  - Fluid restriction: limit intake to 1-1.5 L in 24 hours post-dose to prevent hyponatremia; monitor Na+ in children and elderly especially
  - Assess response at 1 hour (factor levels drawn); document individual response for future reference
  - Tachyphylaxis: efficacy diminishes after 2-3 consecutive doses in 48 hours — communicate to provider if repeat dosing is needed
- **Procedural planning:**
  - Pre-procedure VWF/FVIII levels → determine hemostatic plan (DDAVP vs concentrate)
  - Ensure hemostatic coverage for adequate duration post-procedure (dental: 3-5 days; major surgery: 7-14 days)
  - Have VWF concentrate available even if planning DDAVP (may need rescue)
  - Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs for 7-10 days pre-procedure; acetaminophen for pain
- **Patient education:**
  - Medical ID bracelet recommended (especially Type 2 and 3)
  - Inform all healthcare providers (including dentists) of diagnosis before ANY procedure
  - Intranasal DDAVP (Stimate) for home use — teach proper technique, storage (refrigerated), and when to use
  - Avoid aspirin and NSAIDs unless specifically cleared by hematologist
  - Iron supplementation if menorrhagia-related anemia
  - Pregnancy planning: pre-conception hematology consultation essential
sections.keyPoints:
- VWD is the most common inherited bleeding disorder (~1% prevalence of VWF mutations, ~0.01% clinically symptomatic), caused by quantitative or qualitative VWF defects
- VWF has dual hemostatic roles: platelet adhesion (primary hemostasis) and FVIII carrier protein (secondary hemostasis)
- Type 1 (partial quantitative, 60-80%) is most common; Type 2 (qualitative, 20-30%) has four subtypes; Type 3 (complete deficiency) is rare and most severe
- Mucocutaneous bleeding is the hallmark: menorrhagia, epistaxis, dental and surgical bleeding, easy bruising
- DDAVP is first-line for Type 1 and some Type 2 — releases endothelial VWF stores; contraindicated in Type 2B and ineffective in Type 3
- VWF-containing concentrates (Humate-P, Vonvendi) are used when DDAVP is insufficient, contraindicated, or for Type 3
- Tranexamic acid is a highly effective adjunctive therapy for mucosal bleeding
- VWD is significantly underdiagnosed, especially in women — menorrhagia should prompt VWD screening; average diagnostic delay exceeds 10 years

clinicalPearls: **Menorrhagia IS the presenting symptom of VWD in most women — screen for it.** Up to 80% of women with VWD have heavy menstrual bleeding, yet average time to diagnosis is over 10 years because it is dismissed as "normal." Any woman with menorrhagia, especially with a family bleeding history, deserves VWF testing. This is one of the most underdiagnosed conditions in medicine.

**DDAVP in Type 2B will make things WORSE, not better.** Type 2B VWD has gain-of-function VWF that spontaneously binds platelets. Giving DDAVP releases more of this abnormal VWF → more spontaneous platelet binding → worsening thrombocytopenia → paradoxically increases bleeding risk. Always confirm VWD type before administering DDAVP.

**Stimate nasal spray is NOT the same as regular DDAVP nasal spray.** Stimate is 15x more concentrated (1.5 mg/mL vs 0.1 mg/mL). Patients who get regular DDAVP nasal spray from pharmacy instead of Stimate will not achieve hemostatic VWF levels. Verify the product at the bedside and during discharge education.

**VWF is an acute-phase reactant — a single normal result does NOT exclude VWD.** Stress, surgery, inflammation, pregnancy, oral contraceptives, and exercise all elevate VWF levels. A patient can have clinically significant VWD with a "normal" VWF level drawn during an acute illness or stressful hospitalization. Repeat testing in a quiescent state is essential.

**Postpartum hemorrhage in VWD is often DELAYED.** VWF levels rise 2-3x during pregnancy and may normalize by delivery in Type 1. But they crash rapidly postpartum. The greatest bleeding risk is days 3-14 after delivery, when the patient is home. Educate patients to monitor for delayed PPH and ensure hematology follow-up within the first week postpartum.

signs: **Mucocutaneous bleeding (hallmark):**
Menorrhagia — most common symptom in women; present in ~80% of women with VWD; often the presenting complaint; leads to iron deficiency anemia
Epistaxis — recurrent, often bilateral, prolonged (>10 minutes); common in children
Easy bruising — disproportionate to injury; large, palpable bruises
Prolonged bleeding from dental extractions, dental cleaning, tonsillectomy
GI bleeding — especially in Type 2 and Type 3 (may involve angiodysplasia — acquired vascular malformations associated with VWD)
Postpartum hemorrhage — despite VWF levels rising during pregnancy (Type 1), levels drop rapidly postpartum → delayed PPH at 1-3 weeks
**Type 3 (severe) features:** Hemarthrosis and deep tissue bleeding resembling severe Hemophilia (due to very low FVIII levels); less common than in hemophilia but can occur
**Bleeding score tools:** ISTH Bleeding Assessment Tool (BAT) standardizes bleeding history; score ≥4 in adult women, ≥3 in adult men, ≥2 in children suggests further evaluation
Iron deficiency anemia (from chronic mucocutaneous blood loss, especially menorrhagia)
Postoperative hemorrhage (especially after tonsillectomy, dental surgery, obstetric procedures)
Postpartum hemorrhage (primary and delayed)
GI bleeding from angiodysplasia (especially in Type 2A and Type 3)
Hemarthrosis and arthropathy (Type 3 predominantly)
Severe hemorrhage in Type 3 (including intracranial hemorrhage, though rarer than in hemophilia)

young-onset-colorectal-cancer.title: Young-Onset Colorectal Cancer

sections.definition: Colorectal cancer (CRC) diagnosed in patients under the age of 50. Young-onset CRC is a clinically and molecularly distinct entity from CRC arising in older adults: it is more frequently left-sided (rectosigmoid), more likely to present at advanced stage due to diagnostic delay, and more often harbors microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) or mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR) biology. The rising incidence in younger populations over the past three decades has fundamentally changed screening guidelines and demands that clinicians maintain a high index of suspicion for colorectal malignancy in patients who would previously have been considered too young for this disease.
sections.epidemiology:
- **Incidence:** Rising 1-2% per year in the under-50 population since the mid-1990s, even as overall CRC incidence in older adults has declined due to screening; young-onset CRC now accounts for approximately 12% of all new CRC diagnoses in the United States (~20,000 cases/year)
- **Projected trends:** CRC is expected to become the leading cause of cancer death in the 20-49 age group by 2030; incidence in 20-29 year-olds has doubled since the 1990s; rectal cancer incidence rising even faster than colon cancer in young adults
- **Screening guideline shift:** The American Cancer Society (2018) and US Preventive Services Task Force (2021) lowered the recommended CRC screening start age from 50 to 45 for average-risk individuals in response to this epidemiologic trend
- **Racial disparities:** Black Americans have the highest CRC incidence and mortality across all age groups; young Black patients present at later stages and have worse survival than White counterparts; disparities are compounded by lower screening rates and reduced access to timely follow-up
- **Sex distribution:** Slight male predominance in young-onset CRC (~55% male); rectal cancer specifically has a stronger male predominance
- **Geographic variation:** Highest rates in the United States, Australia, and parts of Europe; emerging data show rising incidence in Asia as well
sections.risk-factors:
### Hereditary and Genetic
- **Family history:** 10-20% of young-onset CRC patients have a first-degree relative (FDR) with CRC; family history remains the single strongest risk factor
- **Lynch syndrome (hereditary nonpolyposis colorectal cancer, HNPCC):** The most common hereditary CRC syndrome, accounting for 3-5% of all CRC and a disproportionately higher share of young-onset cases (up to 15-20%); caused by germline mutations in mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2, EPCAM); autosomal dominant; lifetime CRC risk 40-80%; also increases risk of endometrial, ovarian, gastric, urinary tract, and other cancers
- **Familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP):** Germline APC mutation; hundreds to thousands of colonic adenomas by adolescence; near-100% CRC risk without prophylactic colectomy; accounts for <1% of all CRC but presents almost exclusively in young patients
- **Other polyposis syndromes:** MUTYH-associated polyposis (MAP), Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, juvenile polyposis syndrome, serrated polyposis syndrome

### Inflammatory
- **Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD):** Both Crohn's Disease and Ulcerative Colitis confer increased CRC risk; risk begins to rise 8-10 years after diagnosis of extensive colitis; dysplasia surveillance with chromoendoscopy is standard of care

### Modifiable and Environmental
- **Obesity:** Increasing body mass index is associated with higher CRC risk, particularly in men; the obesity epidemic in younger generations may be a driver of rising young-onset CRC
- **Processed and red meat consumption:** Classified by WHO/IARC as a Group 1 (processed) and Group 2A (red meat) carcinogen for CRC
- **Alcohol use:** Moderate to heavy intake (>2 drinks/day) increases risk
- **Sedentary lifestyle:** Physical inactivity independently increases CRC risk
- **Western dietary pattern:** High in refined sugars, processed foods, and saturated fats; low in fiber, fruits, and vegetables
- **Possible emerging factors under investigation:** Gut microbiome alterations, early-life antibiotic exposure, artificial sweeteners — epidemiologic associations but causation not yet established
sections.pathophysiology:
> *First principles: Cell Proliferation and Death — CRC arises from APC/β-catenin pathway disruption (chromosomal instability) or mismatch repair deficiency (microsatellite instability/Lynch syndrome); younger patients more often MSI-high or have hereditary syndromes. Genetics and Protein Expression — Lynch syndrome = germline mismatch repair gene mutation (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2); familial adenomatous polyposis = APC mutation.*

### Key Differences from Older-Onset CRC
| Feature | Young-Onset CRC (<50) | Older-Onset CRC (>=50) |
|---|---|---|
| **Tumor location** | More often left-sided (rectum, sigmoid) | More often right-sided (cecum, ascending) |
| **Stage at diagnosis** | More likely advanced (stage III/IV) | More stage I/II due to screening detection |
| **Histology** | More mucinous, signet ring cell variants | More typical adenocarcinoma |
| **MSI-H/dMMR rate** | Higher (~15-20%) | ~12-15% overall |
| **Diagnostic delay** | Longer (average 4-6 months symptom-to-diagnosis) | Shorter (screening-detected or earlier workup) |
| **Prognosis stage-for-stage** | Similar or slightly better (fitter, fewer comorbidities) | Varies with comorbidity burden |

### Molecular Biology
- **Microsatellite instability-high (MSI-H) / mismatch repair-deficient (dMMR):** More common in young-onset CRC; caused by either germline MMR mutations (Lynch syndrome) or sporadic MLH1 promoter hypermethylation; MSI-H tumors have a distinct biology — dense lymphocytic infiltrate, better prognosis in early-stage disease, and excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitors
- **Mucinous and signet ring cell histology:** More prevalent in young-onset CRC; signet ring cell variant carries a worse prognosis with poorer chemotherapy response
- **CMS (Consensus Molecular Subtypes):** Young-onset CRC is enriched for CMS1 (MSI-immune) and CMS4 (mesenchymal, associated with worse prognosis); less representation of CMS2 (canonical, WNT/MYC-driven)
- **Chromosomal instability (CIN):** Still the most common pathway in young-onset CRC overall; APC-KRAS-TP53 adenoma-carcinoma sequence applies; but MSI-H pathway is overrepresented compared to older CRC

### Why the Diagnostic Delay?
- Clinicians and patients both underestimate CRC risk in young adults
- Rectal bleeding is attributed to hemorrhoids without further workup
- Change in bowel habits is attributed to irritable bowel syndrome or diet
- Iron deficiency anemia in menstruating women is attributed to menstrual blood loss alone
- Young patients may delay seeking care or minimize symptoms
- Average delay from symptom onset to diagnosis: 4-6 months in young-onset CRC vs. <2 months in screening-detected older-onset CRC
sections.clinicalFeatures:
### Signs & Symptoms
- **Rectal bleeding / hematochezia (most common):** Often bright red blood per rectum given the predominance of left-sided/rectal tumors; frequently dismissed by both patients and clinicians as hemorrhoidal bleeding
- **Change in bowel habits:** New-onset constipation, diarrhea, or alternating pattern; narrowing of stool caliber ("pencil stools"); change persisting >4 weeks warrants investigation
- **Abdominal pain:** Crampy, colicky pain; may be intermittent; can localize to the left lower quadrant or be diffuse
- **Iron deficiency anemia:** Occult blood loss; may present as unexplained fatigue, exercise intolerance, pallor; in men or postmenopausal women, iron deficiency anemia is CRC until proven otherwise; in premenopausal women, do not automatically attribute to menstruation without checking a stool occult blood or considering colonoscopy
- **Unintentional weight loss:** Suggests advanced disease; often accompanies anorexia and general malaise
- **Tenesmus:** Persistent feeling of incomplete evacuation; associated with rectal tumors
- **Bowel obstruction (late presentation):** Colicky pain, distension, vomiting, obstipation; may be the presenting symptom when tumor is obstructing the lumen — particularly left-sided tumors with smaller lumen diameter
- **Rectal mass palpable on digital rectal exam:** Low rectal tumors may be felt on DRE; this exam is often omitted but is critical

### Complications
- Bowel obstruction (partial or complete) requiring emergent surgical intervention
- Bowel perforation at tumor site or proximal to obstruction
- Peritoneal carcinomatosis (especially with mucinous/signet ring histology)
- Liver metastases (most common distant site)
- Lung metastases
- Malignant ascites
- Tumor-related hemorrhage
- Locally advanced pelvic disease (rectal cancer) — ureteral obstruction, fistula formation (rectovaginal, rectovesical)
sections.diagnosis:
### Key Labs and Studies
- **Colonoscopy with biopsy (gold standard):** Full colonoscopy is mandatory — not flexible sigmoidoscopy — because synchronous lesions occur in 3-5% of CRC cases; biopsy confirms histology, grade, and allows molecular testing
- **Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA):** Baseline level before treatment; prognostic value (CEA >5 ng/mL associated with worse outcomes); primary use is in surveillance for recurrence post-treatment; not a screening test
- **CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast:** Standard staging workup; evaluates for liver metastases, lung metastases, lymphadenopathy, peritoneal disease
- **Rectal MRI (for rectal cancer):** Critical for local staging of rectal tumors; defines relationship to mesorectal fascia (CRM — circumferential resection margin), depth of invasion through bowel wall (T-stage), and pelvic lymph node involvement; guides decision between upfront surgery vs. neoadjuvant chemoradiation
- **PET/CT:** Not routinely used in initial staging; may help clarify equivocal findings on CT or assess for occult metastatic disease
- **CBC:** Iron deficiency anemia (low MCV, low ferritin, low iron, elevated TIBC)
- **CMP:** Liver function (elevated ALP/GGT may suggest liver metastases), renal function, electrolytes
- **Tumor molecular profiling:**
  - **MMR/MSI testing (mandatory in all young-onset CRC):** Immunohistochemistry for MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2; and/or PCR-based MSI testing; identifies dMMR/MSI-H tumors for both prognostic and therapeutic implications (immunotherapy eligibility) AND for Lynch syndrome screening
  - **RAS (KRAS, NRAS) and BRAF testing:** Guides anti-EGFR therapy eligibility (cetuximab/panitumumab only effective in RAS wild-type, left-sided tumors); BRAF V600E mutation is prognostic (poor) and therapeutic target
  - **HER2 testing:** Emerging therapeutic target in CRC
  - **NTRK fusion testing:** Rare but highly actionable — larotrectinib, entrectinib

### Lynch Syndrome Screening
**All young-onset CRC should trigger Lynch syndrome workup regardless of family history:**
1. **Universal tumor testing:** MMR IHC (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) on the biopsy/resection specimen
2. **If MLH1 loss detected:** Reflex to BRAF V600E testing and/or MLH1 promoter methylation analysis to distinguish sporadic (methylation-positive) from Lynch (methylation-negative, germline MLH1 mutation)
3. **If any MMR protein loss suggests germline origin:** Refer for genetic counseling and germline testing
4. **If MSI-H with intact IHC (uncommon):** Still consider germline testing, especially in young patients
5. **Genetic counseling referral:** All young-onset CRC patients should be offered genetic counseling regardless of tumor testing results — up to 25% may have an identifiable germline mutation (Lynch, FAP, MUTYH, or other)
6. **Cascade testing:** If a germline mutation is identified, first-degree relatives should be offered testing; this is how Lynch syndrome families are identified and enrolled in appropriate cancer surveillance

### Staging (AJCC 8th Edition)
- **Stage I:** T1-T2, N0, M0 — tumor invades submucosa or muscularis propria, no nodal or distant spread
- **Stage II:** T3-T4, N0, M0 — tumor invades through muscularis propria into subserosa/pericolorectal tissues or invades other organs
- **Stage III:** Any T, N1-N2, M0 — regional lymph node metastases present
- **Stage IV:** Any T, any N, M1 — distant metastatic disease (liver, lung, peritoneum most common)
sections.management:
### Surgical
- **Colon cancer:** Segmental colectomy with en-bloc mesenteric lymph node dissection (minimum 12 lymph nodes for adequate staging); surgical approach depends on tumor location (right hemicolectomy, left hemicolectomy, sigmoid colectomy)
- **Rectal cancer:** Total mesorectal excision (TME) is the surgical standard; low anterior resection (LAR) with TME for mid/upper rectal tumors; abdominoperineal resection (APR) with permanent colostomy for very low rectal tumors where sphincter preservation is not feasible; temporary diverting ileostomy is common with LAR to protect the anastomosis
- **Emergency surgery:** Required for obstruction or perforation; may involve Hartmann's procedure (resection with end colostomy and rectal stump closure)
- **Fertility-sparing considerations:** Critical in young patients — pelvic radiation and certain chemotherapy agents (alkylators) can impair fertility; discuss fertility preservation (sperm banking, oocyte cryopreservation) BEFORE initiating treatment

### Adjuvant Chemotherapy
- **Stage I:** No adjuvant chemotherapy
- **Stage II:** Adjuvant chemotherapy for high-risk stage II (T4, poorly differentiated, lymphovascular invasion, <12 nodes examined, perforation, obstruction); capecitabine or 5-FU alone, or FOLFOX for highest-risk features; note: MSI-H stage II has excellent prognosis and may NOT benefit from 5-FU-based chemotherapy
- **Stage III (node-positive — standard of care for adjuvant chemotherapy):**
  - **FOLFOX (5-FU, leucovorin, oxaliplatin):** Standard regimen; 3 months for low-risk stage III (T1-3, N1); 6 months for high-risk stage III (T4 or N2) — IDEA trial established shorter duration for low-risk
  - **CAPOX (capecitabine + oxaliplatin):** Oral alternative to FOLFOX; equivalent efficacy; 3 months may be sufficient for low-risk stage III
  - **Oxaliplatin neurotoxicity:** Cumulative, dose-limiting; cold-triggered peripheral neuropathy; shorter treatment duration (3 vs. 6 months) significantly reduces neuropathy

### Rectal Cancer-Specific Treatment
- **Total neoadjuvant therapy (TNT) — emerging standard:** All systemic chemotherapy (FOLFOX/CAPOX) and chemoradiation given BEFORE surgery; improves pathologic complete response rates and may enable organ preservation ("watch and wait") in complete responders
- **Neoadjuvant chemoradiation:** Long-course (5 weeks of 5-FU-based chemoradiation) or short-course radiation (5 fractions over 1 week) followed by chemotherapy
- **Watch-and-wait (organ preservation):** For clinical complete response after neoadjuvant therapy; intensive surveillance with DRE, MRI, endoscopy; avoids radical surgery and permanent ostomy in select patients; increasingly offered in young patients where quality-of-life preservation is paramount
- **Immunotherapy for dMMR rectal cancer:** Landmark data showing that dostarlimab (anti-PD-1) achieved 100% clinical complete response in a small cohort of dMMR rectal cancer patients without requiring surgery, radiation, or chemotherapy — practice-changing for this molecular subset

### Metastatic Disease (Stage IV)
- **Oligometastatic disease:** Potentially curable with resection of primary + metastasectomy (especially liver-limited disease) + perioperative chemotherapy; 5-year survival 25-40% with complete resection of liver metastases
- **First-line systemic therapy:** FOLFOX or FOLFIRI + bevacizumab (anti-VEGF); or FOLFOX/FOLFIRI + cetuximab/panitumumab (anti-EGFR, only for RAS wild-type, left-sided tumors)
- **MSI-H/dMMR metastatic CRC:** Pembrolizumab (first-line, KEYNOTE-177) or nivolumab +/- ipilimumab; superior to chemotherapy in dMMR tumors; this is particularly relevant for young-onset CRC given higher MSI-H rates
- **BRAF V600E-mutant:** Encorafenib + cetuximab (BEACON regimen)
- **Later lines:** Regorafenib, trifluridine/tipiracil (TAS-102), fruquintinib; clinical trials
sections.nursingConsiderations:
### Pre-Surgical Preparation
- **Mechanical bowel preparation:** As ordered (typically polyethylene glycol solution); ensure patient compliance and adequate hydration; monitor for dehydration and electrolyte disturbance, especially in elderly or those with renal impairment
- **Enhanced recovery after surgery (ERAS) protocols:** Carbohydrate loading the night before, limited fasting, multimodal analgesia planning, early ambulation expectations — educate the patient preoperatively
- **Stoma site marking:** If ostomy is planned, ensure enterostomal therapy (WOCN) nurse has marked the site preoperatively while patient is sitting, standing, and lying down; placement avoids skin folds, belt line, and scars

### Ostomy Education and Care
- **Ileostomy (common with rectal cancer/LAR):** High-output risk (>1500 mL/day) leading to dehydration and electrolyte derangement (hyponatremia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, metabolic acidosis); teach patients to monitor output volume and color; oral rehydration solutions (not plain water); when to call the surgeon
- **Colostomy (APR):** More formed output; stoma care and pouching system education; skin assessment for peristomal irritation
- **Psychosocial impact of ostomy:** Especially profound in young patients; body image, intimacy concerns, social embarrassment; refer to ostomy support groups (UOAA — United Ostomy Associations of America); some young patients may benefit from connecting with peer mentors of similar age
- **Ileostomy reversal timeline:** Typically 8-12 weeks after primary surgery; confirm anastomotic integrity with contrast study before reversal

### Chemotherapy-Specific Nursing
- **Oxaliplatin cold sensitivity:** Educate patients about acute cold-triggered neuropathy — avoid cold drinks, cold air, touching cold surfaces during and for several days after infusion; provide warm blankets; do NOT use ice chips for mucositis prevention during oxaliplatin infusion
- **5-FU/capecitabine hand-foot syndrome (palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia):** Inspect hands and feet regularly; moisturizing creams; avoid friction, heat, tight footwear; dose reduction if grade 2+
- **Central venous access device care:** Most CRC patients receive chemotherapy through an implanted port; monitor for infection, thrombosis, extravasation
- **DPD deficiency screening:** Dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) deficiency causes fatal toxicity from 5-FU/capecitabine; some institutions now screen with DPYD genotyping or uracil level before first dose; know your institution's policy

### Psychosocial Support (Critical for Young Patients)
- **Age-specific distress:** Young patients with CRC face unique psychosocial challenges — career disruption, parenting while ill, financial toxicity, relationship stress, loss of perceived invulnerability; distress screening at every visit
- **Fertility preservation counseling:** MUST occur before treatment initiation; refer to reproductive endocrinology; options include sperm banking (men), oocyte or embryo cryopreservation (women), ovarian transposition before pelvic radiation; document that the conversation occurred
- **Sexual health:** Pelvic surgery and radiation affect sexual function in both men (erectile dysfunction, retrograde ejaculation) and women (vaginal stenosis, dyspareunia, premature menopause from pelvic radiation); proactively address these concerns — patients rarely bring them up on their own
- **Survivorship care:** Young survivors face decades of surveillance and long-term treatment effects; transition to survivorship care plan with clear follow-up schedule; monitor for late effects (oxaliplatin neuropathy, cardiovascular risk from 5-FU, secondary malignancies)
- **Genetic counseling referral:** Ensure all young-onset CRC patients are referred for genetic counseling; this has implications not just for the patient but for their children, siblings, and parents

### Surveillance After Curative Treatment
- CEA every 3-6 months for 2 years, then every 6 months to year 5
- CT chest/abdomen/pelvis annually for 5 years (more frequent in stage III)
- Colonoscopy at 1 year post-surgery, then at 3 years, then every 5 years if normal; more frequent if advanced adenomas detected
- Rectal cancer: proctoscopy/EUA every 3-6 months for 2 years (especially watch-and-wait patients)
sections.keyPoints:
- Young-onset CRC (age <50) is rising at 1-2% per year and now represents ~12% of all CRC diagnoses — CRC screening now starts at age 45
- Young-onset CRC is more often left-sided (rectosigmoid), diagnosed at advanced stage, and harbors MSI-H/dMMR biology at higher rates than older-onset CRC
- Diagnostic delay (average 4-6 months) is the central problem — rectal bleeding and bowel habit changes in young adults are dismissed as hemorrhoids or IBS
- All young-onset CRC should undergo MMR/MSI testing and genetic counseling referral regardless of family history — up to 15-20% have Lynch syndrome
- Lynch syndrome (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) is the most common hereditary CRC syndrome (3-5% of all CRC); identified through universal tumor MMR testing + germline confirmation
- MSI-H/dMMR status has direct therapeutic implications: excellent response to immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab) and potentially practice-changing organ preservation in dMMR rectal cancer
- FOLFOX is the standard adjuvant regimen for stage III; 3 months is sufficient for low-risk stage III (IDEA trial)
- Fertility preservation must be discussed BEFORE treatment initiation — this is a time-sensitive, irreversible concern
- Young patients face unique psychosocial burdens — proactive screening for distress, financial toxicity, sexual health, and body image concerns is essential nursing care

clinicalPearls: **Young patients with rectal bleeding deserve a workup, not just a hemorrhoid diagnosis** — the most dangerous assumption in young-onset CRC is that youth equals protection; a 32-year-old with six months of rectal bleeding who is told "it's just hemorrhoids" without a digital rectal exam or referral is a diagnostic failure; if symptoms persist beyond 4 weeks or rectal bleeding is unexplained, a colonoscopy is indicated regardless of age

**All young-onset CRC gets Lynch testing — no exceptions** — even without a family history, up to 15-20% of young-onset CRC is Lynch syndrome; universal MMR IHC on the tumor specimen is the standard of care; a missed Lynch diagnosis means the patient, their siblings, their parents, and eventually their children miss out on life-saving cancer surveillance; this is one test with multigenerational impact

**Do not assume age protects against cancer** — the median age of CRC diagnosis is 66, but the fastest-growing demographic is 20-49 year-olds; the very fact that young patients "shouldn't" have CRC is exactly what causes the diagnostic delay; unexplained iron deficiency anemia in a 28-year-old man or a change in bowel habits lasting more than a month in a 35-year-old woman should prompt the same workup you would order for a 65-year-old

**MSI-H status in young-onset CRC is both a clue to Lynch syndrome and a therapeutic opportunity** — dMMR/MSI-H tumors respond remarkably well to immune checkpoint inhibitors; in metastatic CRC, pembrolizumab outperforms chemotherapy for MSI-H disease; in rectal cancer, dostarlimab has shown complete clinical responses without surgery; when you see MSI-H on a pathology report, think both "genetic counseling" and "immunotherapy eligibility"

**Fertility counseling before chemo is not optional — it is time-sensitive and irreversible** — once alkylating agents or pelvic radiation are administered, the window for fertility preservation may close permanently; nursing advocacy means ensuring this conversation happens before the first cycle, not after; document that the discussion occurred and that a reproductive endocrinology referral was offered

signs: **Rectal bleeding / hematochezia (most common):** Often bright red blood per rectum given the predominance of left-sided/rectal tumors; frequently dismissed by both patients and clinicians as hemorrhoidal bleeding
**Change in bowel habits:** New-onset constipation, diarrhea, or alternating pattern; narrowing of stool caliber ("pencil stools"); change persisting >4 weeks warrants investigation
**Abdominal pain:** Crampy, colicky pain; may be intermittent; can localize to the left lower quadrant or be diffuse

**Iron deficiency anemia:** Occult blood loss; may present as unexplained fatigue, exercise intolerance, pallor; in men or postmenopausal women, iron deficiency anemia is CRC until proven otherwise; in premenopausal women, do not automatically attribute to menstruation without checking a stool occult blood or considering colonoscopy
**Unintentional weight loss:** Suggests advanced disease; often accompanies anorexia and general malaise
**Tenesmus:** Persistent feeling of incomplete evacuation; associated with rectal tumors

**Bowel obstruction (late presentation):** Colicky pain, distension, vomiting, obstipation; may be the presenting symptom when tumor is obstructing the lumen — particularly left-sided tumors with smaller lumen diameter
**Rectal mass palpable on digital rectal exam:** Low rectal tumors may be felt on DRE; this exam is often omitted but is critical
Bowel obstruction (partial or complete) requiring emergent surgical intervention
Bowel perforation at tumor site or proximal to obstruction
Peritoneal carcinomatosis (especially with mucinous/signet ring histology)
Liver metastases (most common distant site)
Lung metastases
Malignant ascites
Tumor-related hemorrhage
Locally advanced pelvic disease (rectal cancer) — ureteral obstruction, fistula formation (rectovaginal, rectovesical)

apheresis-port-access.title: Apheresis Port Access

sections.definition:
The **Bard Powerflow apheresis port** is an implanted venous access device increasingly seen in the MultiCare system, particularly in patients with **Sickle Cell Disease** who require frequent apheresis (therapeutic red cell exchange). While it functions like a traditional port (medication administration, lab draws, apheresis), it has a **different design and different access requirements**.

**The critical difference: There is nothing visually obvious to differentiate an apheresis port from a traditional port on external exam.** Clinicians have mistakenly accessed these with standard Huber needles, causing device damage and patient harm.

**Primary Contact:** Melissa Johnson, Vascular Access Nurse Specialist (MultiCare)

---
sections.how-to-identify-an-apheresis-port:
### Step 1: Ask the Patient
