- Alert, oriented patients often **know what type of port they have**
- Ask: "Do you have a regular port or an apheresis port?" and "How is your port usually accessed?"

### Step 2: Check Prior Imaging
- Review prior CXR or other chest imaging in Epic — apheresis ports look different on X-ray than traditional ports
- **This should already be part of your standard port access workflow** (checking if port is power-injectable or not)

### Step 3: Palpation
- Port structure **feels different on palpation** compared to traditional ports
- **Do NOT base access technique choice on palpation alone** — confirm with patient history and imaging

### Step 4: Epic Documentation
- Check Epic for prior documentation of port type and access technique
- Power-injectable status will be documented if previously accessed

---
sections.access-technique:
### Apheresis Port Access
| Parameter | Apheresis Port | Traditional Port |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Needle/Catheter** | **14g or 16g peripheral IV catheter (≥1.75")** | Huber needle (non-coring) |
| **Angle** | Per IV catheter technique | 90-degree perpendicular to septum |
| **Power injectable?** | Yes — up to 300 PSI when accessed with BD Insyte Autoguard PIV catheter | Varies by port model |

### What NOT to Do
- ❌ **Do NOT access with a Huber needle** — this will puncture the line
- ❌ **Do NOT access at a 90-degree angle with a standard port technique**
- ❌ **Do NOT guess** — if unsure of port type, contact IV Therapy before accessing

### If You Are Not Trained to Access
- **Contact your IV Therapy team** or **ambulatory infusion center** for access or troubleshooting
- Do not attempt access if you have not been trained on this specific device
- Leaders/NPDs: Route all training requests through Melissa Johnson, Vascular Access Nurse Specialist

---
sections.important-reminders:
**Apheresis ports are still central lines:**
- All care outlined in the **Central Venous Access Device Policy** (and care bundle) is applicable
- Standard central line maintenance, dressing changes, and infection prevention apply
- Can be used for: medication administration, lab draws, apheresis, contrast injection

---

clinicalPearls: **"If in doubt, don't access — call IV Therapy."** An incorrectly accessed apheresis port means device damage, potential extravasation of whatever you're infusing, and the patient needs surgical device replacement. The cost of a phone call is zero; the cost of a punctured line is enormous.

**Sickle cell patients are the primary population with these ports.** If a sickle cell patient presents with an implanted port, your index of suspicion for apheresis port should be HIGH. Ask before you access.
**The patient is your best resource.** These patients get accessed frequently for apheresis and almost always know their port type and how it's accessed. Listen to them.

arterial-line-setup-and-monitoring.title: Arterial Line Setup and Monitoring

sections.why-arterial-lines-are-placed:
| Indication | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Continuous hemodynamic monitoring** | Beat-to-beat BP in vasopressor-dependent, hemodynamically unstable patients |
| **Frequent ABG sampling** | Avoids repeated arterial sticks; ventilated patients may need ABGs q1–4h |
| **Noninvasive BP unreliable** | Dysrhythmias (AFib), obesity, severe vasoconstriction, continuous infusions requiring tight titration |
| **Intra-operative/procedural** | Cardiac surgery, liver transplant, neurosurgery |
| **Damped noninvasive readings** | Peripheral vascular disease, edema |

---
sections.common-sites:
| Site | Pros | Cons |
|------|------|------|
| **Radial (most common)** | Superficial, compressible, dual hand supply (ulnar collateral) | Small vessel; can be difficult in hypotension/vasospasm |
| **Femoral** | Large vessel, easy in emergencies, reliable in hypotension | Infection risk, limits mobility, difficult to dress |
| **Brachial** | Good access | End-artery — no collateral → distal ischemia risk if occluded |
| **Dorsalis pedis** | Alternative if radial unavailable | Small, less reliable |

> **Always perform Allen's test before radial A-line:** Compress both radial and ulnar arteries, have patient make fist several times until hand blanches, release ulnar — hand should flush pink within 5–7 seconds. Positive test (hand flushes) = adequate ulnar collateral = safe to use radial.

---
sections.equipment-setup:
### What You Need (gather before calling provider)

**For the line itself:**
- Arterial line kit (20g or 18g catheter-over-needle or Seldinger kit)
- Sterile gloves (provider size)
- Sterile drape/field
- Chlorhexidine prep sticks
- Lidocaine 1% with 25g needle and 3 mL syringe
- Transparent occlusive dressing
- Arm board + tape (for wrist extension)

**For the transducer system:**
- Pressurized IV bag setup:
  - 500 mL NS in pressure bag
  - Inflate pressure bag to **300 mmHg** — this creates a continuous 3 mL/hr flush to prevent clotting
- Transducer tubing set (non-compliant, arterial-specific — do NOT use regular IV tubing)
- Transducer (disposable)
- Flush device (built into most arterial line kits — the "pigtail" pull activates a fast flush)
- Monitor cable connecting transducer to bedside monitor

### Priming the System (do this before the line goes in)
1. Spike the NS bag with the transducer set
2. Inflate pressure bag to 300 mmHg
3. Prime all tubing — flush out ALL air bubbles (air = artifact and air embolism risk)
4. Cap the transducer end (will connect after line is placed)
5. Attach transducer to IV pole at the level of the **phlebostatic axis** (4th intercostal space, midaxillary line)

> **Clinical Pearl: Air bubbles in arterial line tubing cause waveform dampening AND are a direct air embolism risk** — because arterial lines are high-pressure systems that can entrain air. Flush every bubble out before connecting. Check visually.

---
sections.zeroing-and-leveling-the-two-most-import:
### Leveling
- The transducer must be at the **phlebostatic axis** — 4th ICS, midaxillary line — regardless of patient position
- If the transducer is too LOW → falsely HIGH reading (gravity adds hydrostatic pressure)
- If the transducer is too HIGH → falsely LOW reading
- **Rule of thumb:** For every 1 inch the transducer is above the phlebostatic axis, the reading drops ~2 mmHg

### Zeroing
- Performed to eliminate atmospheric pressure from the reading
- **How to zero:**
  1. Turn the stopcock: open to air (patient side closed)
  2. Press "Zero" on the bedside monitor
  3. Wait for the value to read "0" or "---"
  4. Turn stopcock back to patient (close to air)
  5. Confirm waveform reappears
- **When to zero:**
  - Initial setup
  - After any repositioning of patient or transducer
  - If readings don't match clinical picture
  - Every shift (per unit protocol)

> **Clinical Pearl: "Level and zero before you trust."** Any time you get a BP reading that doesn't match how the patient looks, check transducer position and rezero before calling the provider.

---
sections.reading-the-arterial-waveform:
### Normal Waveform Components

```
     Peak (systolic)
      /\
     /  \
    /    \  Dicrotic notch (aortic valve closes)
   /      \/\
  /           \___  (diastolic)
 /
Anacrotic limb
(upstroke)
```

| Component | What It Represents |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Anacrotic limb (upstroke)** | Rapid ventricular ejection — slope reflects contractility |
| **Systolic peak** | Maximum aortic pressure; reflects cardiac output and vascular resistance |
| **Dicrotic notch** | Aortic valve closure — should be visible as a small notch on the downslope |
| **Diastolic runoff** | Pressure decay as blood flows into periphery |
| **Diastolic value** | Baseline pressure maintained by vascular tone |

### MAP (Mean Arterial Pressure)
- **MAP = DBP + 1/3 (SBP – DBP)** — or read directly from monitor
- Target MAP ≥65 mmHg for most critically ill patients (≥70–80 for TBI or known hypertension)
- MAP reflects organ perfusion pressure more reliably than systolic alone

---
sections.waveform-troubleshooting:
| Problem | Appearance | Causes | Fix |
|---------|-----------|--------|-----|
| **Overdamped** | Rounded, blunted waveform, falsely LOW systolic, falsely HIGH diastolic | Air bubbles, clot at tip, kinked tubing, loose connection | Fast flush, check for bubbles/kinks, aspirate clot (per protocol), check connections |
| **Underdamped** | Tall narrow spike, falsely HIGH systolic, exaggerated dicrotic notch | Excessive tubing length, compliant tubing, resonance | Use non-compliant arterial tubing, shorten setup |
| **Loss of waveform** | Flat line | Catheter out of artery, clot, stopcock turned off | Check stopcock position, attempt fast flush, call provider |
| **Positional dampening** | Waveform changes with wrist position | Catheter tip against vessel wall | Reposition wrist (extend or flex slightly), re-tape |

### Fast Flush Test (Square Wave Test)
- Pull the fast-flush pigtail for 1 second and release
- A square wave followed by **1–2 oscillations then return to baseline** = optimally damped system
- Excessive oscillations = underdamped; slow return = overdamped

---
sections.blood-sampling-from-arterial-line:
### Technique
1. **Waste first:** Withdraw 3–5 mL blood (clears the flush solution from the line) into a waste syringe — do NOT inject back
2. Draw your sample into the appropriate tube/syringe (heparinized syringe for ABG; appropriate tube for labs)
3. Fast flush line after drawing (restores continuous flush)
4. Cap and label samples immediately
5. Document in chart: time drawn, site

### ABG-Specific
- Use pre-heparinized ABG syringe
- Eliminate air bubbles immediately after draw (air changes O2/CO2 values)
- Transport on ice if >15 min delay to analyzer
- See ABG and VBG Interpretation for interpretation

> **Clinical Pearl: Label arterial line tubing "ARTERIAL" in red.** It is a Joint Commission requirement and a critical safety standard — inadvertent IV medication injection into an arterial line can cause distal limb ischemia and necrosis.

---
sections.ongoing-nursing-monitoring:
### Every Assessment
- [ ] **Waveform quality** — damped? Loss of dicrotic notch? Correlate with clinical picture
- [ ] **Insertion site** — redness, swelling, bleeding, hematoma
- [ ] **Distal circulation** — color, cap refill, warmth, sensation, motor function of the hand/foot distal to the line
- [ ] **Pressure bag** — verify maintained at 300 mmHg (will slowly deflate over time)
- [ ] **Dressing** — intact, dry, dated

### Complications to Monitor and Report
| Complication | Signs | Action |
|-------------|-------|--------|
| **Distal ischemia** | Pale/blue/cool hand, absent pulse, pain/numbness | Notify provider immediately — may require line removal |
| **Hemorrhage** | Blood at insertion site, tubing disconnection | Apply pressure; reconnect tubing securely; call provider |
| **Infection (LABSI)** | Erythema, warmth, purulence at site, fever | Notify provider; consider line removal; cultures |
| **Accidental removal** | Waveform loss, blood | Apply firm pressure for 5+ minutes (arterial = higher pressure than venous) |
| **Thrombus/occlusion** | Loss of waveform, inability to flush | Attempt aspiration (if protocol allows); call provider; do NOT force flush |

### Alarm Management
- Set systolic alarm limits appropriately (typically ±20–30 mmHg from current BP)
- MAP alarm: low limit 60–65 mmHg for most patients
- Waveform loss alarm: respond immediately — this is a hemorrhage risk

---
sections.removal-of-arterial-line:
Typically MD/APP order required; some units have RN-removal protocols:
1. Stop any infusions running through the line
2. Remove dressing and suture (if sutured)
3. Withdraw catheter smoothly
4. Apply firm direct pressure for **5 minutes minimum** (longer if coagulopathic)
5. Apply pressure dressing; assess distal circulation after
6. Document removal time and site assessment
7. Hold pressure longer for femoral sites (10+ minutes)

---
sections.references:
- Hemodynamics — MAP, cardiac output, vascular resistance concepts
- ABG and VBG Interpretation — arterial blood sampling and interpretation
- Central Line Setup and Assist — central venous access, CLABSI prevention
- Vasopressors and Inotropes — medications requiring tight hemodynamic monitoring
- Mechanical Ventilation Management — ventilated patients needing frequent ABGs

clinicalPearls: **"Level and zero before you trust"** — a transducer 4 inches above the phlebostatic axis will read 8 mmHg low. That's the difference between a MAP of 65 and 57. Know your zero.
**Label arterial tubing in red** — every time, no exceptions. A medication pushed into an arterial line is a sentinel event.
**Watch the hand, not just the number** — an art-line reading a MAP of 70 means nothing if the hand distal to it is dusky and cold. Distal circulation checks are a nursing responsibility.

**The waveform tells a story** — a tall narrow spike with exaggerated dicrotic notch = hyperdynamic circulation (sepsis, fever). A flat, blunted wave = low cardiac output (cardiogenic shock, severe hypovolemia). Learn to read it, not just the numbers.
**Pressure bag must stay at 300 mmHg** — below 300, the flush mechanism fails and the line clots. Check it every shift and whenever you see dampening.

auscultation.title: Auscultation

sections.definition:
Auscultation is the skill of listening to internal body sounds with a stethoscope. In ED nursing, the two most critical applications are **cardiac auscultation** (heart sounds, murmurs) and **pulmonary auscultation** (breath sounds, adventitious sounds). This is a skill that improves ONLY with deliberate practice — you can't learn it from a note alone, but this note gives you the framework to practice systematically.

**The honest truth:** Auscultation is hard. Noisy EDs, obese patients, noisy breathing, and limited time all make it harder. The goal is not perfection — it's **pattern recognition**: normal vs. abnormal, bilateral vs. unilateral, clear vs. not clear. That distinction alone drives critical clinical decisions.

**Related notes:** Health Assessment Foundations · Cardiovascular System · Respiratory System · Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction · Acute Heart Failure

---
sections.the-stethoscope:
### Parts That Matter
- **Diaphragm** (flat, larger side): Best for **high-pitched sounds** — most breath sounds, S1, S2, aortic/mitral regurgitation murmurs, bowel sounds. **This is what you use 90% of the time.**
- **Bell** (smaller, cupped side): Best for **low-pitched sounds** — S3, S4, mitral stenosis murmur. Use with **light pressure** (pressing hard converts the bell into a diaphragm)
- **Tubing:** Shorter and thicker = better sound transmission. Avoid tubing draped over your neck pressing against skin/clothing (creates artifact noise)

### Tips for Better Sound
- **Skin contact:** Always on bare skin. Clothing, gowns, and blankets create artifact crackle sounds that mimic adventitious lung sounds
- **Quiet environment:** Turn off the TV, close the door if possible. In a noisy ED, do the best you can but acknowledge the limitation
- **Warm the diaphragm** before placing on patient (rub it or hold it — cold stethoscope causes the patient to tense up and breathe shallowly)
- **Ask the patient to breathe deeply** through their mouth (nasal breathing is quieter; mouth breathing generates louder breath sounds)
- **For obese patients:** Press firmly with the diaphragm; ask patient to sit upright and lean forward; listen for longer at each site

---
sections.cardiac-auscultation:
### The 4 (+1) Auscultation Sites

Listen at ALL sites systematically — don't skip any:

| Site | Location | Valve Heard Best | Landmark |
|------|----------|-----------------|----------|
| **Aortic** | 2nd ICS, RIGHT sternal border | Aortic valve | Find sternal angle (angle of Louis), go to the right |
| **Pulmonic** | 2nd ICS, LEFT sternal border | Pulmonic valve | Same level, other side |
| **Erb's Point** | 3rd ICS, LEFT sternal border | Aortic + general murmur radiation | Between pulmonic and tricuspid areas |
| **Tricuspid** | 4th ICS, LEFT sternal border (lower left sternal border) | Tricuspid valve | Follow left sternal border down |
| **Mitral (Apex/PMI)** | 5th ICS, midclavicular line | Mitral valve | Find the PMI (point of maximum impulse) — usually 5th ICS MCL |

> **Memory aid: APE To Man** — Aortic, Pulmonic, Erb's, Tricuspid, Mitral (top to bottom, right to left)

### Normal Heart Sounds

| Sound | What It Is | What You Hear | Where Best Heard |
|-------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| **S1** ("lub") | Closure of **mitral and tricuspid** valves (AV valves) | Marks the BEGINNING of systole | Apex (mitral area) |
| **S2** ("dub") | Closure of **aortic and pulmonic** valves (semilunar valves) | Marks the END of systole / beginning of diastole | Base (aortic/pulmonic area) |

**S1 and S2 together** = "lub-DUB ... lub-DUB" — the normal cardiac cycle

**How to tell S1 from S2:**
- **Palpate the carotid pulse simultaneously** — the beat you feel coincides with S1 (systole)
- S1 to S2 (systole) is a SHORTER interval than S2 to next S1 (diastole) at normal heart rates
- S1 is louder at the apex; S2 is louder at the base

### Extra Heart Sounds

| Sound | Timing | What It Means | Where Best Heard | Clinical Significance |
|-------|--------|--------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **S3** ("Kentucky") | Early diastole — just after S2 | Rapid ventricular filling into a volume-overloaded or dilated ventricle | **Apex, with the BELL, left lateral decubitus position** | **Pathologic in adults >40** — indicates heart failure, volume overload. Normal in children and young adults |
| **S4** ("Tennessee") | Late diastole — just before S1 | Atrial contraction against a STIFF (non-compliant) ventricle | **Apex, with the BELL** | Indicates diastolic dysfunction, LVH, hypertension, aortic stenosis. **Always abnormal** |
| **S3 + S4 together** | "Tennessee-Kentucky" | Summation gallop with tachycardia | Apex | Ominous — suggests severe heart failure |

**Hearing S3 and S4:**
- These are LOW-pitched — use the **BELL with light pressure**
- Have the patient in **left lateral decubitus** (lying on left side) — brings the heart closer to the chest wall
- They are SUBTLE. In a noisy ED you may not hear them. That's okay — document what you DO hear
- **S3 in a dyspneic patient = heart failure until proven otherwise**

### Murmurs — The Basics

A murmur is turbulent blood flow through valves or abnormal cardiac structures. You don't need to diagnose the murmur — you need to **detect it, describe it, and report it**.

#### Describe a Murmur Using:
1. **Timing:** Systolic (between S1 and S2) vs. diastolic (between S2 and next S1)
2. **Location:** Where is it loudest? (Which auscultation site)
3. **Radiation:** Does it radiate? (Aortic stenosis → carotids; mitral regurgitation → axilla)
4. **Intensity (Grade):**

| Grade | Description |
|-------|------------|
| I | Very faint, heard only with concentration in a quiet room |
| II | Quiet, but heard immediately upon placing stethoscope |
| III | Moderately loud, no thrill |
| IV | Loud with palpable thrill |
| V | Very loud, heard with stethoscope partly off chest, thrill present |
| VI | Heard with stethoscope off chest |

5. **Quality:** Harsh, blowing, rumbling, musical
6. **Shape:** Crescendo, decrescendo, crescendo-decrescendo (diamond), holosystolic (plateau)

#### The Murmurs You're Most Likely to Encounter in the ED

| Murmur | Timing | Location | Radiation | Sound | Think... |
|--------|--------|----------|-----------|-------|----------|
| **Aortic stenosis** | Systolic (crescendo-decrescendo / diamond) | 2nd ICS RSB (aortic) | Carotids | Harsh, medium-pitched | Elderly, calcific valve; syncope, angina, HF |
| **Mitral regurgitation** | Systolic (holosystolic / plateau) | Apex (mitral) | Left axilla | Blowing, high-pitched | Chronic MR (dilated LV) or ACUTE MR (papillary muscle rupture post-MI — emergent!) |
| **Aortic regurgitation** | Diastolic (early, decrescendo) | 3rd ICS LSB (Erb's) | — | Soft, high-pitched, blowing | Wide pulse pressure, bounding pulses. Best heard sitting up, leaning forward, end-expiration |
| **Mitral stenosis** | Diastolic (mid-late, rumbling) | Apex | — | Low-pitched rumble (use BELL) | History of rheumatic fever; AFib common |

> **The ED rule:** Systolic murmurs are common and often benign (flow murmurs in pregnancy, fever, anemia). **Diastolic murmurs are ALWAYS pathologic** — always report.

> **New murmur in an acutely ill patient** (MI, endocarditis, trauma) = emergent echo. This could be a mechanical complication (papillary muscle rupture, VSD).

---
sections.pulmonary-auscultation:
### Auscultation Sites — Systematic Pattern

Listen **bilaterally and symmetrically** — always compare the same location left vs. right before moving to the next level:

**Anterior chest (at least 6 sites):**
- 2nd ICS bilaterally (apices)
- 4th ICS bilaterally (middle lobes / lingula)
- 6th ICS bilaterally (bases)

**Posterior chest (at least 6 sites — preferred if patient can sit up):**
- Above scapulae bilaterally (apices)
- Between scapulae bilaterally (upper lobes)
- Below scapulae bilaterally (lower lobes)

**Lateral chest:**
- Midaxillary line, 4th-6th ICS bilaterally

> **Posterior is BEST for lung sounds** — more lung tissue, less bone/muscle interference. If the patient can sit up, listen from behind.

### Technique
1. Use the **diaphragm**
2. Ask patient to breathe **deeply through their mouth** — in and out
3. Listen to **one full respiratory cycle** (inspiration + expiration) at each site
4. **Compare side to side** at the same level — the most important comparison
5. Note: inspiration, expiration, any additional (adventitious) sounds

### Normal Breath Sounds

| Sound | Where Heard | Character | I:E Ratio |
|-------|-------------|-----------|-----------|
| **Vesicular** | Over most of both lung fields | Soft, rustling, heard mainly during INSPIRATION, fading in expiration | I > E (3:1) |
| **Bronchovesicular** | Over mainstem bronchi (1st-2nd ICS anteriorly, between scapulae posteriorly) | Moderate pitch, heard equally in inspiration and expiration | I = E (1:1) |
| **Bronchial (tracheal)** | Over trachea only | Loud, high-pitched, tubular, heard mainly during EXPIRATION | E > I |

> **Key principle:** If you hear bronchial breath sounds where you should hear vesicular sounds (i.e., in the periphery of the lung), that's **abnormal** — it means there is consolidation (pneumonia), atelectasis, or fluid conducting sound directly from the airways. This is a significant finding.

### Adventitious (Abnormal) Breath Sounds

| Sound | Character | Timing | Indicates | Clinical Significance |
|-------|-----------|--------|-----------|----------------------|
| **Crackles (rales)** — fine | High-pitched, short, popping/crackling; like rolling hair between fingers near ear | Usually END of inspiration | Fluid in alveoli, atelectasis, fibrosis | **Bilateral crackles in dyspneic patient = think heart failure or Flash Pulmonary Edema** |
| **Crackles (rales)** — coarse | Low-pitched, louder, bubbling/gurgling | Inspiration and sometimes expiration | Secretions in larger airways, pulmonary edema, bronchitis | Ask patient to cough — if crackles clear, they were from secretions, not alveolar fluid |
| **Wheezes** | High-pitched, musical, continuous | Usually EXPIRATION (can be both) | Airway narrowing (bronchospasm, mucus, edema) | Think Asthma, COPD, allergic reaction. **Absence of wheezing in a severe asthmatic = ominous (no air movement)** |
| **Rhonchi** | Low-pitched, snoring/gurgling, continuous | Expiration (sometimes inspiration) | Secretions in large airways | Often clears or changes with coughing |
| **Stridor** | High-pitched, harsh, monophonic | INSPIRATION (usually) | Upper airway obstruction (above glottis) | **EMERGENCY:** Foreign body, angioedema, epiglottitis, croup. Prepare for airway intervention |
| **Pleural friction rub** | Creaky, grating, like leather rubbing | Both inspiration and expiration | Inflamed pleural surfaces rubbing | Pleurisy, PE, pneumonia. Distinct from pericardial rub (pericardial rub persists when breath held; pleural rub stops) |
| **Diminished/absent** | Quieter than expected or no sound | — | Pneumothorax, pleural effusion, atelectasis, severe bronchospasm, obesity | **Unilateral absent breath sounds in trauma = tension pneumothorax until proven otherwise** |

### The Critical ED Lung Sound Patterns

| What You Hear | Where | Context | Most Likely | Action |
|--------------|-------|---------|-------------|--------|
| **Bilateral fine crackles, bases to apices** | Both lungs | Dyspnea, hypertension, JVD | Pulmonary edema / Flash Pulmonary Edema | BiPAP, NTG, diuretics |
| **Bilateral fine crackles, bases only** | Both lower lobes | Elderly, immobile, post-op | Atelectasis (or early HF) | Incentive spirometry, ambulation; assess for HF |
| **Unilateral crackles** | One side | Fever, cough, pleuritic pain | Pneumonia | CXR, antibiotics |
| **Unilateral absent breath sounds** | One side | Trauma, sudden SOB, chest pain | Pneumothorax or hemothorax | CXR (or skip to needle decompression if tension) |
| **Bilateral wheezes** | Both lungs | SOB, history of asthma/COPD | Bronchospasm | Albuterol, assess severity |
| **Inspiratory stridor** | Upper airway | Swelling, foreign body, voice changes | Upper airway obstruction | Prepare for emergent airway |
| **"Silent chest"** | Bilateral | Severe asthma, not moving air | Near-respiratory arrest | **This is worse than wheezing** — intubation may be imminent |

---
sections.abdominal-auscultation:
Less critical in the ED than cardiac and pulmonary, but still relevant:

### Technique
- Use **diaphragm**, light pressure
- Listen in **all 4 quadrants**
- Listen for **at least 15-30 seconds per quadrant** before declaring absent bowel sounds (may need up to 5 minutes to confirm absent — but in practice, 1-2 minutes total is reasonable in the ED)
- **Auscultate BEFORE palpation** (palpation can stimulate bowel sounds)

### Findings

| Sound | Character | Significance |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Normal** | Intermittent clicks and gurgles, 5-30/min | Normal peristalsis |
| **Hyperactive** | Loud, frequent, high-pitched, "tinkling" or "rushing" | Early bowel obstruction, gastroenteritis, diarrhea |
| **Hypoactive** | Rare, quiet sounds | Ileus, post-operative, peritonitis (late finding) |
| **Absent** | No sounds after extended listening | Peritonitis, paralytic ileus, advanced obstruction |
| **Bruits** | Continuous swooshing/blowing sound over a vessel | Vascular: renal artery stenosis (flanks), abdominal aortic aneurysm (midline epigastric), hepatic artery stenosis |

> **Abdominal bruits:** If you hear a bruit over the aorta or renal arteries, report it. This may indicate Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm or renal artery stenosis.

---
sections.how-to-get-better-at-auscultation:
This is the practical section — because reading about sounds is not the same as hearing them.

### Deliberate Practice Strategy
1. **Listen to every patient, even when it's not required** — take 30 extra seconds during vitals to listen to heart and lungs. Volume of exposure = pattern recognition
2. **Start with normals** — you can't recognize abnormal until you have a solid mental model of normal. Listen to 100 normal hearts and lungs before worrying about murmurs
3. **Compare side to side, always** — this is the single most important technique for lung sounds. Is the right the same as the left? If not, why?
4. **Use your phone:** There are excellent auscultation apps with real recordings (Littmann Learning, 3M Stethoscope app). Listen to S3, S4, murmurs, crackles, wheezes while commuting
5. **Correlate what you hear with imaging** — listened and heard crackles? Check the CXR. What did you hear corresponds to what you see. Over time, your ears calibrate
6. **Ask experienced nurses and providers** — "I hear something at the apex I'm not sure about — can you listen?" Nobody will fault you for wanting to learn
7. **Practice in quiet moments** — the worst time to improve your auscultation skills is during a code. Practice during down time, on stable patients, when you can take your time
8. **Your Shoreline heart sounds MP3s** are at `C:\Users\stace\Dropbox\old coursework\shoreline_nursing\7-adult-heart-sounds-mp3` — revisit these regularly

### The Honest Reality in the ED
- **It's loud.** Monitors beeping, people talking, IV pumps alarming. You won't always hear subtle findings
- **Patients are often supine and can't sit up.** Posterior lung exam isn't always possible. Do the best you can with anterior/lateral
- **Obese patients are harder to auscultate.** This is normal. Press firmly, listen longer, use optimal positioning when possible
- **You will miss things.** Everyone does. The key is consistent, systematic assessment so that what you CAN hear informs your clinical picture. Over time, your ears get better

---

clinicalPearls: **"Bilateral crackles + dyspnea + JVD = heart failure until proven otherwise."** You don't need to identify the exact type of crackle. If both lung bases are wet and the patient is struggling to breathe, you have a clinical picture that demands action. See [[Acute Heart Failure]], [[Flash Pulmonary Edema]].
**"A silent chest is scarier than a wheezy chest."** A severe asthmatic who stops wheezing isn't getting better — they've stopped moving air. This is pre-arrest. Prepare for intubation.

**"If you hear a new murmur in a sick patient, that's an emergency."** New murmur + acute MI = possible papillary muscle rupture or VSD. New murmur + fever = possible endocarditis. New murmur in any acutely ill patient = urgent echo.

**"Use the bell for S3 and S4 — and barely touch the skin."** These low-frequency sounds are filtered out by the diaphragm and by pressing too hard with the bell. Light contact, left lateral decubitus, quiet room. If you've never heard an S3, it's probably because you've always used the diaphragm.

**"Compare left to right, not top to bottom."** The power of lung auscultation isn't hearing each sound in isolation — it's detecting ASYMMETRY. One side quieter than the other? One side crackling and the other clear? That asymmetry is the finding.
relatedNotes: Heart Failure with Reduced Ejection Fraction

blood-administration.title: Blood Administration

sections.definition:
Blood product administration is a high-risk nursing procedure requiring strict verification, monitoring, and documentation. Unlike most IV infusions, transfusion errors can be immediately fatal — and the majority of fatal transfusion reactions trace back to preventable identification or clerical errors at the bedside. Every step of this procedure exists because someone died when it was skipped.

This note covers the **procedural workflow** — the step-by-step bedside process. For detailed pharmacology of blood components (indications, dosing thresholds, compatibility charts), see Blood Products and Transfusion.

---
sections.scope-of-practice:
- **RNs can independently administer blood products** in all 50 US states per RN scope of practice
- Most facilities require **annual blood administration competency** (didactic + skills validation)
- Two licensed nurses (RN or LPN in some states) must perform bedside verification — this is **not optional and cannot be delegated**
- LPNs: scope varies by state; in Washington State, LPNs may monitor an established transfusion but may not initiate
- Students: may assist with monitoring under direct supervision but do not serve as one of the two verifying nurses
- The RN is responsible for the **entire transfusion episode** — from consent through post-transfusion assessment

---
sections.blood-products-quick-reference:
| Product | Purpose | Expected Effect | ABO Requirement | Typical Volume | Typical Rate |
|---------|---------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|-------------|
| **PRBCs** | Increase O2 carrying capacity | 1 unit raises Hgb ~1 g/dL, Hct ~3% | ABO/Rh compatible required | ~300 mL/unit | 1.5-2 hr/unit (after initial slow start) |
| **FFP** | Replaces ALL clotting factors | Corrects INR/PT; no numeric "per unit" rise | ABO compatible required | ~250 mL/unit | 30-60 min/unit |
| **Platelets** | Restore platelet count for hemostasis | 1 apheresis unit raises plt ~30,000-50,000/mcL | ABO preferred but NOT required | ~200-300 mL | 15-30 min (rapid) |
| **Cryoprecipitate** | Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, vWF, Factor XIII | Pooled 10 units = 1 dose; raises fibrinogen ~70 mg/dL | ABO preferred | ~150 mL (pooled 10 units) | 10-30 min (rapid) |

> **Key distinction:** Platelets do not strictly require ABO match (ABO-preferred is ideal but cross-matching is not required). PRBCs and FFP **must** be ABO compatible. In emergencies: O-negative PRBCs and AB plasma are universal.

---
sections.pre-transfusion-checklist:
### 1. Order and Consent
- [ ] Verify provider order specifying product, number of units, rate, and any special requirements (irradiated, CMV-safe, leukoreduced, washed)
- [ ] Obtain **informed consent** — patient (or surrogate) must understand risks, benefits, and alternatives
- [ ] Document consent in the medical record before blood leaves the blood bank

### 2. Type and Screen / Crossmatch
- [ ] **Type and Screen (T&S):** Determines patient's ABO/Rh type and screens for unexpected antibodies
- [ ] **Crossmatch:** Verifies compatibility between specific donor unit and patient's plasma
- [ ] **72-hour rule:** T&S is valid for 72 hours. If the patient has been **transfused or pregnant within the past 3 months**, a new sample is required because new antibodies may have formed
- [ ] Specimen must be properly labeled at the bedside at the time of draw — mislabeled specimens are rejected by the blood bank

### 3. IV Access
- [ ] **18-gauge or larger** peripheral IV for PRBCs (allows adequate flow, prevents mechanical hemolysis)
- [ ] **20-gauge is acceptable** for non-PRBC products or when larger access cannot be obtained — but flow rate will be slower
- [ ] Central venous catheters: acceptable for transfusion per manufacturer and facility policy
- [ ] **Dedicated line or Y-tubing with NS only**
- [ ] **NEVER run with:**
  - Lactated Ringer's (LR) — calcium in LR chelates citrate anticoagulant in blood products, causing clot formation in the tubing
  - Dextrose solutions (D5W, D5NS) — causes RBC hemolysis
  - Any medication — nothing is added to or piggybacked into blood products, ever

### 4. Blood Tubing and Filter
- [ ] **Standard blood administration set** with inline 170-260 micron filter (traps clots, fibrin, and debris)
- [ ] Filter must be used for ALL blood products — no exceptions
- [ ] Change tubing and filter per facility policy (typically every 4 hours or every 4 units, whichever comes first)
- [ ] **Blood warmers** — used for rapid/massive transfusion, cold agglutinin disease, or neonates; NOT routine for standard transfusion

### 5. Baseline Assessment
- [ ] **Vitals within 30 minutes prior:** Temperature, heart rate, blood pressure, respiratory rate, SpO2
- [ ] Assess for signs of fluid overload at baseline (especially in elderly, Chronic Kidney Disease, heart failure patients)
- [ ] Document baseline lung sounds, JVP, and peripheral edema in high-risk patients
- [ ] Verify patient has no history of transfusion reactions — if prior reactions, ensure pre-medication orders are in place (typically acetaminophen + diphenhydramine)

---
sections.two-nurse-bedside-verification:
This is the **single most critical safety step**. Most fatal acute hemolytic reactions result from wrong-patient/wrong-unit errors.

### What Both Nurses Verify — Together, at Bedside

| Check | Source 1 | Source 2 |
|-------|----------|----------|
| **Patient identity** | Ask patient to state full name and DOB | Verify against wristband (not room number, not verbal confirmation alone) |
| **Unit number** | Blood product bag label | Blood bank tag/requisition slip |
| **ABO/Rh type** | Blood product bag label | Blood bank tag AND patient's T&S results |
| **Expiration date/time** | Blood product bag label | Confirm not expired |
| **Patient MRN** | Patient wristband | Blood bank tag |
| **Special requirements** | Order (irradiated, CMV-safe, etc.) | Product label confirms requirements met |

### Verification Rules
- Both nurses must be **physically present at the bedside** — no phone verification, no verifying from the nurses' station
- **Each nurse independently checks** — do not read information aloud for the other nurse to confirm; each reads and compares independently
- If **ANY discrepancy** exists — STOP. Do not administer. Contact blood bank immediately
- Document both nurses' names and verification time

---
sections.administration-procedure:
### Starting the Transfusion

1. **Prime blood tubing** with NS through the blood filter
2. **Spike the blood product bag** using the other arm of the Y-tubing
3. **Start SLOW:** 2 mL/min (~50 mL/hr) for the **first 15 minutes**
   - Most severe transfusion reactions (acute hemolytic, anaphylaxis) occur within the **first 15 mL**
   - By running slowly, you limit the volume of incompatible blood delivered before recognizing the reaction
4. **Stay with the patient** for the first 15 minutes — do not leave the bedside
5. **Obtain vitals at 15 minutes** — compare to baseline
6. If no signs of reaction, **increase to ordered rate** to complete the unit within the appropriate timeframe

### Vital Signs Schedule

| Timepoint | Action |
|-----------|--------|
| **Baseline** | T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — within 30 min before starting |
| **15 minutes** | T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — compare to baseline; assess for reaction |
| **30 minutes** | T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 (some facilities combine with 15-min or omit) |
| **End of unit** | T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — document completion time and total volume |
| **1 hour post** | T, HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — assess for delayed reactions (especially TRALI) |

### The 4-Hour Rule

- Each unit must be **completed within 4 hours** from the time it leaves the blood bank
- Blood products at room temperature for >4 hours support bacterial growth — the unit must be discarded
- **Plan your rate accordingly:** if a unit is 300 mL and must finish in 4 hours maximum, you need at least 75 mL/hr after the slow start period
- In patients at risk for TACO (Transfusion-Associated Circulatory Overload), you may need to request single units with slower rates — but each unit still must finish within 4 hours

### What Can and Cannot Run with Blood

| Compatible | Incompatible |
|-----------|-------------|
| **0.9% Normal Saline (NS)** — the ONLY compatible fluid | Lactated Ringer's (calcium chelates citrate) |
| | Dextrose solutions (D5W, D5NS — cause hemolysis) |
| | **Any medication** — no exceptions |
| | TPN, lipids, any other IV fluid |

> **Rule: Nothing goes in the blood line except blood and NS.** If the patient needs medications during transfusion, use a separate IV site.

---
sections.transfusion-reactions-recognition-and-re:
### Reaction Identification Table

| Reaction | Onset | Key Signs | Cause | Severity | Immediate Action |
|----------|-------|-----------|-------|----------|-----------------|
| **Acute Hemolytic** | Minutes | Fever, chills, flank/back pain, hemoglobinuria (dark red urine), hypotension, tachycardia, DIC | ABO incompatibility (wrong blood to wrong patient) | **Life-threatening** | **STOP immediately** — NS wide open — send blood bank samples — call provider STAT |
| **Febrile Non-Hemolytic (FNHTR)** | During or within 4 hr | Fever (temp rise >=1C), chills, rigors — VS otherwise stable | WBC antibodies or cytokines in product | Mild-Moderate | Stop transfusion — rule out hemolytic first — acetaminophen — may restart if confirmed FNHTR |
| **Allergic (Mild)** | During transfusion | Urticaria (hives), pruritus, localized flushing — NO respiratory or hemodynamic compromise | Sensitivity to donor plasma proteins | Mild | Slow or pause rate — diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV — may resume if symptoms resolve and vitals stable |
| **Anaphylactic** | Within minutes | Hypotension, bronchospasm, stridor, angioedema, diffuse urticaria, cardiovascular collapse | Anti-IgA antibodies (in IgA-deficient patients) or severe plasma protein allergy | **Life-threatening** | **STOP immediately** — epinephrine 0.3-0.5 mg IM — supportive care — future: washed or IgA-deficient products |
| **TRALI** | Within 6 hr | Acute respiratory distress, bilateral pulmonary infiltrates on CXR, hypoxemia, possible fever/hypotension | Donor antibodies activate neutrophils in recipient's pulmonary vasculature | **Life-threatening** | **STOP** — supplemental O2 — may need intubation — **NOT fluid overload so NO diuretics** — supportive care |
| **TACO** | During or within 6 hr | Dyspnea, hypertension, JVD, crackles, peripheral edema, elevated BNP | Volume overload — too much, too fast (elderly, HF, CKD) | Serious | Slow or stop — sit patient upright — diuretics (furosemide) — O2 — notify provider |
| **Delayed Hemolytic** | 2-14 days post | Unexplained drop in Hgb, new jaundice, positive DAT (Coombs), dark urine | Anamnestic (memory) antibody response to prior transfusion antigen exposure | Moderate | Usually self-limited — notify blood bank — new T&S — supportive — monitor renal function |
| **Bacterial Contamination** | During or shortly after | High fever, rigors, severe hypotension, shock — rapid clinical deterioration | Bacteria in product (highest risk: platelets stored at room temp) | **Life-threatening** | **STOP** — cultures from patient AND product bag — broad-spectrum antibiotics STAT — fluid resuscitation |

### TRALI vs. TACO — The Critical Distinction

Both cause respiratory distress during or after transfusion. The treatment is **opposite** — getting this wrong causes harm.

| Feature | TRALI | TACO |
|---------|-------|------|
| **Blood pressure** | Normal or LOW | HIGH |
| **JVD** | Absent | Present |
| **BNP** | Normal | Elevated |
| **CXR** | Bilateral infiltrates (looks like ARDS) | Pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, pleural effusions |
| **Fluid balance** | Normal or negative | Positive |
| **Treatment** | Supportive — O2, possible ventilation — **NO diuretics** | Diuretics, fluid restriction, sit upright |
| **Mechanism** | Immune-mediated lung injury | Hydrostatic pressure overload |

---
sections.transfusion-reaction-protocol-step-by-st:
When **any** transfusion reaction is suspected:

1. **STOP the transfusion immediately** — clamp the blood tubing
2. **Keep the IV line open** with NS through a new line or via the Y-tubing NS port
3. **Stay with the patient** — do not leave
4. **Assess ABCs** — airway, breathing, circulation — intervene for life threats first
5. **Obtain a new set of vital signs** immediately
6. **Notify the provider** — describe signs and symptoms, timing, and vital sign changes
7. **Notify the blood bank** — they will issue instructions for specimen collection
8. **Send to the blood bank:**
   - The blood product bag (even if empty) with attached tubing
   - A freshly drawn patient blood sample (lavender and red top tubes — for repeat type and crossmatch, DAT, hemolysis markers)
   - A post-reaction urine specimen (check for hemoglobinuria)
9. **Do NOT discard anything** — the blood bag, tubing, and all associated labels are evidence
10. **Document thoroughly:** time of reaction, signs/symptoms, interventions, provider notification, blood bank notification, specimens sent, patient response

---
sections.massive-transfusion-protocol-mtp:
MTP is activated for **life-threatening hemorrhage** when standard transfusion cannot keep pace with blood loss. The procedural workflow changes significantly.

### Activation Criteria (facility-specific, but commonly)
- Anticipated need for >=10 units PRBCs in 24 hours
- Active hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >120)
- Estimated blood loss >1500 mL or ongoing uncontrolled bleeding
- Clinical judgment — do not wait for lab results to activate

### MTP Procedural Differences from Standard Transfusion

| Standard Transfusion | Massive Transfusion |
|---------------------|---------------------|
| Slow start at 2 mL/min x 15 min | **No slow start** — rapid infuser wide open |
| Two-nurse verification for each unit | Two-nurse verification still required — but done rapidly |
| Standard blood tubing | **Rapid infuser device** (Level 1, Belmont) — delivers warmed blood at up to 500-1500 mL/min |
| Room temperature infusion OK | **Blood warmer required** — rapid cold blood infusion causes hypothermia, coagulopathy, cardiac dysrhythmias |
| Single unit, then reassess | **Coolers of products delivered in ratios** — typically 6 PRBCs : 6 FFP : 1 apheresis platelet per round |
| Post-transfusion labs after completion | **Frequent labs during MTP:** CBC, coags, fibrinogen, ionized calcium, ABG q30-60 min |

### The 1:1:1 Ratio
- Goal: **1 unit PRBCs : 1 unit FFP : 1 unit Platelets** (balanced resuscitation)
- Prevents **dilutional coagulopathy** — if you only give PRBCs without replacing clotting factors, the patient will bleed to death with a normal Hgb
- Cryoprecipitate added when fibrinogen drops below 150-200 mg/dL
- Tranexamic Acid (TXA): administer within 3 hours of injury onset (CRASH-2 trial evidence)

### Calcium Replacement During MTP
- Citrate preservative in blood products **chelates ionized calcium**
- Massive transfusion causes **hypocalcemia** which worsens coagulopathy AND causes myocardial depression
- Monitor ionized calcium frequently — replace with **calcium chloride 1g IV** (central line preferred) or calcium gluconate 3g IV (peripheral OK) when iCa <1.0 mmol/L
- See Fluid and Electrolyte Balance for calcium physiology

---
sections.documentation-requirements:
Every transfusion episode must include documentation of:

| Element | Details |
|---------|---------|
| **Consent** | Date, time, who obtained, patient/surrogate understanding |
| **Two-nurse verification** | Both nurses' names, time of verification, confirmation that all identifiers matched |
| **Product information** | Unit number, product type, ABO/Rh, expiration, blood bank issue time |
| **Start time** | Time transfusion initiated |
| **Vital signs** | Baseline, 15 min, 30 min, end of unit, 1 hr post (minimum) |
| **Rate and volume** | Initial rate, rate changes, total volume infused |
| **Stop time** | Time transfusion completed or discontinued |
| **Patient response** | Tolerance of transfusion, any signs/symptoms, post-transfusion assessment |
| **Reactions** | If any: time of onset, signs/symptoms, interventions, provider notified, blood bank notified, specimens sent, patient outcome |
| **IV site** | Gauge, location, condition throughout transfusion |

---
sections.special-considerations:
### High-Risk Populations for TACO
- Elderly (>65)
- Heart failure patients
- Chronic Kidney Disease / dialysis patients
- Small body habitus / low body weight
- **Prevention:** Transfuse one unit at a time, slower rate (max 1 mL/kg/hr), consider pre-transfusion diuretic (furosemide 20-40 mg IV), monitor I&O closely

### Jehovah's Witness Patients
- May decline all or some blood products — respect autonomy
- Document informed refusal thoroughly
- Discuss alternatives with the provider: cell salvage (intraoperative), erythropoietin, iron supplementation, Tranexamic Acid (TXA), permissive anemia
- Some patients accept certain fractions (albumin, clotting factors) but not whole blood or PRBCs — clarify specifically

### Emergency / Uncrossmatched Blood
- When time does not allow for full crossmatch: **O-negative PRBCs** and **AB plasma**
- A rapid crossmatch (electronic or immediate spin) takes ~5-10 minutes
- Full crossmatch (antiglobulin phase) takes ~45-60 minutes
- In trauma: do not delay resuscitation waiting for crossmatched blood — uncrossmatched O-negative saves lives

---
sections.cross-references:
- Blood Products and Transfusion — Pharmacology reference for blood components, compatibility charts, indications, and dosing thresholds
- Coagulation and Hemostasis — Clotting cascade physiology and factor functions
- Coagulation Factor Concentrates — Specific factor replacement products (PCC, factor VIII, fibrinogen concentrate)
- Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy — Pathophysiology of hemorrhagic coagulopathy and resuscitation strategy
- Sepsis — Bacterial contamination reactions may present as sepsis
- Shock — Hemorrhagic shock requiring massive transfusion
- Fluid and Electrolyte Balance — Calcium physiology and compartment fluid dynamics
- Chronic Kidney Disease — High-risk population for TACO

---

#procedure #transfusion #ed-critical

clinicalPearls: **The first 15 minutes are the most dangerous.** Acute hemolytic reactions and anaphylaxis declare themselves within the first 15 mL of infusion. The slow start exists to limit the volume of incompatible blood before you can recognize and stop the reaction. Never leave the bedside during this window.

**Never add anything to blood.** No medications, no dextrose, no LR, no piggybacks. The only fluid that touches blood tubing is 0.9% NS. If your patient needs antibiotics or pressors during a transfusion, use a different IV site. There are no exceptions.

**When in doubt, stop the transfusion.** If you are uncertain whether a patient is having a reaction, the safest action is always to stop. You can investigate, call the provider, and send labs — but you cannot un-transfuse incompatible blood. Err on the side of stopping.

**TRALI vs. TACO — one needs oxygen and time, the other needs diuretics — know the difference.** TRALI is immune-mediated lung injury that mimics ARDS; giving diuretics will cause hypovolemia in a patient who is not volume-overloaded. TACO is straightforward fluid overload; withholding diuretics worsens pulmonary edema. BNP and blood pressure are your differentiators.

**Calcium is the silent killer in massive transfusion.** Citrate in stored blood chelates ionized calcium. After 4-6 units of rapid transfusion, your patient may develop critical hypocalcemia — manifesting as coagulopathy that will not correct with more blood products, plus myocardial depression that mimics cardiogenic shock. Check ionized calcium early and replace aggressively.

**The bedside verification is where you prevent the fatal error.** Most transfusion fatalities are not caused by exotic immunologic reactions — they are caused by giving the right blood to the wrong patient. Two nurses, at the bedside, independently checking identifiers. Do not rush this step. Do not treat it as a formality.

**Temperature matters.** A fever of >=1C above baseline during transfusion is a reaction until proven otherwise. It may be a benign febrile non-hemolytic reaction — or it may be the first sign of acute hemolysis or bacterial contamination. Stop, assess, and rule out the dangerous causes before assuming it is benign.

button-battery-ingestion.title: Button Battery Ingestion

sections.mechanism-of-injury:
Button (disc) batteries cause injury through **electrical current**, NOT chemical burn or pressure alone:

1. **Hydroxide radical generation** — even a depleted battery generates enough current in moist tissue to electrolyze water → OH⁻ (hydroxide) generation at the negative pole → local alkaline liquefactive necrosis
2. **Tissue pH can reach 12–13** at the injury site within minutes → full-thickness burns within 2 hours
3. **Most dangerous location:** Esophagus — snug fit creates sustained tissue contact. Can erode into the aorta → tracheoesophageal fistula → aortoesophageal fistula → **fatal hemorrhage**

**Key fact:** Lithium 3V coin cells (20 mm diameter — the most common type in remote controls, car key fobs, hearing aids) are most dangerous due to their larger size (lodge more easily) and higher voltage.

---
sections.recognition-and-triage-pam-training:
> **Source:** MHS Honey Use with Battery Ingestion Protocol (Revised 1/26/2026) via Clinical Educator (Pam)

### When to Suspect Button Battery Ingestion

- Child with sudden-onset dysphagia, drooling, or food refusal
- Coughing, choking, gagging, or respiratory distress in a young child
- Caregiver witnessed or suspects ingestion of a small round object
- Crying, neck or chest discomfort in a non-verbal child
- Vomiting with or without blood
- ANY child <5 years with unexplained symptoms — **ask about small batteries at home**

> **A child can be completely asymptomatic with a lodged esophageal battery.** No symptoms does NOT rule out esophageal foreign body.

---
sections.immediate-management:
### 1. Imaging STAT

- **2-view X-ray (AP and lateral) of neck, chest, and abdomen** — stat
- Button battery appearance: **"Double halo" or "step-off" on AP view** — the battery has a rim shadow from its bilaminar structure. This distinguishes it from coins (which appear smooth/solid)
- Determine battery location:
  - **Esophagus** → emergency endoscopy (≤2 hours)
  - **Stomach or beyond** → management depends on age, symptoms, and battery size (see below)

### 2. Honey Protocol — MHS Specific `#pam-training`

> **Indication:** Patients **>1 year of age** with confirmed or suspected button battery ingestion, while awaiting endoscopy (esophageal batteries only — not for stomach/beyond)

**Goal:** Honey creates a thin protective layer over the tissue, neutralizes the alkaline hydroxide injury, and may slow tissue damage during the interval to endoscopy.

**This is a bridge — NOT a substitute for emergency endoscopy.**

| Parameter | Details |
|-----------|---------|
| **Who** | Patients >1 year of age ONLY |
| **Contraindications** | <1 year (botulism risk), honey allergy |
| **Dose** | **10 mL (10 g) every 10 minutes for 1 hour** (maximum 6 doses = 60 mL total) |
| **Route** | Oral — given in a dosing cup, drawn up with oral syringe |
| **Timing** | Start as soon as battery ingestion is confirmed OR strongly suspected; do NOT wait for imaging if clinical suspicion is high and imaging is being obtained |
| **Storage** | Honey may be stored with nutrition supplies as a non-drug food item |
| **If using packets** | Write expiration date on container; packets are measured in grams (1 g ≈ 1 mL) |
| **Do NOT mix with medications** |  |

**Sucralfate** (1 g/10 mL suspension) may be used as an alternative if honey is unavailable — same dosing interval, same rationale.

### 3. Emergency Endoscopy

- **Esophageal battery = GI emergency — call GI/Peds Surgery immediately**
- Goal: Removal within **2 hours** of suspected ingestion if possible
- NPO immediately — do not give additional liquids (honey administration is done per protocol while awaiting endo)
- Prepare for OR/procedure suite

### 4. Post-Removal Monitoring

After endoscopic removal:
- Esophagoscopy to assess extent of injury
- Monitor for delayed complications: stricture, tracheoesophageal fistula, aortoesophageal fistula
- High-risk patients (burns at endoscopy) → admit for monitoring; CT angiography may be needed
- Even after removal, tissue necrosis can progress for days — **delayed hemorrhage can be catastrophic**

---
sections.location-specific-management:
| Battery Location | Symptoms? | Action |
|-----------------|----------|--------|
| **Esophagus** | Any | Emergency endoscopy ≤2 hours. Honey protocol while awaiting |
| **Stomach** | Asymptomatic, age ≥5, battery ≥15mm | Serial X-rays q4h; if in stomach at 4h → endoscopy |
| **Stomach** | Symptomatic (vomiting, pain, hematemesis) | Endoscopy regardless |
| **Stomach** | Age <5 years, battery ≥20mm | Endoscopy within 24h |
| **Beyond pylorus** | Asymptomatic | Daily X-rays until passed; stool checks |
| **Beyond pylorus** | Symptomatic | Endoscopy vs. surgery based on location |

---
sections.nursingConsiderations:
- **ESI 1–2 for any suspected esophageal battery** — this is a time-sensitive emergency
- **Do not give regular food or drink** while awaiting evaluation — NPO except honey per protocol
- **Initiate honey protocol immediately** once esophageal battery is suspected or confirmed; document time of each dose
- **Monitor for respiratory distress** — an esophageal battery can press on the posterior trachea → stridor, respiratory compromise
- **Prepare parents** — this is extremely frightening. Parents often feel guilty. Clear, calm communication is essential. Explain that the honey is a bridging measure and endoscopy is happening
- **Document battery characteristics if known:** Brand, voltage (3V lithium = highest risk), diameter (≥20mm = highest risk for getting stuck)

---
sections.references:
- MHS Honey Use with Battery Ingestion Protocol (Revised 1/26/2026) via Clinical Educator (Pam)
- Litovitz T, et al. Preventing Battery Ingestions: An Analysis of 8,648 Cases. Pediatrics 2010
- National Capital Poison Center: Button Battery Ingestion Triage and Treatment Guidelines (buttonbattery.org)
- Anfang RR, et al. pH-neutralizing esophageal irrigations as a novel mitigating strategy for button battery injury. Laryngoscope 2019

#procedure #pediatric #toxicology #ed-critical #pam-training

clinicalPearls: **The double halo on X-ray is pathognomonic** — learn to recognize it. Coins look like a smooth circle. Button batteries have a distinct double ring on AP view and a step-off on lateral. If you see a smooth coin, it's still foreign body management — but if you see the halo, it's an emergency.

**The esophagus is the kill zone** — a battery in the stomach or bowel is concerning and requires monitoring, but it is rarely immediately fatal. A battery lodged in the esophagus can kill within hours. Triage urgency accordingly.

**Start honey before imaging if you can** — if a caregiver saw the child put something round in their mouth and the child is now drooling, start honey and get imaging simultaneously. Every minute matters.
**Hearing aids are the #1 source** — grandparents' hearing aids are the most common source of ingested button batteries in young children. Ask about hearing aids in the home.
relatedNotes: Chief Complaint - Overdose and Toxic Ingestion

central-line-setup-and-assist-nursing-ro.title: Central Line Setup and Assist (Nursing Role)

sections.why-central-lines-are-placed:
| Indication | Rationale |
|-----------|-----------|
| **Vasopressors/inotropes** | Many require central delivery (norepinephrine, vasopressin — peripheral infiltration causes tissue necrosis) |
| **No peripheral access** | Failed peripheral IVs in critically ill, obese, or IV drug use patients |
| **CVP monitoring** | Hemodynamic assessment (less common now with US guidance) |
| **Rapid volume infusion** | Large-bore introducer sheaths (8.5 Fr) for massive transfusion |
| **Long-term medications** | TPN, chemotherapy, prolonged antibiotics |
| **Hemodialysis access** | Temporary dialysis catheter (Mahurkar) — different from standard CVC |
| **Aspiration of air embolism** | Right atrial placement for known or suspected air embolism |

---
sections.common-sites-know-the-trade-offs:
| Site | Pros | Cons | Infection Risk |
|------|------|------|---------------|
| **Internal jugular (IJ)** | Good landmark, US-guided, low PTX risk | Neck movement, carotid artery risk | Moderate |
| **Subclavian** | Most comfortable for patient, lowest infection risk | Higher PTX risk, non-compressible if arterial puncture | Lowest |
| **Femoral** | Easy access, safe in coagulopathy/emergencies | Highest infection risk, DVT risk, limits mobility | Highest |
| **Antecubital (PICC)** | Low infection risk, nurse-placed | Slower insertion, not truly "central" at insertion | Low |

---
sections.equipment-setup-the-central-line-tray:
### What's In the Kit (Standard CVC Kit)
Most kits contain everything — open it using the inner sterile field as your work surface:
- Drapes (large fenestrated sterile drape + table drape)
- Chlorhexidine prep sticks (2%)
- Syringes (10 mL × several)
- Needles (finder/scout needle, introducer needle)
- Guidewire (J-tip) — handle with extreme care
- Dilator
- Central venous catheter (triple-lumen 7 Fr is most common; note lumen colors: white=distal, blue=medial, brown=proximal)
- Suture (silk 2-0 or 3-0)
- Needle driver, scissors
- Sterile dressing supplies
- Caps/clamps for lumens

### Additional Supplies You Prepare (Outside the Kit)
- Sterile gloves (correct size for provider — ask before opening)
- Sterile gown
- Full sterile drape (head-to-toe maximal barrier precautions)
- Surgical cap and mask — **for everyone in the room**
- Sterile saline for flushing (draw up 10 mL × 3 for each lumen)
- Heparinized saline per unit protocol (some units, or for dialysis catheters)
- Ultrasound machine with sterile probe cover kit (for US-guided placement)
- Suture removal kit if needed post-procedure

---
sections.your-pre-procedure-checklist:
### Environment
- [ ] Announce to room: "Central line going in — everyone mask up"
- [ ] Limit personnel in room during procedure (reduces contamination)
- [ ] Position ultrasound machine on correct side — provider's dominant hand side
- [ ] Turn off overhead fan/AC vents if possible (reduces airborne contamination)

### Patient
- [ ] Explain procedure and obtain verbal consent (or document emergency exception)
- [ ] IV access for sedation/analgesia if ordered (lidocaine is standard local anesthetic)
- [ ] Position per site:
  - **IJ:** Trendelenburg 15°, head turned AWAY from insertion side
  - **Subclavian:** Supine, rolled towel between shoulder blades, slight Trendelenburg
  - **Femoral:** Supine, slight external hip rotation, leg slightly abducted
- [ ] Shave site if needed (hair → increased infection risk)
- [ ] Cardiac monitor and continuous SpO2 on patient
- [ ] Time out completed: patient, site, procedure, allergies confirmed

### Maximal Sterile Barrier Precautions (NON-NEGOTIABLE)
- Cap/hair cover on provider AND all personnel in room
- Surgical mask on provider AND all personnel in room
- Sterile gown on provider
- Sterile gloves on provider (double-gloving common)
- **Full body sterile drape** over patient (not just a small field drape)
- This is a CLABSI prevention bundle requirement — not optional

---
sections.during-the-procedure:
### Your Positioning
- Stand on the **opposite side** of the bed from the provider (or at the foot) — maintains sterile field
- Do NOT reach across the sterile field
- If you need to hand something: open packaging and drop onto the sterile field OR hand to the provider's non-sterile assistant while they retrieve

### Real-Time Roles
- **Call out** if you see a break in sterile technique — politely but immediately: "I noticed the [item] touched the [surface] — would you like to re-glove/replace?"
- **Hand ultrasound probe** with sterile cover in place before procedure starts (provider applies cover to probe themselves using sterile technique)
- **Medication administration:** Lidocaine 1% is drawn up and handed or labeled on the sterile field
- **Monitor patient:** SpO2, cardiac rhythm, patient comfort and communication
- **Guidewire alert:** When the guidewire is out, call it out and watch — it MUST be accounted for at all times. Lost guidewires = retained foreign body sentinel event.

> **Clinical Pearl: "Guidewire in, guidewire out — call it every time."** Before the catheter goes in, confirm the guidewire is still visible and controlled. Before dressing goes on, confirm it's removed. This is a never-event if lost.

### Complications to Watch During Procedure
| Sign | Possible Complication | Action |
|------|----------------------|--------|
| Arterial blood (bright red, pulsatile) | Arterial puncture | Call out immediately; provider applies pressure; do NOT insert catheter |
| Sudden drop in SpO2 or respiratory distress | Pneumothorax (subclavian/IJ) | CXR immediately, prepare for chest tube |
| Dysrhythmia on monitor | Guidewire in right ventricle | Alert provider immediately; pull wire back |
| Hypotension | Vasovagal, blood loss, pneumothorax | Assess, treat cause |
| Air entrainment (Trendelenburg) | Air embolism | Left lateral decubitus + Trendelenburg, O2, call provider |

---
sections.post-procedure:
### Immediate
- [ ] CXR ordered STAT — confirm tip position (goal: junction of SVC and right atrium), rule out pneumothorax
- [ ] Do NOT use line until tip confirmed on CXR (unless emergent situation — document)
- [ ] Flush each lumen with NS, confirm blood return
- [ ] Apply sterile occlusive dressing (chlorhexidine-impregnated disk + transparent film dressing)
- [ ] Label each lumen (many units use colored caps: distal = medication, proximal = IVF, medial = blood draws — per protocol)
- [ ] Sutures: ensure provider sutured catheter to skin; document suture type

### Lumen Management
| Lumen | Typical Use |
|-------|------------|
| **Distal (white)** | Vasopressors, continuous infusions |
| **Medial (blue)** | CVP measurement, blood draws |
| **Proximal (brown)** | IV fluids, boluses |
- Confirm with provider which lumen is designated for which use
- Never leave a lumen clamped without cap — air embolism risk

### CLABSI Prevention Bundle (Every Day Until Line Out)
- [ ] **Daily necessity review:** Is this line still needed? Document medical necessity every shift
- [ ] **Dressing intact and dry:** Change if soiled, wet, or lifting — use chlorhexidine dressing protocol
- [ ] **Dressing change:** Every 7 days with CHG disk; earlier if compromised (sterile gloves + mask + cap)
- [ ] **Cap change:** Needleless caps changed per unit protocol (typically q96h or with tubing changes)
- [ ] **Hub scrub:** Scrub the hub with alcohol or CHG-alcohol wipe for 15 seconds before each access
- [ ] **Line necessity:** Document daily, advocate for removal as soon as no longer needed

> **Clinical Pearl: "Lines are like surgery — every day they're in is a risk."** The CLABSI rate goes up each day a CVC is in place. Your daily necessity check and advocacy for removal is patient safety work.

---
sections.dialysis-catheters-temporary-differences:
- Larger lumen (11–12 Fr), stiffer catheter, typically femoral or IJ
- **Two lumens: red (arterial — blood out) and blue (venous — blood return)**
- Heparinized after each dialysis session — volume varies (1–2 mL per lumen)
- Only nephrology/dialysis team accesses for dialysis
- If emergency access needed: flush heparin lock out first, confirm with provider
- Femoral dialysis catheters: patient CANNOT ambulate — document bedrest order

---
sections.midline-vs-picc-vs-cvc-quick-reference:
| Type | Length | Tip Location | Vesicants OK? | Blood Draw? |
|------|--------|-------------|---------------|-------------|
| Peripheral IV | 1–3 in | Peripheral vein | No | Limited |
| Midline | 3–8 in | Axillary/basilic vein (NOT central) | No | Limited |
| PICC | 40–55 cm | SVC/RA junction | Yes | Yes |
| CVC (IJ/SC/fem) | 15–20 cm | SVC/RA junction | Yes | Yes |

---
sections.documentation-checklist:
- [ ] Time out completed
- [ ] Site, laterality (right/left), catheter type and size
- [ ] Number of lumen and designated use per lumen
- [ ] Provider name, number of attempts
- [ ] Patient tolerance, complications
- [ ] CXR ordered and result (tip position, PTX ruled out)
- [ ] Sterile dressing applied, type
- [ ] Line necessity documented

---
sections.references:
- Arterial Line Setup and Monitoring — arterial access, transducer setup
- EZ-IO Intraosseous Access — emergency access alternative
- Vasopressors and Inotropes — medications requiring central delivery
- Sepsis — central line in the sepsis bundle
- Infection and Infectious Disease — CLABSI pathophysiology
- CDC CLABSI Prevention Guidelines — National Healthcare Safety Network
- MultiCare Central Line Bundle Protocol (per unit policy)

clinicalPearls: **Full body drape is not optional** — studies show maximal sterile barrier precautions reduce CLABSI by over 50% compared to small drapes. If the provider tries to use a small drape, politely bring the full drape.
**Bright red pulsatile blood = arterial** — call it immediately and loudly. A catheter placed in the carotid or subclavian artery can be life-threatening if not recognized before dilation.
**Guidewire is a never-event risk** — you are the safety net. Watch it, count it, call it out when it goes in and when it comes out.
**CXR before use** — except in true extremis (cardiac arrest, no other access), wait for CXR confirmation. An infusion into the mediastinum or pleural space is catastrophic.
**Hub scrub every single time** — "scrub the hub" is not optional. Alcohol or CHG wipe, 15 seconds of friction. This is the most common CLABSI prevention failure.

chest-tube-management.title: Chest Tube Management

sections.indications:
| Indication | Drainage Type | Tube Size (Adult) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| **Pneumothorax** | Air | 14–22 Fr (small bore often sufficient) |
| **Tension pneumothorax** | Air (emergent) | 28–36 Fr or needle decompression first |
| **Hemothorax** | Blood | **28–36 Fr** (large bore required) |
| **Massive hemothorax** | Blood | 36–40 Fr |
| **Pleural effusion** | Fluid | 14–24 Fr (small bore/pigtail often used) |
| **Empyema** | Purulent fluid | 28–36 Fr |
| **Post-surgical** | Air/fluid | Varies by procedure |
sections.equipment-setup-nursing-role:
### Gather Supplies
- Chest tube insertion tray (or individual components)
- Chest tube of appropriate size (per provider order)
- Chest drainage system (Atrium, Pleur-evac — water seal or digital)
- Sterile water for water seal chamber (fill to indicated line)
- Wall suction (usually -20 cmH₂O unless otherwise ordered)
- Local anesthetic (lidocaine 1% with epinephrine)
- Suture material, petroleum gauze, sterile dressings
- Tape, clamps (2 padded)
- PPE for all team members

### Drainage System Setup (Before Insertion)
1. Open drainage system, keep upright
2. **Fill water seal chamber** with sterile water to the 2 cm mark
3. If ordered: fill suction control chamber to prescribed level (usually -20 cmH₂O)
4. Connect to wall suction — gentle, continuous bubbling in suction chamber confirms adequate suction
5. Do NOT connect to patient until chest tube is placed and secured
sections.during-insertion-nursing-responsibilitie:
1. **Position patient:** sitting upright, arm raised on affected side, or lateral decubitus
2. **Time-out** — verify correct patient, correct side
3. **Administer sedation/analgesia** as ordered (procedural sedation or local only)
4. **Assist provider:** hand supplies, maintain sterile field
5. **Connect drainage system** when instructed
6. **Observe for immediate output** — type, color, amount
7. **Secure tube** — provider sutures; nurse applies petroleum gauze at insertion site, then occlusive dressing
sections.post-insertion-monitoring:
### Continuous Assessment

| Parameter | What to Monitor | Frequency |
|-----------|----------------|-----------|
| **Tidaling** | Water level moves with respiration — NORMAL and expected | Continuous |
| **Air leak** | Bubbling in water seal chamber — expected after pneumothorax | Continuous |
| **Drainage output** | Color, amount, rate | Hourly initially, then per order |
| **Dressing integrity** | Dry, intact, occlusive | Every shift + PRN |
| **Chest tube position** | Not kinked, not clamped (unless ordered), dependent | Continuous |
| **Patient assessment** | Respiratory status, breath sounds, SpO₂, pain | q1h initially |
| **CXR** | Confirm tube position and lung expansion | Post-insertion + per order |

### Drainage Output — When to Notify Provider

| Finding | Action |
|---------|--------|
| **> 200 mL/hr for 2+ consecutive hours** | Massive output — notify STAT, may indicate continued bleeding |
| **Sudden large output (> 1500 mL immediately)** | Auto-transfusion may be needed, MTP consideration |
| **Drainage suddenly stops** | Check for kinks, clots, tube displacement — NOT resolving |
| **Change from serous to bloody** | New bleeding source |
| **Purulent drainage** | Infection — obtain culture |
| **Air leak increasing** | Bronchopleural fistula or system leak |

### Tidaling and Air Leak Assessment

**Tidaling (Water Seal Fluctuation):**
- Water level rises during inspiration (spontaneous breathing) or falls during inspiration (mechanical ventilation)
- **Normal and expected** — confirms tube is in pleural space and patent
- **Absence of tidaling** = either lung is fully re-expanded (good) OR tube is kinked/clamped/obstructed (bad) — differentiate with clinical assessment

**Air Leak Assessment:**
- Bubbling in water seal chamber during expiration or cough = air leak
- Grade air leak using the system's scale or descriptively (intermittent vs continuous, small vs large)
- **Expected** in pneumothorax — should gradually decrease over days
- **Increasing or new air leak** = bronchopleural fistula, tube displacement, or system leak
- **To differentiate system leak vs patient leak:** briefly clamp tube close to patient — if bubbling stops, leak is from patient; if continues, leak is in the system
sections.maintaining-the-system:
### DO:
- Keep drainage system **upright and below chest level** at all times
- Keep tubing free of dependent loops (fluid trapped in loops creates back-pressure)
- Encourage patient to cough, deep breathe, use incentive spirometry
- "Milk" or gently squeeze tubing toward drainage system if clots are visible (per facility protocol)
- Mark drainage levels with time on the collection chamber
- Keep tube clamps at bedside (for emergency use)

### DO NOT:
- **Never clamp a chest tube** without a provider order — clamping with an air leak causes tension pneumothorax
- Never raise drainage system above chest level
- Never disconnect tubing without clamping first
- Never strip/forcibly milk the tube (can create dangerous negative pressures)
sections.troubleshooting:
| Problem | Assessment | Action |
|---------|-----------|--------|
| **Tube dislodged/falls out** | Insertion site visible | Cover with petroleum gauze + occlusive dressing (tape 3 sides, leave 1 open as flutter valve), notify provider STAT |
| **System knocked over** | Check connections, water seal level | Refill water seal, check for air leaks, reassess patient |
| **Continuous bubbling in water seal (no air leak expected)** | System leak | Check all connections, tighten, replace if needed |
| **Subcutaneous emphysema** | Crepitus on palpation near site/chest | Expected mildly; if rapidly increasing → tube malposition or inadequate drainage |
| **Patient suddenly SOB/chest pain** | Tension pneumothorax risk | Vital signs, assess breath sounds, CXR, notify provider STAT |
sections.chest-tube-removal-nursing-role:
1. Provider orders removal (typically after: air leak resolved, minimal drainage, lung expanded on CXR)
2. Gather: petroleum gauze, occlusive dressing, tape, suture removal kit
3. **Pre-medicate for pain** (removal is uncomfortable)
4. **Patient instruction:** take deep breath in and bear down (Valsalva) OR exhale fully — provider preference
5. Provider removes tube quickly during Valsalva/end-expiration
6. Apply petroleum gauze + occlusive dressing immediately
7. Post-removal CXR to confirm no pneumothorax
8. Monitor for 1–4 hours post-removal (respiratory status, site assessment)
sections.documentation-in-epic:
- Insertion time, provider, tube size, insertion site
- Type and amount of suction applied
- Initial drainage (amount, color, consistency)
- Hourly I&O (drainage output)
- Tidaling and air leak assessment each shift
- Dressing changes and site assessment
- Patient response and tolerance
- CXR results
sections.cross-references:
- Rapid Sequence Intubation — airway management that may accompany chest tube placement
- Trauma Nursing Assessment (TNCC) — hemothorax/pneumothorax in trauma
- Code Blue Management — tension pneumothorax as reversible cause (H's and T's)
- Respiratory System — pleural space anatomy
- ABG and VBG Interpretation — respiratory status monitoring

clinicalPearls: **Tidaling is your friend** — it confirms the tube is patent and in the right place. Absence of tidaling is NOT always good news; assess clinically.

**If a chest tube falls out** — petroleum gauze, occlusive dressing, tape 3 sides (leaving one side open creates a flutter valve). Taping all 4 sides can cause tension pneumothorax if air is still leaking.
**Never clamp a chest tube for transport** if there's an active air leak. Disconnect from suction, keep water seal, and keep the system below chest level during transport.
**Output > 200 mL/hr** from a hemothorax is the threshold where thoracotomy becomes a consideration. Track output meticulously and communicate early.

**Milking/stripping** is controversial — aggressive stripping creates dangerously high negative pressures. Gentle sequential compression toward the drainage system (if your facility allows) is preferred for clot management.

code-blue-management.title: Code Blue Management

sections.definition:
A Code Blue is the hospital-wide emergency response to cardiac or respiratory arrest requiring immediate resuscitation. The term activates a multidisciplinary team trained in Advanced Cardiovascular Life Support (ACLS) to deliver high-quality CPR, rapid defibrillation, pharmacologic intervention, and post-arrest care. Survival is directly tied to the speed and quality of the response — every minute without CPR and defibrillation decreases survival by 7-10%.

In the ED, cardiac arrest may present as a primary event (witnessed collapse, found down) or as a deterioration of an already-critical patient (septic shock progressing to PEA, respiratory failure leading to hypoxic arrest). Regardless of the mechanism, the nursing response follows the same framework: **recognize, activate, perform high-quality CPR, follow ACLS algorithms, and document meticulously.**

---
sections.team-roles-multicare-health-system-model:
Effective resuscitation depends on clearly defined roles assigned at the start of the code. At MHS, the following roles are standard:

| Role | Responsibilities |
|------|-----------------|
| **Primary RN (Code Leader — Nursing)** | Directs nursing care during the code. Assigns tasks to available staff. Manages medication preparation and administration. Ensures documentation is occurring. Communicates with physician/LIP. Maintains situational awareness of the room |
| **LIP / MD (Code Leader — Medical)** | Directs the overall resuscitation. Manages the airway or delegates to anesthesia/RT. Interprets rhythms and calls for shocks, medications, and interventions. Makes decisions on reversible causes. Calls time of death or directs post-ROSC care |
| **Respiratory Therapist (RT)** | Manages airway and ventilation. Performs bag-valve-mask (BVM) ventilation. Assists with or performs endotracheal intubation. Manages ventilator settings post-intubation. Obtains ABGs |
| **Pharmacist** | Draws up and prepares resuscitation medications. Verifies doses and timing. Oversees medication administration. Tracks cumulative doses of epinephrine, amiodarone, and other agents. Alerts team to drug interactions or contraindications |
| **Compressor (rotating)** | Performs chest compressions. Rotates every 2 minutes to prevent fatigue. Calls out when fatigued. Focuses solely on compression quality |
| **Recorder / Scribe** | Documents all interventions with timestamps. Records rhythm checks, shocks delivered, medications given, and times. Tracks 2-minute cycles and prompts team for pulse/rhythm checks |
| **Support Staff** | Retrieves supplies and equipment (crash cart, backboard, defibrillator). Obtains additional IV access. Manages crowd control and room flow. Contacts family, chaplain, or social work as needed |

**Key principle:** Assign roles verbally and by name at the start of the code. "Sarah, you're on compressions. Mike, you're documenting." Closed-loop communication prevents errors.

---
sections.high-quality-cpr-aha-2020-guidelines:
