- Central line: Site, date of insertion, lumen labeling
- Arterial line: Site, wave quality, zeroed appropriately (transducer at phlebostatic axis = 4th ICS, MAL)
- ICP monitor/EVD: Level, drainage amount and character, alarm parameters
- NG/OG: Type, position verification, last residual
- Foley: Output, urine character, reason for catheter (use catheter care bundle to minimize CAUTI risk)

### Recognizing and Escalating Deterioration
NTICU "call the provider NOW" triggers:
- ICP >20 mmHg sustained >5 minutes despite interventions
- CPP <50 mmHg
- Cushing's Triad: **↑SBP + bradycardia + irregular respirations** = herniation, neurological emergency
- New blown pupil (unilateral fixed and dilated = herniation until proven otherwise)
- GCS drop of ≥2 points
- Unexplained SpO2 <90%
- New onset seizure
- SBP deviating from target (ICH: >160, or <120; SCI: MAP <85)
- Sudden change in ETT CO2 waveform or vent alarms
- EVD drainage: sudden large increase (fresh blood = rebleed), or sudden stop (clogged)

---
sections.domain-4-neurological-assessments:
### GCS — Glasgow Coma Scale
The GCS is your **objective, reproducible** measure of consciousness. Three components, always reported separately AND as sum.

| Component | Score | Description |
|-----------|-------|-------------|
| **Eye Opening (E)** | 4 | Spontaneous |
| | 3 | To voice |
| | 2 | To pain |
| | 1 | None |
| **Verbal Response (V)** | 5 | Oriented |
| | 4 | Confused |
| | 3 | Words only |
| | 2 | Sounds only |
| | 1 | None |
| | T | Intubated (reported as "1T") |
| **Motor Response (M)** | 6 | Follows commands |
| | 5 | Localizes to pain |
| | 4 | Withdrawal to pain |
| | 3 | Flexion — decorticate (arms flex, legs extend) |
| | 2 | Extension — decerebrate (all extension, back arches) |
| | 1 | None |

**GCS thresholds:**
- ≤8 → consider intubation (airway protection concern)
- ≤12 → ICU-level monitoring
- 15 → normal
- Intubated patient: report as "GCS 1T" or "GCS 9T" (omit verbal, add T notation)

**Posturing types:**
- **Decorticate (flexion):** Arms flex/fist toward core — damage to corticospinal tract above brainstem
- **Decerebrate (extension):** Arms extended and pronated, back arching — upper brainstem damage → WORSE prognosis

> 🔬 **Why posturing tells you WHERE the injury is:**
> Motor control is hierarchical — higher brain centers normally suppress and modulate lower ones. The corticospinal tract (motor cortex → spinal cord) modulates movement, and the brainstem provides baseline postural tone.
>
> - **Decorticate posturing** = the cortex and its descending tracts are damaged or disconnected, but the brainstem is still functional. With cortical suppression removed, the brainstem's flexion reflex circuits emerge unopposed → arms flex (toward the "core" = toward the brain that's trying to protect itself). This is bad, but the brainstem is intact.
> - **Decerebrate posturing** = damage is below the cortex, in the upper brainstem (midbrain/pons). The brainstem itself is injured and being compressed. Extensor tone dominates with no higher modulation at all → arms and legs extend, back arches. This is herniation territory — worse prognosis.
>
> The progression from decorticate → decerebrate → flaccid = herniation happening in real time. See Nervous System, Electricity and Excitability (reflex arcs and supraspinal modulation).

> **Clinical Pearl:** Posturing is NOT purposeful movement. Score it as M3 (decorticate) or M2 (decerebrate), not M4/M5.

### Pupillary Assessment
Assessed with penlight in dim environment. Report: **size (mm), equality, reactivity.**

| Finding | Significance | Action |
|---------|-------------|--------|
| PERRL (Pupils Equal, Round, Reactive to Light) | Normal baseline | Document size (e.g., "3 mm PERRL") |
| **Unilateral fixed and dilated (blown pupil)** | Uncal herniation — CN III compression by tentorial herniation | **Emergency — call provider immediately** |
| Bilateral pinpoint (1–2 mm) | Pontine hemorrhage; opioid toxicity | Concern for brainstem injury vs. opioids |
| Bilateral fixed and dilated | Brain death, severe bilateral herniation | Critical — brain death exam |
| Hippus (rhythmic dilation) | May be normal variant, or early CN III involvement | Trend and report |
| Anisocoria without change | Physiologic (up to 1 mm difference normal) | Document — compare to baseline |

> 🔬 **Why a blown pupil means herniation:**
> Pupil size is controlled by two competing systems: the **parasympathetic (constriction)** via CN III (oculomotor nerve, traveling with the nerve from the midbrain to the eye), and the **sympathetic (dilation)** via the cervical sympathetic chain. The parasympathetic fibers of CN III run on the *outside* of the nerve — they are therefore compressed first by any mass effect.
>
> When brain tissue herniates through the tentorial notch (uncal herniation), the uncus of the temporal lobe pushes down and compresses CN III. The fragile parasympathetic fibers are crushed first → pupil loses constriction → sympathetic tone dominates unopposed → **pupil dilates and fixes**. This is a direct, anatomically precise sign of herniation. Unilateral (same side as the herniating mass) → fixed and dilated. Both pupils fixed and dilated → bilateral herniation or brainstem death.
>
> This is why the pupil check is the single most time-sensitive assessment in NTICU — it's a direct window into whether the midbrain is being compressed right now. See Nervous System, ICP Monitoring and Management.

**Pupil Documentation Example:** "R: 3 mm, brisk; L: 3 mm, brisk. PERRL."

### NIHSS — NIH Stroke Scale
Used in stroke patients (all neuro ICU stroke admissions). Score 0–42.
**Full NIHSS required:** at admission, pre-treatment, 2 hrs post-tPA, handoff, shift handoff, any neuro change.

NIHSS assesses: LOC, Gaze, Visual fields, Facial palsy, Motor arms, Motor legs, Limb ataxia, Sensory, Language/aphasia, Dysarthria, Extinction/inattention

### Serial Neurological Checks — What You're Looking For
**Any of these = notify provider immediately:**
- ↓GCS ≥2 points from baseline
- New or worsening focal deficit (new arm/leg weakness, new facial droop)
- Pupil asymmetry (new anisocoria >1 mm) or change in reactivity
- New gaze deviation
- Seizure activity
- New posturing
- Cushing's Triad: Rising SBP + bradycardia + irregular breathing

**Documentation tip:** Always include GCS components separately (E/V/M), pupil findings with size and reactivity, specific deficit descriptions (e.g., "R arm 4/5 strength, downward drift x 10 sec"). "Neuro stable" without specifics is insufficient.

---
sections.domain-5-trauma-care:
### Spinal Precautions
See Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury for full pathophysiology and nursing detail.

**Core principle: Assume spinal injury in ALL trauma patients until imaging clears the spine.**

**Spinal precautions include:**
- **Cervical collar (C-collar):** Maintained until C-spine is cleared by CT/MRI + provider order; do NOT remove without order
- **Logroll technique:** For ALL repositioning — 3-person logroll minimum (one holds head/neck neutral; two roll body in one unit); no rotation, flexion, or lateral bending of the spine
- **Backboard:** Use for transport and initial stabilization; remove ASAP (pressure injury risk begins immediately)
- **HOB:** Flat or as ordered; some SCI patients require specific positioning per neurosurgery
- **C-collar skin care:** Inspect skin under collar q8h; pad over bony prominences; report skin breakdown

**Logroll communication:**
- Designated person controls head: "On my count — 1, 2, 3, roll"
- All team members move simultaneously
- Person at head is in charge of the call; body does NOT move without head person's count

**Clearing the C-spine (criteria for removing precautions):**
1. CT scan negative for fracture/instability AND
2. Patient neurologically intact (or MRI also negative) AND
3. Provider writes order to discontinue

### ICP Management
See also: Intracerebral Hemorrhage, Ischemic Stroke for condition-specific protocols. See ICP Monitoring and Management for the full procedure note.

**Normal ICP:** 0–15 mmHg (adults)
**Treatment threshold:** >20–25 mmHg sustained

**Cerebral Perfusion Pressure (CPP):**
> **CPP = MAP − ICP**
> **Goal CPP: ≥50–70 mmHg** (optimizes cerebral blood flow without ↑ICP)

> 🔬 **The Monro-Kellie Doctrine — why the skull is a trap:**
> The skull is rigid bone — it cannot expand. Inside: brain parenchyma (~80%), blood (~10%), CSF (~10%). Total volume is fixed. When a hemorrhage, edema, or hydrocephalus adds volume, something else must compress or be displaced. Initially the body compensates elegantly: CSF gets pushed out into the spinal subarachnoid space; venous blood is squeezed from the dural sinuses. These mechanisms buy time — but they are limited. When they're exhausted, the pressure-volume curve goes exponential: small additions in volume → massive ICP spikes.
>
> **This is why traumatic hemorrhages can cause "talk and die" presentations** — the patient is initially conscious (compensation working), then crashes when compensation fails. The same milliliter of blood that was tolerated an hour ago now crosses the exponential threshold.
>
> **Every ICP intervention targets one component of the Monro-Kellie equation:**
> - Mannitol/HTS → ↓ brain parenchyma volume (shrinks edematous brain cells)
> - EVD drainage → ↓ CSF volume
> - Hyperventilation → ↓ cerebral blood volume (vasoconstriction)
> - Sedation/NMB → ↓ cerebral metabolic demand → ↓ blood flow needed → ↓ blood volume
> - Decompressive craniectomy → breaks the rigid-box constraint entirely (makes the skull larger)
> See Homeostasis and Feedback Loops (compensatory mechanisms and decompensation), Fluid and Electrolyte Balance.

**ICP Monitoring Devices:**
| Device | Location | Function | Notes |
|--------|----------|---------|-------|
| **EVD (External Ventricular Drain)** | Lateral ventricle | Measures ICP AND drains CSF | Most versatile; zero at EAM (tragus); transducer level determines drainage threshold |
| **Intraparenchymal bolt** | Brain parenchyma | Measures ICP only; no drainage | More stable waveform; less infection risk |

**EVD Nursing:**
- Zero transducer at: **External Auditory Meatus (EAM) = tragus = foramen of Monro level**
- Drain height ordered as cmH2O above/below EAM (provider order required; do NOT adjust without order)
- **Higher drainage chamber = higher pressure required to drain = less drainage**
- Clamp EVD before repositioning patient; unclamp after repositioning and re-leveling
- Document output: amount, color (clear CSF normal; cloudy/bloody = report)
- Sterile dressing; check for CSF leak around insertion site; report signs of infection (fever + AMS)

**ICP Waveform:**
- Normal: P1 (percussion wave) > P2 (tidal wave) > P3 (dicrotic notch)
- **Elevated P2 wave = decreased compliance → ICP likely rising** (treat proactively)

**Signs and Symptoms of ↑ICP:**
*Early:* Headache, nausea/vomiting, change in LOC, visual changes, lethargy, irritability
*Late:* Pupillary changes, seizures, posturing (decorticate then decerebrate), coma
*Terminal:* **Cushing's Triad** — widened pulse pressure (↑SBP) + bradycardia + irregular breathing

> 🔬 **Why Cushing's Triad looks the way it does — this is brainstem homeostasis fighting herniation:**
> When the brain herniates downward through the foramen magnum, the brainstem (specifically the medulla) is compressed. The medulla contains the cardiovascular control center. As it's compressed, it senses critically low cerebral perfusion and fires a massive, last-ditch sympathetic surge — the **vasopressor response** — to try to push blood into the brain:
> - **↑ SBP (systolic hypertension, wide pulse pressure):** Maximal sympathetic vasoconstriction → enormous rise in BP, trying to maintain CPP against the rising ICP
> - **Bradycardia:** The baroreceptors sense the extreme hypertension and fire the vagal brake to slow the heart — this is the baroreceptor reflex operating at its limit. The brainstem creates the hypertension; the baroreceptors cause the bradycardia in response
> - **Irregular breathing (Cheyne-Stokes or agonal):** Compression of the respiratory centers in the medulla/pons → loss of normal respiratory rhythm
>
> This is NOT a treatment response — it is the body's dying attempt to save itself. Cushing's Triad = imminent brainstem death. Act immediately. See Homeostasis and Feedback Loops (baroreceptor reflex, decompensation).

**ICP Management — Tiered Approach:**

**Tier 1 (First-line, always running):**
- HOB 30 degrees, head midline (no neck flexion or lateral tilt — impedes venous drainage)
- Keep legs flat (not elevated above heart — venous return to head)
- Minimize stimulation (quiet room, cluster care, dim lights)
- **Normothermia** — fever AGGRESSIVELY treated; acetaminophen + cooling blanket; treat source
- Adequate analgesia (pain ↑ICP)
- Stool softeners (straining/Valsalva ↑ICP)
- Avoid PEEP increases if possible

> 🔬 **Why fever is so devastating to the injured brain:**
> Cerebral metabolic rate (CMRO2) increases ~7% for every 1°C rise in temperature. The injured brain is already oxygen-delivery limited — neurons in the penumbra are barely surviving on marginal perfusion. Fever dramatically increases their oxygen demand precisely when delivery is compromised. Additionally, hyperthermia directly extends excitotoxic injury: more temperature → more glutamate release → more NMDA receptor activation → more Ca²⁺ influx → more cell death. Every degree of fever is directly neurotoxic. This is why fever management in neuro ICU is not comfort care — it is neuroprotection. See Oxygen Delivery and Consumption.

**Tier 2 (When Tier 1 insufficient):**
- Osmotherapy: Mannitol 0.25–1 g/kg IV bolus or HTS (3% continuous or 23.4% bolus)
- Short-acting sedation (propofol, dexmedetomidine)
- **Mild hyperventilation if herniation**: Target PaCO2 35–40 (normal); brief controlled hyperventilation to 30–35 as bridge during herniation only
- CSF drainage via EVD (if in place)

**Tier 3 (Refractory, escalation):**
- NMB (neuromuscular blockade — cisatracurium) with continuous EEG monitoring
- Barbiturate coma (pentobarbital) — monitor for hypotension

> 🔬 **Why barbiturate coma lowers ICP:** Pentobarbital is a barbiturate — it acts at GABA-A receptors, but unlike benzodiazepines (which only work when GABA is present), barbiturates can open GABA-A Cl⁻ channels DIRECTLY, even without endogenous GABA. At coma doses, this produces a profound, global suppression of neuronal firing. With virtually no neurons firing, the brain's metabolic demand (CMRO2) plummets. Lower metabolism → less blood flow needed → cerebral blood vessels can constrict → ↓ cerebral blood volume → ↓ ICP. The EEG shows **burst suppression** — the target pattern showing the metabolic "savings mode" has been achieved. The risk is systemic hypotension (GABA-A is also in vascular smooth muscle → vasodilation), which then threatens CPP. Everything in Tier 3 is a tradeoff.

- Decompressive craniectomy (last resort — surgical ICP relief)

### Trauma Workflow — NTICU Admission
When a trauma patient arrives to NTICU from the ED or OR:

1. **Primary survey verification:** ABCs confirmed, airway secured, hemodynamics stabilizing
2. **Spine precautions active:** C-collar, logroll until imaging reviewed/cleared by neurosurgery
3. **Imaging review:** CT head (hemorrhage? midline shift? herniation?), CT spine (fracture? instability?)
4. **Neurological baseline:** GCS, pupils, NIHSS (if stroke component), motor/sensory levels (if SCI)
5. **Lines confirmed:** ETT position, A-line zeroed, central line patent, ICP monitor if placed
6. **Drips ordered and running:** Vasopressors for MAP goals, sedation if intubated, antiseizure prophylaxis
7. **Labs:** CBC, BMP, coags, ABG, type & screen
8. **Family notification:** Social work, chaplain services, early goals-of-care discussion when appropriate
9. **Documentation:** Time of arrival, transfer-in assessment, all vital signs, drip rates documented

### TBI-Specific Care Points

**Common TBI injuries in NTICU:**
- Epidural hematoma (EDH): Arterial bleed → "Lucid interval" then rapid deterioration → neurosurgical emergency
- Subdural hematoma (SDH): Venous bleed → slower course; chronic SDH in elderly/anticoagulated
- Diffuse Axonal Injury (DAI): Shearing of axons → immediate coma, no focal lesion on CT; worst prognosis
- Contusions: Coup-contrecoup (brain bounces in skull); may see delayed hemorrhagic conversion

> 🔬 **Why TBI has a "primary" and "secondary" injury — and why secondary injury is what we can actually prevent:**
> - **Primary injury** = the immediate mechanical damage at the moment of impact — axons shear, blood vessels tear, cells die. We cannot reverse this. It happened at the scene.
> - **Secondary injury** = the cascade of biochemical events triggered by the primary injury that CONTINUES for hours to days, killing additional neurons that survived the initial impact. This is what NTICU care is trying to prevent:
>   1. Excitotoxicity: Damaged neurons release massive glutamate → NMDA receptors open → Ca²⁺ floods in → mitochondria overwhelmed → cell death
>   2. Cerebral edema: Disrupted BBB + cellular swelling → ↑ ICP → ↓ CPP → ischemia → more death
>   3. Oxidative stress: Free radicals generated by Ca²⁺ influx and ischemia-reperfusion → lipid peroxidation → membrane destruction
>   4. Neuroinflammation: Microglia activate → cytokine release → BBB further disrupted → more edema
>   5. Systemic insults: Hypotension, hypoxia, hyperthermia, hyperglycemia, seizures — all amplify the cascade
>
> **Every NTICU intervention — MAP goals, temperature control, glucose management, seizure prophylaxis, ICP management — is targeted at preventing or limiting secondary injury.** The primary injury is done. The secondary injury is the battlefield. See Inflammation and Immune Response, Cell Proliferation and Death, Electricity and Excitability.

**TBI DO NOTs:**
- Do NOT insert nasogastric tube if basilar skull fracture suspected (raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, hemotympanum, CSF rhinorrhea/otorrhea) → oral route only
- Do NOT hyperventilate prophylactically (use only for acute herniation bridging)
- Do NOT allow hypotension (MAP <70) or hypoxia (SpO2 <94%) — double-hit injury

**Basilar skull fracture signs:**
- **Raccoon eyes** (bilateral periorbital ecchymosis)
- **Battle's sign** (mastoid ecchymosis)
- **Hemotympanum** (blood behind tympanic membrane)
- **Halo sign** / CSF rhinorrhea or otorrhea

### Autonomic Dysreflexia — SCI-Specific Emergency

> 🔬 **Why autonomic dysreflexia is a hypertensive emergency unique to SCI:**
> In an intact nervous system, painful stimuli below the level of a spinal cord injury would trigger a sympathetic response — but that response would immediately be modulated by inhibitory signals descending from the brainstem and hypothalamus.
>
> After SCI at T6 or above, those descending inhibitory signals are severed. A noxious stimulus below the injury (full bladder = #1 cause; constipation; pressure injury; tight clothing) → massive, uninhibited sympathetic discharge below the lesion → **extreme peripheral vasoconstriction → BP spikes dangerously (often 20–40 mmHg above baseline, sometimes SBP >200)**. The brainstem senses this via baroreceptors and fires vagal slowing of the heart (bradycardia) and vasodilation above the injury (flushing, sweating above the level) — but it cannot reach down below the injury to stop the vasoconstriction. The loop is broken.
>
> **First treatment = find and remove the trigger** (check catheter for kink/blockage, check for fecal impaction, skin check). If BP is not coming down: nifedipine or nitroglycerin to acutely vasodilate. This is one of the clearest examples of Homeostasis and Feedback Loops pathology — an intact feedback mechanism (baroreceptor reflex) that has lost its effector arm below the injury.

---
sections.domain-6-interdisciplinary-collaboration:
### NTICU Team Roles

| Team Member | Role in NTICU |
|------------|--------------|
| **Neurosurgeon / Neurocritical Care MD** | ICP management, surgical decisions, EVD management orders |
| **Neurologist** | Seizure management, stroke decisions, EEG interpretation |
| **Intensivist** | Overall critical care management, vent management, hemodynamics |
| **Bedside RN (YOU)** | Continuous monitoring, assessment, drip titration, family communication, documentation |
| **Respiratory Therapist (RT)** | Vent management, SBTs, suctioning, pulmonary care |
| **Physical Therapy (PT)** | Early mobility, positioning, SCI rehab coordination |
| **Occupational Therapy (OT)** | ADL retraining, splinting, cognitive assessment |
| **Speech Language Pathology (SLP)** | Swallowing screen → formal eval, communication boards |
| **Pharmacy** | Drip calculations, therapeutic monitoring, kinetics |
| **Social Work** | Family support, goals of care, discharge planning |
| **Chaplain** | Spiritual care, family presence in crises, end-of-life |

### SBAR for NTICU Handoff
**Situation → Background → Assessment → Recommendation**

Example for a TBI patient:
> **S:** "I'm calling about Mr. Johnson in Room 4, 52-year-old s/p motorcycle collision with TBI and SDH. He's had a GCS drop from 9T to 6T over the last 30 minutes."
>
> **B:** "He was admitted 6 hours ago, post-craniotomy, on propofol and fentanyl drips. Last GCS was 9T at 0600. ICP monitor shows ICP now 28, CPP 51. Receiving mannitol and HOB is 30 degrees. Most recent CT 4 hours ago showed stable SDH."
>
> **A:** "I'm concerned about re-bleed or herniation given the GCS drop and rising ICP. His pupils are still equal at 3 mm brisk bilaterally, but I'm not liking this trend."
>
> **R:** "I need you to come evaluate him now. I've already increased HOB, tightened the sedation, and the RT is at bedside. Should I start a mannitol bolus? Does he need a STAT CT?"

### Shift Handoff Essentials for NTICU
What every NTICU nurse wants to know at handoff:
1. Diagnosis and surgical/procedure history
2. **Neuro baseline** (GCS, pupils, deficits) — current vs. admission
3. Spine precautions? ICP monitor? EVD? (and current ICP/CPP trends)
4. Vent settings (mode, Vt, FiO2, PEEP) and most recent ABG
5. Drips running — vasopressors (current rate + MAP goal), sedation (RASS goal + current score), antihypertensives, seizure meds
6. Significant labs or imaging results
7. Pending orders, consults, or procedures
8. Family status and goals of care discussions

---
sections.domain-7-safety-and-infection-control:
### Seizure Precautions
Standard for ALL neuro ICU patients (ordered on admission):
- Padded side rails up at all times
- Suction equipment at bedside and functional (Yankauer)
- Oral airway at bedside
- Call light within reach (if alert)
- Never leave patient alone if actively seizing
- Oxygen and bag-valve mask at bedside (all ICU rooms)

**What to do during a witnessed seizure:**
1. **Stay with patient** — call for help, do NOT leave
2. Position: turn patient to side (recovery position if possible) — protect airway from aspiration
3. Protect from injury — nothing in mouth, move hard objects away, do NOT restrain convulsing limbs
4. Time the seizure (start clock immediately — >5 min = status epilepticus → emergency)
5. Maintain airway — may need oral airway, suctioning
6. Oxygen via NRB or BVM if needed
7. Notify provider — have lorazepam or midazolam drawn if anticipated
8. Post-ictal assessment: GCS, pupils, focal deficits, vital signs, SpO2
9. Document: time onset, duration, type of movement, loss of consciousness, postictal state, meds given

> 🔬 **Why the post-ictal state happens:** During a seizure, neurons fire at maximum rate for minutes. This massively depletes ATP, glucose, and oxygen in the affected cortex (VO2 skyrockets, DO2 cannot keep up), generates lactate and H⁺, and exhausts neurotransmitter stores. The post-ictal "fog" — drowsiness, confusion, transient weakness (Todd's paralysis) — is the brain in metabolic recovery: replenishing ATP, clearing lactate, re-establishing ion gradients that the Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase had to frantically restore. The neurons are exhausted and recovering — not additionally injured (usually). This is also why a prolonged post-ictal period in a known epileptic may actually represent an ongoing subclinical seizure — EEG is the only way to know. See Oxygen Delivery and Consumption, Electricity and Excitability.

**In intubated/sedated patients:** Seizures may be purely electrical (no visible motor activity). Continuous EEG (cEEG) or Ceribell (spot EEG) may be ordered — monitor EEG parameters as ordered.

### Sterile Technique in NTICU — When It Applies
- **EVD dressing changes:** Sterile technique (sterile gloves, sterile field, sterile dressings)
- **Central line dressing changes:** Sterile technique per CVC bundle
- **Arterial line dressing changes:** Clean technique; sterile if ordered by protocol
- **Suctioning (open):** Sterile catheter technique; new catheter each time
- **Urinary catheter care:** Clean technique for pericare; sterile for catheter insertion
- **VAP bundle oral care:** Clean technique (Yankauer suction, chlorhexidine swabs)

### CAUTI Prevention (Foley bundles)
- **Daily necessity reassessment** — remove catheter as soon as accurate output measurement not required or patient can void
- Secure catheter to prevent traction
- Urinary drainage bag always BELOW bladder level
- Closed drainage system — never disconnect unless necessary
- Pericare with every bowel movement
- Daily documentation of indication

### Infection Control Measures
- **Standard precautions:** All patients — gloves, eye protection for splash risk
- **Contact precautions (MRSA, VRE, C. diff, MDR organisms):** Gown + gloves; dedicated equipment per room
- **Droplet precautions (influenza, meningitis):** Surgical mask within 3 feet
- **Airborne precautions (TB, COVID, varicella):** N95, negative pressure room
- **C. difficile:** Wash hands with SOAP AND WATER (alcohol gel is NOT effective against C. diff spores)
- **Hand hygiene 5 moments:** Before patient contact, before aseptic task, after body fluid exposure, after patient contact, after contact with patient environment

---
sections.quick-reference-the-nticu-conditions-you:
| Condition | Key Drips | Key Monitoring | Nursing Priority |
|-----------|----------|----------------|-----------------|
| **TBI / SDH / EDH** | Hypertonic saline, mannitol, levetiracetam, sedation | ICP, GCS, pupils q1h | Prevent secondary injury: ICP, normothermia, normoglycemia, no hypotension/hypoxia |
| **SAH (aneurysmal)** | Nimodipine 60mg q4h, nicardipine drip | BP (SBP <140), vasospasm watch Days 4–14 | Quiet environment, seizure precautions, BP target |
| **ICH (hemorrhagic stroke)** | Nicardipine or labetalol (SBP 140–160), reversal agents | BP, hematoma expansion signs | Neurological decline = STAT CT |
| **Ischemic stroke post-tPA** | BP drip if hypertensive | NIHSS q15m × 2h, then q30m × 6h, q1h × 16h | Hemorrhagic transformation watch |
| **Traumatic SCI (cervical)** | NE/phenylephrine (MAP ≥85–90), DVT prophylaxis | Respiratory function (ascending edema), HD | Spinal precautions, autonomic dysreflexia |
| **Status epilepticus** | Benzo → fosphenytoin/levetiracetam → propofol/midazolam infusion | Continuous EEG if intubated | Stop seizure → protect airway → find cause |
| **Post-craniotomy** | Antiseizure prophylaxis (Keppra), pain management | Wound, drain output, ICP if monitored | Positioning, CSF leak surveillance |

---
sections.night-before-review-checklist:
**Read these notes in the vault:**
- [ ] Traumatic Spinal Cord Injury — autonomic dysreflexia protocol by heart
- [ ] Intracerebral Hemorrhage — ICH nursing section + MHS pathway
- [ ] Ischemic Stroke — MHS stroke protocol, BP targets by type
- [ ] Vasopressors and Inotropes — receptor profiles, titration safety
- [ ] Sedatives and Anxiolytics — RASS scale, propofol vs. dex, ABCDEF bundle
- [ ] Antiepileptics — status epilepticus protocol
- [ ] Neuromuscular Blocking Agents — TOF monitoring
- [ ] Osmotic and Hypertonic Agents — mannitol vs. HTS
- [ ] ABG and VBG Interpretation — 5-step method
- [ ] Mechanical Ventilation Management — modes, alarms, VAP bundle
- [ ] ICP Monitoring and Management — EVD nursing, tiered management

**First-principles anchors to hold in mind on the floor:**
- Every vasopressor is a receptor pharmacology story → alpha/beta → Ca²⁺ → smooth muscle contraction
- Every ICP intervention targets one component of the Monro-Kellie equation
- CO2 is the brain's vasodilator — ventilation rate is a neuro management tool
- Every secondary injury prevention measure is blocking excitotoxicity, edema, or metabolic crisis
- Cushing's Triad = the medulla fighting herniation with its last sympathetic salvo

**Know these numbers cold:**
- Normal ICP: 0–15 mmHg; treat if >20–25
- CPP = MAP − ICP; goal ≥50–70
- GCS ≤8 → intubation threshold
- Cushing's Triad: ↑SBP + bradycardia + irregular breathing = herniation
- Lung-protective ventilation: 6–8 mL/kg IBW
- SCI MAP goal: ≥85–90 × 5–7 days
- ICH BP goal: SBP 140–160 (MHS)
- SAH BP goal: SBP <140, MAP >65
- RASS target in neuro ICU: 0 to −2
- Status epilepticus: >5 continuous minutes or ≥2 seizures without recovery

**Questions to ask your preceptor:**
- "What's the weaning protocol on propofol for patients you're trying to extubate?"
- "How often do you check TOF on your NMB patients?"
- "Walk me through your shift assessment on a fresh TBI patient"
- "What's the EVD management protocol — who can adjust the drain height?"
- "What does your unit use for fever management in high-ICP patients?"

---

obstetric-hemorrhage-postpartum-hemorrha.title: Obstetric Hemorrhage (Postpartum Hemorrhage — ED/OB Nursing)

sections.definition:
- **PPH:** Blood loss ≥500 mL after vaginal delivery, or ≥1,000 mL after cesarean
- **Severe PPH:** Blood loss ≥1,000 mL with signs of hemodynamic instability (regardless of delivery route)
- **Cumulative blood loss (CBL):** Total blood loss tracked from delivery onward — document continuously

> **Clinical Pearl: Ob patients compensate remarkably well** — blood volume increases 40–50% in pregnancy. A woman can lose 1,500 mL before showing classic shock signs. By the time HR is 120 and BP is dropping, she has already lost a lot. Trust the bleeding, not the vitals.

---
sections.the-4-ts-causes-of-pph:
| Cause | Mechanism | Frequency |
|-------|-----------|-----------|
| **Tone (uterine atony)** | Uterus fails to contract after delivery → open spiral arteries bleed freely | ~80% of PPH |
| **Trauma** | Lacerations (cervical, vaginal, perineal), uterine rupture, hematoma | ~10% |
| **Tissue** | Retained placenta or placental fragments prevent uterine contraction | ~5% |
| **Thrombin** | Coagulopathy (DIC, pre-existing coag disorder, TIC) | ~5% |

---
sections.awhonn-pph-risk-assessment-3-phase:
Source: AWHONN PPH Risk Assessment Table v1.3 — used at admission, pre-birth, and postpartum

### Phase 1: Admission Risk Stratification

| Risk Level | Factors |
|-----------|---------|
| **Low risk** | No prior uterine surgery, singleton pregnancy, ≤3 prior vaginal deliveries, no known bleeding disorder, no active bleeding |
| **Medium risk** | Prior cesarean or uterine surgery, multiple gestation, >4 prior deliveries, large fibroids, large baby (macrosomia), prior PPH, obesity (BMI >40), magnesium sulfate use, prolonged oxytocin (>24h), chorioamnionitis |
| **High risk** | Placenta previa/accreta/percreta, Hgb <8 g/dL, active bleeding on admission, known coagulopathy, platelet count <100k |

### Phase 2: Pre-Birth Risk Update
Reassess if clinical status changes — new factors may push patient to higher risk category

### Phase 3: Postpartum Risk Assessment
- Reassess at delivery and q15 min for first hour
- Any of the following = escalate: CBL ≥500 mL vaginal / ≥1000 mL cesarean, HR >110, BP drop, uterine atony persisting after initial bimanual massage

---
sections.stage-based-response-ob-hemorrhage-stage:
### Stage 1 — Abnormal Bleeding (CBL 500–999 mL vaginal / 1000–1499 mL cesarean)

**Recognize:**
- Bleeding more than expected
- Uterus boggy or not contracting

**Respond — Stage 1 Checklist:**
- [ ] Announce: "This is a Stage 1 OB Hemorrhage"
- [ ] Quantify blood loss (weigh pads/chux — 1g = 1 mL) — cumulative
- [ ] Uterine massage (fundal massage) — bimanual compression if atony
- [ ] Administer uterotonic medications (see below)
- [ ] Establish large-bore IV access × 2 (if not already)
- [ ] Draw labs: CBC, BMP, coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen), type and screen
- [ ] Foley catheter (full bladder impedes uterine contraction)
- [ ] Increase IV oxytocin rate per order
- [ ] Notify provider/OB if not already present
- [ ] Document all interventions and timing

### Stage 2 — Continued Hemorrhage (CBL 1000–1499 mL vaginal / 1500–2499 mL cesarean, or signs of instability)

**Respond — escalate:**
- [ ] Announce: "This is a Stage 2 OB Hemorrhage" — activate hemorrhage response team per protocol
- [ ] Notify charge nurse, OB attending, anesthesia, blood bank
- [ ] Type and crossmatch (upgrade from screen)
- [ ] Activate MTP (Massive Transfusion Protocol) if indicated — see Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy
- [ ] Additional uterotonic agents (second and third-line)
- [ ] Consider Bakri balloon or surgical intervention (OB decision)
- [ ] Continuous vital signs q5 min
- [ ] Warm blankets/warming measures (hypothermia worsens coagulopathy)
- [ ] Prepare for potential OR transfer

### Stage 3 — Severe Hemorrhage (CBL ≥1500 mL vaginal / ≥2500 mL cesarean, or hemodynamic instability)

**Respond — all hands:**
- [ ] Announce: "This is a Stage 3 OB Hemorrhage"
- [ ] MTP activated — initiate 1:1:1 pRBC:FFP:platelets
- [ ] Anesthesia at bedside — vasopressor support if needed
- [ ] OR prep for hysterectomy if medical management failing
- [ ] Arterial line for continuous BP monitoring
- [ ] Ongoing labs q30–60 min (CBC, coags, fibrinogen — target fibrinogen >200)
- [ ] TXA (tranexamic acid) 1g IV over 10 min if within 3 hours of delivery
- [ ] Document everything — time, CBL, blood products, medications, team response

---
sections.uterotonic-medications:
| Drug | Dose/Route | Mechanism | Notes |
|------|-----------|-----------|-------|
| **Oxytocin (Pitocin)** | 10–40 units in 500–1000 mL NS IV infusion | Uterine contraction | First-line; give as infusion NOT IV push (causes hypotension) |
| **Methylergonovine (Methergine)** | 0.2 mg IM | Sustained uterine contraction | **Contraindicated in hypertension** (causes vasoconstriction) — check BP before giving |
| **Carboprost (Hemabate)** | 250 mcg IM q15–90 min (max 8 doses) | Prostaglandin → uterine contraction | **Contraindicated in asthma** (bronchospasm); causes GI side effects |
| **Misoprostol (Cytotec)** | 600–1000 mcg rectal/sublingual/buccal | Prostaglandin | Can use when other uterotonics unavailable; less potent; causes fever |
| **TXA (Tranexamic acid)** | 1g IV over 10 min; repeat 1g if bleeding continues after 30 min | Antifibrinolytic | WHO recommends within 3h of delivery; reduces PPH mortality |

---
sections.non-pharm-procedural-interventions:
| Intervention | What It Does | Nursing Role |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Bimanual uterine compression** | External fundal massage + internal compression | Assist provider; fundal massage is nurse-initiated |
| **Bakri balloon / uterine tamponade** | Balloon fills uterine cavity → tamponades bleeding | Setup: foley-like balloon, inflate with NS per order, attach drainage bag to monitor output |
| **Surgical ligation / hysterectomy** | Last resort to control bleeding | OR prep, blood bank notification, family communication |
| **Uterine artery embolization (IR)** | Radiological occlusion of uterine arteries | IR suite prep, maintain hemodynamic stability during transport |

---
sections.nursing-assessment-postpartum-hemorrhage:
### Every 15 Minutes (First Hour Postpartum)
- [ ] **Fundal assessment:** Location (should be firm, midline, at umbilicus), tone (firm vs boggy)
- [ ] **Lochia:** Saturating >1 pad/15 min = abnormal; clots >golf ball = significant
- [ ] **Vital signs:** HR, BP, RR, SpO2 — note trends not just single values
- [ ] **Cumulative blood loss:** Weigh all pads, chux, under-buttock pads (1g = 1 mL)
- [ ] **LOC and color:** Pallor, diaphoresis, confusion = decompensating
- [ ] **Urine output:** Via Foley — should be ≥0.5 mL/kg/hr; drop = poor perfusion

### Fundal Massage Technique
- Support the lower uterine segment with one hand above the symphysis pubis
- Cup the fundus with the other hand and apply firm, circular massage
- A properly massaged uterus should become firm ("grapefruit hard") within seconds
- If boggy after massage + uterotonics → escalate

---
sections.special-situations-in-the-ed:
### OB Patient Presenting to ED Postpartum
- Any woman <6 weeks postpartum with heavy vaginal bleeding should be triaged as potential PPH
- Get OB history: delivery date, type of delivery, was placenta delivered completely?
- Bedside US: retained products of conception (RPOC) appear as hyperechoic tissue in uterine cavity
- Activate OB/GYN consult early — do not manage PPH as a lone ED team

### Ectopic Pregnancy / Early Pregnancy Hemorrhage
- NOT the same as PPH — see Preeclampsia and Eclampsia and FAST Exam for ruptured ectopic
- Rapid hCG + bedside pelvic US + volume resuscitation + OB/GYN emergency

---
sections.dic-in-obstetric-hemorrhage:
Massive PPH → consumes clotting factors → DIC → bleeding worsens despite transfusion

**Lab targets in OB hemorrhage with DIC:**
- Fibrinogen >200 mg/dL (obstetric DIC often depletes fibrinogen first — treat aggressively with cryoprecipitate)
- Platelets >50k (>75–100k if ongoing bleeding)
- INR <1.5
- pH >7.2 and temperature >35°C (hypothermia and acidosis destroy clotting — keep warm, resuscitate)

**Cryoprecipitate:** 10 units = raises fibrinogen ~50 mg/dL — order proactively in OB hemorrhage

---
sections.documentation-requirements:
- [ ] Time of delivery and placental delivery
- [ ] Cumulative blood loss at each stage (weighed and estimated)
- [ ] Time of each intervention (massage, medications, blood products — with dose and route)
- [ ] Vital signs trend
- [ ] Lab results with time drawn
- [ ] Team notifications (charge, OB, anesthesia, blood bank) with times
- [ ] Stage announcements with times
- [ ] Patient response to each intervention
- [ ] Infant status and location (NICU, skin-to-skin, with family — document separately)

---
sections.references:
- AWHONN PPH Risk Assessment Table v1.3 — source: Pam material (obstetric_hemorrhage_risk.pdf)
- AWHONN PPH Stage 1 Checklist — source: Pam material (obstetric_hemorrhage_checklist.pdf)
- Preeclampsia and Eclampsia — hypertensive emergency in pregnancy
- Trauma-Induced Coagulopathy — MTP protocol, 1:1:1 resuscitation
- Blood Products and Transfusion — product types, nursing administration
- Coagulation Studies — fibrinogen, PT/INR, DIC labs
- Neonatal Resuscitation — concurrent infant management

clinicalPearls: **Boggy uterus = call it immediately and start massaging** — uterine atony is 80% of PPH and responds rapidly to massage + oxytocin if caught early. The longer you wait, the more blood is lost.
**Weigh everything** — visual estimation of blood loss is notoriously inaccurate (underestimates by 30–50%). Weigh pads, towels, chux. 1 gram = 1 mL.

**Oxytocin IV push causes hypotension** — always give as an infusion. The bolus dose reflex that happens in the OR is a controlled, anesthesia-managed situation. On the floor or in the ED, always dilute and infuse.
**Check BP before Methergine** — it causes vasoconstriction and can precipitate hypertensive crisis in a preeclamptic patient. This is a classic error.
**Fibrinogen drops early in OB DIC** — before PT/INR goes crazy, fibrinogen plummets. Order fibrinogen levels early and repeat with each blood draw. Cryoprecipitate is the fibrinogen treatment.

**The patient who's "fine" can not be fine very fast** — pregnant and postpartum patients vasodilate massively and compensate for a long time. A sudden BP drop means they've already lost significant volume. Trust the CBL trend.

oced-clinical-alerts-and-quick-reference.title: OCED Clinical Alerts and Quick References

sections.button-battery-ingestion-honey-protocol:
> *Source: MultiCare Policy (Reviewed 1/26/2026)* `#pam-training`

**Honey can be used as a treatment option during the interval between ED presentation and endoscopic removal of a retained button battery.**

| Parameter | Detail |
|-----------|--------|
| **Who** | Patients **>1 year of age** |
| **Dose** | 10 mL every 10 minutes for 1 hour |
| **How** | Pour in dosing cup, draw up with oral syringe |
| **Storage** | May store with nutrition supplies/patient food items as non-drug item |
| **Expiration** | Manufacturer expiration unless contaminated; write expiration on container if decanted from packets |
| **Do NOT** | Mix with medications |
| **Contraindications** | Patients **<1 year of age** (botulism risk). Patients allergic to honey products |
| **Availability** | Bulk bottles via nutrition services; packets via food distributor (US Foods); packets measured in grams = equivalent to mL |

> **Rationale:** Honey creates a viscous alkaline barrier that may reduce esophageal injury from the battery's electrochemical burn. This is a bridging measure — **endoscopic removal is still required.**

---
sections.pertussis-ppe-and-isolation:
> *Source: MHS Infection Prevention (v2.0, 10/2024)* `#pam-training`

**Isolation:** Droplet Precautions (NOT airborne — different from measles)

**Room:** Standard single patient room. For cohorting, contact Infection Prevention.

**PPE for staff:**
- Mask (surgical — N95 NOT required for pertussis)
- Eye protection
- Gown and gloves

**Room cleaning:** Standard cleaning procedures. Staff don gloves and gown for cleaning. Pay attention to hand-touch surfaces. Remove/clean privacy curtain if stay >24 hours or visibly soiled.

**Key distinction from measles:**
| Feature | Pertussis | Measles |
|---------|-----------|---------|
| Transmission | Droplet | Airborne |
| Mask type | Surgical mask | N95 required |
| Room type | Standard single | Negative pressure preferred |
| Room closure after patient | No special waiting period | 2 hours with door closed |

---
sections.co-monitoring-on-bipap-mhs-limitation:
> *Source: Clinical Educator (Pam)* `#pam-training`

**Q: Can you attach CO₂ monitoring (capnography) to BiPAP?**

**A: No — not with our current MHS monitoring setup.**

- Mainstream capnography sensors sit in the breathing circuit, increasing dead space and weight at the mask interface — not suitable for tight-fitting NIV masks
- CO₂ may be diluted or waveform altered with BiPAP seal
- Some systems use sidestream/microstream capnography that can work with NIV — **MHS does not currently have these**
- If you need to assess ventilation in a BiPAP patient → order an **ABG or VBG**

---
sections.code-activation-perfectserve-communicati:
> *Source: Clinical Educator (Pam) — Pharmacy feedback* `#pam-training`

When activating a code via PerfectServe, **include in your message:**
1. **That this is a CODE** (stroke, STEMI, trauma, etc.) — pharmacists covering 3 OCEDs + main campus need to prioritize
2. **Last known well time** (especially for stroke)
3. **Any relevant EMS information** (e.g., "initial field glucose was 24," "EMS gave 2 rounds epi," "GCS 6 in the field")

> Pharmacists have reported that without "code" in the message, they cannot prioritize appropriately when covering multiple facilities simultaneously. Clear, concise communication saves time.

---
sections.ranger-bloodfluid-warming-system-model-2:
> *Source: 3M Ranger Operator's Manual via Clinical Educator (Pam)* `#pam-training`

**The Ranger is a blood/fluid warming system used in trauma, MTP, and any rapid fluid resuscitation requiring warm fluids.**

Key points for nursing:
- Follow manufacturer instructions for setup (operator's manual available at rangerfluidwarming.com)
- Use **only Ranger-compatible disposable sets**
- Optimum performance at flow rates appropriate to clinical scenario
- Used in conjunction with MTP and trauma resuscitation — see Blood Administration, Trauma Nursing Assessment (TNCC)
- Prevents hypothermia during rapid volume resuscitation (critical for the lethal triad)

---
sections.decontamination-shower-setup-hm1001c:
> *Source: DQE Standard Decontamination Shower assembly instructions* `#pam-training`

**The standard decon shower is a portable, rapid-assembly shower for hazmat/chemical decontamination.**

Quick assembly steps:
1. Connect bottom U-bases and extenders
2. Insert shower uprights into corners (valve upright nearest water supply)
3. Aim shower heads vertically inside opposite upright
4. Connect top U-bases and extenders → attach to uprights
5. Connect hand-held spray hose to valved upright
6. Attach water supply hose (requires ≥15 GPM at 30-50 PSI)
7. Place shower enclosure on top with window on spray upright side
8. If water pressure >90 PSI → use included pressure reducer

**Optimal flow: ≥15 GPM (30-50 PSI)**

> Know where your facility stores this equipment and practice assembly before you need it. Decon situations are time-critical and high-stress.

---
sections.oxygen-as-a-drug-key-reminders:
> *Source: Clinical Educator (Pam) — Medical Air vs Oxygen Nebulizing guide* `#pam-training`

**Oxygen is classified as a prescription medication** because it:
- Has specific indications and a dose (FiO₂/flow rate)
- Has therapeutic effects AND can cause harm if over/under-used

**Oxygen toxicity:**
- **Pulmonary:** FiO₂ ≥60% for ≥24 hours → damage to alveolar-capillary membrane, inflammation, reduced surfactant → worsening oxygenation despite high FiO₂
- **CNS:** Rare in routine ED care; more relevant in hyperbaric O₂

**Hyperoxia is NOT benign:**
- Associated with increased mortality post-cardiac arrest
- Worse outcomes in stroke and MI
- Increased lung injury in critical illness

**Best practice:**
- ✅ Use lowest FiO₂/flow to meet ordered SpO₂ goals
- ✅ Reassess frequently (post-procedure, transport, sedation)
- ✅ Wean promptly when clinically appropriate
- ✅ Document indication, flow/FiO₂, and patient response
- ❌ "Leaving oxygen on just in case" can cause harm

---
sections.holiday-heart-syndrome-pam-training:
**Acute atrial arrhythmia triggered by binge drinking, overeating, stress, and cold exposure.**

- Look for palpitations post-feast, especially around holidays
- **Management:** Rate control (beta-blockers), aggressive hydration, correct electrolytes (K⁺, Mg²⁺)
- Order: ECG telemetry, electrolytes, renal function, troponin (rule out demand ischemia)
- Check labs for dehydration from alcohol
- **Patient education:** Limit alcohol (≤1 drink/day ♀, ≤2 ♂), balanced meals, stress management, medication adherence (people skip meds during holidays)
- If arrhythmia persists > 24 hours → cardiology follow-up
- See Atrial Fibrillation, ECG Interpretation

---
sections.norovirus-outbreak-control-pam-training:
**Key points for ED nursing:**
- **Hand hygiene:** Soap and water ONLY — alcohol-based sanitizer is NOT effective against norovirus
- **Isolation:** Contact precautions (gowns + gloves). Add droplet/extended contact if projectile vomiting
- **Cleaning:** Bleach wipes (blue-top) — clean within 10-foot radius after vomiting episodes, 2–3× daily for high-touch surfaces
- **Precautions continue:** ≥ 48–72 hours after symptoms resolve (longer for infants/immunocompromised)
- **Staff:** Exclude symptomatic staff until 48 hours post-last symptom. Notify Employee Health and manager
- **Patient care:** ORS over IV if tolerated. Monitor for severe dehydration (especially infants, elderly, immunocompromised)

---
sections.ottawa-ankle-and-knee-rules-pam-training:
### Ottawa Ankle Rules — X-ray Indicated If:
**Malleolar zone pain** PLUS any of:
- Tenderness at posterior edge/tip of lateral malleolus (distal 6 cm fibula)
- Tenderness at posterior edge/tip of medial malleolus (distal 6 cm tibia)
- Inability to bear weight (4 steps, limping counts) immediately after injury AND in exam

### Ottawa Foot Rules — X-ray Indicated If:
**Midfoot zone pain** PLUS any of:
- Tenderness at base of 5th metatarsal
- Tenderness at navicular bone
- Inability to bear weight (4 steps)

### Ottawa Knee Rules — X-ray Indicated If Any ONE:
- Age ≥ 55 years
- Isolated patellar tenderness (no other bone tenderness)
- Fibular head tenderness
- Cannot flex knee to 90°
- Cannot bear weight (4 steps) immediately and in ED

**Notes:** Validated for adults ≥ 18 yrs. Not reliable if intoxicated, confused, or multiple injuries. Valid for injuries ≤ 10 days old. Sensitivity ~98–100% for clinically significant fractures. Reduces unnecessary imaging by 25–35%. Nurses can apply at triage where protocols allow.

---
sections.oxygen-delivery-devices-quick-reference-:
| Device | Flow Rate | FiO₂ | Indication |
|--------|----------|------|-----------|
| **Nasal cannula** | 1–6 L/min | 24–44% | Mild hypoxemia, stable patients |
| **Simple face mask** | 6–10 L/min (min 6) | 40–60% | Mild-moderate hypoxemia, short-term |
| **Non-rebreather (NRBM)** | 10–15 L/min | 60–95% | Severe hypoxemia, trauma, shock |
| **High-flow nasal cannula (HFNC)** | 20–60 L/min | 21–100% | Severe respiratory distress, acute hypoxemic failure |

**HFNC:** Delivers heated, humidified oxygen. Ensure humidifier functioning. Check for nasal skin irritation. See also: CPAP vs BiPAP section below.

---
sections.cpap-vs-bipap-quick-differentiation-pam-:
| Feature | CPAP | BiPAP |
|---------|------|-------|
| **Pressures** | ONE constant pressure | TWO pressures (IPAP higher, EPAP lower) |
| **Best for** | OSA (keeps airway open) | COPD, CHF, neuromuscular weakness, complex cases |
| **Key advantage** | Simple, first-line for sleep apnea | Easier exhalation, better ventilatory support |
| **When to use BiPAP over CPAP** | CPAP fails or patient can't tolerate constant pressure | — |

**Memory:** **C**PAP = **C**onstant. **Bi**PAP = **Bi** (two pressures).

---
sections.nauseavomiting-in-inferior-mi-the-bezold:
**Why do inferior wall MIs cause more N/V than anterior MIs?**

- Inferior MI (usually RCA occlusion) triggers the **Bezold-Jarisch reflex** — a cardioinhibitory response from ischemia in the inferoposterior wall
- Stimulates vagal afferent C fibers → parasympathetic surge → classic triad of **bradycardia + hypotension + vasodilation**
- Vagal innervation of the stomach and upper GI tract → nausea and vomiting
- Reported in up to **69% of inferior MIs** vs 27% of anterior MIs
- **Clinical significance:** N/V + bradycardia in a chest pain patient → think inferior MI. Get that ECG.
- See ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction, ECG Interpretation

---
sections.hemorrhage-vs-hematoma-intracranial-blee:
- **Hemorrhage** = active bleeding from a ruptured vessel (blood still flowing)
- **Hematoma** = collection of pooled blood outside the vessel (often clotted)

| Type | Location | CT Appearance | Key Feature |
|------|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Epidural** | Between skull and dura | Biconvex (lens-shaped) | **Lucid interval** → rapid deterioration. Usually middle meningeal artery |
| **Subdural** | Between dura and arachnoid | Crescent-shaped | Bridging veins. Common in elderly/anticoagulated. Acute/subacute/chronic |
| **Subarachnoid** | Subarachnoid space (CSF) | Blood in cisterns/sulci | **"Worst headache of life"**, neck stiffness. Often ruptured aneurysm |
| **Intracerebral** | Within brain parenchyma | Hyperdense mass | Hypertension #1 cause. Direct tissue damage + mass effect |
| **Intraventricular** | Inside ventricles | Blood in ventricles | Often secondary. Hydrocephalus risk |

---
sections.medication-antidotes-quick-reference-pam:
| Agent/Drug | Antidote |
|-----------|---------|
| Acetaminophen | Acetylcysteine (NAC) |
| Anticholinesterase | Atropine or pralidoxime |
| Anticholinergics | Physostigmine |
| Benzodiazepines | Flumazenil |
| Beta-blockers | Glucagon |
| Calcium channel blockers | Glucagon |
| Coumadin/Warfarin | Vitamin K |
| Cyanide | Amyl nitrite, sodium nitrite |
| Digoxin | Digibind (digoxin immune Fab) |
| Dopamine (extravasation) | Regitine (phentolamine) |
| Ethylene glycol | Fomepizole |
| Heparin | Protamine sulfate |
| Iron | Deferoxamine |
| Methotrexate | Leucovorin calcium |
| Opioids (narcotics) | Naloxone (Narcan) |
| Potassium (hyperkalemia) | Calcium, insulin/D50, albuterol, kayexalate |
| Tricyclic antidepressants | Physostigmine, sodium bicarbonate |

See also: Reversal Agents, Antidotes and Toxicology Agents

---
sections.hepu-anti-xa-for-ufh-lab-clarification-p:
**HepU = anti-Xa assay calibrated specifically for unfractionated heparin (UFH)**

- Standard anti-Xa can be calibrated for either LMWH or UFH — the term alone doesn't specify which
- **MHS uses HepU** (not generic "anti-Xa") for monitoring IV UFH drips
- **Therapeutic range for UFH:** 0.3–0.7 IU/mL
- Using LMWH calibration for UFH monitoring gives inaccurate results — always order the correct assay
- See Anticoagulants, Coagulation and Hemostasis

---
sections.hospital-hold-and-single-bed-certificati:
### When to Place a Hospital Hold
Patient is: a danger to self or others, gravely disabled, or a danger to property. Cognitive impairment + elopement risk may be held until mental health assessment is complete.

**Exclusions:** Lack of decision-making capacity alone is NOT enough. If DCR evaluates and determines no detainment needed → hold must be discontinued.

### Process
1. Physician/APP orders Hospital Hold in Epic
2. LMHP (Social Worker) performs behavioral health risk assessment
3. Offer voluntary admission before DCR referral
4. If patient refuses → refer to DCR for evaluation
5. Search belongings for safety; dress patient in **spice-colored scrubs**
6. Notify: Security, Charge Nurse

### Nursing Responsibilities
- Confirm Hospital Hold order + related safety orders (Suicide Precaution, CIWA, Restraints)
- Provide spice-colored scrubs
- Use Constant Observer policy if indicated
- **Patients on hold cannot leave AMA** — follow elopement policy if attempted
- Document all interventions and patient status in EMR

### SBC (Single Bed Certification)
- Requested by DCR when no psychiatric bed is available
- Hospital (not OCED) must accept SBC and meet treatment requirements
- Daily behavioral health professional contact required
- SBC ends when: patient transfers to MH facility, detention no longer needed, or expires after 30 days
- **OCEDs are NOT SBC-certified units** — SBC applies to the hospital. Patient may remain in OCED temporarily while awaiting transfer
- If pushback on transfer → escalate to Hospital Supervisor → Risk Management → Administrator-on-Call

---
sections.antihypertensive-mar-bp-documentation-pr:
> *Source: MHS Practice Alert — MAR Blood Pressure Documentation for Antihypertensive Medication Infusions (Rev. 12/8/2025)*

**New BP field added to the MAR** for titratable antihypertensive medications:

**In-scope medications:** Nicardipine, esmolol, labetalol, nitroglycerin, nitroprusside

**When to document:** At every **Rate Change** and every **New Bag/Bottle/Cartridge**

**How:**
- Use the new MAR BP field to document or confirm BP
- Can validate an existing BP from flowsheets OR manually enter most recent BP from monitor
- May continue to titrate from the pump in emergent situations
- After emergency titration: return to MAR → select Rate Change timestamp → edit administration → add/verify BP
- Validate BP **pre and post** titration per order parameters (e.g., nicardipine Q5min)

**Note:** BPs that are validated/filed in flowsheets auto-populate in MAR. Infusion Verify does NOT display order parameters or store BP values.

---
sections.rhogam-and-blood-type-ordering-clarifica:
> *Source: Robin Underwood, RN (Transfusion Safety Officer) via Pam, 6/19/2025*

### Common Ordering Errors
**Rhogam (ABO, Rh, Fetal Screen)** ≠ **Type and Screen (ABO, Rh, Antibody Screening)** ≠ **ABO/Rh**

| Order | What It Is | Tube/Specimen |
|-------|-----------|---------------|
| **Rhogam** | ABO, Rh, Fetal Screen — determines Rh status and eligibility for Rhogam | Lavender top |
| **Type and Screen (TNS)** | ABO, Rh, Antibody screening — for blood product setup/crossmatch | Pink top, **banded specimen** (1 pink 6 mL or 2 microtainers) |
| **Blood Type (ABO/Rh)** | Simple blood type only — for plasma, platelets, or cryo setup | Lavender top, regular label |
| **Confirmatory Blood Type (CBT)** | FDA requirement: 2 separately drawn samples before type-specific RBCs | Lavender top, drawn at different time from TNS |

**Key FDA requirement:** Two separately drawn samples must be tested for ABO before issuing type-specific/compatible RBCs.

**TNS is valid for 3 days** from draw until 23:59. CBT valid for 7 days.

### Rhogam Eligibility Workflow
- Rh-negative patient with negative Rh history at MHS → result as "Eligible" with dosage
- Rh-negative patient with no MHS history or positive Rh history → testing completed to determine eligibility
- Patient > 19 weeks → can give 1500 units immediately → fetal bleed screening for additional dosing
- OPA fires for Physician/APP to order medication after result

See Blood Products and Transfusion

---

*See also: Measles · Flash Pulmonary Edema · Blood Administration · Trauma Nursing Assessment (TNCC) · Asthma · Code Blue Management · Ischemic Stroke · ECG Interpretation · Alcohol Use Disorder*

#procedure #pam-training #quick-reference #oced

oxygen-delivery-devices.title: Oxygen Delivery Devices

sections.the-oxygen-hierarchy:
From least to most invasive/supportive:

```
Room air (21% FiO2)
    ↓
Nasal cannula (24–44%)
    ↓
Simple face mask (35–50%)
    ↓
Venturi mask (24–60% — precise)
    ↓
Non-rebreather mask (60–80%+)
    ↓
High-flow nasal cannula / HFNC (up to 100%, heated/humidified)
    ↓
CPAP / BiPAP (non-invasive positive pressure)
    ↓
Mechanical ventilation (intubation)
```

---
sections.device-by-device-reference:
### 1. Nasal Cannula (NC)

**FiO2 delivered:** ~24–44%
**Flow rate:** 1–6 L/min
**Rule of thumb:** Each 1 L/min adds approximately 4% FiO2 above room air (21%)

| Flow (L/min) | Approximate FiO2 |
|-------------|-----------------|
| 1 | ~24% |
| 2 | ~28% |
| 3 | ~32% |
| 4 | ~36% |
| 5 | ~40% |
| 6 | ~44% |

**Best for:**
- Mild hypoxia (SpO2 88–93% on room air)
- Stable patients who need low supplemental O2
- Patients who need to eat, drink, or talk comfortably
- Home O2 for COPD, CHF

**Limitations:**
- FiO2 varies with breathing pattern (mouth breathing, tachypnea = dilution of O2)
- Maximum useful flow: 6 L/min (higher flows are drying and irritating without humidification; don't meaningfully increase FiO2 above 6L without humidification)
- Not adequate for moderate-severe hypoxia

**Nursing notes:**
- Position prongs correctly: curve downward into nares
- Add humidification (bubble humidifier) at flows >4 L/min for comfort
- Check for skin breakdown at nares and ears (especially with prolonged use)

> **Clinical Pearl: In COPD, start low and titrate — target SpO2 88–92%**, not 98%. Hypercapnic COPD patients rely partly on hypoxic drive; over-oxygenation suppresses respiratory drive and can precipitate CO2 retention. See COPD.

---

### 2. Simple Face Mask

**FiO2 delivered:** ~35–50%
**Flow rate:** 5–10 L/min (minimum 5 L/min to flush CO2 from mask)

| Flow (L/min) | Approximate FiO2 |
|-------------|-----------------|
| 5–6 | ~35–40% |
| 6–7 | ~40–45% |
| 7–8 | ~45–50% |

**Best for:**
- Moderate hypoxia requiring more O2 than NC can deliver
- Short-term use (post-procedure, transport)
- Patient needs more than 6 L/min NC but doesn't need NRB

**Limitations:**
- Must run at ≥5 L/min — below this, CO2 accumulates in the mask (rebreathing)
- Imprecise FiO2 (varies with breathing pattern)
- Patient cannot eat or drink
- Claustrophobic patients tolerate poorly

**Nursing notes:**
- Ensure mask fits snugly over nose and mouth; mold metal nosepiece
- Flows below 5 L/min are contraindicated — patient rebreathes CO2

---

### 3. Non-Rebreather Mask (NRB)

**FiO2 delivered:** ~60–80% (up to ~90% with a good seal)
**Flow rate:** 10–15 L/min

**How it works:**
- Reservoir bag holds 1 L of O2 that fills between breaths
- One-way valve between bag and mask: patient inhales from the reservoir (100% O2), exhales out through side ports — does NOT rebreathe exhaled CO2
- Side port valves prevent room air entrainment on inhalation

**Best for:**
- Moderate-severe hypoxia requiring high FiO2
- Carbon monoxide poisoning (high FiO2 displaces CO from hemoglobin — use 100% O2 via NRB or intubation)
- Initial stabilization before escalating to HFNC/BiPAP/intubation

**Limitations:**
- FiO2 limited by fit and seal — full face contact needed
- Does not provide PEEP (no alveolar recruitment)
- Cannot deliver truly 100% FiO2 without a tight seal
- If patient is tachypneic and breathing volume > reservoir refill rate, FiO2 drops

**Nursing notes:**
- **Inflate the reservoir bag before applying to patient** — pinch the tubing to fill the bag
- Ensure bag inflates/deflates with each breath — if bag fully collapses, increase flow
- Ensure the one-way valve between reservoir and mask is in place
- Check for and correct mask leak (around nose)

> **Clinical Pearl: NRB + CO poisoning** — give the highest FiO2 you can as fast as you can. High FiO2 shortens carboxyhemoglobin half-life from 5 hours (room air) to ~60–90 minutes (100% O2). See Antidotes and Toxicology Agents.

---

### 4. Venturi Mask

**FiO2 delivered:** Precise — 24%, 28%, 31%, 35%, 40%, or 60% (set by color-coded adaptor)
**Flow rate:** Adaptor-specific (marked on each valve)

| Adaptor Color | FiO2 | Required Flow |
|--------------|------|--------------|
| Blue | 24% | 2 L/min |
| White | 28% | 4 L/min |
| Yellow | 35% | 8 L/min |
| Red | 40% | 10 L/min |
| Green | 60% | 15 L/min |

**How it works:**
- Venturi effect: O2 flows through a narrow jet, entraining a fixed amount of room air — the ratio is precise and consistent regardless of breathing pattern
- Most reliable FiO2 delivery of all low-flow devices

**Best for:**
- COPD exacerbation — precise O2 titration, target 88–92% without over-oxygenating
- Any patient where controlled FiO2 matters (hypercapnic respiratory failure)

**Limitations:**
- More complex to set up (must match adaptor to flow rate — check the colour chart)
- Cannot deliver high FiO2 (max 60%)
- Less comfortable than NC for long-term use

**Nursing notes:**
- Always confirm the adaptor matches the ordered FiO2 and the correct flow rate is set
- If flow rate doesn't match the adaptor, FiO2 will be wrong
- Change adaptor when FiO2 order changes — do NOT just change the flow

---

### 5. High-Flow Nasal Cannula (HFNC / Vapotherm / Optiflow)

**FiO2 delivered:** 21–100% (titrated independently of flow)
**Flow rate:** 1–60 L/min (high flow is the defining feature)

**How it works:**
- Heated, humidified O2 delivered via wide-bore nasal cannula at very high flows
- High flow flushes nasopharyngeal dead space with O2, providing a reservoir effect
- Generates low-level PEEP (~1–2 cmH2O per 10 L/min flow) — improves alveolar recruitment
- Humidification dramatically improves comfort at high flows

**Best for:**
- Moderate-severe hypoxemia as a step-up from NRB before intubation
- Post-extubation support
- Cardiogenic pulmonary edema (generates mild CPAP effect)
- Immunocompromised patients (avoids intubation and associated infection risk)
- COVID-19 hypoxemia management

**Limitations:**
- Does not replace BiPAP for hypercapnic failure (COPD, obesity hypoventilation) — minimal CO2 clearance
- Expensive; requires specific equipment
- Mask-free — better tolerance than BiPAP for claustrophobic patients

**Nursing notes:**
- Set FiO2 and flow rate independently — start 40 L/min flow, FiO2 per SpO2 target
- Warn patient it will feel like strong wind in their nose — reassure it's normal
- Check nares for pressure injuries (wide-bore cannula can cause breakdown)
- If patient deteriorates on HFNC (ROX index declining, increasing WOB) → escalate to BiPAP or intubation

**ROX Index (HFNC failure predictor):**
- ROX = (SpO2/FiO2) / Respiratory Rate
- ROX <3.85 at 2h, 4h, or 6h = high risk of HFNC failure → prepare for intubation

---

### 6. CPAP / BiPAP (Non-Invasive Positive Pressure Ventilation — NIPPV)

*(See also Mechanical Ventilation Management for full detail)*

| Mode | What It Provides | Best For |
|------|-----------------|---------|
| **CPAP** | Continuous positive pressure (single pressure) | Obstructive sleep apnea, cardiogenic pulmonary edema (recruits flooded alveoli) |
| **BiPAP** | Two pressures: IPAP (inspiratory) + EPAP (expiratory) | COPD exacerbation (drives CO2 out), hypercapnic respiratory failure |

**Key settings:**
- CPAP: 5–15 cmH2O
- BiPAP: IPAP typically 10–20 cmH2O, EPAP 4–8 cmH2O (PS = IPAP − EPAP, usually 5–10)
- FiO2: titrated to target SpO2

**Contraindications to NIPPV:**
- Inability to protect airway (altered LOC, GCS <8)
- Active vomiting (aspiration risk with mask)
- Facial trauma/deformity (can't seal mask)
- Respiratory arrest
- Hemodynamic instability refractory to treatment

**Nursing notes:**
- Mask fit is critical — a leak means no PEEP
- Skin breakdown under mask bridge (forehead, nasal bridge) — use protective foam
- Reassure patient frequently — claustrophobia is real; brief coaching improves tolerance dramatically
- BiPAP for COPD should show: improved RR, improved tidal volume on capnography, decreased WOB within 30–60 minutes

---
sections.escalation-decision-framework:
```
Patient hypoxic → Apply NC at 2–4 L/min
    SpO2 still <92? → Increase to 6 L/min or switch to simple mask
    Still inadequate? →
        COPD / hypercapnic: Venturi mask 28% → BiPAP
        Non-COPD moderate: NRB at 15 L/min
        Non-COPD severe: HFNC 40–60 L/min
        Deteriorating on HFNC or BiPAP failing? → INTUBATION
```

---
sections.monitoring-on-supplemental-o2:
| Parameter | Target | Action if Abnormal |
|-----------|--------|-------------------|
| **SpO2** | ≥94% (92–98% most patients; 88–92% COPD) | Titrate O2 device upward or downward |
| **RR** | 12–20 | Tachypnea = increased WOB → reassess device adequacy |
| **Work of breathing** | No accessory muscle use | Accessory muscles = escalate |
| **LOC** | Alert and oriented | Confusion/somnolence = CO2 retention or hypoxia → ABG, escalate |
| **ETCO2** (if on capnography) | 35–45 mmHg | Rising CO2 on high FiO2 = hypercapnic failure → BiPAP or intubate |

---
sections.special-situations:
### Nebulizer Delivery of Medications
- Albuterol, ipratropium, etc. can be nebulized via:
  - Mouthpiece (most efficient)
  - Face mask
  - T-piece attached to NRB or simple mask
  - Inline on ventilator circuit
- Drive gas: air or O2 at 6–8 L/min
- **For COPD patients: use air-driven nebulizer** if concerned about hypercapnic drive — avoid high FiO2 driving gas
- See Bronchodilators for drug details

### Apneic Oxygenation During RSI
- HFNC at 40–60 L/min running throughout preoxygenation and apneic period
- Maintains SpO2 by bulk flow O2 delivery even without respiratory effort
- Can extend safe apnea time significantly
- See Rapid Sequence Intubation and Intubation Setup and Assist

### Humidification
- Required for: HFNC (built in), any NC >4 L/min (bubble humidifier), prolonged O2 use
- Without humidification: drying of mucosa, impaired mucociliary clearance, patient discomfort, epistaxis

---
sections.references:
- Source: Pam material — oxygen_devices.pdf (OCED clinical education)
- Oxygenation and Ventilation — V/Q matching, hypoxic mechanisms
- Mechanical Ventilation Management — BiPAP, CPAP, invasive ventilation
- Rapid Sequence Intubation — apneic oxygenation
- COPD — oxygen targets, hypercapnic drive
- Bronchodilators — nebulizer delivery

clinicalPearls: **Match the device to the clinical need** — a COPD patient in mild exacerbation on NRB may look better on the monitor but be heading toward CO2 narcosis. Venturi mask or BiPAP is correct; NRB is wrong.
**An NRB reservoir bag that fully collapses = inadequate flow** — increase to 15 L/min. The bag should deflate only partially on each breath.
**Venturi adaptor color + flow rate must match** — changing the flow without changing the adaptor changes the FiO2 without you knowing. Always check both.

**HFNC is not BiPAP** — HFNC provides minimal PEEP and excellent oxygenation but does not drive CO2 out. In hypercapnic patients (COPD, OHS), BiPAP is the correct escalation from NC/Venturi, not HFNC.

**SpO2 of 100% is not always the goal** — targeting 94–96% in most patients is appropriate. Hyperoxia causes vasoconstriction (bad for post-cardiac arrest patients), absorption atelectasis, and oxygen toxicity in ventilated patients.

procedural-sedation.title: Procedural Sedation

sections.definition:
Procedural sedation and analgesia (PSA) is the administration of sedative, analgesic, and/or dissociative agents to produce a controlled depression of consciousness — allowing patients to tolerate painful or anxiety-provoking procedures while maintaining cardiopulmonary function. It is a core ED competency and one of the most common high-risk interventions nurses participate in outside the OR.

Sedation exists on a **continuum**: patients may progress deeper than intended based on individual response, drug interactions, comorbidities, and hemodynamic status. The critical nursing responsibility is **continuous assessment and readiness to rescue** from a deeper level than planned.

**Related notes:** Procedural Sedation Agents · Rapid Sequence Intubation · Reversal Agents · Opioid Analgesics · Code Blue Management

---
sections.sedation-continuum:
Understanding the continuum is essential — you must be able to rescue from one level deeper than intended.

| Level | Responsiveness | Airway | Ventilation | Cardiovascular |
|-------|---------------|--------|-------------|----------------|
| **Minimal (Anxiolysis)** | Normal response to verbal commands | Unaffected | Unaffected | Unaffected |
| **Moderate ("Conscious")** | Purposeful response to verbal or light tactile stimulation | No intervention needed | Adequate spontaneous | Usually maintained |
| **Deep** | Purposeful response only to repeated or painful stimulation | May need assistance | May be impaired | Usually maintained |
| **General Anesthesia** | Not arousable, even to pain | Often requires intervention | Often impaired; may need PPV | May be impaired |

> **Key principle:** If you plan moderate sedation, you must be competent to rescue from deep sedation. If you plan deep sedation, you must be able to manage a patient who reaches general anesthesia — this means airway management skills and resuscitation readiness.

---
sections.indications:
- **Orthopedic:** Fracture/dislocation reduction (shoulder, hip, ankle), abscess I&D
- **Cardiovascular:** Synchronized cardioversion (unstable but not pulseless)
- **Wound management:** Complex laceration repair, burn debridement
- **GI:** Foreign body removal, abscess drainage
- **Diagnostic:** CT/MRI in combative or pediatric patients (last resort)
- **Other:** Chest tube insertion, joint aspiration/injection in high-anxiety patients

---
sections.contraindications-and-risk-factors:
### Absolute Contraindications
- Patient who cannot be safely monitored (inadequate equipment/personnel)
- Hemodynamic instability requiring resuscitation (address the emergency first)
- Known or suspected difficult airway WITHOUT backup plan
- ASA Class V (moribund — not expected to survive 24h without surgery)

### Relative Contraindications / Higher Risk
- ASA Class III–IV (significant systemic disease)
- Morbid obesity / obstructive sleep apnea → higher risk of airway obstruction
- Extremes of age (elderly: prolonged drug effects; pediatric: dosing sensitivity)
- Recent food intake (see NPO guidelines below — but does NOT prevent emergent PSA)
- Severe hepatic/renal impairment (altered drug metabolism)
- History of substance use (altered tolerance, cross-tolerance)
- Pregnancy (teratogenicity concerns; consult OB if non-emergent)

### ASA Physical Status Classification

| Class | Description |
|-------|-------------|
| ASA I | Normal healthy patient |
| ASA II | Mild systemic disease |
| ASA III | Severe systemic disease with functional limitation |
| ASA IV | Severe systemic disease that is life-threatening |
| ASA V | Moribund, not expected to survive 24h without intervention |

> **MultiCare policy:** ASA IV or V patients, or those with hemodynamic instability or airway compromise, are candidates for Monitored Anesthesia Care (MAC) — escalate to anesthesia if available.

---
sections.pre-procedure-assessment:
### Required Before Sedation
1. **History and physical** documented and signed by LIP — focused on:
   - Cardiac and respiratory history
   - Previous sedation/anesthesia experiences and complications
   - Current medications (especially CNS depressants, MAOIs)
   - Allergies
   - Substance use history (affects tolerance)
2. **Airway assessment** — Mallampati score, neck mobility, jaw opening, obesity, beard, facial trauma
3. **ASA classification** assigned
4. **Informed consent** obtained (risks, benefits, alternatives — separate from procedure consent)
5. **NPO status** documented (see below)
6. **Baseline vitals** including mental status, pain level, SpO2, and level of consciousness

### NPO Guidelines (MultiCare)

| Ingested | Minimum Fasting |
|----------|----------------|
| Clear liquids | 2 hours |
| Breast milk | 4 hours |
| Infant formula, non-human milk, light meal | 6 hours |
| Full meal, fatty foods | 8 hours |

> **ED reality:** NPO status is at the discretion of the sedating LIP for emergent procedures. Recent PO intake increases aspiration risk but does NOT prevent necessary emergent sedation. Document the decision and rationale. **Ketamine preserves airway reflexes** and is often preferred when NPO status is uncertain.

---
sections.equipment-and-setup:
### Required at Bedside (SOAP-ME Mnemonic)

| Letter | Equipment |
|--------|-----------|
| **S** | **Suction** — Yankauer, on and tested |
| **O** | **Oxygen** — NC, NRB mask, BVM with PEEP valve; capable of >90% FiO2 for ≥60 min |
| **A** | **Airway** — OPA/NPA in appropriate sizes, laryngoscope/video laryngoscope available |
| **P** | **Pharmacy** — Sedation agents drawn up, Reversal Agents at bedside (naloxone + flumazenil) |
| **M** | **Monitoring** — Continuous cardiac monitor, pulse oximetry, NIBP (q5min), **capnography** |
| **E** | **Equipment** — IV access confirmed and patent, crash cart within immediate reach |

### Additional
- Backboard or hard surface under patient if CPR needed
- Defibrillator immediately available
- Second IV access recommended for high-risk patients
- Capnography (ETCO2) should be available — detects hypoventilation **before** SpO2 drops

---
sections.personnel-requirements-multicare-policy:
### Moderate Sedation
- **LIP** privileged for the procedure AND planned sedation level
- **Sedation-qualified RN** dedicated to monitoring (NOT assisting with procedure)
- RN may administer medications under direct LIP supervision

### Deep Sedation
- **LIP** privileged to provide deep sedation
- In the ED/ICU: LIP may perform procedure AND provide deep sedation **only if** a sedation-competent RN (± RT) is present to monitor
- RN may administer initial and incremental doses under direct LIP supervision and presence
- If patient becomes too deeply sedated → **LIP stops procedure immediately** → BLS/ACLS interventions

> **Scope of practice:** The sedation-monitoring RN does NOT assist with the procedure. Their sole responsibility is continuous patient assessment. This is non-negotiable.

---
sections.agent-selection:
See Procedural Sedation Agents for detailed pharmacology. Quick reference:

| Agent | Sedation | Analgesia | Amnesia | Key Advantage | Key Risk |
|-------|----------|-----------|---------|---------------|----------|
| **Propofol** | +++ | — | ++ | Ultra-short acting, smooth recovery | Apnea, hypotension, injection pain |
| **Ketamine** | +++ | +++ | ++ | Preserves airway reflexes and respiratory drive | Emergence reactions, laryngospasm (rare), vomiting |
| **Etomidate** | +++ | — | + | Hemodynamic stability | Myoclonus, adrenal suppression (single dose = clinically insignificant) |
| **Midazolam + Fentanyl** | ++ | ++ | +++ | Deep amnesia, reversible | Respiratory depression, prolonged sedation in elderly |
| **Ketamine + Propofol ("Ketofol")** | +++ | ++ | ++ | Balances hemodynamics — ketamine offsets propofol hypotension | Requires two-drug preparation |
| **Nitrous oxide (N2O)** | + | + | + | Self-administered, ultra-rapid offset | Requires scavenging system, limited depth |

---
sections.monitoring-during-sedation:
### Continuous Monitoring
- **Cardiac rhythm** — continuous telemetry
- **Pulse oximetry** — continuous SpO2; alarm set ≤92%
- **Capnography (ETCO2)** — most sensitive early indicator of hypoventilation
  - Loss of waveform = apnea
  - Rising ETCO2 = hypoventilation (often precedes SpO2 drop by 30-60 seconds)
- **Level of consciousness** — response to verbal and tactile stimulation

### Intermittent Monitoring (Minimum q5 minutes)
- Blood pressure
- Respiratory rate (visual count — not just monitor number)
- Pain assessment
- Depth of sedation (document response level)

### Documentation
- Baseline vitals and mental status BEFORE any medication given
- Time, dose, and route of EVERY medication administered
- Vitals q5 minutes during procedure (minimum)
- Any adverse events and interventions
- Time procedure completed
- Recovery assessment (see discharge criteria)

---
sections.complications-and-rescue:
### Common Complications

| Complication | Recognition | Intervention |
|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Hypoxia** (SpO2 <92%) | SpO2 alarm, cyanosis, ETCO2 changes | Jaw thrust, supplemental O2, BVM if needed |
| **Apnea** | Loss of ETCO2 waveform, no chest rise | Stimulate patient, jaw thrust, BVM ventilation |
| **Airway obstruction** | Snoring, stridor, paradoxical chest movement | Head-tilt/chin-lift, jaw thrust, OPA/NPA, consider repositioning |
| **Aspiration** | Vomiting + obtunded patient, desaturation | Suction immediately, left lateral position, intubate if unable to protect airway |
| **Hypotension** | MAP <65, SBP <90 | IV fluid bolus, reduce/stop sedation, consider Vasopressors and Inotropes if persistent |
| **Laryngospasm** (ketamine) | Stridor, inability to ventilate, high-pitched noise | Positive-pressure ventilation via BVM; if refractory → succinylcholine 0.5-1 mg/kg IV |
| **Emergence reaction** (ketamine) | Agitation, confusion, hallucinations during recovery | Dim lights, quiet environment, low-dose midazolam 0.5-1 mg IV |

### Reversal Agents (at bedside for every case)

| Agent | Reverses | Dose | Caution |
|-------|----------|------|---------|
| **Naloxone** | Opioids (fentanyl, morphine) | 0.4-2 mg IV q2-3min | Shorter half-life than most opioids → re-sedation risk. Monitor ≥2h post-reversal |
| **Flumazenil** | Benzodiazepines (midazolam) | 0.2 mg IV over 15 sec, repeat q1min (max 1 mg) | Seizure risk in chronic benzo users or mixed OD. Contraindicated in benzo-dependent patients |

> **No reversal exists for propofol, ketamine, or etomidate.** These are managed with supportive care (airway management, ventilation, time).

---
sections.post-procedure-recovery:
### Monitoring Requirements
- Minimum **30 minutes** of monitored recovery after last dose of sedation agent
- Vitals q5 minutes until meeting discharge criteria
- Continuous pulse oximetry and cardiac monitoring during recovery
- If reversal agent given (naloxone or flumazenil) → minimum **2 hours** additional monitoring due to risk of re-sedation

### Modified Aldrete Score (Discharge Readiness)
